"Come sit here," she said. She moved over on the bench, making room, and he came and sat beside her. She settled against his side the way she had learned to — the specific position that required no arrangement, just the settling. "Nine days," she said. "Nine days until the IPC," he said. "Yes," she said. They looked at the outer machine district together. The Sunday-morning quiet — fewer people in the junctions, the construction crews not working, the generators at weekend output. The junction lights still on. The notation channels running their warmth through the stone below in the morning pulse. "The briefing format," she said. "I've been thinking about the order." "Tell me," he said. "I want to start with what Belobog is," she said. "Before the battle, before the Stellaron events, before any of the specific events. What Jarilo-IV is as a planet. The geological history — Svarog's four hundred years." She paused. "Then the Second Stellaron. Then the First Stellaron. The battle. What each of us did." She paused. "And then what happened after. The combined frequency. The channel output. The Snezhnaya site extension. The equalization framework. Everything that Belobog has become." "Yes," he said. "That's the right order." "The Stonehearts are going to have read the IPC's geological data before they arrive," she said. "They'll know the 0257 readings, the combined-frequency data, the channel output increase. But they'll be reading data. I want them to see the context around the data." She paused. "The context is: Belobog is a city that has been fighting for its existence since the Separation. Every resource it had went into survival. The notation network, the Silvermane Guard, the Underworld infrastructure, all of it built under constraint." She paused. "And now the constraint is lifting. And what the city does when it can breathe is — build." He was quiet for a moment. "Tell them that," he said. "At the start. Before the geological history. Set the frame." "You think that's the right opening?" she said. "The IPC understands resource allocation and survival conditions," he said. "They've assessed dozens of planets. What they don't always see is what a city chooses when survival stops being the primary calculation." He paused. "That's what you should show them. That's what makes Belobog different from the assessment they have in their files." She thought about this. "The equalization framework," she said. "The first thing Belobog did when the Stellaron threat was resolved was make the Underworld equal. Not build a monument. Not expand the military. Not develop a new trade position. Equalize." "Yes," he said. "That's what I should tell them first," she said. He looked at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-IV planning a presentation to the IPC while sitting on a bench in the Landau house with her boots off on a Sunday morning. "You're very good at this," he said. She glanced at him. "At planning briefings?" "At knowing what matters," he said. "What to lead with. What the actual thing is underneath the information." She looked at the window. At the outer machine district. "I learned it from running a planet for two years," she said. "With insufficient resources and two Stellarons." "You had the instinct before that," he said. "You had it at seventeen. The winter assessment." She looked at him. "You were at the winter assessment." "I watched you run it," he said. "You were running it from the third question. The formal assessment structure is designed to take twelve questions to reach the actual problem. You had it at three." She looked at him. At nine years of him watching her run things. "You never said anything," she said. "It wasn't my assessment," he said. "I was there for the patrol report review." "But you noticed," she said. "I notice things about you," he said. With the complete flatness of someone stating a fact. She looked at the window for a moment. Then she put her head on his shoulder. He stayed exactly where he was. "The session with the Stonehearts," she said. "When they ask about the 0257 event and you tell them the sequence — the northern ridge first, then the battle — I want to be in the room." "You'll be in the room," he said. "Not as the Guardian," she said. "As—" She paused. "As the person who was at the contact point while you were in the zone. The other side of the same event." "Yes," he said. "That's the right framing." She was quiet. The Sunday morning. The Landau house. "After the IPC," she said. "Yes?" he said. "The surface transit route opens," she said. "And we go up. Before the group trip. Just us." "Yes," he said. "I want to go in the morning," she said. "Early. Before the construction crew arrives at the Snezhnaya site." "The route opens on a Thursday," he said. "The construction crew starts at 0700." "0500," she said. He looked at her. "0500." "I want the sky before anyone else is in it," she said. He thought about this. About Bronya, who had been managing everything that was on the planet below the dome for two years, wanting to stand under the actual sky before anyone else was in it. "0500," he agreed. She held her tea. He stayed beside her. The notation channels ran their warmth through the stone below the Landau house and the Sunday morning was what it was and nine days from now the IPC would arrive and everything would change again and for right now none of that was required. —— Monday morning Luka was in the workshop early. He had been in the workshop before Svarog finished his system check, which meant he had arrived before 0600. He was at the secondary bench with the surface mission framework and the curriculum document and the briefing notes from the 0900 on Tuesday, going through the final revisions. The Clara consultation component was in — he had messaged Bronya on Saturday and received authorization within the hour, which was fast even by Bronya's standards and which told him she had already been thinking about it. Svarog completed the system check at 0612. He came to the main workshop and looked at Luka. "The surface mission," Svarog said. "Nine days to the IPC," Luka said. "Twelve days to the route opening. I want the mission framework finalized before the IPC arrives so it's available for review if they ask." "They will ask," Svarog said. He went to the secondary display and pulled up the geological mapping data for the Snezhnaya site. "The formation data from Serval and Dunn's discovery on Saturday," he said. "The channel mineralization effect." "I read the report," Luka said. "How does it affect the surface mission?" "The surface channels at the Snezhnaya site are extending through the same formation type," Svarog said. "If the combined-frequency output is mineralizing the formation under the outer machine district's northwest channel, the same process is likely occurring at the Snezhnaya site's geological depth." He paused. "The surface channel conductance quality at the site may be higher than the geological survey predicted." "Which means my sustained-contact window estimates could be conservative," Luka said. "Yes," Svarog said. "The twelve-to-fifteen-second window was based on secondary network output levels. If the mineralization has increased the Snezhnaya site's channel quality, the window might be closer to eight." Luka looked at the mission framework. At the sustained-contact methodology he had built with Seele at the nine-beta practice session. "Eight seconds with the source-material threshold at twelve," he said slowly. "That means Seele reaches the two-way conductance threshold before I've completed the instrument read." "Yes," Svarog said. "The sequencing changes," he said. "If her read is faster than the instrument read in optimal conditions, she should go first. I use the instrument read to validate her source-material read, not the other way around." Svarog looked at him. "That is a significant methodological shift." "It's the accurate version," Luka said. "The source-material read is primary. The instrument read is validation." He paused. "I had it backwards because I was calibrating from dome conditions. At the site, with the mineralization potentially increasing the channel quality, her read is the faster and more accurate of the two." He was already rewriting the mission framework. Svarog watched him work. "Luka," he said. "Yes," Luka said. "When you come back from the surface mission," Svarog said. "The debrief should be here as well as the Guard briefing room." Luka looked up. "Here." "The surface channel data belongs in the workshop analysis as well as the military framework," Svarog said. "What you find at the Snezhnaya site — the channel quality, the formation density, the Fragmentum behavior — is geological data as well as tactical data." "Yes," Luka said. He looked at the framework. "I'll come here first. Then the Guard briefing." "Good," Svarog said. He went back to the back room. Luka went back to the framework revision. Clara arrived at 0830. She came in, saw Luka at the secondary bench, looked at the framework documents, and sat at the workbench without commenting on any of it — the specific workshop ease of someone who had been in this space long enough to read the room's quality and know what it required. After twenty minutes she said, without looking up from the notation work she had opened: "The methodology reversal." He looked at her. "What?" "You just changed the sequencing," she said. "Seele's read first, instrument second. You've been working with your pen going over the same section for twenty minutes." He looked at the section. He had been over it four times. The pen marks were visible. "Yes," he said. "It's right," she said. "How do you know?" he said. "The source-material read is primary," she said. "I've been saying this in different ways since the first corridor contact with your arm. The instruments are backup." She paused. "You know this. You just built the framework from the wrong end first." "From dome conditions," he said. "From what you knew," she said. "Now you know the site conditions better and you're fixing it." She went back to the notation. "That's how methodology works." He looked at the section. He went back to revising. The Moles arrived at 0900. Toma came in first and immediately identified that something significant was being worked on at the secondary bench and sat at the sensor display to be available without being in the way. Pino went to the northwest channel wall, which was where Pino went. Oleg sat on the floor, produced something from the jacket, ate it, and opened the notebook. The workshop did what it did. The notation channels ran their warmth through the stone. The outer machine district did its Monday morning outside. —— Monday evening, Seele was in the bath. This was a recent development — not the bath itself, which existed in the residential stack's third-floor unit and had always existed, but the specific quality of using it at the end of a corridor-practice day. The nine-beta session had run long, the floor-contact leverage work extending into a second series when Seele identified a variation she wanted to test, and by the end of it the Underworld's stone corridors had produced the specific chill that the warming infrastructure hadn't reached yet, and the bath was the correct response. Luka had gone to the workshop to do the framework revision. He came back at 2100 and the apartment was warm and smelled like the cedar oil Seele used, which he had gotten used to as the smell of the apartment being occupied in the specific way that meant she had been doing the things she did when she was home and comfortable. He came to the bathroom door. It was ajar. "How was Svarog?" she said. "Good," he said. "He found a geological finding that changed the surface mission sequencing." "Which finding?" "The channel mineralization from Serval and Dunn's analysis," he said. "The combined-frequency output is increasing the formation density at the site. The surface channels might be higher quality than the survey predicted." She was quiet for a moment. "Which means my threshold is faster," she said. "Yes," he said. "I revised the framework. Your source-material read is primary at the site. Instrument read is validation." Another pause. "You had it backwards," she said. "Yes," he said. "I could have told you that months ago," she said. "You did tell me," he said. "In nine-beta when we validated the methodology. I was slow to apply it to the site-condition modeling." "Come in," she said. Not an invitation with any performance attached to it. Just practical — the door was ajar, they lived here, the conversation was ongoing. He came in and sat on the low stool by the wall. The bathroom was warm and steamed and he had his framework notes in one hand and the lamp from the other room producing enough light through the open door to read by. "The sequencing change," she said. "Walk me through it." He walked her through it. The mineralization finding, the way it changed the channel quality projection, the implication for her source-material threshold at the site, the revised methodology. She listened with the attention she gave to tactical information — full, specific, asking the right questions in the right places. "The instrument validation," she said. "If my read and the instrument disagree at the site, which takes precedence?" "The source-material read," he said. "The instrument is measuring what the network outputs. You're measuring the network directly. Your read is always closer to the source." "Put that in the framework explicitly," she said. "Source-material read supersedes instrument read in all conditions. The instrument exists to confirm." He wrote it in the notes. She looked at the ceiling. "The variation I found in the second series today," she said. "The oblique floor-contact approach from the right side." "What about it?" he said. "The surface stone at the Snezhnaya site is described as rough-textured on the northern approach but smooth-textured at the central survey point," she said. "The oblique approach works better on rough texture. The standard approach works better on smooth." She paused. "We should run both approaches in nine-beta's varying-surface section before the mission." "Thursday?" he said. "Thursday," she said. "And we take the oblique approach at the northern survey point and the standard at the central." He wrote it. The bathroom was warm. The steam. The cedar oil. She was looking at the ceiling with the expression she used when she was working through the full tactical picture — the same expression she had when she ran the notation-channel contact reads, or when she was mid-approach in the corridor, the face that was completely present and working. He watched her and wrote the notes. After a while she stopped working through it. The expression changed — the tactical layer settling, the room coming back into focus. "The framework is done," she said. "Almost," he said. "After Thursday's session." She looked at him. At the notes in his hand. At the lamp's light coming through the open door. She looked at him for a moment the way she looked at him when she had finished working and was simply looking. "Put the notes down," she said. He put them on the stool. "Come here," she said. He came here. —— Wednesday afternoon, Dunn was in the Landau house. This was becoming a regular occurrence — not formalized, just the way that the Landau house had been open to an expanding orbit of people over the past months. Lynx had made it clear to him, without stating it explicitly, that coming by was acceptable. He came by on Wednesdays when his afternoon patrol review ran light enough to give him a free hour, which was more often than he had expected. This Wednesday the house was quiet. Lynx was at the clinic. Gepard was at the palace for a briefing that ran until 1800. Serval was at the workshop with the mineralization data analysis that had extended the morning session into the afternoon. Dunn was in the main room with a guitar. He had not played in eight years. The guitar was in the secondary closet of the Landau house, which was where instruments went when the people who played them stopped playing — Serval's old frequency equipment was there too, the early prototypes from before she had the university resources. He had found the guitar on his third visit to the house and had opened the case and had not touched it. He touched it today. It was out of tune. Eight years in a case was enough for significant drift, and Belobog's cold produced specific drift patterns in the string tension. He spent the first fifteen minutes tuning it by ear, which was slower without a reference pitch but produced more accurate results because he was listening rather than reading. Then he played. Not the band's repertoire. That was Serval's music — she had written most of it. He played what he had been playing in the years before the band, the basics, the exercises that were not about expression but about keeping the hands working through the patterns. He played for thirty minutes. He stopped because Lynx came back early. She came through the main room door and stopped. She looked at him. At the guitar. She did not say anything for a long time. "Don't," he said. "I'm not going to say anything," she said. "You have an expression," he said. "I always have an expression," she said. "It doesn't mean anything." "It means you're going to tell Serval," he said. "I'm not going to tell Serval," she said. "It's not mine to tell." She hung up her coat. She went to the kitchen. She came back with two cups of tea and set one on the table beside him. "That was the Harkov piece," she said. "From before the band." He looked at the guitar. "Yes." "I didn't know you knew it," she said. "Serval didn't write it," he said. "She never played it. I learned it separately." Lynx sat in the chair across from him. She held her tea. "Play the rest of it," she said. "I won't say anything. Just play." He looked at her. At Lynx, twenty-two years old, who had been managing the Landau house and everything adjacent to it since she was twelve and who was now sitting across from him with tea and asking him to finish the piece. He played the rest of it. —— Wednesday evening, Serval found Gepard on the outer wall. She had the frequency equipment case over one shoulder and the expression she wore when she had been working on something all day and had arrived at a finding she wanted to say out loud to someone who would understand the full scope of it. She came up the access steps and he turned and she said: "The mineralization is accelerating." He looked at her. "The channel output went up another half percent since Monday," she said. "That's not the equilibrium rate. The equilibrium rate is slower." She set the equipment case down on the wall's flat surface and opened the secondary analysis display. "The mineralization is creating a feedback loop — the denser formation conducts the frequency better, the better conductance increases the output, the increased output accelerates the mineralization. It's running faster than my model predicted." "Is it a problem?" he said. "No," she said. "The ceiling exists. The formation can only reach a maximum density and then the rate will slow. But the timeline to the fifteen-percent equilibrium is shorter than I said." She looked at the display. "Possibly six weeks instead of two months." He looked at the display. At the combined-frequency stability readings, the output curve, the formation density projection. "The anchor assessment," he said. "Dunn is moving to weekly checks," she said. "We discussed it Saturday. The northwest joint is the most stressed point and I want the data weekly until the formation density stabilizes." She paused. "He should also check the southeast anchor. I didn't have his southeast data in the Saturday analysis and I want the comparison." "I'll tell him," he said. She looked at the outer machine district below the wall. At the generator stacks, the junction lights, the construction equipment at the eastern staging area. "The IPC," she said. "Six days," he said. "The formation data should be in the presentation," she said. "The mineralization discovery changes the channel output timeline significantly. Topaz is going to have the orbital frequency data — she'll see the acceleration herself. Better that Pela's framework addresses it directly than they arrive with a question the documentation doesn't answer." "Tell Pela," he said. "I did," she said. "This afternoon. She was already adding it when I left." He looked at her. At Serval, who had been running the frequency analysis since the day after the battle and who had found the latent capacity and the mineralization and the acceleration and who had been telling Pela everything as she found it, which was the correct behavior and which Gepard had not needed to instruct. "You're staying," he said. Not a question. "I'm staying," she said. He looked at the outer machine district. At the city doing its Wednesday evening. "Good," he said. She looked at him. "That's all you're going to say about it?" "What else would I say?" he said. "Something," she said. "Most people would say something." "You're my older sister," he said. "You've been where you needed to be for thirty years and you'll be where you need to be for thirty more. I'm not going to make a speech about it." She looked at him. At the specific quality of Gepard managing his own feelings about his sister choosing to stay in his city by stating it as a simple fact and moving on. "You could at least look pleased," she said. "I am pleased," he said. "You look exactly the same as when you're not pleased," she said. "I'm aware," he said. She made the sound — the exasperated fond version, the one she had been making for thirty years. She picked up the equipment case. "The southeast anchor data," she said. "Tell Dunn today. I want it in the framework before Thursday." "I'll tell him now," he said. She went back down the access steps. He pulled out his phone and messaged Dunn: *Southeast anchor assessment. Priority. Serval wants the data for the IPC framework. Tonight if possible.* Dunn replied in three minutes: *I'll go now. It'll take forty minutes.* Gepard looked at the outer machine district. At the city doing its Wednesday evening, the construction crew wrapping up the day shift, the junction lights coming on in sequence. His sister was staying. He looked at the city for another moment. Then he went back to the patrol folder. —— Thursday morning, Sampo walked east. Three junctions east from the main market. The building was the fourth from the corner — a residential stack, six floors, the ground floor workshop still running the repair operation that had been there since before anyone currently living in it could remember. He had walked past it three times since the access provisions opened. He had not stopped. He stopped today. He stood at the corner and looked at the building. At the ground floor workshop with its light on and the sound of something mechanical running inside. At the residential floors above it, the windows with their morning light. At the building that had been standing in the outer machine district since the pre-Separation era, the stone in the lower section older than the upper section, the geological record of a building that had been repaired and extended and repaired again over centuries. He thought about Vera at twenty-six. She had found the formation at twenty-two and had been coming back to it for four years — which meant she had been coming back to it since the year before Sampo was born. He had been growing up in the corridors of this district while she was walking the junction twelve approach and learning what the second heartbeat felt like and bringing two children who heard it to the formation chamber. He looked at the building. He thought: she knew these windows. She knew which floor and which room. She came home from the formation chamber and walked into this building. He did not feel grief. He felt the specific weight of continuity — the building standing, the repair workshop running, the windows in the morning light, all of it still there. Vera was in the stone of the cave below. The building was still here. He stood at the corner for a long time. A woman came out of the ground floor workshop — not Vera's building-resident, the workshop. She looked at him. She had the expression of someone who has seen people stand at corners looking at buildings before and has developed the specific patience for it. "You knew someone here," she said. Not a question. "A long time ago," he said. "Most people who stand at that corner knew someone here a long time ago," she said. "It's an old building." She paused. "The family on the fourth floor has been there since my grandmother was young. The woman on the second floor has a cat that everyone in the block has named something different." She paused. "It's a good building. Good buildings collect people." "Yes," he said. "Go in if you want," she said. "The entrance is through the workshop. The workshop owner doesn't mind." He looked at the building. At the entrance through the workshop. He went in. The workshop owner was, in fact, unbothered. Sampo walked through the repair floor — the same stone floors, the same smell of machine oil that he recognized from twenty-two years ago, the specific combination of metal and mineral that was the deep outer machine district's ambient. He came through to the residential entrance. He did not go up to the fourth floor or the second floor. He stood in the residential entrance for a while. He looked at the notice board on the wall — the building's residents and their unit numbers, the shared-space schedule, the community announcements. Ordinary building life. A building full of people, as it had been when Vera was coming home from the junction twelve approach. He touched the wall. The stone was cold and old and real. He said, quietly: "I know your name. The building knows, now." He went back through the workshop. He went out into the outer machine district. He walked back toward the main junction, toward the ward, toward the pastries and the Saturday plans and the path that ran through all of it. —— Tuesday evening, Bronya was at the palace when her phone produced a message from an unknown number. She looked at it for a moment. Then she looked at the mark at her neckline, which was what she did when something arrived that required the Stellaron-attunement layer of her assessment rather than the standard operational one. The message said: *Good evening. I believe Topaz has already been in contact. I thought I should say directly that I am genuinely interested in seeing what Jarilo-IV has become. Not for assessment purposes. Genuinely.* A pause in the timestamp. Then: *Topaz said to send you my best and stop writing messages I'm not going to send. I sent this one anyway. — A* Bronya looked at the message for a long time. Then she typed back: *I'm glad you sent it. We're looking forward to having you both here.* She paused. She added: *There's a workshop in the outer machine district you should see. It's where the most interesting things have been happening.* His reply came in forty seconds: *I've been told about the workshop. Topaz visited it on the previous occasion. She described it as: significant. Which for Topaz is the equivalent of most people saying it changed my understanding of several things simultaneously.* Bronya made the sound — the real one. She typed: *That's an accurate description.* *We arrive Thursday*, he sent. *We'll be ready*, she sent. She put the phone down. She looked at the city outside the palace window. At the outer machine district, the junction lights, the notation channels conducting their warmth through the geological structure of a planet that was becoming something the IPC had not encountered before. Eight days. She sent a message to Gepard: *Aventurine sent me a personal message. Thursday.* His reply: *I know. Topaz sent me one too.* She looked at her phone. *Topaz sent you a personal message?* He replied: *She said: I look forward to the conversation about the 0257 event. And then she said: I have questions about your ice. And then she said: these are not IPC questions. These are my questions.* Bronya looked at this. She typed: *She's a Stoneheart. She serves Qlipoth's legacy. An Emanator of Preservation on Jarilo-IV is—* *Significant*, Gepard sent. *Her word, apparently.* *Aventurine's word too*, Bronya sent. *About the whole planet.* A pause. Then Gepard: *They're going to be a lot to handle.* Bronya looked at the outer city. At what eight years of the IPC being a background concern — first as a theoretical future, then as a monitored partner, then as an unconditional ally — had been building toward. The Stonehearts, arriving in eight days, with personal messages and genuine interest and questions that were not IPC questions. *Yes*, she sent. *But I think we're ready for a lot.* *Yes*, she sent. *But I think we're ready for a lot.* She put the phone down. She looked at the city outside the palace window. At the outer machine district, the junction lights coming on in sequence, the construction crew's staging area lit up as the evening shift began. At everything that was there and had always been there and was different now from what it had been before the battles and different again from what it had been before the equalization and different still from what it would be in another year when the heating reached the eastern residential zone and the Snezhnaya access road was operational and the IPC had been here and gone and come back again. She thought about Mira. She had not seen Mira since the day she walked the Underworld. But Seele had mentioned her — at the junction square watching the construction crews, Seele said, every afternoon. Tracking the progress. Waiting for the heat. Bronya looked at the outer city and thought: three months and nineteen days. She got up from the desk. She put on her coat. She went down through the palace's eastern access, through the outer machine district, through the main junction, east toward the residential zone. It was 2030. Late for Mira to be at the junction square. She expected the square to be empty. Mira was still there. She was at the bench at the square's center, looking at the construction crew's eastern staging area with the focused quality of someone who had been watching the same progress markers for weeks and knew exactly what the differences were between today's position and yesterday's. She had a wrapped piece of bread in her hand from somewhere, which she was eating without looking at it because the construction staging area required her full attention. She heard Bronya coming. She turned. She looked at Bronya. At the mark visible at the coat's neckline. At the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-IV who had told her four months ago that the heat was coming. "It's closer," Mira said. "Yes," Bronya said. "Three months and nineteen days." "From when?" Mira said. "From today," Bronya said. Mira looked at the construction staging area. "They moved the big equipment to the secondary access today," she said. "That means they're starting the run from both sides." "Yes," Bronya said. "Running from both ends toward the center simultaneously cuts the timeline." "So it's actually sooner than three months and nineteen days," Mira said. "Possibly," Bronya said. "I'll know better next week when the Phase Two construction team gives me the revised estimate." Mira looked at the staging area. "Will you tell me when you know?" she said. Bronya looked at her. "Yes," she said. Mira considered this. "How?" she said. Bronya thought about this. The Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-IV communicating timeline updates to a nine-year-old via — what channel exactly. She thought about Seele running the notation corridor sessions through the eastern zone twice a week. "Seele," she said. "She comes through this section twice a week. I'll tell her, she'll tell you." "Okay," Mira said. She looked at the bread in her hand. She ate some. She looked at the staging area again. "Is your grandmother warm enough?" Bronya said. Mira looked at her. "No," she said. "But she has the extra blankets now. The ones Mrs. Sorokina from the second floor brought when the cold got worse. So she's less cold." She paused. "She says three months is nothing. She's waited longer for better things." Bronya looked at the construction staging area. At the equipment that had moved to the secondary access today. At the city that was building something. "What does she say about the heat?" she said. "She says it's the right order," Mira said. "She says: warm the people first, then build the things. She says the buildings will be there either way but the people need to be warm." Bronya was very quiet for a moment. "She's right," she said. "She usually is," Mira said. With the complete serenity of a nine-year-old who had a grandmother who was usually right. Bronya looked at the staging area. She looked at Mira. At the junction square, the construction equipment, the evening, the notation channels running their warmth through the stone below them both. "You should go home," Bronya said. "It's late." "I know," Mira said. "I'm going." She got up from the bench. She did not leave immediately — she looked at the staging area one more time, the final check, and then she looked at Bronya. "She also says—" She stopped. "My grandmother. She says thank you. She told me to tell you if I ever saw you again." She paused. "I don't know what for specifically. She didn't say. But she said to tell you." Bronya held Mira's gaze for a moment. "Tell her she's welcome," Bronya said. "And tell her she was right about the order." Mira considered this with the seriousness of a messenger taking on a specific task. "Warm the people first," she said. "Yes," Bronya said. "That's the order." Mira went home. Bronya stood in the junction square for a while after she left. The construction equipment at the staging area. The notation channels warm in the stone. The eastern residential zone doing its Thursday evening. Three months and nineteen days. She went back to the palace. The outer machine district did its Thursday evening around her as she walked — the junction lights in sequence, the notation channels warm in the stone, the generators at evening output. The construction crew was wrapping up the day shift at the eastern staging area, the equipment being checked and staged for tomorrow. Normal. Ordinary. The city being itself. She thought about Mira's grandmother saying: warm the people first, then build the things. She thought about the equalization framework, forty-seven pages of legal language that she had signed six weeks ago and which had been changing the Underworld every day since. The medical station expansion, seventeen days ahead. The heating connections running from both ends toward the center. The teachers redeployed, the food supply network operational, the junction access reclassifications making routes available that had been maintenance-only for decades. She thought: the buildings will be there either way. She thought: yes. She came through the palace's eastern access at 2105. Pela was still at the monitoring station — not because the work required it, but because Pela's presence in the monitoring station at 2105 on a Thursday meant she had found something in the combined-frequency data that she wanted to sit with before writing it up. Bronya came to the monitoring station door. "The mineralization acceleration," Pela said, without looking up. "I know," Bronya said. "It's in the framework now," Pela said. "I updated the timeline projection. Six weeks to the fifteen-percent equilibrium." She paused. "The IPC is going to see the acceleration in their orbital data. The framework addresses it." "Good," Bronya said. Pela turned to look at her. She looked at Bronya's expression — the Pela assessment, the one she applied to everything. She said: "You went to the junction square." "Yes," Bronya said. "Was Mira there?" Pela said. Bronya looked at her. "You know about Mira." "Seele mentioned her," Pela said. "And you mentioned her to Seele. And Seele told me about the notation corridor sessions through the eastern zone." She paused. "I know about Mira." Bronya looked at the monitoring station. At the combined-frequency data on the display, steady and climbing. "Her grandmother says: warm the people first, then build the things," Bronya said. Pela was quiet for a moment. "Yes," she said. "That's the right order." "That's what I said," Bronya said. They looked at the monitoring data together for a moment. The six-week timeline. The fifteen-percent equilibrium. The planet running at designed capacity for the first time in its documented history. "Go home," Pela said. Bronya went home. —— END OF CHAPTER 68 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 67: Before the Sky Chapter Text Sampo came back to the ward at midnight. Natasha was still there. She had not left because she had said she would be there and she was the kind of person who meant what she said, and also because she had been running the chart review again — not because it needed a second pass, but because sitting with the charts was something her hands knew how to do and sometimes the hands needed something to do while the mind waited. He came through the ward door at midnight with the outer machine district's Saturday-night quiet behind him and the expression that was not the performance layer and was not the underneath-it layer either. Something she had not seen before. Something that had arrived from whatever the conversation with Sparkle had opened. She set the charts down. He sat in the chair by the east window. He looked at his hands for a moment, then at her. "She knows about Vera," he said. "She knew the shape of it before I told her. She said Vera's in the Elation — the running toward at eleven years old, the dark winter, all of it." He paused. "She said the path doesn't bypass the ward evenings. It goes through them." Natasha waited. "The conversation is eventually," he said. "Not now. When Belobog is further along, when the city doesn't need — when the shape of what comes next is clearer." He paused. "She said she'd come back when eventually arrives." "And you said yes," Natasha said. He looked at her. "I said I'd walk. When eventually arrives." He paused. "She said: that's not a decision for now. It's a direction." The ward was very quiet. "Sampo," she said. "Yes," he said. She looked at him across the space between the desk and the chair. "I meant what I said after the battle," she said. "I'm not going to ask you to stay still for my sake." "I know," he said. "That's not—" He stopped. He found the right version. "The eventually isn't a threat. It's a fact about a future I haven't arrived at yet." He paused. "What I wanted to tell you — what I came back to tell you — is that when Sparkle talked about what the path runs on, she named Vera. And then she named the ward evenings, which means she named you." He looked at her. "Not as something the path bypasses. As something the path runs through." He paused. "I don't know what that means yet. I'm telling you because you asked for the specific version." She looked at him. At Sampo, who had been carrying a name-shaped absence for twenty-two years and who had just sat in the junction square and named it to an Aeon's representative and had come back to tell her what the Aeon's representative had said. "The specific version," she said. "Yes. That's what I asked for." "Vera said I'd be extraordinarily happy," he said. "She was right." He paused. "I'm telling you that too. Not as context. As the actual thing I'm saying." She was quiet for a long moment. "The dark filling pastries on Saturday," she said. "From the market." "Yes," he said. "I'll be there," she said. He exhaled. He leaned back in the chair. He looked at the ceiling and he made the sound that was not the performance version of a laugh — the real version, the specific warmth of someone who had arrived at the end of a very significant evening and was still here and was glad. "Good," he said. —— On Monday morning, Gepard went to find Dunn before the patrol review. He found him in the operations corridor with the patrol assignment board, which Dunn had been updating since 0545 because Dunn's approach to the patrol review was to have everything visible and correct before the review rather than during it. "The IPC consultation," Gepard said. "Two weeks." Dunn looked at him. "Bronya's transmission yesterday." "Yes," Gepard said. "The security arrangements for the visit — I want you running them. Not the standard palace visitor protocol. Something that accounts for the fact that the Stonehearts have combat capabilities significantly above the standard visitor." Dunn looked at the patrol board. "The Stonehearts are not threats," he said. "Aventurine and Topaz have been to Belobog before. The Cornerstone and Numby are known quantities." "They're not threats," Gepard said. "They're also not standard visitors. The security arrangements should reflect that they can handle themselves if something goes sideways in the Restricted Zone vicinity." He paused. "The visit will include the Restricted Zone. Bronya wants them to see the contact point and the claimed-frequency readings." He paused. "The Restricted Zone access requires a different security posture than the palace." Dunn wrote something on the board. "I'll revise the visitor protocol for the Restricted Zone section," he said. "Lighter coverage, higher quality." He paused. "Karev and Ferenz for the Restricted Zone escort. They worked alongside the Stonehearts during the First Stellaron battle. The familiarity will help." Gepard looked at him. "That's the right call." Dunn looked at the board. He did not say anything for a moment. Then, without turning around: "Gepard." "Yes," Gepard said. "The Snezhnaya security framework," Dunn said. "The presentation tomorrow." "I'll be there," Gepard said. "0900." "I added a section," Dunn said. "The training curriculum architecture. Based on what Luka's been building in the corridor sessions." He paused. "I want your input on whether the curriculum section should be a separate document or part of the main framework." Gepard thought about it. "Separate document," he said. "The curriculum is a living document — it'll change as the surface conditions are documented. The main framework should be stable." He paused. "Luka should be at the 0900." Dunn turned to look at him. "You want Luka in the presentation." "The curriculum is his," Gepard said. "He should present it." Dunn nodded. "I'll tell him." "Tell him today," Gepard said. "He should have tonight to prepare." Dunn wrote it in the notebook. Gepard went back toward the operations room. He stopped at the corridor junction. "Dunn," he said. Dunn looked up. "The twelve messages," Gepard said. "I kept them because they were worth keeping. You should know that." He paused. "You've been worth watching develop for eight years. Every step of it." Dunn was very still. "That's all," Gepard said. He went. Dunn stood at the patrol board for a moment. He looked at the notebook in his hand. He wrote the 0900 note. He went back to the patrol assignment updates. His expression, for the next several minutes, was doing something he was not doing anything about. —— Wednesday evening, Seele was reading when Luka came home from the framework revision session. He had been at the workshop with Svarog since 1900, incorporating the source-material conductance methodology into the surface mission documentation. He came in at 2200 and put the equipment bag by the door and came to sit on the floor near the low seat where she was reading, the way he sat when he had been somewhere else for three hours and wanted to be near her without requiring anything from the proximity. She lowered the book. She looked at him. He had the quality of someone who had done a significant piece of work and arrived at the end of it with the specific satisfaction of something being correctly built. "How was Svarog?" she said. "He rewrote the validation section," he said. "The instrument/source-material comparison — he built it into the formal methodology with three levels of confidence threshold. Threshold one is instrument-only. Threshold two is source-material-only. Threshold three is both in agreement." "He made it rigorous," she said. "He made it bulletproof," he said. "If the IPC wants to review the surface mission methodology during the consultation visit, it will withstand their assessment." She looked at him. "You're thinking about the IPC visit." "Everyone is thinking about the IPC visit," he said. "It's eleven days away." "What specifically are you thinking about it?" she said. He looked at the equipment bag. "Topaz gave you the source code," he said. "On the previous visit. She gave it to you because she thought it belonged here." He paused. "And what came from the source code being here is — everything that's happened since. The notation network functioning at designed capacity. The combined frequency. The Snezhnaya site's pre-existing network extension." He looked at her. "The IPC is going to see what giving you the source code produced. And what it produced is extraordinary." She looked at him. At what was underneath the mission-framework language. "You want her to know," she said. "It's hers," he said. "She brought it and it grew into something she couldn't have anticipated, and I want her to see the full scope of what she started." He paused. "That's all." She looked at the book in her hands. She thought about Topaz, who had noticed that Clara was special and had done something about it, quietly, in the way that Topaz did things. "She'll see it," Seele said. "Bronya will make sure she sees all of it." "Yes," he said. She looked at him. At the floor, at Luka sitting beside the low seat with the quality of someone who had done good work and come home. She put the book down on the seat beside her. She reached over and put her hand in his hair — the new haircut, the close-cropped version she had trimmed, grown slightly since then into its current shape. He went still. "Come up," she said. He came up from the floor onto the low seat beside her. She curled against him the way she had developed over months — the specific positioning that was hers, that required no arrangement or announcement. His arm around her. "Two and a half weeks," she said. "Two and a half weeks," he said. "I've been thinking about the sky description," she said. "The one you gave from the ridge. The deep blue-violet." "The violet is at the edges," he said. "Where it darkens toward the void." "The void is visible," she said. "At that latitude in winter, yes," he said. "The atmospheric column above is thin enough. You can see the dark where the atmosphere ends." She was quiet for a moment. "I want to see where the atmosphere ends," she said. "From the surface. Standing under the actual sky and being able to see where it stops." He looked at her. He thought about her mother's book and the fifty readings of the marked passage and the moon that was still promised and the surface that was two and a half weeks away. "You will," he said. She turned to look at him — the close-on-the-low-seat version, the lamp light. "Luka," she said. "Yes," he said. She kissed him. Not the quick version. The one that arrived when neither of them was going anywhere and there was nothing requiring management. He kissed her back with the same register. The lamp light. The notation channels warm in the floor below. The outer machine district doing its Wednesday evening outside. She pulled back after a moment and looked at him. "The framework," she said. "It'll still be there tomorrow." "Yes," he said. "And Svarog made it bulletproof," she said. "Yes," he said. "Then it doesn't need more attention tonight," she said. He looked at her. "No," he said. "It doesn't." She turned the lamp down. Not off — down, to the low setting that left the room in warmth rather than darkness. He stayed exactly where he was. She stayed exactly where she was. The outer machine district did its Wednesday evening around them, and the notation channels ran their warmth through the stone, and neither of them said anything else for a very long time. —— Natasha was still there. She had not left because she had said she would be there and she was the kind of person who meant what she said, and also because she had been running the chart review again — not because it needed a second pass, but because sitting with the charts was something her hands knew how to do and sometimes the hands needed something to do while the mind waited. He came through the ward door at midnight with the outer machine district's Saturday-night quiet behind him and the expression that was not the performance layer and was not the underneath-it layer either. Something she had not seen before. Something that had arrived from whatever the conversation with Sparkle had opened. She set the charts down. He sat in the chair by the east window. He looked at his hands for a moment, then at her. "She knows about Vera," he said. "She knew the shape of it before I told her. She said Vera's in the Elation — the running toward at eleven years old, the dark winter, all of it." He paused. "She said the path doesn't bypass the ward evenings. It goes through them." Natasha waited. "The conversation is eventually," he said. "Not now. When Belobog is further along, when the city doesn't need — when the shape of what comes next is clearer." He paused. "She said she'd come back when eventually arrives." "And you said yes," Natasha said. He looked at her. "I said I'd walk. When eventually arrives." He paused. "She said: that's not a decision for now. It's a direction." The ward was very quiet. "Sampo," she said. "Yes," he said. She looked at him across the space between the desk and the chair — the specific distance of the ward, which she had been sitting across for three years without naming what the distance was. "I meant what I said after the battle," she said. "I'm not going to ask you to stay still for my sake." "I know," he said. "That's not—" He stopped. He found the right version. "The eventually isn't a threat. It's a fact about a future I haven't arrived at yet." He paused. "What I wanted to tell you — what I came back to tell you — is that when Sparkle talked about what the path runs on, she named Vera. And then she named the ward evenings, which means she named you." He paused. "Not as something the path bypasses. As something the path runs through." He looked at her. "I don't know what that means yet. I'm telling you because you asked for the specific version." She looked at him. At Sampo, who had been carrying a name-shaped absence for twenty-two years and who had just sat in the junction square and named it to an Aeon's representative and had come back to tell her what the Aeon's representative had said. "The specific version," she said. "Yes. That's what I asked for." "Vera said I'd be extraordinarily happy," he said. "She was right." He paused. "I'm telling you that too. Not as context. As the actual thing I'm saying." She was quiet for a long moment. "The dark filling pastries on Saturday," she said. "From the market." "Yes," he said. "I'll be there," she said. He looked at her. "That's your response." "That's my response," she said. He exhaled. He leaned back in the chair. He looked at the ceiling and he made the sound that was not the performance version of a laugh — the real version, the specific warmth of someone who had arrived at the end of a very significant evening and was still here and was glad. "Good," he said. "Good." —— Thursday morning, Bronya was in the Restricted Zone monitoring station at 0700 when Gepard arrived with coffee. This was not something that had ever happened before. She looked at the coffee, then at him. "Lynx's kitchen," he said. "I was at the house for the early wall check." She took the coffee. It was very good — Lynx had opinions about coffee preparation that Bronya had not previously been in a position to benefit from. She drank it and looked at the monitoring instruments and said nothing for a moment. He stood beside her and looked at the combined-frequency stability readings. "Still climbing," she said. "Serval's model says two months to the fifteen-percent equilibrium." "Yes," he said. "The IPC is going to want to see these readings," she said. "The progression data — the daily readings from the day after the battle to now. The rate of increase. What's driving it." "Serval's documentation," he said. "It's in Pela's framework." "I know," she said. "I'm running through what the visit looks like." She put the coffee down. She turned to face him. "I want to talk through what we're going to say. Before the visit. Not as a rehearsal — as a — I want to know what you think the right framing is." He looked at her. "Tell them everything." "That's the answer," she said. "I know that's the answer. I want to talk about the shape of telling them everything." She paused. "The 0257 event — that's yours to tell. The northern ridge, the battle, what the Qlipoth gaze was. I can be in the room but you should say it." "Yes," he said. "The claimed frequency and the contact point — that's mine," she said. "You can be in the room." "Yes," he said. "Svarog for the historical record," she said. "Four hundred years of geological monitoring. The geological context for why the combined frequency is doing what it's doing to the channels." She paused. "He should speak for himself. Not through our presentation." "He will," he said. "You don't need to manage Svarog's participation." She almost smiled. "I'm aware," she said. "But I want to think about the room. The sequence." "The geological history first," he said. "Svarog gives them the foundation — what Jarilo-IV is, what the notation network is, how long it's been here, what the Stellaron events were in the geological record." He paused. "Then the battle. What each of us did. Topaz and Aventurine were here during the Second Stellaron engagement — they have context for the scale of the First Stellaron being larger." He paused. "Then the aftermath. The combined frequency, the channel output, the Snezhnaya site extension. What Jarilo-IV is now that it wasn't before the battles." Bronya looked at the monitoring instruments. "And Clara," she said. "Her account of the primary junction conductance. It should be in the room." "Not Clara herself," he said. "Her written account, the way it's in Pela's documentation. If they want to understand the notation network from the conductor's perspective—" "They'll want to go to the workshop," she said. "Yes," he said. "And if they go to the workshop, they'll meet Clara. But the workshop is Clara's. Not a palace briefing room." She looked at him. "You're very clear on this." "Topaz gave Clara the source code," he said. "Topaz knows who Clara is. If she wants to talk to her, Clara should be in her own environment." Bronya thought about this. About Topaz, who had come to the outer machine district on the previous visit and had gone to Svarog's workshop and had looked at Clara with the expression of someone who already understood what they were looking at. About Topaz giving the source code. About what Topaz was going to feel when she saw what the source code had become. "She's going to cry," Bronya said. Gepard looked at her. "Topaz?" "She's going to see Clara in the workshop with the notation carvings and the channel conductance and everything that grew from the source code," Bronya said, "and she is going to feel something very significant about it and she is going to manage it with the precision of a person who has been managing significant feelings their entire career and she is going to be very close to crying." He was quiet for a moment. "You're probably right." "Aventurine will notice," Bronya said. "And he won't say anything. And that specific silence will be very loud." He looked at her. At the specific quality of Bronya predicting the emotional texture of a future conversation with two people she had met twice. "You've thought about this a great deal," he said. "I've thought about what it's going to feel like to show someone what their gift became," she said. "The source code. What would it feel like to have given something away not knowing what it would grow into, and then to see it?" She paused. "Significant. It would feel very significant." He was quiet. "We should give them time in the workshop," she said. "Not a scheduled briefing. Just — time. Let them walk around. Let Topaz see the notation carvings. Let Aventurine read whatever he reads in a room." "Clara will accommodate that," he said. "Clara will probably show Topaz the twenty-third section," Bronya said. "And Topaz will understand what it says," he said. "And Aventurine will know immediately," Bronya said. "Even before Clara finishes explaining." She paused. "He reads rooms. He'll read that one in thirty seconds." They stood in the monitoring station with the combined-frequency readings and the coffee going cold. The Restricted Zone outside the window, the northern section where the contact point was, the geological structure of the planet that had been changed permanently by what they had both done at the battle. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "When Topaz asks you directly about the 0257 event," she said, "and she will ask you directly — what are you going to say first?" He thought about it honestly. "That it was not the first time something looked at me from that direction," he said. "The northern ridge, month eleven. The press of death. Something was already — present, then. I felt it at the edge. I didn't know what it was." He paused. "At the battle the looking was complete. Fourteen months of being at the edge, and at the battle it resolved into acknowledgment." He looked at his hands. "That's the sequence. I want her to understand the sequence." She looked at him. At the gold ice ambient in the monitoring station's light. At the sequence he had just described — fourteen months of the edge, of being pushed past the ceiling by circumstance, of the Ice Flames arriving from necessity rather than training, and then the battle, and then the acknowledgment. "That's the right framing," she said. "Yes," he said. She picked up the coffee. She finished it. She set the cup on the instrument shelf and looked at the monitoring station around them. "Eleven days," she said. "Eleven days," he agreed. She turned toward the exit. He fell into step beside her. They went out of the Restricted Zone into the outer machine district's Thursday morning, the generators at full output, the construction crew's equipment visible at the eastern staging area, the city doing what it did. —— Friday afternoon, Bronya went to the Phase One medical expansion completion. She came through the Underworld access and turned east and arrived at the medical station from the residential side. The new wing's entrance was open. The construction team had already cleared. The ward had the clean quality of new construction settling into function. Natasha was at the intake desk in the new wing. She looked up when Bronya came in and her expression received it fully — the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-IV standing in the entrance of the new wing of the Underworld medical station that had been built from the equalization framework. "You're here," Natasha said. "Yes," Bronya said. She looked at the new wing. At the intake desk, the two examination rooms, the equipment staged and ready. Everything the preliminary model had described, now present and operational. "How long?" Bronya said. "Sixty-three days from announcement to completion," Natasha said. "Seventeen ahead of estimate." Bronya looked at the examination rooms. At the light quality, the ceiling grating at the correct angle. She looked at Natasha. "The waiting time projection. Forty percent reduction." "Conservative," Natasha said. "Volkov redesigned the intake routing. The geometry is better — I think we'll see closer to fifty." She paused. "I asked him. He told me. I implemented it." Pela arrived at 1445. She came through the entrance, stopped in the doorway, looked at the new wing with the precise assessment she applied to everything. "The intake desk positioning," she said. "Twelve centimeters from the specifications." "Volkov's routing revision," Natasha said. "It improves the intake flow." Pela made a note. "He should write it up. Phase Two design reference." Natasha produced tea. They sat in the intake area and drank it. The outer machine district's Friday afternoon ran its business around them. None of them said anything specific for a while. The ward was warm. Mira was somewhere in the eastern residential zone, probably at the junction square watching the construction crews. "Natasha," Bronya said. "Yes," Natasha said. "The woman who came the day I walked the Underworld," Bronya said. "The forty-three-year-old. Whose mother has a Thursday appointment." "Mrs. Kovak," Natasha said. "Her mother," Bronya said. "What's the condition?" "Chronic joint inflammation," Natasha said. "Managing well. The treatment protocol requires monthly monitoring and has been consistent for three years." She paused. "The new wing means the monthly appointment doesn't require her to wait in the main treatment space. She'll have her own examination room." Bronya looked at the room. At the examination room that would be hers. "Good," Bronya said. Pela was writing something on the tablet. Bronya looked at her. "What are you writing?" Bronya said. "The intake routing deviation," Pela said. "And the projected fifty-percent waiting-time reduction. And—" She paused. "A note for myself. About this." She looked at the room. "I want to remember what this looked like today." Bronya looked at her. At Pela, who had anticipated the IPC transmission and built thirty-one pages of documentation and had also, quietly, asked to be here when the new wing opened and was now writing a note to herself about what it looked like. "Yes," Bronya said. "Me too." —— She was at her desk at 0630 when it came through — the flag marked EXTERNAL ORIGIN / STONEHEART PERSONAL, which was different from the official IPC channel and which she opened with the specific attention she gave to things that were flagged personal by people she knew. It was from Topaz. *Supreme Guardian Rand. Aventurine and I have been reviewing the geological resonance data from Jarilo-VI since the First Stellaron event. We have, as you can imagine, a great many questions. The IPC's formal assessment process is ongoing — we anticipate the formal consultation visit within the next two weeks. However, I wanted to send this message before the formal process begins.* *At 0257 during the First Stellaron engagement, the geological monitoring arrays that the IPC maintains at orbital distance around Jarilo-VI recorded a frequency event in the Restricted Zone's ice readings that does not correspond to any classification in our existing documentation. The event lasted four minutes and twelve seconds. The frequency signature persisted in the Restricted Zone's channel readings for seventeen days after the engagement.* *I will not speculate in writing about the nature of the event. What I will say is that the IPC has maintained records of significant geological events across forty-three planets and several centuries. The 0257 event is unique in those records.* *Aventurine asked me to add that he is, and I am quoting directly, "extremely interested in this and trying very hard not to send you twelve separate messages about it." He is not succeeding at the trying very hard part. I have been intercepting the messages.* *We look forward to the visit.* *— Topaz (Aventurine sends his regards and, apparently, a seventh intercepted message)* Bronya read the transmission twice. She looked at the claimed-frequency warmth in her hands. At the mark at her neckline. At the palace window and the outer city beyond it, doing its Sunday morning. She forwarded it to Gepard. She forwarded it to Pela. She sent Svarog a message: *Topaz transmission — formal visit in two weeks. Read it.* Then she sat at her desk and thought about two weeks. The formal IPC consultation. Topaz and Aventurine, who had been here before and who had gotten engaged somewhere between the Second Stellaron's defeat and now. The 0257 readings. The frequency event that was unique in the IPC's records across forty-three planets and several centuries. Gepard as Emanator of Preservation. Only she and Svarog knew. Two weeks. She thought about what the visit would look like. Topaz with her precise questions. Aventurine reading every room before anyone knew he was reading it. The 0257 data on the table. The conversation that would follow. She thought about Gepard, on the outer wall right now, reading the same transmission on his phone. Her phone produced a message from him: *Two weeks.* She sent back: *We should talk today. After the wall inspection.* He replied: *I'll come to the palace at 1100.* She put the phone down. She went back to the reports that were always there. The city ran its Sunday morning outside the window, and in two weeks the IPC was coming to Jarilo-IV, and what they found when they arrived was going to change what Belobog was to the IPC forever. She already knew which direction the change would go. —— At 1100, Gepard came to the palace. He came to the study rather than the briefing room — they had been making this distinction since the garden bench, the study for conversations that were not operational and the briefing room for ones that were. This one was both, which meant they sat at the desk with the door closed and the operational reports pushed to one side and the Topaz transmission open on the tablet between them. "The 0257 reading," Bronya said. "They have it from the orbital arrays." "I assumed they would," Gepard said. "The orbital arrays are sophisticated. A frequency event of that magnitude in the Restricted Zone's geological structure would register." "Topaz says it's unique in the IPC's records across forty-three planets and several centuries," Bronya said. "Yes," he said. "She's going to ask what it is," Bronya said. "She already knows what it is," he said. "She's asking because the asking is the protocol. The IPC needs the information through official channels even when they've arrived at the answer through unofficial ones." Bronya looked at him. "You think she knows." "Aventurine knows," he said. "He's been intercepting his own messages about it, according to Topaz. That's not someone with a question. That's someone trying to not get ahead of the official process." He paused. "The IPC exists to support Qlipoth's legacy. An Emanator of Preservation on Jarilo-IV is — significant to them. They would have considered the possibility the moment they saw the 0257 data." Bronya looked at the tablet. At the transmission. At Topaz's careful phrasing: *I will not speculate in writing about the nature of the event.* "When they ask," she said, "what do you want to say?" He thought about this. "The truth," he said. "All of it. The northern ridge, the battle, the Qlipoth gaze, what the ice is now." He paused. "The IPC's relationship to Qlipoth means they're the right people to receive this information. Keeping it from them serves nothing." He looked at her. "And the claimed frequency. That's also yours to tell them, if you choose to." "They'll have questions about both," she said. "Yes," he said. "The combined-frequency effect on the notation channels," she said. "The channel output increase. The Snezhnaya site's pre-existing network extension." She paused. "That's a lot to put on the table." "This is what Jarilo-IV is now," he said. "It's all of it, or it's none of it. The IPC should know what they're dealing with." She looked at him. At the gold ice ambient in the study's light — present, real, entirely his, the visible evidence of what had happened to him on the night of the battle. At Gepard, who had been carrying this specific piece of information alone for months and who was now saying: tell them everything. "Svarog," she said. "He should be in the room," Gepard said. "He's been here for four hundred years. He's the geological record. Whatever they want to know about Jarilo-IV's history, he has it." "Clara," she said. "When it's relevant," he said. "The notation network's extended reach to the Snezhnaya site — that finding came from Clara's direct channel read. If they want to understand the channel system, she's the right person to explain it." He paused. "But she's not presenting to the IPC. She's in the workshop. If they want to go to the workshop, they can go to the workshop." Bronya looked at the transmission. At Topaz's name at the bottom, and Aventurine's regards, and the seven intercepted messages. "They got engaged," she said. He looked at her. "Yes. I saw the rings on the previous visit. I didn't say anything." "Neither did I," she said. They sat with this for a moment. The IPC's two Stonehearts, engaged. Topaz who had given Clara the source code on the previous visit and who had said very few things about Belobog when she talked about it, which meant she felt a great deal. Aventurine who had read every room before anyone knew he was reading it and who had intercepted seven messages to himself. "Two weeks," Bronya said. "Two weeks," Gepard agreed. "I need to tell Pela," Bronya said. "She already knows," he said. "You forwarded the transmission." "She knows the transmission," Bronya said. "I need to tell her what the visit will cover so she can prepare the documentation." She paused. "The notation channel analysis, the Snezhnaya geological data, the formation decay monitoring since the battle. Two weeks is enough time if we start today." "It is," he said. She was already reaching for the secondary tablet to message Pela. He watched her — at the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-IV shifting into operational mode, the unhurried Sunday-morning quality exchanged for the precise forward-motion of someone who had identified what needed doing and was doing it. He had watched this transition for two years and it was still, he found, something worth watching. She looked up and caught him looking. "What?" she said. "Nothing," he said. "I'm watching you work." She looked at him for a moment. "There's a patrol review at 1300," she said. "I know," he said. "You have enough time to eat before it," she said. "I know," he said. "The kitchen has what Lynx left yesterday," she said. "You could eat here." He looked at her. At Bronya, who was in the middle of drafting a Pela message about IPC documentation preparation and who had just, without breaking the operational thread, invited him to eat in the palace kitchen. "Yes," he said. "All right." She went back to the message. He went to the kitchen. The Sunday morning continued. —— Monday brought Pela's documentation framework. It arrived on Bronya's desk at 0800, which was earlier than Bronya had expected and was a testament to Pela having started it Sunday afternoon the moment the transmission arrived. It was thirty-one pages. Bronya read it. She looked at the thirty-one pages. She looked at Pela. "The secondary appendix," she said. "The notation channel analysis section — you've included Serval's latent capacity findings." "Serval gave me the full dataset on Saturday," Pela said. "I asked her to." "You asked her on Saturday," Bronya said. "Before the transmission came through Sunday." "I had a general sense that the IPC visit was coming," Pela said. "The timing of Topaz's previous message and the formal assessment protocol they follow suggested a two-to-three week window." She paused. "I started building the framework last week." Bronya looked at her. "You anticipated the transmission." "I modeled the probability," Pela said. "And you asked Serval for the dataset." "And I asked Svarog for the historical geological monitoring records that predate the battle," Pela said. "And I asked Dunn for the security deployment documentation from the engagement." She paused. "And I asked Clara for a written account of the notation network's conductance behavior during the battle. In her own words. Not technical language." Bronya looked at the thirty-one pages. She thought about Pela starting this last week, before the transmission, building the framework on probability modeling. "Clara's account," she said. "Where is it?" Pela turned to appendix four. Clara had written three pages. They were in Clara's specific vocabulary — precise and direct, the kind of clarity that came from someone who read the warmth through her palms and had learned to describe what the warmth said in language that other people could follow. The notation network's behavior during the battle, the primary junction contact, the combined-frequency event at 0257 described from the conductor's position rather than the instrument's. Bronya read all three pages. "This is very good," she said. "Yes," Pela said. "She wrote it in one sitting. She said—" Pela paused, and for a moment the professional register showed something underneath. "She said she wanted to write it in her own words so that whoever read it understood what it felt like, not just what it measured." Bronya looked at the three pages. At Clara's account of conducting the primary notation network junction during a Stellaron battle, the warmth that she had felt, the way the golden ice's propagation had arrived in the channels as a completely new frequency and what that had been like from the inside of the network. "The IPC is going to want to meet her," Bronya said. "Yes," Pela said. "If that happens, I'd recommend the workshop rather than the palace briefing room." "Agreed," Bronya said. She closed the appendix. "The thirty-one pages — two weeks is enough to refine this into a presentation format?" "I've already started the presentation format," Pela said. "That's page twenty-eight onward." Bronya turned to page twenty-eight. There were four pages of presentation structure — sequenced, with the logical ordering that made Pela's documentation so effective. Not a defensive presentation. An informational one. Here is what Jarilo-IV is now, here is how it became that, here is what we believe the implications are. "It's excellent," Bronya said. Pela nodded once. She picked up the tablet. She started to leave. "Pela," Bronya said. She turned. "You've been building this for a week," Bronya said. "On probability. Before confirmation." "Yes," Pela said. "That's—" Bronya stopped. She found the right thing. "That's exactly right. Thank you." Pela looked at her. The expression she used when something had been received correctly. "The Phase One medical expansion completion is Friday," she said. "You said you wanted to be there." "I'll be there," Bronya said. "I'll come," Pela said, for the second time. With the same completeness. She left. —— The Phase One medical expansion completed on Friday at 1430. Natasha ran the final equipment installation check at 1355 and confirmed completion to the construction team supervisor at 1423. The supervisor signed off. The new wing was operational. Bronya arrived at 1435. She came through the Underworld access from the outer machine district, which was the route she always used now when she went to the eastern residential zone. She came through the main junction and turned east and arrived at the medical station from the residential side rather than the maintenance-section side. The specific approach Mira used. The new wing's entrance was open. The construction team had already left — they were ahead of schedule and had the Phase Two groundwork to begin, and the medical station's new wing was Natasha's domain the moment the final check was signed. The ward had the clean smell of new construction settling into the function it was built for. Natasha was at the intake desk in the new wing. She looked up when Bronya came in. She did not say anything immediately — she looked at Bronya in the entrance of the new wing, and her expression did the thing it did when something significant was received. "You're here," Natasha said. "Yes," Bronya said. "I didn't know you were coming today," Natasha said. "I told Pela," Bronya said. "I thought she might have—" "She did not tell me," Natasha said. She paused. "She may have thought it was better as a surprise." "Pela doesn't believe in unnecessary surprises," Bronya said. "No," Natasha agreed. "She believes in perfectly timed ones." Bronya looked at the new wing. At the intake desk, the two new examination rooms visible through the open doors, the equipment staged and calibrated and ready. The ceiling grating at the correct angle, the light quality warm and adequate. Everything that the preliminary model had described as the wing's function, now present and operational. "How long did the Phase One take?" Bronya said. "Sixty-three days from announcement to completion," Natasha said. "The original estimate was eighty." "Seventeen days ahead," Bronya said. "Seventeen," Natasha confirmed. Bronya looked at the examination rooms. "The waiting time projection," she said. "Forty percent reduction." "Conservative," Natasha said. "I think it'll be closer to fifty. The intake flow in the new wing is more efficient than the original station's — the geometry is better, the patient doesn't have to cross the main treatment area to reach the secondary examination space." She paused. "Volkov noticed it on the third day of construction. He redesigned the intake routing." She looked at the intake desk. "We didn't change the construction. We changed the procedure. The construction was already right." Bronya looked at her. "He redesigned the routing." "He's been at this station for four years," Natasha said. "He knows where the inefficiencies are." She paused. "I asked him. He told me. I implemented it." She said this with the complete simplicity of someone describing the most natural sequence in the world. Pela arrived at 1445. She came through the same entrance, stopped in the doorway, looked at the new wing with the precise assessment she applied to everything, and said: "The intake desk positioning is twelve centimeters further from the examination room doors than the original specifications." "Yes," Natasha said. "Volkov's routing revision. It improves the intake flow." Pela took out her tablet. She made a note. "The construction documentation should reflect the deviation," she said. "For the Phase Two design reference." "I'll have Volkov write it up," Natasha said. Pela put the tablet away. She looked at the new wing. At the equipment, the examination rooms, the intake desk. At the thing that had been built from the equalization framework's medical access provision, completed seventeen days early. She didn't say anything specific. She stood in the doorway for a while and that was the response. Bronya, who had been watching her, found this entirely correct. Natasha produced tea from somewhere that no one asked about. The three of them sat in the new wing's intake area and drank it, and the outer machine district's Friday afternoon ran its business around them, and the station was warm. —— Saturday morning, Luka and Seele ran corridor nine-beta from the secondary entrance. Not training — the actual surface mission preparation. Three weeks out.
