Luka had determined that the sustained-contact methodology for the surface channel read needed physical practice at sub-level-surface conditions before they arrived at the actual surface, and the secondary entrance's approach path through the nine-beta outer section had the mineral composition most similar to what Svarog's geological survey predicted for the Snezhnaya site. "Similar composition, not identical," Seele said, at the entrance. She had the new scythe on her back, the blade in carry configuration. She had also, without comment, brought the surface mission equipment bag that Luka had staged two weeks ago. "No," he said. "The variance from the actual site will show up in the first contact and I want to know exactly what it is before we're at the site without the workshop a junction away." "So we practice at the wrong conditions so we can identify the error when we get the right conditions," she said. "Practice at the approximate conditions so the error at the actual conditions is a known variable rather than an unknown," he said. She looked at him. "That's the same thing." "The framing matters," he said. "It doesn't," she said. "We are doing it either way," he said. She went into the corridor. He followed. The outer section of nine-beta in the early morning had a specific quality — the notation channels at their highest daily conductance during the morning cycle, the warmth in the stone more present than at any other point in the day. Luka had known this for nine months but had not been doing surface mission preparation when he knew it, so he was now mapping the knowledge onto the new context. He put his chrome hand on the secondary entrance's channel junction. The warmth came through in the standard eight seconds of primary junction contact. He held it for twelve. He held it for fifteen. He took his hand off. "How was it?" Seele said. "The fifteen-second contact produced a significantly more complete read than the twelve," he said. "The marginal gain from twelve to fifteen was larger than I expected." "Svarog's model predicted diminishing returns after twelve," she said. "His model was for the primary junction network," he said. "This is the secondary entrance junction. Lower output. Different diminishing-return curve." He looked at the junction. "The Snezhnaya site channels are at the same output level as the secondary network, not the primary. Which means the sustained-contact window at the site might be closer to fifteen than twelve." She came to stand beside him. She put her hand on the junction without being asked. She held it for fifteen seconds. She took her hand off. "The butterfly quality responds at twelve seconds," she said. "Not at eight. Not at ten." She paused. "At twelve the channel warmth is at the threshold that the source material in me responds to." He looked at her. "The butterfly quality responds to channel contact?" "When I'm touching the channel junction with sustained contact," she said, "there's a point where the warmth from the channel and the warmth from the source material in me start — talking to each other. I don't have better language than that." She paused. "It happens at twelve seconds. Before twelve it's just warmth in my palm. After twelve it's a two-way conductance." He thought about this. "The surface channels at the Snezhnaya site," he said. "If the two-way conductance threshold is twelve seconds there—" "Then my read of the surface terrain won't be just the sensor data," she said. "It'll be the source material read. Which is probably more accurate for Fragmentum threat assessment than the sensor data anyway." He was very still. "You should add this to the mission framework," she said. "Her palm on the surface channel junction for twelve seconds minimum as the primary threat assessment methodology. The sensor equipment as backup." "That significantly changes the surface mission structure," he said. "Yes," she said. "Which is why we found it three weeks out instead of in the field." He looked at her. At Seele, who had just extracted a completely new data point from a fifteen-second practice contact and had immediately identified its operational implications and the correct adjustment to the mission framework. He thought about telling Gepard that Seele should be on the Snezhnaya security architecture. He thought about whether that was something Gepard had already considered. "I'll update the framework," he said. "Today," she said. "Today," he agreed. They ran the rest of the corridor section. She ran the approach arc modifications for the rough-stone floor — now, three weeks in, the adjustment was muscle memory rather than conscious correction. He ran the sustained channel contacts at each junction point, building the conductance map that the surface mission would use as its baseline. By the time they reached the corridor's end, they had twelve pages of data between them — his instrument readings, her source-material reads, the comparison between the two methodologies at each junction point. "The comparison at junction seven," she said, looking at the data. "They agree," he said. "Exactly," she said. "The instrument reading and the source-material read are identical at junction seven." She paused. "That's the validation point. If the two methodologies agree at junction seven, they're both calibrated correctly." He looked at junction seven in the data. At the two identical readings, one from the instrument, one from her palm. "We validated the methodology," he said. "On accident," she said. "On purpose," he said. "By running the comparison." She looked at him. "You didn't design it as a validation exercise." "No," he said. "But it became one. Which is how good methodology works — the structure creates the validation even when you didn't explicitly build for it." She considered this. "That's also how Svarog builds equipment," she said. "He builds for the primary function and the secondary validations emerge." "I learned the approach from watching him," he said. She looked at him. "Add that to the framework documentation," she said. "The instrument/source-material comparison as built-in validation methodology. Svarog will want to know this is in the framework." He wrote it. She watched him write it. The corridor was warm and the notation channels ran their warmth through the stone and the city did its Saturday morning above them. "Three weeks," she said. "Three weeks," he said. They went back toward the workshop. —— That afternoon, Dunn was in the Restricted Zone doing the north anchor assessment when Serval arrived. He heard her this time. Her footfall was not quiet — she moved through the world at a specific pace and volume that matched her general approach to things, which was direct and present and not particularly stealthy. He finished the reading he was taking before he turned around. "The north anchor," she said. "Stable," he said. "The combined-frequency shift last week pushed the geological stress into the northwest joint, but the anchor absorbed it correctly." He showed her the notebook. "The joint resonance is within the acceptable range. I'll check it again in two weeks." She looked at the readings. "The northwest joint," she said. "That corresponds to the location where the claimed frequency's harmonic is highest in the channel network." "Yes," he said. "I correlated the anchor stress map with Serval's channel readings last week." He paused. "I sent you the geological data." "I read it," she said. "The correlation was what I expected — the highest harmonic frequency point should create the highest geological stress in the surrounding anchor structure." She paused. "It's not a problem. The anchor is built for higher stress than this. But I want to track it." "I'll flag it in the fortnightly reports," he said. "Separate column." "Good," she said. She was looking at the north anchor. At the joint. At the readings in the notebook. He waited. "I've been thinking," she said, "about what you said Wednesday." He looked at her. "More deliberately," she said. "Starting when." He waited. "I want to be clear about something," she said. "I've been here for— it'll be three weeks tomorrow since I came back with the express train. Three weeks in the outer machine district. Working at the workshop with Svarog. Coming to the Landau house for dinner." She paused. "I'm not in a hurry to leave." He was quiet. "I have the university position," she said. "The frequency analysis research. The lab." She paused. "I also have the channel stability project, which is going to take at minimum six months to reach its primary findings. And I've been doing the channel stability project from here." She looked at the Restricted Zone around them. "I'm saying: I'm not treating this as a visit anymore." Dunn looked at her. At Serval, thirty years old, who had taken the express train three weeks ago and had been in the outer machine district since. At the anchor joint she had just asked him to track separately. At the notebook in his hand. "The Landau house has a guest room," he said. "I know," she said. "Lynx already stocked it." He looked at her. "You talked to Lynx." "She offered," Serval said. "She has a gift for making things that are relevant become available without anyone having to ask for them directly." "She does," he said. "The fortnightly reports," she said. "Send them to me directly. Not to the palace. To my equipment terminal in the workshop." "Yes," he said. "And the Saturday dinners at the Landau house," she said. "Or whatever Lynx does when she has the house. I want to keep coming." "Yes," he said. She looked at him. At Dunn, who had been not-assuming things for eight years and who was now receiving the information that the not-assuming had been correct and was responding with yes, yes, yes. "Dunn," she said. "Yes," he said. "You're very calm," she said. "I've been thinking about this since Wednesday," he said. "I've had time to process." "Since Wednesday you've been calm about it?" she said. "I've been hoping for it," he said. "Which is different from calm." She looked at him. At the north anchor assessment and the geological readings and the specific combination of precision and patience that was Dunn doing the thing he did. She thought about the bread before she reached for it and the dinner and the geological readings sent before she asked and the eight years. "Come to the workshop tomorrow," she said. "I want to show you the channel stability analysis. The full version, not the summary." "0900?" he said. "0900," she said. She turned to leave. He went back to the north anchor. She stopped at the Restricted Zone boundary. "Dunn," she said. "Yes," he said. "The rhythm guitar," she said. "I've been thinking about what it would sound like now." She paused. "If you still play." He looked at her from across the Restricted Zone. "I still play," he said. "Good," she said. "Eventually I want to hear it." She went. He stood in the Restricted Zone with the anchor notebook in his hand and the specific warmth of a person who had just been told that what they were hoping for was happening, and he did not make an announcement of it. He wrote the final reading in the notebook, closed it, and went back to the assessment. —— Sunday evening, Clara was alone in the workshop at 2100. The Moles had gone home. Svarog was in the back room. She was at the secondary bench with the third untranslated character above it — the reaching warmth, the function not yet filled, the one that was going to arrive when it arrived. She had been sitting with it for thirty minutes. Not reading it — she had read it. She had the warmth fully memorized: outward, reaching, further than the current range. She was sitting with the question of who. The third character was not Luka. She had confirmed this at the workshop session on Saturday — Luka's function in the notation was the second character, the one who goes first, the outward reach from the dome into the unknown. The second character was already being filled. She could feel it in the channel warmth when Luka had been in the workshop — a specific quality in the conductance that had changed since the surface mission preparation began. The third character was something else. Something further out. She put her hand on it. The warmth said: outward. And below that: beyond the planet. She took her hand off very quickly. She looked at the carving. At the third character. At the sub-notation she had just accessed for the first time — not visible until the contact had been sustained for longer than she had previously held it. Beyond the planet. She looked at the back room. Svarog was visible through the doorway, running his evening monitoring checks. She thought about who they knew who was going to go beyond the planet. Who was already in the process of going. Who the path was pointing toward, when eventually arrived. "Svarog," she said. He appeared in the doorway. "The third character," she said. "I held the contact longer." "What did it say?" he said. "Beyond the planet," she said. He was very still. She looked at him. "It's Sampo," she said. Svarog looked at the third character. He looked at Clara. He did not say anything for a long time. "Yes," he said finally. "That is consistent with the direction the path of Elation takes when it continues." "The Elation's outward reach," she said. "The function that goes further than the notation network's current range." She paused. "When he leaves Belobog with Sparkle — that's not just his personal path. It's a function in the network." "A carrier," Svarog said. "The way the notation channels carry the warmth through the stone — he carries something outward when he goes. What he was, what he learned here, what Vera gave him." He paused. "Belobog sends something with him." Clara looked at the carving. "We're not going to tell him this," she said. "No," Svarog said. "The order is: the function fills, then the record acknowledges." "He has to choose to walk the path because it's his path," she said. "Not because the notation said he would." "Yes," Svarog said. "But we know," she said. "We know," he agreed. She looked at the three characters. The second — Luka, going first to the surface terrain, building the outward reach from the dome. The third — Sampo, carrying something beyond the planet when the time came. The first — Svarog, the keeper of the record since before. She thought about the functions and the people and the specific way that functions had been filled by people who had arrived at their own choices in their own time, and the record had only acknowledged afterward, and that had always been the right order. She thought about the IPC coming in two weeks. She thought about Bronya and Gepard and the 0257 readings that were unique across forty-three planets. She thought about what Jarilo-IV was becoming. She put her hand on the secondary bench beside the core sample. The notation warmth conducted through immediately — the full primary channel network at its 2100 evening output, warm and present and larger than it had been six months ago, the combined frequency running through every junction from the outer machine district to the Snezhnaya site and beyond. Oleg was right, she thought. It runs all the way to here and further. Everywhere the channel reaches, it's warm. And the warm is real even where we haven't walked yet. She took her hand off the bench. She got her bag. "Goodnight, Svarog," she said. "Goodnight, Clara," he said. She went out into the outer machine district's Sunday evening, which was the city being itself, warm and specific and entirely real, and above the dome the cold light sky was there, three weeks away for two people who had been waiting for it, and beyond the sky the path of Elation was going somewhere further, and the notation network extended from the outer machine district all the way to the Snezhnaya site's geological foundation and ran its warmth through every junction in between. Everything that was going to happen was already in the ground. She pulled her coat tighter against the evening cold. Above the dome, the sky was where it had always been. In two and a half weeks Luka and Seele would stand under it. In eleven days the IPC would arrive and the conversation about 0257 would happen and Belobog would become something different to them than it had been. And in whatever eventually meant, Sampo would walk the path outward and carry something of Belobog with him. She walked through the outer machine district. The junction lights. The notation warmth in the stone under her feet. The construction crew's staging equipment for the day shift that would start at 0600 tomorrow. At the main junction she stopped. She put her hand on the junction wall. The notation channels conducted their warmth through immediately — the full primary network at the 2100 evening output, warm and present and running at a conductance quality that had not existed six months ago and would continue growing toward the fifteen-percent equilibrium that Serval's model described. She held her hand on the wall for a long time. Longer than necessary for a reading. Just to feel it. The warmth was real. She took her hand off the wall. She went home. —— END OF CHAPTER 67 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 66: Warmer Chapter Text Wednesday at the Landau house, the fireplace was going by the time they arrived. Lynx had lit it at 1730 and had gone out, which was her way of giving the house without being asked. She left a note on the kitchen counter that said: *food in the pot, help yourselves, I'm at Pela's,* which Bronya read and found so specifically Lynx that she stood in the kitchen smiling at it for a moment before setting it down. Gepard looked at the note over her shoulder. "She planned around us." "She planned around us last week too," Bronya said. "I think this is a permanent arrangement." "We should thank her," he said. "She'll be annoyed if we make it formal," Bronya said, and went to find bowls. They ate by the fireplace. Not at the formal table — they brought the bowls and sat on the low bench in the main room that was in front of the fire, close enough to feel the warmth directly. Outside it was the standard Belobog cold. The fire made the main room into a different climate, amber and close, the kind of warmth that had nothing to do with the dome's temperature regulation. Bronya had her boots off. Gepard noticed this and found it a very specific thing — the specific ease of a person who had decided the Landau house main room was somewhere boots could come off. She had her legs tucked underneath her on the bench, the bowl of Lynx's soup in her hands, looking at the fire with the unhurried quality she had on Sunday mornings and almost nowhere else. "Tell me about the ridge," she said. "The furthest north you went." He thought about it. He described the terrain — the ice formations older than anything in the dome's geological vicinity, the crystallization patterns different, less organized, the geological record going back further than the dome's formation surveys reached. The natural basin two hundred meters across, the Fragmentum density in it higher than anywhere else at that range. The way the late-afternoon winter light came in at a low angle and refracted through the ice strata. "It looked like the notation network," he said. "The light refracting through the strata. The pattern of it." She was looking at him now rather than the fire. "I didn't have the language for that at the time," he said. "But looking back — yes. The basin's light in the ice looked exactly like the channel warmth in the stone." "The Architects or the planet," she said quietly. "Or both," he said. "I don't know which. Clara might." She looked at the fire. She turned the bowl in her hands. The soup was very good — Lynx had a specific approach to it that Bronya had stopped trying to reverse-engineer. "The Ice Flames," she said. "In the basin." "Not in the basin," he said. "Month eleven was further north. The basin was month four — that's where I started to understand what the ice could do when the standard ceiling wasn't available." He paused. "Month eleven was when I found out what lived past the ceiling." "The press of death," she said. Quietly. The phrase from the classified debrief. "Yes," he said. She was looking at him with the expression she used when she was taking something in fully — not managing it, not processing it into policy language. Just holding it. "When the Qlipoth looked," she said, "at the battle. What did it feel like?" He had not been asked this before. "Like being seen from the inside," he said. "Not from the outside — the way you see someone's expression or posture. From inside. As if what I was made of was suddenly visible to something that could read it." He paused. "And what it read was sufficient. Not more than sufficient. Just: this person's will to protect this city is sufficient for what this moment requires." Bronya looked at the fire. "That's exactly right," she said. "What?" he said. She looked at him. "When I stopped receiving and started taking at the contact point," she said. "What I felt in the Stellaron's force wasn't awe. It wasn't power. It was resistance — the specific resistance of something being overmatched by will and knowing it." She paused. "It wasn't afraid of my strength. It was overmatched by my refusal." "Refusal," he said. "To yield," she said. "Every version of yielding. And the refusing kept going and the force kept pushing and eventually the refusing was the stronger force." She looked at her hands. "The form came after. Not from power. From refusing hard enough that the force had nowhere to go except into my terms." He looked at her. At the claimed-frequency warmth in her hands. At the mark at her neckline. At Bronya, who had stood at the contact point for two hours and fifty-seven minutes and overmatched a Stellaron through the specific force of refusal. "You are," he said, finding the true version rather than the performative one, "the most specific person I know." "Specific how?" she said. "Every decision you make is exactly the right size for the thing it addresses," he said. "The equalization framework. The Mira conversation. The garden bench." He paused. "Refusing the Stellaron." She was quiet for a moment. "Come here," she said. She had her hand out — not the formal face-gesture, not the outer-wall version. Just her hand, open. He took it. She pulled him closer on the bench until they were properly beside each other rather than sitting-at-the-fire beside each other. She leaned her head against his shoulder. He put his arm around her. "Tell me about the sky," she said. "The furthest north view. What the sky actually looks like." He held her and he told her about the sky above the northern ridge at extension range. The real planetary light — not the dome's regulated ambient, the actual sharp cold light that came in at a low angle in winter and didn't diffuse the way the dome's light did. He described the color of it: not grey, which was what people assumed of a cold sky, but a deep blue that was almost violet at the edges where it darkened toward the void. No clouds at extension range in winter — the atmosphere too thin and cold for cloud formation. Just the blue-violet and the light coming in sideways. She had her eyes closed while he described it. When he stopped she said: "Say the part about the color again." He said it again. She exhaled slowly. "I want to see that," she said. "The actual sky." "When the transit route is operational," he said. "Before the group trip," she said. "Yes," he said. She was quiet for a long moment. The fire had settled into its longer-burning state. The bowl was cool now. Neither of them moved. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. She lifted her head. She looked at him — close on the bench, the firelight between them. She put her hand on his face, her way. His hand came up to cover hers. She kissed him. The fireplace version — warm, unhurried, nothing to prove. He kissed her back. She put her other hand against his chest and they stayed in the warmth while the fire did its long burn. She settled back against his shoulder eventually. "Your coat," she said. "What about it?" he said. "It's very structured for a fireplace bench," she said. He looked at her. She was looking at the fire with the expression that was not quite neutral. He took off the coat. She looked at him without the coat. The fire was warm. She looked at the mark at her own neckline — the three-pointed thorn, dark against skin — and then she reached up and undid the top button of the green coat, and then the second one. He looked at her. She wasn't looking at him. She was looking at the fire, which was not a coincidence but was also not performance — it was the specific way she did things she had decided to do, which was without making an event of the deciding. "Bronya," he said. "Yes," she said. "Soon," she had said in the monitoring station. And now it was the Landau house and Lynx was at Pela's and the fire was going. "Is this—" he started. "Yes," she said. She looked at him. "If that's what you were going to ask." He looked at her for a moment. At the coat with two buttons open. At the mark visible at the neckline. At the fire behind her. He reached over and undid the third button. She looked at his hand on her coat. She looked at him. The corner of her mouth — the real one. "Manage less," she said. Her own instruction, applied to herself, with the specific warmth of someone following through. "Yes," he said. The fire kept going. Lynx had stocked it well. —— The morning after, Bronya was at the palace at 0600. Gepard was on the outer wall. This was their standard — it had been the standard since before the garden bench, the outer wall at 0600 for him and the desk at 0545 for her, and the standard had not changed because neither of them needed it to. What had changed was the message she sent at 0603: *the eastern residential construction crew started early again. I can see from the study window.* He replied in three minutes: *third day running. Pela should know the Phase Two schedule is changing.* *Already messaged her*, she sent. *Of course you did*, he sent. She sat at her desk and looked at the city and felt the specific quality of the morning, which was the same quality as before and also completely different. The claimed-frequency warmth in her hands was what it always was. The mark at the neckline of the coat was where it always was. The desk and the operational reports and the city below the palace window were all exactly where they had always been. What was different was something she was not going to try to put into exact language because the language would be smaller than the thing. She went back to the reports. At 0720 Pela knocked. Bronya said come in. Pela came in with the tablet face-down, which meant no emergencies, and a second cup of tea that she had apparently also brought, which meant she had been in the kitchen and had thought to do this, which was Pela doing something she didn't announce. "Thank you," Bronya said. Pela put the tea on the desk. She looked at Bronya. Briefly — the Pela assessment, the one she applied to everything. She said nothing about whatever she found. "The Phase One medical expansion," she said. "Completion estimate moved up to this Friday." "I'll go," Bronya said. "When it's complete. I want to be there." "I'll come," Pela said. "Good," Bronya said. Pela sat in the chair across the desk. She opened her tablet. She was technically working and also, Bronya recognized, staying. This was a thing Pela did when she had decided company was appropriate without announcing the decision. Bronya found it extremely correct behavior. They worked in comfortable quiet. The city did its morning outside the window. —— That afternoon, Serval found Dunn at the monitoring station. He was doing his weekly security assessment of the Restricted Zone perimeter — not a patrol, a structural check, the kind that required a toolkit and specific knowledge of the perimeter's geological anchors. She had not planned to come here. She had come from the workshop, where she had been running the combined-frequency stability analysis with Svarog, and the route back to the palace had taken her past the Restricted Zone entrance, and she had gone in. He was at the east anchor. He had his toolkit open. He did not hear her coming — the structural check involved listening to the anchor joint resonance, which required concentration, and he was concentrated. She watched him work for approximately ninety seconds. He ran the resonance check: pressed the calibration instrument to the anchor joint, held it for the three-second measurement interval, read the result, recorded it in the field notebook, moved to the next point. Clean, efficient, entirely unselfconscious because he did not know he was being watched. She thought: he works the same way alone as he does in front of people. He turned at the next anchor point and saw her. He did not startle. He looked at her for one second, assessing her presence with the Dunn efficiency, and then he went back to the anchor joint. "Give me two minutes," he said. She waited two minutes. He finished the joint, recorded the reading, closed the toolkit. "The stability analysis?" he said. "Stable," she said. "Still climbing toward the fifteen-percent equilibrium." She paused. "I came because I walked past the entrance and then I was inside." "Okay," he said. "That's not a complete explanation," she said. "No," he said. "But it's the accurate one." She looked at him. At the toolkit. At the field notebook with the neat rows of readings. "The anchor resonance is fine?" "East section is fine," he said. "I have the north section this afternoon and the west tomorrow." "Do you do this every week?" she said. "Fortnightly," he said. "The geological structure in the Restricted Zone shifts more often than the outer wall because of the contact-point channel activity. The anchors need more frequent assessment." He paused. "It's become more frequent since the battle. The combined frequency increases the shift rate." "I should have a look at the geological data from those shifts," she said. "I'll send you the readings," he said. "Tonight." She looked at him. At the fortnightly perimeter assessment that nobody had assigned him but that he had decided was necessary and had been doing, and now would send her the geological readings without being asked to. "Wednesday at the Landau house," she said. He looked at her. "I've been thinking about it," she said. "What about it?" he said. Careful. Not assuming. "The assembly of things that work together," she said. "What was in it." "Vegetables from the junction market and something from the eastern stall," he said. "And whatever Lynx had in the pantry." "The conversation," she said. "Through all of it." She paused. "I want more of that." He was very still. Not cautiously — fully present in the stillness. "Yes," he said. "So do I." "The Landau house," she said. "Or the ward evenings when Natasha is there, which are different. Or just—" She gestured at the Restricted Zone around them. "Here, apparently." "Here works," he said. She looked at the east anchor. At the toolkit. At the field notebook. "Send me the geological readings tonight," she said. "I will," he said. She went back toward the Restricted Zone entrance. She stopped at the threshold. "Dunn," she said. "Yes," he said. "In the band," she said. "You were the best rhythm guitarist we had." She paused. "I've told you this before. I'm saying it again because it's still true." He looked at her from across the Restricted Zone. "I know," he said. And then — the corner of his mouth, very slightly. "I mean that. Not the deflection version." She looked at him. She left. —— Lynx had lit it at 1730 and had gone out, which was her way of giving the house without being asked. She left a note on the kitchen counter that said: *food in the pot, help yourselves, I'm at Pela's,* which Bronya read and found so specifically Lynx that she stood in the kitchen smiling at it for a moment before setting it down. Gepard looked at the note over her shoulder. "She told Pela she was coming?" "Probably last week," Bronya said. "She planned around us," he said. "She's very good at that," Bronya said, and went to find bowls. They ate by the fireplace. Not at the formal table — they brought the bowls and sat on the low bench in the main room that was in front of the fire, close enough to feel the warmth directly. Outside it was the standard Belobog cold. The fire made the main room into a different climate, amber and close, the kind of warmth that had nothing to do with the dome's temperature regulation. Bronya had her boots off. This was a small thing and also, Gepard found, a very specific thing — the specific ease of a person who had decided the Landau house main room was a place where boots could come off. She had her legs tucked underneath her on the bench, the bowl of Lynx's soup in her hands, and she was looking at the fire with the unhurried quality she had on Sunday mornings and almost nowhere else. "The ridge," she said. "Which part of it?" he said. "What it looked like," she said. "From the northern extension. The terrain beyond the maps." He thought about it. "The ice formations are older than anything in the dome's geological vicinity," he said. "The crystallization patterns are different — less organized, wider strata, the geological record goes back further than the dome's formation surveys reach." He paused. "There's a section in month three where the terrain drops into a natural basin, maybe two hundred meters across. The Fragmentum density in the basin is higher than anywhere else at that range. But the basin also — it catches the surface light differently. Late afternoon in winter, the light comes in at a low angle and the ice strata refract it." He stopped. "What?" she said. "It looked like the notation network," he said. "The light refracting through the strata. The pattern of it." She was looking at him now rather than the fire. "I didn't have the language for that at the time," he said. "I'd never seen a notation channel at depth. But looking back at it — yes. The basin's light in the ice looked exactly like the channel warmth in the stone." "The Architects built the notation network to match what was already in the geological structure," she said. "Or the geological structure was already doing what the notation network formalizes," he said. "I don't know which. Clara might." "I'll ask her," Bronya said. She went back to the fire. She turned the bowl in her hands. The soup was very good — Lynx had a specific approach to soup that involved things she refused to disclose and which Bronya had stopped trying to identify. "The Ice Flames," she said. "In the basin." "Not in the basin," he said. "Month eleven was further north. The basin was— earlier." He paused. "The basin was where I started to understand what I had. What the ice could do when the standard ceiling wasn't available." He paused. "Month eleven was when I found out what lived past the ceiling." "The press of death," she said. Quietly. The specific phrase from the classified debrief. "Yes," he said. She was looking at him with the expression she used when she was taking something in fully — not managing it, not processing it into policy language, just holding it. "When the Qlipoth looked," she said, "at the battle. What did it feel like?" He thought about this. He had not been asked this directly before. The Serval conversation had been about the month eleven event. Bronya had asked the ridge version. This was the first time anyone had asked about the moment itself. "Like being seen from the inside," he said. "Not from the outside — the way you see someone's expression or their posture. From the inside. As if what I was made of was suddenly visible from somewhere that could read it." He paused. "And what it read was — sufficient. Not more than sufficient. Not extraordinary. Just: this person's will to protect this city is sufficient for what this moment requires." Bronya looked at the fire for a moment. "That's exactly right," she said. "What?" he said. She looked at him. "When the contact point shifted," she said. "When I stopped receiving and started taking. The Stellaron's force — what I felt in it when I was pulling it toward me wasn't awe. It wasn't power. It was—" She paused. "Resistance," she said. "The specific resistance of something that was being overmatched by will and knew it. It wasn't afraid of my strength. It was overmatched by my refusal." "Your refusal," he said. "To yield," she said. "I refused every version of yielding. And the Stellaron kept pushing and I kept refusing and eventually the refusing was the stronger force." She looked at her hands — at the mark visible at the neckline, the three-pointed angular thorn. "The form came after," she said. "Not from power. From the specific act of refusing hard enough that the force had nowhere to go except into my terms." He looked at her. At the claimed-frequency warmth in her hands. At the mark. At Bronya, twenty-six years old, who had stood at a contact point for two hours and fifty-seven minutes and overmatched a Stellaron through the specific force of refusal. "Bronya," he said. "Yes," she said. "You are," he said, and then he stopped and found the version that was true rather than performative, "the most specific person I know." She looked at him. "Specific how?" "Every decision you make is exactly the right size for the thing it addresses," he said. "Not larger. Not managed down to smaller. The precise size." He paused. "The equalization framework. The Mira conversation. The garden bench." He looked at the fire. "Refusing the Stellaron." She was quiet for a moment. The fire. The warmth. The main room of the Landau house. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "Come here," she said. He looked at her. She had her hand out — not the formal hand-on-face gesture, not the outer-wall version. Just her hand, open, asking. He took it. She pulled him closer on the bench — not dramatically, just closing the distance between them until they were properly beside each other rather than sitting-at-the-fire beside each other. She leaned her head against his shoulder. He put his arm around her. The fire. The warmth. The bowl still warm in her other hand. "The ridge," she said, into his shoulder. "Yes," he said. "Tell me about the view," she said. "The furthest north you went." He held her and he looked at the fire and he told her about the furthest north he went, the terrain beyond the geological survey maps, the sky above the ridge at extension range. He described the cold light as it actually was — not the dome's regulated ambient but the real planetary light, sharp and low and present in a way the dome's diffusion stripped out. He described the formation structures at the ridge's secondary cluster, the ones that looked nothing like the dome's Fragmentum patterns because they hadn't been exposed to any human activity since before the Separation. She listened to the whole thing with her eyes closed, her head on his shoulder. When he stopped she said: "Say the part about the sky again." He said it again. She exhaled slowly. "I want to see that," she said. "The actual sky. Not the dome version." "When the transit route is operational," he said. "Before the group trip," she said. "Just us." "Yes," he said. "Like Luka and Seele," she said. "Like Luka and Seele," he agreed. She was quiet for a while. The fire was lower — not out, just settled into the longer-burning state that came after the initial heat. His arm around her. Her head on his shoulder. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. She lifted her head. She turned to look at him — the full version of looking at him, close on the bench, the firelight. She put her hand on his face. Her way, the way that was hers. His hand came up to cover hers. She kissed him. The fireplace version — not the outer wall version with the cold around it and the nine years of weight behind it, not the garden bench version with the conviction of a decision finally made. This version was warm and close and had nothing to prove. He kissed her back and she put her other hand against his chest over the coat and they stayed in the warmth of the Landau house main room while the fire did its long burn. She eventually settled back against his shoulder. He stayed exactly where he was. "Your coat," she said. "What about it?" he said. "It's very structured," she said. "For a fireplace bench." "I didn't know we were going to be at the fireplace bench," he said. "You could take it off," she said. He looked at her. She was looking at the fire with the expression that was not quite neutral. He took off the coat. The fire. The warmth. The bench. Her head back on his shoulder, which was now a different temperature without the coat in the way. "Better," she said. —— Thursday morning, Seele woke up before the alarm. This was common. The outer machine district's early generators had a specific cycle that she had been waking to for years before she had the notation network's warmth to replace it — a low output increase at 0515 that announced the day's beginning before anything else did. She had learned to stop resisting the 0515 the same way she had learned most useful things, which was through accepting that the resistance was costing more than the yielding. Luka was still asleep. This was less common — he usually woke before her, the spinal integration's morning calibration cycle pulling him into alertness before the outer district did. But he'd been at the circuit for three hours yesterday and the previous night's work review had run until 2300 with Gepard reviewing the surface mission protocols. He was on his back. The chrome arm was at his side in the way it settled when he slept, the source material warmth conducting gently outward at the integration points, the spinal integration visible at the back of his neck. Asleep he looked approximately like himself and also exactly like himself, the two things being not quite the same. She looked at him for a while without doing anything about it. She thought about four weeks. The surface transit route coming in four weeks. The documentation mission in week two of the route's operation. The cold light sky above the dome, above the northern ridge, above the Snezhnaya site that was not a city yet but would be. She thought about the moon promise, which was in a different category from the surface trip — the surface trip was real and imminent and the moon was real and not-yet, the two things running on completely different timelines. Luka understood the difference. He had never collapsed the two into one. He said surface when he meant surface and moon when he meant moon and she appreciated the precision, which was one of approximately forty-seven things she appreciated about him depending on the day. The integration points on his arm were warm where they pressed against the sheet. She could feel the warmth from here, the old-gold quality of the source material conducting through the chrome at the standard at-rest rate. She got up carefully. She went to the kitchen. She made tea — not coffee, which was his thing and which she had developed a complicated relationship with over the past months that had resolved into: she would drink it if it was already made but would not make it herself, a distinction she defended vigorously. Tea was hers. She stood at the kitchen counter and watched the outer machine district's early morning through the window. The construction crew's eastern staging area was already active. Someone was running a calibration check on the boring equipment at 0520, which was early even by construction standards, which meant the Phase Two schedule was running sufficiently ahead that they were taking advantage of the morning window. She made a note to tell Bronya. Bronya tracked every schedule deviation. The tea was ready. She heard him get up. Footsteps — the particular pace of Luka's morning movement, unhurried but present. He came into the kitchen. He looked at her. He looked at the tea. "Good morning," he said, with the roughness of someone who had slept properly. "The crew is running early," she said. "Phase Two. The boring equipment calibration started at 0520." He looked out the window at the staging area. "Three days ahead of the current schedule?" he said. "At least," she said. He went to the counter. He started the coffee. She watched him do it — the chrome arm moving through the familiar sequence, the integration points glowing faintly in the kitchen's early light. She had been watching him make coffee in this kitchen for months and it was still, she found, something worth watching. "Seele," he said, without turning around. "Yes," she said. "You're watching me make coffee," he said. "Yes," she said. "You do that," he said. "Yes," she said. She wasn't going to apologize for it. She had decided some time ago that the specific things she did in this apartment because she felt comfortable doing them were not things she was going to apologize for. Watching him make coffee was in that category. So was leaning into him when they sat. So was the fact that she had started keeping her good scythe on the hook by the door rather than in the workshop, because it belonged wherever she spent the most time and she spent the most time here. He turned around. He was leaning against the counter with the coffee starting behind him, looking at her with the expression that meant he was comfortable enough to just look back. "Four weeks," she said. "Four weeks," he said. She held her tea. He watched her hold it. The kitchen had the 0520 quality — early, warm, the district beginning its day outside, neither of them needing to be anywhere for forty minutes. "I want to ask you something," she said. "Yes," he said. "The moon," she said. "The actual version. When the IPC's ships arrive and we go—" She paused. "What do you want it to look like?" He was quiet for a moment. He thought about it honestly rather than quickly. "I want it to be the two of us and no ceremony," he said. "Not a mission. Not a delegation. Not a historic event for Belobog." He paused. "Just us and whatever the surface of the moon looks like and the amount of time we want to be there." "That's going to be very difficult to arrange," she said. "I know," he said. "I'll figure it out." She looked at him. "You're going to find a way to take us to the moon without it becoming an official occasion." "I'm going to try," he said. "What's the plan?" "I don't have one yet," he said. "Four weeks is the surface. The moon is further. I have time to develop the plan." She looked at him. At the chrome arm in the kitchen's early light. At Luka, who had promised her the moon on a specific night with full intent and who was now stating, with complete seriousness, that he would figure out how to make it not an official occasion. "Okay," she said. "You trust me on this," he said. "I trust you on everything," she said. Simply. The true version. He looked at her for a moment with the expression that was the full version — no management layer, just him receiving what she had said. Then he turned back to the coffee. She drank her tea. —— On Thursday afternoon, Pela found something in the Snezhnaya geological data that required a conversation. She brought it to Bronya's desk at 1400. She put the tablet down with the specific quality of someone who had been sitting with a finding for two hours and had decided the sitting-with was sufficient. "The secondary extraction cluster," she said. "Page seven of the updated survey." Bronya read page seven. The secondary cluster had a geological formation in its deepest accessible stratum — the layer that sat directly above the connector zone between the two clusters — that was not mineral deposit. It was notation carving. "Pre-Svarog notation," Pela said. "Consistent with the outer machine district's secondary channel carvings. Svarog confirmed it this morning." Bronya looked at the page. At the survey image — the geological core sample, the notation carvings visible at the stratum's cross-section. The characters she did not have the vocabulary to read. "Clara," she said. "She'll need the core sample to read it," Pela said. "The channel contact is what she uses for translation. A geological core sample is — I don't know if it works the same way." "Ask Svarog," Bronya said. "He said it might work," Pela said. "He said the notation warmth is in the material itself, not just in the network structure. If the carving is deep enough in the sample—" "Get the sample to Clara," Bronya said. "Today." Pela already had it in her bag. —— Clara looked at the geological core sample for a long time before she touched it. It was sitting on the secondary bench, wrapped in the geological preservation case that the survey team used for notation-relevant finds. Svarog was in the back room. Pela had delivered it at 1500 and explained the situation and left, because Pela was efficient. The Moles were at the workshop — Oleg on the floor with the notebook, Toma at the secondary sensor display out of habit rather than necessity, Pino with his hand on the northwest channel wall because that was where Pino's hand went. "What is it?" Oleg said. "A geological core sample from the Snezhnaya secondary cluster," Clara said. "There's notation carving in it." "Pre-Svarog?" Toma said. "Svarog thinks so," Clara said. "I'm going to try to read it." She opened the preservation case. The core sample was approximately thirty centimeters of pale grey stone, the cross-section showing the geological strata in miniature — each layer a slightly different composition, the notation carving visible as a dark line running through the third stratum from the bottom. The characters were small. The carving was old in the way old things were old — not degraded, just settled, the kind of deep old that had reached a final state and wasn't going anywhere further. She put her palm on the carving. The warmth came through immediately. Not the workshop's channel warmth — something adjacent, the same register but without the network structure behind it. Like hearing a voice through a wall rather than directly: the quality is there, the meaning is slightly harder to parse. She held her palm on the carving and let it run through her and thought about the second and third characters above the secondary bench and what their warmth felt like compared to this. This warmth said: here. And below that: also here. And below that, fainter, requiring more contact to extract: the same network. Extended. She took her hand off the sample. She looked at it. "Clara?" Pino said. "The notation network extends to the Snezhnaya site," she said. "It's already there. It's been there as long as the outer machine district's network has existed." She looked at the core sample. "The carving says — approximately — that the network is the same network. It's not a separate installation. It's an extension of the same network that runs under us." "The Architects built it that far out," Toma said. "Or the network grew," Clara said. "Or both." She paused. "The notation warmth reads as continuous. Not a junction point or a connection. Continuous. The same material, the same frequency, running from here to the Snezhnaya site at depth." The workshop was quiet. "Svarog," Clara called. He came to the doorway. "The network reaches the Snezhnaya site," she said. "It's been there already. The carving in the sample confirms continuous extension." He looked at the core sample. He looked at Clara. He was quiet for a moment with the specific quality he had when he was incorporating something into a model that had just changed significantly. "The second extraction cluster's lower stratum," he said. "The carving is in the third stratum from the bottom," she said. "The third stratum is below the connector zone," he said. "Which means the network extension is at a depth that connects to the primary channel structure." He paused. "This changes the timeline for the Snezhnaya notation infrastructure. We don't need to build the channel extension. We need to find the existing one." "How long will that take?" Clara said. "Less than building it from scratch," he said. "I have the geological survey data for the Snezhnaya site. I can model the channel extension's depth profile from the survey and the sample data." He paused. "Two weeks, possibly three." "Bronya should know today," Clara said. "I'll tell her," Svarog said. He went back to the back room. He sent the message to Bronya from there, which took approximately forty-five seconds, because Svarog was efficient in his own way. Clara looked at the core sample. At the notation carving in the third stratum. At the warmth that was the same warmth, continuous, running from the outer machine district to the Snezhnaya site at depth, present and waiting since before the site was a site. "Oleg," she said. "Yes," he said. "Write it down," she said. He wrote it down. —— Friday evening, Bronya was in the Restricted Zone's monitoring station when Gepard found her. She was not at the contact point — the monitoring station, the outer section, the instruments. She had come to check the combined-frequency stability readings that Serval had updated yesterday, which she could have done from the palace but had chosen to do in person because the monitoring station's geological instruments produced a reading quality she could not replicate from the palace displays. This was something she had been doing once a week since the battle. She stood in front of the instruments and she let the claimed-frequency warmth in her hands tell her what the instruments confirmed. The readings were stable. Still climbing toward the fifteen-percent equilibrium Serval had modeled. Steady, warm, entirely hers. Gepard came in at 1840. He had the patrol folder under his arm, which he had apparently been carrying when he decided to come here, which meant he had not planned to come here and had come anyway. He looked at the instruments. He looked at her. "The stability readings," she said. "I know," he said. And then — quickly, before she could say anything — "They're consistent with Serval's model. I checked them from the outer wall." He looked at her. "I didn't come for the readings." "Why did you come?" she said. "You were here," he said. Simple. True. She looked at him. At the gold ice ambient in the monitoring station's cool air. At Gepard, who had been carrying a patrol folder somewhere and had rerouted when he noticed she was in the Restricted Zone. She crossed the monitoring station and put her arms around him. Not the face. Not the hand. Both arms, the way she had done in the construction chamber months ago — but different now, the weight of it different, warmer. He held her with the arm that wasn't holding the patrol folder, and then he put the patrol folder on the nearest instrument shelf and used both. "The fireplace Wednesday," she said, into his chest. "Yes," he said. "We should do that more," she said. "Yes," he said. She stayed where she was. The monitoring station. The instruments. His arms. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "Wednesday," she said. "When you took the coat off." He was quiet. "I want to," she said. "I'm not—" She pulled back enough to look at him. "I'm not managing this into the timing. I'm telling you what I want. That's the managing-less version." He looked at her. At the mark at the neckline of the green coat. At Bronya, who had decided Friday in the frozen garden that the not-yet was over and had been following through on that decision in every direction since. "When?" he said. "Not tonight," she said. "Tonight I want to eat and sit by the fire if Lynx has it going and not think about the monitoring reports." She paused. "But soon." "Soon," he said. She looked at him for a moment. "You're very calm about this," she said. "I have been thinking about this for nine years," he said. "I am — not calm. I am managing it." She made the sound. The real one, short and genuine. "So am I," she said. "Good," he said. "We'll manage together." She looked at him. At the gold ice in the monitoring station's light. At the nine years and the clock and all the rest of it, which had arrived here, at the Restricted Zone monitoring station on a Friday evening, with both of them managing in the same direction. She kissed him. Quick, close, the monitoring station version. His hands on her back. "Come on," she said. "Lynx's fire or we eat at the palace." "Lynx's," he said immediately. "I thought so," she said. She picked up her coat. He picked up his patrol folder from the instrument shelf. They went out of the monitoring station together, into the Restricted Zone's evening, toward the Landau house. —— Saturday morning, the Moles came to the workshop early. Clara was already there. This had been the order for most of the past nine months — she arrived first, they arrived after, the workshop settled into whatever the day required. What the day required today was the surface terrain analysis session that Luka had asked for: he wanted someone to run the channel-network extension data against his surface mission threat assessment framework, to see if the pre-existing notation channels at the Snezhnaya site would change his documentation parameters. He arrived at 0900 with Seele and a bag of equipment. Toma was at the sensor display. Oleg was eating something from the jacket. Pino had his hand on the northwest channel wall. Clara was at the secondary bench with the core sample and the surface mission instruments laid out. Luka looked at the layout. "You organized this," he said to Clara. "Svarog gave me the instrument calibration parameters last night," she said. "I set it up this morning." She paused. "The network extension changes your documentation methodology for the surface mission." "How?" he said. "The channels are already at the Snezhnaya site," she said. "Your arm will feel them on the surface. That means your surface Fragmentum assessment can use the channel contact the same way you use it in the dome — the notation-through-surface-contact approach, not just the external sensor equipment." She pointed at the core sample. "The warmth is continuous. Same frequency, same conductance quality." Luka looked at the core sample. He looked at Seele. Seele looked at the core sample. "The ricochet approach," she said. "What?" Luka said. "The floor-contact leverage," she said. "In nine-beta, the rough stone allowed more purchase because of the mineral composition. If the Snezhnaya site's surface stone has the same notation-channel mineral composition as the dome's geological structure—" "The surface approach geometry is different from the tunnel geometry," he said. "Obviously," she said. "But the principle might transfer. If the channel warmth is there, the mineral conductance is there." She looked at Clara. "Can you tell from the sample what the surface conductance coefficient is?" Clara put her palm on the sample again. She held it. "Lower than the primary network channels," she said. "Higher than the circuit floor." She took her hand off. "The surface stone is denser than the dome's formation material. Less porous. The warmth conducts differently — more slowly but more thoroughly." Seele looked at Luka. "More thoroughly," she said. "More sustained contact," he said. "Less immediate but longer-duration effect." He thought about this. "The approach methodology would need adjustment. The surface version is a different technique from the tunnel version." "Slower," Seele said. "Which means the timing changes." "Yes," he said. "The timing changes." They looked at each other. The workshop was quiet except for Oleg eating. "This is why you asked for the analysis session," Toma said, from the sensor display. "Yes," Luka said. "The threat documentation parameters depend on knowing the surface channel behavior before I write the framework." He looked at Clara. "Can you extract more from the sample? The conductance profile across different contact durations?" "Give me two hours," Clara said. "We have the morning," he said. She went to work. Seele sat on the secondary bench beside the sample with the tactical analysis — running the surface approach modifications against the known surface terrain from the survey data. Luka opened the threat assessment framework document and began revising the parameters. The Moles settled into their respective functions: Toma at the display, Pino at the channel wall, Oleg eating from the jacket and providing occasional comments that were more useful than they appeared. Outside, the outer machine district ran its Saturday morning. The construction crew was ahead of schedule. Mira was probably at the junction square watching them. Inside, the workshop was warm. —— At noon, they broke for food. The jacket produced sufficient quantity to constitute a meal, which was not surprising but was, as Luka told Oleg, statistically improbable. "The jacket has reliable supply chains," Oleg said. "Where," Seele said. "Various locations," Oleg said. "The market. The bakery at junction eleven. Occasionally unexpected places." "Unexpected places," Seele said. "I don't always know what the jacket has until I reach into it," Oleg said. "This is not a deficit. This is a quality." Seele looked at him. She looked at the jacket. She looked at Luka. "He's serious," Luka said. "I know he's serious," she said. "I'm trying to understand the epistemology." "The jacket knows things I don't," Oleg said. "I accept this." "Has it ever been wrong?" Toma said. "Once," Oleg said. "It produced a calibration chip at an inappropriate moment. But the chip was still technically useful. So I'm not sure it counts as wrong." "The Serval data download chip," Clara said. "That time," Oleg said. "But the chip was eventually returned and was used correctly. So the jacket was technically right about its utility." "The jacket anticipated a future need," Pino said. "The jacket does that," Oleg said, with complete serenity. Seele looked at all of them — at Clara and the core sample, at Toma's sensor readings, at Pino's hand still on the channel wall even during lunch, at Oleg and the jacket. At the workshop that had been doing this for nine months and which had, over those months, developed the specific quality of a place where extraordinary things happened alongside completely ordinary things without anyone finding the combination unusual. She had not spent much time in the workshop before the past few weeks. Her orbit had been the outer machine district's junctions, the tunnel network, the medical station, the residential stack. The workshop had been Luka's and Svarog's and Clara's. She was here now and it was — right. The warmth of it, the notation channels, the pre-Svarog carvings on every wall. Clara's nine months of accumulated understanding in every surface of the room. Svarog in the back room, doing his monitoring with his four primary arms and his four hundred years. "The surface mission parameters," she said to Luka, when the lunch had settled back into work. "Revised," he said. "The conductance profile Clara extracted changes the documentation approach in the first phase. The sustained-contact method replaces the immediate-contact method for the initial assessment." "Your arm," she said. "The sustained contact — how long at the surface channel depth?" "Clara says twelve to fifteen seconds for the initial read," he said. "Compared to three at the primary junction depth." "That's a significant exposure window," she said. "Manageable," he said. "As long as the Fragmentum density at the site is consistent with the survey data." "It should be," she said. "The survey was two weeks ago." "Surface conditions vary faster than dome conditions," he said. "The variance within a two-week window is—" She stopped. She looked at him. "You're going to add a pre-contact density check to the mission protocol." "Already in the revised framework," he said. She nodded. She looked at the framework on his tablet. She looked at the parameter revisions. She made two notes in the margin. He looked at the notes. He incorporated them. He didn't say anything about incorporating them. He just incorporated them, which was the correct response. "Three weeks and six days," she said. "Four weeks as of last Monday," he said. "Three weeks and six days as of today." She looked at the core sample. At the notation carving in the third stratum. At the pre-existing network extension running below the Snezhnaya site, patient and warm, waiting. "The sky," she said quietly. "Yes," he said. She went back to the tactical analysis. —— Sunday evening, Sampo was in the northwest corridor at 2100 when his phone produced a message from a number he did not have saved. This was unusual. Sampo had very few unsaved numbers — his approach to contact management was thorough in the specific way that someone who had spent twenty years navigating the outer machine district's unofficial networks approached contact management, which meant everyone he dealt with was in the phone and everyone in the phone had been evaluated. He read the message. *Good evening. I find myself in Belobog earlier than anticipated. The city has improved significantly since my last visit. I thought you might like to know I'm here. — S* He looked at the message for a long time. He went to the ward. Natasha was doing the evening chart review. She looked up when he came in — at his expression, which she had become very good at reading in the past three years, and which was currently communicating something she had not seen before. "What happened?" she said. He held up the phone. She read the message. She was quiet for a moment. "Earlier than anticipated," she said. "Yes," he said. "Which means," she said, "the conversation is now." "Apparently," he said. He put the phone in his pocket. He sat in the chair by the east window. He looked at his hands. "I'm not afraid of it," he said. "I thought I would be. I'm not." He paused. "I'm—" He paused again. "Ready is the wrong word. Present is closer." Natasha put down the chart. She turned to face him fully. "I told you I wanted to know how it goes," she said. "After," he said. "After," she confirmed. She paused. "When are you meeting her?" "She said she's in the city," he said. "She didn't specify where." He paused. "She'll find me. She always does." Natasha looked at him. At the expression she had not seen before — not the performance layer, not the managed-Sampo register. Something underneath both of those, present and settled. "Sampo," she said. "Yes," he said. "Whatever direction the conversation points," she said. "You come back and tell me. Whatever it is." "Yes," he said. "Specifically," she said. "I know," he said. And then — catching himself — "I mean that. Not the deflection version. The actual meaning." She looked at him. "I know the difference," she said. He looked at his hands. He looked at the ward around him — the warm space that had been part of his daily life for three years, the east window with the outer machine district visible, the chart review on Natasha's desk, the pastry from Saturday's market still in the bag on the counter. "Vera said I'd be extraordinarily happy," he said. "She was right." He paused. "About this. Whatever comes next — this was the part she was right about." Natasha didn't say anything. She looked at him with the expression that was hers when she was hearing something that mattered and was not going to cover it with a response. He stood up. "I'm going to go find her," he said. "She's somewhere in the district. She'll have found the most interesting location." "The market?" Natasha said. "Probably the junction square," he said. "She likes open sightlines." He paused. "I'll be back. Tonight, if the conversation is short. Tomorrow, if it runs long." "I'll be here," she said. He looked at her for a moment. Then he went out into the Sunday evening, into the outer machine district that was his the way it had always been his, and he went to find Sparkle. —— He found her at the junction square. She was at the bench at the center — the one where Clara and the Moles sat on weekday afternoons, the one with the sightlines to three junction approaches and the construction crew's staging area. She was sitting with one leg tucked under her, looking at the construction equipment with the focused quality of someone who found industrial machinery genuinely interesting. She turned when she heard him coming. "Sampo," she said. The warmth in the word. The Sparkle version of warmth, which was specific and real under the performance. "You're early," he said. "The path moved faster than expected," she said. "The path of Elation doesn't negotiate timetables." She looked at the construction equipment. "What are they building?" "Access shaft," he said. "For the Snezhnaya mineral extraction." He sat down beside her. "We defeated two Stellarons." "I know," she said. "The resonance was felt significantly beyond Jarilo-IV." She looked at him. "You were involved." "Everyone was involved," he said. "You were specifically involved," she said. "The northwest corridor routes. The access paths that weren't in any official map." "I know those corridors," he said. "Yes," she said. "You've been walking them since you were eleven." She looked at him with the attention she used when she was looking past the surface and finding the actual structure. "Something changed," she said. "Since I was last here. Something in you changed." "Many things changed," he said. "Something specific," she said. "Something you've been carrying." He looked at the junction square. At the construction crew's equipment. At the eastern residential zone beyond it, the building three junctions east where Vera's family had lived. "I know her name now," he said. Sparkle was very still. "The person from the dark winter," he said. "I was eleven. I watched through the gap in the stone at junction twelve. She came through the activation and she came out wrong and she told me to run toward and I ran." He paused. "I've been carrying a name-shaped absence since then. Twenty-two years." He paused. "Her name was Vera." He heard Sparkle exhale. Not with surprise — with the specific quality of someone receiving information that completes a shape they had been sensing for a long time. "Vera," she said. "She said I'd be extraordinarily happy," he said. "She was right." He paused. "I want you to know that before we talk about the path. Because the path started there. At junction twelve, at eleven years old, running toward instead of away. That's where the Elation started." He looked at her. "And it's been going since. Through everything. Through the twenty-two years of not knowing her name. Through what we built here." He paused. "This is where I'm walking from." Sparkle looked at him for a long time. "Tell me about Vera," she said. Not the path. Vera first. She had understood. He told her. The junction square did its Sunday evening around them — the construction crew gone for the day, the residential section quiet, the market stalls closed, the notation channels conducting their warmth through the stone below the bench. He talked until the district was fully dark and the junction lights were the only light, and Sparkle listened the way she listened when she was genuinely receiving rather than performing it. When he finished she was quiet for a moment. "The Elation," she said. "It runs on specific things. Not on happiness in the general sense — on the specific joy that arrives from specific things." She looked at him. "Vera's running-toward is in you. The dark winter's running-toward. Everything you've built in this district since then." She paused. "The ward evenings." "Yes," he said. "The path doesn't require you to leave those things," she said. "The path of Elation goes through them. It doesn't bypass them." He looked at her. "You came earlier than expected," he said. "To tell me that." "I came earlier than expected," she said, "because you were ready for the conversation. The path is not patient. When you're ready, it arrives." She paused. "Are you ready?" He looked at the junction square. At the construction equipment. At the building three junctions east. "Tell me what the conversation is first," he said. "The real version. Not the preamble." She looked at him with the expression that was her actual expression — the one underneath the mask, the one that was genuinely warm and genuinely serious simultaneously. "The path continues," she said. "There are things it needs you for that exist beyond Belobog. Not immediately — Belobog needs you for a while yet, and the path knows that. But eventually." She paused. "When eventually arrives, I'll come back. And the conversation at that point will be: will you walk with me." "And now?" he said. "Now," she said, "I came to tell you that the path sees Vera. The running-toward at eleven years old. The dark winter. The name-shaped absence." She paused. "The Elation that runs on joy that has been specifically earned — Vera's in that. She's in the path. She has been since junction twelve." She looked at him. "You're not walking from her. You're walking with her. The way she asked you to." The junction square was very quiet. "Running toward," he said. "Always," Sparkle said. He sat with that for a while. The outer machine district. The district that was his. The building three junctions east. The ward that was three junctions in the other direction. "All right," he said. "I'll walk." "Not yet," she said. "When eventually arrives." "All right," he said again. "When eventually arrives." She looked at him. The warmth in it. "Go back to your ward," she said. "She's waiting." He looked at her. "You know about Natasha." "I know about everything that runs on the Elation," she said. "That's what I am." She stood up from the bench. She looked at the district around them — at the junction lights and the construction crew's staged equipment and the notation channels warm in the stone below. "Belobog is exceptional," she said. "Even by the standards of things I've seen." She paused. "Vera knew." "She knew," he said. "Go," Sparkle said. He went. —— Saturday night in the residential stack, the lamp was on. It was always on at this hour. Seele had developed an opinion about the lamp early in their time in this apartment — specifically, that the lamp at this hour produced the right quality of light for reading and for being in the same room as Luka without either activity requiring announcement. The lamp was on and she was reading and he was at the kitchen table going through the surface mission framework revisions, which he had been doing since 2100, and it was now 2230 and neither of them had said anything to each other for approximately forty minutes. This was comfortable in the way that only very specific kinds of together were comfortable. She lowered the book. "The conductance coefficient," she said. "Still working on it," he said, without looking up. "The twelve-to-fifteen-second window feels long for initial assessment in an unknown environment," she said. "The threat window during sustained contact is significant." "I know," he said. "I'm trying to shorten it." "What's the minimum?" she said. "Eight seconds according to Clara's extract," he said. "But eight seconds is a floor, not a target. Eight seconds gives you half the read quality of twelve." "Half the read quality might be sufficient for initial assessment," she said. "You don't need the full picture on first contact. You need threat/no-threat." He looked up from the tablet. He looked at her. He looked back at the tablet. He wrote something. "You're right," he said. "I know," she said. "Don't say—" he started. "I mean it literally," she said. "I know I'm right. That's not the deflection version." He looked at her again. The corner of his mouth. He went back to writing. She went back to reading. After a while she said: "Are you done?" "Almost," he said. She got up from the low seat. She went to the kitchen and stood behind him and looked at the revised framework over his shoulder. He let her look. She read three paragraphs. "Change the initial assessment note on page four," she said. "The threat/no-threat threshold is too conservative for surface conditions. You wrote the dome threshold. Surface Fragmentum behaves differently — higher variance, lower baseline density. Your no-threat threshold should be lower." "How much lower?" he said. "Ask Svarog," she said. "He'll have the surface baseline data from the geological surveys." He wrote: *check Svarog — surface Fragmentum baseline for threshold revision.* He underlined it. She stayed behind him. She put her hands on his shoulders. He went still in the specific way he went still when she did something that landed where it landed. She looked at the top of his head — the new haircut, the one she had trimmed in the workshop before the last contact event, the version of him that had been building across months and the cave and the arm and all the rest. She looked at the spinal integration visible at the back of his neck. She thought about the million cycles and what they had built in him and what the shape of that thing felt like from the outside, which was: like standing next to someone whose foundation was deeper than the ground they were standing on. "Three weeks and six days," she said. "Yes," he said. She bent down and kissed the back of his neck, which was warm and chrome-adjacent where the integration met the skin. He exhaled. She stayed there for a moment. "Come to bed," she said. He saved the document. He closed the tablet. He came. —— END OF CHAPTER 66 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 65: The Distance Between Here and There Chapter Text Sunday breakfast at the palace was not a formal affair. Bronya had started it approximately four months ago, not through any announcement or policy decision but through the accumulation of Sunday mornings where the right people happened to be in the palace at breakfast time. It had settled into a recurring thing: Pela, who was always in the palace; Bronya, who was always in the palace; whoever else had drifted in through the course of the previous evening and had stayed. The kitchen produced eggs and bread and whatever the junction market had sent up that week, and they ate at the small table by the east window rather than the formal dining room, and they talked about things that were not always operational. Clara arrived at 0840. She came through the palace's eastern entrance, which was closer to the outer machine district and which Bronya had quietly made clear was available to her. She had Toma with her — just Toma, which Bronya had come to recognize as Clara's processing configuration, the same as when she brought only Toma to the twenty-second section briefing. Pela was already at the table with her monitoring tablet, which she had put face-down beside her plate in a gesture that was her version of not working. Bronya was at the counter getting tea. "Sit," Bronya said. "There's bread and eggs." They sat. Clara looked at the table — at Pela's face-down tablet, at the east window with the outer city visible, at the bread. She took a piece. She ate it. Toma took eggs. For a while nobody said anything specific, which was comfortable rather than awkward — breakfast had its own pace and the question Clara had come with didn't need to be the first thing.
