Pela said, eventually: "The second extraction cluster survey." "I read it," Bronya said. "I know you read it," Pela said. "The Svarog geological buffer note. He said—" "The cave is not a resource," Bronya said. "I have it memorized." "He said it very specifically," Pela said. "I want to make sure it's in the formal documentation as a designated boundary, not just an operational note." "Write it up," Bronya said. "I'll sign it this afternoon." Pela picked up her tablet. She turned it face-up, typed three lines, turned it face-down again. This took twelve seconds. She went back to her eggs. Clara watched this exchange. "Pela," she said. "Yes," Pela said. "Does the tablet turn face-down at every breakfast or just this one?" Clara said. "Every breakfast with people in it," Pela said. "The tablet turns face-down and stays face-down unless something is time-sensitive." "The cluster survey wasn't time-sensitive," Clara said. "No," Pela said. "That was me needing to write it before I forgot." She looked at Clara. "I would have forgotten by Monday." "The documentation is the most important part," Clara said. "Always," Pela agreed, with the completeness of someone who had been saying this for two years and would keep saying it. Clara ate some bread. She looked at Bronya, who was sitting across from her with the morning quality — the unhurried version, the one that existed before the Guardianship started requiring things from her. "The twenty-third section," Clara said. "Tell me," Bronya said. "It's the people," Clara said. "The first twenty-two sections are the network's architecture. The twenty-third describes who uses it." She paused. "By function. The Guardian who receives and claims. The Emanator who preserves. The two who remember. The keeper of the lineage." She looked at her hands, then back up. "That last one is me." Bronya was quiet. "Svarog says he doesn't know," Clara said. "Whether the Architects knew specifically who would fill the functions, or whether they described the functions and people happened to fill them." She paused. "I want to know what you think." Bronya looked at her. She didn't answer immediately — not because she was managing the answer, but because she was arriving at it. "I think," Bronya said, "it doesn't change what you did." Clara looked at her. "If the Architects knew your name, or if they described a function that happened to fit you — either way, you were the one with your hands on the notation junctions for two hours and eleven minutes," Bronya said. "Either way, it was your hands that held it." She paused. "The section describes you because you are that. Not the other way around." Clara was quiet for a moment. "That's the version Seele said too," she said. "Sort of. She said the role exists, I fit it, which one came first doesn't change the fitting." "She's right," Bronya said. "You always agree with Seele," Clara said. "She's usually right," Bronya said. "That's what Gepard says about Pela," Clara said. Pela looked up from her plate. "He says that?" "He said it to Luka last week," Clara said. "In the junction corridor. I was coming the other direction." She paused. "He said: Pela's models are right. Trust the models." Pela looked at her plate. Her expression did the thing it did when she was receiving something she was not going to perform a reaction to, which meant she filed it and it went somewhere important. "The twenty-third section," Bronya said, bringing them back. "The two who remember. That's Seele and Luka." "Yes," Clara said. "Does it say anything about what they remember?" Bronya said. "Not specifically," Clara said. "Just that the function of the memory carriers is to — I'm still translating the exact notation. Something like: complete the circuit. The network requires the return of what it held." She paused. "The second Stellaron held Seele and Luka's memories. Returning them completed a function that the notation network was designed around." She paused. "The network worked better after the contact event at the formation chamber. The channels have been running more efficiently since then. Serval noticed it in the frequency data." "She did," Bronya said. "She presented the analysis yesterday." "And the Emanator," Clara said. "Does Gepard know that's what the section calls him?" "Not yet," Bronya said. "Are you going to tell him?" "When there's a right moment," Bronya said. "There's also Svarog," Clara said. "Is there a function for Svarog in the twenty-third section?" Bronya looked at her. "Is there?" "I don't know yet," Clara said. "There are three characters in the section that I can't translate. They're in a notation register I haven't encountered in the previous twenty-two sections." She paused. "I think one of them might be his function. But I'm not sure." "Keep going," Bronya said. "I will," Clara said. She took more bread. "Bronya." "Yes," Bronya said. "Is it strange," Clara said, "to be in the twenty-second section? The Guardian who receives and claims?" Bronya thought about the honest version. "No," she said. "Because the section describes something that was already happening. I didn't read it and become those things. I read it and recognized them." She paused. "What was strange was knowing that someone built the description before it happened. But Serval told me we found the activation pathway ourselves, from the data. Nobody handed us the answer. We found it." She paused. "The section describes the function. The people filled the function. In that order." Clara looked at her. "That's the order that matters." "Yes," Bronya said. "The city isn't a prediction. The people in it aren't characters in a notation section. The section describes what happened to people who made their own choices and arrived where they arrived." Clara was quiet for a moment. She looked at the east window. At the city outside. "Oleg wrote it in the notebook," she said. "What I said when I came back from the workshop yesterday." "What did you write?" Bronya said. "I wrote: the network was built for all of us. I read it and then I sat on the workshop floor for a while. Then I came to the junction square. Mira waved. Oleg waved back. The heat is coming in three months and twenty-seven days." Bronya looked at her. "Oleg put it in the notebook without reading it first," Clara said. "He didn't look. He just put it in." "That's the correct approach," Pela said, from behind her tablet which had turned face-up again for something. "It is," Bronya said. They ate. The east window. The morning. The outer city running its Sunday. —— After breakfast, Toma stayed to help Pela with something that had become relevant to the monitoring data and which Toma had opinions about. Clara went back to the outer machine district alone. She came to the workshop. Svarog was in the back room. Pino was at the northwest channel wall — he was always at the northwest channel wall when he arrived before Clara, which he frequently did on Sunday mornings, the walk from wherever he had been the night before routing past the workshop the way walking routes tend to route past the places that matter. He looked at her when she came in. "How was breakfast?" "Good," she said. "Bronya said the function came first and we filled it." "She's right," Pino said. "Everyone says she's right," Clara said. "She usually is," he said. Clara sat down on the secondary bench. She looked at the twenty-third section's carving above it. At the three characters she couldn't read. She looked at them for a while. "Pino," she said. "Yes," he said. "The three characters I can't translate," she said. She pointed at the right section of the carving. "What do you read in those?" He came to the bench. He stood beside her and looked at the three characters. He had his hand on the northwest channel junction, which was not unusual — Pino's hand went to the channel junction the way most people's hands went to their pockets. "The first one," he said slowly. "I've seen it before. In corridor nine-beta, at the secondary junction point." He thought. "Something like — witness. Or record. Between the two." "Keeper of the record," Clara said. "Maybe," he said. "That's Svarog," she said. Pino looked at the character. He looked at the back room where Svarog was working. He looked at Clara. "Tell him," he said. "I'm still translating," she said. "Tell him when you're done," he said. "Yes," she said. She put her hand on the carving. The warmth conducted through immediately — the twenty-third section was the most responsive she had encountered, the notation channels in this section running at higher output than anywhere else in the workshop. She read the first of the three characters again, let the warmth tell her what she knew. The warmth said: keeper. And below that: of the record. And below that, smaller, the sub-notation she had missed in the previous passes: since before. "Svarog," she said. He came to the doorway. "The first of the three," she said. "Keeper of the record since before." She paused. "That's you, isn't it." He looked at the character. He did not say anything for a moment. "Yes," he said. "How long have you known?" she said. "Since approximately the one hundred and third year of my monitoring," he said. "I encountered the character in a secondary channel notation in the outer district's geological structure. I could not translate it fully. I knew the category it belonged to." He paused. "I knew it referred to a continuous monitoring function. I suspected it referred to me." "You suspected for three hundred years," Clara said. "I suspected," he said. "Now you've confirmed it." "Does it change anything?" she said. He looked at the carving. "No," he said. "I would have continued regardless." "I know," she said. "I'm asking if knowing changes anything." He thought about this. "It names the thing I was already doing," he said. "Which is — clarifying. But only that." Clara looked at the second character. She felt the warmth. She said: "The second one is harder. I think it's the function that comes after the Snezhnaya expansion. The one that needs all the others to be stable first." "Leave it for now," Svarog said. "The second character will wait." "How long?" she said. "Until you're ready for it," he said. "And until the Snezhnaya expansion is far enough along that the context makes sense." He paused. "Some things should arrive in order." Clara looked at the second character. She looked at the third. She took her hand off the carving. "Okay," she said. She got up from the bench. She went to the northwest channel junction and stood beside Pino. She put her hand on the wall below his. The channel was warm. —— Sunday afternoon, Sampo walked the outer machine district with Natasha. This had not been planned — Sunday afternoons were not a scheduled thing between them, they just found themselves in the same place at the same time with some regularity. He had come by the ward to tell her something about the market's second-kind pastry (which he had now tried and which was not, in his assessment, superior to the dark filling ones, but which was interesting), and she had been doing the last of her documentation and had looked up and said: "I'm done in ten minutes. Walk with me." He waited ten minutes. She put on her jacket. They walked. The outer machine district on a Sunday afternoon had its own specific rhythm — less construction noise than weekdays, more residents in the junction corridors, children at the square, the junction market running at its weekend pace. They walked without a specific destination, which was its own kind of destination. "Sparkle," Sampo said. Natasha looked at him. "She's going to come back," he said. "She said she would. She usually does what she says she'll do, in her way." He paused. "I've been thinking about the conversation." "What have you decided?" Natasha said. He thought about this. They were at the second junction, the one with the generator access ladder that he had used as a shortcut since he was nine years old. He looked at it. "I haven't decided anything," he said. "I'm trying to figure out what the question even is." He paused. "The path of Elation is — it's not a place I'm going to. It's something I walk. I've been walking it since the dark winter. The question Sparkle is going to ask isn't whether I'm on the path. The question is whether the path goes outward from Belobog." He paused. "And I don't know the answer." "What would you need to know it?" Natasha said. "I don't know that either," he said. They walked. "The market on Saturday," he said. "The through-route. Vera's building." He looked at the junction ahead. "I've been carrying her for twenty-two years without knowing her name. And now I know the name and I know the building and I know that the path she was on before the dark winter was — toward something that existed in those corridors." He paused. "She said I'd be extraordinarily happy. And I am." He looked at Natasha. "I am. This is that." She was looking at him with the expression she had when she was listening fully and not preparing an answer. "But the path keeps going," he said. "That's the thing about Elation. It doesn't stop at happy." He paused. "Sparkle understands that. I don't know if I'm ready for her to be right about it." "Ready for what, specifically?" Natasha said. "For it to go somewhere beyond this," he said. "Beyond the northwest corridor and the market and—" He stopped. He looked at her. "Beyond the ward evenings," he said. "That's the honest version." She was quiet. "I told you months ago," he said, "that what was between us is the most real thing I've had since before the dark winter. I meant it completely." He paused. "And the path of Elation is real too. Both things are real and I'm trying to figure out if they can both be true at the same time." "Can they?" Natasha said. "I don't know," he said. "I want them to be." She looked at the junction. At the corridor ahead. At Sampo, who had been running these routes for twenty years and who was standing at the second junction telling her something difficult because it was true and he had decided that telling her true things was the correct approach. "Sampo," she said. "Yes," he said. "The path going outward from Belobog," she said. "When Sparkle comes back and the conversation happens — are you going to tell me when it starts?" He looked at her. "Yes," he said. "Before you decide anything," she said. "Yes," he said. She nodded. "Then I'll be here for the deciding," she said. "Whatever direction it points." He looked at her. At the expression that was not managed or performed. "You're not going to tell me what I should decide," he said. "No," she said. "It's your path." "Most people would have an opinion," he said. "I have plenty of opinions," she said. "On most things. On this one—" She paused. "On this one I think you have to walk it and find out." He looked at the junction corridor. At the outer machine district around them. He thought about Vera's building three junctions east and the woman who had died and the name that had been in the Stellaron's memory for twenty-two years and the pastries and the path that kept going regardless. "Ward evenings," he said. "Yes," she said. "Every week," he said. "Every week," she said. "Until one of us says otherwise." He looked at her. "I'm not saying otherwise." "I know," she said. They kept walking. —— Monday morning Gepard was on the outer wall at 0600 and Bronya was not. This was unusual. She was usually at the wall within fifteen minutes of him — it was not coordinated, it was just the way the mornings had run for a year. He noted her absence and went back to the patrol folder. At 0630 his phone produced a message: *I'm in the Underworld. Eastern residential. Come if you want.* He signed the patrol report. He left the duty officer with the standing instructions. He went to the eastern residential zone. He found her at the junction square. She was standing near the bench at the square's center, looking at the eastern generator access, where the construction crew's equipment was staged but nobody was working yet — 0630 was before the construction shift started. The city was in its early-morning quality, the junction lights still on, the residential sections quiet. He came to stand beside her. "Mira's building," she said. She nodded at the fourth building from the east corner. "The cold room faces the uninsulated wall on the north side." She paused. "They're running the thermal connection through the eastern section in week seven of the Phase Two work. That building is in week seven." He looked at the building. "Three months and twenty-seven days," he said. "Three months and twenty-four now," she said. "We're three days in." He looked at her. At the coat in the early morning cold, the mark just visible at the neckline. She was not doing anything operational. She was standing in the junction square of the eastern residential zone at 0630 on a Monday looking at a building. "You came here before the wall," he said. "I wanted to see what the morning looks like from here," she said. "Before anyone's working. Before it's an active construction site." She paused. "There's a woman in that building who came to the medical station the day I walked the Underworld. She said she didn't think she'd see this in her lifetime." She looked at the building. "Three months and twenty-four days. She gets to see it." He stood beside her. "It's cold," he said. "Yes," she said. "Not as cold as the garden bench," he said. She glanced at him. The corner of her mouth. "Nothing is as cold as the garden bench at that hour," she said. "We stayed for forty minutes," he said. "I know," she said. And then, because she was working on managing less: "I would have stayed longer if you hadn't eventually pointed out that the stone was conducting cold through both our coats." "It was practical information," he said. "It was," she said. "I was slightly annoyed about it." He looked at her. "You wanted to stay on the frozen bench longer." "I wanted to stay in the moment longer," she said. "The bench was incidental." She paused. "But yes. The bench also." He was quiet for a moment. The junction square. The construction equipment staged in the early light. The building with the cold room. "Bronya," he said. "Yes," he said. "The Landau house has a fireplace," he said. "In the main room. Lynx keeps the wood stocked for winter." He paused. "If you want to sit in the garden again, we can. But if you want warmth, the fireplace is available." She looked at him. At the specific quality of the offer — not performed, just practical, just real. "When?" she said. "This week," he said. "When the work allows." "Wednesday," she said. "After the construction review." "Wednesday," he agreed. They stood in the junction square for a while longer. The morning came up around them — the construction crew beginning to arrive at the staging area, the residential section sounds starting, the junction market's early setup. Mira was not there yet. It was too early for Mira. But in three months and twenty-four days the heating connection would reach her building and the cold room would not be cold anymore. "Come on," Bronya said. "You have the patrol review at 0700." "I know," he said. They went back toward the outer wall. At the junction entrance she stopped and looked back at the building once. Then she walked. —— Wednesday arrived with a secondary survey report from Svarog that Gepard put on Bronya's desk at 0900 alongside a note that said: *geology interesting, timeline unchanged, Snezhnaya access road running ahead of schedule, see page four for the deviation.* Bronya read page four at 0915 while Pela was explaining something about the eastern Underworld medical redistribution, and she wrote in the margin: *confirm with Svarog Thursday, flag if the deviation affects the Phase One buffer.* She slid it back across the desk and went back to listening to Pela. Pela noticed the margin note. She picked up the report. She put it in the to-confirm stack with the efficiency of someone who had been routing operational notes for two years. At 1400 the construction review ran forty-five minutes. Dunn presented the Snezhnaya preliminary framework — the six-page schematic he had brought to the north wall on Saturday, now expanded to twenty-two pages with threat assessment models and personnel deployment scenarios and a three-phase implementation timeline. He presented it with the direct clarity of someone who had been building it for three weeks and knew exactly what he had. Gepard read it while Dunn presented. He did not interrupt. When Dunn finished, Gepard said: "The personnel ratios on the surface patrol in phase two." He pointed at page fourteen. "The surface terrain documentation Luka is building will inform these numbers. Hold this section as a working model until the first surface mission generates data." "I left it as provisional," Dunn said. "The flag is on page fourteen." Gepard looked at page fourteen. There was a flag. "Good," he said. Bronya looked at the framework. "The civilian transit integration," she said. "Page nineteen. The Guard interface with the civilian transit authority — who coordinates that?" "I put it as TBD," Dunn said. "I don't know yet who the civilian transit authority is, since we don't have one." "We're building one," Bronya said. "Starting with the transit route certification. I'll need the Guard interface structure from you before the transit authority is formalized so I can build the handoff protocols into the authority's founding documents." She paused. "Two weeks." "Two weeks," Dunn said. He wrote in the notebook. After the review, at 1530, Gepard and Bronya went to the Landau house. Lynx was there. She had known they were coming — Gepard had told her Tuesday — and she had made the specific Lynx preparation which was: the fireplace lit, the main room warm, food available but not formally staged because it was not a dinner, it was an evening. She looked at them when they came in, said: "Main room," and went to her own room. The main room had the fireplace and two chairs and the Landau house's accumulated quality of being a place where people were comfortable. Gepard had grown up in this room. Bronya had been here enough times in the past year that it had stopped feeling like a formal visit and started feeling like a place she also knew. She sat in one of the chairs. He sat in the other. The fireplace. The warmth. The main room quiet except for the fire. "Tell me about the northern ridge," Bronya said. He looked at the fire. "You said," she said, "when I found the classified debrief — that you'd tell me the real version. The one that wasn't in the report." She paused. "I've been patient." "You have been," he said. "I'm done being patient," she said. Not harshly. Just directly. He looked at the fire for a long moment. "Month one," he said. "The first week I spent establishing the patrol perimeter. Standard protocol. The terrain at the ridge at extension range is nothing like the standard Restricted Zone maps — the maps stop at the approved perimeter and beyond that it's geological estimation." He paused. "The estimations were off. The terrain was harder. The Fragmentum behavior at that range is different — less organized, more ambient, present everywhere rather than concentrated at the formation points." He paused. "I adapted." She was listening with the full attention she gave to things she had been waiting for. "Month four," he said. "The first Doomsday Beast encounter." He stopped. He looked at the fire. "The debrief called it maximum mortality risk. That's the accurate term. I encountered it at the northern ridge's secondary formation cluster and it was — not what I had training for." He paused. "I was alone. Whatever I had was whatever I had. Nothing more was coming." "What did you do?" she said. "I found the articulation points," he said. "Same principle as anything. The armor, the joints, the weak geometry. I used the ice architecture the way I use it against the Stellaron formations — constraining the movement space, reducing the options." He paused. "It took seven hours." She was quiet. "After," he said, "I was in Natasha's ward. Four days. She didn't know what I'd been doing, just that I came back from the ridge at reduced capacity and needed the ward." He looked at the fire. "The three officials who sent me received my report. They classified it. They retired within the year." He paused. "I spent month five on the ridge trying to understand what I had done and whether I could do it again if I had to." "Could you?" she said. "I found out in month eleven," he said. "The Trampler formation. Multiple. The press of death — that's the honest description, not the classified one. At the absolute edge of what survives." He paused. "That's where the Ice Flames came from. Not from training. From the edge." He looked at his hands. The gold ice ambient — present, normal, completely his. "Whatever exists past the ceiling doesn't come from working toward it. It comes from being pushed past where the ceiling was." She looked at his hands. At the gold ice. "You were alone for a year," she said. "Yes," he said. "Nobody knew," she said. "Three officials and Natasha's ward records," he said. "The ward records don't specify the source of the capacity reduction. She never asked." She was quiet for a moment. "I read the debrief," she said. "It took me forty minutes and the word unprecedented appeared once." She paused. "I didn't sleep well that night." "I know," he said. "You came to the ward." "You had NORTHERN RIDGE: NOTES open on the tablet," she said. "And you didn't close it when I looked." "No," he said. "You were ready for me to find it," she said. "I'd been ready for a while," he said. "You were going to find the debrief eventually. I wanted to have the real version ready when you did." She looked at the fire. At the warmth of the Landau house main room. At the fireplace Lynx kept stocked. "Thank you," she said. "For telling me." "It's not difficult to tell you," he said. "You're the right person to tell." She looked at him. At the specific quality of that sentence — not performed, not offered as a compliment. Just the accurate version of the thing. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "I want to go to the ridge," she said. "When Snezhnaya is operational and the surface transit is running. I want to go to the northern ridge and see what it looks like." He was quiet for a moment. "It's not remarkable terrain," he said. "It's cold and geological and the Fragmentum at that range looks exactly like the documentation." "I know," she said. "I want to see it anyway." He looked at the fire. "All right," he said. "When the transit is running. We'll go." She looked at the fire too. At the warmth between them. At the main room of the Landau house, which was not the frozen garden bench and was not the outer wall at night but was — she decided — its own specific version of the right place. "Lynx left food," she said. "She always does," he said. "Should we eat it?" she said. "Yes," he said. They went to the kitchen. —— Thursday afternoon, Luka went to the outer machine district's secondary shaft entrance. Not to descend. To stand at the entrance and look at the shaft. The shaft had been sealed after the second Stellaron battle — not permanently, but with the standard geological seal that the construction team applied to access points that were not in active use. The seal was in place. The cave below was quiet. The Great Glacial Aegir, as Gepard had named it, was not accessible from this point anymore without the authorized removal of the geological seal. He stood at the entrance for a while. He thought about what was down there. The golden form, resting in the ice. Vera's endurance in the stone. The Architects' ruins at the far end. The notation carvings along every wall, including the ones Clara was still learning to read. He thought about what was not down there anymore. The second heartbeat. The urgency of it. The weight of what had been held. He put his chrome hand on the shaft entrance wall. The notation channels conducted warmth through immediately — the primary network at full operational output, the combined frequency from the golden ice and the claimed frequency running at fifteen percent above the previous baseline according to Serval's latest measurement. The warmth was — different from when he had first started touching the junction walls nine months ago. Fuller. Like a thing that had grown into its full register. He held his hand on the wall. He thought about Svarog saying: tell him. About Gepard saying: it's going to be you. About Seele saying: you were excited, in the corridor entrance at nine-beta, four minutes early. He thought about the rifle calibration and the surface terrain documentation and the mission proposal he had written out on Tuesday and given to Gepard on Wednesday morning, which Gepard had read in six minutes and signed. The surface threat assessment mission was authorized. The transit route was five weeks away. The mission would happen in week two of the route's operation — not the first day, which would be the group trip, but the second week, when the transit was established enough for the equipment mission. He and Seele would go up in the second week. He thought: five weeks. He took his hand off the wall. He thought about the million cycles. Not the content — the shape of it, the enormous warm shape of a cost that had been paid. He thought about the way the shape lived in him: not as weight, not as grief, as something that was simply part of what he was. He had been that person — the one who ran at impossible things for her — before the cycles, and the cycles had made the running into something that had a name in the structure of the planet. He was still that person. The name in the structure didn't change it. He walked back toward the workshop. Svarog was at the main bench when Luka arrived. He was running the geological monitoring checks — the daily review of every channel junction in the outer machine district's network. His four arms moved through the checks with the particular efficiency of someone who had done this thousands of times and had not, in all those thousands of times, found a reason to do it more quickly. "The shaft entrance," Luka said. "I felt your contact," Svarog said. "Through the channel monitoring." "The warmth is different," Luka said. "From when I first started using the junctions." "Yes," Svarog said. "The combined frequency has increased the channel conductance efficiency. Your arm reads the channels at a higher resolution now than it did in month one." He paused. "It should continue improving as the frequency stabilizes." "Serval said the stabilization takes three to six months," Luka said. "Her timeline is consistent with the geological modeling," Svarog said. He went back to the checks. "The surface mission. Gepard authorized it." "Wednesday," Luka said. "The surface mineral data will be — different from what the dome's interior geological structure produces," Svarog said. "The notation channels extend to the Snezhnaya site but they thin significantly at the dome's boundary. Your arm will feel the difference." "How different?" Luka said. "Come back after the first surface contact and tell me," Svarog said. "I have models. The models will be wrong in specific ways. I would like to know how." Luka looked at him. At Svarog, who had been monitoring the geological structure of this planet for four hundred years and who was asking to be told how his models were wrong. "All right," he said. "Also," Svarog said, not looking up from the monitoring checks, "the weapon." "Yes?" Luka said. "The cannon form's output at surface-level Fragmentum density," Svarog said. "I built the calibration for dome-interior conditions. The surface has lower ambient Fragmentum density but the Fragmentum that is present behaves differently — more diffuse, higher variability. The danger-scaling mechanism may respond differently." He paused. "Test it carefully. Don't fire a large burst until you've tested the surface calibration at low output first." "I wasn't planning to," Luka said. "I know," Svarog said. "I'm saying it anyway." Luka looked at him. "You're concerned about the mission." "I built the weapon," Svarog said. "I am always concerned when the weapon is being used in conditions I didn't calibrate for." He paused. "Bring Seele." "She's already coming," Luka said. "Good," Svarog said. He went back to the monitoring. Luka stood in the workshop for a moment. The bench, the equipment, the notation carvings on the walls. The workshop that had been his second home for nine months. He picked up the equipment bag he had come for — the surface assessment instruments, the specialized geological sensors that Svarog had built for the mission. He put it on his shoulder. "Svarog," he said. "Yes," Svarog said. "Thank you," Luka said. "For the weapon. For the arm. For—" He stopped. He found the actual thing he was trying to say. "For the workshop." Svarog looked at him. The full optical array. The four arms momentarily still. "You built a good part of it yourself," Svarog said. "Not at first," Luka said. "No," Svarog said. "Not at first." He paused. "The workshop is yours as much as mine. It has been for some time." Luka held that for a moment. He nodded. He left. —— Friday morning, Seele was in the residential stack looking at a map. Not the geological maps, not the Fragmentum assessment charts. A map of the surface terrain in the area around the dome — the physical survey map, the one that the Snezhnaya construction team had been using, which showed the terrain the way physical terrain surveys showed it: not geological function, just ground. The shape of it. The distance from the dome edge to the Snezhnaya site. The northern ridge, marked at the map's edge as beyond survey range. Luka was at the kitchen counter making coffee. He always made enough for two and a half people, which was exactly right for both of them combined. "Five weeks," she said. "Five weeks," he said, without looking up. "What's the terrain like between the dome and the site?" she said. "Mixed ice strata, exposed pre-Separation bedrock, consistent with what the notation channels map at depth," he said. "Rough. No infrastructure yet. The construction road is the first infrastructure." She looked at the map. "And the sky," she said. He looked up. She was looking at the map with something underneath the operational question. "Yes," he said. "We've both been under the dome our entire lives," she said. "Every place we've ever been is under the dome." She paused. "The sky is going to be different." "Colder than the dome," he said. "The dome regulates temperature. Outside it the ambient is the planetary baseline. Minus twenty to minus thirty at this latitude in the current season." She looked at the map. "Are you worried about it? Any of it?" He thought about the honest version. "No," he said. "I'm—" He paused. "I've been thinking about what it looks like without the dome in the way. Since the night of the moon promise." He brought the coffee to the table. He set her cup in front of her. "Your mother's book describes it." "She marked the passage," Seele said. "The specific passage about the stars being visible during the day at this latitude in winter. Because the atmosphere is thin and the sky is dark." She paused. "I've read it probably fifty times." He sat down. Outside the window the outer machine district was doing its Friday morning — the generators cycling up, the junction lights still on, the construction crew beginning their staging for the day shift. "Five weeks," she said again. Not with impatience. With the specific quality of something being held carefully because it mattered. "Five weeks," he agreed. He drank his coffee. She looked at the map. The outer machine district did its morning, and above the dome the cold light sky was there, waiting. —— At 1000 on Friday, Gepard was in the palace operations corridor when he ran into Serval. This was not unusual — Serval had been spending more time at the palace since her return, running the frequency analysis sessions with Bronya and Svarog and using the palace's secondary monitoring equipment for the combined-frequency stability work. What was unusual was the expression she was wearing, which was the expression she wore when she had been thinking about something for a while and had decided to say it. "Walk with me," she said. He walked with her. They went through the palace's eastern corridor, through the secondary operations room, and out to the small courtyard that connected the palace's administrative wing to the monitoring stations. The courtyard was cold. Serval didn't seem to notice. She turned to face him. "Tell me about month eleven," she said. He looked at her. "You told Bronya," she said. "She didn't tell me. I can see it in how she looks at your hands." She paused. "I'm your older sister. I want to hear it from you." He was quiet for a moment. He looked at the courtyard. At the cold air. At Serval, twenty-eight years old, who had been cataloguing things about him since they were children and who was looking at him with the full force of thirty years of paying attention. "Month eleven," he said. "The Trampler formation. Multiple." He paused. "I was at the absolute edge of what survives. The press of death — the specific condition where what you have is not enough and something past the ceiling arrives because it has to." "The Ice Flames," she said. "The Ice Flames," he said. "You've been carrying that alone since the northern ridge," she said. "Bronya knows now," he said. "You've been carrying it for most of the time I've known you as an adult," she said. "I have been running frequency analysis on your ice expression for a year and you never said: Serval, there's something in the data you're going to find interesting." "I didn't know if you'd find it," he said. "You knew I'd find it eventually," she said. "You kept me looking." He was quiet. "Was it mine to know?" she said. Not with accusation. A real question. "No," he said. "It was mine to know and mine to carry." He paused. "And then Qlipoth looked at Jarilo-IV and it stopped being something I was carrying alone, and at that point it became something Bronya should know and then something you should know." He paused. "That's the order it arrived in." She looked at him for a long time. "The Trampler formation," she said. "Alone. Month eleven. You came back." "Yes," he said. "You spent month twelve understanding what you'd done," she said. "Trying to," he said. "Some of it I still don't fully understand." She looked at his hands. At the gold ice ambient. At the specific luminous deep cold of the Emanator's expression at rest. "When you were at the edge," she said. "What did you think about?" He looked at the courtyard. "The city," he said. "The wall. The Guard." He paused. "And Bronya. Not — at the time I didn't have the vocabulary for what I thought about her. But looking back at it, what I was thinking about was her." Serval was very still for a moment. "Nine years," she said. "Yes," he said. She exhaled. She looked at the courtyard wall. She looked back at him. "You owe me," she said. "Years of watching you not say things." "I know," he said. "Don't say I know," she said. The corner of his mouth. "I'm aware of what I owe you," he said. She made the sound — the sharp almost-laugh that meant something had landed correctly but she was not going to fully reward it. She pointed at him. "You've been practicing," she said. "You told me to," he said. "I told Bronya to manage less," she said. "I told you to stop saying I know. Both of those in the same evening." She paused. "Are you two competing?" He looked at her. "No." "Because it feels like you're both working on the same assignment," she said. "Serval," he said. "Yes?" she said. "Thank you," he said. "For the three weeks and the express train and the latent capacity analysis." He paused. "And for everything before that." She looked at him. At her younger brother, who was not good at saying things and who had just said something specific and direct and true. "Yes," she said. Simply. "You're welcome." They stood in the courtyard for a moment. "Dunn," he said. She looked at him. "What about him?" "Wednesday," he said. "The Landau house kitchen." "You know about Wednesday," she said. "Lynx told me Thursday," he said. "Of course she did," Serval said. "She was pleased," he said. "I know," she said. "She had the pleased expression when I came out for breakfast." She paused. "It's— it's not something I planned. I just walked there." "I know," he said. "Stop saying—" "I'm aware that you didn't plan it," he said immediately. She laughed. The real one — short and genuine and surprised by itself. "You're infuriating," she said. "So I've been told," he said. "By whom?" she said. "Bronya," he said. "Also Dunn. Also you, periodically." "You have a consistent feedback pattern," she said. "Yes," he said. He looked at the courtyard. At the morning. "Dunn is a good person," he said. "I know that," she said. "He's been watching you since he was sixteen," he said. "Not—" He paused. "Not in a concerning way. In the way that people watch someone who matters to them before they have the language for why they matter." She was quiet. "He kept the framework," Gepard said. "The Snezhnaya security framework. He built it three weeks before I told him about the role. He had six pages ready before the conversation started." He paused. "He had been waiting to be asked. He didn't assume." He looked at her. "He's been not-assuming things about you for eight years." She looked at the courtyard wall. At the sky above the courtyard. At the Landau house in the direction she knew it was. "He made dinner," she said. "Yes," Gepard said. "He had the pastries from the new Underworld market," she said. "And he was making an assembly of things that work together and it took forty minutes and we talked through all of it." She paused. "It was—" She stopped. "Good?" he said. "Exactly right," she said. Precisely. "It was exactly right." Gepard looked at her. At Serval, who catalogued frequencies and found the latent channel capacity and took the express train when the data couldn't wait and who was standing in the palace courtyard on a Friday morning telling him the dinner was exactly right. "Good," he said. The same word. The complete version. —— Later that afternoon, in Natasha's ward, a small thing happened that nobody marked in any log. Sampo came by at 1500 — not the evening visit, the afternoon one, which was less regular but had been occurring with increasing frequency since the northwest corridor through-route opened. He came in, sat in the chair by the east window, and did not say anything specific for approximately four minutes, which was their established allowance for transition between whatever he had been doing and what he was going to say. At minute four, he said: "I'm going to tell Sparkle about Vera." Natasha looked up from the report. "When she comes back," he said. "The conversation about the path — I'm going to tell her about Vera before anything else. So she knows what the path has already been." He paused. "The dark winter, the running toward, the twenty-two years of carrying a name-shaped absence." He paused. "She should know the ground before she talks to me about where the path goes." Natasha put the report down. "What made you decide?" "The market on Saturday," he said. "The building three junctions east." He paused. "Standing in front of a building where someone lived and knowing their name and knowing the building is still there — that's a kind of continuity I didn't know I was carrying." He looked at his hands. "I don't want to talk about the path forward without Sparkle knowing what I'm walking from." She looked at him. At the expression that was not the performance layer. "Yes," she said. "Tell her." "The ward evenings," he said. "They're part of it too. What I've built here." He looked at her. "That's also something she should know about before she talks to me." Natasha was quiet for a moment. "Sampo," she said. "Yes," he said. "When she comes back," she said. "Whenever the conversation happens — I want to know how it goes. After." "Yes," he said. "I'll tell you after." "Specifically," she said. "Not the summary. The actual conversation." He looked at her. At Natasha, who had always had a precise vocabulary for what she needed and was using it clearly. "Specifically," he agreed. She picked up the report. He sat by the east window. Outside, the outer machine district did its Friday afternoon. The ward was warm. After a while he said: "The fourth pastry kind. From the market." "What about it?" she said, without looking up. "The woman gave me the recommendation hierarchy," he said. "The fourth kind is listed as investigate further. I've been thinking about when to go back." "Saturday," she said. "With you?" he said. "With me," she said. He settled in the chair. The report rustled. The ward did its quiet afternoon. Outside, the construction crew on the eastern section wrapped up the day's work. —— That evening, Clara was in the workshop alone. Not alone in the Svarog sense — Svarog was in the back room — but the Moles had gone home and Pela had finished the channel monitoring session and the workshop had the late-evening quality that Clara had learned to inhabit over nine months: the warmth of the notation channels, the geological hum of the outer machine district, the secondary bench with the twenty-third section's carving above it. She had been working on the third untranslated character for two hours. It was different from the first two. The first had been the keeper of the record — Svarog, which had arrived when Pino identified the base meaning and Clara had felt the full warmth of it through the channel contact. The second was still opaque to her; she had been honest with Svarog about that and he had said: leave it for now, which she was doing. The third character was simpler in structure than the second but more resistant in a different way — not because the notation was complex but because the warmth the channel conducted when she pressed her palm to it was a warmth she did not have a category for yet. It was not her warmth. Not Svarog's. Not the Guardian-warmth or the Emanator-warmth or the memory-carriers' warmth. It was — reaching warmth. The warmth of something that went outward. She held her hand on the carving for a long time. She thought about the section's descriptions. The Guardian received and claimed. The Emanator preserved. The memory carriers returned. The lineage keeper held. Svarog's function was the record. The reaching warmth. The something that went outward. She thought about Serval, who ran frequency analysis and took the express train when the data was significant. She thought about Pela, who wrote twelve seconds of notes and immediately turned the tablet face-down again. She thought about Luka and Seele, who were going to stand on the surface in five weeks and document a terrain that nobody had documented before. She thought about Dunn, who had built a twenty-two-page threat framework in anticipation of a conversation that hadn't happened yet. She took her hand off the carving. "Svarog," she said. He came to the doorway. "The third character," she said. "I don't have a translation. But I have a description." She paused. "It's the warmth that goes outward. The function that reaches beyond the network's current range." She paused. "I think it's what happens after Snezhnaya. The next reach." Svarog looked at the carving. "Yes," he said. "That is consistent with what I suspected." "Who is it?" she said. "I don't know yet," he said. "The function hasn't been filled yet." He paused. "Which means the person hasn't arrived yet, or hasn't arrived at the part of themselves that fills the function." Clara looked at the third character. At the reaching warmth that the channel conducted when she touched it — present, warm, not yet occupied. "It's going to be someone we know," she said. "Most likely," he said. "And we'll know when it happens," she said. "The same way we know the others happened — from the data, after the fact." "Yes," he said. "The order is always: the function is filled, then the record acknowledges it." Clara looked at the carving. At the three characters above the secondary bench, the first translated, the second waiting, the third occupied by the warmth of something not yet here. She took her notebook from the bench and wrote: *Third character: the function that reaches outward. Not yet filled. When it is, the channel will tell me. Wait for the warmth to change.* She closed the notebook. "Svarog," she said. "Yes," he said. "Do you know what the second character says?" she said. He was quiet for a moment. "I have a hypothesis," he said. "I don't want to tell you my hypothesis before you arrive at the translation yourself." "Why?" she said. "Because your translation will be more accurate than my hypothesis," he said. "You read the warmth directly. I read the monitoring data and make inferences." He paused. "The monitoring data is incomplete in ways your direct channel contact is not." She looked at him. "You trust my translation more than your four hundred years of data." "I trust the combination," he said. "Your translation and my context together. But the translation should come first." He paused. "You're close. The second character is in the same register as the third. It's a function that also extends outward, but more specifically." She looked at the second character. "Something that goes further than the third," she said. "Or more precisely," he said. "Different reach, not greater." She looked at it. She thought about what she knew — the Guardian, the Emanator, the memory carriers, the lineage keeper, the record keeper, the reaching warmth of the third, the more-precisely-reaching of the second. She thought: what is more precise than reaching outward? She thought: naming. Naming what you find when you reach it. She put her hand back on the second character and let the warmth tell her what it knew. The warmth said: beyond. And below that: the one who goes. And below that, the sub-notation she was only now finding: first. She took her hand off the carving. "The one who goes first," she said. Slowly. "To the beyond." She paused. "That's Luka." Svarog was very still. "Luka and Seele are the first people from Belobog to go to the surface," she said. "And Luka is specifically — he's the one who proposed the documentation mission. He's the one building the Snezhnaya security framework. He's the first one going into new territory." She paused. "Is that right?" "It is consistent with the data," Svarog said. "You knew," she said. "I suspected," he said. "I didn't want to influence your translation." She looked at the second character. At the first and the third beside it. She thought about Luka in corridor nine-beta with three pages of notes and the patience to wait for the right moment to say the right thing. "He doesn't know," she said. "No," Svarog said. "Are we going to tell him?" she said. "When the time is right," Svarog said. "After the first surface mission. After the documentation exists. After the function is filled enough that the telling is a confirmation rather than a weight." She looked at the carving. "The order is always: the function is filled, then the record acknowledges it," she said. "Yes," he said. "We wait," she said. "We wait," he confirmed. She closed her notebook. She put it in the workshop bag. She looked at the secondary bench and the three characters above it — two translated, one waiting, all three real. "Five weeks," she said. Not to Svarog specifically. Just out loud. "Yes," Svarog said. "Five weeks." Clara picked up her bag. She said goodnight to Svarog, who was already back at the monitoring. She went out into the outer machine district's Friday evening, which was the city being itself, warm and specific and entirely real. —— END OF CHAPTER 65 Not the geological maps, not the Fragmentum assessment charts. A map of the surface terrain in the area around the dome — the physical survey map, the one that the Snezhnaya construction team had been using, which showed the terrain in the way that physical terrain surveys showed it: not geological function, just ground. The shape of it. The distance from the dome edge to the Snezhnaya site. The northern ridge, marked at the map's edge as beyond survey range. Luka was at the kitchen counter. He had been making coffee in the way he made coffee, which was methodical and slightly excessive in volume — he always made enough for two and a half people, which worked out correctly for the two of them and the amount they each actually drank. "Five weeks," she said. "Five weeks," he said, without looking up. "What's the surface terrain like between the dome and the Snezhnaya site?" she said. "Geological survey says: mixed ice strata, exposed pre-Separation bedrock, consistent with what the notation channels map at depth," he said. "The surface is — rough. No infrastructure yet. The construction road is the first infrastructure." She looked at the map. "And the sky," she said. He looked up. She was looking at the map with the expression she used when she was thinking about something that was not the immediate operational question but was underneath it. "Yes," he said. "We've both been under the dome our entire lives," she said. "Every place we've ever been is under the dome." She paused. "The sky is going to be different." "It's going to be cold," he said. "Colder than the dome," she said. "Significantly," he said. "The dome regulates temperature. Outside it the ambient temperature is the planetary baseline. Minus twenty to minus thirty at this latitude in the current season." She looked at the map. "Are you worried about it?" she said. "The cold?" he said. "Any of it," she said. "Going up." He thought about the honest version. "No," he said. "I'm—" He paused. "I'm looking forward to it in a way that's slightly larger than I expected." He brought the coffee to the table. He set a cup in front of her. "The documentation is important and real. And also I've been thinking about the sky since the moon promise." She looked up at him. "Since the night you promised," she said. "Since the night," he said. "I've been thinking about what it looks like without a dome in the way." He sat down. "Your mother's book describes it." "She marked the passage," Seele said. "The specific passage about the stars being visible during the day at this latitude in winter. Because the atmosphere is thin and the sky is dark." She paused. "I've read it probably fifty times." "I know," he said. She looked at the map. He looked at the coffee. Outside the window the outer machine district was doing its Friday morning — the generators cycling up, the junction lights still on, the construction crew beginning their staging for the day shift. "Five weeks," she said again. "Five weeks," he said. "And then we go up." She folded the map. She put it next to the equipment bag that was already staged by the door. She picked up the coffee. "The surface assessment instruments," she said. "Svarog's sensors." "In the bag," he said. "Calibrated for surface conditions. He said the first large-output test on the weapon should wait until I've tested the calibration at low output." "He's right," she said. "He said you were coming," Luka said. "He told you to bring me," she said. "He said it like he was giving instructions," Luka said. "He trusts me with the surface conditions more than he trusts you," she said. "He trusts us both," he said. "He's concerned about the weapon calibration and he knows you'll enforce the protocol." He paused. "He's not wrong." She looked at him. "I'd tell you to test it at low output first," she said. "I know," he said. "That's why he's not wrong." She looked at the bag by the door. At the equipment staged for a mission five weeks away. At the map folded on the table. At the outer machine district outside the window, which was everything she had ever known and which was about to have a beyond. "Luka," she said. "Yes," he said. She looked at him. "When we're up there," she said. "The documentation and the assessment and all of it — that's the morning. And then there's whatever comes after the morning." "Yes," he said. "I want to stand still for a while," she said. "When the work is done. Just stand still and look." He looked at her. At the expression that was the full version — no layers, nothing catalogued, just Seele thinking about the cold light sky she had been reading about since she was four years old. "We'll stand still," he said. "As long as you want." She looked at the map on the table. She picked up her coffee. "Five weeks," she said, for the third time. Not with impatience. With the specific quality of something being held carefully because it mattered. "Five weeks," he agreed. He drank his coffee. Outside, the outer machine district did its Friday morning, and above the dome the cold light sky was there, waiting for them to arrive. —— END OF CHAPTER 65 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 64: Saturday Chapter Text Saturday morning, Natasha's ward was busy until 1045. She had known it would run long. Saturday assessments always ran long, not because the patients arrived late but because Saturday patients were the ones who had been managing something during the week and had reached the weekend with the specific exhaustion of people who had been managing things and were now, finally, somewhere they did not have to manage anymore. They talked more on Saturdays. Natasha let them. At 1048 she walked the last patient out, came back to her desk, looked at the clock, and made a decision about the report she had been planning to write in the fifteen minutes before 1115. The report could wait. She changed out of the clinic coat, put on her jacket, and went to the northwest corridor. Sampo was already there. He was standing at the through-route entrance — the one at the third junction that had been a dead end for twenty years — with a paper bag from the new market, looking at the corridor on the other side of it with an expression that Natasha had learned to read as him processing something he was not going to announce. "You're early," she said. He turned. "You're early," he said. "I'm approximately on time." "You said 1115." "It's 1112," he said. She looked at him. "That's early." "That's the margin of error for street navigation," he said. He held up the bag. "Dark filling. Three kinds." He paused. "There was a fourth kind that looked interesting but the woman at the counter said it was not appropriate for a first visit to the market and I should try the three established ones first and come back." Natasha looked at him. "She gave you a recommendation hierarchy." "She was very specific about it," Sampo said. "I wrote it down." He pulled out a small piece of paper with four items listed in the woman's handwriting. "She also gave me her name. For the return visit." Natasha took the paper. She looked at it. At Sampo's description of the fourth pastry in the margin: *"suspicious but compelling — investigate further."* She looked at him. "You annotated her recommendation," she said. "That's field documentation," he said. "It's a pastry list," she said. "It's a pastry list that will save us time on the second visit," he said. "Natasha. Do you want the dark filling ones or do you want to stand here discussing my documentation methodology?" She took a pastry from the bag. It was very good. She acknowledged this with a specific expression that Sampo, who had known her for three years, read as high-quality confirmation. "Okay," he said. He took one too. "Come look at the through-route." They stood at the third junction, eating the pastries, looking at the corridor that had opened two weeks ago. The residential section on the other side was visible — the different quality of light, the ceiling grating at a different angle, the sounds of Saturday morning in the residential zone rather than the maintenance-section ambient they were used to. "I ran through it Monday," Sampo said. "I walked it Thursday," Natasha said. He looked at her. "You didn't tell me." "You didn't ask," she said. "You said you'd run it Monday and I didn't know if you wanted company." He looked at the corridor. "I would have wanted company," he said. "I know," she said. "That's why I went Thursday after you went Monday. I wanted to see it on my own first before I saw it with you." He looked at her. At Natasha, who had walked a newly opened corridor specifically on the day after he walked it, so that she could see it for herself before they saw it together. He thought about what that meant and the specific weight of it. "The market is at the end of it," he said. "Yes," she said. "Did you find the pastries?" "I found them," she said. "Thursday. I didn't buy any." She paused. "I figured we'd do that together." He looked at her for a moment. Then he looked at the corridor. "Come on," he said. "Let's walk through." They walked through the through-route — the twenty meters of corridor that had been a dead end for twenty years, now connecting the maintenance section to the residential zone. On the other side, the light was brighter. The market was there — not a large market, four stalls and a permanent stand, the junction market that had opened three weeks ago as part of the equalization framework's commercial access provisions. People were moving through it. Saturday morning rhythm, people with bags, a group of children at the corner watching the construction crew do something to the eastern generator access. Sampo stood at the market entrance and looked at it. "When I was eleven," he said, "this section was on the other side of the maintenance wall. I knew it existed. I'd never been to it." Natasha was quiet. "The wall came down in the Separation cleanup," he said. "But the access stayed restricted because the junction reclassification hadn't gone through." He looked at the market. "Thirty years. I've been running past the end of that corridor for thirty years and there was a market on the other side." She put her hand on his arm. Not saying anything. Just there. He looked at her hand. He covered it with his. "Vera grew up in this section," he said. "Her family's building is three junctions east." Natasha didn't say anything. "I found it two weeks ago," he said. "When the access opened. I walked past it on Monday." He paused. "There's a workshop on the ground floor now. Someone's been running repair work out of it." He paused. "The building is still standing." "Yes," she said. "I didn't go in," he said. "Just walked past." "Okay," she said. He looked at the market. At the stalls, the people, the children at the corner. At the junction that had been on the other side of the wall for thirty years. "I want to come back," he said. "To the market. Every Saturday." "Yes," she said. "With you," he said. She looked at him. At the expression that was not the performance layer — the one underneath it, the one that she had been building the vocabulary for across three years of ward evenings and corridor conversations and pastries on the small table by the east window. "Yes," she said. "Every Saturday." He exhaled. Nodded once. He picked up the paper bag with the remaining pastries. "The second kind," he said. "The woman said it's better warm. There's a heating station at the third stall." Natasha looked at the third stall. "Lead the way," she said. —— At the corridor nine-beta entrance at 0900 on Saturday, Luka arrived four minutes before Seele. This was unusual. Seele was not late — she was not the kind of person who was late in the morning, the Underworld having trained punctuality into her the way it trained most things, by making lateness costly. Four minutes before her arrival meant Luka had moved faster than usual, which Seele identified when she got there. "You're early," she said. "Four minutes," he said. "You're not usually four minutes early." "I wanted to check the corridor approach angle before we started," he said. "The floor geometry in nine-beta is different from the circuit. The floor-contact leverage might need adjustment." She looked at him. "You scouted the corridor." "I ran the first three junctions," he said. "The floor density is consistent with the sub-level approach geometry. The ceiling is lower — four meters rather than six. The circuit approach won't transfer directly." She unslung the scythe — the new one, the dark-metal geometric blade — and ran a test arc in the corridor entrance. The blade cleared the ceiling with less margin than the circuit. She adjusted her grip position. "Two degrees on the horizontal arc," she said. "Three," he said. She ran the arc again. "Three," she agreed. They looked at each other. "Ready?" he said. "I've been ready," she said. "You're the one who showed up early to scout." "I showed up early to be efficient," he said. "You showed up early because you were excited," she said. He opened his mouth. Closed it. "You were," she said. "I wanted to check the floor geometry," he said. "Luka," she said. "You showed up four minutes early to a corridor practice session. You scouted the first three junctions." She paused. "You were excited." He looked at her. At the scythe in her hand and the three-degree arc adjustment and the specific expression she had when she had identified something correctly and was waiting for him to confirm it. "Yes," he said. "Fine." She grinned — quick and real, the one that came out when she was genuinely pleased about something. "Let's go," she said. They went into the corridor. —— The floor-contact leverage in nine-beta was different from the circuit. The floor of nine-beta was older stone, rougher texture, with the mineral composition that had been conducting the notation network's warmth for decades longer than the circuit floor. The friction coefficient was higher. Seele's floor-contact approach needed less initial force and more directional precision — the rough texture gave more purchase on contact but less slide, which changed the mechanical transfer of the leverage entirely. She worked it out by the third attempt. Luka watched the first two attempts, saw what she was working with, and when the third approach started wrong — the initial force too high for the rougher surface — he said: "Less push, more direction," and she adjusted mid-approach and it worked. "That's it," she said. "The texture gives you the purchase," he said. "The approach force is redundant." "You figured that out on attempt two," she said. "Yes," he said. "Why didn't you say it on attempt two?" she said. "You were mid-approach," he said. "Interrupting would have cost the attempt." She looked at him. "You waited for the right moment." "I waited for the moment where the information was useful," he said. "On approach two you were already committed. On approach three you had enough from the two attempts to hear the correction before you committed." She stood in the corridor, scythe at rest, looking at him. At the chrome arm and the spinal integration and Luka, who had watched two failed attempts and waited specifically for the moment where saying the thing would actually land. "That's—" She stopped. "What?" he said. "That's very good teaching," she said. "You're a very good teacher." He looked at her. She was not saying it as a performance — she was looking at him with the direct quality she used when she was stating something she had just arrived at and meant completely. "Thank you," he said. "I mean it," she said. "You could teach this." She paused. "When Snezhnaya has a training program. When the Guard expands. You could run the corridor training." He thought about this. "The Guard training is Gepard's domain." "Gepard could authorize it," she said. "And you could run it." She paused. "You taught me the floor-contact adjustment in three attempts without once making me feel like I was failing the first two." He was quiet for a moment. "The first two weren't failures," he said. "They were data." "I know," she said. "But that's how you saw them. Not everyone sees a failed approach as data." She paused. "You could teach people to see it that way." He looked at the corridor. At the floor with the rough mineral texture and the notation channels conducting their warmth through it. He thought about a training program. About passing on the specific thing — not just the technique but the framework, the data-not-failure approach, the patience to wait for the right moment to say the right thing. "I'll think about it," he said. "Good," she said. She raised the scythe. "Again." They ran the approach again. And again after that. By the ninth repetition, Seele's floor-contact leverage in nine-beta's rough stone corridor was producing the same quality of output as the circuit's polished floor, just through a different mechanical pathway. Luka noted the approach angle on a small piece of paper he had brought, because he had known they would need a record. She saw him writing it down. "You brought paper," she said. "For the calibration notes," he said. "You scouted the corridor and brought paper," she said. "And I was only four minutes early," he said. "That's very restrained." She looked at him for a second. Then she laughed — real, short, completely genuine. He looked up from the paper at the sound of it. "Very restrained," she said. "Extremely," he said. She shook her head, still smiling. "Give me the rifle calibration section," she said. "Let's get it done." —— They emerged from nine-beta at 1230. The weapon's rifle calibration had confirmed the two-degree correction was holding — six range repetitions, consistent result, the sight adjusted correctly. The floor-contact leverage was documented. The ceiling arc adjustment was documented. Luka had three pages of notes and Seele had the muscle memory, and both of them agreed that was the correct division of the work. At the corridor's main entrance, they found Svarog. He was not waiting for them specifically — he was doing the junction monitoring checks that he did every Saturday morning, the physical inspection of the notation channel junctions that his instruments could not fully replicate. He heard them coming and turned. He looked at the notes in Luka's hand. "Productive morning," he said. "Nine-beta floor geometry," Luka said. "Different from the circuit. The mineral texture changes the contact leverage mechanics." "Yes," Svarog said. "The geological age of the stone is significantly greater than the circuit facility's construction material. The texture differential is approximately forty percent rougher surface coefficient." Luka looked at him. "You knew." "I knew the geology," Svarog said. "I did not know how it would interact with her approach technique. That required testing." He looked at Seele. "The floor-contact leverage development — how long?" "Three attempts to adapt," she said. He looked at Luka. "You coached the adaptation?" "I waited for the right moment to say the correction," Luka said. Svarog was quiet for a moment. Then: "Seele is right about the training program." They both looked at him. "I heard," he said simply. "The junction acoustics." He paused. "A systematic training methodology using your approach framework — data-oriented, patient correction timing, documented calibration — would benefit the Guard's expansion into Snezhnaya territory significantly." He paused. "Gepard should know you're capable of this." "Gepard has the training programs," Luka said. "Gepard has the current training programs," Svarog said. "You would build new ones. For new terrain." He looked at Luka steadily. "Do not wait for someone to notice. Tell him." Luka looked at the three pages of notes. At the scythe in Seele's hand, the blade settled in the carry configuration. He thought about nine attempts to adapt the floor-contact leverage and what the documentation of those nine attempts actually represented. "I'll tell him," he said. "Good," Svarog said. He went back to the junction inspection. Seele looked at Luka. "You're going to tell him," she said. "I said I would," he said. "Today?" she said. "Today," he said. "He said tell him, not plan to tell him." She looked at him. At the specific quality of Luka having received a direct instruction from Svarog and intending to follow it immediately, which was — she recognized — very much how he operated with things he respected. "I'll come with you," she said. "You don't have to," he said. "I want to," she said. "And I was there for the development. My data is part of the documentation." He looked at her. He looked at the notes. He nodded. "All right." —— Gepard was in the palace briefing room, which was where he was on Saturday afternoons when the operational reports came in and he was doing the week's final review. He looked up when Luka and Seele appeared at the door. "Captain," Luka said. "General," Gepard said, because he was still getting used to the title and it still occasionally came out as Captain from people who had known him before the promotion. He did not correct this. He gestured at the chairs. They came in. They sat. Luka put the three pages of notes on the table. Gepard looked at the notes. "Nine-beta?" "This morning," Seele said. "We ran the corridor to adapt the floor-contact leverage development from the circuit." She paused. "The mineral texture changes the mechanics significantly. The documentation is the adaptation framework." Gepard looked at the notes. He read the first page — the floor coefficient analysis, the approach angle adjustments, the ceiling arc modification, the notation of which attempts produced which outcomes and why. He turned to the second page. He read it with the focused attention he gave patrol reports. He looked up. "You documented the failure conditions," he said. "They weren't failures," Luka said. "They were data." Gepard looked at him. "You documented the approach conditions that produced incorrect output and the conditions that produced correct output and the specific variables that differentiated them." "Yes," Luka said. "And you coached the adaptation without interrupting mid-attempt," Gepard said. "The mid-attempt correction is almost always lost," Luka said. "It lands better between attempts when they can actually hear it." Gepard was quiet for a moment. He looked at the three pages. He looked at Luka. "You want to talk about a training program," he said. "Svarog told me to tell you," Luka said. "He said the approach framework would benefit the Guard's expansion into Snezhnaya territory." "Svarog told you," Gepard said. "He was doing the junction inspection," Seele said. "He heard us." Gepard looked at her. At the specific quality of Svarog hearing a conversation in the corridor and then inserting himself with a precise recommendation and then withdrawing. This was extremely consistent with his experience of Svarog. "He's not wrong," Gepard said. "Snezhnaya will need trained personnel who can operate in surface terrain. The Guard's current training methodology is built for the dome's geological constraints. New terrain means new programs." He paused. "You'd need a framework for surface mineral composition, surface Fragmentum behavior, external weather conditions." He paused. "And the approach to failure conditions as data — that's not in the current curriculum." "I know," Luka said. Seele looked at Luka and then at Gepard and did not say anything because Luka had said he would tell him and he was telling him, and she was present for documentation purposes and this particular conversation belonged to the two of them. Gepard leaned back slightly. "You've thought about this," he said. "Seele mentioned it during the session," Luka said. "I've been thinking about it since. An hour." He paused. "It's not a complete proposal. It's a direction." "The direction is right," Gepard said. Luka was quiet. "I want to tell you something," Gepard said. Luka looked at him. "When Snezhnaya has its first permanent installation," Gepard said, "I'm going to need someone to run the security architecture from the ground. Not just the patrol rotation — the full framework. The threat assessment methodology for surface terrain, the training curriculum, the personnel development." He paused. "I've been thinking about who that should be." He stopped. He looked at Luka directly — which was not a thing Gepard did casually, the direct look, the full attention. "It's going to be you," he said. "I haven't said this until now because the timeline wasn't clear. The timeline is clearer. You should know." The briefing room was very quiet for a moment. Luka said: "That's a significant responsibility." "Yes," Gepard said. "You're giving it to me before the facility exists," Luka said. "I'm telling you now so you can build toward it," Gepard said. "The three pages of notes are the beginning of the methodology. Keep going." Luka looked at the notes. He looked at Gepard. He looked at Seele, who was already looking at him with an expression that said she had known this was coming and was glad he was hearing it directly. "Yes," Luka said. "All right." Gepard nodded. He went back to the patrol reports. Then he stopped. He said, without looking up: "Dunn," and he reached for the pen. "What?" Luka said. Gepard looked up. "Not you. I was—" He stopped. He put the pen down. "I need to tell Dunn about Snezhnaya," he said. "Specifically. That I think he should run the strategic security coordination for the full expansion." Seele's mouth curved slightly — she turned it into a neutral expression immediately. "I'll tell him today," Gepard said. "While I'm in the mode." "In the mode?" Luka said. "Of telling people things directly," Gepard said, with the flatness of someone who was aware that this was slightly self-critical but was stating it accurately. Luka looked at Seele. She was very carefully not making the sound. "Good," Luka said. —— Gepard found Dunn at the north wall at 1430. He walked up beside him. Dunn was doing the afternoon inspection with his field notebook open, making notes in the specific compressed handwriting he used for field records. He glanced at Gepard. "Sir," he said. "Dunn," Gepard said. He stood beside him at the wall, looking at the same section. "I want to tell you something." Dunn closed the notebook. He turned to face him. This was not something he did during wall inspections — the notebook stayed open for wall inspections. The closing meant he was giving full attention. "Snezhnaya," Gepard said. "When it's operational. The full expansion security architecture. The threat framework, the personnel deployment, the coordination with the construction teams." He paused. "I want you running it." Dunn was very still for a moment. "Not just the perimeter detail," Gepard said. "The full strategic architecture. Every installation as it opens, every team we put on the surface, every new geological threat we don't have documentation for yet." He paused. "That's your job if you want it." "I want it," Dunn said. Immediately. No hesitation. Gepard looked at him. "You're going to need to expand your threat framework significantly. Surface terrain is completely different from the dome's geological constraints. We have almost no documentation for the surface mineral behavior or the Fragmentum density patterns outside the dome."
