"I've been building preliminary models since the survey results came in," Dunn said. Gepard stared at him. "Since the survey." "Three weeks ago," Dunn said. He opened the field notebook. He turned it to show the page — not the day's wall inspection notes, a preliminary threat framework schematic for surface installation security that took up six pages. Gepard looked at the six pages. "I've been waiting for you to bring it up," Dunn said. "I didn't want to assume." Gepard looked at the framework. At the six pages of preliminary work that Dunn had been building for three weeks in anticipation of a conversation that hadn't happened yet. "You could have told me," Gepard said. "You could have told me," Dunn said. The wall. The north section. The dome above. The two of them at the precise location where the twelve messages had been sent eight years ago about the shift assignment. "Yes," Gepard said. "I could have." "I kept the framework current," Dunn said. "Every time new survey data came in I updated the models." He paused. "I was going to bring it to you this week. I just wasn't sure when." "Bring it tomorrow," Gepard said. "Full briefing. I want to see the complete models." "Tomorrow morning," Dunn said. "0900." "0900," Gepard agreed. Dunn closed the notebook. He looked at Gepard for a moment. At the Emanator of Preservation of Jarilo-IV who was also the General of the Silvermane Guard and who had also, apparently, kept twelve messages from eight years ago and had been thinking about Snezhnaya's strategic security for longer than he'd said. "Gepard," Dunn said. "Yes," Gepard said. "Serval mentioned you kept the messages," Dunn said. Gepard was quiet for one second. "She told you." "She told me she told you I kept them," Dunn said. "She said I should know." "She's right," Gepard said. He looked at the wall. "You were learning something worth learning. The record was worth keeping." Dunn was quiet. "You know how to commit now," Gepard said. "You have for a long time." He paused. "I should have said that directly instead of keeping a record of it." The north wall. The afternoon. The dome above them. "Yes," Dunn said. Not with reproach. Just accurate. "Tomorrow," Gepard said. "0900. Show me the full framework." "0900," Dunn said. He went back to the inspection. Gepard went back toward the palace. They did not make any further ceremony of it, because the thing that had needed to be said had been said, and the thing that would happen tomorrow at 0900 was the work that followed it. —— Saturday evening, Bronya was in the study when Serval knocked. She had known Serval was going to come. Svarog had messaged her at 1600: *Serval has a complete analysis. She would like to present it to both you and Gepard together. This evening, if you are available.* She had replied: *The study. 1900.* At 1900 exactly, Serval knocked. Gepard was already in the study — they had both been there since 1830, not doing anything specific, just occupying the same space in the way they had been doing since Friday night with a slightly different quality. The frozen garden was behind them. The conversation on the bench was behind them. They were not performing anything different. They were just in it together, and the being-in-it had a different quality than before. Serval came in with the equipment case. She stopped when she saw them both. She looked at Bronya. She looked at Gepard. She looked at the space between them, which was not physically different from before but which had a quality that Serval, who catalogued frequencies for a living, identified in approximately four seconds. "Something happened," she said. "Sit down, Serval," Gepard said. "Something definitely happened," she said. She sat down. She put the equipment case on the table. She looked at them both. "Are we addressing this or are we doing the analysis first?" "Analysis first," Bronya said. "That's efficient," Serval said. "Also suspicious." She opened the case anyway. "All right. The analysis." She laid the tablets on the table. She pulled up the channel frequency display — the three weeks of post-battle data, the baseline comparisons, the latent capacity indicators that she and Svarog had identified on Thursday afternoon. She organized it in the same logical sequence she had used with Svarog. Then she turned the display to face them. "The notation channels are running below their designed capacity," she said. "They have been running below designed capacity for — probably as long as the network has existed. I don't know when the network was built. Svarog doesn't either." "Clara reached the twenty-second notation section this week," Bronya said. "She says the sections describe functions that the network was built for. She hasn't said anything about it running below capacity." "She might not have the instrumentation to detect it," Serval said. "What I'm seeing is in the frequency range that requires my specific equipment and three weeks of comparative analysis." She paused. "It's not something you'd know from the notation language alone." She pointed at the display. "The latent capacity indicators — these frequency signatures in the channel data that don't correspond to the network's active output. They've been there since the first recordings I took four months ago. I thought they were interference. They're not. They're dormant channel capacity." She looked at Bronya. "The combined frequency — the golden ice propagation and the claimed frequency — activated the dormant capacity," she said. "The channels are running nine percent above their previous output because they're running nine percent closer to what they were designed to run at. Not above their capacity. To their capacity." She paused. "The combined frequency is the specific input the channels were built to conduct." Bronya looked at the display. At the latent capacity indicators and the activation pathway. She looked at Gepard. "The golden ice and the claimed frequency together," she said. "Together," Serval said. "Separately they activate different sections of the dormant capacity. Together they activate the full latent network." She paused. "Svarog and I found the activation pathway on Thursday. We couldn't have found it without his four hundred years of monitoring data and my equipment and comparative analysis together." She looked at Bronya and then at Gepard. "We found it ourselves, from the data. We didn't have anything pointing us toward it." "No," Bronya said. "You didn't." "Is that—" Serval stopped. "Is that relevant?" "Yes," Bronya said. "The analysis belongs to you and Svarog. Not to anything that predicted it. You found it because you looked." Serval processed this. "Okay," she said. "I'm going to accept that at face value and move on." She turned back to the display. "The practical implications: the channel output increase is going to continue. Svarog models it stabilizing at approximately fifteen percent above the previous baseline once the combined frequency's harmonic is fully established throughout the network. That might take three months. It might take six. He doesn't have the historical data to predict the stabilization timeline precisely." "And the fifteen percent output increase does what?" Gepard said. "Improves the notation network's conductance efficiency throughout the geological structure of the outer machine district and the surrounding area," Serval said. "Which means the claimed frequency and the golden ice propagate more efficiently. Which means the ambient Fragmentum suppression in the district is stronger. Which means the extraction operations in the Snezhnaya area have a more stable geological environment." She paused. "It also means that if there is ever again a threat to Jarilo-IV that requires the notation network to carry extreme energy — like the First Stellaron battle — the network is better equipped for it." She looked at them both. "The thing you two did at the battle didn't just end the threat. It improved the planet's defensive infrastructure." She paused. "Significantly." The study was quiet. "Thank you," Bronya said. "For the three weeks and the express train and the Thursday afternoon with Svarog." "The data was interesting," Serval said. "I would have looked regardless." She paused. "Also it's Jarilo-IV. It's my home. I wasn't not going to look." She closed the case. She sat back. She looked at them both again. At the space between them and the quality of it. She pointed between them. "Now," she said. Gepard looked at Bronya. Bronya looked at Serval. "Friday evening," Bronya said. "The Landau house garden." Serval's expression did something. It was not the catalogued professional expression. It was the full-face version, the one that arrived when something she had been tracking for a long time and had been waiting to arrive finally arrived. "The garden," she said. "The bench," Gepard said. With the flatness that was him not performing anything. Serval looked at him. At her younger brother. At Gepard, twenty-five years old, who had been counting things since he was seventeen. "Finally," she said. "Yes," he said. "How long?" she said. "Nine years," Bronya said. Serval stared at her. "Nine years." "From his side," Bronya said. "I was counting other things." "What were you counting?" Serval said. Bronya paused. Then she said: "I was counting how long I could manage it and call that the right choice." Serval looked at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-IV, twenty-six years old, who had been managing the thing between her and Gepard into the correct timing for two years of the Guardianship and some number of years before that and had finally decided that the management was the wrong approach. "And?" Serval said. "And I stopped," Bronya said. Serval looked at them both. She took a breath. She said: "Good." Simply. Without further performance. Good. The complete version of everything she might have said. She stood up. She picked up the equipment case. She looked at Gepard. "Nine years," she said. "Yes," he said. She pointed at him. "I have been watching you count things about her since we were teenagers," she said. "You owe me years of pretending not to notice." "I know," he said. "Don't say I know," she said. The corner of his mouth. "I'm aware that I owe you," he said. She made a sound — something between a laugh and a resigned exhale. She pointed at Bronya. "Manage less," she said. Not unkindly. Directly. "I'm working on it," Bronya said. "Work faster," Serval said. She went out. The study door closed. In the quiet after, Bronya said: "She's going to talk about this with Dunn." "Within forty-eight hours," Gepard said. "And Dunn is going to tell Luka." "Dunn is not the type to gossip," Gepard said. "He's not," Bronya said. "But Serval is going to tell him and he's going to be glad and the being-glad is going to be visible on his face the next time he sees Luka and Luka is going to ask." Gepard thought about this. "That's probably accurate," he said. "It's fine," Bronya said. "They should know." She paused. "Everyone who was at the Landau house dinner should know." He looked at her. "You want to tell them." "I want to not manage whether they know," she said. "Which is slightly different." She paused. "Serval knows. Svarog will know by tomorrow. Clara is going to figure it out the next time she sees us in the same room." She paused. "Seele already knows." "She knows?" Gepard said. "She's my best friend," Bronya said. "She knew on the outer wall. Before I said anything." He looked at her. "Before the outer wall version," he said. "Or the garden version?" "Both," Bronya said. "She was at the ledge the morning after the outer wall. She knew." She paused. "She laughed for approximately a full minute when I told her you'd asked if it was effective." He was quiet for one second. "She laughed," he said. "Genuinely," Bronya said. "Fully." "At me," he said. "At both of us," Bronya said. "We're very slow." He looked at the study. At the wall with the operational maps, the desk with the reports, the window with the outer city visible. At Bronya, twenty-six years old, in the study she had been running the planet from for two years. "We're very thorough," he said. "Yes," she said. "We are." She picked up the patrol report she had set aside when Serval arrived. She read it. He picked up the supply chain assessment beside it. He read that. They sat in the study on Saturday evening doing the work that was always there to do, and the quality of doing it was different from before, and neither of them made any announcement about the difference. —— After the briefing room, Luka found Seele in the outer machine district's eastern junction. She was running the approach arc that they had worked on in the morning — not the full sequence, just the first movement, the three-degree adjustment that had taken three attempts to arrive at. She ran it against the junction wall, the scythe making the controlled arc, and she was doing it with the precision of someone who was drilling a correction into muscle memory rather than just practicing. He watched for a moment without saying anything. She knew he was there — she had heard the footfall — but she was mid-repetition and she finished it. "How was Gepard?" she said. "Good," he said. "He's giving me the Snezhnaya security architecture." She turned. She looked at him with the full attention that meant she was receiving something significant. "He said that directly?" "He said: it's going to be you," Luka said. "I want you to start building toward it." He paused. "He also said he was going to tell Dunn about Snezhnaya. He said it while I was still in the room. He was going to do it today." She grinned — quick and real. "He said while you were still there that he was going to do the thing you told him to do." "He said he was in the mode," Luka said. "Of telling people things directly." Seele's grin got wider. "He said that." "He said it like it was a technical condition," Luka said. "A temporary operational state that he was making use of while available." "That is," Seele said, "the most Gepard thing anyone has said since Bronya told me he asked if it was effective." "What was effective?" Luka said. "I'll tell you later," she said. She lowered the scythe to the carry position. "The Snezhnaya architecture. What's your first step?" "The surface mineral composition documentation," he said. "We have Svarog's geological survey but not the specific threat behavior data for Fragmentum in surface conditions outside the dome. I need to build that from scratch." He paused. "I was thinking the first surface patrol — when the transit route is operational — should include systematic threat documentation. Not just the walk. Actual geological and Fragmentum condition assessment." She looked at him. "You want the first surface access to be a working mission." "The group trip will be the group trip," he said. "That's social. The threat documentation mission should be separate. Earlier." He paused. "You and me and the equipment. Before the transit route is officially open, if Gepard authorizes it." She was quiet for a moment. She looked at the junction wall where she had been running the arc. At the outer machine district around them. "The surface," she said. "Yes," he said. "You want to go up before everyone else," she said. "For work." "For work that needs to happen before the civilian transit opens," he said. "The threat documentation should precede the civilian access, not follow it." She looked at him. At the chrome arm and the careful framing of something that was also — underneath the documentation rationale — him wanting to go up with her before the group went. Not for the moon. For the surface. For the cold light sky that neither of them had stood under yet. "You're right that the documentation needs to happen first," she said. "Yes," he said. "You're also," she said, "not telling me the whole reason you want to go up before the group." He was quiet. "Luka," she said. "I want to see the sky with you," he said. Plainly. "Before it becomes an event. Before there are twenty people and speeches and all of it." He paused. "I want there to be one morning where it's just the two of us and the sky and it's not anything except that." She held his gaze for a moment. "The documentation mission is real," he said. "I'm not inventing it." "I know it's real," she said. "Both things can be true." She looked at the outer machine district around them. At the dome above. "Yes," she said. "Yes to which part?" he said. "Both," she said. "Yes to the documentation mission and yes to the morning before the group." She paused. "Talk to Gepard about the authorization. Make the case on the documentation grounds — which are real and valid. And then on the morning we go up, the documentation is real and the other thing is also real, and they're both exactly what they are." He nodded. "All right." She looked at him. She reached up and adjusted the collar of his jacket — the specific straightening gesture that she had started doing sometime in the last month, the small domestic adjustment that had arrived into their interactions without discussion and had stayed. He let her do it without comment, which was its own kind of acknowledgment. "Come on," she said. "I want to show you what I worked out with the horizontal arc in nine-beta." "I was there," he said. "I refined it after you left," she said. "The third-junction geometry has a different ceiling angle than the entrance. I found something." He looked at her. At the scythe and the something she had found. "Show me," he said. —— Clara reached the twenty-third notation section on Saturday morning at 0800. She had woken at 0630, which was still early but was not the 0545 that the notation network's morning conductance usually produced, and she had gone to the workshop, and Svarog had been in the back room, and she had gone directly to the secondary bench and put her hands on the twenty-third section's notation carving and read. She read it for an hour and twenty minutes. At 0800 she took her hands off the wall and sat on the floor. Svarog came to the workshop doorway. He looked at her. She looked at the carving. "The twenty-third," she said. "Yes," he said. "It's the people," she said. He waited. "The first twenty-two sections are — the network, the channels, the geological functions, the mineral compositions, the structural architecture of everything." She paused. "The twenty-third section is the people. Specifically." She looked at the carving. "The Architects documented who was going to use the network. Not by name. By function. By what they would be and what they would do." She paused. "The Guardian who receives and claims. The Emanator who preserves. The two who remember." She paused. "The keeper of the lineage." She looked at her hands. "That's me." "Yes," Svarog said. "They documented me," she said. "They documented what you are," he said. "Which is not quite the same thing." She looked at the carving. At the notation above the secondary bench that she had been learning to read for nine months. "It's very close to the same thing," she said. "Yes," he said. "It is." She sat with it for a while. Svarog went back to the back room. She sat on the workshop floor with the twenty-third section and the specific weight of having found her own description in a document that predated her by centuries. After a while, she got up. She went to the back room doorway. "Svarog," she said. "Yes," he said. "The two who remember," she said. "That's Seele and Luka." "Yes," he said. "Is it in the twenty-third section because they remembered," she said. "Or because the Architects knew they would?" He looked at her. At twelve-year-old Clara, who had been reading notation for nine months and who had arrived at the question that split the document's meaning into two completely different things. "I don't know," he said. She looked at him. "You don't know." "No," he said. "I've been monitoring this network for four hundred years and I do not know whether the Architects knew who would fill these functions or whether they described the functions and the people arrived to fill them." He paused. "Both explanations are consistent with the data." Clara thought about this for a long moment. "I'm going to ask Bronya," she said. "She'll have a perspective," Svarog said. "She usually does," Clara said. She went back to the workshop. She sent a message to Bronya: *the twenty-third section is done. There's a question I want to ask you. Not urgent. When you have time.* Bronya replied in twelve minutes: *Sunday morning. Come for breakfast.* Clara sent back: *Okay.* She put the phone away. She looked at the twenty-third section one more time. Then she went to find the Moles, because it was Saturday and they were somewhere in the outer machine district doing something, and the twenty-third section was significant but it was also still Saturday. —— She found them at the junction square in the eastern residential zone. This was not surprising. The junction square in the eastern residential zone had become, in the six weeks since the access provisions opened it to through-traffic, the specific location that the four of them gravitated toward when there was no immediate workshop task and the notation sessions were done. It had good visibility — you could see three junction approaches and the construction crew's eastern staging area from the square's center bench — and it had the bakery stall that had opened in week three of the access provisions. Toma and Pino were at the bench. Oleg was at the bakery stall. "Clara," Toma said. "How was the twenty-third?" "Big," Clara said. She sat down. "Bigger than I thought." "Good big or difficult big?" Pino said. "Both," Clara said. They sat for a moment. Oleg came back from the bakery stall with a paper bag. He distributed its contents without saying anything first, because this was Oleg's approach to emotional support — provide the food and then the conversation would follow at its own pace. Clara ate the pastry. It was good. "The twenty-third section," she said, "has descriptions of who would use the network. By function." She paused. "I'm in it." Toma looked at her. "You're in the section." "The keeper of the lineage," Clara said. "That's the description. That's me." Pino looked at his hands — reflexively, the way he always looked at his hands when the notation network was being discussed and he was the one who had been holding the channel wall. He looked at Clara. "Is that—" He paused. "Is that okay? Being in it?" Clara thought about the question. "I think so," she said. "It's a lot to sit with. But yes. I think it's okay." "You're also twelve," Oleg said. "Yes," Clara said. "The Architects documented you when you were twelve," Oleg said. "Technically they documented the function," Clara said. "Not a specific age." "But you're twelve," Oleg said. "Yes," she said. "And the Architects knew you were going to be here at twelve," he said. "Or they knew the function would be filled by someone," Clara said. "I'm not sure which." "That's the question," Toma said. She was watching Clara carefully with the focused attention she used when something mattered. "Which one is it?" "I don't know," Clara said. "I'm going to ask Bronya tomorrow." She paused. "Either way it's—" She stopped. She found the right word. "Big," she said. "It's just big." Toma put her arm around Clara's shoulders. Not saying anything. Just there. Oleg produced a second pastry from the bag. He held it out. "For the bigness," he said. Clara took it. She leaned into Toma slightly. The junction square ran its Saturday afternoon around them — the construction crew on the eastern staging area, the people moving through the new through-routes, the bakery stall doing steady business, the city doing what it did. Mira was at the square's edge, watching the construction crew, predictably. "Mira," Pino said quietly. "I see her," Clara said. "She's here every afternoon," Toma said. "She's tracking the construction progress," Clara said. "She's been doing it since the first week." She looked at the construction crew. "The heating connection is running ahead of schedule. Three months and twenty-seven days, not four months." "You know the timeline?" Pino said. "Bronya knows the timeline," Clara said. "I heard it in the briefing." Mira turned and saw them. She waved — the wave of a nine-year-old, full arm, completely unself-conscious. Oleg waved back with equal commitment. Mira apparently found this satisfying because she grinned and went back to watching the crew. "Three months and twenty-seven days," Toma said. "And then she gets heat," Clara said. They sat with that for a moment. The junction square, the construction crew, the bakery stall, Mira watching the progress. The outer machine district doing its Saturday. "Clara," Oleg said. "Yes," she said. "Write it in the notebook," he said. She looked at him. He held out the notebook — the current one, the fourth, the one that had started after the second Stellaron. He held it out along with the pen. She took them. She wrote: *Saturday. The twenty-third section: the people. The Guardian, the Emanator, the two who remember, the keeper. The network was built for all of us. I read it this morning and then I sat on the workshop floor for a while. Then I came here. Mira waved. Oleg waved back. The heat is coming in three months and twenty-seven days.* She closed the notebook. She gave it back to Oleg. He took it. He put it in the jacket without looking at what she had written. "Good," he said. They stayed in the junction square until the construction crew broke for the afternoon, and Mira finally went home, and the bakery stall sold its last item, and the Saturday light shifted toward evening. —— That night, Serval found Dunn at the Landau house. She had not sent a message first. She had come from the workshop — the channel analysis with Svarog had run until 1700, then the briefing with Bronya and Gepard, then a walk through the district while she processed the conversation. She had ended up at the Landau house the way she ended up places when she was processing things and her feet made the navigational decisions without input. Lynx was not surprised to see her. Lynx opened the door, took one look at Serval's expression, and said: "Dunn is in the kitchen. He brought food." "Of course he did," Serval said, and went to the kitchen. Dunn was at the counter with the specific quality of someone who had made a decision to produce food and was executing the decision methodically. He had been to the junction market — there was a paper bag from the new Underworld market on the counter alongside the regular ingredients — and he was doing something with vegetables and the contents of the bag that had the structured quality of a person who planned meals in advance. He looked up when she came in. He did not say anything for a moment. He looked at her expression. "The analysis went well?" he said. "The analysis was excellent," she said. She sat down at the kitchen table. "It's not the analysis I'm processing." He went back to the vegetables. "What are you processing?" She thought about how to say it. The frequency analysis version, the channel capacity version, the Bronya-and-Gepard version. "Gepard," she said. "And Bronya." She paused. "Something happened Friday night." Dunn's knife stopped for a fraction of a second. He kept cutting. "In the garden," he said. She looked at him. "You knew." "I noticed the quality of the briefing room this afternoon," he said. "When Luka and Seele came in. Gepard was—" He paused. "He was in the mode, as you said. The telling-people-things-directly mode. He had clearly committed to it in a way that suggested something had shifted." He paused. "I didn't know what. I suspected." "The garden," Serval said. "The bench. Full version." Dunn was quiet for a moment. He put the knife down. He turned to look at her. His expression — not the professional one, the real one, the one she had been cataloguing more and more in the past three months — was the expression of someone who was genuinely glad about something. "Good," he said. Exactly the same word she had used. The complete version. "Yes," she said. He went back to the vegetables. "Nine years," she said. "He said nine years." "From the winter assessment," Dunn said. "When they were seventeen." She stared at him. "You know about the winter assessment?" "He mentioned it once," Dunn said. "Three years ago. He didn't say why. He said: the winter assessment, she stayed six minutes past the end. That was the sentence. I didn't ask further." Serval looked at the counter. At Dunn, who had been holding a single sentence for three years without asking what it meant. "You keep things," she said. "Like he keeps things." "Eight years of working alongside someone," Dunn said. "You develop similar habits." "That's what he said about the almost-laugh," Serval said. "People who spend time together develop similar expressions." "He said that?" Dunn said. "When I told him Bronya makes the same sound," she said. "The almost-laugh." "She does," Dunn said. "I've noticed it for six months. Since the Stellaron battle." He paused. "It's specifically his sound. She adopted it." Serval looked at him. At Dunn, twenty-four years old, who had been tracking the development of a specific involuntary vocal expression across two people for six months without saying anything about it to anyone. "You notice a great deal," she said. "I watch things," he said. "It's useful." "Yes," she said. She looked at the vegetables. At the paper bag from the new market. "What's in the bag?" "Pastries," he said. "The dark filling kind. From the new market." "Sampo's recommendation," she said. "His and Natasha's," Dunn said. "Separately, in the same week. I took it as a consensus." Serval made the sound — the quick real laugh. Dunn looked at her when she made it. "What?" she said. "Nothing," he said. "I like that sound." She looked at him. At the twenty-four-year-old who had been in her band at sixteen and who had come to the Landau house on Saturday with pastries from the new market and vegetables and was making dinner without being asked. "Dunn," she said. "Yes," he said. "You've been waiting," she said, "for the right moment." He was quiet. "You knew about the band," she said. "You knew I knew. You passed me the bread before I reached for it on Tuesday. You came to the Landau house tonight without sending a message first." She paused. "You've been waiting for the right moment to — what, exactly?" He put the knife down again. He turned to face her properly. He was not performing composure — he was just himself, Dunn, twenty-four years old, looking at her with the same directness he brought to security frameworks and wall inspections. "To tell you," he said. "That I would like the things that are already happening to continue happening. The dinners. The Tuesday frequency conversations. The walks after the construction briefings." He paused. "I'd like them to happen more deliberately. Not accidentally." Serval looked at him. "That's what I've been waiting for the right moment to say," he said. She was quiet for a moment. Not processing the words — she had arrived at the words ten seconds after they started. Processing the specific quality of being asked plainly, directly, by someone who had been waiting for the right moment and had decided this was it. "You picked Saturday night in the Landau house kitchen," she said. "You came here," he said. "That felt like a signal." "It wasn't calculated," she said. "I walked here." "I know," he said. "That's why it felt like a signal." She looked at him for a long moment. "I was in your band," she said. "You were sixteen." "Yes," he said. "That was eight years ago," she said. "Eight years," he said. He did not explain the eight years. He looked at her and let the eight years be exactly what they were. She thought about Tuesday dinners and frequency conversations and the bread passed before she reached for it. She thought about Gepard and the nine years and the winter assessment and the six minutes. She thought about Bronya deciding on Friday night that the not yet was over. "More deliberately," she said. "Yes," he said. "Starting when?" she said. "Starting whenever you'd like," he said. She looked at him. At the kitchen. At the paper bag with the dark filling pastries from the new market. "The pastries," she said. "Yes?" he said. "Are they the good kind?" she said. "Sampo and Natasha both recommended them independently," he said. "That's a high bar," she said. "Consensus," he said. She took a pastry from the bag. She ate it. It was, in fact, very good. She acknowledged this. "Starting now," she said. He looked at her. "The more deliberately," she said. "Starting now works." He turned back to the vegetables. His expression was doing the thing — the real one, the one she had been cataloguing. She watched it and filed it. "What are you making?" she said. "I don't have a name for it," he said. "It's an assembly of things that I know work together." "Does it take long?" she said. "Forty minutes," he said. "Good," she said. She settled in the chair. "Tell me about the north wall structural assessment. The one from this afternoon's inspection. You were writing in the notebook." He glanced at her. "You noticed that." "I notice things," she said. "Tell me." He told her about the north wall. The assessment, the findings, the notation he had made. She asked specific questions about the northwest section's joint integrity and he answered them and asked her, in return, about the combined frequency's effect on wall-adjacent notation channels, and she explained the activation pathway she had found with Svarog. They talked through the vegetables and the assembly-of-things-that-worked-together and the forty minutes, and Lynx came to the kitchen door at minute thirty-eight, looked in at them, and went back to her room with the expression of someone who was making a note for later. —— That night, Bronya went to the outer wall alone. Not for inspection — she had no patrol folder, no tablet, no operational purpose. She went because the outer wall at night was a specific kind of space that she had needed periodically for two years, the space where the city was visible and the dome was above and there was nothing to manage. She stood at the wall for a while. She thought about the frozen garden. The bench. The full version of I love you and the nine years behind it. She thought about Serval: *manage less. Work faster.* She thought about the man at the first junction whose daughter had homework. The woman whose mother had a Thursday appointment. Mira, nine years old, watching the construction crew. She thought about the twenty-second notation section and what Clara had said: *you're part of it. Specifically.* She thought: I don't know if the Architects knew I was going to be standing on this wall at this moment. I don't know if the twenty-third section describes me or describes the function that I happen to fill. Either way, I'm here. Either way, I'm the one making the decisions. She thought: the planet is not a script. The city is not a prediction. The nine-year-old in the junction square is not a function in a notation section. She thought: these are just people. And I'm responsible for them. And that's the thing that matters. She looked at the outer city. At the dome. At the notation channels conducting their warmth through the geological structure below. At the outer machine district, the junction lights, the quiet of a Saturday night. At everything that was there and had always been there and was, now, different from what it had been. A footstep on the access steps behind her. She didn't turn. She knew the footfall. Gepard came to stand beside her. He did not say anything. He looked at the city. "You followed me," she said. "I noticed you weren't in the study," he said. "Then I noticed where you weren't, and I came here." "You don't have to come every time I'm on the outer wall," she said. "I know," he said. She looked at him. At the gold ice ambient in the cold air. At the person who had been counting the minutes she stayed past the ends of things since they were seventeen years old. "I was thinking," she said. "Yes," he said. "About managing less," she said. "What it looks like in practice." He looked at the city. "What does it look like?" She thought about it. "Telling you when I'm on the outer wall," she said. "Instead of coming here and expecting you to figure it out." "You could have told me," he said. "Before you came." "Yes," she said. "I could have said: I'm going to the outer wall to think. Do you want to come?" She paused. "I didn't." "No," he said. "I came here and you followed anyway," she said. "Yes," he said. "But that's me not waiting to be asked. That's not you managing less." She turned to look at him. At the very specific quality of Gepard making the distinction between his own behavior and hers, clearly and without softening it. "You're right," she said. "I'm aware," he said. And then he looked at her with the flat expression that meant he had done the thing she kept telling him not to do and he knew it. She made the sound. The real one. "You did that deliberately," she said. "I'm practicing the distinction between I know and I'm aware," he said. "Serval told me it's a problem." "It is a problem," Bronya said. "She said it at the door when she left," he said. "After the analysis." "What exactly did she say?" Bronya said. "She pointed at me," he said. "And she said: stop saying I know when what you mean is I understand and I'm choosing not to address it differently. She said it's intellectually dishonest." Bronya looked at him. "She said that." "She spent three weeks running frequency analysis on a Stellaron-grade phenomenon," he said. "She had opinions ready." "She was right," Bronya said. "Yes," he said. "She usually is." They stood on the outer wall. The dome above. The city below. The notation channels warm in the stone. "Next time I'm coming to the outer wall," Bronya said, "I'm going to tell you first." "Yes," he said. "And you're going to come," she said. "Yes," he said. "And we're going to stand here like this," she said. "Yes," he said. She looked at the city for a moment. She put her hand on his arm — not the face, the arm, the simple contact. He put his hand over hers. Gold ice and claimed frequency and the city below and the dome above. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "I'm glad it's nine years," she said. "Looking back at it. The not yet and the counting and all of it." She paused. "I'm glad it accumulated. I'm glad we were thorough." He was quiet for a moment. "I would have kept counting," he said. "As long as it took." "I know," she said. And then, because she was working on managing less and the *I know* was real and not deflection: "I'm glad you didn't have to." He held her hand on his arm. The city ran its Saturday night below them. The dome was above. The outer wall was cold and exactly where they were supposed to be. —— END OF CHAPTER 64 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 63: What We're Building Chapter Text The train arrived forty minutes early. Serval had sent a message at 1320 saying she was on the express, which ran thirty minutes faster than the standard service and which cost considerably more, and that this was Svarog's problem for building her frequency equipment to produce results that she could not wait a day to discuss. The message was addressed to Svarog, copied to Gepard with the annotation: *don't tell Lynx I'm early, I want to see what the house looks like when nobody's expecting me.* Gepard told Lynx. This was not a difficult decision. Lynx's reaction when unexpected guests arrived to a house that was not prepared was a specific and entirely avoidable category of disruption, and thirty years of being her younger brother had given Gepard a clear understanding of which disruptions were avoidable and should therefore be avoided. He sent Lynx a message at 1323. Lynx sent back a thumbs up and then went to the Landau house, checked the guest room that she had already prepared, found it acceptable, and came back. At 1340 she sent Gepard: *I knew you'd tell me. You always tell me.* He sent back: *You always look like that when people arrive early.* *What look?* *The one where you're reassessing the evening.* A pause. Then: *...fair.* —— Serval arrived at the outer machine district at 1412 with her frequency equipment case on her shoulder and the expression of someone who had been running calculations in her head for the duration of a three-hour train journey and had arrived at a number she was not entirely comfortable with. Svarog was at the workshop entrance. He looked at her. At the equipment case. At the expression. "Show me," he said. She came in. She set the case on the secondary bench. She did not make small talk, which was not standard for Serval but was not alarming — Serval's small talk was a reflection of her energy level and her energy level was currently completely focused on whatever was in the frequency data. She opened the case. She pulled out the recording equipment and the analysis tablets and she laid them across the bench in the order that would let her explain the findings in the most legible sequence. Then she looked at Svarog. "The standard Stellaron activation frequency," she said. "4.7 kilocycles. What it looked like in the channel data when the First Stellaron breached." "I have the recordings," Svarog said. "I know you have them," she said. "I've had them for three weeks. And for three weeks I've been running the comparison between the 4.7 activation readings and the frequency at 0257 when—" She stopped. She looked at her tablets. She started again. "The reading at 0257. The moment the contact point shifted. The moment the signal at the Restricted Zone's northern section changed character." She looked at him. "I've been calling it the claimed frequency. In my notes. Because that's what it looked and felt like — a claim being made. The Stellaron's activation energy being claimed by something." "Yes," Svarog said. He was watching her. "The claimed frequency runs through the notation channels now," Serval said. "It's in every junction I've tested since I've been back. It's not at the Stellaron's 4.7. It's at a different frequency entirely." She pointed at the tablet's display. "3.1. The same kilocycle range the First Stellaron was at during the 'considering' period. But the quality is completely different from the Stellaron's signal. It's — warmer. More organized. It's conducting through the channels the way source energy conducts, not the way activation energy does." She looked at Svarog. "The Stellaron's activation energy was claimed and is now running through the notation network as a new kind of source frequency," she said. "That's the data. That's what I've been sitting with for three weeks." She paused. "What I haven't been able to explain is how. A Stellaron's activation energy is not something that a person claims. It's not — that's not a category of thing that happens." "And yet," Svarog said. "And yet," Serval said. "The data is the data." She looked at him. "Svarog. What happened at 0257?" Svarog looked at her. At the equipment case. At the tablets. At thirty years of Serval Landau's specific quality of reaching for the accurate answer even when the accurate answer was inconveniently large. "That," he said, "is a question for Bronya." —— Serval found Bronya at 1500 in the outer machine district's eastern junction, watching the access shaft construction. The shaft was in its third day — the groundwork had been completed, the initial bore was down eleven meters, and the construction crew was on the fourth section of casing. It was loud and specific and entirely unlike anything that had existed in this section of the outer machine district before the decision to build it. Bronya was watching from the junction's secondary entrance, not with a tablet and not in her operational posture, just watching. She heard Serval coming — Serval walked at a specific pace and the outer machine district's acoustic quality made people audible from twelve meters — and she said, without turning: "You're early." "I was on the express," Serval said. "I know. Gepard told me." Bronya watched the crew lower the next casing section. "Did you go to Svarog first?" "Yes," Serval said. She stood beside Bronya. She looked at the shaft construction. "He told me to find you." "I know," Bronya said. "He messaged me forty minutes ago." Serval looked at her. At the mark visible above the coat's neckline. At the coat — the green one, the new one, which Serval was seeing for the first time and which she was filing rapidly because it told her something about where Bronya was that the standard coat did not. "Bronya," Serval said. "What happened at 0257?" Bronya watched the casing descend. She watched the crew run the seal check, watched the shift supervisor sign off on the section, watched the next piece staged for lowering. She said: "The Stellaron tried to replace me. To use me as its conduit the way it used Cocolia." She paused. "I decided that if there was going to be a claim made at the contact point, I was going to be the one making it." She paused. "So I did." Serval was quiet for a moment. "You claimed the Stellaron's activation energy," she said. "I claimed the force at the contact point," Bronya said. "It was the Stellaron's. Now it's mine." She paused. "More accurately — now it's Belobog's. It runs through the geological channels because the channels are Belobog's. I carry it because I'm the Guardian. But the force isn't mine as a personal possession. It's the planet's." She looked at the mark for a moment — the gesture she did unconsciously now, the brief touch to the place where the mark was as an acknowledgment of its presence. "The mark is the record of the claim being made and held." Serval looked at the mark. At the angular, thorn-pointed dark symbol visible above the neckline. She had been seeing it since the battle's aftermath and had been running it through every frequency framework she had and had not found a classification for it. "The claimed frequency at 3.1," Serval said. "The warm organized quality. That's the activation energy running through the channels on your terms rather than the Stellaron's." "Yes," Bronya said. "And the combined effect — the golden ice's propagation plus the claimed frequency — that's what's been increasing the channel output," Serval said. "Not a design. Not a built-in progression. Just — the channels receiving two new frequencies and finding more capacity to run them." "I don't know the mechanism," Bronya said. "I know what I did. What the channels do with it is their business." She looked at the construction crew. "Ask Svarog about the channel mechanics. He'll have the best data." "I will," Serval said. She was quiet for a moment. "And the golden ice." Bronya looked at the shaft. "That's not mine to explain," she said. "Ask Gepard." Serval looked at her. At the specific quality Bronya was in — not closed, not managed, just very clearly indicating that a particular door was not hers to open. "At dinner?" Serval said. "That's up to him," Bronya said. Serval filed this. She looked at the access shaft construction. The bore going down into the geological structure of the outer machine district, toward the mineral deposits, toward the resource that was going to power Jarilo-IV for the next three centuries. "Bronya," Serval said. "Yes," Bronya said. "The shaft is going down eleven meters," Serval said. "Fourteen by the end of tomorrow," Bronya said. "Fifty-eight to reach the first extraction point. The casing goes to sixty to allow margin." "How many people are on the crew?" Serval said. "Twenty-three on the primary shift," Bronya said. "Seventeen on the secondary. Dunn is running the security detail for the first two weeks until the access protocol is standardized." "You know every detail," Serval said. Not with surprise — with recognition. "It's the first one," Bronya said. "Of everything we're building. The first physical thing that wasn't there before the battles." She watched the casing section descend. "I'm going to know every detail of the first one." Serval stood beside her. They watched the crew work together — the specific combined competence of people who knew their roles and were doing them well. The shift supervisor's voice carrying across the junction. The bore operator's response. The casing crew's confirmation call. "You're building a city," Serval said. "We're building a city," Bronya said. "I'm signing the forms." She paused. "The people are building the city." Serval looked at her. "That's a very you distinction." "It's the accurate one," Bronya said. They watched the shaft construction for a while longer, standing side by side in the outer machine district's afternoon, and neither of them said anything, because the shaft going down into the rock was a thing that spoke for itself. —— Dinner at the Landau house that Tuesday was — crowded. This was not the plan. The plan had been Bronya, Gepard, Serval, Lynx. The plan became Bronya, Gepard, Serval, Lynx, Dunn, Luka, Seele, Natasha, Sampo, Pela, Clara, and Oleg, who had arrived with Pela on the grounds that Pela had been invited and had failed to specify whether the invitation was singular. Clara had arrived because Toma had mentioned the dinner to her and she had asked if she could come and Lynx, who had been told by Gepard to invite Clara whenever the dinner was a significant-people gathering, had said yes immediately. Oleg had arrived because Oleg had a sense for when significant gatherings were happening. He had brought food from the jacket, which turned out to include three entire baked goods from the junction bakery, which was exactly what twelve people in the Landau house kitchen at 1900 needed. Lynx looked at the twelve people in her kitchen and said: "I'm going to need a bigger table configuration." "I'll move the one from the study," Gepard said. "It's heavy," Lynx said. "I'll move it," he said. "It's very heavy," she said. "It has a marble inlay." "Lynx," he said. "I'm noting the weight," she said. "For liability purposes." "Thank you," he said. He moved the table. It was, in fact, very heavy. —— They ate at two tables arranged end-to-end in the Landau house's main room, which was not the configuration the room had been designed for but which worked in the specific way that configurations work when the people using them are glad to be together and the food is good. Serval had been at the workshop with Svarog for three hours before dinner and had arrived at the Landau house in the specific state of someone who had received a great deal of new information and was sitting with it at a comfortable distance. She was at the far end of one table with Dunn on her left, which was not a planned arrangement but which had the quality of something that had settled naturally when the twelve people found their places. Dunn was — quietly pleased about this. He was eating and participating in the general conversation with the specific professional management of someone who was sitting next to the person he had been working up to sitting next to for approximately six months and was not going to do anything about it in the Landau house in front of ten other people. He passed her the bread when she reached for it two seconds before she reached for it. She noticed. She took the bread. She said: "Thank you." And then she said: "How was the wall inspection today?" "Good," he said. "The northwest section is settled. I'm putting Karev on the outer maintenance rotation starting Thursday." "Is Karev's ice stable enough for the outer rotation?" she said. "He was running hot in the final engagement." "He's been doing recalibration sessions with Ferenz since the battle," Dunn said. "Three weeks, four sessions. Ferenz says his compression ceiling went up two levels." "That's significant," Serval said. "Yes," Dunn said. "You've been watching his development," she said. "I watch all of them," he said. She looked at him. "You do," she said. "You know every one of their individual development trajectories." She paused. "I remember when you were the junior officer who couldn't commit to a command decision without running it by Gepard first." Dunn looked at her. "That was eight years ago." "Seven and a half," she said. "I remember because Gepard was running the outer wall rotation and you sent him twelve messages in one morning about the shift assignments." "I remember those messages," Dunn said. "He kept every one of them," she said. "He showed me once. He said they were a record of you learning how to commit." Dunn was quiet for a moment. "He showed you those." "He was proud of you," Serval said. "He is proud of you. He does not often say it in ways that land correctly — that's a Landau trait, not just his. But he kept the messages." Dunn looked at the table. At the bread he had passed her. At the specific moment of being told, by the person he had been working up to sitting next to for six months, that the person he respected most had been proud of his development and had kept the record of it. "He's terrible at saying things," Dunn said. "Profoundly," Serval agreed. "But excellent at keeping the evidence." They ate for a moment. "I heard you found something in the frequency data," Dunn said. "Something significant." "Yes," she said. "Are you going to tell us at dinner?" he said. "I'm deciding," she said. "The data is Bronya's and Gepard's to share on their own terms. I can explain my analysis once they've said what they've said." Dunn looked down the table. At Gepard, who was eating and listening to Luka describe something at moderate volume, and at Bronya, who was talking to Pela about the second extraction cluster survey schedule. They were not at the same end of the table. They were doing this thing that Dunn had been watching for two years, which was existing in the same room with the easy awareness of each other's position and the complete lack of need to perform it. "You've been analyzing it for three weeks," Dunn said. "Three weeks and four days," Serval said. "And you took the express," he said. "Yes," she said. He looked at her. "So it's very significant." "Yes," she said. She looked at him. At the Colonel of the Silvermane Guard who had been watching everyone's development trajectories for eight years and who had passed her the bread before she reached for it and who had, according to Clara's offhand remark two weeks ago, been in her old band. "Dunn," she said. "Yes," he said. "Did Gepard really keep the messages?" she said. "I don't know," he said. "I've never checked." He paused. "But it sounds like him." She looked at the table. "It does," she said. They ate. —— The dinner had the quality that good dinners have when the people at them are glad to be together and have been through significant things and are now in the specific warmth of the other side of the significant things. Sampo was at the center of one table section with Natasha on his right, and they were doing the thing they had been doing for months which was existing alongside each other with the specific ease of two people who had worked out what they were and were not performing it. He was telling a story about something that had happened in the northwest corridor maintenance route on Monday — something that involved a stuck generator access panel, a structural assessment, and his own coat getting momentarily attached to the panel's release mechanism — and he was telling it at the volume and with the specific embellishments that made stories about stuck generator panels worth hearing. Natasha was listening. She was also occasionally correcting his estimates of how long specific parts of the story had taken, which she was doing with the composed precision of someone who had been doing Sampo's structural fact-checking for long enough to do it without breaking the story's momentum. "—and then the panel did the thing it does, which is fall open with full force in the direction of whoever is standing closest—" "You were standing closest," Natasha said. "I was standing in a considered position," Sampo said. "The panel made a choice." "You said you were standing closest to check the release mechanism." "I said I was standing in the vicinity of the release mechanism." "You said closest," she said. "I may have simplified for narrative flow," he said. "You did," she said. "The important part," he said, "is that the panel made a choice, my coat was involved, and I had to explain to Dunn why there was a maintenance report that referenced a Silvermane Guard access panel and a civilian coat in the same sentence." "What did Dunn say?" Clara said from across the table, because Clara had been tracking the story with full attention. "He said," Sampo said, "'Why is your coat in the maintenance report.'" He paused. "And I said it was a load-bearing garment in the moment of panel contact. And he looked at me for approximately four seconds and then signed off on the report." "He signed off?" Luka said. "He is a very pragmatic Colonel," Sampo said. "He signed off on a maintenance report that cited a civilian coat as load-bearing," Luka said. "He reviewed the evidence," Sampo said. "The evidence was correct." "Your coat was structurally involved in the panel repair," Seele said. "The panel is secured and functioning," Sampo said. "My coat is also secured and functioning. I consider this a positive outcome." "Svarog's opinion of the outcome," Natasha said, "was that you should have used the standard release protocol." "I used the available resources," Sampo said. "Your coat is not a release protocol resource." "It was available," Sampo said. "Svarog notes everything in the maintenance log. He wrote 'civilian coat: unconventional application, effective result.'" The table reacted to this. Oleg made a sound of approval. Clara wrote something in the small notebook she had started bringing to gatherings. Gepard, at the far end, did not look up from his conversation with Luka but the corner of his mouth did a thing that Lynx, who was watching for it, noted with satisfaction. "He put it in the log," Seele said. "He documents accurately," Sampo said. "It's one of his most reliable qualities." "That phrase," Natasha said, looking at him, "could be his epitaph and it would be entirely correct." "I'll tell him," Sampo said. "Don't," Natasha said. "He'll have thoughts about epitaphs and we'll be there for three hours." —— After dinner, the table arrangement was not dismantled immediately because nobody had anywhere urgent to be. Lynx produced tea and the baked goods from Oleg's jacket were consumed in a second wave that nobody had planned for but everyone participated in. The conversation had broken into smaller threads — Serval and Pela at one end running through something on a tablet with the specific combined energy of two people who had both been building toward the same question from different angles and were now addressing it together. Clara and Oleg were at the table's corner with Clara's notebook open, Clara writing and Oleg eating the last of the baked goods and providing commentary. Luka and Gepard were at the window in the specific standing-posture of people who have moved away from the main table to have a quieter conversation. Dunn was helping Lynx with the dishes. This had not been planned or offered — he had simply stood up when Lynx started stacking, and he had taken the stack from her, and now he was at the kitchen sink with his sleeves rolled up and the specific efficient quality of someone who had been maintaining their own living space for long enough that dishwashing was an automatic function that didn't require conscious attention. Lynx looked at him. "You don't have to do that," she said. "I know," he said. She looked at him for a moment. At the Colonel of the Silvermane Guard doing her dishes at the Landau house kitchen sink with his sleeves rolled up. She took the towel from the rack and started drying what he handed her. "Serval," she said. He didn't break rhythm. "What about her?" he said. "She's been at the table with you for two hours," Lynx said. "The seating arrangement was—" "Deliberate," Lynx said. "I arranged it." He stopped. He looked at her. At his younger sister's expression, which was the expression she used when she had done something specific and intentional and was owning it. "Lynx," he said. "You've been working up to sitting next to her for six months," Lynx said. "I accelerated the timeline." He looked at her for a long moment. Then he went back to the dishes. "I notice you're not asking whether I minded," he said. "I know you didn't mind," she said. She dried a plate. "Did you talk to her?" "Yes," he said. "About what?" "The northwest wall inspection," he said. "Karev's recalibration sessions. The frequency data." He paused. "She told me Gepard kept the twelve messages." Lynx looked at him. "The messages from when you were learning to commit to command decisions?" "Yes," he said. Lynx was quiet for a moment. "He kept those?" she said. "She said he showed her once," Dunn said. "Years ago. As a record of me learning." Lynx dried another plate. She thought about her brother, who kept things. Who had been counting things about Bronya since they were seventeen. Who kept a patrol folder that he read every night. Who had apparently kept twelve messages from a junior officer learning to commit to decisions because the messages were a record of something worth recording. "That's Gepard," she said. "Yes," Dunn said. "He's terrible at saying things," Lynx said. "But he keeps everything." "Serval said the same," Dunn said. They did the dishes. The kitchen was warm. Through the wall, the sound of the main room's conversation — Clara's notebook, Oleg's commentary, Seele laughing at something, Sampo's story about a different generator panel story he had apparently been saving for post-dinner. "Lynx," Dunn said. "Yes," she said. "Did you tell her I was in the band?" he said. "No," Lynx said. "Did you tell anyone I was in the band?" he said. "Only Clara," she said. "About four weeks ago. She was asking about Serval's history with frequency analysis and I mentioned the band as context." Dunn dried his hands on the towel. "And Clara told—" "She told Oleg," Lynx said. "Who told Pino. Who told Toma. Who told Seele, I believe, last week." Dunn looked at the ceiling. "In my defense," Lynx said, "I only told Clara." "That is not a defense," he said. "Clara tells people." "Clara documents and the documentation spreads," Lynx said. "It's slightly different." He looked at her. She looked at him. The Landau house kitchen at 2030, the two of them at the sink, her brother doing her dishes. "She knows you were in the band?" he said. "She knew before dinner," Lynx said. "And she didn't say anything," he said. "She's not going to say it in front of everyone," Lynx said. "She's going to say it at the right moment." She paused. "She's Serval. She finds the right moment for everything." He thought about this. About Serval, three weeks away, not saying anything about the band. About Serval at the table beside him, asking about Karev's development and the northwest wall. About the bread passed before she reached for it and the look she had given him when she said thank you. "Okay," he said. "Okay?" Lynx said. "The right moment," he said. Lynx smiled. The real version. "You've learned to commit," she said. "Seven and a half years," he said. "Gepard kept the evidence." —— At the window, Luka and Gepard had been talking about the shaft. This had been Luka's opening because the shaft was the thing Gepard had been thinking about since the construction started and Luka could see when Gepard was thinking about something from across a dinner table. The shaft made sense as a starting point. "Fifty-eight meters," Gepard said. "To the first extraction point. The crew is at fourteen by tomorrow." "Dunn's security detail," Luka said. "Two weeks," Gepard said. "Until the access protocol is standardized. After that it's the standard Silvermane patrol rotation." "He's good at it," Luka said. "The detail work. The specific logistics of securing something new." "Yes," Gepard said. He looked at the window. At the outer machine district visible below the Landau house's elevation — the junction lights, the generator output, the district running its evening. "He's going to be good at Snezhnaya," he said. "When the construction is far enough. He's going to want to run the security planning for the first permanent settlement." "Have you told him that?" Luka said. "Not specifically," Gepard said. "He'll arrive at it." "You could tell him specifically," Luka said. Gepard looked at him. "I told him he was promoted at the ceremony," he said. "He knew what that meant." "You could also just say," Luka said, with the specific flatness of someone making a point, "that you think he's going to be good at Snezhnaya." Gepard was quiet for a moment. "I'll consider it," he said. "You kept the twelve messages," Luka said. Gepard looked at him. "Serval told you." "Serval told Dunn," Luka said. "Dunn told Seele over the dishes. Seele told me just now." He paused. "That's a very short chain." Gepard looked at the window. "I kept them," he said. "They were a record of a person learning something worth learning." "You could tell him that," Luka said. "He knows," Gepard said. "Gepard," Luka said. "You are the most capable person I know. You are also, as a communicator of things you feel, significantly below your own standard." Gepard looked at him. This was a specific kind of sentence and it had been delivered with the specific directness that Luka applied to things he was confident about. Gepard processed it. "That's accurate," he said. "Yes," Luka said. "Tell Dunn about Snezhnaya. Tell him specifically." Gepard was quiet. Through the window, the district. The shaft construction site visible as a lit area in the eastern section. "Yes," he said. "All right." Luka nodded. He looked at the window. "The chrome arm is running above standard tonight," he said. "The dinner warmth in the room — the notation channels under the Landau house run warm on evenings like this. The arm picks it up." "I didn't know the channels ran under the house," Gepard said. "They run under the whole district," Luka said. "The arm can feel them through any mineral contact. The house's foundation is mineral." He looked at the chrome surface. "It's not uncomfortable. Just — present. Like background warmth." Gepard looked at the arm. "Does it happen often?" "More as the channel output increases," Luka said. "Svarog says the nine percent increase is consistent and still climbing." He paused. "He doesn't know why." "Neither does Serval, apparently," Gepard said. "She has a theory," Luka said. "She always has a theory," Gepard said. "Usually it's correct." "This one is going to require your input," Luka said. "And Bronya's." Gepard looked at the window. At Bronya across the room, finishing her conversation with Pela, the mark just visible above the coat's neckline. At the claimed frequency warmth that Serval had been measuring for three weeks and had taken the express to discuss. "I know," he said. "You're not going to tell me what it is," Luka said. "It's not mine to tell first," Gepard said. Luka looked at him. "Yours and Bronya's." "Yes," Gepard said. Luka nodded. He looked at the window. He said: "Seele and I are going to the surface when the transit route opens." "Everyone is," Gepard said. "I know," Luka said. "I mean—" He paused. He found the version. "I mean that she and I are going specifically. Not as part of the group. Together. Before the group, probably. Early morning. Before it gets official." He paused. "I want the first time she stands on the surface to be just the two of us." Gepard looked at him. At the twenty-three-year-old with the chrome arm and the spinal integration and the moon promised to someone and the specific quality of having decided something. "Yes," Gepard said. "Do that." "The route will be ready in six weeks," Luka said. "Six weeks," Gepard said. They stood at the window. The district below. The stars not visible through the dome but known to be there regardless. The route to the surface being built, day by day, toward the cold light sky. —— At 2130, when the dinner had fully concluded and the dishes were done and the table arrangement was restored to its standard configuration, Serval found Gepard at the Landau house entrance where he was getting his coat. She had the frequency equipment case on her shoulder. She looked at him. "Gepard," she said. "Serval," he said. "The golden ice," she said. "In the battle data. And in the current ambient readings of the notation channels." She paused. "It's not what it was before the battle." "No," he said. "The frequency signature changed," she said. "Before the battle, your ice produced a thermal expression I could measure — it was specific to you, higher compression range than any other operator I've documented, but recognizably within the same category as standard ice expression." She paused. "After the fourth Manchester Smash. At 0300. The signature changed completely." She paused. "The new signature is outside any category I have documentation for. It's cold. It's also — luminous, in the frequency sense. It produces light at frequencies that standard ice doesn't produce." He looked at her. At his younger sister — his older sister — who had been running frequency analysis on his ice expression for three weeks and had taken the express train to discuss it. "Yes," he said. "What changed?" she said. He was quiet for a moment. Not avoiding — thinking about how to say it in the version that was true without being the version he owed specifically to Bronya first. "Something looked at me," he said. "During the battle. At the lowest point, when the secondary form had the full output and I was past the ceiling." He paused. "Something looked at me and — recognized what I was doing. What I'd been building toward. And the ice changed to reflect that recognition." Serval looked at him. "Something," she said. "Yes," he said. "Something geological?" she said. "Something larger," he said. She was quiet. She was doing the thing she did when she was absorbing a significant piece of data that required her existing frameworks to either expand or be replaced. He had been watching her do this since she was twenty. "Larger," she said. "As in—" "Ask Bronya," he said. "Together. When it's right." She looked at him. "You and Bronya both know what it is." "Yes," he said. "And you're both going to tell me together," she said. "When it's right," he said. She looked at him for a long moment. At her younger brother — thirty years, the band, six years in the Guard, eight years on the Restricted Zone, a year on the northern ridge, the Manchester Smash, the golden ice, the thing larger than geological. At Gepard, thirty years of being the person she knew best. "Fine," she said. "But when it's right—" "I'll tell you," he said. "And Bronya," she said. "She'll be there," he said. She looked at him one more time. Then she reached up and — quickly, with the specific Serval decisiveness that made things happen before anyone had time to organize a response — she put her arms around him. He froze for half a second. Then he held her back. The gold ice ambient in his hands and his sister in his arms in the Landau house entrance and the three weeks and the express train and thirty years of her being the one who ran frequency analysis on everything. She stepped back. "The data is extraordinary," she said. "Whatever it is." "I know," he said. "Don't say I know," she said. He made a sound. The involuntary version — the almost-laugh that he shared with Bronya and that Lynx would have recognized immediately. Serval heard it. She pointed at him. "That's new," she said. "What is?" he said. "That sound," she said. "You've been making it for about a year and you do it specifically when something is correct and you're glad it's correct." She studied him. "Bronya makes the same sound." "People who spend time together develop similar expressions," he said. "That's not what that is," Serval said. She picked up the equipment case. "Good night, Gepard." "Good night," he said. She went out. He stayed at the entrance for a moment. He looked at the outer machine district. He thought: Serval catalogues everything. She is going to be very systematic about this. He thought: good. Someone should be. —— Wednesday morning, Bronya was at her desk at 0600 when Pela knocked. This was standard — Pela's first knock of the day was usually between 0600 and 0615, with the monitoring data and the overnight reports and the construction updates. Bronya had been at the desk since 0545 with the equalization framework's second implementation report, which covered the twelve days of active deployment and which she was reading with the specific attention she paid to things that mattered. "Come in," she said. Pela came in. She had the tablet and the specific expression she wore when the monitoring data had something in it that required Bronya's attention but was not an emergency. Bronya had learned to read the gradations of Pela's expression within the first month of the Guardianship and the current gradation said: significant but not urgent. "The second extraction cluster survey," Pela said. "The geological team completed the mapping overnight. The report is in." She put the tablet on the desk. "The second cluster is accessible from a shaft angle that is eleven degrees less vertical than the primary shaft. The extraction timeline from the second cluster is consistent with the original model — eighteen months to forty percent of Belobog's energy requirement at full operational rate." Bronya read the survey summary. "The bedrock composition between the two clusters," she said. "Is it consistent?" "Slightly higher mineral density in the connector zone," Pela said. "Svarog says the higher density is beneficial for the notation channel conductance in that region — the channels will run more effectively there once the extraction operations are established." She paused. "He also says the connector zone's mineral composition suggests there may be a third cluster beneath the second, at approximately two hundred meters of additional depth. He's marked it as a future survey item." "Not this phase," Bronya said. "Not this phase," Pela agreed. Bronya looked at the survey map on the tablet. At the shaft angles and the cluster positions and the connector zone and the depth markers. At the geological structure of the planet she was responsible for. "The Great Glacial Aegir buffer," she said. "Confirmed at three hundred and twelve meters," Pela said. "The nearest extraction point is three hundred and twelve meters from the cave's primary structure. Svarog reviewed the buffer last night after the survey came in. He says the buffer is adequate and he is comfortable with the extraction proceeding." "If Svarog is comfortable," Bronya said. "He was also specific," Pela said, with the slight quality she used when she was reporting something that had a characteristic quality. "He said: 'The cave's geological integrity is the boundary. The extraction proceeds within those boundaries. The cave is not a resource — it is a record.'" Bronya looked at the tablet. At the cave's position on the map — the marked buffer zone, the golden form resting in the frozen space below, the Architects' ruins at the far end. Three hundred and twelve meters from the nearest drill point. "A record," Bronya said. "That's what he said," Pela said. Bronya looked at the map for a moment longer. Then she signed the survey approval. She slid the tablet back to Pela. "Construction update," she said. "Shaft one is at seventeen meters this morning," Pela said. "Two days ahead of schedule. The crew is requesting authorization for a third shift if the pace holds — they want to maintain the schedule advantage." "Authorize it," Bronya said. "Overtime rate plus the standard construction safety supplement." "Already drafted," Pela said. "I needed your authorization to send." "Send it," Bronya said. Pela sent it. She looked at Bronya. At the equalization framework report that Bronya had been reading when she came in. "The second implementation report," she said. "I'm reading it," Bronya said. "The eastern residential zone heating connection is three days ahead of schedule," Pela said. "The crew wants to know if they can redirect the freed time to the northern section's infrastructure groundwork." "Yes," Bronya said. "Redirect it." "Before or after you read the report?" Pela said. Bronya looked at her. "I can authorize and read simultaneously," she said. "I know," Pela said. "I ask because the decision requires the safety supplement authorization for the redirected crew, and that authorization is on page twelve of the report." Bronya turned to page twelve. She read the relevant section. She signed the authorization. "Send it." "Sent," Pela said. "Bronya." "Yes," Bronya said. "The eastern residential zone heating connection," Pela said. "Three days ahead of schedule. Which means—" "Mira gets heat in three months and twenty-seven days instead of four months," Bronya said. Pela was quiet for one second. "Yes," she said. "Good," Bronya said. She went back to the report. Pela stood at the desk for a moment. At Bronya reading the implementation report and knowing which nine-year-old was waiting for the heat and knowing the exact revised timeline. She said: "I want to be there when the connection is complete." Bronya looked up. "Which connection?" "The eastern residential zone," Pela said. "When the heating comes on. I want to be there." Bronya looked at her. At the sixteen-year-old who had been carrying the monitoring data and the reports and the analytical weight of the Guardianship for two years and who wanted to be in the eastern residential zone when the heat came on. "Yes," Bronya said. "We'll go together." Pela nodded once. She did not say anything else. She left. —— Thursday afternoon, Serval was at the workshop with Svarog when she found the thing she had been looking for. She had been at the secondary bench since 0900, running the channel frequency analysis with Svarog's monitoring data and the pre-battle baseline recordings and the three weeks of post-battle frequency data that she had brought from her university lab. The combined frequency — the golden ice's propagation through the notation channels plus the claimed frequency conducting at 3.1 — had been the focus. She had been building toward the mechanism question: not what was causing the channel output increase, but how. The specific pathway by which two new frequencies interacting with a pre-existing channel network produced a nine percent output increase and still climbing. At 1437, she found the pathway. She stared at it on the display for ninety seconds before she said anything. "Svarog," she said. He came to the secondary bench. He looked at the display. He was quiet for twelve seconds. This was an unusual length of quiet for Svarog, who was not generally prone to extended processing pauses. "Yes," he said. "The channels," she said. "They're not just conducting the new frequencies. They're — the frequencies are activating latent channel capacity. Capacity that was in the network but not being used because there was nothing to use it." She paused.
