"The golden ice conducts at a frequency the channels were built to amplify. The claimed frequency conducts at a frequency the channels were built to complement. Together, the two new frequencies are unlocking channel capacity that has been sitting dormant since — since whenever the network was built." Svarog looked at the display. "The channels have been operating below their designed capacity," she said. "For however long the network has existed. And the combined frequency is bringing them to designed capacity." She paused. "The nine percent increase isn't abnormal. It's the network functioning as it was built to function." She looked at Svarog. "It was never at nine percent below capacity because of a flaw. It was below capacity because it didn't have the right frequencies to run on." Svarog was quiet for a moment. "Yes," he said. "You knew," she said. "I suspected," he said. "I have been monitoring this network for four hundred years. I noticed the latent capacity indicators in the secondary channel data approximately one hundred and fifty years ago. I did not have an explanation for them. I noted them and continued monitoring." Serval looked at him. "One hundred and fifty years ago you noticed that the network was running below its designed capacity and you—" "Noted it and continued monitoring," he said. "There was nothing to be done about it. The network required specific inputs. I did not know what those inputs were." "And now you do," she said. "Now we do," he said. "Because of what Bronya and Gepard produced at the battle." He paused. "The network was built for them. Not for them specifically — for inputs that would come from this planet's specific history. The inputs arrived. The network is responding." Serval looked at the display. At the latent capacity indicators and the activation pathway and the combined frequency running through every channel junction in the outer machine district. "This is what you brought me in to help explain," she said. "Yes," he said. "I could not explain it without the frequency analysis. You could not explain it without the channel monitoring data." He paused. "Together, we have the explanation." "And Bronya and Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "They should know." "I'd like to be there," Serval said, "when you tell them." "I know," Svarog said. "I expected you to say so." She looked at the display. At the channel network, nine percent more itself than it had been, still climbing, the latent capacity activating in stages as the combined frequency ran deeper through the geological structure. "Svarog," she said. "Yes," he said. "The network was built for specific inputs from this planet's history," she said. "And the specific inputs turned out to be what a Guardian does when the Stellaron tries to replace her, and what a person does when they've been fighting for their city for long enough that something foundational notices." She paused. "That's what built the right frequencies." "Yes," he said. "We found that out," Serval said. "Without the channels telling us. Without any prior prediction." She paused. "We just — looked at the data and found it." "Yes," Svarog said. "That is how the best findings arrive." Serval looked at the data for one more moment. Then she turned from the secondary bench and started writing up the findings report. Not for the palace — for the record. For the specific record of: Thursday, 1437, the outer machine district workshop, two people looking at the data together and finding what was there. —— Thursday evening, Seele and Luka came back from the circuit at 1900. They had been there since 1600 — the sparring session, then the individual training, then the post-session assessment routing that had now become their standard Thursday structure. Seele had gone to the circuit medic for the wrist. The assessment was minor. She had gone to Natasha afterward for the standard follow-up that Luka had decided was the optimal sequence, which Natasha had accepted as the reasonable protocol it was while also noting that Luka was running his own secondary assessment of the wrist's recovery and that she would like him to communicate that assessment verbally rather than just acting on it. He had said: the lateral stress is on the distal joint. Recovery at three days. She had said: that's what I said. On the way home from Natasha's ward, Seele said: "You gave her your assessment first." "I gave her the data," he said. "You told her the lateral stress location and the timeline before she'd finished her own assessment," Seele said. "I had already run the assessment," he said. "Sharing redundant data is inefficient." "She didn't find it redundant," Seele said. "No," he said. "She found it presumptuous." "A little," Seele said. "I'm going to stop doing it," he said. "You're not," she said. He thought about this. "No," he said. "Probably not." She made the sound. The real one. They came into the residential stack and up to the third floor and Seele put the key in the door and they came into the room that was theirs — had been theirs in the sense of hers for months and was now theirs in the sense of both of them, the shift having happened gradually enough that neither of them had made a ceremony of it. The lamp was on the shelf where it always was. The scythe went on the hook by the door, the new one, the dark-metal geometric blade in its carry configuration. The weapon went on the hook beside it — the compact form, all three modes collapsed into the carry handle. She went to change out of the circuit clothes. He went to the kitchen — they had developed a post-session routine where she changed and he made tea, not because it had been discussed but because it was the natural distribution of the parallel tasks and they had been running parallel tasks long enough to know which ones to pick up without conferring. She came back in soft clothes, the circuit clothes in the laundry pile. She looked at him in the kitchen — the chrome arm at the standard hang at his side, the sandevistan integration at the back of his neck visible above his collar, the ordinary domestic activity of making tea after a sparring session. She sat on the low seat by the window. The outer machine district outside — the junction lights, the generator stacks, the notation channels conducting their warmth through the stone. She looked at the city for a while and didn't say anything. He brought the tea. He sat beside her. They sat in the lamp-lit room with the tea and the city outside and the notation warmth in the stone beneath them, and they did what they did at this hour, which was nothing specific and everything important. The comfort of two people who had been through the impossible together and were now in the ordinary on the other side of it. "The wrist," he said. "It's fine," she said. "I know," he said. "I'm noting it anyway." She looked at him. "Verbalization exercise?" "Yes," he said. "The wrist is at two-day recovery rather than three. Natasha's ice pack is helping." "My left shoulder from the ceiling approach," she said. "Minor. Not worth Natasha." "Agreed," he said. "Your right elbow," she said. "The impact from the third exchange." "Three hours to full range of motion recovery," he said. "Already improved." She looked at him. "Are we going to do this every Thursday?" "The verbalization helps," he said. "I know," she said. "That wasn't a complaint." He looked at her. At the lamp light on the left side of her face and the city behind her through the window and the butterfly quality in her skin, present in the low warmth of the room the way it was always present. He had been looking at her face in lamp light for a year and he still found the specific quality of it — the unmanaged version, the version that existed when she wasn't in an operational context and didn't need any of the catalogued expressions — something worth looking at carefully. She caught him looking. "What?" she said. "Nothing," he said. "You're doing the specific version of looking at me that means something," she said. "I'm not doing a version," he said. "I'm looking at you." She looked at him. "You know I can tell the difference." "I know," he said. "So what is it?" she said. He thought about the right version. "I was thinking," he said, "that the lamp light looks the same as it did the first time you were in this room. When you showed me the moon chapter." He paused. "And you look completely different from that night and exactly the same." She was quiet for a moment. "Completely different how?" "The butterfly quality," he said. "The way you hold yourself since the cave. The way you're not managing anything when you're in this room." He paused. "You were managing something every second of the first time you were here. Not performing — managing. There's a difference." "There is," she said. "Now you're not," he said. "You're just here." She looked at him. "I'm comfortable," she said. Simply. The accurate word. "Yes," he said. "With you," she said. "Specifically." "I know," he said. "Don't say I know," she said. But it was warm when she said it — the reflex without the edge. "I'm aware of it," he said. She made the sound. She leaned her head against his shoulder — not the deliberate lean, the comfortable one, the one that happened because it was where her head went. He put his arm around her and she settled into it and they looked at the city through the window. "The sparring Thursday," she said. "The floor-contact leverage." "Yes?" he said. "I want to develop it into the operational range," she said. "Not just the circuit floor. I want to know if it transfers to tunnel geometry." "Corridor nine-beta?" he said. "Or the sub-level approach route," she said. "Somewhere with lower ceiling and harder floor." She paused. "Will you run the geometry work with me?" "Yes," he said. "Saturday?" she said. "Saturday," he agreed. They sat with the tea going cold and the city outside and the notation warmth in the floor below them. The moon was twelve years away and the heat was coming for Mira in three months and twenty-seven days and the surface was six weeks away and the tunnel work was Saturday. "Luka," she said. "Yes," he said. "The circuit today," she said. "The moment you adjusted to the floor-contact leverage in the second repetition." She paused. "I want you to know that I noticed. The speed of it. It's — I'm saying that it's one of the things about you that I find specifically worth noticing." He was quiet for a moment. The lamp light. The city. Her head on his shoulder. "You adjusted to my wall-angle arc on the fourth repetition," he said. "That's a new response pattern for sub-level geometry." She looked up at him. "Is that your version of saying thank you?" "That's my version of saying it back," he said. She held his gaze for a moment. Then she turned back to the window. "Okay," she said. "Okay," he agreed. The lamp stayed on. The city stayed warm. They sat. —— Friday morning, Sampo was in the northwest corridor at 0715. He ran this route four mornings a week — the specific route he had been running since he was eleven years old, the route he had run on the morning of the dark winter, the route that was his relationship to this district in its most direct form. He ran it because it was his and because the district changed around it and because the running was a way of knowing what was changing. What was changing: the third junction on the northwest route, which for twenty years had been a dead end because of a structural support that cut the corridor at head height, was now open. The structural support had been removed as part of the Phase One infrastructure work — the engineering team had reinforced the adjacent wall, the support was no longer necessary, and the dead end had become a through-route to the residential section behind it. He ran through it for the first time on Monday. He had stopped at the end of the dead end that was now a through-route and had stood in it for approximately three minutes. The corridor was the same stone it had always been. The light was different — the new section had ceiling grating that let in the generator output at a different angle. The sound was different — residential, rather than the maintenance section ambient he was used to. He had thought: twenty years I have been stopping here. He had thought: I know this whole district except this. He had run through. This morning he ran through again, and this time a woman was coming from the residential side. She was carrying grocery bags and she was clearly not expecting the corridor to connect, because she stopped when she saw him and then she recalibrated and came through. "Morning," she said. "Morning," he said. "This is new," she said, meaning the through-route. "It is," he said. "They took out the structural support on Monday." She looked at the corridor. "I've been living in this residential section for twelve years," she said. "I always thought the dead end meant this was just maintenance space." "It was," he said. "Now it's a through-route," she said. "Now it's a through-route," he agreed. She went through with her grocery bags. He watched her go. He stood in the corridor for a moment. He sent a message to Natasha: *there's a through-route at the third northwest junction now. I ran it this morning. you should walk it sometime. It comes out by the new market entrance on the residential side.* She sent back four minutes later: *I know — one of my patients mentioned it Tuesday. I went Thursday. The new market has a very good dark filling pastry selection.* He stopped running for a moment. He looked at his phone. *You found the pastries*, he sent. *Of course I found them*, she sent. *I was looking. You mentioned them on Tuesday.* He stood in the third northwest junction's new through-route and looked at his phone. At Natasha, who had walked the new route on Thursday specifically because he had mentioned it and who had located the pastries in the market because she had been looking. *I'll get them Saturday*, he sent. *When is your morning free?* *Saturday I have assessments until 1100*, she sent. *1115*, he sent. *1115*, she sent back. He kept running. The corridor was warm — the notation channels running their warmth through the stone, the morning pulse of the outer machine district's generators cycling up. The district doing its morning the way it always did, and slightly different from how it had been doing it yesterday, and further different from how it would do it in three months. He thought about Sparkle. He had been thinking about Sparkle with increasing frequency over the past week — not with urgency, but with the specific quality of thinking about something that was going to become relevant and that he was getting ready for. She would come back. She had said she would. The conversation about the path of Elation and what came after Belobog and what the shape of his life was going to be was going to happen, and he was going to need to have figured some of it out before it did. He thought about Natasha at 1115 on Saturday with the dark filling pastries from the new market. He thought about Vera, who had said he would be extraordinarily happy and who had been right, in the ways that mattered, even if the path there was not the path anyone would have designed. He ran the northwest route to its end and turned back. —— Friday afternoon, Bronya was in the Restricted Zone. Not the northern section — not at the contact point. The Restricted Zone's outer perimeter, where the monitoring stations were, where Gepard ran the patrol rotation and where Pela's instruments took the regular signal readings. The contact point was a place she went with purpose. The perimeter was a place she went when she wanted to think. The signal at the perimeter monitoring station was running at its new standard — the claimed frequency and the golden ice propagation both present in the readings, the combined frequency at a stable resonance that Serval had been calling the operational harmonic. No escalation. No unusual behavior. Belobog's geological foundation running the frequencies that Bronya and Gepard had introduced and running them with the specific smoothness of a system doing what it was built to do. She thought about what Serval had found Thursday. The latent capacity in the channels. The combined frequency activating what had always been there. She thought: they found that out themselves. From the data. Without any prior framework telling them what to look for. She thought: that's right. That's the correct way to find things. She thought about the twenty-second notation section and Clara's description of what it said. The center. The receiving and processing and conducting outward. She had been carrying that description for a week and looking at it from different angles and the angle that kept being the most useful was Seele's angle — not what the Architects intended, but what she was already doing and what her position on this planet was specifically suited for. Not a prediction. A description of something that was true. The description arrived after the fact. She thought: we find things out when we find them out. The planet is not a script. She thought about the nine-year-old Mira, waiting for heat. The man at the first junction, whose daughter had homework. The woman whose mother had a Thursday appointment. She thought: this is what the planet is. Not the notation sections. Not the channel frequencies. These specific people in these specific corridors. She thought about Gepard — about the outer wall and the nine years and the clock and is it effective. About the thing she had said to Seele: we're very thorough. She thought: I have been managing the thing between us for a very long time. I have been calling it professionalism and then I have been calling it timing and then I have been calling it not yet. She thought: I am done calling it not yet. She went to find him. —— He was on the outer wall. He was always on the outer wall at this hour. She came up the access steps and he heard her coming and he turned. She looked at him. At the gold ice ambient in the outer wall's cold air, the luminous deep quality of it present in his expression even at rest. At the patrol folder under his arm. At Gepard, twenty-five years old, standing on the outer wall at the end of a Friday. "Come with me," she said. He looked at her. "Where?" "The Landau house," she said. "It's Friday. Lynx has food. We're going to eat at the house and then I want to sit in the garden." "The garden is—" He paused. "The garden is frozen." "Yes," she said. "I know. I want to sit in it anyway." He looked at her for one more second. Something in her expression was different from the standard — not alarming, not urgent. The specific quality of someone who has decided something and is moving forward. "All right," he said. "Let me sign off the patrol report." "I'll wait," she said. He signed off the patrol report. He gave it to the duty officer. He came back to her. They walked to the Landau house together, which they had done before, which was not new. The outer machine district running its Friday evening — the junction lights coming on, the generators at standard output, the notification channels warm in the stone below the junction streets. Bronya walked beside him with the specific quality of someone who was not managing anything. He noticed. He did not say anything. He walked beside her and let the not-managing be what it was. —— Lynx had food. She also had Pela for approximately forty minutes, who had come to drop off a report and had been given dinner and had eaten it with the specific efficiency of someone who is genuinely hungry and is not going to argue with the food in front of her. Pela left at 2000 with the report unsigned because Bronya had said she would read it tomorrow and Pela had looked at Bronya's expression and decided that tonight was not a read-the-report night and had gone without pushing it. Then it was Lynx and Bronya and Gepard. Lynx ate with them, talked about the medical school application process she was working through with one of her junior colleagues, mentioned that Dunn had done the dishes on Tuesday which she was noting as a positive contribution to the household even though he was not technically a member of it, and then at 2045 said: "I'm going to study," and went to her room, which was the specific Lynx version of I am giving you both the house. The house was quiet. Bronya looked at Gepard. "The garden," she said. "It's cold," he said. "Yes," she said. They got their coats. They went to the Landau house's back garden, which was the specific feature of the house that Bronya had always found interesting — a surface-district house with an actual garden, the pre-Separation stone walls running the perimeter, the generator duct running along the north wall that kept the garden slightly warmer than the open outer district. Snow over everything. The bench at the center. They sat on the bench. The cold was the Belobog cold. The dome above them. The junction lights visible at the garden's edges. The generator duct producing its low hum. Snow on the garden path and the garden walls and the stone bench they were sitting on, which Bronya had apparently decided to sit on regardless. "It is very cold," Gepard said. "Yes," she said. "You wanted to sit in the frozen garden specifically," he said. "Yes," she said. He looked at her. At the mark just visible above the coat's collar. At the green coat in the snow. At Bronya, sitting in the frozen Landau house garden on a Friday evening. "Bronya," he said. "Yes," she said. "What did you decide?" he said. She looked at him. "What makes you think I decided something?" "You have been not managing anything since the outer wall," he said. "For three years I have watched you manage everything. Tonight you are not managing anything." He paused. "You decided something." She looked at the garden. At the snow. At the dome above the garden walls. At the cold light of Belobog's standard evening. "I have been," she said slowly, "saying not yet to something for a very long time." "Yes," he said. "Not because the something was wrong," she said. "Because the timing—" She stopped. She started again. "Because I needed things to be resolved first. The Stellarons. The equalization. The expansion framework. Things that needed to be done before I could—" She stopped. "Before I could give the other thing its full space." "The other thing," he said. She looked at him. "You," she said. "This. What we are." She paused. "I have been managing it into the timing that I thought it deserved. And tonight I realized—" She paused. "The timing was never going to be complete. There was always going to be another thing. There is a city to run. There will always be a city to run." She paused. "The only timing that exists is the one I decide is right." He was very still. "And I'm deciding," she said. "That right is now." He looked at her. At the snow. At the frozen garden. At Bronya, twenty-six years old, on the Landau house bench in the cold, looking at him with the expression she used when she was not managing anything. He said: "Bronya." "Yes," she said. "I have been waiting," he said. "For seventeen months since the stairwell and the hand on the jaw and the cabinet and all of it. I have been waiting for you to get to now." He paused. "I would have waited as long as it took." "I know," she said. Warmly. "Don't say—" He stopped. He looked at her expression. "I know," she said again. Gently. The real version — the one that meant she knew the full scope of it and was naming the scope, not deflecting it. He breathed once. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "I love you," she said. Not the outer wall version — the plain fact on the patrol report. This one was the other version. The version that had been accumulating for the full two years of the Guardianship and the year before that and the four years before that and all the way back to seventeen years old and the winter assessment and the six minutes she had stayed. The version that had the full weight of everything it was. "I love you," he said. In the same register. The full weight. "I have been saying it in my head for a very long time and I will never stop saying it." She put her hand on his face. The specific way that was hers. He covered it with his. The gold ice ambient in his skin and the claimed frequency warmth in her palm and the frozen garden and the cold. He kissed her. On the Landau house bench, in the snow, with the dome above and the city below and the generator duct humming on the north wall. She kissed him back the way you kissed someone when the not yet was over. The garden was cold and they did not move from the bench for a very long time. Inside the house, Lynx put down her study materials and sat with the quiet that was the house giving two people the space they had needed, and she was glad. —— At 2230 on Friday night, in the residential stack's third floor, the lamp was on. Seele was on the low seat with a book — not the moon chapter, something new, something she had picked up at the junction market on Thursday and had been reading in the evenings. Luka was on the floor. Not push-ups — sitting, his back against the seat, the chrome arm resting on his knee, the notation channels warm in the floor below. She had been reading for forty minutes. He had been sitting for forty minutes. They had been doing this the way they did things — together without it requiring management. She turned a page. He looked at the window. At the outer machine district outside. At the notation channels conducting their warmth through every junction in the city. He thought about the Nihility. About the cut at the circuit on Sunday that he had noticed too late, the urgency register lower than it should have been. He thought about it without alarm and without performance — just the clear-eyed accounting that the verbalization mechanism was for. He said: "I want to tell you something." She lowered the book. She looked at him. Not alarmed — just present, her full attention arriving immediately. "The cut at the circuit on Sunday," he said. "I noticed it too late." "I know," she said. "Not just late," he said. "Late because the urgency register is lower." He paused. "When I assessed the cut, there was a moment where I processed it and the response was — lower volume than it should have been. The same data, but quieter. Like something that is happening at a lower priority." She was looking at him carefully. "That's the Nihility," he said. "Natasha and I talked about it. The growing indifference to harm to myself. It's been running for months. I think it's accelerating slightly." He paused. "I'm not in danger from it. I don't think it's going to become dangerous. But I want you to know that it's there and it's moving and the verbalization is — the verbalization is me staying honest about it rather than pretending it's not what it is." She was quiet for a moment. "Are you afraid of it?" she said. He thought about this. "No," he said. "I'm aware of it." "There's a difference," she said. "Yes," he said. "The path is mine. I'm not afraid of what I am." He paused. "I'm telling you because you're the external system and because I said I would tell you." "Yes," she said. "And because I want to keep telling you," he said. "Not just when it's significant. The ongoing state of it, too. Even when it's not alarming." She looked at him. At the chrome arm and the spinal integration and Luka, twenty-three years old, telling her the ongoing state of something that was specifically his in the specific register of someone who was not afraid of what he was but was choosing to stay honest about it. "Okay," she said. "Okay?" he said. "I'll be here for the ongoing state of it," she said. "Same as always." He looked at her. "Good," he said. She went back to her book. He went back to looking at the window. After a few minutes she said, without looking up from the page: "The new scythe's blade balance is slightly different from the old one." "Left?" he said. "Three degrees," she said. "I adjusted the grip position Saturday. It's better now but I'm still learning where to compensate." "The roof-approach arc from the tunnel entries," he said. "The three-degree difference matters there more than in the corridor geometry." "I know," she said. "I'm working on it." "Saturday," he said. "We can test the roof-approach geometry in corridor nine-beta." "Saturday," she agreed. She turned a page. "Your new weapon's rifle form," she said. "The sight calibration." "It's off by two degrees right," he said. "I adjusted it Thursday. I need one more range session to confirm the correction." "Add it to Saturday," she said. "After the corridor work," he said. "After," she agreed. They were quiet for a while. The lamp light. The book. The notation channels warm in the floor. The outer machine district outside, doing its Friday night. She said, eventually, still reading: "Luka." "Yes," he said. "The moon," she said. "Yes," he said. "Six weeks," she said. "Six weeks to the transit route," he said. "And then we go early. Before the group." She lowered the book. She looked at him over it. "Before the group," she said. "Yes," he said. "Just us," she said. "Just us," he said. She looked at him for a moment. At the chrome arm in the lamp light. At Luka, who had been counting and waiting since he was nine years old and who had promised her the moon on a specific night with full intent and who was now telling her: six weeks. "Okay," she said. She went back to her book. He went back to the window. Six weeks. —— END OF CHAPTER 63 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 62: The Ground We Stand On Chapter Text Clara knocked on the palace door at 0817. Not 0900. The message she sent Bronya at 0800 said: *I know I said before 0900, but I'm at the palace now if you're available. The twenty-second section is — I'd rather tell you sooner.* Bronya was available. She had been at her desk since 0600, which was her standard, and she had read Clara's message from the previous night about the twenty-second section going faster than expected, and she had been at her desk with the anticipatory quality of someone who is waiting for information to arrive and is prepared for it to be significant. She came to the door herself. Clara was there in the outer corridor with Toma — just Toma, which told Bronya something about the quality of what Clara was carrying, because Clara brought all three Moles to large things and only one to things she needed to process alongside someone she trusted with the processing. "Come in," Bronya said. "Both of you." They came in. They went to the study — not the formal briefing room, the study. Clara sat in the chair. Toma sat on the low bench by the window, in the position that was not the primary seat in the conversation but was not outside the conversation either. Bronya sat across from Clara. "Tell me," she said. Clara told her. The twenty-second section described the phase that came after the anchor was established. After the Stellarons were resolved, after the mining operations were running, after the expansion was underway. It described what Jarilo-VI's role in the broader network was meant to become — not as a resource, not as a military or political asset, but as a specific kind of center. A point where things could be received and processed and sent outward in a form that was useful. The Architects' notation described this with the geological metaphor they used for everything: the way that a geological formation received the pressure of the planet's forces, processed them through its specific mineral structure, and conducted the result outward through the channels it had been building across centuries. Jarilo-VI was meant to be that, at a larger scale. For what, specifically, the twenty-second section described in terms that Clara was still translating — the notation was in the most complex structural category she had encountered so far, requiring the full complement of what she had learned across nine months to read at all. But the shape of it was legible. The direction. Bronya listened to the shape and the direction. She looked at the window. At the palace. At the outer city visible through it. She said, after a long silence: "How long has Svarog known the shape of this?" "He said the second century of his monitoring," Clara said. "He knew the direction the twentieth section was pointing. He didn't have the content." "But he came here knowing," Bronya said. "Knowing that whatever this planet was going to be, it was going to be — larger than a city holding a dome against the cold." "Yes," Clara said. "I think he came here knowing that the planet had something specific to become and that the becoming required someone to watch over it until it was ready." Bronya looked at the window for a long moment. "He waited four hundred years," she said. "He's patient," Clara said, with the specific dry quality she had developed for observations that were both entirely accurate and somewhat remarkable. Bronya made the sound. Not quite the almost-laugh — something adjacent, the sound of a situation that was too large for a standard response and too real to be dismissed. She looked at Clara. "The twenty-third section," she said. "I'm going to read it today," Clara said. "Tell me when you've finished it," Bronya said. "Whatever time." "Whatever time," Clara confirmed. Toma, from the low bench by the window, said: "Bronya." Bronya looked at her. At the twelve-year-old who had been keeping notebooks and writing things down and arriving alongside Clara at significant moments since the first corridor entry. "Yes," Bronya said. "Clara didn't sleep much last night," Toma said. Not an accusation. An accurate report. "I know," Bronya said. She looked at Clara. "You're going to sleep before you read the twenty-third section." "I'm fine," Clara said. "You read until midnight and started again at seven," Bronya said. "You're going to sleep first. Svarog's orders or mine, take your pick." Clara looked at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-VI presenting this as an either/or between herself and Svarog. The corner of Clara's mouth did the thing. "Svarog's," she said. "His are easier to argue with." "Go to the workshop and sleep," Bronya said. "The twenty-third section will still be on the wall." "It will," Clara agreed. She got up. She paused at the study door. "Bronya." "Yes," Bronya said. "The twenty-second section," Clara said. "What it describes — the center, the receiving and processing and conducting outward." She paused. "I think you're part of it. Specifically." She looked at the mark visible above the neckline of Bronya's coat. "The claimed frequency. What you're carrying. I think the Architects planned for that too — not you specifically, but someone like you, at this specific moment." Bronya looked at the mark. At the angular thorn-point dark symbol on her sternum, visible above the neckline. "Go sleep," she said. Warmly. "I'll think about it." "You're going to think about it all day," Toma said, standing from the window bench. "Probably," Bronya said. They left. —— She did think about it all day. She thought about it in the morning briefings, where the construction update from the Snezhnaya team reported that the access road groundwork had begun and was proceeding at two days ahead of schedule, and where Dunn's Underworld equalization deployment report confirmed that the northeast corridor junction repair was complete and the residential zone access had been restored and the population was using it. She thought about it in the afternoon policy review, where she signed the formal equalization framework document that established the Underworld's access rights in explicit legal language that had not previously existed in Belobog's civic record — not an announcement, not a policy position, a legal framework, the kind that remained after administrations changed and could not be informally reversed because it had the force of statutory law. She was proud of that document. It had taken three weeks of drafting and two weeks of review and it was forty-seven pages of precise legal language that Pela had reviewed and corrected and that Svarog had reviewed and confirmed was consistent with the geological and operational realities of the Underworld's infrastructure. She signed it. She sent it to the public record. She thought about the twenty-second section and what it meant to be a center where things were received and processed and conducted outward. At 1600 she went to the outer machine district. Not to the workshop specifically. To the Underworld. —— The outer machine district's Underworld access had, in the thirty-two days since the announcement, been changing in ways that were visible and ways that were not. The visible changes: the medical station expansion, the new teachers, the food supply, the construction crews working on the infrastructure projects that the equalization policy had unlocked. The not-visible changes: the specific quality of the people moving through the junction corridors. The way they moved. The specific difference between the movement of people who are surviving and the movement of people who believe the surviving is going to continue. Bronya had been to the Underworld twice since the announcement — once with Gepard for an operational review, once with Natasha to walk the medical station expansion. She had not come alone. She came alone today. She came down through the outer machine district's standard Underworld access at 1620, through the junction corridor that connected the surface district to the upper Underworld levels, and she walked. Not with a purpose — to see. To see what the thirty-two days of the equalization work had produced in the place where it was being produced. At the first junction she stopped. An older man was at the junction's secondary corridor entrance, looking at the notation carving above it. Not the Architects' notation — the standard Belobog notation, the system that had been in use for centuries, marking the corridor's function. He was looking at it with the specific expression of someone who had been passing this junction for twenty years and was looking at it differently now. Natasha had told her that a portion of the equalization work included junction notation updates — the secondary corridors that had been marked as service-only access points were being reclassified as general access, the notation changed to reflect the change in policy. He saw her. He recognized her. She could see the recognition land in his face — not the formal recognition of the Guardian, the personal one, the recognition of someone who had watched an assembly in the main junction and had heard their city's leader read out names and acknowledge their existence. He said: "Supreme Guardian." "Just Bronya," she said. He looked at her. At the mark visible above the neckline. At the coat. At the woman who had come down into the Underworld alone without an escort. "My daughter started at the new school on Monday," he said. "The surface curriculum. The teachers—" He stopped. He was not a person who had a great deal of practice speaking to the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-VI directly. He found the word. "She came home Tuesday and she had homework. Real homework, the kind the surface schools give." He paused. "She's never had homework before." Bronya looked at him. "Is she doing all right with it?" she said. "She's struggling," he said. "The gap is significant — the surface curriculum is ahead of where the Underworld teachers had her." He paused. "She cried a bit, Tuesday evening." He paused. "And then she sat back down and did the homework." He looked at the notation above the junction. "She said she was going to catch up. That the gap was only a problem if you stopped working on it." Bronya was quiet for a moment. "She's right," she said. "Yes," he said. He looked at her. "We've been here," he said. "My family. Three generations. My grandparents came down when the generators were still being built. They stayed because the work was here and the Underworld was where the work was." He paused. "In three generations, nobody from my family has been above the access point." He looked at the reclassified notation above the junction. "My daughter might be the first one." Bronya said: "If she wants to come up, she's welcome. If she wants to stay and build what's here, she's welcome to that too. What she shouldn't have to do is stay because there's no other option." He looked at her. "That's—" He paused. "That's what it is, isn't it. That's the difference." "Yes," she said. "That's the difference." He nodded. He looked at the junction notation. He looked back at Bronya. "Thank you," he said. Not formally — directly. The way people said things to other people when the gratitude was personal rather than ceremonial. She held his gaze. "Thank you for telling me about your daughter," she said. "Tell her the gap is only a problem if you stop working on it. That's the right thing." He smiled — a quick thing, real. He went back the way he had come. She stood at the junction for a moment after he left. She looked at the reclassified notation. At the corridor beyond it. At the Underworld below her, doing what it was doing on a Thursday afternoon when things were changing. She kept walking. In the western residential corridor, three women were sitting at the junction crossway with the specific quality of people who had made this junction their afternoon social point for a long time. They watched her walk through with the same calibrated attention the older man at the first junction had used. One of them — a woman in her sixties with the sharp eyes of someone who had been reading people in the Underworld for a long time — said: "You're the one who's doing the insulation." "Working on it," Bronya said. She stopped. "Phase Two infrastructure, six months." "My building is the one on the north end," the woman said. "The one that faces the rock face on the cold side." "I know the building," Bronya said. She did — it was in the Phase Two priority list, the cold-facing residential block that was structurally the most impacted by the Underworld's temperature differential. "It's one of the first in the Phase Two schedule." The woman looked at her. "You know the specific building," she said. "Yes," Bronya said. "You read the lists," the woman said. Not a question. "All of them," Bronya said. "The equalization framework, the infrastructure schedules, the medical station expansion plans. The individual building assessments." She paused. "I know the names of every junction in the Phase One and Phase Two infrastructure zones." The woman looked at her for a long moment. The other two women were quiet. "My husband used to say," the woman said, "that the surface people didn't know our junctions existed. That to them the Underworld was a power supply. A maintenance concern." She paused. "He died eight years ago. I'm glad he didn't have to — he would have been—" She stopped. She looked at the junction. "He would have been angry, at first. That it took this long. And then he would have been glad." "He would have been right to be angry," Bronya said. The woman looked at her. "Yes," she said. "He would have been." She paused. "But you're here now. And you know the junction names." She paused again. "That's something." "Yes," Bronya said. "It is." She stayed at the junction crossway for twenty minutes, talking with the three women about the insulation schedule and the medical station and the educational redistribution. The woman in her sixties had a granddaughter who had transferred to the new curriculum and was, she reported, doing the same thing as the man's daughter at the first junction: struggling, sitting back down, working at it. One of the other women had a son who had taken a job with the Snezhnaya construction crew, which meant he was going to be working on the surface, which meant he was going to be the first person in their family to spend regular time above the dome. The third woman was a retired Underworld generator technician who had opinions about the mining operation's infrastructure design and shared them in significant detail, and who, Bronya noted, was correct about approximately seventy percent of them. She made a note in her phone. She was going to send the generator technician's structural concerns to Svarog. When she left the junction crossway, the woman in her sixties said: "Come back." Not as a pleasantry. As a genuine request. "I will," Bronya said. "When the Phase Two construction starts, I want to see it." "Come before," the woman said. "Come see what it looks like before." She paused. "You should know what it looks like before." Bronya looked at her. At the specific Underworld residential corridor, at the cold-facing north wall visible at the end of it, at the rock face beyond. "Yes," she said. "I'll come before." —— The medical station expansion was at sixty percent. She knew this from Natasha's weekly reports. Seeing it was different from knowing it from reports in the way that seeing things was always different. The station's eastern wing was new construction — the framework was up, the walls were going in, the medical equipment was staged in the corridor outside waiting for the space to be ready to receive it. Three Wildfire medics were doing patient assessments in what would become the new intake area as soon as the walls were done, using temporary dividers and the focused efficiency of people who had been making do with what they had and were getting more and intending to use it well. Natasha's desk was visible through the temporary divider from the original station's doorway. She was not at her desk — she was doing a patient assessment in what would become the second exam room, visible through the open door, her back to the door and her full attention on the patient in front of her. Bronya did not interrupt. She looked at the station. At the staged equipment. At the three Wildfire medics doing intake with the temporary dividers. At the framework of the new wing. At what this space was becoming. A woman in her forties came out of the intake area and nearly walked into Bronya. She stopped. She looked at Bronya. She looked at the mark at the neckline. She said: "Oh." "Sorry," Bronya said. "I was in the way." The woman looked at her. "You came down here," she said. "Yes," Bronya said. "In person," the woman said. Not with suspicion — with the specific quality of someone calibrating a thing that was unexpected against a world where unexpected things had recently started happening. "I wanted to see how the expansion is going," Bronya said. The woman looked at the medical station. At the new wing framework. At the staged equipment. "It's going," she said. "I've been coming here for eight years. It was always — adequate. You know? It did what it could with what it had." She paused. "My mother comes twice a month. She has a chronic condition. Natasha manages it." She paused. "I asked Natasha last week how the expansion was changing the capacity." She looked at Bronya. "She said when the new wing is complete they'll be able to run twice as many concurrent assessments. She said waiting times for non-emergency appointments should drop by forty percent." "That's the projection, yes," Bronya said. "I looked up what the surface stations run," the woman said. "Waiting times, capacity, staff-to-patient ratios." She paused. "We're not going to match them. Not yet. But we're going to be closer than we've been in three generations." She looked at Bronya. "I didn't think I was going to see this. In my lifetime." She paused. "I'm forty-three years old and I didn't think I was going to see this." Bronya looked at her. At the forty-three-year-old woman who had been coming to this medical station for eight years and who had calibrated her expectations to what was available and had not expected the available to change. "I'm sorry it took this long," Bronya said. The woman looked at her. "Are you," she said. Not dismissively — the genuine question. "Yes," Bronya said. "I've been Guardian for two years and the equalization should have been the first thing I did. The Stellarons were — they were urgent, they required the response they required, and in the time those responses were happening the Underworld was what it had been." She paused. "I should have found a way to do both simultaneously. I didn't. I'm sorry." The woman was quiet for a moment. She looked at the framework of the new wing. "You're doing it now," she said. "Yes," Bronya said. "My mother's appointment is Thursday," the woman said. "She's going to be in the intake area that's in the corridor at the moment, with the temporary divider." She paused. "And in four months, she's going to be in the new wing." She looked at Bronya. "That's something." "Yes," Bronya said. "It is." The woman looked at the mark at the neckline. At the coat. At the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-VI standing in the Underworld medical station corridor on a Thursday afternoon. "Thank you," she said. Simply. The same way the man at the junction had said it. Directly. "Thank you for telling me," Bronya said. The same answer. The accurate one. —— She was in the Underworld for two hours. She walked the eastern residential zone, where the construction crews were working on the infrastructure upgrade that would connect the zone to the surface heating system — not the dome's ambient temperature management, the actual residential heating network that surface districts had been connected to for decades. The residents who were home in the afternoon watched her walk through without formal acknowledgment. Nobody pretended not to see her. Nobody was performing indifference. They simply existed alongside her presence, which was a specific quality of acceptance rather than formal recognition. A group of children playing in the junction square stopped when she passed. One of them — a girl, maybe nine years old, with the specific Underworld-child quality of someone who had grown up knowing the physical limits of the space she moved through — looked at Bronya and said: "Are you the one who's making the heat come?" "I'm working on it," Bronya said. "When is it coming?" the girl said. "About four months," Bronya said. The girl nodded. "Okay," she said. "We'll wait." She went back to the game. Bronya stood in the junction square for a moment after the girl went back to the game. She thought about four months and the girl playing in the square and what heating the eastern residential zone in four months meant for a nine-year-old whose experience of her city was bounded by the Underworld's temperature limits. She thought: this is the work. Not the notation sections, not the Snezhnaya survey, not the mining operations. This is the work — the nine-year-old who will have heat in four months. She thought about the Architects and the eighth-hundred-year plan and the anchor and the trajectory. She thought: the trajectory points here. Through this child. Through Anya and the food supply crate and the man at the junction and the woman at the medical station. Everything the Architects built pointed at this as a necessary step, and the step is real, and the child is real, and the heat is coming in four months. She went back to the surface at 1820. —— Gepard was on the outer wall when she got there. He turned when she came up the access steps — the same turn, the same recognition of her specific footfall, that had been there for two years of her coming up those steps. He looked at her. At the specific quality she was in after two hours in the Underworld. He did not ask what she was doing down there. He had known when she sent the message that she was going alone. "There's a girl," she said, "in the eastern residential zone. Nine years old. She asked me when the heat was coming." "What did you tell her?" he said. "Four months," she said. He was quiet for a moment. "She said okay," Bronya said. "She went back to her game." He looked at the outer city. At the dome. At the notation channels conducting their warmth through the geological structure of a city that was, in its Underworld as much as its surface, doing the work of becoming. "Four months," he said. "Four months," she said. She stood beside him on the outer wall. The evening was coming in. The cold was the standard Belobog cold — not the Underworld cold, which was the Belobog cold without the surface district's heating network moderating it, but the outer wall cold, which was the dome's edge and the raw temperature of a planet that had been cold since the Separation. He was looking at the outer district, and she was looking at it too, and neither of them was saying anything, and the not-saying was full of the day. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. "The nine-year-old," she said. "And the man at the junction whose daughter is doing homework. And the forty-three-year-old woman whose mother has a Thursday appointment." She paused. "They all said thank you. Directly. Not formally." He said: "That's different." "Yes," she said. "That's different. The formal thanks — the assembly, the ceremony, the public acknowledgment — that's one thing. A person in a corridor who tells you that their daughter has homework and then says thank you directly is—" She stopped. She found the right version. "That's the real thing. The actual weight of it." He looked at her. At the coat. At the mark. "You've been carrying a planet for two years," he said. "Yes," she said. "And tonight you feel the weight of it differently," he said. She looked at him. "Yes," she said. "I feel the weight of it as the specific weight of specific people, rather than the weight of a population." She paused. "It's—" She paused again. "I think it's the correct weight. I think this is what the weight is supposed to feel like." He said: "You're extraordinary." It came out plainly — not as a formality, not as a performance. The plain statement of someone who has been watching a person for two years and has arrived at an opinion and is stating it. She looked at him. At the outer wall. At the cold light above the dome. "Don't make me cry on the outer wall," she said. "I'm not making you do anything," he said. "You said the word extraordinary," she said. "It's accurate," he said. "It's extremely inconvenient," she said, "for my current composure." "Your composure is excellent," he said. "It is usually excellent," she said. "You are testing it." He was looking at her with the expression — the full version, no register left, just him looking at her. He had said she was extraordinary and meant it completely and was not going to take it back. "Gepard," she said. "Yes," he said. She looked at him for a long moment. The outer wall. The evening. The cold. The dome. The person she had been standing beside for two years in every room that mattered. "I love you," she said. Not heavily — plainly. The same register he had used when he said extraordinary. Just a fact, stated on the outer wall. He looked at her for one second. Then: "I love you." Same plain register. Back. She looked at him. "You said that very easily." "I've been saying it in my head for nine years," he said. "It comes easily by now." She stared at him. "Nine years." "The winter assessment," he said. "When we were seventeen. You stayed six minutes past the end. Talking to Pela." He paused. "I watched the clock and didn't know why and worked it out much later." "You counted the six minutes," she said. "Nine years ago." "I counted a great many things," he said. "The six minutes was the first one." She looked at him. At the gold ice ambient in his expression. At the person who had been counting things about her since they were seventeen and who had just said I love you on the outer wall at the end of a Thursday with the same flatness he used for patrol reports. "That," she said, "is the most Gepard way anyone has ever said I love you." "Is it effective?" he said. She looked at him for another moment. Then she put her hand on his jaw — the specific way, her way, the way that was hers. He covered it with his. The gold ice ambient in his skin and the claimed frequency in her palm and both of them knowing exactly what they were doing. "Yes," she said. "It's extremely effective." He kissed her. On the outer wall, in the evening, with the city below and the dome above, which was where they did things that mattered. She kissed him back with the specific completeness of someone who has been saying I love you in their own head for a very long time and is now saying it properly. "When we go to the surface," she said, against his mouth. "Five minutes," he said. "I think I might need ten," she said. "Ten," he agreed. "We'll take ten." She pulled back far enough to look at him. At the gold ice. At the outer wall. At the cold light above the dome. "Nine years," she said again. "Nine years," he said. "We're very slow," she said. "We're very thorough," he said. The corner of her mouth. The real one. "Yes," she said. "We are." She stood beside him on the outer wall in the evening's coming-on and the notation channels ran their warmth through the stone below and the dome was above and she was, for these specific minutes, not managing anything. —— The Landau house at 1930 had the specific warmth of a house that was used regularly and knew it. Lynx had been the steward of this quality for a long time — the heating that ran correctly, the kitchen that had food in it, the specific readiness of a house that expected its people. She had developed this quality since the age of twelve when she had understood that neither of her siblings was going to reliably manage the domestic logistics of the family property and she had the specific organizational capability to do it and so she did. She was at the kitchen table with her medical texts when Serval's message came through. The message said: *Three weeks. I told you three weeks. The train arrives at 1400 on Tuesday.* Lynx put the text down. She sent back: *I know. I put it in the calendar two weeks ago. The guest room is ready.* Serval sent back: *I didn't ask if the guest room was ready.* *I know*, Lynx sent. *It's ready.* A pause. Then: *Good. Also I found something in the frequency data that I need to discuss with Gepard in person. It's significant.* Lynx looked at the message. She sent: *How significant.* *Significant enough that I took an earlier train*, Serval sent. Lynx looked at this for a moment. She sent: *Dinner Tuesday. I'll tell Bronya.* *You're very efficient*, Serval sent. *I manage this house*, Lynx sent. *Efficiency is not optional.* *Fair*, Serval sent. Lynx put the phone down. She opened her calendar. She updated it. She sent a message to Bronya: *Serval is coming Tuesday. Earlier train — she says she has significant frequency data results to discuss with Gepard. Dinner at 1900 if you're available.* Bronya responded in six minutes: *Tuesday at 1900. Yes.* Lynx updated the calendar. She went back to her medical text. The Landau house ran its evening. The heating worked. The guest room was ready. —— On Saturday afternoon, Seele and Bronya met at the restricted zone ledge. This was their place — not formalized, not scheduled, the specific ledge above the outer machine district that they had found together and that had been theirs since the early months of Seele's return to Belobog. It required Seele to cross from the Underworld to the surface through the district access, and it required Bronya to cross from the palace to the district, and neither of them ever complained about the distance. They arrived at approximately the same time, which was also typical. Seele had the scythe at her back in its carry configuration — she rarely went anywhere in the outer machine district's operational territory without it now, less because she expected trouble and more because it had become part of how she moved through the world. Bronya had the coat, the green one, the top button done up because she was not alone with Gepard. Seele looked at the button. She looked at Bronya. "That's a deliberate choice," she said. "Yes," Bronya said, sitting down on the ledge. "Because you're not alone with him," Seele said, also sitting down. "Yes," Bronya said. Seele looked at her. "You've been opening the top button around him specifically," she said. The tone of someone who has observed a pattern and is stating it. "He finds the mark—" Bronya paused, looking for the right word. "Attractive," Seele said. "I was going to say interesting," Bronya said. "You were going to say interesting because you've decided that's the correct word," Seele said, "but the word you'd use in private is different." Bronya looked at the outer machine district below them. At the generators. At the notation channels. "He said I was extraordinary last night," she said. "On the outer wall." Seele said: "What did you say?" "I told him not to make me cry on the outer wall," Bronya said. Seele looked at her for a moment. Then she laughed — the real one, not the catalogued version. Short and genuine. "That's the most you thing you've ever said to me." "He said it plainly," Bronya said. "With no preamble. Like an observation." "That's the most Gepard thing he's ever done," Seele said. Bronya made the sound. The almost-laugh. "Yes," she said. "It was." They sat on the ledge for a while. The outer machine district below them. The dome above. The cold of Belobog's standard evening coming in as the afternoon light faded. "The twenty-second section," Seele said. "Yes," Bronya said. "Clara told me the shape of it," Seele said. "This morning, in the workshop. I asked." She paused. "She told me the center part. The receiving and processing and conducting outward." "What do you think?" Bronya said. Seele looked at the district. At the notation channels conducting their warmth through every junction. She felt the butterfly quality in her skin — the old-gold warmth of the source material that had been her constant companion since the formation chamber. She thought about what it meant to receive and process and conduct outward. "I think," she said, "it describes something you're already doing. Something you've been doing for two years." She paused. "The Guardian's attunement to the Stellaron — receiving the planet's signal, processing it, acting on it. The contact point — receiving the Stellaron's force, processing it through your will, conducting the claimed frequency outward through the notation network." She looked at Bronya. "The Architects built the role for someone who would do what you've been doing. I think that's why it felt possible for you. Why the contact point was yours specifically." Bronya looked at the district. At the outer city. At the dome above it. "Clara said the Architects didn't plan for me specifically," she said. "For someone like me at this specific moment." "That's what she said," Seele said. "But I don't think the distinction matters as much as she thinks it does." She paused. "The role exists. You fit the role. Whether they planned the specific person or the specific conditions that would produce a person — you're the result either way." She looked at Bronya. "You won the contact point. Not anyone like you. You." Bronya was quiet. "Thank you," she said. Eventually. "For saying it that way." "I'm saying it accurately," Seele said. "I know," Bronya said. "That's why it helps." They sat for a while longer. The evening came in properly. The outer machine district lit up in stages — the junction lighting, the residential sections, the generator output settling into the night cycle. They sat for a while longer. The evening came in properly. The outer machine district lit up in stages — the junction lighting, the residential sections, the generator output settling into the night cycle. "Seele," Bronya said. "Yes," Seele said. "The mark," Bronya said. She touched the neckline of the coat — not opening the button, just touching the place where the mark was. "Gepard finds it—" She stopped. She started again. "He said something last night. I love you. Plainly. On the outer wall." Seele looked at her. "Finally," she said. "I said it first," Bronya said. Seele looked at her again. "You said it first," she said. "Yes," Bronya said. "How?" Seele said. "I mean — I know how. I'm asking what made tonight the night." Bronya thought about it. "I came back from the Underworld," she said. "I told him about the man at the junction and his daughter and the woman whose mother has a Thursday appointment. And then I said it." She paused. "I think I said it because the day had stripped everything back to what mattered. And what mattered was him. And the appropriate response to that seemed to be saying so." Seele looked at the outer machine district. "He said I love you back," Bronya said. "Immediately. And then he said he'd been saying it in his head for nine years." Seele was quiet for one second. Then: "Nine years." "The winter assessment," Bronya said. "We were seventeen. He counted the six minutes I stayed past the end." Seele looked at her. "He counted the six minutes when you were seventeen." "Yes," Bronya said. "He watched the clock," Seele said. "Yes," Bronya said. "And he has been—" Seele started, and then stopped. She looked at the city. She looked back at Bronya. "That is," she said, "the most Gepard thing I've ever heard." "I told him that," Bronya said. "In those exact words." "What did he say?" "He asked if it was effective," Bronya said. Seele stared at her. Then she laughed — real, full, the one that came out when something was genuinely funny in the exact right way. "He asked if it was effective," she said. "He did," Bronya said, and she was making the sound too, the almost-laugh becoming the real version, because nine years and the clock and is it effective were all simultaneously the most ridiculous and most correct thing she had ever heard. They sat on the ledge above the outer machine district and laughed together — not politely, not decorously, genuinely, in the specific way that had been possible since before either of them had words for what they were to each other. Below them the district ran its evening and the notation channels were warm in the stone and the city was building something. When the laughter settled Seele said: "The five minutes." "Ten," Bronya said. "We're taking ten." "When the transit route is ready," Seele said. "When the transit route is ready," Bronya confirmed. She paused. "We're all going. When it's ready. Everyone who was there for both battles. We're all going to stand on the surface and look at the cold light sky." Seele looked at her. "Together," she said. "Together," Bronya said. "It's what we do." Seele looked at the city. At the dome. At what was above the dome. "Twelve years," she said. "To the moon," Bronya said. "To the moon," Seele said. She paused. "We'll see the stars on the ground from above." "Yes," Bronya said. They sat with that for a while. The specific warmth of two best friends who had grown up in a hard place and had found each other again and were sitting above everything that was changing and talking about the moon. "Luka," Seele said, eventually. "Yes?" Bronya said. "The sparring today," Seele said. "At the circuit." "How did it go?" Bronya said. "He adjusted to my floor-contact leverage in two repetitions," Seele said. "He identified his left-lane habit the moment I named it." She paused. "He's — we're extraordinary together in a combat context, and that's not me being partial, it's what the forty minutes of data showed." She paused. "I've been thinking about what that means for the expansion. For when Snezhnaya needs its own security infrastructure." Bronya looked at her. "Silvermane Knight deployment beyond the dome," she said. "Yes," Seele said. "I'm not proposing it. I'm thinking about it." She paused. "What it would mean to run notation corridor sessions in terrain we don't know yet. New tunnels, new geology, new Fragmentum behavior at the surface." She paused. "New everything. And us doing the advance assessment." Bronya looked at the outer machine district. At the notation channels. At the geological structure that connected the surface to the Underworld to the cave to the mining operations. "The Snezhnaya site," Bronya said, "is twelve kilometers from the dome. The geological structure there is stable. The Fragmentum density on the surface, according to Svarog's readings, is significantly lower than in the dome's immediate zone." She paused. "It's not a threat territory. It's—" "New territory," Seele said. "Which is its own category of uncertainty." "Yes," Bronya said. "It is." She paused. "When the time comes, I'd want you and Luka to do the initial assessment. Not because you're the most powerful fighters available — because you're the ones who can read the geological notation and conduct through it if the surface channels require it." She paused. "If they do." Seele thought about this. "The notation network at the surface," she said. "Svarog's survey confirmed the channels run outward from the dome's geological foundation. They reach the Snezhnaya site." "Yes," Bronya said. "So the notation network reaches it already," Seele said. "We'd be going to territory the network already knows." "Yes," Bronya said. "That's the way to think about it." Seele looked at the notation channels in the junction stones below the ledge. At the warmth conducting through them. "All right," she said. "When the time comes." —— "Luka," Seele said, eventually. "Yes?" Bronya said. "He told me about the Nihility," Seele said. "The gap. Natasha's verbalization mechanism." She paused. "He's not afraid of the path." Bronya looked at her. "I know," Seele said. "That should probably concern me more than it does." She paused. "It concerns me the correct amount. Which is: I'm paying attention. I'm the external system. I'm going to keep paying attention." She paused. "The part that doesn't concern me — and I've been thinking about why it doesn't concern me — is that the path is his. It's specifically his. The way my powers are specifically mine and the way your mark is specifically yours." She paused. "The path of Nihility in him is not something being done to him. It's something he is. And I trust who he is." Bronya looked at her. At the person who had been her best friend since before either of them had words for what they were to each other. At the twenty-three-year-old with the butterfly quality in her skin and the scythe at her back and the moon twelve years away and the specific certainty of someone who has been paying attention to the person they love and has decided, based on that attention, to trust them. "Yes," Bronya said. "That's the right thing." "I know," Seele said. "It doesn't need to be anything more than that." She looked at the city below. At the underworld below the city, where the notation channels ran their warmth through the deep geological structure and the mining operations had started and the equalization work was changing what it meant to grow up in the outer machine district. "Bronya," Seele said. "Yes," Bronya said. "There's a nine-year-old girl in the eastern residential zone," Seele said. "She plays in the junction square. Her name is Mira. She's been watching the construction crews work on the heating infrastructure." Bronya looked at her. "You know her." "I run the notation corridor sessions twice a week," Seele said. "The route goes through the eastern residential zone. I've seen her watching." She paused. "Three days ago she asked me what the pipes were for. I explained the heating system. She thought about it for approximately five seconds and then she asked if the heat would reach the room where her grandmother sleeps, which is the coldest room in the building because it faces the uninsulated wall." Bronya was quiet. "The insulation work is part of the Phase Two infrastructure," Seele said. "The Underworld residential insulation. That's six months from now." "The insulation reaches the entire eastern zone," Bronya said. "All four buildings. Including whichever building has the cold room." "I told her that," Seele said. "She said: okay. I'll tell my grandmother." She paused. "Then she went back to watching the construction crew." Bronya looked at the city. "I met a girl today," Bronya said. "Nine years old. She asked when the heat was coming. I said four months. She said okay and went back to her game." Seele looked at her. "Mira," she said. Bronya looked at her. "The same one?" "She plays in the junction square," Seele said. "There's only one nine-year-old who spends that much time watching construction crews." Bronya looked at the city. At the outer machine district. At the Underworld below it. "She's waiting," Bronya said. "She's waiting," Seele said. They sat with that for a moment. "Four months," Bronya said. "Four months," Seele said. The city ran its evening around them and the notation channels ran their warmth through the stone and Mira was somewhere in the eastern residential zone probably watching the construction crews even at this hour, and the heat was coming in four months, and that was the work. —— On Sunday morning, Luka went to the Fighter's Circuit. He went with Seele. This had not happened before. The circuit was his — his tournament history, his regular training sessions, his specific relationship with the space and the fighters who used it. Seele had been to the circuit once, for the Boulder Town Super League tournament, in the Moles-and-Clara audience capacity. She had not been in a training capacity. She had asked on Saturday night if she could come Sunday. He had said yes without asking why, because asking why when Seele asked for something was generally not the correct move — she had reasons, and the reasons would become visible when they needed to be visible. The reason became visible at 0840 when they arrived and she picked up the practice scythe from the circuit's equipment rack. The circuit maintained practice weapons for fighters who didn't bring their own. The practice scythe was not the new scythe — the new scythe, Svarog's build with the geometric blue-light blade segments, was for operational use. For the circuit she used the practice version, which was heavier and less precise and required more physical management to wield correctly. She held it. She made two test arcs — the warm-up arcs, assessing the balance and the weight and the reach. She looked at Luka. "I want to spar with you," she said. He looked at her. "At the circuit." "You know the circuit's geometry," she said. "You've been training here for years. I want to see how you move in a space you know, against someone who knows your habits." She paused. "I want to find the habits I don't know about yet." He looked at the practice scythe. At the circuit's training floor. At Seele with her arms crossed and the practice scythe in her right hand, waiting. "You want to find my bad habits," he said. "I want to find your interesting habits," she said. "Some of them might be bad." He looked at her for another moment. He was thinking about the verbalization mechanism and about Nihility and about the specific quality of sparring with Seele, who had been fighting at high operational capacity for nine months and whose relationship to trajectory physics was not standard. "Terms," he said. "Contact only," she said. "No deployment of the full sources. Standard sparring rules." "I'm not going to go easy," he said. "I know," she said. "I'm not asking you to." "You have the ricochet habit from the tunnel geometry," he said. "You're going to try to use the circuit ceiling for leverage." "Probably," she said. "The circuit ceiling is six meters lower than the sub-levels," he said. "I know," she said. "I've been thinking about how to adjust for it." He looked at her. At the practice scythe. At the circuit's training floor with its familiar geometry — the dimensions he knew by muscle memory, the angles, the corner bounce characteristics, the specific qualities of a space he had been moving through since he was sixteen. He had fought in this space hundreds of times against dozens of opponents. He had never fought Seele. "Forty minutes," he said. "Forty minutes," she agreed. They went. —— The forty minutes produced three things. The first: Seele's ricochet habit from the tunnel geometry did not translate to the circuit ceiling at six meters, and she went three minutes before adjusting, and the adjustment she made when she made it was to abandon the ceiling approach entirely and move to floor-contact leverage instead, which was something she had not used in the tunnels because the tunnel floors were not solid enough for it. The circuit floor was. The floor-contact leverage was — Luka filed it immediately as the most interesting combat adaptation he had seen outside of direct engagement. The second: his own habits, specifically the ones he had not been aware of. She identified two in the first twenty minutes and told him directly, in between exchanges, without pausing for the exchanges to fully resolve. "You favor the left side of the lane when you're pushing forward," she said, during a mid-exchange reset. "It's very slight. You've been doing it since the sub-levels." He checked. She was right. He adjusted. Two minutes later she identified the second habit — the specific micro-hesitation before he extended the chrome arm to its full reach, less than a second, a residual from the early days of the integration when the extension range was still being calibrated. He had not known this was still there. He adjusted. The third: they were, together, extraordinarily good. Not in the sense of their combined power output in an engagement — they both knew that from the battles. In the sense of their specific tactical complementarity. She anticipated his adjustments before he made them. He adjusted to her floor-contact leverage in two repetitions, which was faster than any opponent she had encountered. When they were both at full focus in the same combat space, the exchange had the quality of two people thinking in the same language — not the same thoughts, the same structure, which was more efficient and more unsettling. At the forty-minute mark they both stopped simultaneously, without anyone calling it. They were both breathing harder than a standard sparring session warranted. The circuit was quiet around them. She looked at him. At the chrome arm. At the spinal integration visible above the back of his collar. At Luka, twenty-three years old, who had adjusted to her floor-contact leverage in two repetitions and had identified his own micro-hesitation the moment she named it. "You're better than I expected," she said. "You're better than I expected," he said. "That's mutual," she said. "We should do this more often," he said. "Yes," she said. She lowered the practice scythe. She looked at the circuit. At the space she had just spent forty minutes learning. "The micro-hesitation," she said. "The left-lane habit," he said. They looked at each other. "Both of those are worth fixing," she said. "Yes," he said. "We should come back Thursday." "Thursday," she said. "After Natasha." "After Natasha," he agreed. She handed the practice scythe back to the equipment rack. He did the equipment check on the chrome arm — the standard post-session assessment, running through the integration's load data. He said: "Left shoulder is slightly warm from the last exchange. Not medically significant." She looked at him. At the verbalization. "Good," she said.
