"Svarog thinks I'll reach them when I'm ready for them. I think I'm ready for them now. We have an ongoing disagreement about this." From the back room, without turning around: "You are not ready for them yet." "I have considered your reasoning," Clara said, "and I respectfully disagree." "You are twelve years old," Svarog said. "Almost thirteen," Clara said. "When you are thirteen," Svarog said, "we will revisit the disagreement." Clara looked at Topaz with the expression of someone who had lost this argument many times and was prepared to lose it again while maintaining her position. Topaz, who had spent eleven years in the IPC dealing with exactly this quality of principled sustained disagreement, looked at Clara with the expression of someone who recognized a peer. "The nineteenth section," Topaz said. "What does it describe?" "The geological structure of the planet below the notation network's depth," Clara said. "The relationship between the notation channels and the deep crust. The specific mineral composition of the formation deposits." She paused. "It describes the deposits the IPC survey team identified. It describes them in the Architects' notation language, which means the Architects knew about them." Topaz looked at her. "When do you think the Architects were aware of the deposits?" "Before they built the notation network," Clara said. "The nineteenth section appears to predate the upper notation system by — Svarog estimates several centuries. The Architects built the network with the deposit distribution mapped already." "They built the network with the extraction infrastructure in mind," Topaz said. "Yes," Clara said. "That is what I believe." Topaz looked at the notation carving above the secondary bench. At the Architects' language, visible and partially decipherable to someone who had been learning it for nine months. At the twelve-year-old who was learning it. "Clara," she said. "Yes?" Clara said. "When you reach the twentieth section," Topaz said, "I would very much like to know what it says." "I'll tell you," Clara said. "You'll be one of the first." —— The professional context took approximately two hours. Topaz reviewed the IPC survey team's report findings and confirmed the mineral deposit classifications with the focused precision of her analytical training. She had questions about the geological buffer assessment methodology that Svarog answered with the specific patience of someone who had been monitoring the relevant geological systems for three decades longer than the IPC had been tracking them. She had questions about the notation network's interaction with the deposit distribution that Clara answered, arriving at the workshop at 1530 with Pela because Clara had been invited to the meeting by Bronya specifically and Pela had simply appeared because intelligence work was what Pela did. Aventurine looked at Clara. He had looked at her during the previous visit as well — the specific quality of his attention when it landed on someone he had not fully assessed yet, the reading-the-room quality at the person-scale. He had assessed her then. He was reassessing her now, because Clara at twelve had been the person who held the notation network during the Second Stellaron battle, and Clara now — still twelve, almost thirteen — was the person who had held the full primary junction for seven hours and nineteen minutes during the First Stellaron battle, and those were not the same category of person. "You held it for the full engagement," he said. "Yes," Clara said. "Seven hours and nineteen minutes," he said. "Yes," Clara said. "Approximately." "You are twelve years old," he said. "Almost thirteen," she said. He looked at her for a moment. "Topaz told me about the Architects," he said. "The lineage. The source code." He paused. "She said it was one of the most significant things she had encountered in eleven years of field work." Clara looked at him. At the entertained quality. At the specific register underneath the entertained quality that she had learned, from the previous visit and from the few messages that had passed between Belobog and the IPC in the intervening months, was genuine. "It is significant," she said. "I'm still learning the full notation system. Svarog is teaching me." She paused. "There are sections I can't read yet." "How many sections?" he said. "Seventeen," she said. "Of the full system as currently mapped." She paused. "Down from twenty-three." He looked at her for another moment. Then he smiled — not the performed version, the real one, which was different in the specific way that Topaz had apparently learned to distinguish and which Clara was now learning to distinguish. "You'll get the other seventeen," he said. "I know," Clara said. Aventurine laughed — brief, genuine, at the twelve-year-old who had answered his observation about her learning with the simple confidence of someone who had already decided she would finish the thing. "Yes," he said. "I believe you will." —— The underworld upgrade had been underway for twelve days when the IPC arrived. Not planned yet — executing. This was the specific quality of Bronya's administration that Guard officers and Wildfire medics and outer machine district residents had learned over two years: when Bronya Rand decided something, the deciding and the doing were not separated by a planning period of significant length. The planning had already been done. The announcement was the beginning of the execution, not the beginning of the planning. Twelve days in, the eastern underworld medical station had received seven new Wildfire medics from the surface redeployment, a full supply of the standard ward medications that had been available on the surface but not in the eastern station, and a structural assessment crew that was already three days into planning the expansion of the station's capacity by sixty percent. The educational redistribution had placed fourteen surface-trained teachers in underworld classrooms across three of the residential districts, and the curriculum equalization had been announced with the specific directness of a policy that was not subject to debate. The food supply stabilization was four days from its first full implementation — the logistical work of connecting the underworld districts to the surface supply network, which was harder than it sounded and which Dunn's team was managing with the specific efficiency of people who had recently managed a five-hour Stellaron battle and found food supply logistics comparatively tractable. Svarog had been in the underworld every day for twelve days. Not because his presence was required for any of the specific projects — the Silvermane Guard and the Wildfire and the palace's operational teams were managing the execution. He was there because the outer machine district and the underworld below it had been his city for longer than any other institution on the planet had existed, and the twelve days of change happening below the surface were the largest single transformation in the underworld's conditions in his four-hundred-year monitoring record, and he was not going to miss any of it. He walked the underworld in the mornings. He spoke to the residents he encountered. He looked at the medical station expansion progress and the educational redeployment and the supply network installation and the faces of the people who were watching it happen and understanding that it was not temporary. He filed detailed logs. Not for any strategic purpose. Because the things that were happening in the underworld in those twelve days deserved to be recorded completely. On the sixth day, a woman he had known for twenty-two years — one of the district's long-term residents, who had lived in the outer machine district her entire life and had raised two children there and had been Natasha's patient for eight years — stopped him in the junction corridor and said: "Is this real?" "Yes," he said. She looked at him. At the four primary arms. At the oldest consciousness on the planet, who had been in the outer machine district for longer than anyone could account for. "You're not going to take it back," she said. Not exactly a question. "No," he said. "The Supreme Guardian does not take things back." She looked at him for another moment. Then she nodded. She went back to what she had been doing. He filed this in the logs with the specific attention it deserved. —— The underworld upgrade had been underway for twelve days when the IPC arrived. Topaz closed her briefing tablet. Aventurine, who had been contributing to the professional context with the analytical capability that was his primary skill and which the entertained quality sometimes caused people to underestimate, closed his own files. Bronya looked at them. "The engagement," she said. Topaz looked at her. "Yes," she said. "When?" Bronya said. "Three weeks ago," Topaz said. "The timing was — not planned to coincide with anything." She paused. "We have been together for a long time. The question was when the right moment was. The right moment was three weeks ago." She looked at Aventurine. Something passed between them at the speed of people who had been reading each other for years — a small thing, entirely private, visible in the shift of Topaz's expression from the professional register to the warmer one. "Congratulations," Gepard said. Simply and genuinely. Aventurine looked at him. At the sincerity in it. "Thank you," he said, which was also simple and also genuine, and which Gepard received with the specific quality of the genuinely sincerely meant. "Who asked whom," Bronya said. Topaz looked at her. "I asked him," she said. A beat. "She did," Aventurine confirmed. "I had been planning to ask for — approximately five months." He looked at Topaz. "She asked three weeks ago. I said yes immediately." He paused. "I had apparently been planning for five months what she decided in an afternoon." "I had not decided in an afternoon," Topaz said. "I had decided significantly earlier than the afternoon. The afternoon was the execution." "You did not tell me you had already decided," Aventurine said. "You did not ask," she said. "I was planning," he said. "I noticed you were planning," she said, "which is why I asked before you finished." He looked at her. The corner of his mouth — the real one, the one that was not the entertained quality but something underneath it, the specific private expression of someone who is in the presence of the person they are most entirely themselves with. "Yes," he said. "That is exactly what happened." Bronya looked at them. At the rings. At the specific quality of two people who had known each other a very long time and had arrived somewhere together and were sitting in the arrival. She thought about the bench in the study. The lamp. The gold ice ambient in his hands against her coat. She looked at Gepard. He was already looking at her. —— Later, in the evening, Bronya and Topaz walked the outer machine district alone. Not for any formal purpose. Topaz had asked, and Bronya had said yes, and they had left the workshop while Aventurine and Gepard were in the process of a conversation about geological buffer assessment methodologies that had been, initially, about the Snezhnaya site, and had developed into a conversation about strategic defense perimeter principles, and then into a conversation about chess, which neither of them had announced was happening but which had been happening for approximately twenty minutes before anyone pointed it out. "Aventurine plays chess?" Bronya had said. "He plays everything," Topaz had said. "He is also very good at most of it, which he does not always announce." "Gepard is good at chess," Bronya said. "I know," Topaz said. "This is going to take a while." They left. The outer machine district in the evening was its specific self — the generators running their output, the junction corridors lit, the notation channels conducting their warmth through the stone. The gold warmth and the prismatic claimed frequency still present in the channels, two weeks on, not fading. Topaz walked through the district and she looked at everything she looked at with the attention she brought to things she was genuinely interested in, which was her only speed — she did not have a casual looking register, she looked at things properly or she did not look at them. She looked at the notation channel junctions. At the pre-Svarog carvings above the corridor entrances. At the geological structure. "The deposit distribution," Topaz said. "The twelve-kilometer radius. It extends under the outer machine district." "Yes," Bronya said. "We noticed." "You have been walking above it for generations," Topaz said. "The people of this district have been walking above it for generations." "Yes," Bronya said. Topaz was quiet for a moment. "I have been in this work for eleven years," she said. "I have been to nineteen planets in those eleven years. I have seen the full range of what the IPC encounters — systems that are straightforward asset acquisitions, systems that are complicated political situations, systems where the goal is containment and management." She paused. "Belobog is not in any of those categories." "No," Bronya said. "What happened here in the last year," Topaz said, "is outside every classification framework I was trained on." She looked at Bronya. "The Second Stellaron — a memory-holding entity defeated through the combination of the Architects' notation network, the children who carried the Architects' lineage, and two fighters whose connection to the Stellaron predated the city above it. The First Stellaron — an eight-hundred-year-old geological entity of the Aeon of Destruction's making, defeated. An Emanator acknowledged. A Guardian who claimed the Stellaron's frequency." She paused. "And mineral deposits that could power this planet for centuries, sitting below a geological survey site that Svarog identified three hundred and eighty years ago." Bronya looked at her. At the specific quality Topaz had when she was being honest about something that mattered to her. "You're saying it's not a coincidence," Bronya said. "I'm saying," Topaz said, "that I have been in this work for eleven years and I have not encountered a planet where everything converged correctly at the moment it needed to. Where the resources were there. Where the people were who they needed to be. Where the timing of every significant event aligned with the readiness of the city to respond to it." She paused. "This planet has been preparing for this moment for eight hundred years. Possibly longer." She looked at the notation channels. "The Architects built the network. Svarog arrived and maintained it. The deposits accumulated. The lineage was preserved. The right people were born in the right city at the right time." She paused. "I don't know what to call that. I'm a debt-assessment specialist. I don't have the vocabulary." Bronya looked at the outer machine district around her. At the junction corridors and the notation carvings and the generators and the residential sections where people were doing their evenings. At the dome above. "We call it Belobog," she said. Topaz looked at her. "This is Belobog," Bronya said. "This is what this city is. It has always been this city. We just now have enough resources and enough cleared ground to show it fully." She looked at Topaz. "And the IPC as an unconditional ally." Topaz held her gaze for a moment. Then she said: "Yes. You do." They walked back to the workshop in the outer machine district's evening, and the notation channels were warm, and the city ran its specific self around them. —— In the workshop, Aventurine and Gepard had been playing chess for approximately thirty-five minutes when Serval walked in. She had come from the north wall, where she had been running frequency measurements on the notation channels' post-battle stabilization, and she had come to the workshop because the workshop was the center of the network data and the workshop had better equipment for the secondary readings she needed. She came in through the side entrance, put her equipment down, and looked at the chess board. She looked at Gepard. "You're losing," she said. "I'm not losing," Gepard said. "Your queen is in a position that Aventurine is going to use in approximately three moves," she said. "I know where my queen is," Gepard said. "Then you know you're losing," she said. Aventurine, across the board, looked at Serval with the expression he wore when something had surprised him favorably. "You read the board quickly," he said. "I played with him for eight years," she said, indicating Gepard. "You learn the patterns." She looked at the board. "He always keeps the queen too far from his defensive structure when he's thinking about something else. His defense is better when he's fully focused." She paused. "He's not fully focused right now." "What am I thinking about?" Gepard said. "I don't know," Serval said. "Bronya is outside with Topaz and you're in here playing chess, so probably Bronya." She picked up her equipment. "That's three moves," she said to Aventurine. "The knight first, then the bishop, then the queen." Aventurine looked at the board. He made the knight move. Gepard looked at the board. He looked at Serval, who had already moved to the secondary display and was not looking at him. "You're very helpful," he said to her. "You're welcome," she said. Aventurine moved the bishop. He looked at Gepard. "She's correct," he said. "It's the queen next." "I know," Gepard said. "I'm reconsidering." "The position doesn't improve with reconsidering," Aventurine said. "You'd need to have placed the rook differently six moves ago." Gepard looked at the board. He looked at Serval. He looked at the board. "You were right," he said to Serval. "I was thinking about something else." "I know," she said, not looking up from the secondary display. "Three moves." Aventurine moved the queen. —— Bronya came back from the walk with Topaz at 1900. She came into the workshop and she looked at the chess board — at the completed game, at Aventurine's quiet satisfaction and Gepard's expression of someone who had lost and was being precise about understanding why — and she looked at Serval at the secondary display. "She told him he was losing," Gepard said. "He was losing," Serval said. "I was reconsidering," Gepard said. "You had lost three moves ago," Serval said. "Reconsidering was not the relevant action." Topaz looked at the board. She looked at Aventurine. "You won," she said. "Yes," he said. "Against Gepard Landau," she said. "With Serval's help," he said. "I gave you the first move," Serval said. "You executed the rest." Aventurine looked at Serval with the specific quality he used when he was genuinely assessing someone. "You play?" he said. "I teach," she said. "Gepard has been a decent opponent for eight years. He would be better if he didn't think about Bronya during the midgame." "Serval," Gepard said. "It's an observable pattern," she said. "It's in the data." Topaz looked at Bronya. Bronya looked at Topaz. Something passed between them at the speed of two people who had been talking for two hours and had arrived at a mutual understanding of each other's specific quality of humor. "The engagement," Bronya said, to the room. Aventurine and Topaz both looked at her. "You didn't tell anyone else," she said. "When did you arrive?" "Two hours ago," Topaz said. "And you're telling the room now," Bronya said. "We're telling the workshop," Topaz said. "Which is the same thing, effectively." Clara, who had been at the notation bench and who had been listening to the entire conversation with the focused attention she brought to things she found interesting, turned around. "Congratulations," she said. "Both of you." They looked at her. "I noticed the rings when you arrived," she said. "I was waiting to see when you'd say something." She paused. "Topaz asked?" "Yes," Topaz said. "Good," Clara said. She turned back to the notation bench. Aventurine stared at her. "She's twelve years old," he said. "Almost thirteen," Bronya said. "She was paying attention to my hands when she was doing notation analysis," he said. "Clara pays attention to everything," Bronya said. Aventurine looked at Clara's back. At the notation bench. At the twelve-year-old who was going to be thirteen and had held the primary junction for seven hours and nineteen minutes and had noticed his ring before anyone else said anything about it. "I like this city," he said. Not in the entertained quality. As a plain statement of fact. "Good," Topaz said. "We're going to be coming back regularly." "I know," he said. She looked at him. "You sound pleased about that." "I am pleased about that," he said. "I've been pleased about Belobog for six months. This is not new information." "It's new for you to say it plainly," she said. "I say things plainly when it's appropriate," he said. "You say things obliquely when you want to see how people respond," she said, "and you say things plainly when you've decided you don't need the response to tell you something because you already know." She paused. "You already know you like this city." He looked at her. The specific expression — private, entirely hers, the one that was not for the room. "Yes," he said. "I do." She looked at him for a moment. She looked at the room — at Gepard and Bronya and Serval and Clara at the notation bench and Svarog in the back room and the workshop that was the center of everything that had happened in Belobog in the last year. She looked at the workshop and she thought about eleven years of IPC field work and nineteen planets and what this one had done in a year. "We'll come back," she said to Bronya. "As often as the site assessments require. And — probably somewhat more often than that." Bronya looked at her. "The guest rooms at the palace are available," she said. "Both of you. For as long as you need them." Topaz held her gaze for a moment. "Thank you," she said. The way Topaz said things she meant, which was directly and without decoration. "You're welcome," Bronya said. The same way. Bronya made the promotion in the afternoon briefing, quietly and without ceremony, because Dunn was the specific kind of person who preferred the thing to happen without the production around it. She handed him the commission. He looked at it. He looked at her. "The Colonel rank," he said. "Yes," she said. "That's—" He stopped. He looked at the commission. "Thank you, Supreme Guardian." "Bronya," she said. He looked at her. The specific expression of someone who has been given something large and is calibrating how to hold it. "Thank you, Bronya," he said. She looked at him. At twenty-four years old and the cracked rib still in the process of fully healing and eight years of the Guard done correctly and every one of those years visible in how he stood. "You held the north wall," she said. "For five hours. With a cracked rib. From the fifth wave onward." She paused. "The north wall held because you held it. That is what the Colonel rank is for." He was quiet for a moment. "Sir," he said — to Gepard, who was in the room. Gepard looked at him. "Colonel." Dunn looked at the commission. At the room. At both of them. "Good," he said. Simply. The word he used when something was correct. —— Three weeks after the battle, on a night when the outer machine district was quiet and the notation channels had settled into the specific warmth of a city that was learning what it was now that the immediate survival work was done, Gepard came to the palace at 2100. Not for a briefing. Not for the casualty reports or the reconstruction schedule or the Snezhnaya geological assessments. For the other thing — the thing that had been accumulating across two years of every room they had shared and had become specific and named since the bench in the study and the leaning and the mark and the gold ice and all of it. Bronya met him in the study. The lamp was on. The new coat was on the chair. She was in the standard indoor clothes she wore in the palace when nobody required the formal version of her, which was a specific quality of not-performing that he had become familiar with across two years of the 0700 office and the 2100 study. She looked at him when he came in. She looked at the coat on the chair. She looked at him. She had, for the past two weeks, been developing the specific awareness that Gepard's attention — when it was on her in the way it was on her when they were alone in a room that didn't require either of them to be performing anything — occasionally landed specifically on the top button of the coat. The button that, when open, showed the mark. She had been thinking about this with the focused precision she brought to things she was thinking about. She picked up the coat. She put it on. She looked at him. She opened the top button. Not dramatically — the specific subtle gesture of someone who is doing something they have decided to do and is not making a production of having decided it. The top button opened. The mark visible at the edge of the neckline. She held his gaze across the study. He looked at the mark. At her. The corner of his mouth did the thing. "You did that deliberately," he said. "Yes," she said. "You've been doing it deliberately for two weeks," he said. "I haven't been doing it for two weeks," she said. "I've been thinking about doing it for two weeks." He looked at her. "What's the difference." "Tonight is the first time," she said. "The previous two weeks were consideration." He looked at the mark. At her. At the study. At the coat with its top button open and the angular dark symbol at the center of her chest visible above the neckline. "Bronya," he said. "Yes," she said. "You are going to drive me to an extraordinary degree of difficulty," he said. "I know," she said, with the specific warmth of someone who has decided something and is satisfied with having decided it. He crossed the study. He did not do it quickly — the specific deliberate pace of someone who knows exactly where they are going and is choosing the pace that fits the moment. He stopped in front of her. He looked at her — at the coat, at the mark, at her face — and then he put his hand on her jaw and tilted her face up and kissed her. She kissed him back with the full weight of two years of the 0700 office and the bench in the study and the monitoring station entrance and everything between, which was quite a significant weight, and which she put into it completely. His thumb brushed the edge of the mark on her chest — not asking, just present, the warmth of his hand against the cold of the claimed frequency's permanent mark. She felt the gold ice ambient in his skin. The two things, coexisting. She said, against his mouth: "The top button was definitely deliberate." He made the sound — the actual sound, not the controlled version, the brief involuntary thing that she was collecting with the same focus she brought to collecting accurate information about anything that mattered to her. "I know," he said. "I know you know," she said. "I just wanted to confirm it." He pulled back far enough to look at her. His thumb still at the edge of the mark. His expression — the full version, the one that had no register left because both registers were done. "Close the door," he said. "It's already closed," she said. He looked at her. "Is it." "I closed it when you came in," she said. The corner of his mouth again. The real one. "Of course you did," he said. "Of course I did," she confirmed. —— Across the outer machine district, in the east junction residential stack, the lamp was on. The lamp was on most evenings now because Seele read by lamplight and preferred it to the overhead lighting, which was a preference Luka had learned approximately three days after they started spending more evenings in the same space and had adapted to without comment because adapting without comment was his specific mode for things that were easy to accommodate and that he wanted to accommodate. The lamp was on, and Seele was on the low seat by the window, and Luka was on the floor doing his maintenance work on the weapon, and they had been in the same room for approximately four hours without either of them feeling the need to be anywhere else or do anything different, which was the specific quality of their evenings that Seele had come to notice over the past three weeks with the same focused attention she brought to corridor readings. She put down the book at 2230. She looked at him. He was at the component that required the most precise calibration — the cannon's output regulator, which he checked weekly because the danger-scaling mechanism was sensitive and he preferred to know its current threshold before any situation required him to use it. He was focused on the calibration in the specific way he was focused on things that required precision, which was completely. She looked at him for a long moment. "Luka," she said. "Yes," he said, without looking up from the calibration. "Come sit with me," she said. He looked up from the calibration. At her. At the window. At the outer machine district's night visible through it — the generator stacks lit, the notation channels warm in the geological structure below, the dome above doing its night sequence, the cold of Belobog's standard environment outside and the lamp's warmth inside. He put the component down. He came to the low seat. She moved over. He sat beside her. She took the specific position she had been developing an interest in over the past three weeks — her side against his side, her head against his shoulder, her feet up on the seat beside her, the chrome arm warm where it rested against her back. She had been calibrating the precise version of this position across approximately twenty iterations over the past three weeks, and she had now arrived at the configuration that was correct, which was: this. He put his arm around her. Not as a decision — as the automatic response to her being in this position, which was what it had become. His arm around her. Her against him. The lamp. The city through the window. "Better," she said. "Better than what?" he said. "Better than you being over there calibrating," she said. "The calibration needs to be done weekly," he said. "I know," she said. "You can finish it tomorrow." He looked at the component on the floor. At the window. At the city doing its night. At Seele against him in the lamp's light. "Tomorrow," he agreed. She had her hand on the chrome arm — not holding it, just resting against it, the specific contact with the source material's old-gold warmth conducting through the chrome surface into her palm. She had developed, over the past month, the specific habit of contact with the chrome arm in quiet moments. Not as a deliberate thing — as what happened when her hand went where it wanted to be. He noticed. He always noticed. He did not mention it because she had not mentioned it, which was the specific code of two people who were figuring out what they were to each other in practice and doing it at the correct speed. "The moon," she said, after a while. "The moon," he said. "When Snezhnaya is built," she said, "and when the mining operations start, and when Belobog has the energy resources to build the things it wants to build — what's the timeline?" "Svarog's estimate is five years for the first generation of surface infrastructure," he said. "Ten years before the energy processing is at full operational scale." He paused. "Serval has a different estimate. She says seven and twelve." "Serval is usually right about timelines," Seele said. "She says twelve years minimum before they have the capacity for anything that could reach orbit," he said. "Let alone the moon." Seele was quiet for a moment. "Twelve years," she said. "Approximately," he said. She was quiet again. He waited, because he had learned that Seele's quiets had specific content and she would say the content when she had it ready. "I'll be thirty-five when we go to the moon," she said. "Thirty-five," he said. "Yes." "That's—" She thought about it. "Fine," she said. "That's fine." "It is fine," he said. "We'll be good at thirty-five," she said. "We're good now," he said. She tilted her head back slightly so she could look at him without fully moving. Her head still on his shoulder, just angled. He looked down at her at the same time she looked up. The specific configuration of two people who have arranged themselves for comfortable long-term proximity and are looking at each other from inside that arrangement. She said: "You smell like machine oil." "I was doing maintenance," he said. "I know," she said. "I don't mind." He looked at her. At the corner of her mouth. At the window over her shoulder, the city through it. "Seele," he said. "Yes," she said. He held her gaze for a moment. He thought about the junction corridor at 2237 on the morning after the battle, and the cave at nine years old, and the lamp-lit room where the moon was promised, and all the versions of this across all the months of it. "I love you," he said. The afternoon version. The lamp-lit version. The version that existed when nothing extraordinary was happening and it was simply true. She looked at him for a moment with the full expression. "I love you," she said. Same register. Same weight. She moved — not dramatically, just the small adjustment of someone who had decided to be closer. Her legs across his. Her head more fully against his shoulder. His arm tighter around her. They sat like that in the lamp's light with the city through the window and the chrome arm warm and the moon twelve years away and both of them entirely fine with twelve years. —— It was Toma who noticed, approximately two weeks later, that Seele was in the workshop more than usual. Not for the notation channel work — Seele did her notation channel practice in the corridors, not in the workshop. Not for any particular professional reason. Seele was in the workshop at 0900 on a Tuesday, sitting on the secondary bench with the frequency documentation she was reviewing and occasionally adding a correction to, which was work she could do anywhere, which meant she was choosing to do it here. Luka was at the back room's entrance, doing an equipment check with Svarog that had started at 0830 and was still going. Toma looked at this. She looked at Seele on the secondary bench. She looked at Luka at the back room entrance. She looked at Oleg, who was on the floor. "She comes when he's here," Toma said, in an undertone that was approximately as subtle as Oleg's appetite. "Yes," Oleg said, at the same volume. "She didn't used to do that," Toma said. "She does that now," Oleg said. Clara, from the notation bench, without turning around: "Are both of you planning to do this quietly or is this going to be a full conversation." "It's an observation," Toma said. "It's a loud observation," Clara said. "I was not being loud," Toma said. "You were being twelve," Clara said. Toma looked at her. "We are all twelve." "Almost thirteen," Clara said. "Clara," Toma said. "She is in the workshop," Clara said, "because she likes being around him. This is not complicated. Please put this in the notebook, Oleg, and let's move on." Oleg wrote something in the notebook. Toma looked at it. "That's not what happened," Toma said. "That's what I want the record to say happened," Oleg said. Seele, across the workshop, did not look up from the frequency documentation. But the corner of her mouth did the thing. —— On the nights when Luka wasn't doing maintenance on the weapon and Seele wasn't reviewing documentation and there was nothing operational requiring either of them, the lamp was still on. It was on the third week in a row that they had been in the same space most evenings when Seele looked at the window — at the outer machine district's night, the specific blue-cold of Belobog's standard environment outside — and said: "It's cold tonight." "It's always cold," Luka said. "More than usual," she said. She looked at him. "The lamp's warmer." He looked at her. At the window. At the specific quality of the invitation that was not an invitation so much as an accurate observation, which was Seele's mode for everything she wanted. He got up from the floor. He sat beside her on the low seat. She took the position — her side against him, her head on his shoulder, his arm around her, the chrome arm warm. The established configuration. They sat in it for a while. The city outside. The lamp inside. She said, eventually: "The moon chapter." "Yes?" he said. "The part where it describes what the moon looks like from the surface of a planet without a dome," she said. "The specific quality of it — no atmosphere filter, no illumination system." She paused. "What do you think it looks like?" "Bright," he said. "The light is direct. No diffusion." "How bright?" she said. "I looked it up," he said. "When the Architects were building the dome, the pre-Separation records document what the night sky looked like unfiltered. They describe it—" He paused, finding the text. "They describe it as cold light. Specific and close, even from a great distance. Like something looking back at you." She turned her head slightly so she could see his face. "Cold light," she said. "Cold light," he said. "Like the gold ice," she said. He looked at her. At the specific connection she had made, which was the correct connection, which he had not made himself. "Yes," he said. "Somewhat like that." She looked at him for another moment. Then she tilted her face up. The lamp behind her. His face above her. The specific configuration that had been building across three weeks of the correct position on the low seat in the lamp's light. She kissed him — the slow version, the one that was not urgent, the one that existed in the quality of two people who have all the time they need. He kissed her back the same way. When they came apart she was looking at him with the expression — the full one, no catalogue, no management. All of it. "I'm not going anywhere," she said. Not urgently. As a fact. "I know," he said. "Then stop—" She stopped herself. She looked at him. She tried again. "You hold yourself very carefully sometimes," she said. "Like you are being precise about not taking up too much space." He was quiet for a moment. "I'm aware of that," he said. "I don't want you to do that," she said. "When we're here. When the lamp is on and it's just us." She paused. "I want you here. Not careful. Here." He looked at her. At the lamp. At the city through the window. "Seele," he said. "Yes," she said. "I'm here," he said. "I know," she said. "I want you to know that I know." She paused. "And that I'm not going anywhere either." He held her gaze. The lamp. The chrome arm where it was. The city. Twelve years to the moon and all of Belobog standing and both of them here. He kissed her again. This time differently — with the specific difference that existed when someone had been told they do not need to be careful and has believed it. She put her arms around him and she did not move away and neither did he and the lamp stayed on and the city did its night around them and neither of them needed it to be anything other than what it was. —— What followed across the next several weeks was, by any accounting, a gradual thing. Not by design — Luka was not a person who designed things in this register, and Seele had no interest in design when the thing itself was what she was interested in. It was gradual in the way that things were gradual when the rightness of them was already established and what remained was the pace, which was its own kind of information. The lamp on more evenings than not. The low seat. The specific configuration. The warmth of the chrome arm. The moon twelve years away and neither of them worrying about it. The first night he stayed — not planned, arrived at, the lamp off and the city outside and neither of them having decided to move — they were very quiet in the way of two people who have arrived somewhere together and are still orienting to the arrival. He kept the specific careful quality Seele had told him not to keep, because one request does not undo a habit, and she said: "You're doing it again," which was not an accusation, just the accurate observation. "I know," he said. "Stop," she said. He breathed. He let himself stop. She put her hand on the chrome arm in the dark. The warmth of the source material conducting through the chrome surface into her palm. He put his hand over hers — the biological left hand, the one he had held her with since they were nine years old. "Better," she said. "Better," he agreed. They slept. The city outside. The notation channels warm in the stone. The moon twelve years away and both of them entirely fine with twelve years. —— Natasha noticed the change in Sampo approximately two weeks after the IPC's visit concluded. Not a dramatic change — Natasha had been observing Sampo with the focused attention she brought to things she cared about for four years, and she had a precise picture of him in her working model. What she noticed was small: he came to the ward on his standard Tuesday and Thursday schedule, and on Wednesday he also came. He had not been coming on Wednesdays. He came on Wednesday and he sat in the chair across from her desk while she finished the afternoon assessments and they talked, and the talking was the specific talking of two people who had been doing this for years and were developing the specific ease of people who are no longer managing anything about what they are to each other. On the third Wednesday he said: "Is this inconvenient?" She looked up from the assessment file. "No," she said. "You're working," he said. "You're not interrupting the work," she said. "You're providing ambient company, which I find useful during assessments." He looked at her. "Ambient company." "Yes," she said. "Your presence in the room is — a comfortable background quality. It helps me focus." He stared at her. "Natasha," he said. "Yes?" she said, returning to the file. "That is simultaneously the most clinical and the warmest thing anyone has said to me," he said. "I know," she said, and turned a page. He looked at the ward. At the files on her desk. At the room that he had been coming to since he was fourteen years old for various reasons that had accreted into their current configuration, which was: he came here because she was here, and being here was where he wanted to be. He reached into the jacket. He produced two dark filling pastries. He put one on her desk beside the file she was working on. "Ambient pastry," he said. She looked at the pastry. She looked at him. She picked it up. "Thank you," she said. "You're welcome," he said. He ate his pastry. She ate hers. The ward ran its afternoon around them. Outside, the outer machine district did its specific thing of being a city that was building something. It was ordinary. It was exactly right. —— Serval came back to Belobog two weeks after the IPC's visit, with her frequency equipment and forty-three additional pages of analysis and, apparently, a plan that she had been developing on the train. She came to the Landau house, which had become the standard point of arrival for Serval when she came to the outer machine district, and she found Dunn there. Not because Dunn had known she was coming — he had not known, she had not told him. He was there because Lynx had asked him to help with a structural assessment of the house's northern wall, which had developed a crack from the vibration during the First Stellaron's engagement, and because helping Lynx with structural assessments was a thing he did when Lynx asked him to because Lynx was seventeen and had been running the Landau house logistics since before Gepard had fully understood that Lynx was running the Landau house logistics. Serval came through the door and found Dunn on a ladder with a measuring instrument. She looked at him on the ladder. He looked at her from the ladder. "You came back," he said. "I have forty-three more pages of analysis," she said. "And a working hypothesis about the long-term behavior of the combined notation frequency that I would like to discuss with Svarog and Gepard and possibly Bronya." She looked at the ladder. "What are you doing?" "Structural assessment," he said. "The northern wall has a crack." She looked at the crack. She looked at the ladder. She looked at him. "The crack is at two hundred and forty centimeters," she said. "The vibration load during the First Stellaron engagement would have produced a resonance in this wall at the third harmonic of the dome's standard frequency. The crack would originate at the point of maximum harmonic amplitude, which is—" She walked to the wall. She looked at it. "There," she said, pointing. "If you run the measurement from that point you'll find the crack extends approximately thirty-eight centimeters to the left and twenty-two to the right before attenuating." Dunn looked at where she was pointing. He measured. He looked at her. "Thirty-seven and twenty-three," he said. "Close enough," she said. "The attenuation rate varied slightly from the model. The wall's mineral composition is slightly denser than standard construction material, which the model didn't account for." He looked at her. At the frequency equipment on her hip. At the forty-three pages of analysis she was still holding in her other hand. At Serval Landau, twenty-eight years old, who had just arrived on a train from wherever she came from and had walked into the Landau house and immediately started doing structural acoustics analysis on the northern wall while still wearing her traveling coat. "You analyzed the wall," he said. "It was obvious," she said. "I've been up here for twenty minutes," he said. "You didn't have the harmonic model," she said. She looked at him. "Do you want help with the repair assessment or are you—" "Yes," he said. "I want help with the repair assessment." She put the forty-three pages down on the table. She pulled up the frequency equipment — not the full instrument, the compact wall-analysis attachment that she apparently carried as a standard component. She looked at the crack. "Come down from there," she said. "You're measuring from the wrong reference point." He came down from the ladder. He stood beside her at the wall. She ran the analysis. She explained it as she went — the harmonic pattern, the propagation, the repair recommendations. He listened. Not because the structural repair of a wall crack was inherently interesting, but because Serval explaining something she understood was a specific quality of experience that he had, since the age of sixteen, never been able to fully account for. She moved through the analysis with complete confidence in her framework, and she explained it with the same efficiency she brought to everything, and he listened to all of it. "Serval," he said, when she reached the repair recommendations. "Yes?" she said, still looking at the wall. "The dinner," he said. "The one at the Landau house. After the ceremony." She looked at him. "Yes." "You argued about the vegetables," he said. "With Gepard." "The vegetables were incorrectly prepared," she said. "He knows this. I was correct." "Bronya sided with you," he said. "Bronya also knows the correct preparation," she said. He looked at her. At the wall. At the analysis report she had generated in approximately eight minutes. "I'd like to have dinner again," he said. "Here, or somewhere else. Whenever you're available." She looked at him. The direct look, the one she used when she was giving something her full attention. "I'm available Thursday," she said. "Thursday," he said. "The wall repair recommendation is: standard mineral paste at the primary crack point, with resonance dampening applied at the harmonic origin." She handed him the analysis output. "Tell Lynx to use the dampening compound. Standard paste alone will crack again at the first significant vibration event." "Right," he said. He looked at the output. At her. "Serval." "Yes," she said. "Thursday," he said. "You said Thursday." "I said Thursday," she said. "Do you need me to write it down?" "No," he said. "Good," she said. She picked up the forty-three pages. "I'm going to Svarog's workshop." She went. He looked at the crack in the wall. At the analysis output in his hand. At the door she had gone through. He looked at the crack. "Dampening compound," he said, to the wall. The wall did not respond, but Lynx, appearing in the doorway with a cup of tea, said: "She's right, by the way. The dampening compound." He looked at Lynx. "I've been in this house a long time," Lynx said. "Serval Landau is usually right about structural resonance and other things." "I know," he said. "Good," she said. She handed him the tea and went back to whatever she had been doing. —— On the night the Topaz and Aventurine visit concluded and they took the train back to wherever IPC Stonehearts took trains to, Bronya was in the palace study with the lamp on. She had her phone in her hand. She sent a message to Topaz: *Safe travels. We'll see you at the next visit.* Topaz responded in four minutes: *Yes. The next visit will be for the Snezhnaya site assessment. Aventurine wants to bring the survey instruments himself, which I support because it means we have an excuse to come back sooner.* Bronya looked at this. She sent: *Tell him the structural geology team would appreciate the Cornerstone's probability-field assistance during the deep-crust mapping. It is not standard survey equipment but it is effective.* Topaz sent back: *I'll tell him. He'll be delighted. He likes being considered useful for something other than his smile.* Bronya sent: *He is also useful for the chess.* Topaz sent: *Do not tell him that. He already thinks too well of the chess.* Bronya made the sound. She put the phone down. She looked at the window. At the city. At the dome. At the notation channels conducting their warmth through the geological structure of a planet that had, in the past year, done things that the IPC had no classification for, that Pela had forty-seven pages on, that Svarog had four hundred years of monitoring that had been building toward, that two twelve-year-old children had held the notation network for, that an Emanator and a Guardian and all of their people had fought for. She thought about Topaz and Aventurine on the train. The rings on their fourth fingers. The specific warmth of two people who had known each other a long time and had arrived somewhere together. She thought about the bench in the study and what had happened in the study three weeks ago and the top button of the coat and the mark and the gold ice and all of it, which was not a small thing and was also entirely hers. She looked at the new coat on the chair. She picked up the phone. She sent a message. It said: *Are you awake.* The response came in under a minute: *Yes. North wall check.* She sent: *Come to the study.* A pause. Then: *Ten minutes.* She put the phone down. She looked at the lamp. At the city through the window. At Belobog, twelve years from the moon, building something new on the other side of everything it had survived. She was twenty-six years old and the Supreme Guardian of Jarilo-VI and the city was standing and there was work to do and also, at 2100 in the palace study, there was this — the lamp on and ten minutes and all the rest of what it was. She reached up and opened the top button of the coat. The city ran its night. —— END OF CHAPTER 59 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 57: What Belobog Became Chapter Text The signal went to zero at 0313. For a full four seconds after Pela's message hit the operational comms, nobody moved. Not Dunn on the north wall, who had been braced against the merlon for three hours with his left arm pressed to his side because something had cracked in his ribs in the sixth wave and he had not stopped and had not said anything because stopping was not an option and saying anything was not relevant. Not Karev on the forward line, who had been pulling fighters through crack-and-finish combinations for five hours with the specific mechanical efficiency of someone who had stopped thinking about it and had started simply doing it until there was nothing left to do. Not the Guard officers on the outer wall whose names nobody would write down in the historical record but who had been there, every one of them, for five hours — holding walls that shook under the spawns' impacts, cracking crystalline formations with the limited ice expressions that Belobog's cold had put in most of them since childhood, not at Gepard's ceiling, not even close, but real and present and enough. Not the Wildfire medics at the collection points who had been running triage in conditions that made the Second Stellaron's medical response look like a training exercise. Nobody moved for four seconds. Then Dunn said: "EVERYBODY HEAR THAT?!" He said it at full volume. The north wall said it back in echo. And then — three hundred meters away, at the forward line — Karev's voice: "SIGNAL ZERO! FORWARD LINE, IT'S DONE!" And then the outer wall erupted, and the forward line erupted, and the northwest corridors where Sampo had been running routes erupted, and the collection points where the injured were being staged erupted, and the sound that came out of Belobog's defense forces at 0313 on a Thursday morning was not a disciplined acknowledgment and was not a tactical response. It was five hours of maximum mortality danger coming out of three hundred people simultaneously. It bounced off the dome. —— Dunn let himself breathe for exactly twelve seconds. He counted. Not because twelve seconds was a meaningful number but because his body required a specific amount of time to make the recalibration from fighting to stopped, and twelve seconds was apparently what the recalibration took. He breathed, and he felt the rib, and he processed the signal reading, and he looked at the north wall's state — six positions compromised, two officers down and already being moved by the Wildfire team that had reached the wall at 0241, the crystalline formations in the outer sections in various stages of decay — and after twelve seconds he raised the comms. "North wall, hear me," he said. "The signal is zero. The Stellaron is defeated." He let the words sit for a moment. "We have injured. We have positions that need closing. The decay rate on the remaining spawns means we have another forty minutes of active management before the district is clear." He looked at the north wall — at the people on it, Guard officers in varying states of damage and depletion, every one of them still standing who could still stand. "Every one of you was extraordinary tonight. We finish it. Then we rest." A Guard officer three positions to his left — Ivanova, twenty-two years old, who had been cracking spawn shields with her ice expression since the fourth wave and whose left hand was wrapped in a medikit field dressing — said: "Sir." "Ivanova." "Petrov," she said. Not a question. Not a request. The name. He looked at her. "I know," he said. She nodded. She turned back to the wall. She kept her position. He kept his. —— At the forward line, Karev had been running three consecutive days of patrol before the activation and had been fighting for five hours after them and was presently approximately as tired as a human being could be while still functional, which was very tired, and he was also twenty-six years old and furious and glad and grief-struck and none of those things were going away soon so he put them all in his chest and kept moving. "KAREV!" From across the forward line, Ferenz — who had taken a hit from a standard spawn in the seventh wave that had spun him into the junction wall and cracked something in his shoulder. He had not stopped either. "KAREV THE NORTHWEST CLUSTER IS LOSING COHERENCE, WE CAN PUSH—" "PUSH IT!" Karev said. He turned to the twelve officers of the forward line that were still standing. "NORTHWEST CLUSTER IS LOSING COHERENCE — EVERYBODY ON IT! WE'RE CLEARING THIS LINE!" The forward line moved. Twelve officers toward the northwest cluster. Twelve people who had been fighting for five hours and were not done yet, covering ground at the pace of people who were not done yet, which was the only pace available. Karev ran with them. —— Sampo completed his thirty-third corridor trip at 0316. He knew it was his thirty-third because he had been counting. He had been counting because the specific knowledge of how many times he had run this route tonight was the thing he was going to put in whatever report Bronya eventually asked for, and Bronya was eventually going to ask for a report, and Sampo believed in accurate reports. The thirty-third trip delivered three evacuated residents from the southeast residential block's secondary shelter to the western boundary collection point, where the Wildfire triage team received them with the specific efficient warmth of people who had been doing this for five hours and had gotten very good at it. He was standing at the western boundary collection point when the signal reading came through his comms at 0313. He looked at the comms for one second. He looked at the three residents he had just delivered — an older man who had a cut on his forehead from the junction ceiling when the first wave hit, a woman holding a child that was asleep against her shoulder, the child's face completely peaceful in the way only very small children could sleep through catastrophe. He looked at the sleeping child for a moment. Then he said, to the older man: "You're going to be okay." The man looked at him. At the jacket. At the wind-roughed state of someone who had been running for five hours. "Did they—" he started. "Yes," Sampo said. "They did." The man sat down on the collection point bench and put his face in his hands. Sampo looked at the western boundary — at the dome of Belobog, at the outer district beyond the boundary, at the city that was still standing. He thought about Natasha. He thought about where she had been since 2247, which was the trauma surgery center because the trauma surgery center had needed her more than the ward had and Natasha went where she was needed. He thought about the fact that he had a thirty-fourth corridor trip to complete because the northwest route was not fully cleared yet. He went. —— The Ancient Gear Giants began their return to the underworld at 0321. Not all at once — the eight that were still operational returned in sequence, the damaged ones supported by the functional ones in the specific careful logistics that Svarog had built into their operational framework decades before tonight. The fifth unit, which had lost the left arm assembly in the seventh wave at 0019, moved with the sixth unit supporting its left flank. The second unit, which had taken the direct fundamental-force discharge at 0248 that had compromised its chest cavity, moved slower than the others. Svarog's unit — the lead unit, the one he had integrated himself into — was the last to return. He stood it at the outer machine district's main junction entrance for three minutes before descending. The city around him was doing what it was doing at 0321 after a five-hour battle — the sound of it, the specific sound of a place that had been through the hardest thing it had ever faced and was still producing the sounds of a functional city. Generators. Comms traffic. The Guard units on the perimeter. Wildfire medics. The sound of people who had been in maximum mortality danger for five hours starting the careful work of coming back to the ordinary world. He listened to it for three minutes. Then he began the descent. From the main unit's integration chamber, he said: "Well done, Belobog." Not loudly. The city did not need him to say it loudly. He said it the way he said things he meant, which was simply. The Giant descended. —— Natasha finished the last of the trauma cases at 0334. The trauma surgery center had received fourteen cases tonight — six from the forward line, three from the outer wall, four from the northwest corridors, one from the east junction where a Wildfire medic had taken a spawn hit that he had not reported until the battle was over because he had kept treating people until the battle was over and only then sat down. Natasha had been operating since 2318. The surgical team — herself, Dr. Volkov who had been pulled from his scheduled rest shift, two surgical nurses who were excellent — had handled all fourteen. Twelve were stable. Two were not. She stood outside the trauma center at 0334 and she knew which two, and she knew their names, and she was going to sit with that knowledge in a moment. First she needed to breathe. She stood outside and she breathed, and the air of Belobog at 0334 after the First Stellaron's defeat was exactly the same temperature as it had been before, which was cold, which was right, which was Belobog. Her comms produced a message at 0336. It was from Sampo. It said: *Are you all right. I'm finishing the northwest route. Where are you.* She looked at the message for a long moment. She typed: *Trauma center. Come when you're done. I'll be here.* He sent back: *Twenty minutes. Maybe fifteen.* She sat down on the trauma center's exterior steps. The stone was cold. She had been on her feet for five and a half hours and the stone was cold and that was fine. She looked at Belobog. At the dome. At the first light that was starting in the geological dome's upper illumination panels, the pre-dawn cycle starting the way it always started, the way it had been starting since before anyone alive had been born. The city was still here. She thought about that specifically. The city was still here, and she was still here, and Sampo was finishing his last corridor route, and the twelve stable cases inside were going to be okay. And two people who had been at their posts when the tail hit and when the junction ceiling came down were not going to be okay. She sat with both of those things. —— Gepard came out of the Restricted Zone at 0319. He did not come out of the Restricted Zone gracefully. He came out through the secondary monitoring station entrance because that was the nearest exit from the northern section and his legs were doing what legs did after five hours of combat at the level the night had required, which was: still working, but barely, and making the specific case that sitting down was a thing that should happen soon. He came through the entrance and he sat down on the monitoring station bench and he looked at his hands. The gold ice was still there. Not active — the expression at rest produced the ambient gold the way the standard expression had always produced the ambient blue. The Emanator acknowledgment was not a state that ended with the battle. It was what his ice was now. He sat with that for a moment. He was bleeding from six locations, which he had noted earlier and Bronya had also noted and which he was going to address. Not immediately. He looked at the Restricted Zone through the monitoring station's exterior window — at the absence of the First Stellaron, which was not a visible absence, just the geological stillness of the northern section's stone without an eight-hundred-year-old force of destruction sitting in it. He thought about Petrov. He had read the casualty report at 0315 — two confirmed, additional assessments ongoing. Petrov at the north wall in the fifth wave, the tail he hadn't seen coming. Gora in the east corridor in the seventh, going back for Tarkov, the junction ceiling coming down. He sat with that. Bronya came through the monitoring station entrance at 0321. She was still in her form — the claimed-frequency form, the crown and the lances and the cape of ice crystal shards, the prismatic markings on the bodysuit. The form was not something that switched off when the battle ended. She had looked at her hands when the secondary form fell and the form was simply there, the way the golden ice was simply there, the specific permanent thing that was earned rather than borrowed. She sat down beside him on the monitoring station bench. They were both quiet for a moment. "Two," she said. Not as a question. "Petrov and Gora," he said. "Yes." She closed her eyes for a second. She opened them. "I know about Petrov," she said. "I didn't know Gora." "Ninth-year Guard officer," Gepard said. "East corridor section. He—" He stopped. Restarted. "He went back for Tarkov. Tarkov went down in the east corridor when the junction ceiling hit and Gora went back into it." He looked at his hands. "The ceiling came down before he got Tarkov clear." She was quiet for a moment. "Is Tarkov—" "Stable," he said. "In the trauma center. Natasha has him." She nodded. She looked at the Restricted Zone through the window — at the northern section's stone, at the cracks radiating outward from the contact point, the prismatic light of the claimed frequency still present in the cracks even now, the geological expression of what she had done still visible in the stone. "I'm going to go to the Guard families," she said. "As soon as I can. Today, if—" "I'll come with you," he said. She looked at him. At the six bleeding locations he had not addressed. At the state of someone who had been past the ceiling for hours and was sitting on a monitoring station bench. "You need the ward first," she said. "I need to be there when you go to the families," he said. "Those are not exclusive." She looked at him. Then she reached over and put her hand over his — the left hand, the golden ice ambient in the skin, the warmth of Preservation's ice against the cold of the claimed frequency's prismatic ice. "The ward first," she said. "Then the families," he said. "Then the families," she agreed. Neither of them moved for a minute. They sat on the monitoring station bench in the northern section entrance with the gold ice and the prismatic ice and the dawn starting outside and the city making its coming-back-to-normal sounds, and they let the minute be what it was. Then she stood. He stood. She did not take her hand away when they both stood — she moved it to his arm instead, her hand on the inside of his elbow, steady. "Come on," she said. He went. —— Luka was still sitting in the east junction corridor when Seele found him. This was, as promised, where he was — back against the junction wall, chrome arm resting on his knee, the weapon in its compact carry configuration, the dawn light starting to come through the grating above. He heard her footsteps in the corridor twenty meters before she arrived because he had known the sound of her walking since he was nine years old even when she was walking quietly. She came around the junction corner and she looked at him. He looked at her. She was covered in — various things. The engagement outputs of five hours of contact with Fragmentum forms and spawns, which were not pleasant aesthetically but were not his concern, his concern was the fact that she had a cut above her left eyebrow that had been bleeding and had been pressed with something and had mostly stopped and the left shoulder from the tail hit in the ninth wave was carried slightly different from the right. She looked at him. He was covered in similar things and had a gash along the right forearm, the chrome arm's upper section, that he had not noticed until the third wave had ended and which was not in the chrome — that part of him did not bleed — but at the biological connection point at the shoulder where the chrome met the shoulder socket, the impact from the sixth wave's harder spawn had hit the junction hard enough to produce a slow bleed that he had wrapped with his jacket lining. They looked at each other. "Your shoulder," he said. "I know," she said. "Your junction." "I know," he said. She sat down beside him on the floor of the junction corridor, back against the wall, shoulder to shoulder. She tilted her head and it landed on his shoulder, the chrome arm warm where her cheek rested against the upper assembly. He put his arm around her. They sat there for a while. "The moon," he said. Eventually. "The moon," she confirmed. "Still happening." "Still happening," she said. She sounded — not tired in the way of someone who needed to sleep, though she needed to sleep. Tired in the way of someone who had given everything they had and was resting in the specific warmth of having given it all and still being here. "Luka." "Yes." "That was the hardest thing I've ever done," she said. Not with drama. Factually. "I know," he said. "Don't say I know—" "I watched you do it," he said. "I was watching the whole time. You were—" He stopped. He didn't have the word for it immediately. He found it after a moment: "Extraordinary. You were extraordinary. Every single thing you did tonight." She was quiet for a second. "The cannon," she said. "When you loaded up the large burst on the harder spawn in the north face." "The cannon," he agreed. "That was not a casual amount of power," she said. "The situation was not casual," he said. "The cannon scales to the danger level. The danger level was at its maximum at that point." She laughed — real, brief, the unguarded kind that came out when something was both funny and exactly right. "Is that — is that going to keep happening? You just get more dangerous the worse things get?" "That's the mechanism, yes," he said. "Svarog built that deliberately, didn't he." "He did say," Luka said, "when he showed it to me, that he designed it specifically for my personality." She laughed again. He could feel it against his shoulder. He held her a little tighter and she let him and neither of them said anything for a while. The junction corridor was quiet. Above them, through the grating, the dome was starting its dawn sequence — the illumination panels shifting from their night register to the beginning of the morning light. In a few hours the outer machine district was going to be doing the post-battle cleanup that would take days. In a few hours the Guard and Wildfire casualty reports were going to be finalized. In a few hours Bronya was going to address whatever needed addressing and Gepard was going to be in Natasha's ward and Sampo was going to be sitting with Natasha somewhere quiet and Svarog was going to be in his back room reviewing the night's geological logs. In a few hours. Right now: the junction corridor. The warm chrome. The quiet. "Come on," Seele said eventually. "Ward." "Ward," he agreed. He stood and pulled her up and she went without arguing, which told him more about how the shoulder was actually doing than anything she had said about it. —— Natasha had been right about fifteen minutes. Sampo made it to the trauma center exterior in fourteen, which he counted as a win. He was winded in a way that he had not been winded since he was twenty and doing the kinds of things that nineteen-year-olds in the outer machine district did. Thirty-three corridor trips at maximum sustainable pace for five hours, with the addition of the northwest boundary combat operations for the last two hours of it, had produced a state in him that was going to require, he assessed, at least twelve hours of sleep before he was going to be good for anything else. He sat down beside Natasha on the exterior steps. She looked at him. He looked at her. He looked at her hands — at the surgical quality in how she was holding them, the careful stillness of someone who had been operating for hours. "How many?" he said. "Fourteen cases," she said. "Twelve stable." He processed this. He had heard the casualty count. He had heard which two. He sat with the gap between twelve stable and fourteen for a moment. "Volkov was with you?" he said. "Volkov was with me," she said. "He was good tonight." "He's always good," Sampo said. "He just doesn't work well with people who underestimate him." "Nobody underestimated him tonight," she said. He nodded. He looked at the dome. At the pre-dawn. At the city still doing what the city did, which was continuing. "The corridor trips," she said. "Thirty-three," he said. "Thirty-three full trips. Cleared the northwest routes, got forty-two people out of the southeast residential block's secondary shelter to the collection point, and ran comms support for the Guard units in the non-standard routes that Dunn didn't know about." She looked at him. "Forty-two people." "Forty-two," he said. "Including a sleeping child. I like to think the child didn't notice anything was wrong." "The child probably didn't," she said. He was quiet for a moment. Then: "Natasha." "Yes." "I—" He stopped. He tried again. "I have been running routes in this district since I was eleven years old," he said. "I know these corridors the way — the way I know your ward. The way I know the specific pastry you are going to have with your second cup of tea. By feel." He looked at the district. "Tonight I ran them for the city." He paused. "Not for any other reason. Not because there was an angle or a specific operational objective beyond what was obvious. I ran them because forty-two people needed getting out and I was the one who knew where the routes went." She was looking at him. "That is a different thing," he said, "from how I have spent most of my adult life. And I wanted—" He stopped again. Found it. "I wanted to say it to you. Because you are the person I say things to." She moved. Not dramatically — just shifted on the step so she was closer to him, her shoulder against his arm, her head tilting until it rested on his shoulder the way it sometimes did in the ward after long nights. He let her. He put his arm around her and she let him. "You know what Sparkle said," she said. Not a question. "I know what she said," he said. "The path of Elation continues," she said. "Past Belobog." "Yes," he said. "After," she said. "When both Stellarons are—" She caught herself. "When this is resolved. When it's really over." "When it's really over," he said. "She said she'd come back. She said we'd have the conversation." "And you'll go," Natasha said. He was quiet for a long moment. He looked at the city. He thought about the forty-two people, the sleeping child, the thirty-three corridor trips. He thought about Vera and the outer machine district and the specific quality of what it meant to be from somewhere. "Eventually," he said. "Yes. I think so." He looked at her. "But Natasha — the conversation we had. The one where you said you wouldn't ask me to stay still for your sake." "I meant it," she said. "I know you meant it," he said. "I also know—" He stopped. He tried to say the accurate version. "I know that what there is between us is not a small thing. And I know that the path of Elation continuing past Belobog does not have to mean it ends here." He looked at her. "I just don't know the shape of it yet." She was quiet for a while. "I'm also a doctor," she said. "In the outer machine district. My ward is here. My patients are here." She paused. "But I am also—" She found it. "I am also not exclusively those things. The ward does not need me every hour of every day in a way that a person can't be in two places sometimes." He looked at her. "Natasha." "I'm not saying I'll follow you across the galaxy," she said. "I'm saying the shape of it might be larger than you think it is." She looked at the city. "Talk to Sparkle. When she comes back. Have the conversation. And then come tell me what the conversation was." He looked at her. At the specific person who had been his equilibrium since he was fourteen years old and had not known it until he was twenty-eight and was sitting on trauma center steps in the dawn of the morning after the greatest battle in Belobog's history. "You are," he said, "extremely inconvenient to care about." She made a sound that was almost a laugh. "I have been told," she said, "by several patients who did not want to change their habits." He pulled her a little closer. She let him. They sat on the trauma center steps while the dawn happened. —— Clara and the Moles greeted the morning in the workshop. This was not planned. What was planned was: Clara at the notation bench until the signal went to zero, then Svarog's assessment, then sleep. What happened was: the signal went to zero at 0313 and Clara took her hands off the bench and sat on the floor and the Moles sat on the floor around her and Svarog was in the back room reviewing the geological logs and the workshop was warm and nobody particularly wanted to move yet. Toma had her notebook. She had been writing things down all night — not Oleg's annotation style, the broad contextual accounting, the kind of record that put things in their right scale. He was reading back what he had written. "The notification of the activation at 2247," she said. She was reading from the notebook. "Svarog's first reading at 2251. The wing breach of the Restricted Zone at 0007. The—" He looked up. "Do you want me to keep reading, or is that—" "Keep reading," Clara said. He kept reading. Oleg was eating from the jacket, the specific Oleg consumption that was apparently independent of time of day or external events. Pino had his hand on the northwest channel wall, which was where his hand went when he needed to be grounded in something, and was listening with his eyes half-closed. Clara listened to the account of the night in Toma's careful voice and thought about the notation network conducting golden ice and prismatic claimed frequency simultaneously through channels the Architects had built for exactly this. She thought about being twelve years old and holding those channels for seven hours and nineteen minutes. She was going to be thirteen in two months. She thought: I am going to be thirteen and I have already done this. It was not a prideful thought. It was just — accurate. She sat in the accuracy of it, the way she sat in anything that was precisely true. "The fourth Manchester Smash," Toma was saying, reading from the notebook. "At approximately 0259, based on the geological tremor the seismic display registered. The note here says: the fourth one was different. He ran at it." "He did run at it," Oleg confirmed. "I saw it through the grating. He was across the zone in — I don't know how to describe it. Fast." "The third-category movement," Clara said. "Plus whatever the golden ice was doing to his threshold." "It was gold," Oleg said. "His ice was gold." They were all quiet for a moment with that. "I put it in the notebook," Oleg said. "When the gold came on. It was 0257 approximately. The note says: gold. The ice is gold now. That is the most significant thing that has happened in this workshop since the arm, which is the most significant thing that has happened in this district since the cave." Clara looked at him. "When did you start ranking things?" "This notebook," Oleg said, holding up the current volume, "is the third notebook. The first notebook goes back to the first corridor entry with Luka and Seele. I have been ranking things since the contact event in sub-level one." Toma looked at him. Pino looked at him. Clara looked at him. "Oleg," Toma said. "Yes?" "You have been writing things down since the first sub-level entry," Toma said. "That is — that was months before Pela started the historical record." "I know," Oleg said. "Three notebooks," Pino said. "Three notebooks," Oleg said. "The third one is about half full. At the rate events are occurring, it will be full by—" He considered. "Spring."
