She drove the forms back long enough to get the injured Guard officer to the wall junction where the other relay team was positioned. She went back into the corridor for the second injured Guard officer who was still there. The corridor-class forms had regenerated. She went back in. The Guard officer she was going back for was named Tarkov — nineteen years old, the one Maret had held by the collar at the south wall early in the engagement. He had come back from the Wildfire assessment at 0055 and had been back on the line since then. He had taken a foreleg impact from a standard spawn at 0128 and had been moved to the east corridor relay by Maret. Gora reached Tarkov. She began the extraction. The fragment cluster generated a junction-class form. Not corridor-class — junction-class, the cluster having gathered enough activated material to produce something above what the corridor dispersal canister could address. The junction-class form discharged. Gora was between it and Tarkov. She took the discharge. She went down. Tarkov — nineteen years old, injured, in no condition to be doing anything — pulled himself to the wall junction using the strength in his one uninjured arm. He reached the junction. He yelled for the second relay team. They came. They reached Gora. She was not breathing. Tarkov looked at the relay team's faces and understood what he was seeing. He was nineteen years old and had been in the Guard for two years and had not seen this before. He said nothing. He sat against the wall junction. The relay team worked. They did not stop working — Wildfire did not stop working. But at 0141, seventeen minutes after the fragment cluster had first generated, the relay team made the specific notation in the field record that field records were made for. KIA. Wildfire Specialist Gora. 0141. She was twenty-four years old. —— The battle hardened. Not the engagement — the specific quality of everyone in the engagement. The news traveled through the comms in the way bad news traveled through military communications: without announcement, in the specific tonality of people who were processing something and not stopping to process it because stopping was not available. On the north wall, Dunn heard about Gora at 0143. He did not respond on the comms. He looked at his wall section — eleven Guard officers remaining, Petrov at the boundary receiving treatment. He looked at the primary zone. At the fractured light of the Stellaron losing its crown. At the attack golems still hitting. At Gepard, who had risen from his hands on the stone and was back in motion, the ice still running, the Ice Flames quieter but present. He thought about Petrov. He thought about Gora. He thought: they did not die because we were losing. They died because we were fighting. And we are still fighting. And we are going to win. He said, to the north wall: "FOR PETROV. FOR GORA. KEEP GOING." The north wall kept going. On the forward line, Karev heard about Gora at 0144. He had been addressing the last standard spawn of the sixth wave with Ferenz. The spawn had been on its way down — three layers of ice fracturing, Seele already turning toward him to finish it. He completed the coordination, Seele finished it, and then Karev stood in the dissolved spawn's space with the comms active and heard about Gora. He looked at the line. At nineteen people still on their feet — one had gone to the relay at 0130 with a severe arm injury. He said, to the forward line: "WILDFIRE DOWN IN THE EAST CORRIDOR. GORA." He did not elaborate. They needed to know, not to mourn — there would be time to mourn after — but to know. "KEEP THE LINE. EVERY ONE OF US WHO IS STANDING RIGHT NOW — WE STAND FOR HER. WE STAND FOR PETROV. WE ARE NOT DONE." The forward line kept. Seele stood in the position she'd been in when the comms came through. She looked at the primary zone. At the Stellaron — reduced, damaged, losing coherence, but present. Still present. The fury she had felt when the south spawn dissolved was still there. It had not diminished. She thought it had grown, actually — the specific growth of something that was built from legitimate grievance and was being continuously fed by what the Stellaron was doing to the city she had lived in her whole life. She looked at the Stellaron. She thought: you have taken enough from this city. You do not get to take more. —— At 0149, Gepard took his third major impact. He had been addressing the primary mass's remaining coherent sections — not with the Manchester Smash, with the ice that he had built for eight years of Restricted Zone service. The architecture: walls, structures, the geometric constraint that forced the primary mass to address threats in the pattern he needed it to address them. The mass was losing coherence but the sections that remained were fighting back with what they had. What the primary mass had was the activation frequency at whatever output remained after the crown failure and the fracture network and the third Manchester Smash's propagation. The remaining output was forty percent of activation level. Forty percent of activation level was significantly lower than full activation. It was also significantly higher than anything else in Belobog's recent history. He brought the ice wall. Maximum compression. The specific ice construction he had been producing since the Everwinter Hill posting eight years ago, at its highest density. He brought it across the primary mass's discharge vector. The discharge hit the wall. The wall held for two seconds. Then the wall failed — not from the Stellaron's force exceeding the wall's capacity, but from the fracture network. The Ice Flames in the geological structure had propagated through the Restricted Zone's geological foundation and had changed the physical character of the stone. The stone was colder. The ice walls Gepard built were drawing on the geological structure as their medium — the same geological structure that the Ice Flames had transformed. The ice walls were stronger than they had ever been because the stone they were built from was ice-saturated. But the primary mass's remaining discharge had concentrated on the wall's base, the specific point where the ice wall interfaced with the geological structure. The base cracked. The wall tilted. The discharge came through the tilted wall's gap. He was not in the direct line. He was to the left of the wall because he had learned, over ninety-three minutes of this specific battle, that the primary mass's discharge had a specific angular bias — it tended to aim three degrees south of where it appeared to be aimed. He had been using this knowledge for forty minutes. He was three degrees north of the discharge vector when the wall tilted and the discharge came through the gap. The gap output was not the full discharge. But it was enough. He went down. Not fifteen meters this time — thirty-five meters, the gap output greater than the previous two full-shield impacts. He hit the stone on his left side and did not immediately come back up because the left side had taken the impact and the left side was the one that had accumulated impact damage across the engagement. He breathed. He breathed again. From the lead attack golem, Svarog's voice: "GEPARD." "I'm here," he said. "Are you—" "I'm here." He pushed himself up with his right arm. His left arm was not responding in the way it should. He registered this without specific concern because specific concern was not useful and he was not done. He stood on his right arm and his legs and got his left arm sorted out — not broken, the shoulder joint had taken the impact and the joint needed a moment to sort itself out. He gave it a moment. He stood. He looked at the primary mass. The primary mass had lost another four percent of coherence during the engagement of the past eight minutes — the combination of the attack golems' mechanical force, his ice architecture, and Sampo's continuous Wind Shear deterioration was eating at it. The coherence reading — visible in the fracturing of the activation frequency's output, the specific quality of light through a losing crystal structure — was at thirty-one percent of what it had been at full activation. Sixty-nine percent lost. Not done. He looked at the primary mass. He brought the ice. —— At 0153, Svarog triggered the secondary transformation. The defensive golems — the seven remaining standby configuration units that had been holding the zone's perimeter — had been in their defensive stance since the attack golems had been deployed. They had not been doing nothing in the defensive stance: they had been absorbing damage, taking impacts that would have gone into the Restricted Zone's stone or into the fighters instead, their armored chassis doing what they had been built to do. Four of them had taken significant damage. Two were down to forty percent chassis integrity. One was at twenty-five percent. Svarog had been watching the numbers. He had been watching the attack golems' impact record. He had been watching Gepard. He triggered the secondary transformation. The four most intact defensive golems reconfigured. The same transformation that had produced the Phase 2 attack configuration — the chassis reconfiguration, the horned helmet emerging, the hammer-arm deploying. The lightning arc of the gear mechanisms at full output crackling between the joints. Four more attack units. The three damaged defensive golems stayed in their standby configuration because their chassis integrity was not sufficient to survive the transformation sequence without risk of full failure. Eleven attack golems now. The seven from the first transformation, the four from the secondary. Svarog said, from the lead unit: "FULL ADVANCE. ALL ATTACK UNITS. EVERYTHING." Eleven fifteen-meter mechanical giants — horned, hammer-armed, crackling with mechanical lightning from the gear mechanisms at full output — advanced on the primary mass of the First Stellaron simultaneously. This was what forty years of secret construction produced. Twenty-five units built in the deepest levels of the underworld, maintained in secret, calibrated for this specific engagement. Half of them down — the battle had taken nine units across its two hours of running. The fifteen that remained had given everything Svarog had designed them to give. The eleven attack configuration units hit the primary mass from eleven directions. The sound was — geological. Not the Stellaron's geological output, the geological impact of eleven fifteen-meter masses striking a crystalline structure that was already at thirty-one percent coherence, at every remaining coherent section simultaneously. The primary mass contracted again. Not the strategic contraction of the earlier phase — the involuntary contraction of something that was losing structural integrity and pulling itself in as a survival response. Not a choice. A geological reflex. And the contraction exposed something. The primary coherence point — the single remaining junction in the primary mass where the crystalline structure's remaining coherence was organized from — had been deep in the contracted interior since the second Manchester Smash. The involuntary contraction of the latest attack produced a different geometry from the strategic contraction. The primary coherence point was no longer at the center of the contracted mass. It was at the surface. Svarog said: "GEPARD." Gepard was looking at the same thing Svarog was looking at. The primary coherence point, visible at the surface of the contracted primary mass for the first time since the engagement began. He said: "I see it." —— At 0156, two more standard spawns emerged. They came up through the outer machine district's stone — not the Restricted Zone this time, the district itself. The outer machine district's geological structure, which had been saturated with the Ice Flames' propagation through the notation channels, was now the emergence point. The Stellaron, losing coherence rapidly, was producing spawns through any available geological pathway. The forward line had been depleted by the sixth wave's engagement. Seventeen fighters remaining. The seventh wave of standard spawns would normally have been the forward line's responsibility. Luka was closer. He had come back to the primary zone perimeter after addressing the east spawn from the sixth wave. He was at the Restricted Zone's east boundary when the spawns came up through the outer machine district's geological structure fifty meters behind him. He turned. He switched the weapon to its blade configuration. He ran the sandevistan — not the precision operational speed, the full burst. The specific movement that left afterimages. He covered fifty meters in three seconds and hit the first spawn at the primary mass with the blade while the sandevistan was still running, the full burst's speed carrying into the impact. The blade hit the spawn at a force that was the sum of a fifteen-meter chrome-arm enhanced fighter running at maximum sandevistan speed. The primary mass cracked on first contact — not the layered cracking of Karev's ice-build process, the immediate crack of sufficient concentrated force delivered at sufficient speed. He kept moving. The sandevistan burst's window had two more seconds. He switched mid-motion to the lightning whip configuration — the arm extending into the whip, the segmented chrome of the whip cracking across the second spawn's primary surface, the electrical discharge at the tip carving a gap in the crystalline structure. The whip hit. The gap opened. He switched to cannon for one second and drove a small burst into the gap. The second spawn cracked at the gap. He switched back to blade. He had three seconds before the sandevistan needed its recharge window. He used them — the blade back into the first spawn's crack, driving through the fracture, the crack failing into a fracture and the fracture failing into a dissolution. The first spawn dissolved. Two seconds of recharge window. He stood at the second spawn's position, holding the gap open with the cannon's small burst output while the recharge ran. The recharge completed. He hit the second spawn with the blade through the gap. The second spawn dissolved. He stood in the outer machine district with two dissolved spawn positions around him and he looked at the comms. Seven seconds from spawn emergence to both spawns dissolved. He looked at the time. He thought: they're coming faster. The spawns are coming faster because the Stellaron is losing and it's desperate. He thought: if it keeps producing at this rate, the forward line is not going to hold it. His comms: Gepard. "Luka — primary coherence point exposed at the primary mass surface. I need the zone clear and the attack units holding for forty seconds." "What do you need from me?" "Get to the south face. The approach vector from the south is open. When I move, you hit the south face to keep the mass from rotating and closing the coherence point angle." "On my way," Luka said. He ran back into the zone. —— Sampo threw his forty-seventh dagger at 0158. He had been counting because counting was the kind of thing that kept a person grounded when the alternative was looking at how long the battle had been running and how tired his arm was and whether the Wind Shear stacks he had been building on the primary mass's remaining coherent sections were actually making a meaningful contribution to the engagement. They were making a meaningful contribution. He had established this through the specific observation that the coherence sections where his Wind Shear was concentrated were deteriorating faster than the sections that Svarog's golems were hitting mechanically without the shear compound. The Wind Shear was working. He kept throwing. His arm hurt. He had been throwing daggers at maximum velocity for one hour and thirty-seven minutes. The specific muscle groups that the throw required — the shoulder, the forearm supinator, the wrist — were operating on adrenaline and the specific stubbornness of someone who had spent twenty years operating on exactly those two things and knew how long they lasted. Longer than one hour and thirty-seven minutes, it turned out. Good to know. He looked at the primary mass. At the coherence point now exposed at the surface. He looked at where he was standing — south face, fifty meters from the primary mass. He was about to throw the forty-eighth dagger when Gepard's message came through. He lowered his hand. He looked at the primary mass. At the exposed coherence point. At the geometry. He said, on the comms: "Gepard. The south face approach vector — if you're going for the coherence point, the Wind Shear I have on the adjacent surface sections is going to pull the mass's remaining discharge toward the shear points rather than the approach vector. The shear is a threat signal. It will track the shear first." A beat. Gepard: "You're telling me the Wind Shear creates a distraction." "I'm telling you the Wind Shear creates a specific kind of threat priority for whatever coherence remains in the mass. It will track toward the deterioration." He looked at his daggers. "Tell me when you're ready and I'll stack three more waves simultaneously. The tracking won't recover from three simultaneous stack deployments in the time you need." A beat. "Ten seconds," Gepard said. "Ten seconds," Sampo said. He built the throw. Three daggers, fanned spread, each aimed at a different surface section of the primary mass's remaining coherent faces. The specific throw angle that would produce the maximum bounce spread — not one at a time, all three at once, the multi-dagger deployment he had been working toward for two minutes. Gepard: "Now." He threw. —— What happened in the next forty seconds. The three dagger throws hit simultaneously at 0159:03. The Wind Shear built on three surface sections of the primary mass at once — twelve stacks in three seconds across three different coherence face sections. The remaining coherence of the primary mass, which was tracking threat sources across the full perimeter, received twelve new threat signals simultaneously from three directions and oriented toward them. The primary coherence point's facing shifted — not fully, but enough. The south face angle opened. At 0159:05, Luka hit the south face from seventy meters with the cannon at Restricted Zone proximity output — not the large burst, the precision small bursts in the sequence he had used to hold the crack open during the third Manchester Smash. Not to crack the mass. To hold the facing open, to keep the coherence point at the exposed angle, to counteract the mass's natural tendency to reorient as the Wind Shear tracking subsided. Luka said: "FACING HOLDING! GO!" At 0159:07, Gepard moved. He crossed the zone at the third category of movement. Forty meters from his position to the south face. Two and a half seconds. He had the ice in both hands — not the lance, not the architecture, the full compression that produced the Ice Flames at its edge. He had everything that remained. He hit the primary coherence point with both hands. Both hands. Direct contact. Ice and Ice Flames simultaneously into the single remaining junction through which the primary mass organized its remaining coherence. The coherence point received it. The primary mass — the contracted, fractured, sixty-nine-percent-coherence-lost primary mass of the First Stellaron of Belobog — received the full simultaneous ice and Ice Flames impact at its single remaining coherence junction and the junction held for one second and then it did not hold. The coherence failed. Not the spectacular fracture-network failure of the second Manchester Smash. Not the crown-shattering of the third. Something quieter — the specific geological event of a crystalline structure losing the last point of organizational coherence and becoming, without that point, not a coherent structure anymore. Individual crystal components. Distributed. No longer organized by a central coherence. The primary mass of the First Stellaron began to fragment. Not collapse — fragment. The individual crystal components of the primary mass — each one still charged with the activation frequency, each one still hot with the geological energy of eight hundred years of accumulation — losing their organization and beginning to distribute across the Restricted Zone. The activation frequency, which had been fractured since the crown failure and scattered since the third Manchester Smash, became fully incoherent. A thousand crystal components each radiating at their individual frequency, no longer organized into the unified output of the Stellaron's activation. The sound — if the geological failure of a Stellaron's coherence could be called a sound — was felt rather than heard, through the stone of the Restricted Zone and the notation channels in the geological structure and the walls of the outer machine district and the floors of the Landau house and the palace and the outer residential stack. Everyone in Belobog felt it. —— For approximately six minutes, it seemed like it might be enough. Six minutes of the distributed fragments losing coherence — decaying, the activation frequency in each individual fragment dropping as the coherence that had sustained it was gone. Six minutes of Gepard standing at the south face breathing and Luka at the seventy-meter position holding the cannon and Sampo with no more throw target because the mass had distributed. Six minutes of the forward line addressing the last of the sixth wave's spawns and the outer walls watching the light in the Restricted Zone change from fractured coherence to something that was genuinely diminishing. Six minutes of thinking: we did it. At 0205, the fragments of the primary mass drew together. Not all of them — the fragments at the perimeter of the distribution were too far dispersed. But the center mass — the fragments that had been at the interior of the primary mass, the highest-density activation frequency components, the ones closest to the former coherence junction — those fragments began to accumulate. Not the same coherence as before. Not the organized crystalline structure of the Stellaron's primary mass. Something different. Something that the geological coherence of eight hundred years of accumulated force was doing without the specific crystalline organization — finding a new structural relationship. Not the crown, not the primary mass geometry. A different geometry entirely. A secondary form. It was smaller than the primary mass — forty meters rather than one hundred and fifty. It did not have the crown or the wings. It had the specific architecture of something that had shed its outer structure and was operating on its essential interior, the core of what the First Stellaron's geological force actually was when you removed the eight-hundred-year crystalline housing. The core was not pretty. The core was dark — not the blinding prismatic white of the activation frequency but something that absorbed light rather than emitting it, the specific dark of extreme geological compression. At the points where the activation frequency still ran through it, the dark was fissured with lines of raw energy — not the organized prismatic light of the crown but the raw unorganized energy of something that had shed its housing and was now running on its fundamental force alone. It was forty meters and it looked nothing like what it had been. It was also not diminished. If anything, stripped of the housing, it was more concentrated. From the secondary relay, Serval: "THE FORM IS RECONSTITUTING. THE PRIMARY MASS FRAGMENTS ARE FORMING A SECONDARY CONFIGURATION." A pause. "The activation frequency output is lower than the primary form. But the structural density is — it's denser. It doesn't have a fracture network because it doesn't have the same crystal architecture. It's operating on fundamental force without the crystal medium." Svarog, from the lead unit: "I see it." Gepard looked at the secondary form. Forty meters. Dark. Dense. Operating on fundamental force. He said: "Serval. Weak points." "I'm looking," she said. "It's different from anything I've been calibrated for. Give me a minute." "We have a minute?" A pause. "Maybe." —— The seventh wave of spawns came at 0207. The primary mass's fragmentation and reconstitution had produced them — the activation frequency distributed through the fragments, each one trying to produce the spawn response before the reconstitution drew them back to the secondary form. The result was: eight spawns, all standard configuration, no harder-type, emerging from points across the outer machine district's full perimeter. Not the Restricted Zone's perimeter. The outer machine district's full perimeter. Seele was in the zone at the south face when the perimeter reports started coming through the comms. East wall. North wall. South wall. West boundary. All simultaneously. She looked at the comms reports. She looked at the zone. She looked at the secondary form. She made a calculation. "Gepard," she said. "Yeah." "The seventh wave is distributed across the full perimeter. I need to go." A pause. He was looking at the secondary form. At what Serval was trying to analyze. "Go," he said. She went. She went over the zone boundary and into the outer machine district and she moved the way she moved when everything was available to her, which was: not the trajectory physics of someone her size moving at the speed she was moving. The butterfly quality at its maximum deployment, covering distance across the outer district at the speed of someone who had been told by an Aeon that she would not simply end and had believed it. She reached the east wall spawn in forty seconds. She stripped the shield — it was standard type, no shield, she hit the coherence directly. Thirty seconds to dissolve. She went west. Luka left the zone at 0208. He had heard the perimeter reports. He was not a zone fighter alone — he was whatever the situation required. The situation required him outside the zone. He ran the sandevistan at full burst. He reached the south wall spawn in four seconds and hit it with the blade before the burst had fully resolved. The blade hit and the sandevistan's impact carried through — the spawn cracked on first contact. He finished it with the cannon's small burst before the crack could close. He went north. Sampo was already at junction twelve when the northwest spawn came up through the outer district's stone. He had been running his corridors. He had his daggers ready. He hit the northwest spawn with four throws before it had finished emerging from the stone — four throws, twelve stacks of Wind Shear in two seconds, the spawn's surface structure deteriorating immediately. He hit it five more times. The deterioration reached the coherence junction. The spawn collapsed from the surface inward. "NORTHEAST JUNCTION!" a Guard officer on the northeast wall called on the comms. Sampo was already there — he had been running the northeast connection for twenty minutes, he knew exactly which route was fastest. He ran. —— In the Restricted Zone, at 0211, Gepard was alone with the secondary form. Svarog's unit — the lead Ancient Gear Giant, integrated — had gone to the perimeter. Not voluntarily. Three of the remaining attack units had been hit by the secondary form's raw-force discharge as it reconstituted, and one had gone down, and the remaining formation needed to address the perimeter spawns because the secondary form was not something the mechanical units could engage effectively without the crystalline structure to provide impact targets. The secondary form's density meant mechanical impacts were not having the same cracking effect they had on the crystalline primary mass. Svarog had made the calculation and had gone to the perimeter. Ten defensive golems and one attack golem remained in the zone, holding positions at the secondary form's flanks. Not advancing — holding. The attack golem attempted to hit the secondary form at 0209 and its hammer-arm had rebounded from the form's surface, the fundamental force density of the form's core making mechanical impact insufficient. The one thing that was not insufficient was the ice and the Ice Flames. Gepard knew this because the secondary form was tracking him. Not the golems — him. The specific targeting of something that understood what had been doing it damage and was now orienting toward the source. He stood in the Restricted Zone at 0211 with the secondary form oriented toward him and Svarog at the perimeter and Seele and Luka and Sampo all distributed across the outer machine district addressing the seventh wave. Alone. This was not the plan. The plan had not included a secondary form. The plan had been the primary mass fracture, the crown failure, the third Manchester Smash, the coherence junction, and then — what? He had not had a clean picture of what came after the coherence junction because nobody had had a clean picture of what the First Stellaron did when its primary mass coherence failed. Svarog's records from eight hundred years ago described the escalation reaching 4.1 and subsiding. They did not describe the coherence failure of the primary mass or what the fundamental force did when the housing was gone. Now he knew. The fundamental force formed a new housing. Forty meters, dark, dense, fundamental. He looked at the secondary form. The secondary form produced a discharge — not the organized activation frequency, the raw force expression of something that had shed its crystalline organization and was operating on base force. The discharge hit the zone floor between him and the secondary form and the stone did not crack. It fused. The specific geological fusion of stone receiving force at above-geological temperatures. He moved right. The discharge tracked him. He built the ice wall and the discharge hit the wall and the wall held for two seconds — longer than the primary form's discharge had held it, because the secondary form's discharge was lower output than the primary at full activation. But fused stone where the discharge hit the floor instead of cracked stone, which meant the secondary form's output was concentrated rather than dispersed. Concentrated was more dangerous. He moved again. He built another wall, angled. He was doing the thing he had been doing for two hours — the geometry, the forced positioning, the architecture that made the secondary form address threats in the pattern he needed. But without the crystalline structure there was no fracture network to propagate into, no coherence junctions to target, no Ice Flames propagation through the geological foundation because the secondary form was not geological crystal anymore, it was fundamental force. He needed a different approach. He thought about what the Ice Flames were. Not what they did — what they were. The specific product of ice compression at extreme temperature, the thing that existed at the intersection of cold and the force that produced it. The Ice Flames were not simply cold. They were the anti-frequency of the Stellaron's activation energy, the thing that had been disrupting the crystal structure by operating in the same frequency range. The secondary form's fundamental force was not the same as the activation frequency. But it was in the same territory. He thought: the Ice Flames at full output. Not against the crystal structure. Against the fundamental force directly. Not disruption through a medium — direct opposition. He had not tried this because the primary mass had been crystal and the Ice Flames had worked through the crystal medium. The secondary form was not crystal. He looked at the secondary form. He thought: I don't know if this works. He thought: find out. He brought the ice up. —— On the north wall at 0213, something Dunn had not expected happened. There were ten Guard officers left on the north wall. Two had gone to the relay at various points during the engagement. Petrov was being treated at the wall boundary. He had ten officers, depleted, one of them working through a concussion that the concussed officer had been hiding from Lynx's periodic comms check-ins with professional expertise. A spawn came up through the outer machine district's north section at 0213 — a harder-scaled type, the third of that configuration he'd seen in the engagement. His ten officers were not configured to address a harder-scaled type. The harder-scaled type required Seele's two-step, and Seele was at the east wall. He looked at the spawn. He had six years of Restricted Zone service behind him. He had ice — not the architecture that Gepard built, the focused expression of a young officer who had done his whole career in the coldest assignment in Belobog's military structure. He had been building ice in proximity to the Restricted Zone since he was eighteen. He looked at the spawn. He thought: Seele said source warmth strips the shield. Source warmth through the scythe. I don't have Seele's warmth and I don't have the scythe. He thought: the Ice Flames are a different frequency from ice. The Stellaron's shield layer responds to rebirth frequency. The Ice Flames are in the same frequency territory as the activation energy. He thought: I don't have Ice Flames. He thought: but I have ice at the compression level I've been building for six years. He threw everything he had at the shield layer. Not the lance — the maximum compression he could produce, which was not Gepard's compression but was the specific maximum of six years of Restricted Zone ice practice, concentrated on a point. He hit the shield layer with it. The shield layer cracked. Not dissolved — cracked. Enough. He said: "EVERYONE — THE CRACK IN THE SHIELD! HIT THE CRACK! EVERY ICE EXPRESSION YOU HAVE, ON THE CRACK!" Nine officers hit the crack simultaneously. Nine ice expressions at various levels of capacity, all concentrated on the three-centimeter crack Dunn had made in the shield layer. The shield layer failed. The coherence junctions underneath were exposed. Dunn hit the primary coherence junction with everything he had left. The junction cracked. Not dissolved — cracked. He said: "IS THERE A WILDFIRE SPECIALIST ON THIS WALL RIGHT NOW?" A voice: "MEDICAL SPECIALIST MARET — YES!" "NOT MEDICAL! FRAGMENTUM RESPONSE — DO YOU HAVE THE DISPERSAL?" "YES!" "GET IT INTO THE CRACK NOW! MAXIMUM OUTPUT!" The Wildfire dispersal canister was not a weapon. It was a medical response tool for corridor-class Fragmentum density in evacuation scenarios. It was not rated for spawn-class engagement. It was also what was available. Maret hit the exposed coherence junction with the dispersal canister at maximum output. The dispersal agent interacted with the cracked coherence junction in ways that nobody had predicted because nobody had tried this, and the interaction was not clean and was not by the book and produced the specific result of a junction that was already cracked receiving a destabilizing agent at maximum output. The junction failed. The spawn lost its coherence and dissolved — not in the smooth rebirth-pulse dissolution that Seele produced, a messy geological failure, individual crystal components scattering across the north wall's outer section. But dissolved. Dunn looked at what had happened. He looked at the nine officers. At Maret. At the dissolved spawn. He said, very quietly: "Do it again if you see another one." They did it again. —— Seele reached the northwest boundary at 0219. The seventh wave was down — eight spawns, all addressed. Luka had taken two, she had taken three, Sampo had taken two, the Guard units in coordination with what Dunn's north wall had figured out had taken one. Eight spawns. Down. She stood at the northwest boundary and she breathed. She could feel the cracked ribs from the tail impact at 0057. She could feel the butterfly quality — not diminished, she hadn't thought it could be diminished, it turned out it could be diminished. It was running lower than it had been at its peak. Not gone. Lower. She looked at the comms. At the zone status. At the secondary form. Gepard was alone in the zone with the secondary form. She could see the light from the zone — not the activation frequency, something different. The specific blue-white of the Ice Flames against the dark density of the secondary form's fundamental force. Two opposing expressions of the same force, directly engaging each other in the Restricted Zone of Belobog. She started moving toward the zone. Her comms: "Seele." Gepard. She stopped. "Stay out," he said. "Gepard—" "Stay out." His voice was — not exhausted. Something more specific. The voice of someone who has looked at the situation and made a decision that they are not going to defend to anyone because defending it would take time they don't have. "The secondary form is tracking fundamental force. If I have additional sources in the zone it will distribute its tracking and I need its full attention on me." She stood at the zone boundary. She said: "How are you doing in there?" A pause. "Working on it." She looked at the zone. At the blue-white and the dark. At the forty meters of secondary form and the one person standing in front of it. "Gepard," she said. "Yeah." "We're here," she said. "All of us. We're right here." A pause. "I know," he said. And then he was back in it, the comms going to the perimeter updates, and she stood at the zone boundary with Luka appearing beside her — she hadn't heard him arrive, he was just there — and they watched. —— In the Restricted Zone, at 0223, Gepard learned something. The Ice Flames against the fundamental force directly were not doing what the Ice Flames through the geological foundation had done to the crystalline structure. They were not propagating, not disrupting through a medium. They were opposing — two forces in the same frequency territory meeting at the point of contact and neither giving way. He could not hold that opposition indefinitely. He could hold it for the time he held it, which was the time he had, which was not nothing. What the direct opposition was doing — the thing he hadn't expected — was heating the fundamental force at the contact point. Not warming. Heating in the specific thermodynamic sense of energy transfer at a temperature differential. The Ice Flames' temperature was below the fundamental force's temperature. The temperature differential produced heat flow from the fundamental force to the Ice Flames, which was exactly what thermodynamics predicted and which was also exactly what he needed because the fundamental force losing heat was the fundamental force losing energy. Not fast. Slower than the fracture network had propagated through the crystal structure. But happening. He was holding the Ice Flames in direct opposition with the secondary form's fundamental force and the secondary form was losing energy to the exchange. The secondary form noticed. The discharge changed character — not the raw force expression it had been using, the specific adaptation of something that had been engaged in energy exchange and was trying to stop the exchange. The discharge was now aimed at breaking his contact rather than damaging him through it. He moved left. He rebuilt contact. He was reading the discharge angle from the secondary form the same way he had been reading the primary mass's discharge angle — the same three-degree bias, maintained in the secondary form because it had been the primary mass and carried the same directional character. He moved three degrees north of the discharge. It hit to his right. He rebuilt contact. He had been doing this for twelve minutes. His left shoulder from the 0149 impact was sending specific signals about its opinion of this. He received the signals and noted them and continued. At 0231, the eighth wave of spawns came. Six of them. Two harder-scaled. On the comms, the perimeter reports came in. Luka, at the zone boundary, acknowledged immediately: "Taking east and north." Seele: "South and west." Sampo: "Northwest and the secondary ring if anything comes through." They went. He was still alone in the zone. —— At 0241, things got worse. The secondary form, which had been losing energy to the Ice Flames contact exchange, responded to the energy loss by concentrating. Further concentration — the forty meters of fundamental force pulling inward, not to protect itself but to increase the force density at the contact points. More dense. More resistant to the energy exchange. And at higher density, the discharge was more concentrated. The discharge hit him at 0242. He had the ice at the contact point and the shield and the discharge hit the shield and he went down. He went down and for a moment — not a long moment, two seconds — he could not tell which direction was up. He sorted this out. He got up. He rebuilt contact. He was — he was running on what was left. Not a quantifiable amount. Just: what was left. The specific not-nothing of someone who had given everything that could be given and found that there was still more giving that needed to be done. The Ice Flames. Still there. Lower than they had been. The temperature differential that was producing the energy exchange was lower — the secondary form's concentration meant higher density meant higher temperature meant smaller differential. The exchange was slowing. He looked at the secondary form. He thought: I need to break its concentration. He thought: the only thing that broke the primary mass's concentration was enough force. He thought: the fourth Manchester Smash. He had not planned a fourth Manchester Smash because he had not thought there would be a secondary form. He had not planned a fourth Manchester Smash because at the point where the third Manchester Smash had completed he had been at the position where he was on his hands on the stone and the Ice Flames were ebbing and two more Manchester Smashes had been the accounting he had been working with. There was a secondary form. He looked at what remained. The ice, still there. The Ice Flames at their lower level, still there. The secondary form, forty meters, concentrated, losing energy slowly through the exchange he had been maintaining for thirty minutes. He thought: I cannot stand here and deploy it. The primary mass's surface provided the geological contact that made the Manchester Smash into what it was — the standing still with both hands on the surface and the Ice Flames propagating through the geological network. The secondary form was fundamental force. There was no crystal surface to push through. There was no geological network to propagate through because the secondary form had shed the crystal medium. The only way to get the Ice Flames into the secondary form's fundamental force was direct impact. Not standing still. Running. He thought about this for approximately half a second. He looked at the secondary form. He thought about Petrov on the Restricted Zone floor. About Gora in the east corridor. About Dunn on the north wall with ten officers and no Seele, making something work anyway. About Clara at the notation bench eight hours without moving. About Bronya at the contact point two and a half hours in the full activation frequency without stopping. About Belobog. He ran. —— The full burst speed that the First Class Singularity of the Silvermane Guard could produce was not a quantifiable number. What it was: the accumulated output of eight years of Restricted Zone service and one year of the northern ridge's absolute edge, directed entirely toward forward movement across twenty-five meters of Restricted Zone floor, with the ice in both hands and the Ice Flames at the surface of everything he had, built not as architecture or lance or wall but as impact — the full expression of everything he was and everything he had given to this battle and everything he was not going to stop giving until the city was safe. He crossed twenty-five meters. He hit the secondary form with both hands. Not the standing-still contact of the previous Manchester Smashes. An impact — the specific physical fact of everything in motion hitting everything in front of it, the ice and the Ice Flames and the force of twenty-five meters of acceleration all delivered at the single contact point of his fists meeting the secondary form's fundamental force. The contact was — not the geological propagation of the previous Smashes. Something more immediate. The Ice Flames did not propagate into the geological network because there was no geological network. They entered the secondary form directly at the impact point, carried by the physical impact of the contact, and met the fundamental force at the point where there was no crystal medium between them. The fundamental force at the impact point failed. Not immediately — over three seconds, the Ice Flames and the fundamental force in direct contact at the impact point, the temperature differential producing the energy exchange at the highest rate yet because the distance was zero. The exchange consumed the fundamental force at the impact point completely. And the secondary form, which had been concentrated to resist the energy exchange, had concentrated its force toward the center — which meant the impact point at the perimeter was the lowest-density point. The perimeter cracked. The crack in the secondary form's perimeter, at the impact point, propagated inward — not the fracture network of the crystal structure but the crack of fundamental force failing at its perimeter when the perimeter was hit with enough opposing force directly. The crack reached the center. Gepard was on his knees on the Restricted Zone floor with both hands on the stone and the rebound from the impact still conducting through his arms. He was — the accounting that had no numbers now. He had nothing to measure against. He was at the point where what remained was not a quantity but a fact: he was still here. Still moving. Still on his knees with his hands on the stone. He looked at the secondary form. The crack from the impact was propagating. Slower than the geological fracture network. But propagating. The secondary form was concentrating harder — not an aggressive response, a survival response. Pulling the fundamental force inward, away from the crack, trying to close it by density. He had hit it with everything. He had nothing left to hit it with. He had the ice. He still had the ice, because the ice was not a finite resource in the way he had been treating it for the whole engagement — the ice was what he was, built over eight years in Jarilo-VI's coldest posting, and what he was did not run out. He brought the ice. Not the Flames — the ice. The maximum compression he had been producing since Everwinter Hill, the version he had learned on the northern ridge was higher than he had thought, the ice at the full extent of eight years of practice and one year of absolute pressure. He drove it into the crack. The ice followed the crack inward. Not the Ice Flames — the ice was the medium now, the crack was the path, and the ice at maximum compression was the substance that entered the fundamental force's interior through the path. The interior of the secondary form received the ice. The ice at maximum compression in the interior of a fundamental force structure that had been losing energy through the surface exchange for thirty minutes was: the final edge. The specific edge where the fundamental force, already depleted, already cracked, already losing, met the thing that had been opposing it for three hours and felt the full weight of what three hours of Belobog's resistance had accumulated. The secondary form's crack expanded from the interior outward. It was failing. Gepard was on his knees with both hands on the stone and the ice running through the crack into the interior of a dying Stellaron and he was — the fact of still being here. Nothing more and nothing less. The simple fact. He breathed. The crack was expanding. The secondary form was failing. He breathed again. He was still here. He looked at his hands on the stone. At the ice conducting through the crack. At the Restricted Zone around him — at the golems holding the perimeter positions, at the battle that had been running for three and a half hours, at the city outside the zone's walls. He had given everything. He had given more than everything. He was on his hands and knees on the stone at the absolute bottom of what was possible and the city was still there behind him and the Stellaron's secondary form was cracking from the inside outward and he was still here. He thought: I am not done. He pushed himself up. One knee. Two knees. Both feet. Standing. He looked at the secondary form. He looked at what remained in him — not measuring, just looking. The ice. Still there. The Ice Flames. Still there, so quiet now that they were almost nothing, but there, the specific warmth at the edge of the cold that had been there since the northern ridge and was not going to leave because it was not a resource, it was part of what he was. He raised both hands. The ice came up. The Ice Flames came with it. He was going to take one more step. And at the moment he took the step — at the moment the First Class Singularity of Jarilo-VI, twenty-five years old, at the absolute bottom of everything he had and still moving, took one more step toward the failing secondary form of the First Stellaron with the ice and the Ice Flames in both hands and the will to protect his city not one degree diminished — Something looked at Jarilo-VI. —— It was not light. It was not sound. It was not any physical expression that the geological record could record or the notation network could carry or the frequency analysis equipment could detect. It was presence. The specific, overwhelming, total presence of something at the highest level of the universe looking directly at a specific point on a specific planet at a specific moment. The presence of Qlipoth, the Amber Lord, the Aeon of Preservation — the foundational force whose existence was the reason the Silvermane Guard existed, the reason the title First Class Singularity meant what it meant, the reason the specific role of standing between a city and the things that wanted to destroy it had been given a name and a rank and a tradition. Everyone on Jarilo-VI felt it. Not everyone understood what they felt. Most of the people in the residential safe rooms felt it as a quality of the air changing — a settling, a specific quality of calm that had not been in the air a moment before and was now undeniably present. The Guard units on the outer walls felt it as something behind them, in the sense that what was behind them was the city, and the city was in that moment more present and more worth protecting than it had been in any previous moment of their careers. The Wildfire medic treating Petrov at the zone boundary felt it as the sudden certainty that what they were doing mattered. Not the operational certainty of someone doing their job. The absolute, foundational certainty of someone who has been looked at by the thing that cares about what they are doing and found that the caring is real. Clara felt it through the notation network as a frequency that no Fragmentum response or Stellaron activation had produced, running through the channels the Architects had built. The specific frequency of Preservation itself, expressed through the geological structure of a planet that had just been deemed worth preserving by the fundamental force that made preservation possible. She said, very quietly, to the workshop: "Oh." Svarog, at the perimeter of the Restricted Zone, felt it through the interface of the lead unit's sensor array, and what it registered as to four centuries of accumulated consciousness was: recognition. The specific recognition of something the Aeon of Preservation had been watching for and had finally found. He brought the lead unit to full stop. He looked at the Restricted Zone. In the Restricted Zone, Gepard felt it as — himself. Not his external self, not the First Class Singularity of the Silvermane Guard, not the strongest person on Jarilo-VI. The most internal version of himself, the version that had been running on the wall since he was eighteen and had never been able to fully articulate why. The version that would stand in front of a city for as long as the city needed standing in front of. That version of him felt seen. Not evaluated. Not assessed. Seen — the specific experience of being looked at by something that understood completely what you were and found it worth preserving. The gaze lasted one second. In that second, Gepard felt the specific change — not power added from outside, power recognized from within, the specific recognition of what his ice and his Ice Flames were and what they became when the Aeon of Preservation acknowledged that the person producing them was doing the specific work that Preservation existed to protect. The Ice Flames changed. Not brighter — deeper. The specific quality of something that had been operating in one register and was now operating in its full register, the register it had always belonged to and had not previously had access to. Not more of what they had been. What they had always been, fully. The gaze ended. Gepard looked at his hands. The Ice Flames looked back. —— END OF CHAPTER 55 Her Shield, His Sun Chapter 54: The City That Will Not Fall The second Manchester Smash hit the Restricted Zone at 0031 and the city felt it. Not felt it the way you felt a generator cycling through its night rotation — felt it the way you felt an explosion three streets away, in your chest before your ears registered it, the specific physical fact of something enormous happening in your immediate world. The dome's inner surface vibrated. Windows cracked in the second residential ring — not from the Stellaron's frequency this time, from the impact of the Smash itself propagating upward through the geological foundation at full output. In the northwest corridor where Sampo was still moving people, the floor shook hard enough that two children fell. He caught one. A Guard officer beside him caught the other. They looked at each other over the heads of two kids who were too scared to cry. "That's ours," Sampo said. "How do you know?" the Guard officer said. "Different quality from the Stellaron's output." He set the child down and kept moving. "The Stellaron wants to destroy. That was someone making something." He looked at the ceiling, at the light coming through the junction vent from the direction of the Restricted Zone. "Gepard." The Guard officer looked at the light for a moment. Then he looked at the three families still in the corridor who needed to be in the inner section. "Move," the Guard officer said. They moved. —— In the Restricted Zone, the second Manchester Smash's aftermath lasted approximately forty seconds before the Stellaron responded. It responded badly. The fracture network expanding to cover six stress points simultaneously had not disabled the form. It had enraged it. The activation frequency, which had been dropping toward sixty percent output as the fracture network propagated, spiked back. Not to the original activation level — to something above it. The specific thing that happened when a force that had been building for eight hundred years was structurally damaged and decided that the correct response to structural damage was to produce more force. The light from the Restricted Zone became blinding even from the outer district boundary. Residents who had been watching from windows inside the residential barrier had to look away. Guard units on the outer walls that had been facing the zone turned their faces for two full seconds. Svarog, in the lead Ancient Gear Giant, said: "FORMATION — ALL UNITS SHIELDS! NOW!" The twelve remaining standard-configuration units — the defensive golems, the standby form — rotated their chassis and brought the armored facing toward the Stellaron's primary mass. The three-centimeter steel of the old-world chassis was not going to hold an activated First Stellaron discharge for long. But it was going to hold it for long enough. The discharge hit. Three units took the full focused output and went down — chassis cracked through, internal mechanisms destroyed, the units falling backward onto the Restricted Zone floor. The nine remaining held. Badly — armor buckling, the gear mechanisms at their joints screaming under the kinetic transfer — but held. And then Svarog triggered the transformation. —— He had built both kinds. The defensive golems had been the ones everyone saw — fifteen meters of armored standby chassis, built to absorb impact and hold the line. He had brought twelve of them up in the first wave, alongside the defensive-configured units. The remaining eight had been hanging back. Configured differently. He had not shown them yet because he had been waiting for the moment when the battle required the other thing — not holding, hitting. The transformation was not subtle. The eight hanging units reconfigured from their standard chassis into something that had not been seen in the outer machine district, or anywhere else on Jarilo-VI, because nothing like this had existed on Jarilo-VI before Svarog built it. The standby configuration's armored facing retracted. The internal mechanisms exposed — not as damage, as design. The gear systems at the joints, which in the standby configuration had been covered by the armor, became the frame itself. Larger. The horned helmet section emerged from the chassis as the primary head structure — reinforced, built for impact rather than protection. The right arm, which in the standby configuration had been a flat-faced armored limb, was now something that had a weapon on it — not a cannon, a weapon that was part of the arm itself, a massive mechanical hammer-equivalent built from the same old-world components as the chassis and scaled to the size of a fifteen-meter unit. The left arm stayed as a fist. A fist built for a fifteen-meter chassis to swing with the full mechanical force of old-world engineering. Lightning — the specific crackle of static discharge built up by the gear mechanisms running at full operational rate — arced between the joints of the transformed units. Not a weapon. Just physics. The gear systems at full output produced that kind of charge differential and Svarog had decided that containing it would be inefficient, so he hadn't bothered. Eight transformed attack golems, crackling with mechanical lightning, facing the First Stellaron. Svarog's voice, from the lead unit, at full external speaker volume: "ADVANCE." They advanced. —— The First Stellaron had never faced anything like this. Eight hundred years in the bedrock of Jarilo-VI had exposed it to the geological record of everything that had existed on the surface above it. It had felt the pre-Separation civilization. It had felt the Separation event. It had felt the city of Belobog being built. It had felt the Second Stellaron's activation and defeat. It had never felt twenty-three mechanical giants — thirteen defensive, eight transformed attack configuration — advancing across the Restricted Zone to fight it. The two foremost attack golems reached the Stellaron's lower crystalline structure at 0038 and hit it. Not the measured force of Gepard's ice lance, targeted at specific fracture points. The raw mechanical impact of fifteen-meter fists and hammer-arms swinging with every kilogram of old-world chassis behind them. The attack golems had no finesse. They had not been built for finesse. They had been built to do what Svarog had needed something to do for forty years, which was to give the First Stellaron's crystalline structure something it could not simply discharge away — mass. Physical, geological mass, delivered at the points where the fracture network had already weakened the coherence. The first impact hit the crown fracture. The crown fracture, which the Manchester Smash had expanded to twenty-seven percent of the crown's total mass, received the mechanical impact of a fifteen-meter hammer-arm at full chassis momentum. The fracture did not expand cleanly from this. It splintered — three new fracture lines radiating outward from the impact point, the crystalline structure failing in multiple directions simultaneously. The second impact hit the primary mass at the north face's stress boundary. The third at the east face. The Stellaron screamed again. The specific output of something that was not simply being engaged but was being struck. The sound carried three kilometers in every direction from the Restricted Zone and everyone in Belobog who was awake — which was everyone in Belobog — heard it. Dunn, at the north wall, heard it. He looked at the zone. At the mechanical giants swinging at the First Stellaron. At the light of the activation frequency flickering around the impact points. He said, very quietly, to nobody in particular: "Hit it harder." —— On the forward engagement line, Seele heard the Stellaron scream and felt something that she did not immediately name. She would name it later. The word was: good. She was at the south boundary, dealing with the latest wave of standard spawns — the original type, thirty meters of dark-crystal with the red-orange fissure lines. The third wave of them. The first wave had been four. The second had been four. This one was six, and they had come up from different points simultaneously rather than from a single breach. Her cohort had expanded since the battle started. The forward line had initially been twelve fighters. The twelve Guard officers and Wildfire specialists who had been selected for the forward position before the activation. As the battle had gone on, and as the Fragmentum response had proven harder than the secondary positions could handle without additional support, the contingency plan had activated: eight more Guard officers from the secondary perimeter, pulled to reinforce the forward line. Twenty fighters total. Against six thirty-meter spawns plus the ongoing Fragmentum response. It was not enough to be comfortable. It was exactly enough to be possible. Ivanova — the Guard officer who had been beside her from the beginning — was twenty meters left, addressing the nearest spawn's left foreleg with the ice lance she could produce at her capacity. Not Gepard-level ice. Not even close. The ice that a Guard officer trained on Jarilo-VI could produce was the specific everyday ice of someone who had spent twenty years in proximity to the planet's coldest zones and had developed the ability from environmental exposure and practice and the particular stubbornness of people who lived in Belobog. It was not going to crack a spawn's primary structure. It was going to hold the spawn's foreleg in place long enough for Seele to address the primary structure. Ivanova held the foreleg. Seele went vertical. Thirty meters up, above the spawn's tracking range, the stigmata field extending outward in the same pattern as the south spawn that had dissolved in the previous wave. Eight marks this time rather than twelve — she was learning the most efficient distribution for this specific spawn configuration. Eight marks at the primary mass's key coherence junctions. She drove the scythe down. The rebirth pulse detonated at all eight marks simultaneously. The spawn dissolved. Faster than the previous ones — she was getting better at finding the coherence junctions. She landed. She looked at the next one. "IVANOVA! TWO O'CLOCK!" "I SEE IT!" Ivanova was already moving. On the east side of the forward line, a Guard officer named Karev — thirty years old, the longest-tenured Guard officer on the forward rotation, someone who had been in the Restricted Zone when the Second Stellaron manifested and had held his position then too — was engaging a spawn with two things simultaneously: his ice expression, which was stronger than Ivanova's from longer practice, and his service sidearm, which was not effective against crystalline Stellaron spawns but was something he did as habit when his ice was building charge between uses. He'd been fighting for ninety minutes. He was still fighting. The spawn came at him and he brought the ice wall — not Gepard's sixteen-meter architecture, a four-meter barrier, the most he could produce, angled to deflect the spawn's foreleg impact rather than stop it. The impact hit the barrier at the angle he'd planned and deflected left. The spawn stumbled. In the stumble's opening, Karev drove the concentrated ice lance he'd been building for thirty seconds directly into the spawn's exposed primary mass at the lateral face. The lance hit the crystalline structure and cracked it. Not dissolved it. Cracked it. He looked at the crack. He looked at the spawn. "FERENZ!" he shouted to his partner fifteen meters away. "WEST FACE — CRACK AT THE PRIMARY! HIT IT NOW!" Ferenz, a twenty-six-year-old Guard officer with ice capacity approximately equal to Karev's, hit the crack with her own lance. The crack became a fracture. The spawn shuddered. It didn't dissolve — they weren't Seele, they couldn't dissolve things — but it staggered, the crystalline coherence disrupted at the primary mass, the form's movement becoming unstable. Karev looked at the forward line. He looked at Seele, who was twenty meters away and currently between him and the third spawn. "SEELE!" he shouted. She saw the staggered spawn. She saw the fracture at the primary mass. She went. She didn't use the stigmata field for this one. She drove the scythe directly into the fracture — the existing crack in the crystalline structure — and the rebirth energy at the blade's edge entered the fracture and propagated along it. The spawn dissolved at the fracture line. Both halves. Karev stared at the two dissolving halves for one second. "THAT'S HOW WE DO IT!" he said, to Ferenz, to the forward line, to anyone who could hear him. "CRACK IT FIRST! GET HER TO FINISH IT!" The forward line adapted in real time. Not a plan — an understanding, distributed through twenty fighters who were all smart enough and experienced enough to recognize a tactic that worked. You crack it. She finishes it. The combination of Guard ice capacity and Herrscher of Rebirth stigmata produced results that neither could achieve alone. The sixth spawn went down in four minutes. Seele was breathing hard at the end of it — the stigmata field at the deployment rate this engagement required was not free, and this was the third wave of spawns she'd addressed in ninety minutes in addition to the Fragmentum response work. She breathed. She looked at the Restricted Zone. At the light and the mechanical giants and the Stellaron screaming at 0041 as the attack golems kept hitting. "Everyone all right?" she said, to the line. "DOWN!" someone called — a Guard officer she didn't know, on the north side of the forward line, pointing at a colleague on the ground. Wildfire medic there in fifteen seconds. The Guard officer was up in sixty. Cracked ribs, functional. "KEEP THE LINE!" Karev's voice. Not shouting. The specific carrying voice of someone who'd been doing this long enough to know that the volume that reached everyone wasn't the volume that destroyed your voice. The line kept. —— In the northwest corridors, Sampo ran out of people to move at 0045. Not ran out of people — the corridor section he was responsible for was clear. Everyone who had been in his section was in the inner sections or the medically staffed safe rooms. The Wildfire teams were in position. The Guard detachments were at their corridor junctions. He stood at junction nine with nothing left to do in his designated role. He looked at his hands. He was Sampo Koski, intergalactic sometime-salesman, twenty-eight years old, born and raised in the outer machine district of Belobog in the Underworld where the generators hummed and the lights were warm even when the rest of the city was cold. He had grown up in a place where if you didn't know how to handle yourself in a fight you didn't grow up for very long. He had spent twenty years developing skills that looked, from the outside, like charm and manipulation and the ability to vanish when situations became inconvenient. From the inside, they looked like daggers. He had been keeping this to himself for twenty years not because he was ashamed of it but because the situation that required it had not yet arrived. The situation was here. He reached into his jacket. Not for a pastry or a map or a calibration chip. He reached past all of those things — past the comfortable layer of the jacket's ordinary contents — to the thing that was always there and had never been relevant until now. The specific pocket that held the three daggers he had been carrying since he was sixteen years old. Weighted correctly. Balanced for his throw. He looked at junction nine. He looked at the Fragmentum formation that had been generating there for the past four minutes, which had been growing while he was moving people and which was now, with the last of the civilians clear, nobody's problem but his. He looked at the formation. He said, to the formation: "Hello." He threw the first dagger. It hit the formation at the base junction — the point he'd identified thirty seconds ago because he had been looking at it for thirty seconds, because Sampo Koski had the specific quality of someone who spent every moment of his life in proximity to threats reading exactly where to hit them. The dagger found the junction and the junction cracked. The formation disintegrated. He caught the dagger on the ricochet — the technique he'd been practicing since he was sixteen and had never had the occasion to show anyone, the specific bounce-and-catch that let him chain throws without losing the weapon. Three more formations at junction nine. He threw. Ricocheted. Caught. Threw again. The wind that the daggers trailed behind them — the specific wind of something moving at the velocity he could throw — carried the quality that he had not known was a power until it started expressing itself in his late twenties, the ambient quality of the path of Elation manifesting in the medium he was most comfortable with. His daggers didn't just impact. They lacerated — the wind shear of their passage leaving structured cuts in the Fragmentum crystallization before the physical impact even landed. The formation quality of anything he hit deteriorated before and during and after the strike. The junction nine section was clear in four minutes. He stood in the empty corridor. He thought: that took too long. I need to be faster. He thought: junction twelve is next, if the perimeter data is correct. He went to junction twelve. —— At 0052, the Stellaron produced the harder spawns for the first time. There had been no warning of the change. The previous spawns — the dark-crystal thirty-meter forms with the red-orange fissure lines — had come up through the Restricted Zone's cracks as the form escalated. They had been addressable. Hard, dangerous, costly in terms of time and output from Seele and the forward line's ice capacity. But addressable. The harder spawn came up through the Restricted Zone's southeast face and it was not the same thing. It was the same size — thirty meters. It had the same crystalline body architecture. But where the previous spawns had been dark crystal with fissure lines of passive energy, this one was something different. The crystal structure was almost organic in its arrangement — not the blocky geometric mass of the previous type, curved, layered, the individual crystalline components overlapping like scales. And the energy in the fissure lines was not passive. It was building. Cycling. The specific quality of something that was not just powered but was actively charging. Seele saw it emerge and said, loudly, to no one in particular: "That's new." It oriented on her immediately. The previous spawns had oriented on heat signatures and opposing frequency. This one oriented on the rebirth energy — on the specific frequency of the Herrscher of Rebirth stigmata field, which had been deployed seventeen times in the past ninety minutes and had left a specific frequency signature in the air around Seele's position that this spawn could read. It had been made to address her specifically. "EVERYBODY BACK!" she called. "THIS ONE'S DIFFERENT — BACK FROM ME, NOW!" The forward line backed twenty meters. She didn't. She looked at the spawn. It looked at her. She deployed the stigmata field. The marks hit the crystalline structure and found — nothing. The coherence junctions she was reading weren't coherence junctions. The crystalline structure was arranged to look like it had the standard coherence architecture of the previous spawns, and it did have that architecture underneath, but over it was a secondary layer of crystalline formation that was not coherence but shield. The stigmata marks hit the shield layer and dissipated. The spawn charged. She went vertical. It followed. That was new. The previous spawns had been ground-bound — they were geological formations, heavy, capable of reaching but not of following vertical movement. This one tracked her upward with a speed that she had not anticipated, the curved crystalline body moving differently from the blocky mass of the previous type. It hit her at thirty-five meters. Not a foreleg impact — the tail, which she had not registered as a significant threat because the previous spawns hadn't had tails that moved with intention. The tail hit her at the shoulder and the impact threw her sideways and the butterfly quality absorbed most of the kinetic transfer but most was not all and she came down twenty meters from her previous position and landed hard. She landed and immediately rolled — the specific habit of someone who had been in sub-level three with things hitting her for twenty-three hours. She came up. She looked at the spawn. It was already repositioning for another hit. She thought: the shield layer is over the coherence junctions. The shield layer has to come off first. How? She thought: the Ice Flames disrupted the Stellaron's crystalline structure. Gepard's ice works on crystal. What do I have that— She thought: oh. The old-gold warmth from the Fuli connection — the source energy that the cave had given her, that ran through her hands when she pressed them to the notation surface, that conducted through the scythe's blade when she drove it into forms. It wasn't just stigmata energy. It was a different frequency than the stigmata field. The stigmata was the rebirth pulse. The warmth was the remembrance frequency. Different frequency. Different target. She pressed both hands to the ground — not the stigmata field, the warmth. The source energy from the notation network running through the geological structure of the outer district, conducting into her palms through the contact with the stone. The old-gold warmth came up through her hands and she directed it through the scythe's blade as she stood and she hit the spawn not at the coherence junctions but at the shield layer directly. The shield layer received the remembrance frequency. It dissolved. Not the spawn — the shield. The secondary crystalline layer over the coherence architecture, which had been built from the same geological material as everything the First Stellaron produced, received the specific frequency of the Aeon of Remembrance's connection and it dissolved the way the corridor formations had dissolved when the notation network's source energy reached them. The shield was gone. The coherence junctions underneath it were exposed. She deployed the stigmata field. The marks hit clean. The rebirth pulse detonated. The harder spawn dissolved from the inside. She stood in the space where it had been, breathing hard, the scythe still extended. The whole exchange had taken six minutes and she had three cracked ribs from the tail impact and the butterfly quality was running at its maximum to hold her together through the cracked ribs. "SEELE!" Ivanova, from the line. "ARE YOU—" "I'M FINE!" She looked at her hands. At the warmth in them, the old-gold and the rebirth energy together. "I know how to handle the new ones! WARMTH FIRST, THEN STIGMATA! IT HAS A SHIELD LAYER OVER THE COHERENCE — THE SOURCE WARMTH STRIPS IT, THEN I MARK IT!" She could see the forward line processing this — twenty fighters receiving new tactical information in the middle of an ongoing engagement. "WHAT'S THE WARMTH?" Karev called. "I'LL EXPLAIN AFTER!" she called. "FOR NOW — IF YOU SEE THE SCALED ONE, MAKE ROOM AND LET ME GET TO IT FIRST!" "UNDERSTOOD!" Karev said. Two more harder spawns came up from the Restricted Zone floor at 0059. —— In the palace, Bronya was losing. Not the battle — the battle outside was Gepard's and Svarog's and Seele's and the forward line's and the Guard units on the outer walls and Sampo in the corridors and Natasha's medical teams and all the rest of them, all of Belobog giving everything to it. That battle was hard and it was costly and it was not won yet, but it was not lost. The battle Bronya was losing was at the contact point. She had been at the northern section's contact point since 2240. That was now one hour and twenty minutes ago. One hour and twenty minutes of standing in the full activation frequency of the First Stellaron, which had gone from activation level to above activation level in the aftermath of the second Manchester Smash. The activation frequency at above-activation level was not something the human body was designed to withstand. The geological pressure of it — the force of something enormous pressing upward through the stone — was not a metaphor. It had physical expression in her body. Her hands, pressed to the stone, had been numb since 2340. The bleeding at her nose had become consistent rather than occasional. The prismatic light of the activation frequency, this close to the contact point, meant that she could not fully close her eyes without receiving the light through her eyelids. She was standing in it. She was also starting to hear something that was not the geological pressure. Not the voices — she had learned those, the seismic interpretation of the Stellaron's movement toward the surface. These were different. These were not physical. They were in her mind — not metaphysically, not mystically, in the specific neurological territory of someone whose attunement to the Stellaron had been developing for weeks and who was now, one hundred and twenty minutes into full activation-frequency exposure, developing an attunement to the Stellaron's interior that she had not experienced before and had not been prepared for. The First Stellaron was a force. Svarog had told her that. A force did not think. A force did not speak. A force did not have interior experience in any meaningful sense. But the First Stellaron was also an entity of the Aeon of Destruction, and the Aeon of Destruction was the highest level of power in the Honkai universe, and the specific force that had been compressed into this geological form had been doing something for eight hundred years in the bedrock of Belobog that was not simple accumulation. It had been accumulating with a specific intensity. A specific direction. What she was hearing — what was happening in the neurological territory between her attunement and the activation frequency's full output at the contact point — was not language. It was intent. The specific, overwhelming, geological intent of a force that had one direction and had been building toward it for eight hundred years. The intent was: end. Not Belobog specifically.
