Not her specifically. Not anything specifically. Just: end. The specific character of the Aeon of Destruction — not targeted destruction, not strategic destruction, destruction as the fundamental nature of what this entity was. The ending of things as the baseline state. She was standing in that. She was the guardian of this city. The ending-intent pressed against her — not physically, psychologically. The specific psychological pressure of being in direct contact with a force that wanted to end things, that would end things if she let the contact break, that was currently actively trying to end things on the surface above through the crystalline form and the spawns and the activation discharge. She felt the pressure. She thought about what Cocolia had done. Her mother had been at this pressure and had broken under it — had not broken because she was weak, had broken because she had been afraid and afraid people made decisions that looked like power but were actually the opposite of power. Cocolia had tried to use the Stellaron. She had thought if she could direct it, she could survive it. You couldn't direct it. Svarog had told her. The Guardian's correct relationship to the energy was reception, not direction. The psychological pressure was the Stellaron trying to make her direct it. Trying to make her respond to the ending-intent with action — with the specific action of a Guardian reaching for the Stellaron's power to address the threat it was generating, which was the trap, which was what happened to Cocolia. She held still. This was the hardest thing she had ever done. She had done hard things. She had been the Supreme Guardian of Belobog at twenty-two years old, carrying the full weight of a city through a Stellaron plot, through a second Stellaron, through months of the first Stellaron's signal climbing. She had been in the Restricted Zone for weeks receiving this frequency through her boots. She had read Svarog's eight-hundred-year records and understood intellectually what was at stake. Understanding it intellectually and standing in it while the Stellaron's ending-intent pressed against her psychological architecture was a different category of hard. She heard — not sound, intent — the specific signal that she interpreted as: give in. Let it in. Direct it. She thought about the city. Not abstractly. Specifically. She thought about the outer machine district — the generators, the workshop, Clara at the notation bench. She thought about the residential stacks. She thought about three hundred and fourteen people in the courtyard after the Second Stellaron, the specific sound they had made when she told them they had done the impossible. She thought about Lynx making soup with the specific patience of someone who thought cooking was important. She thought about Dunn at the north wall at 0310 in the morning watching the cold light. She thought about Gepard. She thought about Gepard right now, in the Restricted Zone, not knowing what was happening to her at the contact point because this was not something she was going to put on the comms while he had what he had in front of him. She thought: he said he would come back. She thought: so will I. She received the ending-intent. She did not direct it. She held. The psychological pressure peaked — she felt it peak, the specific moment of maximum pressure, the moment that corresponded, she would understand later, to the second Manchester Smash propagating through the geological network in which she was standing and disrupting the crystalline form's coherence at six simultaneous points. The moment when the Stellaron had been most damaged and was therefore most desperately trying to use any available vector to extend itself. She was the available vector. She held. The peak passed. She was still standing. She was bleeding from her nose and her hands were numb and she could barely see through the prismatic light and she had a cracked rib from the impact transferred through the stone during the peak. She was still standing. The contact point under her hands, which was the Restricted Zone's geological foundation at its deepest accessible point, where the notation network's channels ran closest to the First Stellaron's primary mass, was warm. Not the Stellaron's frequency. The notation network's warmth — the Architects' design, the channels that had been built for this exact pressure and were carrying it. Conducting it upward and outward through the geological structure the way they had been built to. The Ice Flames were in those channels. She could feel Gepard's output in the channels — not perfectly, not with specificity, but as the specific quality of something that was in the same frequency range as the frequency she had been receiving for weeks, the thing that had been adjacent to the Stellaron's ending-intent and was its opposition. The Ice Flames in the notation channels, conducting through the geological foundation of the Restricted Zone, disrupting the crystalline form from everywhere simultaneously. She stood in the warmth of the notation channels and the Ice Flames conducting through them and she held the contact and she did not let the Stellaron's ending-intent have the city. —— At 0103, the Stellaron produced a fifth wave of spawns — six standard and two harder — and the forward line's adaptation was visible in how they responded. They had been doing this for over an hour. Karev and Ferenz had the crack-and-finish coordination locked. Ivanova had found her role as Seele's left-flank anchor — she didn't try to dissolve or crack the spawns, she held their foreleg position long enough for Seele to hit what needed hitting. The other eighteen fighters had organized themselves around a similar principle: your ice holds, her warmth strips, her stigmata dissolves. It was not effortless. People were hurting. The cracked rib count on the forward line was five. One Guard officer was down with a dislocated shoulder that the Wildfire medic had set in the field and which the Guard officer had then refused to call a reason to leave the line. A second Guard officer had taken a tail impact from one of the harder spawns and had a concussion that was being monitored by the Wildfire specialist who had been told very firmly that monitoring was his role and fighting was not. He was monitoring and also using his ice expression to hold positions while someone else addressed them. The Wildfire specialist had looked at this with the specific expression of someone deciding whether to enforce her medical authority and had concluded that she could not enforce it without physically restraining him, which she could not do without leaving a gap in the monitoring coverage. She let him hold positions. She watched his pupils. They kept fighting. —— At 0108, Luka reached the primary zone. He had been on the east boundary for seventy minutes, dealing with the east response zone's Fragmentum formations and the east spawn that had been his assignment and the secondary formations that had generated from the east spawn's dissolution. At 0101, Gepard's message on the comms: "Luka. East boundary status." "Clear," Luka said. "The east response has dropped. Formations are at corridor class and falling." "Then I need you at the primary zone." "On my way." He ran the three hundred meters. He arrived at the Restricted Zone's south face boundary at 0108 and crossed it and stood in the activation frequency field at Restricted Zone proximity and felt the danger level in the weapon go up immediately. Not the east boundary's extreme level. Restricted Zone proximity was a different category. The cannon would be at above-maximum output here. He had not only the cannon. He looked at the primary zone. At Svarog's formation — now fifteen mobile units, eight defensive and seven attack configuration (one attack golem had gone down in the past half hour to a focused Stellaron discharge, leaving black impact craters in the geological surface where it had been). At the Stellaron's primary mass, contracted but not defeated, the fracture network across its structure visible as light-refracting gaps in the prismatic radiation. At Gepard. Gepard was at the crown junction, running an ice wall series — not the Manhattan Smash, not the focused lance, the architecture. A sequence of ice structures placed in the specific geometric constraint that forced the contracted Stellaron's movement to address threats in the pattern he needed it to address them in, buying time for the third Manchester Smash while the attack golems kept hitting and the fracture network kept propagating from their mechanical impacts. He had not used the third Manchester Smash yet. Luka could see why. The Stellaron had contracted — the crown was now the inside of the contracted form, not the outside. Getting the Ice Flames into the form from the exterior required either a different approach vector or opening the form up enough that the crown became accessible again. The attack golems were working on the second option — trying to crack the contracted mass enough to expose the interior — but the contracted form was harder than the expanded form had been. The coherence was concentrated. He looked at the contracted mass. He thought: the cannon can hit the contracted surface from outside. If the damage is concentrated enough at a specific point— He switched the weapon to sword configuration. The three-form retractable weapon — cannon, sword, assault rifle. He had been running the cannon because the cannon was the long-range option and the east boundary had required long range. The primary zone required something different. He switched to the blade form. The weapon extended from its compact state into the sword — not a standard blade, the specific design Svarog had built, weighted for his body's chrome-arm enhanced strength, the edge carrying the same output-scaled material as the cannon. He looked at the contracted Stellaron. He ran the sandevistan at full operational speed and closed from the south face to the contracted mass in four seconds. The distance was forty meters. He hit the contracted mass with the sword at the point where three of the attack golems' previous impacts had concentrated — the specific point where the mechanical impact record was densest and the crystalline structure was most stressed even in its contracted configuration. The blade hit and the output at Restricted Zone proximity was above anything he had run in training. The contracted crystalline structure cracked at the impact point — not the deep fracture of the Manchester Smash, a surface crack, but at the specific point that the attack golems' mechanical impacts had been working toward for thirty minutes. The contracted mass shifted. Not broke — shifted. The internal geometry of the contracted Stellaron changed as the new crack joined the existing mechanical impact damage. The crown, which had been at the interior of the contracted form, was still not accessible. But the geometry had changed. Svarog said, immediately: "THE CRACK YOU MADE — THIRD FROM NORTH ON THE EAST FACE — THAT IS THE APPROACH VECTOR." "For the Smash?" Gepard said, from the north of the zone. "If you enter the Ice Flames from that specific point, the crack will channel the disruption to the crown's interior rather than dispersing through the contracted surface." A pause. "That is the opening." Luka was already stepping back from the contracted mass. He looked at the crack he'd made. He looked at where Gepard was. He said: "You need to hit that exact point." "I need to hit that exact point," Gepard confirmed. "While the attack golems hold the form in place." "While the attack golems hold the form in place." Gepard was moving. "SVAROG — HOLD THE FORM STABLE. ALL ATTACK UNITS — CENTER ON THE CONTRACTED MASS AND HOLD IT." Svarog's voice: "UNDERSTOOD. ALL ATTACK UNITS — TARGET THE CONTRACTED MASS CENTER! HOLD IT STABLE FOR THIRTY SECONDS!" The seven remaining attack golems converged on the contracted Stellaron simultaneously. Fifteen-meter mechanical giants with hammer-arms swinging, converging from seven directions, each hit from a different angle. The contracted mass could not address seven simultaneous mechanical impact sources — it reacted to the most extreme ones, which meant it could not reorient itself away from the south face crack. The crack stayed aligned. Luka was at the south face. He looked at Gepard crossing the zone toward the crack. He looked at the contracted Stellaron being held by seven mechanical giants. He looked at the crack that was the approach vector. He looked at his weapon in sword configuration. He looked at the crack. He switched to assault rifle configuration. Not the cannon — the rifle. Long range, precision, not the raw output of the cannon. The rifle's precision at Restricted Zone proximity, calibrated for the specific point of the crack. He was going to keep the crack from closing. The contracted Stellaron's coherence, even in its damaged state, was trying to repair the crack — the specific geological coherence restoration that crystalline structures attempted when they were fractured. He could see it happening: the crack edges were slowly drawing together as the form tried to restore its compressed coherence. He fired the rifle. Not the large burst — three rapid small bursts, precisely aimed at the crack's edges, disrupting the coherence restoration. The crack stayed open. He fired again. "HOW LONG?" Gepard asked. "AS LONG AS YOU NEED!" Luka shouted back. He kept the crack open. —— Sampo was at junction twelve when the Stellaron hit 0109 and the third Manchester Smash built. He was in the junction twelve corridor, which was the outer district's deepest notation channel access point. The corridor was clear — he had moved the last of the civilians through at 0103. He was addressing the final Fragmentum cluster at the junction twelve access by himself, which was going relatively well given that Wind Shear was devastating to corridor-class Fragmentum formations and he had been building five stacks of it on this cluster since 0105. He had not known, before tonight, that the wind he generated when he threw his daggers was a specific kind of damage. He had thrown daggers since he was sixteen and the wind had always felt like a byproduct — the natural consequence of something moving fast through air. It was not a byproduct. He had figured this out approximately forty-five minutes ago when the third formation he hit had not only cracked at the impact point but had begun deteriorating at every surface simultaneously from the Wind Shear the daggers had embedded. He had looked at this deterioration for approximately three seconds. He had then thrown the rest of his daggers at everything in sight and had found that yes, this was apparently something he could do, and the formations were dissolving at a rate that was considerably faster than the Guard units had been achieving with ice expressions. Good. Efficient. He noted this about himself with the same pragmatic lack of sentiment he brought to most information about himself. He finished the junction twelve cluster at 0111. He looked at the junction. He heard the comms — Gepard's instruction to Svarog, the attack golems converging, the word thirty seconds carrying in the specific way of the battle's next significant moment approaching. He put his daggers back in the jacket. He looked at junction twelve. He looked at the direction of the Restricted Zone. He was not in the engagement plan for the primary zone. He was not a primary zone fighter. He was a corridor navigator and a crowd controller and an unofficial intelligence asset who had been walking these routes for twenty years. He was also twenty-eight years old and had been born in the outer machine district and the outer machine district was being defended against a Stellaron right now by people he cared about and some of them were going to die if the Stellaron wasn't stopped and the Stellaron was going to be stopped in the next thirty seconds or possibly not at all. He went to the primary zone. —— He arrived at the Restricted Zone's south face at 0112, thirty seconds before the third Manchester Smash. He saw Luka at the south face with the rifle aimed at a crack in the contracted Stellaron's surface, firing precision rounds to keep the crack from closing. He saw the attack golems holding the contracted mass from seven directions. He saw the standard defensive golems holding the perimeter. He saw the light of the activation frequency and the fracture network across the contracted form and the specific quality of everything converging. He looked at the Stellaron. He felt something that he had not been expecting to feel tonight, which was: fury. Not the specific operational anger of someone dealing with a tactical problem. The specific personal fury of someone who had grown up in the streets around this zone and who had been coming back to this zone and the underworld under it his whole life and who was looking at something that had been trying to end his city for eight hundred years and was currently trying to end the city while his people were in it and his friends were fighting it and Natasha was at a medical station with cracked ribs she had not told him about yet. He looked at the Stellaron. He took out three daggers. He could not make it hurt in any meaningful way — he was not Gepard, he was not Seele, he was not any of the things that could crack crystalline geological structures. But he could make it hurt the way that a wound left open and unaddressed could hurt — the accumulation of small damage over time, the specific deteriorating effect of Wind Shear stacked and stacked and stacked on a target that could not dispel it. He threw. The daggers hit the contracted Stellaron's south face — not at the crack, away from it, at the surface of the contracted mass adjacent to the mechanical impact points the attack golems had been working. The Wind Shear embedded in the crystalline structure at three impact points. He threw again. Three more, bouncing between the impact points. Five stacks on the first three. The Wind Shear deterioration began — small, geological, the specific erosion of something that was being eaten alive at the surface. Every bit of structural coherence the Stellaron had to divert to the Wind Shear deterioration was coherence it was not directing at the attack golems. Luka saw him. He looked at Sampo with an expression that was partly surprise and partly — not surprise. "WIND!" Luka said. "WIND!" Sampo confirmed. "KEEP IT ON THE SURFACE AWAY FROM THE CRACK!" "ALREADY DOING THAT!" Sampo kept throwing. —— Gepard reached the south face crack at 0113. He looked at it. At Luka keeping it open with precision rifle fire. At Sampo stacking Wind Shear on the adjacent surface. At the seven attack golems holding the form in place. At the full weight of what Belobog had put into this zone for the past ninety minutes. He looked at the crack. He thought about the accounting — not the percentage kind, which he had been doing wrong by trying to manage it in finite terms. He thought about the thing the accounting actually was, which was this: he had the ice and he had the Ice Flames and the ice and the Ice Flames were both his, completely, fully, without the kind of ceiling that you could plan around in advance because you didn't know what was past the ceiling until you went past it. He had been to eighty percent. He knew the Ice Flames were there at eighty. Past eighty was unknown territory. He put both hands against the south face of the contracted Stellaron, aligned with the crack that Luka was holding open, and he thought about the city. He thought about it the way Bronya had taught him to think about it, which was specifically. The people in it. The outer machine district and the generators and the workshop where Clara had been at the notation bench for two months learning to hold the Architects' channels. The residential stacks where children were currently in safe rooms. The Guard units on the outer walls who had been fighting for ninety minutes and had not broken. Dunn at the north wall. He thought about Bronya at the contact point and what that was costing her and how he was not going to let the contact point have been for nothing. He brought both. —— The ice came first — not the lance, not the architecture, the full expression of everything he had built in eight years of Restricted Zone service and one year of the northern ridge's absolute edge. The ice at its maximum compression going directly into the crack, following the channel Luka had kept open, entering the contracted Stellaron's interior through the approach vector Svarog had identified. The ice hit the interior. The crown — the crown of the contracted form, which had been inaccessible from the exterior but was reachable from the interior through the crack — received the ice at maximum compression. The crown fracture, which was at twenty-seven percent of the crown's mass after the second Manchester Smash, received the ice and expanded. Fast. The fracture propagation from the inside of the crown was faster than the surface propagation from the exterior because the interior coherence was already compromised from the fracture network. The Ice Flames came with the ice. Not separately — not the ice and then the Ice Flames. Together. The thing that existed past eighty percent was not a separate entity from the ice. It was the ice at a different compression point, the ice at the temperature where it crossed into something that was not simply cold but was the opposition of the specific warm-crystal frequency of the First Stellaron's activation energy. The Ice Flames were the ice at the point where it became what the Stellaron's frequency could not survive. The notation network received the output. Through the crack and into the interior and through the geological foundation that Bronya was standing on at the contact point, the Ice Flames propagated through the full Restricted Zone. Not just the Restricted Zone — through the notation channels that the Architects had built into the outer machine district's geological structure, through the full network. The propagation this time was different from the second Manchester Smash. The second Manchester Smash had been through the geological foundation outward. This was through the foundation and through the interior of the contracted Stellaron simultaneously. From inside and outside at once. The fracture network, which connected the crown and the six stress points and the primary coherence point, received the Ice Flames from both directions at once. The crystalline coherence of the contracted form began to fail. Not at one point. Not progressively. Simultaneously — the fracture network acting as a distribution system for the disruption, carrying the Ice Flames to every connected fracture point at the same moment. The Stellaron screamed. This scream was different from the others — not the pain output of something that had been struck at a vulnerability, not the resource response of something that had been damaged and was escalating to compensate. This was the structural failure output. The scream of something that was losing its coherence and could feel that loss and could not stop it. The light of the activation frequency began to change. Not go out — change. The blinding prismatic white-and-all-colors of full activation began shifting. The prismatic quality was still there, but the coherence of it was different. Fractured. The specific quality of light refracting through a structure that was losing its geometric integrity. The contracted form expanded — not by choice. The contraction that had been protecting the interior was coming apart as the coherence failed. The form was expanding back toward its original one-hundred-and-fifty-meter extent. From inside the expanding form, through the crack that Luka had kept open, the third Manchester Smash propagated outward in every direction simultaneously. Gepard was on his hands and knees. He had both hands on the south face of what had been the contracted Stellaron and was now the expanding form. He was — He was past eighty. He did not know exactly where, because past eighty turned out not to have the kind of clear accounting that the territory below eighty had. Past eighty was the territory the northern ridge had produced, the specific domain that began when you had gone so far past what you thought was the ceiling that the ceiling concept no longer applied. He was past eighty and the Ice Flames were still running — not controlled by him in the way the ice below eighty had been controlled, running through him and into the geological foundation and through the notation network and out through the full Restricted Zone in every direction simultaneously. He stayed on his hands on the stone. He stayed because the third Manchester Smash needed to complete. He was going to stay until it completed. —— In the notation network's channels, Clara felt the third Manchester Smash before anyone above ground saw its effects. She had been at the notation bench since 1800 the previous evening — eight hours, two hands on the surface, holding the Architects' channels open for the Ice Flames' propagation. She had eaten once, at 2300, while Oleg held a conversation loudly enough to keep her from fully dropping the contact while she had four bites of something. She had not moved from the bench. She felt the Ice Flames come through the third time and they were not the same as the first or second time. The first time, they had been thirty-eight percent of Gepard's output. The second time, more. This time — she did not have a measurement for it. She had a feeling. The specific feeling of someone whose hands had been on a network designed to carry a specific frequency range, and the thing coming through the network right now was at the top of that range and possibly slightly beyond it. The channels were carrying it. The Architects had built them to the highest possible standard. She had always known this intellectually. She felt it now in her palms — the specific feeling of engineering that had been built with sufficient margin that the maximum it had been designed for was not what it was actually producing. There was more left in the channels. The Ice Flames were at the top of what they'd been designed to carry and the channels were carrying them. "SVAROG!" she said. He appeared at the workshop door. He had come back from the Restricted Zone at some point in the past ten minutes — she had not tracked his return closely, she had been holding the bench. "The channels," he said. "I know," she said. "Is he—" "Holding," Svarog said. "For now." She looked at the network under her hands. At the Ice Flames propagating through it. At the Stellaron's signal, which was doing something on the frequency display that she had not seen before: not the activation frequency. Something lower. The specific frequency pattern of crystalline structural failure. "It's failing," she said. "Yes," Svarog said. "The coherence is failing." She thought about what that meant for the city, for the people at the outer walls, for the forward line. She thought about what it meant for Gepard on his hands at the south face of an expanding Stellaron with the third Manchester Smash still running through him. She pressed her hands harder to the bench. She held the channels. She held. —— In the outer district, the change was visible at 0121. The light — the blinding prismatic light of the First Stellaron's full activation frequency — was changing. The coherence of it was fracturing. Not dimming. Fracturing — the specific quality of light coming through a prism that was shattering, the individual prismatic components losing their relationship to each other, the unified white-and-all-colors becoming individual colors that were no longer organized into a unified field. Dunn saw it from the north wall. He had been at the north wall for ninety-three minutes. He had addressed thirty-seven Fragmentum formations in that time, either himself with his own limited ice expression or in coordination with the twelve Guard units on his section. He had two officers on the wall who would need the ward. He had one who was currently at the ward because the Wildfire team had carried him there and nobody had been able to argue with the Wildfire team at the time. He looked at the fracturing light. He had not been in the cave when the Second Stellaron died. He had been on the outer wall then too. He had felt the ground shake when the Manchester Smash froze the cave. He had seen the golden light change when the Second Stellaron's form reverted. He looked at the fracturing light and he knew what fracturing light meant. He said, to the north wall line: "IT'S GOING! WHATEVER YOU HAVE LEFT — USE IT NOW! WE ARE NOT DONE UNTIL IT IS DONE!" The north wall line was twelve Guard officers, various states of damage, various states of depletion. They had been fighting for ninety-three minutes. They had things left. They used them. —— At 0124, the First Stellaron's crown failed completely. The fracture network, which had been propagating through the crystalline structure since the first Manchester Smash hit the crown at the beginning of the engagement, reached one hundred percent of the crown's mass. The crown — the cluster of thirty-meter crystal formations that had been the form's primary radiation source, the thing that had turned the Restricted Zone into a blinding prismatic day — shattered. Not dissolved. Not vanished. Shattered — the crystalline components losing their coherence relationship to each other and separating, the individual formations falling from their positions in the crown cluster, the specific geological catastrophe of something that had been held together by crystalline coherence for eight hundred years losing that coherence in four hours. The activation frequency, without the crown to organize it, became noise. Not nothing — the form was still partially standing, the primary mass still had some coherence in the sections furthest from the fracture network. But the organized, directed, overwhelming activation frequency that had been turning the Restricted Zone into a force-field of destruction was now fractured output. Individual discharges from individual sections of the remaining primary mass, uncoordinated, unfocused. Still dangerous. But not what it had been. The forward line felt it. Seele felt it — the specific quality of the spawns she was addressing losing a degree of coherence, the harder spawn she had been engaged with for twelve minutes suddenly having its coherence cycle break, the shielding layer flickering. She hit it immediately. The shield came off faster than before. The stigmata marks hit clean. The harder spawn dissolved. She stood in the space where it had been and she looked at the primary zone. The light was fractured. She said, to the forward line, at full volume: "THE CROWN IS DOWN! IT'S WEAKENING! EVERYBODY — WE HAVE IT! WE HAVE IT!" The forward line made a sound that was not a tactical acknowledgment. It was twenty fighters who had been at maximum output for over ninety minutes hearing that the thing they had been holding the line against was losing. The sound was — everything. Relief and fury and pride and the specific emotion of people who had been told the city needed them and had given the city everything they had and were now being told it was working. It was a very loud sound for twenty people. From the outer walls, Dunn heard it. He looked at the primary zone. He relayed it: "THE CROWN IS DOWN! CONTINUE ENGAGEMENT! ALL UNITS — CONTINUE!" On the comms, the Guard units on every position received it. And Belobog, which had been fighting for one hour and thirty-four minutes against the greatest threat it had ever faced, pushed harder. —— At 0127, on his hands on the stone of the Restricted Zone, Gepard felt the Ice Flames begin to ebb. Not stop — ebb. The specific quality of an output that had been running past its sustainable point beginning to find its natural resolution, the way a fire that has consumed its fuel does not stop instantly but diminishes. He had been past eighty for fourteen minutes. He had been on his hands on the stone for fourteen minutes. The third Manchester Smash had propagated for fourteen minutes through the geological foundation and the notation network and the interior of the expanding Stellaron. The crown had failed. He had heard it through the geological structure before he'd seen the light change — the specific geological event of the crown fracture reaching one hundred percent and the crystal formations separating, transmitted through the stone he had his hands on. He did not know what he was at now, in the accounting terms he had abandoned. He knew what he felt, which was: very little. The specific very-little of someone who had given what the situation required and was now at the other side of it with what remained. He looked at the Stellaron. The primary mass — what remained of the contracted form's interior, now partially expanded, the coherence failing at every point the fracture network had reached — was not defeated. Not yet. The crown was gone. The activation frequency was noise. But the primary mass was still present, still partially coherent, still the geological substance of an entity that had been in the bedrock of Belobog for eight hundred years. He looked at his hands. He thought: not yet. He got up. He stood. The Ice Flames were still there — not at the output they had been, but there. The specific blue-white fire at the edge of the ice expression, quieter now, present. He looked at the primary mass. He looked at the Restricted Zone — at the attack golems, three of which had gone down in the past half hour, four remaining attack configuration and seven defensive. At Luka, who had run from the south face to address a harder spawn that had emerged during the third Manchester Smash and had come back with the spawn's coherence structure dissolved across the weapon's blade. At Sampo, still at the south face, still throwing, the Wind Shear stacks on the primary mass's surface now at fifteen accumulated wounds where the crystalline coherence had been eaten through. He looked at all of it. He thought: one more. Not the Manchester Smash — he did not have that. What he had was the ice, and the Ice Flames, and the notation network, and the attack golems, and Luka's weapon in all three configurations, and Seele's butterfly wings and stigmata field, and Sampo's Wind Shear, and twenty Guard officers on the forward line with the ice they had built on a cold planet over their whole lives, and Dunn on the north wall, and Clara at the notation bench, and Bronya at the contact point. One more. He raised both hands. He brought the ice. And the battle continued. —— END OF CHAPTER 54 Her Shield, His Sun Chapter 53: What Belobog Can Do The ice plates shattered at 2247 on a Wednesday night, and the light that came through was not the cold light they had been watching for weeks. The cold light had been a shimmer. A glow. A promise of something approaching. What came through the shattered plates was not a promise. It was blinding. Prismatic, overwhelming, the light of a crystal structure one hundred and fifty meters tall radiating at its full activation frequency — not warm, not cold, something that had no temperature in any human category because it was not a thing that had a temperature. It was force expressed as light. It was the Aeon of Destruction's work made geological and then made surface and it was happening in the Restricted Zone of Belobog at 2247 on a Wednesday night and everything that followed happened very fast. —— Gepard felt the activation before the ice plates broke. He was at the outer wall of the Restricted Zone, at the primary position he had selected six weeks ago and had been standing at for the past four hours. He felt the activation through the notation network — through his hands on the wall, through the Ice Flames in his standard expression that had been resonating with the Stellaron's rising signal for days and now received the full output of that signal at activation level. The Ice Flames flared. Not deployed. Not directed. Just — present, at the surface of the ice expression, more present than they had ever been, responding to the specific frequency of a Stellaron in full activation the way a tuning fork responded to the frequency it had been built for. He looked at the Restricted Zone. The ice plates exploded outward rather than inward — the pressure from below was that extreme. They hit the zone's outer wall thirty meters away and shattered against it. Through the space where the plates had been, light was pouring upward out of the earth as if the earth had always been made of this light and had been containing it until now. Then the stone began to move. The ground of the Restricted Zone's northern section rose. Not fractured — rose, a section forty meters across lifting as a single piece on a geological upwelling that had been building for eight hundred years and had now decided it was done building. The stone cracked along pre-existing fault lines that Svarog's records said had been put there deliberately, by whoever or whatever had placed the First Stellaron in the bedrock of Belobog in the first place. The First Stellaron rose through the stone. It came out slowly — not because it was weak. Because it was one hundred and fifty meters of crystalline structure emerging from bedrock, and even geological forces had to work through geological time. The first thing visible was the crown — a cluster of prismatic crystal formations each thirty meters tall on their own, radiating light from every facet simultaneously, the specific blinding white-and-all-colors of something that was not one color but was all of them. Then the shoulders, which were not shoulders in any biological sense but were the specific geometric mass that defined the form's upper section — massive, angled, the crystal structures overlapping each other in interlocking formations that Serval's frequency model had described as the form's primary coherent mass. Then the wings. They deployed before the rest of the form had cleared the ground. One hundred and twenty meters of crystalline wingspan, each wing a network of prismatic crystal structures that moved against each other with a sound — not a sound in any recorded category, something that the air transmitted as pressure before the ears registered it as noise, something that hit the outer district's generator housings from four hundred meters away and made them ring. The entire northern section of the Restricted Zone lit up. Not the cold light. The First Stellaron's full activation frequency, radiating from the form itself, turning the night into a blinding prismatic day that had no shadows because the light came from everywhere simultaneously. Dunn, at the north wall four hundred meters away, said: "Sir." "I see it," Gepard said. "The signal on the display—" "Doesn't matter. I see it." He put his hands on the wall and he looked at the First Stellaron of Belobog — at one hundred and fifty meters of crystalline geological force standing in the northern section of the Restricted Zone, its wings deployed, its crown radiating the activation frequency in every direction, its form oriented not away from the city but toward it — and he thought: all right. He thought: this is the thing. He thought: let's go. —— In the underworld, the rumble started at 2248. Not the kind of rumble that came from the Fragmentum or from geological stress or from the activation of something in the outer machine district above. This was a different kind of rumble — rhythmic, mechanical, the specific sound of machinery that had been dormant for a very long time starting up simultaneously. Sampo was in the northwest corridor when it started. He'd been running the routes since 2200, the ones he'd been walking for twenty years and had now formalized into the non-standard access network he'd given to Gepard. He stopped when the rumble started. He put his hand on the junction wall. The wall was vibrating. Not from the Stellaron's frequency — from something below. Something under the underworld, deeper than the sub-levels, deeper than the Great Glacial Aegir. Something that had been still for decades and had just been told to move. His phone buzzed: Gepard. ACTIVATION. PERIMETER POSITIONS. NOW. He started moving. He was fifty meters from the junction nine access point, which put him sixty seconds from his position on the northwest route. He moved fast, the way he moved when moving fast was what the situation required, which was not elegantly but was absolutely effectively. Behind him the rumble was getting louder. Then the floor of outer corridor seven — the same corridor the second Stellaron had come up through — cracked open. Not the geological crack of something forced upward. The deliberate crack of a hatch that had been engineered to open from below. And through the hatch came the first of Svarog's army. It was fifteen meters tall. It had been built from the same old-world machinery that Svarog had been collecting and processing and engineering in the deepest sections of the underworld for forty years — the pre-Separation components that nobody in the outer machine district had thought to look for because they were classified as scrap and because nobody had thought to ask what Svarog was doing with scrap. The result was not elegant. It was not graceful. It was enormous, armored, mechanical in the specific way of something that had been built to last rather than to look like it would last. Its chassis was the articulated steel of something that had learned what structural failure looked like over forty years of being built to avoid it. Its joints were geared — massive exposed gear mechanisms at every articulation point, built visible because visibility made maintenance possible and maintenance was what kept things running. It had red optical sensors in its head section. It had a cannon-equivalent on its right shoulder. It was fifteen meters tall and it came up through the hatch in outer corridor seven with the specific purposeful quality of something that had been told what it was for and was now doing it. Then the second one came through. Then the third. Then two more through a hatch in junction six that had not existed in any map Sampo had ever seen. Then four more through the junction twelve access, which explained why Svarog had been running extended maintenance cycles in the junction twelve section for the past three months and had been deflecting questions about it by describing the maintenance as routine and being technically correct about this in the way that Svarog was always technically correct while also being comprehensively incomplete. Twelve units. Then sixteen. Twenty. The underworld was shaking with their movement, the vibration of twenty-four fifteen-meter mechanical giants ascending through hatches that had been engineered and hidden and waited for this exact moment. And at the center of the formation, integrated into the largest unit — the twenty-fifth, which was two meters taller than the rest, which had a different chassis architecture, which moved with a quality none of the others had — was Svarog. Not beside it. Not controlling it from outside. Inside it. The four primary arms that Luka had watched conduct workshop equipment for months were now interfaced with the unit's control architecture, the optical array synced to the unit's sensor suite, the four-hundred-year-old consciousness that had been running the outer machine district since before it had a name now running something considerably larger than a workshop. He brought the army up through the underworld and into the outer district and he oriented the formation toward the Restricted Zone, where the First Stellaron's light was already visible from every surface in the outer machine district, and he began to move. Twenty-five Ancient Gear Giants, with Svarog integrated at the center, advancing through the outer machine district toward the greatest threat the planet had produced in eight hundred years. —— Gepard saw them coming from the outer wall. He saw the light first — not the Stellaron's light, something different. The optical sensors of twenty-five mechanical units reflecting the Stellaron's prismatic activation frequency as they crossed the junction corridor toward the zone. The sound next — the rhythmic mechanical thunder of fifteen-meter units moving in formation, the gear mechanisms at their joints, the specific sound of old-world engineering operating at full output. Then he saw Svarog in the lead unit and he looked at this for approximately two seconds. He thought: four hundred years of watching and waiting and he built an army. He thought: of course he did. He moved off the outer wall toward the Restricted Zone. Behind him, at the north wall, Dunn's voice on the comms: "Sir. Are those—" "Svarog's," Gepard said. A pause. "How long has he—" "Apparently quite a while." He didn't stop moving. "Hold the north wall. The Fragmentum response is going to start any moment." "Understood." A pause. "Sir." "Yeah." "Good luck." He stepped into the Restricted Zone. —— The First Stellaron noticed Gepard approximately four seconds after he crossed the boundary. Not because it was looking for him specifically. Because something in the First Stellaron's activation frequency and the Ice Flames in the ice expression responded to each other, the way two opposing forces responded when they came into proximity. The Stellaron turned. It was one hundred and fifty meters of crystalline geological force orienting toward a twenty-five-year-old man at the outer boundary of the zone, and it should have been an absurd sight — the scale of it, the overwhelming prismatic light of it, the wings deployed across a hundred and twenty meters of span, and one person. Gepard brought the ice up. Not the Manchester Smash. Not yet. The standard expression at selection rate, the architecture he'd been building for eight years — walls, structures, the geometric constraint that forced the form's movement into predictable channels. The ice came up across the Restricted Zone's northern section in a pattern that anyone who had been in a cave with him would have recognized: not confrontational, topological. Forcing the terrain. The Stellaron did not respond to topology the way the Second Stellaron had. It came through the first ice wall like the wall wasn't there. The prismatic light hit the ice structure and the ice structure lasted three seconds — three seconds of full geological output against an ice formation — and then the wall was gone. Not shattered. Not melted. The crystal frequency had disrupted the ice structure's coherence and it simply stopped being a wall. Gepard moved left. The wing came through the space where he had been. The downstroke of a sixty-meter crystal wing hitting the ground of the Restricted Zone produced a crater in the stone that was twelve meters deep and twenty meters across. The impact displaced stone like an explosion. Fragments of the geological surface came off the crater at angles that were genuinely dangerous and Gepard was already forty meters away because he had read the wing angle the moment the Stellaron moved and had been moving before the impact. He landed in a crouch at the east section of the zone. He looked at the Stellaron. It was looking at him. The crown's prismatic light was orienting toward his position — not a coincidence, a targeting. The First Stellaron had eyes in the specific sense that its activation frequency could track a heat signature and an opposing frequency signature and was currently doing both. The activation discharge built in the crown. Not the omnidirectional radiation of the ongoing field — a focused output, the activation frequency concentrated toward a specific point. Toward where he was. He brought the shield. Not the wall — the personal shield, the defensive expression at maximum compression. The ice around him in a sphere, dense, the specific compression he'd been able to produce since year four of the Restricted Zone posting. He brought it up and it was good and the discharge hit it and the discharge was not the Second Stellaron's presence-field pulse. The discharge was the activation frequency at point-blank focused output and it hit the ice shield and Gepard went backward fifteen meters across the Restricted Zone floor. He landed on his back. He lay there for one second. The shield had held — his bones were intact, his organs were intact — but the kinetic transfer through the shield had been enough to throw him fifteen meters across stone. He got up. He looked at the Stellaron. He said, quietly, to no one: "All right. I see." He brought the ice up again. —— Svarog's first unit reached the Restricted Zone boundary at 2254, seven minutes after the activation. The twenty-five Ancient Gear Giants entered the zone in formation — not a military formation, they hadn't been built for military maneuvering, but a practical one: the largest units to the front, the lighter units in the flanks, Svarog in the center unit directing the whole formation's movement through the interface with the lead unit's sensor array. The First Stellaron registered the formation. It registered twenty-five fifteen-meter mechanical units advancing across the Restricted Zone and it produced a sound — not the activation frequency discharge, something older. A sound that the pre-Separation records described as the specific output of the First Stellaron's form when it recognized something as a threat rather than a nuisance. A lower, longer, resonant output that wasn't quite sound and wasn't quite seismic force but was both. The lead Ancient Gear Giant took the resonance output directly in the chest. The steel chassis absorbed it — not elegantly, the armor plating at the chest section buckled inward on three faces — but it held. The unit staggered. Svarog steadied it through the interface. "GEPARD!" Svarog's voice, through the unit's external speakers, was louder than it had ever been in the workshop. "THE CROWN SECTION. THE JUNCTION BETWEEN THE PRIMARY CRYSTAL MASS AND THE UPPER FORMATION." Gepard looked at the crown from forty meters away. He saw it — the specific point where the primary crystal mass of the form's head section interfaced with the upper formation, a structural joint, the place where the crystalline coherence was most complex and therefore most potentially vulnerable. "I see it," he said. "If the ice expression enters the joint—" "I see it," he said. He was already moving. The Stellaron was tracking Svarog's formation, which meant it was not tracking Gepard with its full activation discharge. He moved at the speed he had — not the standard patrol speed, not the selection rate, the thing from the northern ridge, the third category of movement that Dunn had once described as: it is not a run, it is a different category. He crossed forty meters of Restricted Zone floor in a time that should not have been possible for someone running on two legs. He hit the crown section joint with the full ice lance — maximum compression from inside thirty meters, the specific focused burst that the northern ridge had required him to produce to survive a Trampler formation in the eleventh month. The lance hit the joint. The joint cracked. Not shattered. Cracked. A hairline fracture three meters across in the crystalline structure of the First Stellaron's crown section, visible because the activation frequency's light was refracting differently at the fracture point. Different color. A gap in the prismatic radiation. The Stellaron screamed. Not the resonance output — a genuine scream, the full-form output of an entity that had been struck at a structural vulnerability for the first time in its geological existence. The sound hit the Restricted Zone from every direction simultaneously and the remaining ice plates at the zone's edge shattered and the generator stacks in the adjacent outer district section bent from the pressure. Gepard was already moving. He was thirty percent capacity expended. He could feel the accounting — the Ice Flames response to the Stellaron's frequency, the lance, the movement. He was at seventy percent. He had the Ice Flames at the surface of the expression and they were demanding more output and he was not giving them more output yet because this was the first minute of a battle that was going to last hours and he needed to still be standing at the end of it. "Formation!" Svarog's voice, from the lead unit. "ADVANCE LEFT FLANK!" The Ancient Gear Giants moved. —— In the outer district, the Fragmentum response hit at 2255. It came from the ground — not the surface formations, not the corridor levels. From the deep geological structure, the First Stellaron's activation frequency radiating through every mineral pathway in the outer machine district simultaneously. The formation density went from baseline to critical in approximately ninety seconds, which was faster than any Fragmentum response in the recorded history of Belobog. The Silvermane Guard held their positions. Dunn's voice on the comms, steady and clear, at 2256: "All units. Primary positions. Do not engage anything above corridor class without backup. Move in pairs. The response is going to peak — hold until it peaks and then the density will fall when the engagement hits the disruption point." He didn't know when the disruption point was. He said it because it was true and because the Guard units at their positions needed something to hold toward and the disruption point was the true thing. When Gepard — when whatever Gepard was going to do worked — the frequency would drop and the Fragmentum response would fall. He just didn't know when. He held the north wall with twelve Guard units and two Wildfire medical teams and the specific quality of someone who had eight years of watching a particular person do impossible things and had therefore developed a reliable model of what was possible. Twelve meters above junction eleven on the outer wall, a junction-class Fragmentum formation materialized in the air. Dunn looked at it. He said: "Twelve o'clock, junction eleven wall. Everyone who can reach it." Six Guard units reached it. They addressed it. It took longer than it should have because junction-class formations in the air did not appear in any training manual. They addressed it anyway. Thirty meters to the left, another one materialized. Then one to the right. Dunn was moving before the right-side one finished generating. "SPREAD THE LINE! DON'T LET THEM CLUSTER—" The Guard spread the line. The formations were addressed before they could cluster. It was not clean — two Guard officers took impacts and were down for sixty seconds each before the Wildfire teams reached them — but they were up again. Standing. Moving. The line held. —— The forward engagement line, where Seele was, hit a different problem. The Fragmentum response at the Restricted Zone's outer boundary was not junction-class. It was sub-level two class and above — the same density as the cave during the Second Stellaron's second stage engagement, but city-wide rather than cave-contained. Seele and her cohort — twelve fighters total, a mix of senior Guard officers and Wildfire specialists who had been selected specifically for the forward line because they had the highest individual threat assessment scores in Belobog — faced the response at its densest point. Seele had the scythe extended before the first form finished generating. The butterfly quality was there. It had been building since the cave, since Fuli had revived her, since the specific change that two hundred meters of tunnel and twenty-three hours of sub-level three had worked in her biology. It was not a switch she flipped. It was a state she was in, the way you were in the state of knowing how to read — you didn't decide to read, you just did. She moved through the forward engagement line at a speed that was not quite standard trajectory. The scythe arc hit the first form at the primary crystallization junction and the old-gold warmth from the Fuli connection conducted through the blade and the form didn't crack — it dissolved. Different from the scythe's standard mechanical disruption. The Herrscher of Rebirth's stigmata had marked the crystalline structure and the mark had undone it. She looked at what the dissolution produced and filed it immediately: the stigmata field was active. The marks she put on forms were carrying the rebirth energy. The scythe was not just cutting — it was unmmaking. "SEELE!" To her left — a Guard officer, Ivanova, twenty-two years, who had been on Dunn's north wall rotation for a year and had specifically requested the forward line. "THE CLUSTER AT TWELVE!" Three forms simultaneously, generating from the same crystallization source. A cluster — the kind that the corridor drills said to address from the source rather than the individuals. "ON IT!" Seele went vertical. Not the small increment. Not the sixty-five meters of sub-level three. What the full butterfly quality looked like when she wasn't suppressing it: she went twenty meters straight up off the forward line's surface, the scythe extending above her, and she hit the cluster's crystallization source from directly above with every ounce of the rebirth stigmata energy she could concentrate into the blade's leading edge. The cluster source dissolved. All three forms, simultaneously, lost their crystallization coherence and dispersed. Ivanova, from below: "How did you DO that?!" "CLUSTER SOURCE!" Seele landed. "ALWAYS GO FOR THE CLUSTER SOURCE! IF YOU CAN'T REACH IT, MARK IT AND I'LL REACH IT!" Ivanova stared at her for about one second. Then she said, "RIGHT!" and went for the next cluster's constituent forms while Seele went for the sources. It was a system. It wasn't elegant — twelve people fighting sub-level-two class formations in a response that was covering the entire outer district boundary was not going to be elegant. But it was working. The Fragmentum forms were not getting through to the residential sections. That was what mattered. To Seele's right, she could see the Ancient Gear Giants moving through the Restricted Zone. The sound of them — the gear mechanisms, the impact of fifteen-meter mechanical forms engaging the Stellaron's wings — was audible even from the forward line. She could also see the light from Gepard's ice expression, the specific blue-white quality of it, moving fast across the zone. She knew what that meant. She'd been in a cave with him. She turned back to the forward line. There were more forms. She went. —— In the inner residential corridors, the panic would have been catastrophic if Sampo's routes hadn't been working. The Fragmentum response was visible from windows — the formations materializing in the streets, the prismatic light of the Stellaron above the outer district, the sound that the activation frequency made at building distance, which was not quite sound and was not quite pressure but was undeniably both. The civilian population of Belobog's outer and middle residential sections was experiencing something it had never experienced before, which was being in a city while a Stellaron was active above it. Sampo was in corridor seven running people toward the inner sections. Not organizing them — physically running with them, calling positions, identifying the gaps in the flow where people were backing up and going to those gaps and moving them through. He knew this corridor better than he knew his own hands. He had walked it so many times that the floor's unevenness was a known fact, that the junction twelve turn was ninety-four degrees not ninety, that the old generator hatch at the northwest section was flush with the floor unless there had been recent maintenance in which case it stuck up three centimeters. He moved people through the corridor at maximum efficiency and in his other hand he had his phone logging positions to the Guard comms channel. A Wildfire medic — young, female, keeping pace two meters behind him — said: "How do you know this route so well?" "I grew up here," he said, without slowing. "LEFT at the junction! All the way left!" She went left. The flow of people went left. The junction opened up. "Grew up here?" she said, continuing to pace him. "Outer machine district. Born and raised." He moved a family group into the faster channel. "Your team at junction twelve?" "Should be." "Should be isn't good enough right now." "Is there a softer way to put that?" "No." He looked at her. She was keeping pace, which was better than most people. "What's your name?" "Ilya." "Ilya. The Fragmentum response is going to peak in the next—" he checked his phone "—forty minutes maximum if the engagement is going the way it should. Your team at junction twelve needs to be the thing between the response peak and the people you just moved to the inner sections. Tell me they're there." She pulled her radio. She got confirmation in eight seconds. "They're there." "Good." He turned another group. "Keep up." She kept up. —— In the Restricted Zone, the first Ancient Gear Giant went down at 2318. The Stellaron's activation discharge, focused and directed, hit the leftmost unit in Svarog's formation squarely in its chest section and the chest section did not hold. The steel of the old-world chassis was not designed to take a focused First Stellaron discharge at activation level. The chassis buckled from the front face to the rear plate and the unit fell — not forward, backward, the impact throwing it onto its back on the Restricted Zone floor. It hit the ground hard enough that the impact was felt on the outer wall two hundred meters away. The units flanking the fallen one immediately shifted formation — closing the gap, keeping the line intact. Svarog's design: if one went down, the rest covered. He had built redundancy into the formation structure because four hundred years of building things had taught him that redundancy was the difference between a system and a plan. "Number twelve is down!" Svarog's voice from the lead unit. "Holding formation!" Gepard was on the Stellaron's right flank when number twelve went down. He heard Svarog, registered the formation gap, registered that the gap was covered. He was at the right flank because he had spent the past twenty minutes driving the Stellaron into a corner of its own movement — not successfully containing it, the First Stellaron did not contain, but forcing it to address threats from multiple directions simultaneously. He brought the ice up on the right flank — not to damage the Stellaron, to force it to turn. If it turned right to address his position on the flank, it had to turn away from Svarog's formation. If it turned back to Svarog's formation, his position on the flank became vulnerable from the opposite side. The Stellaron turned right. He moved left. It was the thing he'd been doing for twenty minutes and it was not a battle-winning strategy — it was a battle-enduring strategy. The attrition of attention. Making the Stellaron divide its targeting between multiple threat sources while Svarog's units ground into it from the front. He was at sixty percent. He could feel the accounting clearly. The Ice Flames were at the surface of the expression and they were not patient — they wanted more output, they were pressing against the constraint of sixty percent the way water pressed against a dam. He held them. Not yet. The first Manchester Smash was coming. He could feel where it was in the accounting — could feel the moment when the battle required it. Not yet. His comms crackled. Serval, from the secondary relay: "The crown fracture is growing. Whatever you hit it with is propagating." "How far?" "Twelve percent of the crown mass is fractured. The activation frequency output is dropping at the fracture points. Keep hitting it." "Noted." He moved. "How long before the propagation reaches the primary coherence junction?" "At current rate? Forty minutes." He looked at his accounting. He had forty minutes in him at current output. He had the Manchester Smash available. He had the Ice Flames pressing against sixty percent. Forty minutes was going to require more than he was currently spending. "Serval." "Yeah." "Where's Luka." —— Luka was at the east boundary of the outer district at 2259. His assignment was the eastern Fragmentum response zone — the secondary concentration point, where the First Stellaron's activation frequency was hitting the geological structure at the specific angle that produced the heaviest formation density outside the primary Restricted Zone perimeter. The Guard units there were good; they'd been trained on the corridor formations, they understood the Fragmentum's movement patterns. What they didn't have was a way to address anything above sub-level-one class. Luka had that. The cannon in its compact position — the weapon Svarog had given him, three-form and output-scaled to the danger level he was operating at. At the east boundary's formation density, the danger level was high. The cannon's small burst output was at twice what it had been in training. He could feel it in the weapon's charge rate, the way it cycled faster, the way the output was eager. He'd dispatched nine formations in the first eight minutes. The sandevistan integration was running at operational ready — not full burst, the spatial awareness mode, the enhanced reaction time and positioning that let him be where the threat was going to be rather than where it was now. He moved through the east boundary's engagement zone like someone who knew the floor plan, which he did, because the east boundary's geological structure ran through the same notation channels he'd been conducting since the corridor entries. At 2307 Gepard's message came through the comms: "Luka. Stellaron position. It's using focused discharge against Svarog's units. If we can give it something else to track on the south face—" "I can reach the south face," Luka said. He was already looking at the distance. Three hundred meters from the east boundary to the south face of the Restricted Zone, through the outer district's junction network. "How long do I have?" "Now would be good." "Two minutes." He looked at the Guard officer beside him — a man named Brask, forty years old, the kind of solidity you built from twenty years of keeping a city's walls. "Brask. Take the east boundary. Formation source at junction eight is the priority." Brask looked at him. He looked at the Stellaron's light visible above the zone's southern wall. He said: "Go." Luka went. He ran the outer district at operational sandevistan — not full burst, the enhanced movement that ate distance without burning the reserve. Three hundred meters through the junction network in under ninety seconds, which was fast enough that the Guard units he passed registered him as movement and then as Luka and then adjusted. He reached the south face of the Restricted Zone at 2309. The south face didn't have the primary engagement happening — the Ancient Gear Giants were at the north, Gepard was moving between the north and east flanks. The south face was the open vector. The Stellaron was not tracking it because nothing was threatening it from the south. Luka looked at the south face. He raised the cannon. The danger level was high. Not the east boundary high — Restricted Zone perimeter high, within one hundred meters of an activated First Stellaron's full output field. The cannon read the danger level and the charge rate tripled. He fired. The burst hit the First Stellaron's lower body section — not the crown, not the primary mass, the lower formation cluster where the geological structure was densest. The burst wasn't the Manchester Smash. But it was his cannon at Restricted Zone proximity output and it hit the lower formation cluster and produced a crack in the crystalline structure that was visible as a color change in the activation frequency's light. The Stellaron noticed the south face. It turned. And it was not tracking Gepard from the east flank with its full discharge when it turned south, which was exactly the point. Gepard's comms: "Good. Hold it." "Holding," Luka said. He moved fifty meters left before the Stellaron's activation frequency built at the south face, because holding didn't mean standing still. —— The Guard on the outer walls did not break. This was not a given. This was something that was tested continuously from 2255 onward, as the Fragmentum response peaked and the formations in the outer district moved from corridor-class to sub-level-class, as the Ancient Gear Giants moved through the streets leaving their impact craters behind them, as the Stellaron's activation frequency made every mineral surface in the outer machine district ring. The Guard units at their positions had been told what was coming and they held their positions and they addressed formations and they went down when impacts hit them and they got back up. Some of them did not get back up immediately. The Wildfire teams were at those positions before the Guard units had finished falling. A Guard officer named Tarkov — nineteen years old, two years in the rotation, on the south wall of the outer district — addressed a junction-class aerial formation at 2315. The formation's area discharge hit him before his second lance connected. He went down. The officer beside him — Maret, twenty-three — took the formation down with the third lance while simultaneously keeping her hand on Tarkov's collar so he didn't slide off the wall. The Wildfire medic at the south wall's relay point was there in thirty seconds. Tarkov was up in ninety seconds. Bleeding from the right forearm and not the same shade of pale he'd been at the beginning of the engagement, but up. He said: "I hate aerial formations." Maret said: "I know. The formation to your left." "I see it," he said. He addressed the formation to his left. On the east wall, two Guard officers spent twenty minutes holding a position against a cluster that should have required a full unit of six to address. They had a Wildfire specialist with them who had not technically been assigned to combat but had decided, looking at the two Guard officers and the cluster, that the assignment was theoretical in the face of what was in front of them. All three addressed the cluster. The cluster eventually yielded. At the northwest residential boundary — Sampo's territory — three Guard units and a Wildfire team held the junction corridor against a formation density that the monitoring station's instruments could not classify because the instruments had been calibrated for Fragmentum densities that were assumed to be the maximum. They held it by doing the things the training had told them to do and by doing them correctly and by refusing, in the specific way of people who were holding something precious behind them, to let anything through. None of them had names that appeared in the battle record. They were background. They were not background to themselves. —— At 2331, the Stellaron produced a sound that was different from everything before it. Not the scream from the crown fracture. Not the resonance output it had been using against Svarog's units. Something that came from deeper in the geological structure, from the primary mass below the visible crystal form — the specific output of something that had been building toward a secondary function since the moment of activation. Svarog identified it in three seconds. "IT IS SPAWNING!" His voice from the lead Ancient Gear Giant, loud enough to carry across the Restricted Zone without the comms. "THE STELLARON IS SPAWNING SEPARATE FORMS! ALL UNITS—" The ground of the Restricted Zone cracked in four places simultaneously. Through the cracks came the spawns. They were thirty meters tall. They had the body architecture of the Stellaron itself — crystalline, primarily, but not the blinding prismatic light of the parent form. The spawns were darker. The crystal structures were the color of deep stone and hot metal, fissured with lines of raw energy that glowed red-orange like something was burning inside the crystalline mass. They had limbs — four, each ending in claws that were not quite claw-shaped but were the geological equivalent of them, the crystal structure thickening to points at the extremities. They had heads — not faces, but orientations, the specific directional quality of something that could track and target. Four spawns. One in the north, one in the east, one in the south, one advancing directly at the outer district boundary. Each one thirty meters tall. Each one heading toward the city. —— "ALL UNITS REDIRECT!" Gepard's voice on the comms. Then: "Svarog — hold the Stellaron. Luka — the east spawn. I'll take north." "SOUTH SPAWN IS IN MY RANGE!" Seele, from the forward line. She had already moved — had already been moving before the comms instruction, had felt the spawn emerge through the geological structure's vibration before it came through the surface. "Seele — the south spawn, clear it before it reaches the boundary. Then get back." "I'll be fast," she said. Which was not quite a promise but was not quite not one either. The comms broke into pieces as everyone went to their respective spawns. Svarog held the formation against the primary Stellaron — not advancing, holding, keeping the Ancient Gear Giants in a defensive line that prevented the primary form from moving toward the city while the engagement dispersed to the spawns. Gepard went north. —— The north spawn was thirty meters of dark crystalline force advancing toward the outer wall where Dunn was holding the line with twelve Guard units. Gepard crossed the Restricted Zone at the speed that was not a run. He reached the north spawn at the outer wall boundary and he looked at it and the north spawn looked at him — or oriented toward him, the specific directional quality of something that had been told by its parent what the most significant threat source was. The north spawn hit first. It brought one of its crystalline foreleg formations down at Gepard's position with a force that was different from the Second Stellaron's armored form — not armor impact, geological impact, the weight of thirty meters of crystalline mass concentrating into a single point. He wasn't there when it hit. He was thirty meters to the right, and the ice came up at the spawn's foreleg formation in an ice lance that hit the base joint of the foreleg where the crystalline coherence was thinnest. The joint cracked. The spawn staggered. Behind Gepard, from the outer wall: Dunn. "Sir — we can help—" "HOLD THE WALL!" Gepard moved. "NOBODY COMES INTO THE ZONE FOR THIS ONE!" "Sir—" "HOLD THE WALL, DUNN!" A pause. "Holding," Dunn said. His voice was very flat and specific in the way it was when he had made a decision to follow an order he profoundly disagreed with and was going to do it correctly regardless. Gepard drove into the north spawn. —— On the outer district's east side, Luka reached the east spawn at 2338. It was coming up through the junction six corridor — not the surface, the corridor itself, which meant it was thirty meters of crystalline spawn emerging through the infrastructure of the outer machine district, cracking junction walls and the generator housings and the notation-channeled stone that the Architects had built into this district's geological foundation. The Guard units in the east corridor had already pulled back. They were not retreating — they were clearing space for Luka, which was the specific intelligence of Guard officers who had been in the same engagement as him for forty-three minutes and had correctly calibrated what their role was relative to what his was. He raised the cannon. The danger level was extreme. Junction corridor, thirty-meter spawn, its crystalline body cracking the corridor walls as it moved, the activation frequency from the parent Stellaron running through the geological structure making every mineral surface vibrate. The cannon's charge rate maxed out. He fired. The large burst — not the standard small burst, the one that needed time to build and had a cooldown and produced output that was calibrated to the danger level of the situation he was in. It hit the east spawn's primary crystalline mass at the shoulder section. The spawn screamed. Same register as the parent Stellaron when the crown fracture hit. The shoulder section cracked — not fractured, cracked, the crystalline coherence disrupted at the impact point. Luka fired again. The cooldown was still running — the small burst this time, a fraction of the output, but directed at the crack the large burst had made. The crack expanded. The east spawn oriented on him. He moved. Full sandevistan burst. The junction corridor disappeared and he was at the other end of it before the spawn's crystalline foreleg hit the floor where he'd been. The impact left a crater in the junction corridor floor six meters across. He looked at it. He looked at the spawn. He thought: *one more large burst.* The cooldown cleared. The danger level — junction corridor, crater at his feet, thirty-meter spawn, full parent Stellaron frequency through the walls — registered as extreme in a new register. The cannon's charge rate went above the previous maximum. He fired. —— Seele reached the south spawn at 2340. It was at the outer boundary of the Restricted Zone's south face, advancing toward the forward line. The twelve fighters of the forward line had already dispersed to address the Fragmentum response increase that the spawn's movement was generating — the spawn's crystalline presence was feeding the response in its immediate area, every form in a two-hundred-meter radius gaining coherence density from the spawn's frequency output. The forward line was occupied. The south spawn was thirty meters of unaddressed crystalline threat at the zone boundary. Seele went at it. She went vertical first — thirty meters up, above the spawn's tracking range, because the spawn's orientation was ground-level and she was going to use the height the same way she'd used the cave's height against the Second Stellaron's second stage. From thirty meters above the spawn, she looked down at its primary mass. The butterfly quality was fully present. Not the small increment. Not the building edge of something approaching its full expression. The specific quality of Seele Vollerei twenty-three years old, having received what Fuli had given her and having trained it into something operational, standing in the air thirty meters above a thirty-meter crystalline spawn of the First Stellaron and knowing exactly what she was going to do. The stigmata field extended outward from her position as she descended. The spawn's crystalline surface received the marks — visible as faint luminescent points on the crystal structure, scattered across the primary mass. Not one mark. Twelve. Twelve points of Herrscher of Rebirth stigmata energy distributed across the spawn's coherence structure. She drove the scythe into the primary mass at the top of her descent with every ounce of the old-gold rebirth energy she could concentrate into the blade. The blade hit the first stigmata point. The stigmata network activated. All twelve marks simultaneously produced the rebirth pulse — the specific energy of something that refused ending, distributed across twelve points in the spawn's crystalline structure.
