The coherence of the crystal mass didn't crack from the outside. It unraveled from twelve simultaneous interior points, the crystalline structure losing its coherence from within. The south spawn dissolved. Not shattered. Not exploded. Dissolved — the way the cave formations had dissolved when the notation network's source material reached them, but faster and more complete. The crystalline mass came apart at its molecular coherence level and the individual crystal components scattered across the south face of the zone as non-aggressive mineral particulate. Seele landed. She looked at where the spawn had been. She said: "Huh." Then her comms: "Seele — south spawn status?" "Dissolved," she said. A pause. "Dissolved?" "I dissolved it." She was already moving toward the primary zone. "Going back." "Seele—" "I'm going back, Gepard. How's the north spawn?" A beat. "Working on it." "Work faster," she said. "Svarog needs the formation." She went back to the primary zone. —— The north spawn went down at 2349. Gepard hit it three times with the ice lance at maximum compression — not the Manchester Smash, the pre-smash expression, the selection-rate output that was the most efficient use of what he had. The third lance hit the primary coherence junction at the spawn's chest section and the crystalline structure fractured across its full width. The spawn's coherence failed. The form came apart from the fracture outward, the crystalline mass losing integrity in the specific way that frequency-disrupted crystals lost integrity, and the north spawn was the mineral particulate at the north face of the Restricted Zone. He looked at his accounting. He was at forty-eight percent. He'd been running the engagement for fifty-seven minutes. He'd been hit by the Stellaron's focused discharge twice — the shield had held both times, the kinetic transfer hadn't done structural damage, but it had cost him. He'd hit the crown fracture. He'd addressed the north spawn. He'd been moving continuously since the engagement started. Forty-eight percent. The Ice Flames were very present. Very ready. Not yet. He went back to the primary zone. —— Svarog's formation had taken losses. Unit twelve was down — the one from the focused discharge. Unit seventeen had lost its right-side cannon equivalent — the weapon section had been destroyed by a wing impact, the unit itself was still mobile and still in the formation. Unit nine had taken a resonance impact that had damaged its primary locomotion — it was moving at thirty percent speed but it was still moving. Twenty-two of twenty-five units still in the field. The Ancient Gear Giants were doing what they had been built for: holding the line against the primary Stellaron while the smaller threats were addressed. They weren't winning. They were working. The difference between holding a line and winning a line was significant and Svarog understood this and had built the units for the former because the latter was not something you built units for. You built units to keep the primary threat busy while the person who could do something about it got into position. He watched through the lead unit's sensor array as Gepard crossed the Restricted Zone from the north. He watched as Seele came from the south at the specific movement quality that made every sensor on every unit track her simultaneously because the sensors were calibrated for threat signatures and she had the specific energy signature of something that the threat classification system could not categorize. He had a hypothesis about that. He filed the hypothesis for after. "GEPARD!" He routed his voice through the lead unit's external speaker at full output. "THE WING JOINT — RIGHT WING — THE STRUCTURAL POINT AT THE PRIMARY ATTACHMENT—" Gepard had already seen it. He was moving. —— This was the moment: 2358, thirteen minutes before midnight, fifty-three minutes into the battle for Belobog. Gepard at the primary Stellaron. Seele to his left. Svarog's formation in the center. The east spawn was down — Luka had taken it down with the cannon's large burst and had then gone to address the Fragmentum response in the east corridor because that was what the situation required. The west spawn, which nobody had mentioned because everyone had assumed Svarog's units would get it and they had, was mineral particulate on the west face of the zone. The primary Stellaron. One hundred and fifty meters. Crown fracture at twelve percent propagation. Wing structural damage from Svarog's formation — three units had impacted the left wing repeatedly and the left wing's articulation was compromised. Right wing fully intact. Forty-eight percent, Gepard's accounting. Not yet. Not the Manchester Smash. Not yet. But closer. He looked at the right wing joint. He looked at the Stellaron. He said: "Seele. Left flank. I need the left wing addressed so it can't deploy a recovery discharge." "Left wing," she said, already moving. "Svarog — the crown. If the front formation drives up—" "Understood," Svarog said. Gepard looked at the right wing joint. The Stellaron was tracking all three of them — him, Seele, Svarog's formation — and the three-direction tracking was costing it. The discharge build time was longer when the form was tracking three threat sources. He could see it in the crown's light quality — the focused output cycle was slower. He moved. He crossed the Restricted Zone floor at the third category of movement — the one that was not a run — and he hit the right wing joint with the ice lance at ninety percent compression. Not maximum. Ninety percent. One step below the Manchester Smash, the most he could spend without spending the Smash itself. The lance hit the wing joint. The wing joint cracked. Wider than the crown fracture. Deeper. The specific fracture of a structural joint in a crystalline form that had been built by geological force and was now being addressed by something that was in the same frequency range and opposing it. The right wing started to fail. The Stellaron noticed. It produced the resonance output — the full-form output, the one that had bent the generator housings in the outer district — directed at Gepard's position. At point-blank range, which was where he was because he was at the wing joint, which was fifteen meters from the primary mass. He brought the shield. Maximum compression. Everything he had in the shield. He had two seconds to build it before the resonance hit and he built it in two seconds because eight years of Restricted Zone service had been building toward that two-second construction time. The resonance hit. He went backward forty meters across the Restricted Zone floor. The shield held. He didn't stop until he hit the inner stone face of the zone's northern boundary. He sat against the stone for a moment. He looked at his accounting. Forty-three percent. The shield had held but the kinetic transfer had cost him five percent — he'd been at forty-eight, now he was at forty-three. Seele appeared at his position. She'd gotten there faster than he expected. She crouched beside him with the scythe in her hand and the butterfly quality radiating warmth and she said, bluntly: "How bad?" "I'm fine," he said. "That was forty meters." "I'm fine." He started getting up. She gave him a hand — not because he needed one, but because it was there. He took it. "The wing joint is cracked." "I know, I could see it from the left flank." She looked at the Stellaron. "The form is going to orient the right wing toward the formation now that the joint is compromised. It's going to try to use the compromised wing before it loses function." "Yes," he said. "So we need to be back before it does that." "Yes." She looked at him. One second of the full look — the one that existed when the situation was real and she wasn't pretending it wasn't. "Don't go past your limit out here," she said. "What you're going to need for the end — you need it to still be there." He looked at her. She looked back. She knew about the Manchester Smash — she'd been in the cave when he'd used it. She knew what it cost. "I know what I'm doing," he said. "I know you know," she said. "I'm saying it anyway." She turned. "Left wing." She went. He went back to the primary zone. At 0003, the First Stellaron deployed the compromised right wing in a downstroke that was aimed at Svarog's formation — the largest cluster of threat in the zone — and the downstroke hit five of the Ancient Gear Giants simultaneously. Two of them went down. Three of them staggered but held. Svarog's voice, from the lead unit: "FORMATION! CLOSE!" Seventeen of twenty-three mobile units closed. The formation contracted. The Stellaron deployed the wing for a second downstroke. Gepard hit the right wing joint again. Same point. Same compression. The joint failed. The right wing dropped. Ninety-meter span of crystalline formation, the activation frequency radiating from every facet, falling against the primary body as the joint gave completely. The Stellaron couldn't prevent it. The joint was gone. The primary body lit up differently. Not the crown. Not the wing. The central mass — the specific light quality that Serval's frequency analysis had identified as the primary coherence point, the place where all the crystalline structure's coherence was organized from. "SERVAL!" Gepard on the comms. "I SEE IT!" From the secondary relay. "THE PRIMARY COHERENCE POINT IS EXPOSING — THE WING LOSS IS CHANGING THE STRUCTURAL GEOMETRY — THE POINT IS REACHABLE NOW, IT WASN'T BEFORE — GEPARD IF YOU CAN HIT THE PRIMARY COHERENCE POINT—" "How much output?" "AS MUCH AS YOU HAVE!" He looked at his accounting. Forty percent. The Ice Flames, pressing. The Manchester Smash. He looked at the Stellaron. At the primary coherence point, newly visible in the geometry of the form without the right wing's structural contribution. At the one hundred and fifty meters of crystalline force standing in the northern section of the Restricted Zone, radiating blinding prismatic light, twenty-three of its opponent's mechanical units still in the field, its crown fractured at twelve percent, both wings compromised. Not defeated. Not close to defeated. But for the first time in the engagement, reachable. He raised both hands. The ice came up. And the Ice Flames came with it. —— He drove both fists into the ground. Not the floor of a cave this time. The geological surface of the Restricted Zone itself, the notation-channeled stone that the Architects had built the network through, the stone that connected to every carving and channel in the outer machine district and the Restricted Zone's own geological foundation. The Ice Flames entered the network. Not the cave-local propagation of the Manchester Smash. The full notation network, every channel, every carving, the Restricted Zone's full geological foundation — all of it receiving the Ice Flames at thirty-eight percent of Gepard Landau's ceiling. The Restricted Zone lit up. Not the Stellaron's light. Not the activation frequency. The specific blue-white of the Ice Flames, propagating through the geological foundation simultaneously, visible at every notation channel point as a different quality of light — colder, more precise, with the specific character of something that was opposing the Stellaron's frequency rather than adding to it. The disruption hit the Stellaron's crystalline form from every direction simultaneously. From below, through the geological foundation. From the sides, through the notation channels running through the zone's stone faces. From above, through the surface propagation. Every mineral surface in the Restricted Zone conducting the disruption. The primary coherence point received it from every direction at once. The Stellaron screamed. The sound hit the outer district from four hundred meters away and the residential stacks' windows shattered and the Guard units on the outer walls held their positions and held their ears simultaneously. The activation frequency from the form spiked and then — for the first time in the engagement — faltered. A flicker in the prismatic light. A break in the activation output. Three seconds of the light becoming inconsistent, the frequency fluctuating, the form's crystal coherence struggling against the disruption propagating through its own geological foundation. Three seconds. Then the coherence recovered. The light stabilized. The Stellaron was still standing. But the crown fracture had expanded. From twelve percent to twenty-seven. The primary coherence point's geometry had shifted — the fracture propagation from the Ice Flames had reached the crown and the crown fracture and the primary coherence point were now connected in the form's structural map. Gepard was on his knees at the impact point. Twenty-nine percent. Serval's voice, from the relay: "Crown fracture at twenty-seven. The primary coherence point is connected to the fracture network. The form is structurally compromised. Not defeated — compromised. The next two Manchester Smashes hit the same geometry and it will fail." "Two more," Gepard said. "Yes." "At what output." A pause. "More than thirty-eight percent each. The disruption needs to exceed the Stellaron's coherence recovery capacity. You need to saturate the fracture network." He looked at his accounting. Twenty-nine percent remaining. He needed two more Manchester Smashes at more than thirty-eight percent each. He was looking at math that did not resolve in the direction of having anything left at the end of it. He got up off his knees. He looked at the Stellaron. "All right," he said. Quietly. To the Stellaron, to the zone, to the city behind him. "Two more." Svarog, from the lead unit: "GEPARD. YOU CANNOT—" "Hold the formation," Gepard said. "I need time. Give me time." A pause. The pause of someone running four hundred years of calculation. Then: "Formation! HOLD THE CENTER LINE! GIVE HIM TIME!" The twenty-three remaining Ancient Gear Giants moved into the center line. And the battle continued. —— END OF CHAPTER 53 —— In the inner palace, Bronya had been fighting since before the ice plates broke. She had gone to the northern section at 2240 — seven minutes before the activation — because the frequency through the palace floor had become something she could not stay inside from. The geological pressure was not a sound anymore. It was a physical presence in the stone, in the ground, in the air of the Restricted Zone. She had stood at the northern section's contact point and she had received the frequency. Not the warm sessions of the previous weeks — this was the full activation frequency, coming up through the stone at power levels that had no precedent in the eight-hundred-year record. She received it and she did not direct it and she did not run. She stood in it. The specific quality of Bronya Rand at twenty-six years old as the Supreme Guardian of Belobog, which was: she did not run from things that were her responsibility. This was her responsibility. The First Stellaron's activation frequency hit her like a physical force — not a kinetic impact, a frequency impact. The specific pressure of the Aeon of Destruction's work at its geological maximum, pressing upward through the stone and through her position at the contact point and looking, in the specific way that an overwhelming force could look, for something to overcome. She didn't let it overcome her. This was not a small thing. The activation frequency at full output would have driven most people off their feet. It had driven the geological structure of the Restricted Zone to fracture — the same geological structure that had been there for eight hundred years. It had shattered ice plates and displaced stone and sent fragment craters twelve meters deep into the zone's surface. Bronya stood in it and she did not move. She thought about what Svarog had told her. *The Guardian's correct relationship to the energy is reception, not direction.* She was not trying to direct the activation frequency. She was receiving it — letting it pass through her the way the notation network let things pass through it, without stopping it, without absorbing it, without becoming it. The contact point's geological structure was the interface. She was standing on it. She was the interface. The activation frequency's pressure did not decrease. It increased. It was going to keep increasing for the duration of the engagement and it was going to be larger at every moment than it had been at the previous moment. She stood in it. Her hands were pressed to the stone of the northern section and the stone was shaking and the light was overwhelming and the frequency was a sound and a pressure and a physical force all simultaneously and she was receiving all of it and she was still standing. From outside, she could hear the battle. The Ancient Gear Giants — a sound she hadn't expected and that she processed in the specific way she processed unexpected things, which was: file it, continue. Svarog had built something. Svarog had been building something for decades without telling anyone. This was consistent with Svarog. She filed it. The Manchester Smash — she felt it through the stone before she heard it. The specific quality of the Ice Flames at full propagation through the notation network, conducted through the geological foundation she was standing on. The difference between the Stellaron's frequency and the Ice Flames' frequency was the difference between two opposing forces in the same frequency territory, and the point where they met — the contact point where she was standing — produced a resonance that she had no vocabulary for. She held the resonance. She thought about what she was doing. Not defending — the Guard, the fighters, the Ancient Gear Giants were defending. She was something else. She was holding the connection between the Stellaron's frequency and the geological network that Gepard's Ice Flames were propagating through. She was the interface through which the two frequencies were producing the disruption that was cracking the form. She was the Guardian standing at the contact point holding the channel open. It was not painless. It was not comfortable. The activation frequency at full output was not a thing you stood in without it costing something. She was bleeding at the nose — she could feel that without touching her face, the specific pressure of the frequency through the contact. Her vision was white at the edges from the prismatic light. Her hands, pressed to the stone of the contact point, were numb. She held the channel. When Gepard deployed the first Manchester Smash, the resonance at the contact point was — she had no word for it. It was the point where the Stellaron's frequency and the Ice Flames' frequency met at maximum output and the result of that meeting was an energy that went in both directions simultaneously. She held it. She thought: this is what the Architects built the network for. She thought: I am the thing that makes the network work. She thought: hold. She held. —— After the first Manchester Smash — after the crown fracture expanded to twenty-seven percent, after the primary coherence point connected to the fracture network, after Gepard went to his knees and got back up — the battle entered its second phase. Deeper. Harder. The Stellaron was compromised but not damaged, not in any sense that was going to resolve quickly. It responded to the Manchester Smash's disruption by contracting the activation frequency — pulling the output inward, making the primary mass denser, the specific geological response of something that had been struck at a vulnerability and was now protecting that vulnerability. The contraction made it smaller. Not one hundred and fifty meters anymore. One hundred and thirty. The crystalline form pulling in on itself, the wings folded against the primary mass, the crown's prismatic radiation concentrated rather than broadcast. Svarog said, from the lead unit: "IT IS CONCENTRATING. THE COHERENCE IS REORIENTING TO PROTECT THE FRACTURE NETWORK." "I see it," Gepard said. He was back on his feet. Twenty-nine percent. The Ice Flames were still present. "The new target geometry—" "The crown is no longer the primary point. The central mass concentration is." Svarog paused. "And it is protected now." "Protected how." "The contraction has rotated the primary coherence point toward the inside of the form. It is no longer accessible from the surface." A silence. "So the second Manchester Smash," Gepard said slowly. "Has to enter the form rather than disrupt it from the surface," Svarog confirmed. "Which requires either penetrating the contracted mass or—" He paused. "Or finding the point where the contraction creates a new vulnerability." "Contraction creates stress points," Seele said, on the comms. She was back at the primary zone. "The same principle as the wing joint. When the form contracted, it created new structural stress at the contraction boundaries." "Where?" Gepard said. "Give me thirty seconds," she said. She went vertical. Twenty meters above the contracted Stellaron, looking down at the form with the butterfly quality's spatial awareness — not just her eyes, the specific perception that existed in the Herrscher of Rebirth's state, the awareness of structural coherence that the stigmata field gave her. The same perception that had let her identify the south spawn's coherence structure and dissolve it from twelve points simultaneously. She looked at the contracted First Stellaron. She found the stress points. "There," she said, on the comms. "The contraction created six stress boundaries where the crystal structure's coherence is thinnest. Three on the north face, two on the east, one on the south." She paused. "If the Ice Flames enter through all six simultaneously—" "They can't enter simultaneously from the surface," Svarog said. "From the notation network they can," Serval said, from the relay. She sounded like she had been running calculations for twenty-five minutes and had just arrived at the answer. "The notation network runs under the full footprint of the Stellaron's position. If the Ice Flames enter the network at the contact point and propagate upward through the channels directly under the Stellaron's mass — the channels exit at the surface through every notation carving in the geological formation. Including the ones under the contracted form's footprint." "From below," Gepard said. "From below," Serval confirmed. "The second Manchester Smash needs to go into the geological foundation under the contracted form. Not at the form itself — under it. The channels will carry the disruption upward into all six stress points simultaneously." He thought about this. He was at twenty-nine percent. He had two Manchester Smashes left in him, possibly — if he was very careful and very lucky. He had one that needed to be aimed at the geological foundation under a one-hundred-and-thirty-meter crystalline mass that was actively contracting to protect its vulnerabilities. He had the Ice Flames at the surface of his expression and they were the thing that made this possible and they were waiting. "All right," he said. Seele landed beside him. "I can mark the stress points from above," she said. "If the stigmata field marks all six simultaneously before you deploy the smash—" "The marks will tell the disruption where to go," he said. "Yes." She looked at him. "Coordinate with me." He looked at her. He looked at the contracted Stellaron. He looked at his hands. "On your mark," he said. She looked at him for one more second. "When you're ready," she said. He was not going to get more ready. "Ready," he said. She went vertical. —— The second Manchester Smash hit the geological foundation of the Restricted Zone under the contracted Stellaron's footprint at 0031, thirty-one minutes into Thursday morning. Seele had marked all six stress points in the three seconds before the smash — the stigmata field at its maximum extension, all six marks placed before the Stellaron could understand what the marking was doing and respond to it. The marks were there, in the crystalline structure's stress boundaries, waiting for the disruption that was coming from below. The Ice Flames entered the notation network at the geological foundation and propagated upward. They hit all six marked stress points simultaneously. The Stellaron's contraction broke from the inside. Not the crown fracture — this was a different geometry entirely. The stress boundaries at all six contraction points cracked simultaneously, the crystalline coherence failing at the exact points that the contraction had made it thinnest. The primary mass — the contracted, protective center of the form — lost its structural integrity at six simultaneous points. The crack propagation connected all six points in three seconds. The fracture network was now complete. The crown fracture. The contraction stress cracks. The primary coherence point in the middle of all of it, connected to every fracture. The Stellaron's activation frequency dropped. Not to zero. Not defeated. But the form had lost the ability to maintain full activation output with a fracture network running through its primary coherence point and connecting to six stress boundary cracks and the twenty-seven-percent crown fracture. The output dropped to sixty percent of activation level. The prismatic light became — less. Still overwhelming. But less. Gepard was at seventeen percent. He was on his knees again at the geological foundation impact point. His hands were bleeding — the Ice Flames at this output had produced a thermal differential that had cracked the skin at his palms. Not severely. Present. He looked at his hands. He looked at the Stellaron. Seventeen percent. One more Manchester Smash. The third one. The one that Serval had said would be needed. He was at seventeen percent and the third Manchester Smash was not a seventeen-percent operation. He got up. He looked at the Stellaron. He thought: past eighty means past one hundred means this is what was always here and I was always going to find it. He thought: the city is behind me. He thought: Bronya is at the contact point and she has been there since 2240 and she is still there. He thought: one more. Svarog's voice, from the lead unit: "GEPARD." "I know," he said. "The form is at sixty percent activation. It is not finished. You need—" "I know," he said. "Give me time." A pause. Then Svarog said something that was not in Honkai Star Rail's written record and was not in the pre-Separation archive and was not in four hundred years of outer machine district management. He said: "I believe you can do it." Gepard looked at the lead Ancient Gear Giant. At the integrated consciousness inside it that had been watching this city longer than anyone alive could account for. He said: "I know." He turned back to the Stellaron. He raised both hands. The Ice Flames came. —— END OF CHAPTER 53 Her Shield, His Sun Chapter 52 The cold light did not go away. This was the thing that settled into the city over the following days — not the briefing numbers, not Pela's updated projections, not even the signal reading itself. Those were abstractions. Numbers on displays that required translation before they landed as real. What landed as real was the cold light, visible every morning through the ice plates in the northern section of the Restricted Zone, slightly brighter each time. You couldn't see it from the city. The Restricted Zone wasn't accessible to civilians on a regular basis, and the northern section specifically had been sealed to non-essential personnel since the Second Stellaron's defeat. But the Guard patrols saw it. Gepard's rotation units saw it. Pela saw it when she went to run readings. Rikov saw it on the overnight shift when the ambient glow was brighter against the darkness. They all said the same thing without coordinating: it's getting brighter. By Thursday night, three days after its first appearance, it was visible from outside the zone's barrier. You could stand at the outer wall and see the faint prismatic shimmer through the ice plates sixty meters away. Dunn called it at 2300 Thursday, standing at the outer wall with the display showing signal 4.0. He looked at the shimmer for a long moment. "Sir," he said. Gepard was beside him. "Yeah." "4.0." "Yeah." Dunn looked at the shimmer. "It was 3.9 this morning." "0.1 kilocycles in sixteen hours," Gepard said. "The rate is accelerating." Dunn was quiet for a moment. He was twenty-four years old and had been in the Guard since he was eighteen and had never missed a day that he was needed, and he had a specific quality in serious situations of looking at what was real instead of performing a reaction to what was real. He looked at the cold light. He looked at the signal reading. He ran the math, which Gepard could see him running. "That puts us at—" "Three weeks on the optimistic end," Gepard said. "If the rate keeps accelerating, could be less." Dunn absorbed this. He looked at the coverage map he was holding. It was the sixth revision this month. He'd done three of the revisions himself because Gepard had been at the outer wall and needed someone he trusted doing the numbers. "The northwest residential gap," he said. "Sampo has the corridor routes. I'll get them integrated into the rotation by morning." "And the medical relay at junction twelve—" "Natasha confirmed it yesterday." Dunn nodded. He looked at the cold light again. He said, quietly, more to himself than to Gepard: "The Second Stellaron was bad enough." "This is different," Gepard said. "Different how." Gepard thought about how to put it. He settled on the honest version. "The Second Stellaron was a thing we could break. It had structure, it had joints, it had a form with weak points that could be targeted. The First Stellaron—" he paused "—is a force. You don't break a force. You redirect it." Dunn looked at him. "Is that what we're doing? Redirecting it?" "That's what the notation network does. The Ice Flames in the network disrupt the crystalline structure. When the form's structural coherence fails, the force has nowhere to concentrate." He looked at the signal display. "That's the theory." "The theory," Dunn said. "We don't have a prior engagement to learn from. Svarog's records describe the eight-hundred-year escalation. They don't describe a successful engagement. They describe the escalation peaking at 4.1 and slowly subsiding over seventy years." "Which means nobody's actually beaten one of these before." "Not that Svarog knows of," Gepard said. "Which is not a small qualifier." Dunn was quiet for a moment. Then he said: "Good thing we have you then." Gepard looked at him. "The Ice Flames," Dunn said. "Nobody else on this planet has them. Nobody else on this planet has probably ever had them." He held Gepard's gaze steadily. "You didn't get them from training. You got them from a year on the northern ridge that should have killed you and didn't. They exist because you went somewhere nobody else would go and you came back." He paused. "They're for this." Gepard looked at his hands. Standard ice expression ambient in the skin — the cool that had been there since he was seventeen, the one that had evolved over eight years of Restricted Zone service and one year at the edge of mortality. The Ice Flames lived at the limit of it. Past the limit. "Yeah," he said. "They are." They stood at the outer wall in the cold of the Thursday night, the cold light visible through the ice plates, the signal at 4.0 and climbing. —— The city was told on Friday. Not everything — Bronya had the same instinct she'd had before the Second Stellaron's activation, which was that information given without context produced panic, and panic produced deaths. What the city needed was not a full briefing. It needed the important parts: something is coming, we know about it, we're prepared, here's what you should do. She stood at the broadcast podium at noon and said: "In the next three to four weeks, Belobog will experience the activation of the First Stellaron. I want to be honest with you about what this means. The First Stellaron is a genuine threat. It is larger and more dangerous than the Second Stellaron, which we defeated six months ago. This is not a situation we are walking into unprepared — we have been preparing for months and we will continue preparing until the moment of activation. "The Silvermane Guard and Wildfire are deploying at full readiness. The outer district will go to restricted access in the next seven days. Residential sections in the outer machine district should have evacuation readiness — the Guard will distribute specific guidance for your section by end of day. "I know this is frightening. I'm not going to tell you it isn't. But I want you to know what I know: the people who are going to stand between this city and the First Stellaron are the same people who defeated the Second Stellaron. They've done something that has never been done before in the history of this planet. They will do it again. "Trust them. Prepare. Take care of each other." She stepped back from the podium. The Strategic Council behind her — twelve people who had watched the broadcast live — had twelve different versions of the expression of a person receiving information about an existential threat. Barros had the version that was also a reassessment. He'd been reassessing her at an increasing rate since the Second Stellaron briefing and had arrived, she thought, at a position considerably different from where he'd started. He said: "How confident are you? Genuinely." She looked at him. "Genuinely: high. We know what's coming. We know what it requires. We have the people to provide it." She paused. "I won't tell you it's guaranteed. Nothing is guaranteed. But I will tell you that I have never been more certain of the people I'm going to war with." He looked at her for a long moment. Then he nodded. Just that. Nodded and was quiet. She went to find Gepard. —— He was at the east wall of the outer district reviewing the coverage map with the specific focused patience he had during the preparation phases — not the urgency of active engagement, the steady work of making sure every detail was correct before the urgency arrived. She came to stand beside him. "The broadcast." "I heard it from the display in the patrol room." He didn't look up from the map. "You said it right." "Barros asked how confident I was." "What did you say?" "High." He looked up then. At her face. He saw what he saw and went back to the map. "Good." She watched him work for a moment. He had a way of working — completely absorbed, nothing performative about it, the map and the positions and the rotation and the coverage getting his full attention without any remainder. She'd been watching it for months, the way he approached operational problems. There was something clean about it. Something that had no wasted motion. "How are you doing," she said. Not about the signal. He set down the map. He looked at her directly, which he did less often than she might have expected — when he was in operational mode, looking directly at her sometimes required putting down whatever he was doing, which was a tell she'd noticed. He was telling her: this is worth putting it down. "I'm good," he said. "I mean that." "Are you nervous?" He thought about this honestly. "I don't know if nervous is the right word. There's—" He paused. "There's something that comes with knowing you're approaching the largest thing you've ever approached. It's not fear exactly. It's more like—" "Like the drop before something significant." "Yes." He looked at her. "You know that?" "The night before the Second Stellaron's activation. Standing at the inner perimeter with the monitoring display running." She looked at the outer district, at the generators and the notation-channeled walls. "The drop before. Yes." He nodded. "That's what it is. But it's not—" He considered. "I came back from the northern ridge knowing something. About what exists past the limit. The Ice Flames came from what existed past the limit." He looked at his hands. "I'm not walking into this without knowing it's possible. I walked into the northern ridge without knowing it was possible and I walked back out. Whatever is past eighty percent—" He paused. "I've earned it." She looked at him. He was twenty-five years old and had been the strongest person on this planet since he was twenty-two and had spent three years acting like that was a responsibility rather than a distinction, which it was, and which was also the thing that made it a distinction. She thought about what it meant that he was not afraid. Not performing fearlessness — actually not afraid. The northern ridge had done something to his relationship with limits. She thought she understood what that was. "When it happens," she said. "If it gets bad. At the wall." "It's going to get bad at the wall," he said. Matter-of-fact. "If it gets very bad." She held his gaze. "Come back." "Yes," he said. Always the same word. Always the same weight. She reached out and adjusted his collar — the small gesture of someone reaching for a reason to touch, which she'd been doing since the kitchen at 0615 all those weeks ago and had stopped pretending she wasn't doing. He let her, which he always did, which was its own kind of answer. "Three weeks," she said. "Possibly less." "Possibly less," he agreed. "A lot can happen in three weeks." He almost smiled. "We've established this." She dropped her hand. She looked at the outer district — the generators, the Guard patrols, the cold of the morning, the dome in its midday simulation. Her city. His city. Both of theirs in different registers. "Come to the Landau house tonight," she said. He looked at her. "Lynx invited me. She's been texting me since Tuesday." She looked at him. "Did you know she texts me?" "I suspected," he said. "She sends me medical information. About sleep. And nutrition." She paused. "She also sent me a soup recipe with the note that it is what soup is supposed to taste like and she implies you do not know this." "The soup was fine." "The soup was, quote, what happens when someone treats cooking as a structural problem rather than a creative practice." She raised an eyebrow. "Her words." He looked at his map. "She's not wrong." "No," Bronya said. "She's not." She turned toward the palace. "Tonight. 1900." "I'll be there," he said. —— The Landau house on Friday at 1900 had a quality it hadn't had before. The previous dinners had been introductions — Bronya and the house finding each other, Lynx running her assessment, Gepard navigating the gap between his professional self and the fact of having his sister and the person he loved in the same room for the first time. Tonight it was something past that. Bronya arrived with a bottle of something she'd been told was appropriate for dinner and which Lynx received with the kind of enthusiasm that communicated: yes, this was the correct thing. "He would have brought bread," Lynx said. "I was going to bring bread," Gepard said. "That's why I got her instead," Lynx said, putting the bottle on the counter. She turned back to the stove. "Sit down, it's almost ready." Bronya sat. Gepard sat. Bronya looked at the kitchen — the same kitchen, the same photos on the wall, the same bookshelves visible through the doorway. She had been here twice now and it still had the quality of something she was still learning. You couldn't learn a home in two visits. Three visits was the beginning of it. "The soup," she said. Lynx pointed at the pot on the stove. "Real soup." "I wasn't going to—" "I know you weren't going to say anything," Lynx said. "I'm saying something." She stirred the pot. "The first time he made soup he said it was fine and I said it was not fine and he said that fine was the standard and I said fine was not the standard for food, fine was the standard for situations that required enduring." She turned. "Am I wrong?" "You're not wrong," Bronya said. "Thank you." She turned back. "He has improved. The soup he made last week was genuinely fine. In the food sense." "I'm hearing this as a compliment," Gepard said. "It was a compliment." Lynx brought the pot to the table. "A small one. Don't inflate it." The dinner was good. The soup was — better than fine. Bronya ate all of it and accepted a second bowl, which Lynx noted without comment in the way of someone who had expected this outcome and was pleased by it. The conversation went: the broadcast (Lynx had watched it, had opinions, all of which were more precise than the Strategic Council's), the medical contingency (Lynx walked Bronya through the mobile response team structure with the brisk efficiency of a nineteen-year-old who had spent two years in Natasha's orbit and had absorbed thoroughness by proximity), and then sideways into things that had nothing to do with the First Stellaron. Lynx said: "Serval told me Dunn used to be in her band." Gepard looked up from his soup. "She mentioned it casually," Lynx said. "But she mentioned it. Which means it's on her mind." She looked at her brother. "Did you know he had a crush on her when he was sixteen?" "Yes," Gepard said. "Did you do anything about it?" "No." "Why not?" "It wasn't my business." Lynx looked at Bronya. "He's like this about everything personal," she said. "Extremely observant. Completely non-interventionist." "I'm not non-interventionist," Gepard said. "You watched Dunn have a crush on Serval for six years and said nothing." "Eight years." Lynx stared at him. "Eight years?" "He was sixteen when she was twenty. The band. He's been—" Gepard paused. "He's been aware of her for a long time." "And you said nothing for eight years." "What was I supposed to say?" "Oh, I don't know. 'Hey Serval, Dunn thinks you're extraordinary'?" "He was sixteen." "She was twenty. Not a big gap." "It was a significant gap at the time." Lynx considered this. "And now?" "Now it's not," he said. "And it seems to be—" He looked for the word. "Developing." Lynx looked at Bronya. "He is the least romantic person alive." "He's romantic in a very specific way," Bronya said. Gepard turned to look at her. She was eating her soup with the expression of someone who had said a completely neutral thing. "A very specific way," Lynx repeated. "Which is what?" "He does the thing where he's very steady," Bronya said. "Reliable. Present when it matters. It's not flashy. But it accumulates." Lynx looked between them. She had the assessing quality that was clearly hereditary. "I see," she said. "It's a good way to be," Bronya added. "I know," Lynx said. She went back to her soup. "I just enjoy watching him be uncomfortable." "I'm not uncomfortable," Gepard said. Lynx and Bronya both looked at him. "I'm not," he said. "Mm," said both of them. He went back to his soup with the quality of a man who had decided that dignity was recoverable but required stopping the current line of conversation. Lynx refilled everyone's bowls without being asked. The dinner continued. After, Bronya helped with the dishes, which was not something she had done before. There wasn't a particular plan to it — Lynx had started clearing the table and Bronya had stood up and helped and Gepard had looked at this and then at his sister, who was looking back at him with the expression that said: I like her. He washed. Bronya dried. Lynx sat on the counter and talked about the mobile response team's route mapping while they worked, which was the Lynx way of covering multiple tasks simultaneously. Gepard listened to both the route mapping and the specific quality of Bronya drying dishes in his kitchen, which was its own kind of information. At 2100 Bronya put on her coat. She was at the door before Gepard came down the hall to say goodbye, and she turned when she heard him. "Thank you," she said. "For—" she gestured at the kitchen, at the house "—this." "Thank Lynx," he said. "It was hers." "I did." She looked at him. "It was also yours." He held her gaze. There was something in the doorway of the Landau house that was different from the corridors and the outer wall and the briefing rooms — all the places they'd had the real conversations. The doorway had a quality of between, of something that existed in the transition between the house and the city. She reached out and touched his face. His cheek, brief, the flat of her palm. He stayed very still. "Three weeks," she said. "Three weeks," he said. "Then we win." She looked at him. The thing behind her eyes that he had been cataloguing since before he had the vocabulary for it — the layers of her, the Guardian and the person underneath the Guardian and the person she was with him specifically. All of it present at once. "Yes," she said. "We do." She walked back to the palace. He stood in the doorway and watched her go, which was not a thing he usually did — standing and watching — but was a thing he did tonight, because some evenings were worth watching from the beginning to the end. Lynx appeared at his elbow from nowhere. "I like her." "I know you do." "I told you I would." "You told me before she arrived. You hadn't actually met her yet." "I had enough data." She leaned against the doorframe. "She's what you need. Not comfortable — that's not the right word. She's—" She thought about it. "She takes you seriously. Not the rank. You." She looked at him. "Not everyone does." He looked at his sister. She was nineteen and had their mother's cheekbones and their father's bluntness and had been doing the work of being a medic in the outer district for two years without ever treating it as anything other than what it was: the most important work available to her. "You're good at this," he said. "At dinner?" "At people." She shrugged. "Medical practice. You learn to read what people actually need versus what they say they need." She pushed off the doorframe. "She needs you to come back. In three weeks. Do that and the rest is fine." "Working on it," he said. "Work harder," she said, and went inside. He stood in the doorway for another moment. The dome in its evening cycle, the city breathing, the cold of the night which was still the same cold it had always been. He thought about the cold light in the northern section and the signal at 4.0 and the three weeks Pela had given them. He thought about what was on the other side of three weeks. He thought about coming back. He went inside and helped Lynx finish the dishes. —— The next morning, Seele went to see Bronya. This was not something they'd been doing regularly — they were best friends who had been in the same city for months and had been treating it like they had infinite time, which they did not. Seele had figured this out at approximately 0800 on Saturday morning while she was reading the broadcast transcript on her phone and thought: three weeks. Possibly less. She sent a message: I'm coming over. Afternoon. Bronya responded: I have the three o'clock briefing. Seele sent: I'll be there at four. There was a pause. Then: all right. She arrived at 1600 and found Bronya in the study with papers and tea and the particular quality of someone who had been in meetings for six hours and had the specific tired of someone whose brain had been running hard rather than their body. Seele looked at her for a second and said: "Are you actually okay or are you just managing it?" Bronya looked up. "Both." "Good." Seele sat down. "Tell me." Bronya set down her pen. She looked at her friend — the specific look she gave Seele that was different from the look she gave anyone else, because Seele was the person she'd known before the Guardianship and the Guardianship and the Second Stellaron and the cold light. Before all of it. The look that existed before the management layer. "The frequency in the northern section," she said. "It's in the palace now. At night. Through the floor." She looked at her hands. "I wake up to it. Most nights. It's not — dangerous. I'm not in danger. It's just—" "Loud?" "Present." She looked at Seele. "Like someone knocking on the door of a house that they know is empty but they're knocking anyway. Insistently." Seele took this in. She thought about the Second Stellaron's second heartbeat — the thing she and Luka had been able to feel through the junction floor, warm and specific, their signatures recognized and responded to. The First Stellaron was different. The First Stellaron was not warm. "Svarog explained it?" she said. "The sound is seismic. The Stellaron moving toward the surface. The Guardian's attunement means I'm the most sensitive receiver." She paused. "I understand what it is. That makes it easier. But it doesn't make it quiet." "No," Seele said. "Understanding something and it not being difficult are different things." "Yes." Bronya looked at her. "How are you? Genuinely." "Genuinely?" Seele thought about it. "I'm—" She looked at the window. "I'm more ready than I was for the Second Stellaron. The cave did something. I don't have the full vocabulary for it but I know what I can do and I know how to do it and when the First Stellaron activates I'm going to do it." She paused. "What I'm not ready for is Luka at the perimeter." Bronya was quiet. "Not that he can't handle it," Seele said. "He can handle it. He's been building toward it for months, the weapon, the training, the chrome arm." She looked at Bronya directly. "I know he can. The part that's hard is knowing I'm going to be in a different section and I won't be able to see." "Gepard," Bronya said. Just the name. "Right," Seele said. They looked at each other. Two people who were in love with people who were going to be at the wall during the worst thing that had ever come for this city. "We're going to be at the forward line," Seele said. "Both of us. We're both in the engagement. I won't be at the wall but I'll be in the middle of it." "I know." "Which is terrifying in a different way but—" Seele stopped. "Actually I'm less scared about that part. The butterfly quality — whatever Fuli did when she brought me back — I can feel it now like a second heartbeat. It's just there. Whatever comes at me, I know I can take it." She paused. "What scares me is the eleven minutes I'm not in the same place as him." Bronya put her hand on Seele's arm. Not a management gesture — a real one. "He's going to come back." "He always says yes to that," Seele said. "He says it the way that means he means it." "It's the way Gepard says it too," Bronya said. "Every time." They sat with that for a moment. The study was quiet. Outside the dome was in its late afternoon simulation, the amber of 1600, the city continuing its business with the specific normalcy that cities maintained even when something enormous was approaching. That normalcy was its own kind of comfort — the shops open, the fight circuit on Tuesday, dinner at Lynx's house. The city continuing meant there was something to protect, which meant the protecting was worth doing. "After," Seele said. "After," Bronya agreed. "When this is over. I want to actually just— spend an afternoon. Not about anything." "I'd like that." Bronya looked at her. "We've spent months in the same city and most of it has been operational." "All of it," Seele said. "Essentially all of it has been operational." She raised an eyebrow. "We've been through two Stellaron plots and we've never just been somewhere together." "The Restricted Zone," Bronya said. "That was—" Seele paused. "That was also operational in its own way." "That's fair." Bronya almost smiled. "After. We go somewhere that isn't the Restricted Zone or the workshop or a briefing room." "The outer district," Seele said. "Boulder Town maybe. Luka has a fight in the circuit. I've been watching him train for months. I want to see the actual fight." "All right," Bronya said. "Boulder Town." She looked at Seele. "And you'll have to teach me how fights work. I've never actually watched one." Seele stared at her. "You're the Supreme Guardian of Belobog." "Yes." "Gepard is the First Class Singularity. He has fought in the circuit. You've been running a city for three years and never watched a fight?" "The Supreme Guardian doesn't typically—" "The Supreme Guardian," Seele said, "has been missing out." She sat back. "Okay. After. Boulder Town. I'll explain everything." "And I'll watch Luka fight." "And you'll watch Luka fight." Seele paused. "He wins. Just so you know. Not every time, but mostly." "How do you feel when he loses?" Seele thought about it. "Like I want to fight the other person on his behalf. But also — it's good for him when he loses. He learns things from it." She paused. "I wait until he's ready to hear that and then I tell him." "That's the right call," Bronya said. "I know." Seele stood up, which was the Seele way of ending a conversation that had reached its natural conclusion. "He's making dinner tonight. I need to get back before he decides to experiment." "Does he experiment with dinner?" "He experiments with everything." She put on her coat. "Last week he made something with the ingredients I bought for a different thing and it was—" she considered "—eighty percent correct. Which is better than it sounds." "It sounds like a very Luka outcome," Bronya said. "It absolutely is." She looked at Bronya from the doorway. "Go to sleep tonight." "I'll try." "Before midnight." "That's an aggressive target." "Before midnight, Bronya." Bronya looked at her. "Before midnight." "Good." Seele went to the door. "Come find me before the activation if you need to talk." She paused. "Actually — come find me regardless. Don't wait until you need to." Bronya looked at her. The Guardian and the person underneath the Guardian and the person she was with Seele specifically — all of it visible at once. "Thank you," she said. Seele went. The study was quiet. Bronya looked at her papers. She looked at the window. She picked up her pen, then put it down. She thought about Boulder Town and the fight circuit and after. She went to bed at 2330, which was before midnight by thirty minutes and which she considered a significant success given the circumstances. —— Luka's dinner, for the record, was good. He'd been working on the dish since the morning — something with vegetables and spices and the specific ambition of someone who had decided to learn cooking the same way he learned everything else, which was by identifying the principle and then applying it. The principle of this dish was: heat transforms. He had applied it moderately successfully. Seele sat at the kitchen table and watched him plate it with the focused concentration he brought to technical tasks. He put it in front of her. She tried it. "Well?" he said. "It's good," she said. "Be more specific." "The vegetables are right. The sauce is—" she tasted again "—close. The spicing is slightly off but in an interesting direction." He sat down. He tried his own. He was quiet for a moment. "The interesting direction," he said. "It's more than I would have done," she said. "But not wrong." He considered this. "More than you would have done but not wrong sounds like a compliment." "It is a compliment. I'm complimenting you." She ate more of it. "It's genuinely good, Luka. Stop waiting for the criticism." He looked at her for a second. "You're going to tell me to adjust the spicing next time." "Yes. But this time it's good." She pointed at his plate. "Eat." He ate. She ate. The apartment had the quality it had most evenings now — the specific warmth of two people who had their own ways of being in a space and had learned each other's rhythms well enough that the space felt like it was working around both of them rather than one of them accommodating the other. She read at the table sometimes while he was still working on something. He sat on the floor sometimes while she read. They had developed a language for the apartment that didn't require words to be functional. "The signal is at 4.0," he said. Not a segue — just the information, put on the table. "I know," she said. "The rate is accelerating." "I know." She looked at him. "Are you going to tell me things I already know all evening or are we going to talk?" He looked at his food. He was doing the jaw thing, which she had pointed out to him twice now and which he still claimed not to do. "I'm trying to figure out how to say something." "You could just say it." "It's—" He paused. "It's not bad. I just want to say it correctly." She put down her fork. She waited. "The training is done," he said. "The weapon is calibrated. The arm is integrated. Everything I was going to do to be ready—" he looked at his hands, the chrome and the biological "—I've done. I'm ready. That's not what I want to say." "What do you want to say?" He looked at her. The expression he had when something was coming up from the place below all his management, the place the cave had opened. "I'm not afraid of the activation," he said. "I'm not afraid of the perimeter or the Fragmentum response or what the First Stellaron looks like when it comes out. None of that." He paused. "What I'm afraid of is — after. Not after the battle. After after. If something—" He stopped. She looked at him steadily. She understood what he was trying to say. He was afraid, not of dying, but of what dying would mean for her. She thought about that. About what it cost him to say it. "Luka," she said. "Yeah." "Do you remember the cave?" "Obviously." "After the third sub-level. When I couldn't make it up the shaft on my own." She held his gaze. "You carried me. Both of you. One arm, fractured shoulder, you carried me up." "Yes." "You're going to do that again," she said. "Not literally. But in the way that matters. You're going to do what the situation requires and then you're going to come back." She paused. "Because that's what you do. That's what I know about you." He looked at her. "I'm also afraid," she said. "Of the eleven minutes when I'm not in the same section. Of the part where I can't see you." She met his eyes. "But I know what you are. I know what you've already done. A million cycles, Luka. You went through a million lives for me. Whatever three weeks of preparation has produced in you—" She shook her head. "You're more ready than anyone on this planet except possibly Gepard." He was quiet for a moment. "Except possibly Gepard," he said. "He's First Class Singularity." "Fair." A pause. "I'm working on it." "I know you are." She picked up her fork again. "Eat your dinner. It's good." He looked at her — the full look, the one without management. "Yeah," he said. "Yeah what?" "Nothing. Just—" He looked at his food. "Yeah." She understood this. She ate. He ate. The apartment was warm. The dome was in its evening simulation. Three weeks, possibly less, and none of it was less real for being in the future. "The moon," she said, after a while. He looked up. "After," she said. "When this is done." "The surface first," he said. "Above the dome. You wanted to see the sky." "The sky without the dome between us and it," she said. "Yes." She looked at him. "And then the moon." "And then the moon," he said. They finished dinner. They did the dishes, which was a domestic operation that had become its own kind of rhythm — he washed, she dried, because she was faster at drying and he was better at the scrubbing that some of his cooking required. They talked about nothing in particular. She described a thing Oleg had said at the workshop about the signal reading that was both accurate and extremely Oleg. He told her about a calibration drill he'd run that afternoon and the specific result that had surprised him. At 2200 she climbed into bed and he sat against the headboard and she lay with her head on his shoulder and they were quiet in the way that was its own kind of talking. "Come back," she said. "Yes," he said. Same word. Same weight. She closed her eyes. —— The signal hit 4.2 on Monday. Rikov logged it at 0130. By 0200 Gepard had revised the coverage map. By 0230 Pela had updated the projection: two and a half weeks, margin of plus or minus four days. Possibly less. By 0300 the cold light through the ice plates at the northern section's base was visible from the outer wall without needing to be directly adjacent to the plates. You could stand at the wall, look across the zone, and see it. Dunn stood at the wall at 0310 with Gepard and said: "It's not a shimmer anymore." "No," Gepard said. The cold light had developed from a faint prismatic shimmer to something with more presence — a glow that moved slightly, the way light moved when the source was moving. Not slow. Moving with the specific patience of something that was several hundred meters underground and was doing so because it had decided to. "It's closer to the surface," Dunn said. "Every day," Gepard said. "The signal rate, the light rate — they're matching. It's climbing." Dunn looked at the glow. He said: "How does it feel? The Ice Flames. When you're close to this." Gepard thought about it. "Like the ice is trying to answer it. The Stellaron's signal and the Ice Flames are in the same frequency territory — not the same frequency, but adjacent. There's a—" He paused. "Recognition. Not friendly. The way two opposing forces recognize each other." "The signal is going to keep climbing," Dunn said. "Until it hits 4.7." "Yes." "What happens at 4.7?" Gepard looked at the cold light. "It comes out." Dunn was quiet for a long moment. "How do you feel about that?" Gepard looked at the glow — the prismatic light through the ice plates, moving slowly, patient and enormous, building toward something that was going to require everything he had and possibly more than he'd ever been certain he had. "Ready," he said. Simply. True. Dunn nodded. He looked at the glow. "Ready," he repeated. Not an echo — his own answer to his own question. They stood at the wall in the cold of the Monday night and watched the First Stellaron climb. —— Pela sent the updated projection at 0800 on Wednesday: two weeks, possibly less, rate of acceleration still increasing. The signal was at 4.3. The cold light was visible from the outer district at night without the aid of any instrument — not bright, not overwhelming, but there. A faint prismatic glow coming from the direction of the Restricted Zone, visible when the dome was in its night simulation and the ambient lighting was low. Three people who didn't know what it was sent messages to the Guard asking what the glow was. Gepard responded with: monitored geological phenomenon, normal patrol activity continues. This was technically accurate. By Friday the signal was at 4.4. The cold light was visible during the daytime cycle. By Saturday it was 4.5. Clara, at the notation bench, pressed her hand to the surface and said: "The lower channels are different." She looked at Svarog. "They're filling." "With what?" Toma said. "The Stellaron's signal," Clara said. "The notation network was built for the source material frequency range. The First Stellaron's signal is at the edge of that range. When it reaches full frequency the network is going to receive it whether we're conducting it or not." She pressed her hand harder. "The channels are filling from the bottom. It's coming up." Everyone in the workshop was quiet. Svarog said: "Two weeks was the estimate." "The estimate was based on rate," Clara said. "The rate is not the same as it was when Pela built the model." She looked at the display. "The network is filling. Svarog, the geological resonance data—" He was already pulling it up. He looked at the readings for four seconds. "The geological pressure is higher than the surface signal reading suggests," he said. "The depth-to-surface transmission is delayed. The signal we're reading at the surface is what the Stellaron's output was fourteen hours ago." Pela looked up from her tablet. "You're telling me the signal is actually higher than 4.5." "By approximately 0.1 to 0.15 kilocycles." "Which means the actual signal is at 4.6 to 4.65," Pela said. She was typing fast. She looked at her model. She looked at the room. "Pela," Bronya said, from across the workshop. Pela looked at her. "The revised projection with the transmission delay factored in—" "Tell me." "One week," Pela said. "Possibly less." The workshop was very quiet. Then Oleg wrote something in his notebook. Toma and Pino looked at each other. Clara pressed her hand to the bench and held the contact. Sampo looked at his jacket pocket for a moment and then at Natasha, who was at the secondary bench and who looked back at him steadily. Serval was already pulling up the frequency analysis. Luka checked his weapon. Seele held her scythe and didn't look at anything in particular for about three seconds, and then her face settled into the expression it had when the decision had been made and the making of it was behind her. Gepard looked at Bronya. She looked at him. "One week," he said. "One week," she said. "Possibly less." He turned to the room. "Full operational readiness from tonight. Every position filled, every route confirmed, every supply point stocked." He looked at Dunn. "The rotation—" "Done by 1800," Dunn said. "The medical points—" "Confirmed this morning," Natasha said. "Lynx has the mobile teams." "The notation network—" "I'm here," Clara said, from the bench. He looked around the room. At twelve people who had been preparing for this for months, who knew their positions and their routes and their roles, who had done what they needed to do. "Everyone knows what they're doing," he said. It was not a question. Everyone nodded. Not at the same time, not performatively — each person's acknowledgment arriving in their own register. Seele's was the corner of the mouth. Luka's was a direct look. Sampo's was a small, specific nod that meant: yeah. I know. I'm ready. "Good," Gepard said. He looked at Bronya last. She was looking at him with the quality she had — all the layers at once. She said nothing. He said nothing. The look contained everything it needed to. "One week," he said. To the room and to her both. —— That night, after the workshop had dispersed and the positions were confirmed and the rotation was locked and the supply points were verified, Gepard sat on the outer wall of the Restricted Zone and watched the cold light. It was brighter tonight. Not by much — the signal was at 4.5 on the surface reading, 4.6 in reality — but enough that the difference from the previous night was measurable without instruments. Tomorrow it would be brighter again. The night after that. One week. Possibly less. He sat with his hands on the wall and thought about the Ice Flames. He thought about what they'd been at thirty-one percent — the blue-white fire at the edge of the standard ice expression, the thing that had frozen a one-hundred-and-fifty-meter cave and propagated through three sub-levels of geological structure. He thought about what they were going to be at a hundred. He had no idea, specifically. He'd never been there. The Ice Flames had come from the northern ridge, from the press of death at its most concentrated, from a year of learning what lived past the ceiling. He'd gone to eighty percent at the Second Stellaron battle and the Ice Flames had been there, real and present and sufficient for what that engagement required. Past eighty, he had the Ice Flames and the notation network and the crystalline structural vulnerability of the First Stellaron's form and Serval's frequency disruption data and Svarog's geological routing and Clara at the bench holding the channels open. He had everything the situation required. He looked at the cold light. He thought about what Dunn had said: *they're for this.* He thought about Bronya saying: *tell me it's going to be all right.* He thought about what was past eighty percent and found, in the specific quietness of sitting alone at the outer wall with the cold light growing below, that he was not afraid of it. Not because he didn't understand the scale. Because he understood it completely. He got up from the wall. He went home. He slept well — better than he'd slept in weeks, the specific deep sleep of someone who has been preparing for a long time and has finally arrived at the place where the preparation is over and the thing itself is what remains. One week. Possibly less. The cold light kept climbing. —— END OF CHAPTER 52 —— The city changed in that last week. Not dramatically. Not in the way cities changed in stories when catastrophe was approaching — no mass panic, no hoarding, no chaos in the outer corridors. Belobog was too used to living with threat for that. The city changed the way it changed when the Fragmentum density went up and the patrols doubled and everyone learned, without being told directly, that something was different about the air. People stayed inside more. The market at junction twelve ran shorter hours. The fight circuit at Boulder Town skipped the Tuesday undercard and Oleg was briefly very annoyed about this. But the shops stayed open. The generators kept running. Svarog held three maintenance sessions in the workshop and conducted them with his usual thoroughness because the generators needed to be in peak condition before the First Stellaron activated and because peak condition was what Svarog demanded from machinery regardless of the circumstances. On Wednesday, five days before Pela's best projection, Clara was at the notation bench when the lower channels did something she'd never felt before. She sat very still for a moment. Oleg noticed. He always noticed. "What is it?" "The lower channels," she said slowly. "They've been filling since Saturday. But now—" She pressed her hand flat. "Now they're connected. The signal from below has reached the depth at which the notation network connects to the deep geological formation." "The cave," Pino said from across the workshop. "Below the cave," Clara said. "Below the Great Glacial Aegir. The notation network runs deeper than the cave. The Architects built the channels through the full geological structure of the outer machine district." She looked at the display. "The First Stellaron's signal is inside the notation network now." Svarog was out of the back room in two seconds. "Show me." She showed him. He looked at the reading for a long time. He was very still in the way he was still when something required all four hundred years of his processing capacity. "The network was built to carry the source material frequency range," he said. "Yes." "The First Stellaron's signal is at the outer edge of that range." "Yes." "This is—" He paused. "The Architects built the network to receive this signal. Not just the source material warmth of the Second Stellaron's notation work. The First Stellaron's activation frequency." He looked at Clara. "The network is going to carry the disruption propagation." "The Ice Flames," Clara said. "When Gepard produces the Ice Flames at the activation level and they enter the geological structure, the notation network will carry them." He looked at the display. "Not just to the surface. Through the full network. To every channel junction in the outer machine district. To every notation carving in the geological structure." "How far does the network reach?" Toma asked. Svarog was quiet for two seconds. "The network reaches the outer boundary of the Restricted Zone in every direction." He paused. "And through the Restricted Zone's geological foundation — which the Architects also built the notation carvings into — to the surface of the Restricted Zone itself." Clara thought about what that meant. The Ice Flames at full output, propagating through a network that reached every carved surface in the outer machine district and the full foundation of the Restricted Zone. She thought about the Manchester Smash at thirty-one percent, which had frozen a one-hundred-and-fifty-meter cave and sent its propagation up three sub-levels to the surface. She thought about one hundred percent. "Svarog," she said. "Yes." "At one hundred percent. If the Ice Flames propagate through the full network." She looked at the display. "What does that look like?" He was quiet for three seconds. "The Restricted Zone," he said, "is one kilometer in diameter. The geological structure beneath it extends to approximately four hundred meters depth. The notation network runs through the full volume." He paused. "At one hundred percent propagation through that network, the Fragmentum response in the surrounding district would collapse simultaneously. The First Stellaron's crystalline form would experience the disruption from every direction at once." He looked at her. "Not like the Manchester Smash, which propagated upward from a single point. From everywhere simultaneously." The workshop was very quiet. "That would disrupt it," Clara said. "The crystalline form." "Yes," Svarog said. "If the disruption is sufficient to reach the form's primary structural coherence, the form fails. The force has nowhere to concentrate. It disperses back through the geological structure rather than manifesting on the surface." "And Gepard," Toma said carefully, "would need to be producing Ice Flames at that output while the First Stellaron is at full activation." "Yes," Svarog said. The workshop processed this. Oleg wrote something in his notebook. Pino and Toma looked at each other. Clara looked at the notation surface under her palm, at the signal from below running through the channels, at what the Architects had built two hundred years ago or eight hundred years ago or longer — how long ago did you build something when you were building it for the moment when everything that mattered was on the line? She thought: they knew. She thought: they built it knowing exactly this. She thought: *those who keep the lineage, the network knows your hands.* "We need to tell Gepard," she said. "He's at the east wall," Oleg said. He was already sending the message. —— Gepard received the message at 1430 and was in the workshop by 1445. Svarog walked him through the reading. Clara showed him the contact — let him put his hand near the notation surface, not on it, just close enough to feel the frequency. The Ice Flames in his hands responded to it, which was the thing he noticed — a low-level resonance in the standard ice expression, like two things in adjacent frequency bands recognizing each other's presence. "The network is carrying the Stellaron's signal," he said. "Yes," Svarog said. "Which means when the activation happens—" "The network will be at full signal load. The frequency the Ice Flames operate at and the frequency the notation network is currently running at are close enough that when the Ice Flames enter the network—" "They propagate through the whole thing," Gepard said. "Yes." He thought about what that meant. He thought about standing at the perimeter of the Restricted Zone while the First Stellaron activated, about producing the Ice Flames at whatever output was required, about the feeling of the ice expression going past eighty percent and into the territory he hadn't mapped. He thought about what it would mean for the propagation to come from everywhere simultaneously rather than from a single point upward. He thought about what Svarog had said: *possibly beyond the Restricted Zone.* "How far does the network reach," he said. It was the same question Toma had asked and Clara already knew the answer but he needed to hear it from Svarog directly. Svarog told him. He was quiet for a long moment after. Then he said: "The full Restricted Zone. Every direction. Simultaneously." "Yes." He looked at his hands. The standard ice expression. The Ice Flames waiting at the edge of it. "At one hundred percent." "At whatever output is required to saturate the network," Svarog said. "I don't know exactly what that output is. The Ice Flames at thirty-one percent propagated through three sub-levels and one hundred and fifty meters of cave. The network is considerably larger than the cave." He paused. "I would estimate you will need to be significantly past thirty-one percent." "Past eighty." "Yes." "And I've never been past eighty." "No," Svarog said. "You have not." Gepard looked at the notation surface. At Clara's hand on it. At the signal from below running through the channels the Architects had built. He looked at all of it and ran the honest accounting: he'd never been past eighty. He didn't know what was there. He knew the Ice Flames were there because he'd felt them at eighty, felt them trying to become something larger. He knew the northern ridge had produced them and the northern ridge had required the ceiling to be higher than he'd thought. He thought: the First Stellaron is going to require the ceiling to be higher than I've thought. He thought: good. "All right," he said. "All right?" Clara looked at him. "I'll find out what's past eighty when I need to." He met her eyes. The twelve-year-old with the Architect lineage and the most complete understanding of the notation network of anyone alive except Svarog. "Hold the channels open." "I'll hold them," she said. "Through the whole event." "Through the whole event," she confirmed, with the certainty of someone who had decided this was the answer and the deciding was done. He nodded. He looked at Svarog. "When the signal hits 4.7 on the surface reading, the actual signal is at 4.8 or higher due to the transmission delay." "Yes." "So the activation happens before 4.7 reads on the surface." "Yes. By approximately fourteen hours." He absorbed this. "I need Pela to update the projection with the transmission delay factored in." "She's already working on it," Oleg said, from the floor, without looking up from his notebook. Gepard looked at him. "How do you know that?" "I told her when I sent you the message," Oleg said. "She responded in two minutes." He turned a page in his notebook. "She says the updated projection will account for the delay. Her current estimate is forty-eight to seventy-two hours. Possibly less." The workshop was quiet again. Forty-eight to seventy-two hours. Not one week. Not five days. Two to three days. Gepard looked at the display. The signal at 4.5, actually 4.6 or higher. The cold light visible from the outer district. The notation channels filling with the Stellaron's frequency. Forty-eight to seventy-two hours. "Tonight," he said. He looked at the room. "Everyone at their positions tonight. Not tomorrow. Tonight." He looked at Clara. "You're at the bench from 1800." "I'll be here," she said. He looked at Sampo, who was standing at the back of the workshop with the non-standard corridor map folded in his jacket. "The northwest routes." "I can run them in my sleep," Sampo said. "Don't run them in your sleep. Be awake." "Noted." He looked at Serval, who was already at the secondary bench pulling up the frequency analysis. She didn't look up. She said: "The disruption parameters are locked. If the Ice Flames enter the network at the right frequency, the First Stellaron's crystalline form is going to have a bad time." She paused. "I've been saying this for two weeks. I continue to say it." He almost smiled. He looked at Luka and Seele, who were beside each other near the workshop entrance. They'd been quiet through the briefing, receiving the information. Seele had the settled expression — the one she had after the cave, the one that meant the decision was made. Luka was looking at Gepard with the direct gaze that meant he was taking the full assessment and filing it. Gepard said: "Two to three days." Luka said: "We're ready." Simple. True.
