Gepard looked at Bronya, who had been in the corner of the workshop since he arrived. She'd been listening to all of it with the quality she had — the full reception, the complete attention. She met his eyes. He said nothing. She said nothing. The look contained what it needed to contain, which was: this is the thing. We know what it is. We're doing it. "Tonight," he said to the room one more time. Everyone moved. —— That evening the workshop was the fullest it had been in months. Clara at the bench. Svarog at the tertiary equipment. Serval running the frequency analysis in continuous mode. Pela at the data station with the updated projection and the live signal feed. Oleg on the floor with the notebook. Toma and Pino running the section six notation documentation that they'd abandoned weeks ago and had apparently silently agreed to finish now, because finishing it felt correct. Lynx with her medical bag at the worktable, doing inventory. Natasha via the relay link, checking in every thirty minutes. Sampo had gone to walk the northwest routes one final time. He'd come back at 2100 and sit on the floor beside Oleg and they'd play cards until 2300 because sitting with the waiting required something to do with your hands. Seele was at the back of the workshop with the scythe extended, running the maintenance. Not drilling — she was done with drilling. She was just making sure everything was exactly right. The edge, the joint mechanisms, the extension mechanism. She ran the cloth along the blade and the motion was its own kind of meditation. Luka was beside her with the weapon in its cannon configuration, running the final calibration. The cannon to blade to rifle to cannon, all the transitions he'd been building for months, the end-state principle she'd taught him in the outer corridor. He ran them slowly, not for time, just for the feel of the sequence being correct. It was correct. He had known it was correct for about a week and this was confirmation rather than practice. He glanced at her. She glanced at him. Neither of them said anything. Neither of them needed to. At 2200 the signal reading updated to 4.6. Actual signal: 4.7 or higher. Threshold: 4.7. Pela looked at the display. She looked at the room. She said: "The transmission delay is real and the signal is at or above threshold already." The workshop went very quiet. "Which means," Gepard said, from the doorway where he'd been standing since 2100, "that the activation isn't in forty-eight to seventy-two hours." Pela looked at the display. She looked at the live feed. She was sixteen years old and she was looking at numbers that told her something that she had to say out loud to make real and she said it. "The activation is imminent," she said. "Hours, not days." Clara pressed both hands flat to the notation surface. The signal from below ran through the channels with a quality that was different from even an hour ago — denser, more present, the specific pressure of something that had been climbing for months and had arrived at the top of the climb. "The lower channels are at capacity," she said. "They're full." Everyone in the workshop looked at her. "It's ready," she said. "The Stellaron. It's at the surface." Gepard looked at Bronya. She was already looking at him. "Now," he said. She said: "Now." —— END OF CHAPTER 52 —— "Now" meant a lot of things simultaneously. For Gepard it meant: outer wall, coverage map, Dunn on the comms, Guard positions confirmed and active. He was out the workshop door in thirty seconds. Dunn was already at the east wall — had been there since 2100 because Dunn had the specific quality of arriving where he was needed before he was told he was needed. "Signal?" Gepard said. "4.6 on the surface read," Dunn said. "4.7 or higher actual." He had the coverage map open. "The northwest residential positions are set. Sampo confirmed the corridor routes fifteen minutes ago." "Medical points." "Natasha is at junction twelve. Lynx is mobile with team three." He paused. "All seven points are staffed." Gepard looked at the Restricted Zone. The cold light was not a shimmer anymore. It was a glow — steady, prismatic, visible from the outer wall without needing to look for it. The kind of light that asked to be seen. That insisted on being seen. "The Guard positions hold until the signal reads 4.7 on the surface," Gepard said. "At that point everyone moves to the secondary position. Nobody in the primary zone." "And you?" Dunn said. Gepard looked at the wall. At the glow through the northern section ice plates. At the notation network running beneath his feet through the geological structure all the way to the cave and below it to wherever the First Stellaron had been building its signal for eight hundred years. "I'm at the primary position," he said. Dunn was quiet for a second. Then: "Understood." "Dunn." "Yeah." Gepard looked at him directly. "The northern wall. When the activation starts. That's yours." "I know it's mine." "Specifically the junction seventeen section. The Fragmentum response is going to concentrate there based on Serval's frequency model." "I know." Dunn held his gaze. "I've read the model four times." "Good." Gepard paused. "Thank you. For—" He stopped. Reconsidered. "For being at every briefing. Every coverage revision. Every 0300 rotation update." He paused. "For being what you are." Dunn looked at him. He had the quality he had in the early mornings — the version of himself that wasn't the professional register. "That's my job," he said. "It's more than your job." A pause. "Yeah," Dunn said. "It is." He looked at the glow. "Go to your position. I have the north wall." Gepard went. —— For Seele, now meant the outer district. Her position was the forward engagement line — not the perimeter, the line between the perimeter and the Restricted Zone's outer boundary where the Fragmentum response was going to be densest at the activation moment. Her and three other fighters who could handle sub-level-class Fragmentum formations at volume. This was her ground. She and Luka walked from the workshop in the direction of their respective positions. They would split at junction nine — he went east toward the cannon position Svarog had calibrated for the weapon's optimal output range, she went north toward the forward line. At junction nine they stopped. She looked at him. He looked at her. She didn't say anything for a moment. She was doing the inventory she did before things — not tactically, personally. Where is he, what is he carrying, what does he need. He was carrying the weapon in compact mode, the chrome arm warm, the sandevistan integration active and ready. He was looking at her with the expression that was below all his management, fully visible, making no effort to be otherwise. "The cannon position," she said. "Junction eleven east. Forty meters from the Restricted Zone boundary." "I know where it is." She'd memorized every position. "I'm going to know where you are the whole time." "Good," he said. "Don't do anything—" "Seele," he said. She stopped. "I know what I'm doing," he said. "You know what you're doing. We've both been building toward this for months." He held her gaze. "We're going to do it and then we're going to come back and argue about what to have for dinner." She laughed. The real one, unplanned. It came out startled and genuine. He smiled — the full version, the one he didn't suppress. "Dinner," she said. "I was thinking the thing with vegetables," he said. "The one you made last week." "The improved version. With the adjusted spicing." "I said the spicing was in an interesting direction," she said. "I didn't say adjust it." "I'm adjusting it anyway." She looked at him. At the chrome arm and the weapon and the spinal integration and the person she had loved since before she had the word for it. She reached out and put her hand on the side of his face. He went still the way he always went still when she did that. "Come back," she said. "Yes," he said. Same word, same register, same absolute weight. She kissed him — not the careful version, the full version, both hands on his face and the full weight of everything behind it. He kissed her back. The junction was quiet around them. The cold light was visible even here, faint but present, coming from the direction of the Restricted Zone. She pulled back. She looked at him. "After," she said. "After," he said. "Dinner. The moon. In that order." "In that order," she agreed. She went north. He went east. At junction nine's far exit, she looked back once — it was an old habit, looking back, the most fundamental thing about her. He was already looking. She went. —— For Bronya, now meant the palace first. Specifically: the three people who needed to know the timeline had compressed before the Guard announced the final evacuation order. She messaged Barros at 2215 with the specific information and no commentary because Barros was old enough and experienced enough to receive specific information without requiring commentary. She messaged the communications director with the public announcement language they'd prepared three weeks ago, set to broadcast in thirty minutes. She messaged the senior Wildfire commander with: activation imminent. Execute final protocol. Then she went to the Restricted Zone. She walked through the outer district — the same walk she'd been taking for months, the route from the palace to the Restricted Zone's outer boundary. The generators, the junction corridor, the notation-channeled walls. The outer district at 2230 in its night cycle, quiet, the dome in its evening blue. She felt the frequency through the stone under her feet. She had felt it for weeks. At first through the Restricted Zone's northern section specifically. Then through the palace floors. Now through the outer district's stone, four hundred meters from the zone boundary, through the soles of her boots. The geological pressure of something enormous shifting, of two hundred meters of ancient crystalline mass completing a journey it had been on for eight hundred years. It was loud tonight. Not deafening — she'd learned over weeks to receive it without being overwhelmed by it. But present in a way it had never been before, filling the geological register, pressing upward through everything between the Stellaron and the surface. She walked to the Restricted Zone boundary. She looked at the cold light. It was not cold anymore. That was the wrong word — it was prismatic, it was bright, it was overwhelming even through the ice plates and the stone and the sixty meters of distance. The light moved and shifted and caught on every mineral surface it reached. Tomorrow morning, if this had not yet resolved, the cold light would not be cold light. It would be something that did not have a prior name in Belobog's vocabulary. She stood at the boundary and she listened to the geological frequency through the stone and she thought about everything that had brought her to this specific moment. Everwinter Hill. The Second Stellaron. The cave's notation. Clara's hand on the bench. Gepard at the outer wall counting thirty-eight minutes while she stood in the northern section. The things they hadn't said yet that were going to be said after. She thought about what it meant to be the Guardian. The real version, not the formal one. The real version was standing at the boundary of your city's greatest threat at 2230 in the evening and being ready. Not because the threat was manageable. Not because you were certain of the outcome. But because this was your city and these were your people and the correct response to a thing this large was to stand in front of it. She crossed the boundary. She went into the Restricted Zone. She went to the northern section. She stood at the contact point where she had stood every morning for weeks and she felt the frequency through the stone directly, no boots between her and the geological structure, the full signal of the First Stellaron pressing upward against the thin layer of ice and stone and ancient formation that separated the surface from the thing below. The cold light was everywhere here. It came through the ice plates and reflected off the snow and caught in the crystallization on the zone's walls and made the northern section into a place that was filled with prismatic, shifting, overwhelming light. She stood in it. She thought: this is the thing. This is the moment. Everything that was required has been done. The people who are going to stand in front of it are standing in front of it. The channels are open. The network is ready. She thought: we're going to win. Not performed. Not reassurance. The genuine product of looking at the full honest accounting — at what had been built over months, at who was at each position, at what Gepard was going to do when the signal broke 4.7 and the First Stellaron came through — and arriving at the conclusion that the preparation was sufficient. They were going to win. She stood in the light and waited. —— At 2247, the surface signal hit 4.7. Clara felt the channel shift before the display updated. She pressed both hands to the bench and held the contact and said, clearly, to the room: "Now." The display updated two seconds later. 4.7 kilocycles. Actual signal: 4.8 to 4.9. In the outer district, through every notation-channeled wall, through every geological junction in the outer machine district's stone, through the pre-Separation carvings that the Architects had built specifically for this moment — the First Stellaron's signal reached the surface. At the Restricted Zone, the ice plates at the northern section's base shattered. Not slowly. Not gradually. All of them simultaneously, in one explosive moment, and through the space where the ice had been came the cold light — not cold anymore, not a shimmer, not a glow. Light. Enormous, prismatic, blinding, the specific quality of a crystal structure one hundred and fifty meters tall radiating at its full activation frequency. The First Stellaron of Belobog rose from the earth. And Belobog rose to meet it. —— END OF CHAPTER 52 Her Shield, His Sun Chapter 51 The First Stellaron's signal moved to 3.2 at 0347 on a Tuesday morning, while most of Belobog slept. Rikov caught it. He was on the night rotation — had been since he came back from the ward after the Second Stellaron battle, a choice that felt right to him in ways he hadn't fully examined. He liked the night shift. Liked being the one who watched things in the dark when everyone else wasn't looking. He saw the jump from 3.1 to 3.2, stared at it for a solid ten seconds to make sure it wasn't a sensor error, checked the backup, confirmed, and sent the message to Gepard. Gepard responded in under two minutes. Which meant he'd been awake. *Confirmed. Log it. Watch for further movement.* Rikov wrote it in the log: 0347. Signal 3.1 → 3.2. Twelve weeks since the Second Stellaron's defeat. He looked at the number and thought about what it meant that a thing that had been holding perfectly still for nearly three months had suddenly decided to move. He didn't think about it for too long. He watched the display instead. The signal held at 3.2 for the rest of the night. —— Gepard told Bronya at 0645 when she came through the palace's outer corridor on her way to the morning briefing. Not in the briefing — before it. In the corridor, which was how they'd been doing things for the past few weeks: the real conversation first, then the formal version. She read the log entry he held out, looked at the number, and was quiet for a moment. "Three point two," she said. "As of 0347." "And it's held." "Held at 3.2 for six hours." He pocketed the log. "Pela is already at the analysis station. She'll have projections by the briefing." Bronya nodded slowly. She looked past him, at the window that faced the outer district, the dome in its morning cycle. She had this thing she did sometimes — not spacing out exactly, more like she was listening for something he couldn't hear. She'd been doing it more often in the past weeks. He'd noticed and hadn't said anything because it didn't seem like the kind of thing you said something about until she was ready to. "Bronya." She looked back at him. "Sorry. Yes. Pela's projections." She turned toward the briefing room. "Come on." He fell into step beside her. Their shoulders were close enough to touch without touching, which was where they'd been for months now and which was its own kind of thing. "You looked like you were listening to something." She glanced at him. "What makes you say that?" "You get a particular look." "I do not have a particular look." "You absolutely do." He held the briefing room door for her. "What are you listening for?" She stopped in the doorway. She considered him — the direct look she used when she was deciding how much to say. Then: "I've been hearing something. In the Restricted Zone specifically. When it's quiet and I'm near the northern section." She paused. "I don't know what it is yet." He did not immediately say something reassuring, because Bronya was not someone who wanted immediate reassurance and he knew the difference between situations that called for it and situations that didn't. He said: "Tell me when you know more." "Yes," she said. And went in. —— Pela's projections were not comforting. She'd been at the analysis station since before 0400 — had apparently been woken by her own monitoring alert, because she'd set the system to flag any signal movement above 3.1 three weeks ago when the Second Stellaron's defeat had pushed the First Stellaron's signal from 2.4 to 3.1 overnight. She had been, Gepard thought, the most prepared person in the room. Per usual. "The 3.1 to 3.2 jump," she said, "is the first movement we've seen since the post-defeat stabilization. That's twelve weeks of stillness followed by a step change." She put the graph up. "I don't have enough data to know if this is the beginning of escalation or an isolated event. But the baseline correlation—" she tapped the display "—suggests that the Second Stellaron's defeat released pressure on the First Stellaron that had been suppressing its activity. Like a rock holding down water. We removed the rock." "And now the water is moving," Gepard said. "Possibly. Or it's a small ripple and it settles again. I genuinely don't know yet." She said it plainly, without apology. "What I can tell you is that we should treat this as the beginning of escalation until evidence suggests otherwise." Around the briefing table, eight people processed this. Serval was drumming a quiet rhythm on the table with two fingers. Sampo was looking at the graph with the specific expression of someone who had hoped the news would be different and wasn't surprised it wasn't. Svarog was at the far end of the table, perfectly still, which was what Svarog looked like when he was running something through four hundred years of accumulated knowledge simultaneously. "The signal at 3.2 compared to the activation threshold," Bronya said. "Give me the gap." "The threshold we're watching for is 4.7 kilocycles. We're at 3.2. The gap is one and a half kilocycles." Pela paused. "But the gap doesn't tell you the timeline. The rate does, and we don't have rate data yet from a single data point." "How long until you have rate data?" "If the signal moves again in the next week, I can extrapolate. If it holds at 3.2 for another month—" she shrugged, "—that's different information." Bronya looked at Svarog. "Does the pre-Separation data give you anything?" Svarog was quiet for three seconds. "The pre-Separation records document an escalation event in the signal approximately eight hundred years ago. It did not reach 4.7. It reached 4.1 before stabilizing." He paused. "The records describe it as a period of significant Fragmentum activity throughout the outer district. Seventy years of elevated threat levels." "Seventy years," Serval said, very quietly. "It stabilized," Svarog said. "But I want to be honest: the conditions then were different. The Second Stellaron was dormant but intact. The Second Stellaron is now defeated. What that changes about the First Stellaron's behavior is not something I have prior data for." Silence. Gepard said: "We monitor. We prepare. We don't assume it stabilizes." He looked at Bronya. She nodded. "We don't assume it stabilizes." —— After the briefing, the day continued the way days continued when something alarming had been established but was not yet immediately actionable. Which is to say: people went back to their work, carried the alarm underneath it, and got on with things. Seele and Luka were in the outer corridor running drills when Luka's phone buzzed. He caught the message, read it, put the phone away. "What?" Seele said. She could tell from how he'd looked at the screen. "Signal moved. 3.1 to 3.2. Overnight." She stopped what she was doing. "Since when?" "Since 0347. Gepard just sent it out to the full channel." He looked at her. "It's been holding at 3.2 for six hours." She was quiet for a moment. She thought about the monitoring logs she'd been reading with Pela — the CONSIDERING notation, twelve weeks of stillness. She thought about what it meant that it had stopped considering and had started doing something. "Okay," she said. "Okay?" "I mean it's not okay. But — okay." She picked her scythe back up. "Come on. The rifle-to-cannon sequence." He looked at her for a second. "You want to keep training." "What else are we going to do right now? Stare at the monitoring display?" She extended the scythe handle. "Come on." He looked at her for another second — that way he had of looking at her where he was running something through and arriving at a conclusion — and then he smiled, small and real, and picked up the weapon. They kept training. The drill was blade-to-rifle to cannon, the sequence Seele had broken down for him two weeks ago using the end-state principle. He was getting faster. Not because the raw speed had increased but because the body was building the pathway, the transitions becoming less like decisions and more like sentences. You didn't decide to move from one word to the next. It just happened. She ran her own sequences while he ran his. The butterfly quality had been settling into her movement in a way she was learning to trust — less like a switch she flipped and more like a third voice that had always been there and was now speaking at normal volume. In the corridor she was fast, faster than she'd been before the cave, fast in a way that had more to do with certainty than speed. She knew where she was going before she started moving. At 1100 she sat against the wall to rest and pulled her knees up. Luka came to sit beside her, close enough that their shoulders touched. The corridor was quiet — outer machine district, the junction they'd been using since the tunnel entries, familiar stone. "How worried are you?" he said. Not about the drills. She thought about it. "More than I was yesterday. Less than I think I should be." She looked at the corridor wall. "Does that make sense?" "Yeah." He turned the weapon in his hands. "It's a number on a display. 3.2. It doesn't feel like anything yet." "The Second Stellaron felt like something," she said. "From the beginning. The heartbeat in the tunnels, the cold, the way the forms came out differently." She paused. "The First Stellaron hasn't felt like anything to me. Except—" She stopped. "Except what?" She hesitated. It felt like something she hadn't quite decided was real. "Bronya's been hearing things. In the Restricted Zone. She mentioned it once and then didn't bring it up again, which means she's still figuring it out." Luka was quiet for a moment. He knew what Bronya hearing things in the Restricted Zone meant, or what it might mean, and he was probably running the same calculation she was: the Guardian with a direct line to the Stellaron's presence. What did it sound like? Was it language? Was it anything comprehensible at all? "That's probably not a good sign," he said. "No," she said. "Probably not." They sat with that. Then he reached over and took her hand — the chrome, warm — and she let him, and they sat in the outer corridor in the quiet and didn't need to fill it. —— Clara and Oleg were at Boulder Town. This was not about anything. Oleg had said that morning, at the workshop, "I'm going to Boulder Town to watch the fights," in the way he said things that were simply true and required no additional justification. Clara had said, "I'll come," and they had gone. Boulder Town's fight circuit ran on a Tuesday schedule — not the main event, the preliminary bouts, the undercard matches that drew the serious practitioners and the people who appreciated technique over spectacle. Oleg found them a position near the back of the crowd where the sightlines were good and the press wasn't bad, and they watched. Clara had not been to a fight at Boulder Town without the specific purpose of watching Luka. This was different — she didn't know the fighters, didn't have a stake in any of the outcomes, which meant she could watch the actual fighting without the layer of investment that came with knowing the person in the ring. She found she liked it more this way. The technique was cleaner when you weren't worried. "That one," Oleg said, pointing at a fighter in the left corner of the near ring. "Watch how she moves before she commits. She telegraphs left before she goes right." Clara watched. The fighter threw left — no, feinted, went right, caught her opponent on the side of the head. The opponent had gone left to cover. "You saw that in the first exchange," Clara said. "First two seconds," Oleg said. He was eating something. "It's the shoulder. The shoulder tilts before the feint." Clara looked at him. He was twelve years old and had been eating from his jacket for the entire trip and had just identified a fighter's tell in two seconds that the opponent hadn't caught in three full exchanges. "When did you learn to read fighters like that?" she said. He shrugged. "Just watching." He offered her something from the jacket. She took it. It was a small pastry of some kind, warm, which was technically impossible given that they had been walking outside for twenty minutes, but she had stopped trying to reconcile the jacket. "Luka taught me some of it," he said. "About the telegraphing. He said most people think the fight starts when the punch does, but it actually starts in the five seconds before. Everything that happens after is just what the setup made inevitable." "That sounds like something Luka would say." "He says a lot of stuff." Oleg paused. "More now than before. He's been—" He searched for the word. "Happier. Since Seele." He said it plainly, without any particular emphasis. "He's always been there, he's always worked hard, but now he also just — he's there when you talk to him. Before he was a little bit somewhere else." Clara thought about this. She thought about Luka at the notation bench at the beginning — the repair work, the quiet efficiency, the person who did the job and didn't say much. She thought about him now — training in the corridor, teaching Oleg about telegraphs, sitting on the floor of the workshop with Seele's head on his shoulder while they read. "People change," she said. "Yeah," Oleg said. He watched the fight. "So do planets apparently." She looked at him. "3.2," he said. He hadn't looked at his phone once since they left. "Toma told me before we left." "It's probably—" "I know," he said. "I'm not worried. I just mean — first the Second Stellaron, now this. Everything's been changing." He ate the rest of the pastry. "I've been writing it down." "The new notebook." "The new notebook." He nodded at the ring. "She's going to win in the next two exchanges." Clara watched. The fighter did win in the next two exchanges. She wasn't sure if Oleg had predicted it or engineered it through some form of focused will, but both outcomes seemed equally possible. "You could train," she said. "If you wanted." He thought about this seriously. "Maybe." He watched the next bout start. "Not yet. There's too much happening. After." "After," she said. They watched the fights for another hour. It was a good hour. —— In the workshop, Svarog was alone. This was not unusual. The workshop was often quiet in the middle of the day, after the morning sessions and before the evening work. He used the quiet productively — running analyses, calibrating equipment, reviewing the pre-Separation records. He had four hundred and twelve years of information management practice. He did not waste time. Today he was looking at the records from the 3.2 escalation eight hundred years ago. The language was old — not the notation network's script, the textual records that the pre-Separation communities had kept before the Separation made the surface inaccessible and most of what had been up there inaccessible with it. He had the records. He had always had the records. He had not shared all of them with anyone yet because some information had a correct time to be shared and he was still determining whether this was that time. The records from eight hundred years ago described the 3.1 to 3.2 escalation this way: *the pale stone began to move of its own accord. the formations in the outermost sections woke without cause and could not be put down. three settlements at the outer boundary were abandoned. the light from beneath the ground — not the warm light but the cold light — was seen through the ice plates at the lower levels.* The cold light. He looked at the Restricted Zone through the workshop window. The northern section, where Bronya had been spending her morning sessions. Where she'd been hearing something. He thought about whether to tell her what the cold light meant. He thought about what four hundred and twelve years of watching this planet had taught him about the First Stellaron. The Second Stellaron had been a memory — an accumulation, a repository, something that held things. The First Stellaron was something different. The Second Stellaron had been made dormant by Vera's sacrifice, by Fuli's intervention, by the specific circumstances of the dark winter. The First Stellaron had never been made dormant. It had simply been — waiting. Beneath the palace. Beneath the Restricted Zone. Older than anything else on this planet. In Honkai Star Rail, every Stellaron was different. Every Stellaron carried its own nature. The Second Stellaron's nature had been storage, accumulation, memory. It had fought when threatened but its fundamental nature was not aggression. The First Stellaron's nature was power. Raw, undirected, escalating power. The energy of destruction compressed into geological form and waiting — not patiently, not benevolently — waiting the way a river waited behind a dam. The force was not interested in Belobog. The force was not communicating with Bronya. The force was going to do what it had always been going to do, which was release. The dam was the geological structure above it. When the signal reached 4.7, the dam broke. Whatever Bronya was hearing in the Restricted Zone was not communication from the Stellaron. It was the sound of something enormous moving closer to the surface. He would tell her. Not today. In a few days, when she'd had more time to sit with the 3.2 reading and ask the right questions. The right questions would bring him to the right answers, and the right answers would land better than if he simply walked in and said: what you're hearing is not language. He closed the eight-hundred-year-old record. He began calibrating the tertiary workshop sensor array for high-signal readings. —— That evening, the inner residential stack on Corridor Eleven had a dinner. This was Lynx's doing, technically, though she had organized it in the indirect way she did things: by mentioning to Gepard that she was cooking Tuesday dinner and then adding, in a carefully casual tone, that she had extra, and then adding, even more casually, that Bronya had mentioned not having eaten a real meal in two days. Gepard had looked at his younger sister in a way that communicated he knew exactly what she was doing, and she had looked back at him in a way that communicated she was aware of this and did not consider it relevant. Bronya arrived at the Landau house at 1900 having been told by Gepard only that dinner was happening and she should come. She stood in the doorway of the kitchen and took in: Lynx at the stove, Gepard at the table cutting bread, and the smell of something that was significantly better than palace kitchen food. "You didn't tell me—" she started. "I told you dinner," Gepard said. "You told me there was dinner. You didn't say it was—" she looked at Lynx "—this." Lynx turned around from the stove. She was nineteen and had the exact same cheekbones as Gepard, which was the most immediately striking thing about her, and she had the manner of someone who had been waiting a very long time to assess this specific situation and was very calmly doing so. "Supreme Guardian," she said. "Lynx," Bronya said. "Sit down, it's ready in ten minutes." Bronya sat. She sat in the chair that was not Gepard's chair, which she'd identified immediately from the angle, and looked at the kitchen with the expression she rarely wore in official settings — unguarded, slightly overwhelmed, the expression of someone who had not been in someone else's home in a very long time. "You cook," she said to Lynx. "Yes," Lynx said. "Gepard doesn't." "I cook," Gepard said. "You make things edible," Lynx said. "That's not the same as cooking." She took something off the heat and brought it to the table. "My mother taught me. Gepard was too impatient to learn properly." Bronya looked at Gepard. He had the expression of someone who was not going to argue with this. "He burned the soup," Lynx added. "I didn't burn the soup—" "You overheated the soup so significantly that it became a different consistency than it was supposed to be." "That's not burning—" "Gepard." Lynx looked at him levelly. "What temperature was it?" A pause. "It was hot." "Mm." Lynx sat down. "It was extremely hot. We ate the soup. But my point stands." She looked at Bronya. "How did you start? Did someone teach you, or—" "I grew up in the palace," Bronya said. "The palace has a kitchen staff." She paused. "I know how to make tea." "That's a start," Lynx said. "Gepard can make tea. He's graduated to eggs." "I have also graduated to eggs," Gepard said. "I'm offended you're presenting this as news." "The eggs last week had shells in them." "One piece of shell. Very small." "Still in the eggs though." Bronya laughed. It came out before she decided to laugh — the unplanned kind, the kind that happened when something was actually funny instead of required-social-funny. Gepard looked at her like it was the best sound in the room, which it was, and then went back to the bread in the way of someone trying to look like he hadn't been looking. Lynx saw this. Lynx filed it. They ate dinner. The food was genuinely good — something with vegetables and warmth and the quality of a meal that someone had actually cared about making. Bronya ate more than she had in days and was privately embarrassed about this and mostly hid it. The conversation went: the signal reading (Lynx knew, she'd heard it from Gepard that morning), what Pela's projections said (cautious), the patrol rotation (Gepard had revised it), and then away from all of that into what conversations went into when the operational urgency was put down for a moment. Lynx described a patient she'd had that week who had injured himself trying to fix a generator grate and had been embarrassed about it the entire time she was treating him, and who had then asked her to pass on a message to Svarog through the medical ward because he didn't feel like he could approach Svarog directly. The message was that the grate design was inconvenient. "Did you pass it on?" Bronya said. "I told Svarog someone sent regards regarding the grate design." Lynx took a bite. "He said he knew." "Of course he knew." "He's been aware of the grate design's issues for fifty years and has redesigned it three times. He said each redesign produced a different set of people injuring themselves on it, and at this point he considers the grate design to be approximately optimal." Gepard said, "Optimal for what?" "That's what I asked." "What did he say?" "He said optimal for reminding people that machinery requires attention." She took another bite. "I think he's right. I just can't agree with him about it for medical reasons." Bronya was smiling. Gepard was trying not to smile. Lynx was eating, which was the Lynx version of victory. After dinner, Lynx managed to find something that needed to be done in a different room. This was also obviously deliberate and also obviously irrelevant as a piece of information. Gepard and Bronya ended up in the sitting room with tea, which Gepard had made, which was fine. "Your sister," Bronya said. "Yes," Gepard said. "She's terrifying." "She's nineteen." He sat in his chair. "She's always been like that." "The assessment look." Bronya wrapped both hands around the cup. "She was assessing me the entire dinner." "She was." "And?" "If she'd found you lacking, you would have heard about it," he said. "The fact that you didn't hear about it is the result." Bronya looked at him. "That's not very reassuring." "It's accurate." He looked at his tea. "She likes you. She told me so before you arrived, because she'd decided in advance." He paused. "She tends to decide things in advance and then proceed with the outcome." "Where does she get that from?" He looked at her. She looked back, and the corner of her mouth was doing the thing it did. He said: "I have no idea what you're implying." "I'm not implying anything," she said, perfectly straight. "I'm asking a genuine question about your sister's character." He looked at his tea again. "She gets it from our mother," he said. "Serval gets the impulsiveness. Lynx and I get the—" "The certainty?" "I was going to say stubbornness." He looked at her. "But certainty is more charitable." "I'm feeling charitable," she said. They sat in the sitting room. Outside the dome was in its evening cycle, the warm gold of 1900. The signal was at 3.2 and holding. The patrol rotation had been revised. Everything that needed to be noted had been noted. Bronya looked at the room — the bookshelves, the photos, the particular quality of a house that was actually lived in rather than professionally maintained. She felt something she couldn't immediately name, which she would have identified later as the feeling of being somewhere that was a home. "The voices in the Restricted Zone," she said. She'd been holding it since the corridor this morning. He put his cup down. "I need to tell someone what they sound like," she said. "Before I know what they are. I need to say it out loud to someone." "Tell me," he said. Simply. She looked at the wall. "It's not language. It's not words. It's—" She thought about it. "Have you ever been near something very large? Like the generators when they're at full output, the subsonic frequency that you feel rather than hear?" "Yes." "It's like that. But not the generators. Something below the generators, below the stone, below everything. And it's — directional. It comes from underneath the northern section specifically, and it moves." She paused. "Like it's aware of where I'm standing." He was quiet. "It started quiet. Two weeks ago I could barely feel it. Now—" She looked at him. "Now I can feel it from twenty meters outside the Restricted Zone. It gets stronger every time I go." "Has it changed quality? The frequency itself." "Yes." She looked at her cup. "It's getting angrier." She said it plainly and it sat in the room between them. Angrier. Not louder — angrier. The specific word a person used when the thing they were describing had an emotional register to it, which was alarming in the context of a Stellaron's geological vibration. "I'm going to talk to Svarog tomorrow," she said. "Good." He leaned forward slightly. "Bronya. Tell me when it gets worse." "I will." She met his eyes. "But I don't want to—" She stopped. "Don't want to what?" "Alarm you before I know what it means." He looked at her for a moment. "You're already alarmed," he said. "I can tell. The difference between you alarmed and you not alarmed is very specific and you are currently alarmed." He paused. "So you may as well tell me." She stared at him. "That is a very impertinent thing to say to the Supreme Guardian." "You're not the Supreme Guardian right now," he said. "You're at my kitchen table drinking tea." A pause. "You're in the sitting room. We moved." He looked around. "I'm not sure when." She also looked around. She had indeed moved from the kitchen table to the sitting room chair without noticing the transition. She looked back at him. Something in the quality of the room had shifted without announcing itself, the way the best things shifted. "Tell me when it gets worse," he said again. "Yes," she said. This time it was an actual yes. They finished the tea. Lynx reappeared from the other room and produced dessert from somewhere, which nobody asked about. Bronya stayed until 2100, which was longer than she'd planned, and walked back to the palace in the dome's evening cool feeling, for the first time in weeks, like she'd actually rested. —— Svarog told her on Thursday. She came to him — came to the workshop in the morning while everyone else was occupied, sat at the secondary bench across from him, and said: "Tell me what you know about the First Stellaron. All of it." He looked at her for a moment. "I know you've been holding things back," she said. "Not from me specifically. From everyone. Because you've been waiting for the right moment." She met his optical array directly. "The signal moved. The right moment is now." He was quiet for three seconds. Then he said: "Yes." He told her. Not everything — four hundred years of accumulated records could not be communicated in a morning, and not all of it was relevant — but the essential things. The eight-hundred-year-old escalation. The cold light seen through the ice plates. The three settlements abandoned at the outer boundary. The nature of the First Stellaron, which was not storage or memory or accumulation but power. Pure, escalating, released when the signal reached 4.7. He told her what the voices she was hearing were. "Not language," he said. "The Stellaron is not communicating. What you are receiving is seismic. The geological pressure of something extremely large shifting position below the surface. The northern section of the Restricted Zone is the point closest to the First Stellaron's primary mass. You are the Guardian. The Guardian's attunement to the Stellaron is real and documented — not a mystical connection, a physiological one, developed through proximity and responsibility. You are the most sensitive receiver in Belobog for the First Stellaron's signal." "Which means I'm going to keep hearing it," she said. "Yes." "And it's going to get worse as the signal escalates." "Yes." She looked at the workshop wall. The notation network ran through the geological structure in every direction. The Architects' pre-Separation carvings. The channels that had been built for the Second Stellaron's energy and that had, weeks ago, held the Manchester Smash's Ice Flames and conducted them upward through six sub-levels. The same channels that were now conducting the First Stellaron's deepening frequency toward the surface. "The battle against the Second Stellaron," she said slowly. "When Gepard used the Manchester Smash. It propagated upward through the notation network to the surface." "Yes." "The notation network connects to the Restricted Zone." "Yes." "If the First Stellaron reaches activation and breaks through—" "It will come through the geological structure the same way the Second Stellaron did," Svarog said. "But larger. Faster. The First Stellaron has been building pressure for eight hundred years. The Second Stellaron was forty-three years of accumulation. This is twenty times that." The room was very quiet. "What does it look like?" she said. "When it activates. The First Stellaron." Svarog turned to the tertiary bench and pulled up the image he had in the geological monitoring data — not a photograph, the Stellaron was not something you photographed, but a composite built from the frequency readings and the geological resonance patterns across eight hundred years of monitoring. What it produced was: crystalline. Overwhelming. A winged form made of prismatic light and raw geological force, radiating in every direction, blinding at the center. Not armor like the Second Stellaron. Not a form that could be contained or addressed at the joints. Something that was light and pressure and sheer destructive output given geological shape. She looked at it for a long time. "We've never beaten something like this," she said. "No," Svarog said. "Belobog has not." She looked at it. Then she said: "We will." She said it the way she said things that were going to be true. Not to reassure herself. Not to reassure him. Because she had looked at the shape of the thing and had run the full honest accounting and had arrived at the conclusion: they would. This was not the performance of confidence. This was Bronya Rand at twenty-six years old, who had come through Everwinter Hill and the Second Stellaron and the twelve weeks since, who had built the city's capacity carefully and methodically and with the specific patience of someone who was preparing for exactly this. She looked at the composite image of the First Stellaron. She memorized it. Then she straightened and said: "I need a timeline projection. Based on the 3.2 signal and the rate data once we have it. And I need to know the notation network's role in this — if it conducted the Manchester Smash upward, it can be used in the engagement." "I have been modeling that for two weeks," Svarog said. She looked at him. "Of course you have." "I was waiting for—" "The right moment." She stood up. "Tomorrow morning. Full briefing. You, me, Gepard, Pela, Serval. I want everything on the table." "Understood," he said. She went to the door. Stopped. "Svarog." "Yes." "The cold light. In the eight-hundred-year-old records." She looked at him. "Have you seen it in the northern section?" A pause. "Not yet." "But you're watching for it." "Yes," he said. "I am." She nodded. She went. He watched the door close behind her and thought about the cold light — the prismatic glow that the pre-Separation records described appearing through the ice plates two years before the escalation reached 4.1. He thought about what it would mean if it appeared now. He thought about the timeline. He went back to the modeling. —— Friday morning, Gepard went running. This was a thing he'd done his whole life — not training runs, not patrol, just running. The outer wall route before the city was fully awake, six kilometers, when the dome was still in the pre-cycle blue before the morning simulation. He'd done it as a kid, before he was in the Guard. He'd done it on the northern ridge during the mission, sometimes at altitude conditions that made it technically inadvisable. He did it now, at twenty-five, because it was the cleanest form of thinking he had. He was thinking about the First Stellaron. He had been thinking about it since Tuesday's briefing, but not the way the briefing invited him to think about it — not the signal numbers, not the timeline projections. He was thinking about what it meant to be the First Class Singularity of Belobog when the First Stellaron activated. What the role was. What the position demanded. The Second Stellaron had been an engagement — a battle, with identifiable forms and tactical approaches and a grid of positions and Gepard managing the courtyard while Seele and Luka handled the aerial forms. It had been large and it had been hard and the Manchester Smash at thirty-one percent had frozen a one-hundred-and-fifty-meter cave, and he had been at eighteen percent on the other side of it. He had done the accounting since then. He'd been doing it quietly, not because he was worried — he didn't think worried was the right word — but because the accounting mattered and needed to be done correctly. At eighty percent of his capacity, the Second Stellaron had been defeated. The First Stellaron was a categorically different threat. Different nature, different scale, different kind of danger. He was thirty-one percent Ice Flames away from one hundred percent. He had never gone to one hundred percent. The northern ridge had been the closest he'd come, and the northern ridge had been — extensive. A year of learning what the ceiling was by pressing against it repeatedly in conditions where pressing against it was the only option. He had come back different from the ridge. More certain, somehow, about exactly where the ceiling was and what was beyond it. He thought: beyond the ceiling is what comes after eighty percent. He thought: that's what the First Stellaron is going to require. He turned at the outer wall's northeast corner and picked up pace on the downhill section. The dome was starting its morning cycle, the blue shifting toward amber. Below him the outer district was beginning to wake up — generators cycling, first patrols out, the particular smell of Belobog's morning that was cold and mineral and clean. He loved this city. He had loved it his whole life. He had spent his entire adult life standing in front of it, and he was not going to stop now. The question was not whether he would stand in front of it. The question was what happened when he went past eighty percent, and whether what he had, past eighty percent, was enough. He ran the last kilometer thinking about this and arrived at no particular conclusion, which was fine. The conclusion would arrive when it was needed. It always did. —— Full briefing, Saturday morning. Bronya, Gepard, Pela, Serval, Svarog, Sampo — because Sampo always ended up in these rooms, and because his outer district intelligence was useful, and because he was going to find out anyway. Natasha via the monitoring station link, because she was a medical practitioner and she needed the operational picture. Svarog ran the data first. The signal had moved again — 3.2 to 3.3 Thursday night, logged by Rikov at 0212. The rate between the two movements: 0.1 kilocycles in forty-eight hours. If the rate held, 4.7 was fourteen weeks away. "If the rate holds," Pela said immediately. "If the rate holds," Svarog confirmed. "The escalation eight hundred years ago did not hold a consistent rate. It accelerated. I expect this one will too." "Which means fourteen weeks is the optimistic end," Gepard said. "Yes." Gepard looked at the graph. Then at Bronya. She was looking at the graph too. She had the quality she'd been developing over the past weeks — the listening quality, the hearing-something-beneath-the-surface quality — and it was more present in the briefing room than it had been in the sitting room Thursday night. She was hearing the frequency through the palace floors. "The notation network," Bronya said. Serval straightened at the word, because this was her area. "What about it?" "When the Manchester Smash propagated through the geological structure, the notation channels carried it upward and outward. All the way to the surface." Bronya looked at her. "Can the network be used in an engagement against the First Stellaron?" Serval looked at Svarog. Svarog looked at Serval. This was the look of two people who had both been modeling the same thing and were now going to have to share the model. Serval said: "The network is a conductor. It carries what enters it at sufficient force and frequency. The Manchester Smash worked because Gepard's Ice Flames are in the source-material frequency range — the same range the notation network was built to carry." She paused. "The First Stellaron's signal is in a different frequency range. The network isn't going to carry it the same way." "But Gepard's Ice Flames are still in the right range," Bronya said. "Yes," Svarog said. "At full output, the Ice Flames would conduct through the network at maximum propagation. What that means at one hundred percent of Gepard's capacity rather than thirty-one is—" He looked at Gepard. "Larger. Significantly larger." The room processed this. Sampo said, from his seat: "How much larger are we talking?" Svarog said: "At thirty-one percent, the Manchester Smash froze a one-hundred-and-fifty-meter cave and propagated upward through three sub-levels. At one hundred percent, the propagation would extend through the full geological structure of the Restricted Zone. The surface." He paused. "Possibly beyond." A silence. Gepard said: "I've never been to one hundred percent." "No," Svarog said. "But you've been to eighty. And the Ice Flames exist past the eighty percent boundary. We know this from the Second Stellaron engagement." He looked at Gepard steadily. "The question is what they become past eighty." Gepard met his gaze. He was thinking about the run this morning, about the accounting, about the ceiling and what was past it. "I'll find out when I need to." "That," Svarog said, "is what I expected you to say." Pela was looking between them with the sixteen-year-old expression of someone who had just received a very large amount of information and was organizing it at high speed. "The First Stellaron," she said. "Its activation form. What are we actually engaging? The Second Stellaron had an armored form, a second stage, specific structural weak points we could target." She looked at Svarog. "What does the First Stellaron have?" Svarog turned to the display. He brought up the composite image — the crystalline winged form, prismatic light, overwhelming scale. The room saw it for the first time. Nobody said anything for four seconds. Then Serval said: "Oh, that's going to be terrible." "Yes," Svarog said. "How big?" "The pre-Separation records estimate the form at one hundred and twenty to one hundred and fifty meters. Larger than the Second Stellaron's seventy." He paused. "The form is not armored. It is crystalline. The structural approach is different — not joint weakness, frequency disruption. The crystallization that constitutes the form responds to opposing frequencies." "Which is my area," Serval said. "Which is your area," he confirmed. She was already pulling out her tablet. He could see her running the resonance math in her head. "The Ice Flames in the notation network," she said. "If they propagate at the right frequency, they're not just force — they're a disruption signal. For a crystalline form—" "They would be exceptionally effective," Svarog said. Serval looked at Gepard. "How do you feel about that?" "Fine," he said. "It's going to require going past eighty percent." "I said fine." She looked at him for a moment longer in the way she had when she was deciding whether to push it. She decided not to. She went back to the tablet. Bronya was watching all of this. She'd been watching throughout the briefing with the quality of someone assembling a full picture — not just the tactical pieces but the human ones. How Gepard held himself when Svarog said one hundred percent. How Serval calibrated her push and pulled back. How Pela was already three steps ahead of the room and trying not to show it in case it made people feel bad. "The city," Bronya said. "When the First Stellaron activates, what does the Fragmentum response look like?" "Larger than the Second Stellaron's," Svarog said. "The Second Stellaron's field was concentrated. The First Stellaron's output is omnidirectional at activation. The Fragmentum response will be city-wide." "City-wide," Gepard said. Not a question. Processing. "Yes." Svarog looked at him. "The Guard and Wildfire cannot hold a city-wide perimeter. Not at activation-level Fragmentum density." "So we don't try to hold the perimeter," Gepard said. "We prioritize the critical zones. Residential corridors. Medical points. Evacuation routes." He was already thinking it through. "Dunn can—" "The outer machine district's notation network," Bronya said, interrupting herself at the same moment she interrupted him. She looked at Clara's regular seat, which was empty — Clara was at the notation bench in the workshop. "The network conducted the Manchester Smash. If the Ice Flames propagate through the network at full output and disrupt the First Stellaron's form—" "The Fragmentum response collapses," Serval finished. "Because the source collapses. The Fragmentum is sustained by the Stellaron's field. No field, no sustained Fragmentum." "How fast?" "Minutes. If the disruption works." "If," Gepard said. "If," Serval agreed. Bronya looked at Gepard. Gepard looked at Bronya. They were doing the thing they'd been doing for months — the conversation in the look, the one that contained the same analysis and arrived at the same conclusion from different directions. "Fourteen weeks," Bronya said. "Optimistic." She looked at Pela. "I want a new projection every time the signal moves. Day or night." "You'll have it," Pela said. "The notation network," she said to Svarog. "I want full documentation of its frequency characteristics for the engagement. If we're using it as the propagation medium, I need to know what it can take." "I'll have it to you by the end of the week." She looked around the table. "We know what's coming. We know what it requires. We have time to prepare." She paused. "Use the time." —— The weeks between the Saturday briefing and the signal hitting 3.6 were the particular kind of weeks that passed like they were moving through something thick. Not slow exactly — there was too much happening for slow. But heavy. Every day weighted with the signal number that morning, the Pela projection in the evening, the patrol logs, the notation network's frequency readings, the quiet work of people preparing for a thing that was still weeks away but getting closer. The shops were still open. The fight circuit still ran on Tuesdays. Lynx still had dinner on Tuesday nights, which had become a standing thing that Bronya now appeared at reliably at 1900 and left at 2100 with the expression of someone who had eaten a real meal and sat in a room that was a home. Seele and Luka: mornings were theirs. Before the drills, before the workshop, before anything operational. They'd developed a specific morning rhythm that Seele privately thought was one of the better things about having someone to be with in the morning — the way the day started before it started, in the quiet of the apartment, coffee and the particular light the dome produced at 0700 when nobody had made it be anything yet. She read. He made coffee wrong and brought it to her anyway and she drank it wrong and didn't say anything about it. He sat next to her and sometimes picked up his own book and sometimes just existed there in the way he had of existing comfortably in a space. "Stop thinking about the signal," she said one morning without looking up. "I'm not thinking about the signal." "You're doing the jaw thing." "I don't do a jaw thing." She finally looked up at him. She looked at his jaw. He was, in fact, doing a jaw thing. "I'm slightly thinking about the signal," he said. "3.4," she said. "It'll be 3.5 next week probably. We have weeks." She looked back at her book. "Drink your coffee." He drank his coffee. She read. The jaw thing stopped. Sampo came by the outer district every few days — he had the corridor routes down at this point, knew every junction in the network, had started walking them so regularly that the Guard patrols had started timing their own routes around his on the basis that if Sampo had already checked a junction it was probably fine. He and Natasha had dinner twice a week. He had a habit of showing up at the ward at the end of her shift with food in a bag and a specific expression she had learned to read as: I missed you and I'm not going to say that so I brought food instead. She let him have the food version. She understood the translation. "You're going to be running the non-standard corridors when it activates," she said one evening, not accusatory, just factual. "Yes," he said. "You're going to be the person getting people through the parts of the underground that the Guard doesn't have mapped." "Also yes." He ate something. "Is there a point to this line of questioning or are you establishing a record?" "I'm establishing that I know what you're going to do and I'm not going to tell you not to do it." She looked at him. "But you're going to check in with the medical point at junction twelve every thirty minutes during the event." "That seems excessive." "Every thirty minutes, Sampo." He looked at her for a moment. He had the expression of someone running the calculation of whether this was worth the argument. He ran it and concluded it was not. "Every thirty minutes." "Thank you." "You're going to be at junction twelve the whole time?" "Yes." She met his eyes. "Every thirty minutes." He said: "Okay." And it was not just the logistical agreement. She knew what okay from Sampo sounded like when it was more than the word. Clara was at the notation bench almost every morning now, running what she called calibration contact — not the activation-level connection she'd held during the Manchester Smash, but the steady background touch that was teaching her how the network felt at different signal levels. When the signal moved and Pela sent the updated projection, Clara would put her hand on the bench and say something about the quality of the contact. She had developed a vocabulary for it that nobody else fully understood but that Svarog seemed to receive as accurate. At signal 3.5, she said: "The lower channels are denser. Like the stone is holding its breath." Oleg wrote it in the notebook. Toma and Pino read it over his shoulder. "That's poetic," Pino said. "It's accurate," Oleg said. "Both," Clara said. She pressed her hand more firmly. The bench ran warm under her palm, the notation network humming at its frequencies. She could feel the difference from three weeks ago — the depth of it, the way the signal from below had changed quality. Not louder. Heavier. The stone holding its breath. The signal hit 3.7 on a Wednesday morning. Rikov logged it at 0401. By the time Gepard had the message and had sent it to the full channel, Bronya was already awake. She'd been woken by the frequency through the palace floor at around 0350. She had sat up in the dark and listened to it for ten minutes before her phone lit up. She told Gepard in the morning, when he came to the palace outer corridor before the briefing: "I can feel it through the floor at night now." He looked at her. She looked back. "How bad?" he said. "Not bad. Just—" She thought about it. "Insistent. Like something that has been patient for a very long time and has decided patience is no longer the priority." He held her gaze. "We're going to be ready." "I know," she said. And she meant it. The corridor was quiet around them. The dome in its morning cycle. The city waking up below them, generators and early patrols and the smell of cold stone. It was their city. It had always been their city, in different registers — his from eight years at the wall, hers from the weight of the Guardianship, both of them from something older and less official that had to do with having grown up in it and having decided, separately and together, that they were not going to let it go. "Come back," she said. "I always do," he said. She held his gaze for a moment. Then she turned to the briefing room. He fell into step beside her. Their shoulders were close enough to touch without touching, which was the same as always, which was its own kind of thing. The signal was at 3.7. Fourteen weeks had become something shorter. —— The cold light appeared in the northern section of the Restricted Zone at 0312 on a Thursday morning. Rikov was not the one who found it. Bronya was, because she'd been in the Restricted Zone since 0245, because the frequency through the palace floor had woken her and had the specific quality that said: now. She stood in the northern section and looked at the light. It came through the ice plates at the lower northern section, where the geological structure was oldest, where the Restricted Zone's stone was deepest and most dense. Prismatic. Cold. Not the amber warmth of the notation network or the blue-green of the Great Glacial Aegir's bioluminescence. Something different. Something that had not been seen in Belobog in eight hundred years. She looked at it for a long time. Then she sent a message to Gepard, who was at the outer wall by 0325. Then she sent one to Pela, who updated the projection by 0340: six to eight weeks. Signal at 3.9 and climbing. Not 3.7. Not a steady rate. Climbing. The cold light was there. The First Stellaron was awake. —— END OF CHAPTER 51 —— Gepard reached the outer wall of the Restricted Zone at 0325 and stood looking at the northern section for a long moment before he crossed into it. The cold light was visible from the wall. That was the first thing. You didn't need to be inside the zone, you didn't need to be close — you could see it from twenty meters out, a faint prismatic shimmer coming through the ice plates at the base of the northern section's geological face. Like something enormous and luminous had settled just below the stone and was radiating upward through the rock. He crossed into the zone. Bronya was at the center of the northern section, standing in the snow, not moving. She had her coat pulled tight and she was looking at the ice plates and the light coming through them. She heard him coming and didn't turn around. "How long has it been doing this?" he said. "I don't know exactly. It was already doing it when I got here at 0250." She finally turned. In the cold light her face was doing something specific — not fear, not awe. The quality of someone who had been preparing for a thing and had just been handed the confirmation that the preparing was correct. "Svarog said the eight-hundred-year record described it appearing two years before that escalation peaked." "We don't have two years." "No." She looked at the light again. "We have weeks. The frequency has changed in the past three days. Pela's going to have a new projection by morning." She paused. "It's not going to be fourteen weeks anymore." He came to stand beside her. They looked at the cold light together — the prismatic shimmer through the ice, the faint but unmistakable glow of something below the stone that had not been below the stone before. Or rather: had been below the stone, had always been below the stone, but had been deep enough and still enough that its presence was not felt. Not anymore. "What does it feel like?" he said. "Right now. Standing here." She thought about it. "Like standing next to a building that you know is going to fall," she said. "Not imminently. But you can feel the foundation failing. You can feel it through the ground." She looked at him. "It's not threatening me specifically. It doesn't know I'm here. It's just — enormous. And moving." "Toward the surface." "Toward the surface." He looked at the ice plates. The cold light. He thought about the composite image Svarog had showed them — the crystalline winged form, one hundred and fifty meters of prismatic destruction. He thought about the Manchester Smash at thirty-one percent and what it had felt like in his hands when the Ice Flames came up. He thought about past eighty. "Go back to the palace," he said. She looked at him. "Not because I'm worried," he said. "Because you've been out here since 0250 and you need to sleep before the briefing." She looked at him for another moment. He could see her running the argument — she didn't need sleep, she was fine, there was work to do. He could see her conclude that he was right. She had that quality of taking correct information seriously regardless of where it came from, which he had always found specifically useful about her. "The briefing is at 0800," she said. "Which is five hours from now." "I don't think I can sleep." "Try," he said. "I'll be here." She looked at the cold light. She looked at him. Something in her face did the thing it did — the layer below the Guardian, the part that was twenty-six years old and tired and here in the Restricted Zone at 0325 looking at something enormous moving under the stone. "Gepard," she said. "Yeah." "Tell me it's going to be all right." He didn't say it immediately. Not because he wasn't certain — he was certain, in the specific way that came from having looked at exactly what was required and knowing you were the person who could provide it. But because she didn't want the immediate version. She wanted the true version. "It's going to be hard," he said. "Harder than the Second Stellaron. Harder than anything this city has faced." He held her gaze. "And it's going to be all right." She looked at him for a long moment. "You mean that." "I mean it the way I mean everything," he said. "Which is completely." She was quiet. Then she exhaled, slow, and something in her shoulders eased slightly. "Go sleep," he said. "I'll try." She looked at the light one more time. "Don't stay here all night." "I'll stay until the patrol rotation." "That's not—" She stopped. "Fine. Until the patrol rotation." She turned toward the zone exit. Stopped. "Gepard." "Yeah." "Thank you." She didn't specify for what. She didn't need to. He watched her cross the Restricted Zone toward the palace. He stayed at the northern section until the 0500 patrol came through, watching the cold light through the ice plates, thinking about past eighty percent and what it was going to require and finding that what he felt about it, underneath the calculation, was something simpler than fear. He was ready. —— Thursday's full briefing landed like a physical thing. Svarog's projection, with the cold light factored in: six weeks. Possible compression to four if the escalation rate matched the historical pattern. Pela's updated model agreed. The signal had moved from 3.7 to 3.9 between midnight and 0400. "Six weeks," Gepard said, when the numbers were on the table. "Possibly four." "Possibly four," Pela said. She did not say possibly more comfortably than that because the data did not suggest it. "The perimeter," Gepard said. He pulled out the coverage map — the one he'd been revising every week since the second Stellaron's defeat, updating for the First Stellaron's different threat profile, adjusting for city-wide Fragmentum response rather than district-concentrated. "The Guard cannot hold a city-wide line. What we can do is hold the critical nodes—" "Show me," Bronya said. He laid the map on the table. He walked her through it — not the summary version, the actual version, the positions and the routes and the personnel assignments and the specific gaps that existed and how he intended to close them. She listened without interrupting, which was how she listened when the information was genuinely what she needed rather than a briefing performed for a record. Serval was next to him, following the map with her own overlaid frequency data. She'd built a model of the notation network's coverage in relation to the projected Fragmentum response zones. "The outer machine district," she said. "The network is densest here, here, and here." She pointed. "If the propagation comes through the network at full output, the Fragmentum disruption will be most effective in these areas. The residential zones in the northwest—" she looked at Gepard "—are the gap." "Sampo," Gepard said immediately. From across the table, Sampo looked up from the map. "I know every corridor between here and the northwest residential sections that isn't in the Guard's formal route map." He paused. "I have been walking them for fun for twenty years. For approximately twenty years I have been doing this for fun." He looked at Gepard. "I feel like this is a moment." "It is a moment," Gepard agreed. "I need those routes documented and I need them done by end of week." "I'll have them to you by tomorrow." "End of week is fine." "Tomorrow's fine. I have nothing else going on." Serval looked at him. "You're very prepared for someone who found out about this six weeks ago." "I found out about the specific details six weeks ago," Sampo said. "I found out about the general shape of it when I was eleven years old and lived in the outer machine district." He shrugged. "Some things you carry." The briefing continued. Natasha ran the medical contingency plan — expanded from the Second Stellaron version, city-wide rather than district-focused, with relay points in every major residential section. Lynx was coordinating the mobile response teams. Clara's role was the notation network — maintaining contact during the activation to keep the channels clear for the Ice Flames' propagation. "How long can you hold it?" Bronya asked her. Clara had come to this briefing specifically. She was twelve and sat at the adult table with the quality of someone who had been doing adult things for long enough that the table size was not the relevant variable. "During the Manchester Smash I held it for eleven minutes. Eleven minutes was what that required." She pressed her hand to the notation carving on the wall beside the briefing table — old habit, the touch a grounding. "For a full First Stellaron activation, I don't know how long it will require. I know I can hold longer than eleven minutes." "Svarog," Bronya said. "She can hold it," Svarog said. Simply. Clara looked at him. He looked at her. The Architect lineage and the oldest consciousness on the planet, agreeing across a briefing table. "She can hold it," he said again, to the room. Nobody argued. Gepard looked around the table when the briefing was winding down. Bronya to his left. Serval with her frequency data. Pela with the projection model. Svarog at the end. Sampo with the corridor map. Natasha via the relay link, already moving. Six weeks. Possibly four. He thought about the run this morning, the outer wall route, the dome in its pre-cycle blue. He thought about eight years at the wall and the northern ridge and what the press of death produced in a person who refused to stop. "We know what's coming," he said. "We've known the shape of it for months. The difference now is that we have the timeline." He looked at Bronya, then at the room. "Four to six weeks. That's what we have. Use every day of it." He put his hands flat on the table and stood. The briefing was over. The work was beginning. —— END OF CHAPTER 51 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 10: "What You Don't Remember" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text There is a moment, three days after the text at 2340, where Gepard walks into the office and she is standing at the window. Not at the desk, not with a folder, not in motion between one task and another — just standing at the window, looking out at the upper city, with her arms loose at her sides and her hair down, which she does not usually wear down during working hours and which she has apparently forgotten about. The morning light comes through the glass and sits on the side of her face, and she has the expression that only exists when she does not know anyone is watching — unmanaged, unheld, completely her own. He stops in the doorway. He knows, with the certainty of someone who has been paying attention for five years, that he should announce himself. A knock, a cleared throat, something to give her the half-second she needs to reassemble the architecture that the window and the quiet morning have temporarily taken down. He does not do this. He stands in the doorway for three seconds and looks at her the way he does not let himself look when she can see him — fully, without the careful peripheral management, without the practiced discipline of looking slightly to the left of the thing he cannot look at directly. Three seconds of looking at the line of her standing in the morning light, at the loose hair and the open hands and the face that is just her face, and then he does the correct thing, which is knock on the open door frame. She turns. She sees him. Something moves through her expression — not the surprise of being caught, exactly, but the specific quality of someone recalibrating from a private state to a shared one, and finding the distance between those two states to be shorter than it used to be. She does not fully reassemble. She looks at him for a moment with the window light still on her face and the hair still down, and she says: "You're early." "The morning brief was moved up. I sent word—" "I saw it." She moves toward the desk, reaching for the clasp on the table to pin her hair back. "I was just—" She stops. The clasp is in her hand. She looks at it. She sets it down. "Give me the perimeter update." He gives her the perimeter update. He stands in the doorway the whole time and does not step fully into the room, because he is aware, with a clarity that is becoming difficult to ignore, that if he closes the distance between the doorway and the desk he is going to be standing approximately where he was standing in the stairwell three weeks ago, and the stairwell still lives in him the way that certain mornings do — specific, present, not settling. She listens to the perimeter update with the hair clasp still on the desk and her hair still down, and at the end of it she looks at him across the room with the expression she has been wearing for thirteen days, the less-sealed one, and she says: "Come in, Gepard. You don't have to stand in the door." He comes in. He closes the distance. He sits in the chair from the corner, which is at the desk, which means he is sitting close enough that if he set his patrol report down and she set her briefing folder down and neither of them moved back, they would be near enough for the quality of near to become a different question entirely. He sets the patrol report on the desk. She does not move back. "The administrator briefing is at ten," she says. "Yes." "I want you there." "I'll be there." She looks at him. He looks at her. The clasp is between them on the desk, and the hair is still down, and the morning light is doing whatever it does, and neither of them picks up their respective documents for a moment that is approximately four seconds long and feels considerably longer. Then she picks up the briefing folder. He picks up the patrol report. And the four seconds are over, and they work, and the thing that was present in those four seconds does not go away — it settles, the way a thing settles when it has been acknowledged without being named, becoming part of the texture of the room. At some point, without planning to, he reaches across the desk to move a map that is covering the eastern perimeter notation she needs, and his hand passes close to hers — not touching, but close, the specific proximity where you are aware of the warmth of another person's skin without contact — and she does not move her hand away. She finishes writing the notation. He moves the map.
