Their hands are near each other for perhaps two seconds in the vicinity of the desk, and then they are not, and neither of them says anything about it, and the thing in the room gets slightly heavier. —— The administrator briefing goes well, which Gepard measures by the fact that no one leaves the room in open panic and Administrator Venn, who has been Bronya's most persistent institutional obstacle for three weeks, asks two intelligent follow-up questions about the Stellaron containment protocol and accepts her answers with the look of a man who is updating his priors in real time. After it, they walk back through the palace together — the route they have been taking together for two weeks, through the administrative wing's outer passage with the north-facing windows. The same route she walked with Seele. The same route he walked with Seele. The corridor that has become, without formal designation, their corridor, the one that belongs to the category of things that have acquired meaning through repetition without anyone deciding they should. She says, midway through it: "Venn asked good questions." "He did." "He's going to be useful. Once he finishes deciding whether to trust me." "He's almost there." She glances at him. "You think so." "The second question. He wouldn't have asked it if he wasn't leaning toward you. He was checking whether your answer would match what he already suspects is true." He pauses. "It did." She is quiet for a step. Two. "You were watching him." "I watch everyone in those rooms." "I know you do." Another pause. The light through the windows makes moving shapes on the corridor floor as they walk, the pale winter sun doing its limited best. "What else did you see." "Pela is going to need a research assistant. She's been working at a pace that isn't sustainable and she won't say so." He glances at her. "She'll accept it if you frame it as resourcing rather than concern." Bronya looks at him. "I'll talk to her today." "And Seele — she's been taking on more than was assigned to her. The civilian alert coordination expanded into resettlement logistics at some point in the last week and no one formally authorized the expansion, she just absorbed it." "Because it needed doing." "Yes. But she needs the formal authority or she's going to hit administrative walls." He pauses. "Give her the title. She's already doing the job." She is looking at him as they walk. He is aware of this — the specific quality of her attention when it is fully on him, which has a weight to it that he has spent years learning to receive without showing that he receives it, with declining success. "You notice everything," she says. "It's my job." "No," she says, quietly. "Parts of it are your job. The rest is—" She stops. They have reached the stairwell landing, the one with the window that looks south, the one where she stood three weeks ago and told him the accurate version. She stops at the top of the landing and turns to look at him, and he stops too, because she has stopped, and because he was not going to walk past her when she stopped, and because this is simply the thing his body does in response to hers, which is another fact he has filed and not looked at. "The rest is you," she says. He looks at her. She is standing at the landing window with the pale light behind her, her hair still down from this morning, the briefing folder in her hand, and she is looking at him the way she has been looking at him with increasing frequency and decreasing management since the array — directly, with the full weight of it, not deflecting to the desk or the map or the folder. She is looking at him and she looks like she has made a decision, or is about to, or is standing at the edge of one. "Bronya," he says, and his voice comes out lower than he intends and he does not correct it. "I know," she says. "I know what I'm doing." "I'm not sure you do," he says, which is not what he means, and she knows it's not what he means, because what he means is I'm not sure I do, and they both know that, and the words are just the professional architecture making one last structural argument before it stops being able to hold the weight. She takes one step toward him. Not a decisive step, not a closing-of-distance step — a single, measured step, the kind that shortens the space between them by enough to change the quality of it, so that he can see the specific grey of her eyes clearly and the morning light catches the edge of her hair and the warmth of her proximity is a fact rather than an approximation. She tilts her head back slightly to look at him, because she is looking up and he is looking down, and this has always been the geometry of standing close to her — the specific angle of it, the way it makes the space between them feel more deliberate than it would be between two people of the same height, because both of them have to choose it. They are both choosing it. "I have the council session at two," she says, which is not what she means either, and they both know it. "I know," he says. "And the east wall final certification is tomorrow morning." "I know." "And Serval is leaving on Thursday." A pause. "She keeps telling me she'll come back." Another pause. "I believe her." "She will," he says. "She always does." They are standing close enough that his exhale moves a strand of her hair, and she does not step back, and he does not step back, and the stairwell landing is very quiet in the way that spaces are quiet when the world has arranged itself around a single point of attention. "Gepard," she says, and his name in her voice at this range, at this volume, in this register, does something to him that he has no adequate description for and does not attempt one. "Yes," he says. She looks at him. She looks at him the way she looked at the frequency guide when the array fractured the feedback loop — like something that has been a sound in her head for a very long time is finally taking a form she can see clearly. And then her hand — the left one, the one that trembled in the array room, the one that she stilled deliberately and he looked away from — comes up, very slowly, and rests against his jaw. She is not reaching up to him the way she reaches for armor pieces or documents or the things on high shelves that require effort. She is simply — placing her hand there. Intentionally. The cold of her palm against the side of his face, the tips of her fingers just past his jaw, and she is looking at him the whole time with those grey eyes that are very close and very serious and entirely unmanaged. He does not breathe. He does not move. He stands on the stairwell landing with her hand on his face and the morning light behind her and five years of not-moving catching up to him all at once, and he brings his hand up — slowly, the specific slowness of someone being deliberate, not tentative — and covers hers. Not moving it. Not pulling it away. Just his hand over hers, holding it there, the warmth of both of them together against the side of his face. She exhales. It is a small sound. The same quality as the array room, when the pressure set down — not dramatic, just honest, just the body acknowledging something real. "You're going to be late to the council session," he says, after a moment, because one of them has to say something and because it is true and because he cannot, in good conscience, be responsible for what he might say instead. "I'm always early to the council session," she says, and does not move her hand. He looks at her. She looks at him. His hand is over hers and her palm is still against his jaw and the stairwell landing has, without any formal announcement, become somewhere completely different from where it was three minutes ago. "Bronya," he says. "Don't," she says, gently. "Don't say we should be careful or that this is complicated or anything—" She stops. Her thumb moves, slightly, against his cheek — an involuntary thing, he thinks, and then thinks she probably didn't mean to do it, and then thinks it doesn't matter, and then stops thinking. "I know it's complicated," she says. "I've known it's complicated for—" She pauses. "A while." "Me too," he says. "I know," she says, and the corner of her mouth moves — the genuine one, the one that is not the almost-smile but is the thing the almost-smile was always pointing toward. "You're not subtle." He looks at her. "You're excellent at many things," she says, and there is something in her voice that is warm and precise and slightly terrible because it is her voice in that register at this range. "Subtle is not among them." "I thought I was managing it," he says. "You were managing it," she says. "I was aware of everything you were managing." A pause. "Since approximately—" She stops. Her eyes do the thing they do when she is calculating, the faint unfocus of someone retrieving a specific memory. "Since the winter assessment in our second year. When you stood outside the briefing room for six minutes before coming in." He stares at her. "I counted," she says. He stares at her. "You were seventeen," she says, and the warmth in her voice has something under it that has been there for considerably longer than three weeks or twelve days or any of the intervals he has been measuring. "And you knocked on an open door, which I had never seen anyone do before, and you stood at attention the entire briefing even though I told you to sit down, and afterwards you told me one thing that was wrong with my analysis and two things that were right and I—" She stops. She looks at him directly. "I noticed," she says. "I have been noticing. For a long time." He holds her gaze. "Why didn't you say anything," he says. "You know why," she says. And he does. He knows exactly why — the title, the rank, the space between them that was constructed carefully and for good reason and which they have been, gradually and then all at once, dismantling. "And I was afraid," she says, which costs her something, and she says it anyway. "That I was wrong. About what I was seeing." "You weren't wrong," he says. "I know that now," she says. His thumb moves across the back of her hand, the same involuntary echo of what she did a moment ago, and she feels it and looks down at their joined hands against his jaw and then back up at him, and the morning light is doing what it does and the stairwell is quiet and neither of them moves for a moment that stretches itself out between them like something that does not want to end. "The council session," he says eventually, very quietly. "In ninety minutes," she says. "Seele is going to wonder where you are." "Seele," she says, with the specific quality she uses when she is thinking about what Seele knows and has chosen not to say, "is going to have opinions about this regardless of when I show up." He breathes. He looks at her. He makes a decision that is perhaps the most deliberate one he has made in five years, which is to turn his head very slightly and press his lips, briefly, to her palm. It is not a large gesture. It does not need to be. The geometry of the stairwell and the five years and the morning light have already done the work — this is just the confirmation of it, quiet and contained and entirely without the drama it would probably warrant if either of them were built for drama. She goes still. Not the bracing stillness. Not the managed stillness. The stillness of someone receiving something they have been waiting for for a long time and are choosing to hold completely, without moving, so as not to lose any part of it. He lifts his head. She is looking at him with no composure requirement anywhere on her face. "Ninety minutes," he says. "Ninety minutes," she agrees. She takes her hand back, slowly, and the warmth of her palm leaves his jaw, and he lets her hand go and she takes it back and they are standing on the stairwell landing again, which is the same place they have been standing, and is entirely different. She straightens. She picks up her briefing folder. She looks at him once more with that face — the face without the armor, the one that has been appearing more and more frequently and which he is going to have to figure out how to look at directly or lose his mind entirely. "Don't be late to the council session," she says. "I'm never late," he says. "I know," she says, and goes. He stands on the landing for a moment. He breathes once. Twice. He does not go anywhere for thirty seconds. Then he picks up his patrol folder, and goes down the stairs, and is not late to the council session, and manages — with considerable effort — to read every line of the agenda. —— In corridor nine-beta, on the same morning, Luka is on his fourth walkthrough of the week when he finds Seele there again. This time she is not carrying a supply crate. She is crouched at the base of the wall where the crystallization is thickest, her hand hovering close to the amber-black surface, not touching, the specific posture of someone who has come here to look at something rather than pass through. She does not hear him coming. He stops several meters back, because the quality of her attention is the kind that should not be interrupted, and he watches her crouch in the tunnel's amber light and look at the wall with an expression he cannot quite read from this distance. He makes a sound with his boot on the ground — deliberate, a small warning — and she turns. She sees him. She stands. They look at each other. "You're here early," he says. "I could say the same." "Fourth walkthrough this week," he says, and does not know why he tells her this. She looks at him. Her eyes are very purple in the tunnel lighting, a specific shade he has not seen except on her, and there is something in her expression that is the same as the last time — the hostile wariness, the evaluation — but there is something underneath it that was not there the last time, or that he did not notice, which is a version of noticing that feels like a hand on the back of his neck. "Why do you come four times," she says. "The job is twice a week." "I know what Sampo told you. I'm asking why you come four times." He considers this. "The tunnels are different every time. Even when nothing changes, they're different." He pauses. "I want to know what they look like when they're different." She looks at him for a moment with an expression he cannot classify. Then she looks back at the wall. "The crystallization here," she says. "It's dulling faster in this section than the others." "I know. I've been logging it." He takes out his sensor by habit and looks at the reading. Yellow. Same as the others. He shows her the screen without deciding to. "Has been for four days." She looks at the screen. She looks at the wall. She does the thing she does — the gathering of words, the arrangement of them — and then she says, without preamble: "Do you ever feel like you know a place you've never been." He looks at her. "These tunnels," she says, still looking at the wall. "I've been in the Underworld my whole life, mostly. And I've been in these corridors twice. But they feel—" She stops. "Familiar." He says nothing. She turns and looks at him with the evaluation back in her eyes, the wariness — but the wariness has a different quality now, the quality of someone who is not wary of a stranger but of something they cannot explain, which is a more unsettling variety of wary. "That's strange," she says. "Isn't it." "Yes," he says. "Does it happen to you." He looks at the tunnel. He looks at the route he has walked from memory four times without checking the map once. He looks at her face and the specific purple of her eyes and the particular way she holds herself in a space, with that coiled readiness, the weight balanced forward on the balls of her feet, the way someone holds themselves when they grew up in an environment that required fast reactions. Something about this posture. Something about this posture is— He does not finish the thought, because it does not have a finish that makes sense. It is more of a shape than a thought — the shape of something half-recognized, like a word in a language you studied once and have mostly forgotten, appearing in a context where you do not expect it and the meaning arrives before the translation does. "Sometimes," he says. She looks at him. He looks at her. The tunnel is quiet. The dulling amber wall is at their backs. The sensor in his hand reads yellow. She says, carefully, like she is testing the weight of it: "I don't know why I keep coming back here." "The supply checks—" "I've done those. I come after the supply checks." She looks at him directly. "I don't know why." He holds her gaze. He does not look away. She does not look away. They stand in corridor nine-beta with the settled crystallization and the quiet and the amber light and the thing between them that has no name because it has no origin they can access, and the recognition that is not recognition because they cannot remember what there is to recognize. "I don't know why I come four times either," he says, honestly. Something shifts in her expression. The wariness does not leave — he suspects it never fully leaves, that it is too structural to her to simply lift — but something around it softens, slightly, in the specific way that happens when two people discover they share something they did not expect to share. "Your readings are accurate," she says, which is, from Seele, approximately equivalent to a formal declaration of truce. "The rate of dulling. Your logs are accurate." "Thank you," he says. "Don't make it a thing," she says, which is exactly what Pela said to Gepard ten days ago in a different corridor of the same building, and which he does not know, and which means nothing except that Belobog apparently produces a specific variety of person who does things because they are right and does not want gratitude for them. She moves to leave. She does not rush it this time — less of the fast purposeful exit, more of the ordinary movement of someone concluding a conversation. At the junction she stops, and he expects the one-more-thing, and she does it, but it is not what he expects. "Luka," she says. He waits. "Have you ever—" She stops. She is looking at the junction wall, the place where seven-alpha and nine-beta branch away from each other, and her expression has the quality of someone trying to say something they do not have the right words for. "Have you ever looked at someone and felt like you already know how they're going to finish their sentences." A pause. "Before they finish them." He looks at her profile in the tunnel light. "Sometimes," he says, which is the same word he used before, and they both know it. She nods. Slowly. She does not explain further and he does not ask her to. She takes the nine-beta branch and her footsteps go quiet around the bend, and he stands at the junction and looks at the wall and breathes the cold tunnel air and tries to find the end of the thought that has no end, the half-recognition that arrived before the translation did. He stays at the junction longer than he needs to. He does not check the map on the way out. —— Notes: He pressed his lips to her palm. It is not a large gesture. The stairwell has already done all the work — that was just the confirmation, quiet and real and exactly what they are. She went still. The bracing stillness was gone. She held it completely. HE LOOKED AT HER WITH NO COMPOSURE REQUIREMENT ANYWHERE ON HER FACE and then said ninety minutes because he is who he is and I love him. 'You're not subtle.' 'I thought I was managing it.' 'You were managing it. I was aware of everything you were managing.' Since the winter assessment in their second year. She counted the six minutes outside the door. She has been noticing since they were seventeen. He stood there when she told him and I think he needed a moment. The four seconds at the desk. The hands near each other. She did not move her hand away. The thing in the room getting slightly heavier. These two in the same space at close range are genuinely dangerous to each other and I mean that in the best possible way. Seele in corridor nine-beta crouching at the wall. Luka stopping so as not to interrupt her. The half-recognition — the shape of something before the translation arrives. 'Have you ever looked at someone and felt like you already know how they're going to finish their sentences.' The thing they cannot name because they cannot remember what there is to name. It is there. It has always been there. They are going to find it eventually and when they do I will not be held responsible for what happens. They were close once. A Fragmentum event took the memory of each other. The attraction is the memory trying to surface without the context to make sense of it. Neither of them knows this yet. Chapter 11 is coming. Things have shifted. The wall is certified. The Stellaron is quiet. The world outside Belobog is about to start paying attention. Comment and tell me you're still here. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 11: "Outside Looking In" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text The letter arrives on a Wednesday. It comes through official diplomatic channels, which means it arrives first on Pela's desk, which means Pela reads it before anyone else does, which means when she brings it to Bronya's office at 0900 her expression is already the carefully calibrated one — the one that is not alarmed and is not performing the absence of alarm, but is simply, precisely, holding something at measured distance while she decides what to do with it. Bronya reads it. She reads it twice. She sets it on the desk with the particular care of someone setting down something that has the potential to be heavy. "The IPC," she says. "The Interastral Peace Corporation," Pela confirms, which is not a clarification Bronya needs and which Pela offers anyway, because naming things precisely is how Pela processes them. "The letter is from their External Relations division. It's a formal inquiry — not a demand, not a directive. An inquiry." She pauses. "They've been monitoring Belobog's energy output signatures for some time. The Stellaron event at Everwinter Hill generated readings they have apparently been trying to account for for several months. The recent disruption of the broadcast signal—" She pauses again. "The array. They detected it." "From outside the planet," Bronya says. "Their monitoring infrastructure is extensive," Pela says, which is the diplomatic version of yes, from outside the planet, and also somewhat further. Bronya looks at the letter. She does not pick it up again. "What do they want." "They want to send a representative. A preliminary assessment visit, they call it — to verify the stability of the Stellaron and evaluate Belobog's capacity for ongoing management." A pause. "The language is very careful. Very courteous." Another pause. "Courteous language from the IPC typically means they have already decided what they intend to do and are giving you the opportunity to agree before they do it without you." Bronya looks at her. "How long have you known about the IPC's monitoring." "I suspected it after the Everwinter Hill readings. I confirmed it after the array." Pela meets her gaze steadily. "I should have said something sooner. I was waiting until I had more complete information." A silence. "I understand," Bronya says, which is not forgiveness and is not condemnation — it is simply the accurate response of someone who has been doing the same thing for two months and knows exactly what waiting for more complete information costs and why people do it anyway. "When do they want to send the representative." "The letter requests a response within two weeks. The visit itself—" Pela looks at the letter. "They suggest one month from the date of our response." "Six weeks," Bronya says. "Approximately." Another silence. Bronya stands, which is the thing she does when she is thinking at a scale that the chair cannot contain — she moves to the window, the south-facing window of the administrative wing, and looks at the city below it. It is a clear day for Belobog — the blizzard has pulled back to a light snowfall, and the inhabited districts are visible from here, the amber warmth of them going about their ordinary business in the ordinary morning. Pela waits. "Tell Gepard," Bronya says, to the window. "I have a message queued—" "Tell him in person." She turns. "I want him in this room today, not by message." She looks at Pela. "Please." Pela nods, and goes. —— He reads the letter standing up, which is how he reads things that require immediate and total attention. He reads it once, and then he reads the specific paragraph about the assessment visit a second time, and then he looks at Pela over the paper. "Six weeks," he says. "Approximately." "What do they actually want." "To assess Belobog's capacity for ongoing Stellaron management," Pela says, and the flatness in her voice is the specific flatness of someone quoting language they do not trust. "Which means they want to know whether to treat Belobog as a sovereign entity capable of managing its own existential risk, or as a situation that requires external — management." He holds the letter. He thinks about the IPC. He knows what most people in Belobog know, which is not very much — a name, a reach, a vague institutional weight that exists mostly in the context of trade agreements and occasional mentions in old correspondence. He knows, because he has spent eight years protecting a city that has been functionally isolated from the rest of the universe, that the scope of what he does not know about the IPC is considerably larger than what he does. He thinks about Bronya. He thinks about what she has built in three months — the council, the protocols, the fractured resonance solution, the wall that held, the Stellaron sitting at 1.3 and flat. He thinks about her at the window this morning, the loose hair, the open hands, the city she has been holding up with the same arms that trembled in the array room. He thinks about an external organization deciding whether those efforts constitute sufficient management. "I'll be at the office in twenty minutes," he says. —— She is at the window when he arrives, which is where she was when he arrived this morning, except this time she is in full armor and her hair is up and her hands are behind her back, which is the difference between the woman at the window in the morning and the Guardian at the window in the afternoon, and he has learned to see both. He closes the door. He does not always close the door, but today he closes it. She turns. "You've read it," she says. "Yes." "Pela gave you her assessment." "Yes." He crosses to the desk and sets the letter down. "What's your read." She looks at the letter. "The IPC does not send courtesy visits. They send evaluations. The language is careful because they want compliance before confrontation — they would rather we welcome them than have to require it." She pauses. "Which means they have leverage they are not yet showing." Another pause. "The question is what they intend to do with it." "Absorb us," he says flatly. She looks at him. "That's what corporations do with assets they consider undertended," he says. "I don't know the IPC's specific mechanisms. But the logic is the same." He meets her gaze. "They've been monitoring the planet for months. They detected the broadcast signal escalation. They detected the disruption. They know we have a Stellaron and they know we've been managing a crisis with it and they want to know whether we're going to keep being capable of that or whether they need to step in." He pauses. "And stepping in has a different meaning when you're the IPC." She is quiet for a moment. "Yes," she says. "That's my read too." She moves from the window to the desk and stands beside it, not behind it — beside it, which puts her a meter from him instead of two, which is a thing she has been doing more frequently since the stairwell, and which he has been noticing with the same discipline he applies to everything he is noticing and not examining. "Six weeks," she says. "What can we do in six weeks." "Document everything," he says immediately, because he has been thinking about this since Pela's office. "The full record of the past three months. The Fragmentum escalation, the behavioral clustering, the feedback loop mechanism, the fractured resonance solution, the array construction. Everything. With Pela's analysis, Serval's calibration data, the structural team's certifications." He pauses. "We show them a body of work that makes the case that Belobog managed this, that Belobog has the people and the methodology and the infrastructure to continue managing it, that external intervention is not only unnecessary but would be actively disruptive to a system that is currently working." She is looking at him the way she looks at him when he is giving her the thing she needed before she knew she needed it. "That's going to take significant time to compile." "Pela can organize it. I'll provide the military records. Serval can provide the technical documentation before she leaves Thursday." He pauses. "You write the summary. The overall account — what happened, how it was addressed, what the current status is." He looks at her. "In your voice. Not a bureaucratic summary. Your voice." She holds his gaze. "Why." "Because whoever the IPC sends is going to be looking for reasons to find this insufficient. A bureaucratic document gives them surfaces to critique. Your account of it—" He pauses, choosing the word. "Doesn't." She is quiet for a moment. Something in her expression moves — the thing it does when he says something she did not expect to agree with as completely as she does. "You have a lot of faith in my voice," she says. "I've been listening to it for five years," he says. "It's not faith." She looks at him. He looks at her. The letter is on the desk between them and the closed door is behind him and the city is below the south window and the stairwell is three weeks ago and also entirely present, the way certain mornings are. She says, very quietly: "Gepard." "Yes." "This morning." She is looking at him with the specific directness she has been deploying since the stairwell — the one that does not deflect, that stays on the thing it is looking at. "In the stairwell." She pauses. "I've been thinking about it." "So have I," he says, which is the understatement of the month and they both know it. "I don't want to—" She stops. Starts again. "I don't want it to be a thing that happened and is now filed somewhere. I'm—" She looks at the desk briefly, and then back at him. "I'm not good at this," she says, which is the most disarmed thing she has said to him that isn't at 2 AM. "I'm not good at — saying the thing directly. In the daylight. Without the dark or the 2 AM to make it easier." "You're doing it right now," he says. "I know. It's very difficult." The corner of her mouth. The genuine one. "You're not helping." "I'm not trying to make it easier," he says. "I want to hear what you're actually saying." She looks at him for a moment. Then: "I don't want to step back from it." A pause. "From — this. Whatever this is." She gestures, slightly, between them, which is the most uncoordinated gesture he has ever seen her make and which lands in his chest like a fist. "I know the timing is—" "The timing is what it is," he says. "The IPC—" "Is six weeks away." He looks at her. "That's six weeks from now. This is now." She holds his gaze for a long moment. The afternoon light from the south window has moved across the room since he arrived, and it sits now on the edge of the desk between them, and outside the city does its ordinary beautiful thing of being alive and continuing. She takes a step. Not the same step as the stairwell — not the slow, measured, deliberate one. A smaller step, the natural closing of a distance that has been getting smaller anyway, until she is standing close enough that he is aware of her armor's warmth and the specific scent of cold air that lives in geomarrow-charged metal and that he associates, at a level below conscious thought, with her specifically. She tilts her head back to look at him. He looks down at her. The geometry of it — the same as the stairwell, both of them choosing it. She reaches up and rests her hand against his chest, over the armor, and looks at him. It is not the jaw. It is not the precise, deliberate placement of the stairwell. It is something quieter than that — a hand over armor, over the geomarrow charge and the breastplate and everything beneath it, and she is looking up at him with no composure requirement and no management and nothing held back. "I noticed you when I was seventeen," she says. "I have been noticing you every day since. And I have spent a very long time deciding whether to do anything about it." She pauses. "I've decided." He covers her hand with his, the same way he did in the stairwell. His thumb moves across her knuckles — deliberate this time, not involuntary — and she watches his hand on hers and then looks back up at him. "Bronya," he says, and she looks at him, and he says: "I know." She blinks. "That's all you're going to say." "I've been waiting for you to decide for five years," he says. "I know." Something happens on her face that is not the almost-smile and not the genuine one but is something she does not usually show — something that is simply, entirely, happy. Brief. Real. Gone before it fully surfaces but present enough to see. "You're very annoying," she says, which is not what she means, and they both know it. "I know," he says, which is not what he means either, and they both know that too. He leans down — slowly, with the same deliberateness as everything between them, nothing rushed, nothing taken — and presses his forehead to hers. She stills. He stills. They stand like that, foreheads together, her hand on his chest and his hand over hers, the armor warm between them and the afternoon light doing what it does and the letter on the desk and the IPC six weeks away and the world outside exactly as large and complicated as it has always been. For now, for this moment, it is also exactly this small. "The documentation," she says, after a while. "Yes," he says. "We should start today." "We should." "Pela is going to need the military records by Thursday if Serval's technical files are going where I think they're going." "I'll have them ready tonight." She pulls back, slightly — not away, just back, to a distance where they can see each other properly. She looks at him. Her hand is still on his chest. His is still over hers. "Okay," she says. "Okay," he says. She steps back. He lets her. She turns to the desk and picks up the IPC letter, and he watches the Guardian's posture settle back into place — the hands behind her back, the precise attention — except that it settles differently now, the same way it settled differently after the array. A little less sealed. A little more chosen. "Sit down," she says, in the Commander's voice, except it has a warmth underneath it now that he can hear from two meters away and that she is not bothering to suppress. "We have six weeks and a significant amount of work to do." He sits down. He picks up the patrol folder. He is, with a certainty that has nothing to do with Fragmentum or walls or broadcast frequencies, going to be completely useless at reading this patrol folder for at least twenty minutes. He reads it anyway. —— That evening, Serval finds him in the corridor outside the construction chamber doing a final charge check on his armor, and she says: "Okay, I'm saying it now." He looks at her. "I said I'd say it at a worse time," she says. "But actually I think now is probably fine because I also want to say congratulations and it's hard to lead with congratulations if I do the other thing first." She crosses her arms. "So. Congratulations." He looks at her for a moment. "How did you know," he says. "You have been smiling at your patrol folder all evening," she says. "Also I ran into Seele and she said Bronya came to the afternoon briefing with her hair down and Gepard was smiling and she said she was 'just noting that.'" Serval pauses. "Also I have known you for your entire life." He looks at the wall. "The thing I was going to say," she says, and her voice changes — drops from the lightness to something underneath it, something that is genuine Serval in the register she reserves for things that matter. "Is that she is — she has been through things. You know what she's been through and you know it better than most, and she is doing well now, and she is stronger than anyone gives her credit for, and she is going to be fine." She pauses. "But she is also — she holds things very tightly because she learned to. And what she needs is someone who will be there when she is ready to put things down, not someone who will try to make her put them down before she's ready." She looks at him. "You know how to do that. You've been doing it for years." A pause. "Don't stop." He looks at his sister. "Serval," he says. "I know," she says. "Don't make it a thing." She unfolds her arms and steps forward and hugs him, which she has done his entire life and which he permits, specifically, because it is her. He puts one arm around her shoulders. She squeezes once and pulls back. "The technical files will be organized and ready for Pela by tomorrow morning," she says, returning to normal register as if she was never in the other one. "The calibration data, the frequency documentation, the array specifications — all of it. In a format that will make the IPC's representative feel like they are reviewing the work of a research institution, not a city that has been improvising." She pauses. "Because it was good work. We did good work here." "We did," he says. "And Gepard." She is already moving toward the stairwell. "The patrol folder. You should probably actually read it." He looks at her. She grins — unguarded, Serval-genuine — and goes. He looks at the patrol folder. He reads it, this time, all the way through. —— Three days later, Serval leaves on the Thursday morning train, and the palace is slightly quieter without her, in the way that all spaces are slightly quieter in the aftermath of someone with significant energy. Gepard sees her off at the platform, which is the kind of thing brothers do and which he would feel embarrassed about if Serval did not also take approximately six minutes to check that he is warm enough and has eaten and has been sleeping adequately, so they are square on the embarrassment front. "I'll come back," she says. "I know." "When things are—" She looks at him. "Well, you'll message me." "When things are interesting," he says, which is the code they have always used, since they were children in the Landau house with the cold corridors and the weight of expectations and the private vocabulary they developed to avoid saying the serious things directly. "When things are interesting," she agrees. She squeezes his arm once. She gets on the train. He watches it go. He stands on the platform in the early morning cold with the Belobog platform noise around him — the sounds of ordinary transit, ordinary movement, people going from one place to another in the ordinary way — and he thinks about the IPC's letter on Bronya's desk and the six weeks ahead and the documentation project that Pela has already organized into a structure that would make a research institution envious. He thinks about the stairwell and the afternoon office and the foreheads together and the armor warm between them and okay said twice, quietly, in the afternoon light. He thinks about the weight of everything that is coming. He thinks about the specific quality of the morning — the light arriving top-down, the city below the platform still half-dark, the spires of the palace catching the first of the dawn. He thinks: worth protecting. He has always thought this. He will keep thinking it. He goes back to work. —— In the Underworld, four days after the nine-beta junction conversation, Luka is in Svarog's workshop when Sampo arrives. Sampo looks at him. He looks at Sampo. Something in Sampo's expression is different from usual — less performance, more the stripped version, the one that showed up at Natasha's ward and in the tunnel report forward and in the council chamber where he brought his notes on a playing card. "The IPC sent a letter," Sampo says. Luka sets down the component he was working on. "What's the IPC." Sampo looks at him for a moment with the expression of someone deciding how much to say and settling on: enough. "An organization," he says. "Very large. Very far-reaching. They monitor Stellarons across — various locations. They've been watching Belobog." He pauses. "They want to send someone. To evaluate." "Evaluate what." "Whether Belobog is managing its own situation adequately." He sits down in the chair — Svarog's chair, the one for human visitors — and leans back with the ease of someone who has been in this workshop many times. "Whether they need to intervene." Luka looks at him. "And if they decide we're not managing adequately." "Then they manage it for us." A pause. "Which is not the same as us managing it." The workshop is quiet for a moment. Svarog, at the back, works without comment, which is his version of listening. "Is Bronya—" Luka starts. "She knows," Sampo says. "Gepard too. Pela's already building the documentation." He looks at Luka. "Which is why I'm here." He leans forward. "The tunnel logs. Your walkthrough records — all eighteen days of them. The sensor readings, the pulse documentation, the crystallization progression, the reorientation event." He meets Luka's eyes. "That's evidence. That's a record of what was happening down here, documented by someone who was in the tunnels while it was happening. The IPC's representative is going to want the full picture — surface and underground. Your logs are the underground half." Luka looks at him. "You want my logs for the documentation," he says. "I want your logs for the documentation." A pause. "They're already organized," Luka says. "I've been keeping them clean since the first walkthrough." He looks at his hands. "I didn't know why at the time. I just — kept them clean." Something in Sampo's face does the thing it does sometimes — the brief, genuine version, visible for a second before it goes back behind the performance. "Good," he says. "That's good, kid." "I'm twenty-one." "I know." Sampo stands. "Send them to Pela. Tonight if you can." He moves toward the door and does the thing, and Luka is beginning to understand that everyone in this city does the thing, which is stop in the doorway. "Luka," Sampo says. "Yeah." "You're going to meet the IPC representative." He says it like a fact, not a prediction. "When they come. Whoever they send. You're going to be in the room." He looks at Luka with the evaluative eyes — the real ones, not the performance. "Because you know those tunnels and you documented them and that's going to matter." A pause. "Just so you know what's coming." Luka looks at him. "Okay," he says. Sampo nods. He goes. Luka sits in the workshop with the component in his hand and the tunnel logs already organized neatly on his phone, and he thinks about the surface and the palace and the IPC and the six weeks ahead, and he thinks about corridor nine-beta and the way the crystallization is dulling and the junction where he keeps staying too long. "Svarog," he says. "Yes," Svarog says, from the back. "What do you know about the IPC." A pause. The sound of deliberate, unhurried work. "Enough," Svarog says, "to know that what Belobog presents to them in six weeks will matter for a very long time." Another pause. "And enough to know that the people preparing that presentation are capable of making it sufficient." A pause. "If they trust each other." Luka thinks about this. "Do they," he says. "Trust each other." Svarog is quiet for a moment. His optical sensors, visible from where Luka sits, carry the expression they carry when he is about to say something true and simple that will nonetheless take a while to land. "More than they did three months ago," he says. "Which is the direction that matters." Luka nods. He sends the tunnel logs to Pela. —— Notes: The IPC letter arrived and Bronya set it down carefully and I felt that. 'I've been waiting for you to decide for five years. I know.' HE SAID I KNOW. She said 'you're very annoying' because she did not have another available response and he said 'I know' again because he IS like that and they were both absolutely useless for twenty minutes afterward and I will not be taking criticism. Foreheads together. Her hand on his chest over the armor. Both of them still. Okay said twice. That's where they are. That's what they have. The IPC can wait twenty more minutes. Serval in the corridor. 'Congratulations.' The real thing she said after: don't stop doing what you've been doing. He put his arm around her shoulders. They are siblings and they are each other's people and I needed to say that clearly. 'When things are interesting.' The private code. He watches the train go and thinks about worth protecting and goes back to work. He's been thinking this since he was seventeen on the outer wall. He will keep thinking it. Luka kept his logs clean without knowing why. 'I didn't know why at the time. I just kept them clean.' He knew. Something in him knew. Svarog: 'More than they did three months ago. Which is the direction that matters.' That's the whole thing. That's all of it. Six weeks until the IPC representative arrives. Chapter 12 is the preparation, and the first cracks in what the preparation reveals. Comment and tell me you're ready. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 12: "What We're Building" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text The documentation project takes over the palace's second floor. This is not a formal designation — no one announces that the second floor has been repurposed, no memo circulates, no administrative order is filed. It simply happens, the way things happen when a group of people with a shared and urgent purpose start moving toward the same space at the same hours for the same reasons, until the space reorganizes itself around them by accumulated habit. Pela's office becomes the nerve center. By the end of the first week, its walls are covered in timeline charts — the Fragmentum escalation plotted in amber, the bloom events marked with dates and density readings, the convergence behavior analysis laid over the patrol data in a layered visual argument that is, in Gepard's assessment, the most convincing thing he has ever seen produced in a civilian office. Pela has been sleeping four hours a night. He brings her coffee at 0700. She accepts it without looking up from her work. This has become their morning routine, and neither of them comments on it. Serval's technical files arrived organized and immaculate, as promised. The calibration data, the frequency documentation, the array construction specifications, the pre-Separation research citations with Serval's annotations — all of it formatted with the rigor of a published research document, because Serval, for all her chaos in person, has never once in her life produced sloppy written work. Pela incorporated it within a day. Gepard sent Serval a message that said: the files are excellent. Serval replied: I know, and also you should eat something. He had, in fact, already eaten something, and the fact that he noticed this with a small specific satisfaction that had nothing to do with food is something he is filing and not examining. Bronya's summary is the last piece. She has been writing it for two weeks, which is longer than anything else in the documentation took, which surprises no one who knows her — she writes the way she does everything, with total precision, and total precision at this scale takes time. He has seen three drafts. The first was technically accurate and completely bloodless, the language of an official report with all the humanity pressed out of it. The second was better — warmer, more present — but it still had the specific quality of someone writing about events at one remove, describing rather than bearing witness. He told her this after the second draft. She had looked at him across the desk for a long moment with the expression she uses when she is deciding whether to argue, and then she had said: "You're right," and taken it back. The third draft, which she gives him on a Thursday morning two and a half weeks into the documentation project, is different. He reads it in the chair from the corner, which is at the desk, which is where he sits now when they work together, the three weeks since the stairwell having compressed the professional distance between them into something that is still professional — still the desk, still the titles in the presence of others, still the careful maintenance of what is appropriate in the rooms they share with other people — but which in private has a different quality entirely. A quality that is warm and increasingly difficult to be rigorous about when she sits across from him and the morning light comes through the north-facing windows and she has the hair down. He reads the summary. He reads it twice. He sets it on the desk and looks at her. "This is what you should have written the first time," he says. She looks at him. Something complicated moves through her expression — not quite hurt, not quite recognition. "I didn't know I could write it this way the first time." "You could have," he says. "You were choosing not to." A pause. "I know." She looks at the draft. "It felt like—" She stops. Starts again. "Like giving them something they could use against us. If the language was too personal, too present — if it read like someone who cares rather than someone who manages—" "People who care are harder to dismiss than people who manage," he says. "The IPC is going to have managers. We're not competing on that ground." He looks at the summary. "This is our ground. A twenty-two-year-old woman who inherited a broken city and a Stellaron and a feedback loop and held all of it for three months while building the solution in the same building as the problem." He looks back at her. "That's not a weakness to write around. That's the entire argument." She is quiet for a long moment. "You say things like that," she says, "and I genuinely don't know what to do with you." "You don't have to do anything with me," he says. "I'm just reading what you wrote." "You're doing considerably more than reading what I wrote." He looks at her. She looks at him. The draft is between them on the desk and the morning light is doing what it does and they are, he is aware, approximately thirty seconds from a conversation that has nothing to do with the IPC documentation and everything to do with the three weeks since the stairwell and the forehead against forehead and the okay said twice and the way she has been sitting across from him every morning in the chair that has become the chair from the corner at the desk that is now simply the desk. He picks up the patrol report. She makes a sound that is nearly a laugh — not quite, suppressed before it surfaces fully, but present enough to hear, and it lands in him somewhere specific and warm. "The summary is good," he says, to the patrol report. "Thank you," she says, to her briefing folder. They work. —— Three weeks before the IPC visit, Pela calls a full documentation review. Everyone is there: Gepard, Seele, Sampo, and — because Pela has been corresponding with him directly since the tunnel log submission and has apparently decided that his organizational instincts merit inclusion — Luka, who arrives at the palace via the service entrance with a folder of printed log summaries and the expression of someone who is trying to look like he knows where he is going, which he mostly does because Sampo walked him through the route the night before. He does not sit on the kitchen counter this time. He takes the chair at the end of the council table and opens his folder and waits. Seele is across the table, two seats down, and she is looking at the wall chart, not at him, but he is aware of her in the room with the same specific awareness he has had since nine-beta, the awareness that has no explanation and does not respond to reason. He does not think about it more than once before Pela starts talking. Pela presents the documentation structure. It is, in the collective opinion of everyone at the table, extraordinary — a complete and coherent record of everything that happened in Belobog over the past three months, organized by theme and cross-referenced by date, with Serval's technical files providing the scientific foundation, Gepard's military records providing the operational record, Luka's tunnel logs providing the underground dimension, and Bronya's summary providing the frame that holds all of it together. "The IPC's representative will want to verify independently," Pela says. "They will bring their own analytical framework. They will want to see the Stellaron directly—" She pauses, and the pause has the specific texture of information being delivered that is not comfortable to deliver. "Which means Bronya will need to take them to the chamber." The room is quiet for a moment. "I know," Bronya says. "And they may want access to the array." "They can have access to the array." A pause. "The array is a solution we built. I want them to see it." She looks at Pela. "What they cannot have is the Stellaron's chamber unsupervised. That's non-negotiable." Pela nods. "I'll include that condition in the response letter." "And the tunnel documentation," Bronya says, looking at Luka. "You'll present that directly." Luka looks at her. He has been in her presence twice — the council meeting and the kitchen, the latter of which is not something he intends to bring up — and both times she has addressed him with the same quality of attention she gives everyone, which is complete and without condescension. He respects this. "Yes," he says. "Good." She looks around the table. "I want this presentation to communicate one thing clearly above everything else: Belobog handles its own problems." She meets every gaze around the table, briefly, in sequence — Pela, Gepard, Seele, Sampo, Luka. "We are not a situation. We are not an asset that requires management. We are a city with a Stellaron and the people and methodology to continue managing it." A pause. "The IPC's representative will be looking for a reason to classify us differently. We are going to make that difficult for them." The room is quiet for a moment. Then Sampo says, with the playing card notes folded in front of him: "Who is it, do we know." Pela hesitates. A very small hesitation, the kind that on Pela means she has information she has been deciding how to present. "The letter did not specify a representative. I made an inquiry through diplomatic channels." She pauses. "The name provided was Aventurine." A beat of silence. "Just Aventurine," Sampo says. "Just Aventurine," Pela confirms. Sampo's expression does something complicated. He looks at the table. He looks at Pela. "Was there a second name with the response." "A Trailblazer contact indicated that Aventurine typically travels with a senior IPC assessor. The name they provided was Topaz." Gepard looks at Bronya. She is already looking at him. Topaz. The name is not unknown to either of them — it is the name attached to Belobog's last IPC contact, the courtesy visit that preceded the letter by several months and which had been, in the assessment of the people who received it, thorough, professional, and not entirely what it appeared to be. Topaz moves like someone who already knows the answer to every question she asks. She had been courteous. She had been precise. She had taken excellent notes. "Topaz was here before," Seele says, and her voice has the careful flatness of someone who met a person once and formed an opinion they are being cautious about sharing. "She's — thorough." "She's IPC," Sampo says, which contains a complete assessment in two words. "So is Aventurine," Pela says, "and the Trailblazer contact described him as—" She looks at her notes. "'Unpredictable' and 'difficult to prepare for,' and I quote, 'in ways that are not always bad but frequently inconvenient.'" The room processes this. "Great," Seele says. "We prepare for Topaz," Bronya says, which is the decision-making version of her voice, clean and final. "We prepare the documentation, the chamber access, the Stellaron data, the full technical record — everything she will want to verify, we give her before she asks for it." She pauses. "Aventurine is a different preparation." "What kind of preparation," Gepard says. "The kind where we don't let him find the gaps first." She looks at him. "Which means I want to know what our gaps are before he does." Gepard looks at her. "What do you think our gaps are." She is quiet for a moment, and it is the honest kind of quiet, the kind where she is not managing what she says but choosing it. "The Stellaron signal is at 1.3 and has been flat for three weeks. The feedback loop is fractured. The wall is certified." She pauses. "But the residual Fragmentum saturation in the Sector C snowfield is still clearing — it will be months before it's fully inert. The tunnel crystallization is dulling but it is not gone." She looks at the table. "And the array is a solution to a symptom. The Stellaron is still there. The broadcast capacity is still there. The disruption is not a cure. It is a management." She looks up. "If Aventurine is good at what he apparently does, he is going to find that within the first twenty minutes." The room is quiet. "So we don't hide it," Luka says. Everyone looks at him. He does not appear to find this uncomfortable. "If the gap is obvious — if he's going to find it anyway — hiding it makes it look like a vulnerability we're ashamed of. Not hiding it makes it look like a variable we're managing." He shrugs, slightly. "You said the documentation should show that Belobog handles its own problems. So show that this is a problem you're handling. Not solved. Handling." The room is quiet for a moment of a different kind. Bronya looks at him. "Yes," she says. "Exactly." Luka nods. He does not make anything of the agreement. Across the table, Seele is looking at him with an expression that is not the hostile wariness — it is something else, something that involves a slight furrow between her brows, the expression of someone recalibrating a prior assessment. He does not look directly at it. He looks at his folder. "The gap is part of the presentation," Gepard says, taking the thread. "We document the current status honestly — the residual saturation, the tunnel crystallization, the ongoing monitoring requirements. We frame it as the active management phase following the acute crisis." He looks at Bronya. "The story isn't that the problem is solved. The story is that the problem is understood, and Belobog is the entity that understands it best." "Which is true," Pela says. "Which is true," he agrees. Bronya looks around the table. Her expression is the one he has been seeing more frequently — the less-sealed one, the one that has something of the stairwell in it, the one that is both the Guardian and her own face simultaneously and does not require choosing between them. "Three weeks," she says. "We have what we need. We make it airtight." —— Two weeks before the visit, Seele and Luka are in corridor nine-beta again. This time it is not accidental. Seele asked Sampo, in a way that was not quite asking, whether Luka would be doing a walkthrough on Wednesday. Sampo said yes, in a way that communicated he understood exactly what was happening and was going to be deeply professional about it. She arrived in nine-beta at the same time as Luka with a supply check that she had already completed the day before and did not need to redo. They both know this. Neither of them says anything about it. They walk the corridor together in the way they have been developing — not companionable exactly, not hostile, but a specific quality of parallel presence, like two people who have agreed to occupy the same space without requiring it to mean something it isn't ready to mean yet. Luka with his sensor, Seele with her supply list she is not reading. The tunnel is quiet. The crystallization dulls steadily at every junction. "The IPC visit," Seele says, at junction four. "Yeah." "Sampo said you're presenting the tunnel logs." "That's what Bronya said." She is quiet for a moment. They walk. "You've never done anything like that," she says. "Presented to — people like that." "No." He checks the sensor reading at junction five. Yellow. Notes it. "Have you." "Yes." A pause. "Once. In the Underworld, before everything. There was a resource allocation committee — Surface Authority oversight. I had to present a case for increasing the medical district's supply allotment." She pauses. "I was sixteen." He looks at her sideways. "Did it work." "No." A pause. "But I made them uncomfortable enough that they approved a partial increase." The corner of her mouth moves — not a smile exactly, but in the direction of one, the expression of someone remembering something with a satisfaction that has outlasted the frustration that accompanied it. "Pela said my approach was 'aggressive.'" "That tracks," he says. She looks at him. He looks at the tunnel. After a moment she says: "You'll be fine. For the presentation." A pause, the effortful kind. "Your logs are thorough. You documented things no one else was positioned to document." He looks at her. She is looking at the junction marker on the wall, not at him, and she is holding the supply list with both hands in a way that is not quite casual. "Thank you," he says. She nods. She does not say don't make it a thing, which means something. They reach the end of the corridor and turn back, and on the return route the quality of the silence between them has shifted — infinitesimally, the way the tunnel crystallization dulls by increments too small to see on any single visit but present across the accumulated record. Not resolved. Not named. Just slightly less in the way of whatever is underneath it. At the main junction, where nine-beta branches away from seven-alpha, Luka stops. He has stopped at this junction on every walkthrough. He does not know exactly why, has never been able to locate the reason in anything observable, and has stopped trying to. He stops and he looks at the branching point and he feels the thing he always feels here — the shape before the translation, the half-word in the forgotten language. Seele stops beside him. She stops because he stopped, and then she looks at the junction, and her expression changes. He watches her look at it. He watches her face do the thing he has seen it do twice before — the recalibration, the assessment being updated — except this time it is not about him, it is about the junction, and her expression is not evaluating. It is something else. Something that looks, briefly and very privately, like recognition. "Seele," he says. She blinks. Comes back. Looks at him. "Sorry," she says. "I just—" She looks at the junction again. "I've been here before." She says it like she is testing whether it is true. "Not in these tunnels. I've been in these tunnels twice. But this specific—" She stops. Her brow furrows. "The angle. The way the branch goes left." He looks at the branch. He looks at the angle. "Like the left corridor in the Underworld market district," he says. "Where it cuts back toward the machine blocks." She stares at him. He stares at what he just said, which arrived without him deciding to say it, the way the route home arrives before the directions do. "How do you know the market district," she says, very quietly. "I don't," he says. "I've never been to the market district." He looks at the junction. "I don't know why I said that." She is looking at him with an expression he has never seen from her before — not the wariness, not the evaluation, not the almost-warmth from the supply check compliment. Something rawer than all of those. Something that is very still and very uncertain and very young. "I grew up near that corridor," she says. "In the market district. When I was—" She stops. "When I was small." He looks at her. She looks at him. The tunnel is quiet. The junction branches left and right. The sensor in his hand reads yellow and the crystallization dulls on the walls around them and something in the space between them is doing the thing it does at this junction, the specific pull of the half-recognition, except this time it has more content than it usually does — a location, a corridor, a branch going left, a detail he should not have and does. "Luka," she says, and his name in her voice has never sounded like that before — not hostile, not careful, just direct, the way you say the name of someone you know. "I don't know," he says, because she is going to ask him how he knows and he genuinely does not know, and he has run out of ways to pretend the half-recognition is nothing. "I don't know how I know that." She looks at him for a long moment. Her hands are at her sides, the supply list forgotten, and she looks at him with those purple eyes in the tunnel light and does not look away. "Me neither," she says. "There's something—" She stops. "There's something I don't remember. That I should." "Yes," he says, because it is true, because he has felt this for weeks and this is the first time it has had words. She nods. Very slowly. The nod of someone acknowledging a fact they are not ready to do anything about but are no longer willing to pretend isn't there. "Okay," she says. She picks up the supply list. She turns toward the main corridor. She walks three steps and stops — the one-more-thing stop, the one everyone in this city does — and she says, without turning around: "When you have a walkthrough. Send me the time." He looks at her back. "Okay," he says. She goes. He stands at the junction for a long time. He looks at the branch going left. He thinks about a corridor in a market district he has never been to and a specific angle of branch and the look on her face when he said it. He thinks about Svarog's words: the direction that matters. He sends Pela his Wednesday walkthrough notes. He goes home through the outer district, by a route he does not need to think about, past junctions he knows without having learned them, and he does not think about it more than five times, which is the most times yet, and which feels, somehow, like progress. —— One week before the visit, Gepard finds Bronya in the construction chamber. Not working — not reviewing the array, not checking the frequency readings that Pela runs weekly to confirm the signal is holding flat. Just standing in the room, in the low lamplight, looking at the array. The same way she looked at the frequency guide during the twenty-percent test. The same way she looked at it the morning of the full output run. He stops in the doorway. He does not knock this time. He watches her stand in the lamplight with the array humming softly at its maintenance charge and her hands behind her back and her hair pinned up and every line of her carrying the specific posture of someone thinking something they have not yet decided to say. "You're worried about something," he says. She does not startle. She knew he was there — she always knows when he is there, which is a fact he filed approximately three months ago and has been quietly carrying since. "The signal is flat," she says. "Yes." "1.3 for three weeks." "Yes." "Which means the array is working." She is still looking at the array. "Which means the presentation to the IPC is strong." A pause. "Which means everything is, by measurable standards, going well." "Yes," he says, for the fourth time. She is quiet. He crosses the room and stands beside her, not behind her — beside her, at the same angle to the array, close enough that the warmth of her armor is a fact and the specific scent of her geomarrow charge is present, and he looks at the array too. They stand like this for a moment in the low lamplight and the quiet hum. "The Stellaron is still there," she says. "Yes." "The broadcast capacity is still there. The disruption is management, not cure." She pauses. "I said this in the review meeting. I believe it. It is the honest framing." Another pause. "But believing something and being comfortable with it are different things." "Yes," he says. She turns her head and looks at him. He looks back. They are close enough in the lamplight that he can see the specific grey of her eyes clearly, the same grey that was close in the stairwell and in the office and in the construction chamber on the morning of the full output run, and which he has been looking at with decreasing management for five years and which currently requires no management at all. "I'm afraid the signal is going to climb again," she says. "That the disruption won't hold. That in six months, or a year, or five years, the feedback loop will re-establish and we'll be back at the beginning." She looks at the array. "And this time there won't be eleven days. There won't be a window." He looks at the array. He thinks about what she is describing — the honest fear, the one she does not say in briefings or put in the summary or allow to surface in council meetings. The fear that lives under the management, that has been living there since Everwinter Hill, that the Stellaron is not a solved problem and the quiet is not a guarantee. "We don't know that it will," he says. "We don't know that it won't." "No," he says. "We don't." He looks at her. "But we know how to build the solution. We know the frequency. We know the mechanism. We have the array. If the signal climbs again—" "If the signal climbs again we run the array again," she says. "I know that." She looks at him. "That's not—" She stops. "That's not the fear. The fear is—" She stops again. Her jaw works slightly, the small movement she makes when she is saying something she has not said out loud before. "The fear is that I am going to be holding this for the rest of my life." She says it very quietly. "That this is what being Guardian means. That the Stellaron is in the foundation of the city I am responsible for and it will be there when I am twenty-five and thirty-five and fifty and the signal may or may not climb and I will be listening for it every day." A pause. "And I don't know if I can—" She stops. She looks at the array. "I don't know what that looks like." He is quiet for a moment. He turns toward her, fully, and she turns toward him, and they are standing in the lamplight of the construction chamber with the array between them and the Stellaron twenty-three meters below and the city above them and everything it contains. He reaches up and takes her face in both hands, the same way she took his jaw in the stairwell — slowly, with intention, giving her the full time to see it coming and decide — and he tilts her face up toward him and he looks at her. She looks back. "You've been holding it for three months already," he says. "And you're not holding it alone." He looks at her. "You will not hold it alone. Not when you're twenty-five. Not when you're thirty-five." He holds her gaze. "That's not a promise I'm making because it's what you need to hear. It's a fact I'm giving you because you always give me the accurate version and I owe you the same." She is very still. Her hands have come up to rest on his wrists, not pulling away, just resting there — the light contact of someone anchoring themselves to something real. "Gepard," she says. "Yes." "You say things like that," she says, for the second time, "and I genuinely don't know what to do with you." "I know," he says. "Stop saying I know." "I know," he says, and the corner of his mouth moves, and she makes the sound that is nearly a laugh, and this time it surfaces fully — brief, real, entirely unguarded — and it fills the construction chamber and the lamplight and the space between them, and he thinks: this. This is also worth protecting. This specifically. She leans her forehead against his chest. He brings one hand to the back of her head, carefully, and holds her there — not tightly, not urgently, just present, just his hand and her forehead and the armor warm between them and the array humming softly and the Stellaron twenty-three meters below doing what it does, which is exist, which it will keep doing, and which they will keep managing, together, for as long as it needs managing. "One week," she says, to his chest. "One week," he says. "And then Aventurine." "And then Aventurine," he agrees. She is quiet for a moment. Then: "Do you think he's going to be insufferable." He thinks about the Trailblazer contact's description. Unpredictable. Difficult to prepare for. "Yes," he says, with certainty. She makes the sound again. Less a laugh, more the thing underneath a laugh. "Good," she says, which is not what she means, and he does not ask what she means, because he already knows, which is that insufferable is a problem they can manage together, and managing problems together is, at this point, simply what they do. He holds her there in the lamplight for a while. Outside, Belobog does what it does. The city breathes. The wall stands. The tunnels are quiet. The Stellaron sits at 1.3 and holds. One week. —— Notes: 'I've been listening to it for five years. It's not faith.' He said it about her voice, about the summary, but it is also about everything else and they both know it and I need to sit down. Luka said 'the gap is part of the presentation' in a room full of people twice his age and experience and he was completely correct and Bronya said 'yes, exactly' and Seele looked at him with an expression that was not the hostile wariness and he did not look at it directly. He is learning. The junction. He said market district. He has never been to the market district. She grew up there. They both stood at the junction with the half-recognition fully between them for the first time and named it, not with an explanation, just with the shape of it. 'There's something I don't remember. That I should.' YES. THERE IS. And they are going to find it and I am going to be completely fine about it. She leaned her forehead against his chest. He put his hand on the back of her head. The fear she named — holding the Stellaron for the rest of her life, not knowing what that looks like. And he gave her the accurate version. Not when you're twenty-five. Not when you're thirty-five. Not the promise made because it's what she needed to hear. The fact. I am going to need a moment. She laughed. Actually laughed, in the construction chamber, in the lamplight, with the array humming. He thought: this is also worth protecting. I am completely fine. Aventurine and Topaz are one week away. Pela's documentation is airtight. The gap is part of the presentation. Belobog is ready. Probably. Chapter 13: they arrive. Comment and tell me you're breathing. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 13: "Two Visitors" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text They arrive on a Tuesday, which Gepard finds faintly offensive. Not for any rational reason. There is nothing wrong with Tuesday as a day. It is simply that Tuesday is the day he runs the full perimeter inspection of all three sectors, which takes four hours and requires his complete attention and cannot be rescheduled without cascading consequences for the patrol rotation, and having two IPC representatives arrive on the same morning as the full perimeter inspection creates a scheduling conflict that is nobody's fault and which he resents anyway. He delegates the final sector of the inspection to Chen — who is capable, thorough, and has been running observation post rotations without complaint for six weeks and has more than earned the responsibility — and arrives at the palace's formal entrance at 0940, which is eleven minutes after the IPC transport landed at Belobog's outer platform and nine minutes before the time Pela specified for the formal reception. He is not late. He takes a moment in the corridor outside the entrance hall to check his armor — charge at ninety-four percent, all seven pieces correctly seated, the formal dress configuration that takes slightly longer to assemble and which he has worn approximately twelve times in his career and which he knows, because Serval told him once with considerable feeling, that he wears well. He does not think about this. He pushes the door open and goes in. —— The first thing he notices about Aventurine is that he is already looking at something he shouldn't be. Not aggressively. Not obviously. The specific quality of attention that belongs to someone who takes in a room without appearing to, who is ostensibly reading the formal welcome documentation that Pela prepared and is actually cataloguing everything within visual range simultaneously, the way a person does when they have been trained — or have trained themselves — to never look directly at what they are actually looking at. Gepard recognizes this immediately, because it is a skill he possesses himself, and because it takes one to know one, and because the thing Aventurine is not-looking-at directly is the doorway Bronya came through, which means he arrived before her, which means he has already been reading the room for however long he has been standing in it without her as the reference point. He is tall, blond, well-dressed in the IPC's particular brand of wealth that manages to look both formal and entirely relaxed, and he has the expression of a man who finds everything slightly entertaining, which Gepard identifies as a professional posture rather than a personality trait within approximately thirty seconds of being in the room with him. Beside him is Topaz. Topaz, Gepard has met once before — briefly, peripherally, during her initial visit to Belobog several months ago. He had not been in the room for the formal meeting, had only passed her in the administrative wing corridor long enough to form the assessment: thorough, precise, moves like someone who already knows the answer to every question she's about to ask. He had been correct. She is looking at Pela's welcome documentation with the attention of someone who has already read the version she compiled from external sources and is now comparing it to the official one, annotating the discrepancies in real time in the back of her mind. She looks up when he enters. "Commander Gepard," she says, which means she knows his name and has placed his face, which means she prepared. "Administrator Topaz," he says, which means he knows her title, which means he also prepared. They understand each other immediately in the way that two prepared people always do. "This is my colleague," Topaz says, with a gesture toward Aventurine that manages to convey affection and professional distance simultaneously. "Aventurine," says Aventurine, extending a hand.
