His eyes are very light — a pale aquamarine, the specific color of something deep and cold — and they are, Gepard notes, currently aimed directly at him, which means he has shifted from not-looking-directly to looking-directly, which means he made a decision about Gepard fast. "I've heard good things." "Have you," Gepard says, which is not a question. "The documentation your administrator sent was thorough." A pause, the precise length of a pause that is doing something — not quite a smile, not quite not a smile, the expression of a man who has made a decision to be charming and is executing it with the facility of someone who has been doing it for a long time. "Unusually thorough." "Belobog has had an eventful few months," Gepard says. "So I gather." The light eyes move past him, briefly, to the door behind him. Not impatiently — just noting. "We're looking forward to meeting the Supreme Guardian." "She'll be here shortly," Gepard says, and does not say: she is already here, she has been here for twenty minutes, she is standing behind the door to the left of the entrance hall waiting for the moment that is precisely right to enter, because Bronya does not arrive to anything — she makes arrivals, and there is a difference. He does not say this because it is not information that needs to be volunteered, and because the fact that Aventurine is watching the door means he has already suspected it, and Gepard sees no reason to confirm the suspicion early. He moves to stand at the position Pela designated, and waits. —— She enters at 0951. He watches Aventurine's face when she enters, because Aventurine's face is the most informative thing in the room at this moment, and what he sees there is: recalibration. The practiced ease shifts — not disappears, just shifts, the way a person shifts when the room changes shape around them. The light eyes track her from the entrance to the center of the room with the specific attention of someone updating an assessment they had already formed, and whatever the assessment updates to is something he puts back behind the expression very quickly. Topaz's response is subtler and in some ways more alarming — a very slight forward lean, the body language of increased attention, and then a return to stillness that is more deliberate than her previous stillness had been. They both made a calculation when she walked in. Gepard files this. Bronya addresses them both with the precise, warm formality she has been deploying with increasing fluency since she became Guardian — the version of her voice that is both accessible and unarguable, that says: I am in charge of this room and I am also pleased you are in it, and both of those things are true simultaneously. She thanks them for coming. She expresses Belobog's interest in a productive assessment. She uses the word assessment, which is the IPC's word and not her own, and she uses it in a way that acknowledges their framing without conceding to it, and Gepard watches Topaz note this and file it. Aventurine says: "Supreme Guardian. Your city has had quite a year." "It has," Bronya says, with an equanimity that does not invite elaboration. "The Stellaron event at Everwinter Hill. The feedback loop. The fractured resonance solution." He pauses. "The array." He tilts his head slightly. "Remarkable, for a city that has been functionally isolated from the broader network for — how long?" "Long enough," Bronya says, "to know how to manage our own problems." Aventurine looks at her for a moment. The entertained-by-everything expression is present and real and also completely decorative, a surface over something that is doing considerably more work. "Yes," he says, pleasantly. "That comes through clearly in the documentation." "Good," Bronya says. "That was the intent." A beat. Then Aventurine smiles — genuinely, Gepard thinks, or at least with more genuine content than the previous smile — and says: "I think we're going to get along well, Supreme Guardian." "I think," Bronya says, "that we are going to have a productive assessment." Which is not the same thing, and everyone in the room knows it, and Aventurine's expression says that he appreciates the distinction. —— The first day is Topaz's. This is not formally scheduled — the itinerary shows a joint welcome session followed by a general documentation review — but it becomes apparent within the first two hours that Topaz and Aventurine operate on different rhythms, with different methodologies, and the documentation review is primarily Topaz's domain. Aventurine is present, seated at the table, and he asks approximately four questions in three hours, each of which is so precisely targeted that Gepard finds himself checking the documentation to see what the question is actually asking, which is never exactly what the words say. Topaz moves through Pela's documentation with the systematic focus of a machine doing work it was built for — not dismissive, not aggressive, simply thorough in a way that is beyond thorough, the way a scalpel is beyond a knife. She asks for clarifications. She cross-references internal sections. She notes three points where the documentation's language implies a certainty that the underlying data does not fully support, and she is correct about all three, and Pela — to her considerable credit — acknowledges all three without defensiveness and provides the underlying data on request. "The tunnel logs," Topaz says, at the two-hour mark, looking at Luka's documentation. Luka, who is at the table for exactly this, straightens almost imperceptibly. Seele, sitting two seats away, does not look at him. Her eyes are on Topaz. Her expression is the professional one — evaluative, careful, nothing given away. "The walkthrough records," Topaz says, "are remarkably detailed for what was essentially an informal surveillance operation." She looks at Luka. "You were documenting in real time." "Yes," Luka says. "Daily logs rather than weekly summaries." "Four times weekly. Sometimes more." "The documentation specifies twice weekly was the assigned frequency." "The tunnels were changing faster than twice weekly covered," Luka says, with the straightforwardness of someone who is not going to pretend the question is more complicated than it is. "So I went more." Topaz looks at him for a moment. Alongside her, Aventurine is looking at Luka with the not-looking-directly look, and Gepard watches him read Luka the same way he read the room when he arrived. "The reorientation event," Topaz says. "Corridor seven-alpha-four, day eleven. You documented it and forwarded it to Commander Gepard within—" She checks. "Forty minutes." "Yes." "What made you photograph the orientation shift." Luka looks at her. "It was different from the previous visit. Things that are different from the previous visit get documented." "That's the methodology." "That's the methodology," he agrees. Topaz makes a note. It is a specific kind of note — not the note of someone writing down information, but the note of someone recording an assessment of a person, which is different and which Luka clocks, Gepard can see it in the slight shift of his posture. Aventurine says, without looking up from the page he is reading: "How did you know the tunnels." Everyone looks at him. He appears to be reading a section of Pela's surface-level analysis and paying no attention to the tunnel logs conversation. Luka says: "What do you mean." "The walking logs." Aventurine turns a page. "On day three you stopped checking the map. The route notation stops referring to junction markers and starts referring to the corridors by the feel of them." He looks up, and the light eyes are doing the direct-looking now. "People navigate by feel when they know a place. You'd been in those tunnels twice." A pause. "How did you know them." The room is quiet. Luka looks at Aventurine. Gepard watches him decide what to say, and watches him land on the honest version, because — as Gepard has observed over the weeks of knowing him — Luka defaults to honest when he cannot locate a reason not to be. "I don't know," Luka says. Aventurine holds his gaze for a moment. The entertained expression is not present. Something else is — something more attentive, more considered. He nods, once, the nod of someone filing an answer, and looks back at the page. Topaz has been watching this exchange. She makes another note. This one, Gepard thinks, is longer than the previous one. —— In the evening, after the first day's session ends and the IPC visitors are settled in the palace's guest quarters, Gepard finds Seele in the administrative wing corridor — their corridor, the one with the south-facing windows — standing at the glass and looking at the city below. He has not seen her look like this before. She looks like she is listening to something. "Seele," he says. She turns. Her expression is the one she had at the nine-beta junction — not the hostile wariness, not the evaluation. The young, uncertain, very still one. "He asked how Luka knew the tunnels," she says. "Yes." "Aventurine." "Yes. He read it in the logs." He stops beside her at the window. "He's perceptive." "He's going to look into it." She says this with the certainty of someone who has been thinking about it since the moment the question was asked. "If he noticed that and thought it was worth asking—" "He noticed everything," Gepard says. "He's been noticing everything since he arrived. That's his job." She is quiet for a moment. "Do you think he knows something. About—" She stops. She looks at the city. "About why Luka knows those tunnels." He looks at her carefully. "Do you know why Luka knows those tunnels." "No," she says, and then, after a pause that has something in it: "Not completely." She presses her fingers briefly against the glass. "There are things I don't remember. I told you that." "You told Luka that. In the tunnel." She looks at him. Something in her expression registers the knowledge that he knows about the nine-beta conversation, and then lets it go — she has, he has observed, a very efficient relationship with information she cannot control. "Yes." A pause. "The junction where the branch goes left. The market district corridor." She looks at her hand against the glass. "There was something else today. When Aventurine asked the question." He waits. "A sound," she says. "In my head. When he asked how Luka knew the tunnels." She says this very carefully, the way you say something you are not sure you believe yet. "A specific sound. Like—" She stops. "Like something moving underground. Not threatening. Just — there. Present. And then it was gone." He is very still. "Seele," he says. "What did it sound like." She looks at him. Her eyes in the corridor light are very purple and very serious. "Like a second heartbeat," she says. "Somewhere below." —— That night, Luka cannot sleep. This is not unusual — he has not been sleeping well for three weeks, since the junction conversation and the branch going left and the market district he has never been to. But tonight is different. Tonight the not-sleeping has a specific content, not just the ambient not-settling of a body that knows something the mind hasn't caught yet. He is in his quarters in the Underworld, in the building adjacent to Svarog's workshop where he has lived for eight months, and it is past midnight, and he is looking at the ceiling. He is thinking about the question. How did you know the tunnels. He is thinking about the honest answer he gave, which is that he does not know. Which is true. Which has been true for six weeks and which he has been managing with the same approach he applies to everything he cannot immediately explain — document it, continue, wait for more information. He is thinking about the branch going left. He is thinking about a sound. He does not know where the sound comes from. It arrived an hour ago and has not left — not a sound he is hearing, not external, but internal, the way the Stellaron's frequency must be internal for Bronya, somewhere below conscious perception, present and rhythmic and not alarming, just there. A second heartbeat. He does not know why he thinks of it that way. He has not said this to anyone. He sits up. He sits on the edge of his bed in the dark of his Underworld quarters and he puts his hands on his knees and he breathes, and the sound is there, low and steady and familiar in the specific way that his body knows the route through those tunnels — not because he learned it, but because it is already in him, already known, the knowledge pre-existing the occasion for it. And with the sound comes something else. A fragment. Not a memory exactly — not enough context for a memory, not enough content. More like the edge of one, the way you see the edge of a photograph but not the image. Purple. The specific purple of a particular pair of eyes in a particular quality of light he cannot place, and a voice he does not recognize but which his body responds to with the specific quality it responds to the junction in nine-beta, with the recognition that arrives before the translation. The fragment lasts perhaps three seconds. Then it is gone. He sits on the edge of the bed in the dark for a long time afterward, his hands on his knees and the sound still present underneath everything, and he thinks about Svarog's words, and about the direction that matters, and about the branch going left in the market district he has never been to. He does not sleep for a long time. But when he does, it is deeper than it has been in weeks. As if something that has been waiting a very long time has found a crack in the surface and put the first thin root through, and the body, recognizing this, releases what it has been holding. —— The second day belongs to Aventurine. He requests, in the morning, to see the Stellaron's chamber. He asks for this before Topaz, before the documentation review continuation, before any of the other items on the formal itinerary, over the breakfast that the palace kitchen provides, in the tone of someone making a casual conversational request that is not casual at all. Bronya says: "Of course. This afternoon." He says: "This morning, if possible." She looks at him. He looks back with the light eyes and the entertained expression and the surface that is doing considerably less work than what is underneath it. She says: "This morning." They go at 0830. Gepard walks with them — with Bronya, Aventurine, and Topaz, through the palace's lower corridors and down the stairs to the construction chamber level, and then further down, through the passage that Bronya unlocked three weeks ago with the geomarrow key she keeps on her person. He has not been to the Stellaron's chamber before. He has been to the construction chamber dozens of times — has spent hours in it, has watched the array take shape in it, has stood in it with Bronya in the lamplight while she named the fear she had been holding for three months. He has been twenty-three meters above this room on many occasions without having been in it. It is different from what he expected, which is that he did not know what to expect and so finds that his body has supplied a default expectation in the form of the Fragmentum bloom sites — amber, crystalline, hostile. The chamber is none of those things. It is a large, low-ceilinged space carved from the palace's foundation rock, lit by a ring of geomarrow lanterns that emit a pale blue-gold light, and at its center, on a raised platform of the same foundation stone, sits a structure that is roughly the size of a large cabinet and which is completely unremarkable in every physical sense. The Stellaron. It does not look like an existential threat. It looks like a piece of stone with unusual light properties. He looks at it and thinks about Bronya hearing it for two months, hearing it climb, hearing the interval change, standing in the construction chamber above this room with the array humming and her hands open at her sides and the word quiet said in the register of someone remembering what quiet was. He looks at Aventurine. Aventurine is looking at the Stellaron with an expression he has not worn at any other point in the visit — stripped of the entertainment, stripped of the calculation. Something direct and unguarded, the expression of a person looking at something they have seen before in different circumstances and are remembering. Gepard files this. Topaz is beside Aventurine, and she is not looking at the Stellaron. She is looking at Aventurine looking at the Stellaron, and her expression has a quality of careful, private attention that is different from every professional expression she has worn in the past day — something warmer, something watchful, the way you watch someone you know well when they are in the presence of something that costs them something. Gepard files this too. "The array is above us," Bronya says, from beside him. Her voice is even. She is looking at the Stellaron the way she looked at it in the construction chamber — present, measured, the woman who has been listening to this thing for months and knows its frequencies personally. "Twenty-three meters. The disruption frequency propagates through the foundation geomarrow." She pauses. "The broadcast signal has been at 1.3 for three weeks." "I know," Aventurine says. "I've been reviewing the readings." A pause. "The readings we transmitted," Bronya says, "or your own." Aventurine looks at her. The entertainment is back, but changed — less a surface and more an acknowledgment. "Both," he says. Bronya looks at him for a moment. She looks at Topaz. She makes a decision that Gepard can read in the precise way her posture shifts — not defensive, not aggressive, the decision to press. "You were monitoring Belobog before the letter," she says. "The broadcast escalation. The bloom events." She looks at Aventurine. "You have your own readings going back further than you've indicated." "We have readings going back some time," Topaz says, which is the careful version of yes. "How long," Bronya says. A pause. "Long enough," Aventurine says, which is the same answer Bronya gave him yesterday, reflected back, and which is a message and they both know it. She looks at him. He looks at her. The Stellaron sits between them on its platform, pale blue-gold in the geomarrow light, at 1.3 and holding, and Gepard stands beside Bronya and watches Aventurine decide how much to give and decides he does not like any of the possible answers. "You've known about the Stellaron under Belobog for longer than the Everwinter Hill event," Bronya says. Not a question. "The IPC has extensive monitoring infrastructure," Topaz says. "I know what the IPC has," Bronya says. "I'm asking how long you've known about this one." The chamber is quiet. The geomarrow lanterns hum. Aventurine is looking at Bronya with the expression he had when she walked into the entrance hall — recalibration, the assessment updating — and when he speaks, it is without the entertainment, without the surface, just the stripped version, which Gepard suspects is something he does not often allow. "Long enough," he says again, and this time it does not mean the same as it did a moment ago. This time it means: longer than you want to know, and I am not going to tell you in this room, and there is a reason for that, and the reason is not what you're afraid it is. Bronya holds his gaze for a long moment. Then she turns back to the Stellaron, and says: "There's something else you should know." Gepard looks at her. She looks at the Stellaron. She has the expression she gets when she has made a decision about information — not withholding it, not managing it, simply choosing the moment. She has been choosing this moment, he realizes, since the letter arrived. She has been waiting for this room, this conversation, this specific exchange that has confirmed what she suspected about how much the IPC already knows. She reaches into her coat and takes out a document. She places it on the surface beside the Stellaron's platform. "Three weeks ago," she says, "I began receiving a secondary signal." The chamber is very quiet. "Not from this Stellaron," she says. "A second one. Deeper." She looks at Aventurine. "You already know there is a second Stellaron beneath Belobog." It is not a question. Aventurine does not deny it. And that is the answer. —— Notes: Aventurine read Luka's logs and asked the one question nobody else thought to ask, in the tone of someone who already had the beginning of an answer. 'How did you know the tunnels.' I have been waiting to write that question for three chapters. Topaz watching Aventurine in the Stellaron's chamber — the warm, careful, private attention. She watches him the way you watch someone you know well when they are in the presence of something that costs them something. There is a history there. We will find out what it is. Bronya waited for that room, that conversation, to say what she knew. She let Aventurine confirm how much the IPC already knew before she gave him the second Stellaron. That is not a concession. That is strategy. She gave him something after he gave her something, and now they are in an exchange rather than an interrogation, and that is entirely on purpose. The second Stellaron is in the room now. It has been named. The IPC knows about it. Bronya knows about it. Gepard is learning it in real time and I will not be held responsible for how that lands. Luka's fragment: purple eyes in unknown light. A voice his body knows before his mind does. He slept deeply afterward, for the first time in weeks. The first root through the crack. There will be more. Seele heard the second heartbeat. She described it as something below. She does not know what it means yet. She will. Chapter 14: the IPC's real assessment begins, and what the second Stellaron means for everything. Comment and tell me you're still breathing. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 14: "The Second One" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text The Stellaron's chamber is very quiet after Bronya says it. There is a second one. Deeper. The geomarrow lanterns hum. The pale blue-gold light sits on the foundation stone walls and on the Stellaron on its platform and on the four people standing around it, each of them holding the weight of the sentence differently. Bronya with the careful steadiness of someone who has been holding it since she first heard the secondary signal three weeks ago and has been waiting for the right room to set it down in. Topaz with the controlled alertness of a person updating a model she had already partially built — Gepard clocks this, the specific way her posture adjusts, the forward lean that suggests what Bronya has said is not entirely new information. Aventurine with something that is, for once, entirely unperformed. The light eyes very still. The practiced ease completely absent. He is looking at Bronya the way you look at someone who has just said the thing you were not sure you were going to say yourself, and the expression underneath the absence of performance is more complicated than anything he has worn in the past day and a half. Gepard with the specific quality of stillness that comes from receiving a fact that reorganizes the last three months retroactively and requires a moment to integrate. He looks at Bronya. She is looking at Aventurine. "You've known," she says. Not accusatory. Just confirming the shape of what the silence has already told her. Aventurine is quiet for a moment — the honest kind of quiet, not the performed kind. "The IPC has monitoring data on Belobog going back considerably further than the Everwinter Hill event," he says. "The first Stellaron under the palace has been in our records for decades. Flagged as dormant, low priority." He pauses. "The second one has been in our records for less time. And it is in a different category entirely." "What category," Gepard says. Aventurine looks at him. The light eyes are doing the direct-looking, without the decorative surface, and there is something in them that is not comfortable to carry. "Anomalous," he says. "The signature does not match standard Stellaron classification. The energy output is inconsistent — it spikes and drops in ways that do not correlate with any activation trigger we have encountered in any other Stellaron in our records." He pauses. "It behaves unlike anything we have documented." "Unlike anything," Topaz says, more precisely, "that we have documentation for." She looks at Bronya. "Which is why the letter was sent. Not because we question Belobog's capacity to manage the first Stellaron — the documentation you provided made your management of the feedback loop crisis clear. But the second Stellaron's anomalous behavior, and the fact that it appeared to respond to the first Stellaron's broadcast escalation over the past several months—" She pauses. "That is something we do not yet understand. And in our experience, things we do not understand about Stellarons are worth traveling for." "It responded to the feedback loop," Bronya says. "The second Stellaron's output changed in correlation with the first Stellaron's broadcast escalation," Topaz says. "Not directly amplifying it. Something more complex than amplification. The two signatures interacted in a way our current models do not adequately account for." Bronya looks at the first Stellaron on its platform. She is in the mode Gepard recognizes — everything directed inward, processing at a speed that does not show on the surface. She is quiet for a long moment. "And when the array disrupted the broadcast," she says. "The first Stellaron's signal dropped. Did the second one change too." Topaz and Aventurine look at each other. The look is brief, the kind exchanged between two people who have discussed this already and have agreed on how much to say. "Yes," Aventurine says. "How," Bronya says. Another brief pause. "The second Stellaron's output, which had been elevated and erratic during the period of the first Stellaron's broadcast escalation, became more stable after the array ran." He pauses. "Not quieter. More ordered." He looks at the Stellaron on its platform. "As if the disruption of the first Stellaron's broadcast affected the second one's behavior — but not by dampening it. By clarifying it." The room holds this. Gepard does not know what clarifying means in the context of a Stellaron's output behavior. He is fairly certain, from the specific quality of Bronya's silence, that she does not know either. He is also fairly certain, from the specific quality of Aventurine's delivery, that the IPC does not know either, and that this is what actually brought them here — not to evaluate Belobog's management of the first Stellaron, but to stand in the same building as the second one and try to understand something they cannot understand from a distance. "Where is it," Gepard says. Bronya looks at him. She has the expression she uses when she is deciding how much to give in a room with the IPC in it, which is different from the expression she uses when she is deciding how much to give in a room without them, and for a moment he thinks she is going to hold this piece back. She doesn't. "Below the Underworld," she says. "The outer district. I have been able to feel the direction of it since I noticed the secondary signal three weeks ago." She looks at Aventurine. "Your data must corroborate that." "The signal propagates upward through the Underworld's bedrock," Topaz says. "The approximate location, based on our external readings, is consistent with the outer machine district." The outer machine district. Gepard makes no outward sign of noting this. Bronya makes no outward sign of noting this. But they look at each other across the chamber, briefly, and the look does not carry a conclusion — they do not have a conclusion, there is nothing specific to connect yet — only the shared awareness of a detail that has now been named in the same room as two people who spend significant time in those tunnels. He does not look at Bronya for longer than a moment. He looks back at Aventurine. "What do you need from us," he says. Aventurine looks at him. Then at Bronya. He makes the same decision he made a moment ago — the costly one, the stripped one. "To understand what we are dealing with," he says. "The documentation you provided on the first Stellaron is excellent. The management of the feedback loop crisis is — genuinely impressive. But the second Stellaron's anomalous behavior is something we need more data on before we can offer any useful framework." He pauses. "And data, in this case, means proximity. Access." "To the second Stellaron's location," Bronya says. "Ideally, yes. In time." He looks at her steadily. "But we are not going to ask you to open a chamber beneath the Underworld on our second day in Belobog. We are here to assess, not to demand." A pause, and the entertained quality re-emerges, just slightly, warmer than the version he arrived with. "Despite what you may have assumed about IPC methodology." Bronya looks at him for a long moment. "I appreciate the distinction," she says, which is not the same as trusting it, and Aventurine's expression says he knows this and finds it reasonable. —— They reconvene in the council chamber at noon. The full group: Bronya at the head, Gepard beside her, Pela with her documentation, Seele, Sampo, Luka, and the two IPC representatives. Topaz produces her own data — the IPC's monitoring records on the second Stellaron, formatted in the IPC's dense and precise style, the kind of document that contains three times more information than it appears to at first reading. Pela looks at it for approximately four minutes and then says: "You have readings going back forty years." "Forty-three," Topaz says. "And the anomalous behavior began—" "Approximately twenty-two years ago." Topaz pauses. "A single spike event, significant enough to flag but brief enough to initially classify as instrument error." She looks at the data. "It was not instrument error." The council absorbs this. Gepard watches the room while appearing to read the data. Seele, two seats down from Luka, is looking at Topaz's documents with the evaluative expression she wears when she is cataloguing something. Luka is reading with the steady focused attention that Gepard has come to associate with him working. Sampo has his playing card notes and is not writing anything on them, which means he is listening more carefully than usual. "The spike event," Pela says. "The energy output at the time — what was the Fragmentum response radius." Topaz checks her data. "Based on the second Stellaron's depth and the bedrock composition, our modeling suggests the Fragmentum response would have been concentrated in the outer tunnel networks of the Underworld. Radius of approximately—" She pauses. "Three hundred meters from the epicenter." "The outer machine district," Pela says, which is not a connection to anything or anyone in the room — it is simply Pela being precise about geography. "The outer machine district," Topaz confirms. "Are there records of the Fragmentum event at that time," Pela says. "From Belobog's own administrative archive." "I checked before we arrived," Topaz says, which means she prepared for this question, which means she expected someone to ask it, which means the administrative records are a known variable in her analysis. "The Surface Authority records from that period in the Underworld are incomplete. A significant portion were lost in a system failure approximately twenty years ago." Pela looks at this. She looks at the data. She looks at the timeline. And then, because Pela's relationship with information does not permit her to leave a notable correlation unvoiced, she says: "A system failure twenty years ago affecting records from twenty-two years ago. The timing is—" She pauses, selecting the precise word. "Notable." "Yes," Topaz says. The room is quiet for a moment. Gepard looks at the data. He looks at the timeline. He looks at the gap where the records should be and thinks about what a Fragmentum event in the outer machine district three hundred meters in radius, twenty-two years ago, would have looked like to the people living there. He does not have a conclusion. He has the shape of a question that he cannot yet complete. He looks, briefly, at Seele. She is looking at the table. Her expression has not changed. He looks at Luka. Luka is looking at the section of Topaz's data describing the spike event's energy profile, his eyes moving through it with the methodical attention of someone who logs things carefully and knows what numbers mean. His expression has not changed either. Gepard files this and moves on. "The anomalous behavior after the spike event," Bronya says. "What specifically makes it anomalous. Not in terms of correlation with the first Stellaron — that's the recent behavior. I want to understand the baseline anomaly." Aventurine looks at her with the expression he has been wearing since the Stellaron's chamber — the one that is doing real work rather than performing. "The output pattern," he says. "Standard Stellarons broadcast. They emit. The energy moves outward, the signal propagates, the Fragmentum in proximity responds." He pauses. "This one doesn't broadcast. Its energy output is—" He pauses again, and Gepard has the impression he is looking for a word that does not quite exist in the standard framework. "Directed inward. Or—" He stops. "Held." "Held," Pela says. "As if the energy is being contained rather than released." He looks at the data. "In every model we have, a Stellaron that holds its energy rather than releasing it should be unstable. The pressure should build. There should be periodic discharge events." He looks up. "There are none. The spike event twenty-two years ago was the only significant discharge in forty-three years of monitoring." He pauses. "It is the most stable Stellaron in our records, by one measure, and the most inexplicable by every other." "It's holding something," Bronya says. "That is the working hypothesis," Topaz says carefully. "We don't know what." Bronya nods, and the nod is the one she uses when she is accepting an answer not because it's complete but because it's the most complete available. "Then that's what we need to find out," she says. "What it's holding." "Yes," Aventurine says. "That is exactly what we need to find out." —— The session runs until 1500 hours. When it ends, Pela immediately begins correlating the IPC's data with her own analysis, which will keep her occupied for most of the evening. Topaz asks to review the construction chamber and array specifications in the afternoon, which Pela provides. Aventurine does not ask for anything specific — he says he would like to walk the city if that is permitted, which Bronya permits with the careful equanimity of someone who understands that a person who reads rooms will read the city too, and that preventing him from doing so would be less informative than letting him. Gepard assigns a soldier to walk with him. Not overtly as an escort — as a guide. He assigns Chen, who is observant, reliable, and has the specific social gift of being present without being intrusive. Aventurine accepts the company with the ease of someone who expected it. The chamber empties. Sampo leaves in the particular way he has been leaving rooms since the morning session of day one — fast, quiet, thinking. Gepard has noticed this. Sampo does not leave rooms fast and quiet unless something has caught under his skin, and whatever it is has been there since the outer machine district was named in the context of a Stellaron event twenty-two years ago. He will ask him about it when the IPC visitors are not in the building. Seele is the last to leave, which is the second time she has been last to leave a council session. She stands at the door with the IPC data in her hand and looks back at the table, and her expression has the quality it had when she told him she doesn't remember things she should. The young quality. The uncertain one. "Seele," he says. She looks at him. "The outer machine district," she says, quietly. "Yes." She looks at the data in her hand. "I grew up near there. After—" She pauses, choosing the word with the same care she always uses. "After my mother died. We moved. The Underworld does not have many options for where to move, and the outer machine district was—" She stops. "Close to where we were already." He looks at her. He does not push. He waits. "I don't know why that feels relevant," she says. "It probably isn't." She straightens, the decision being made in real time to close the door on whatever she was looking at. "It's just geography." "Yes," he says, because she needs him to agree and because he does not have enough to say otherwise — not yet, not without a conclusion, not without more than geography and a twenty-two year old spike event and a gap in the records. She goes. He stands in the empty council chamber for a long moment, and then he goes to find Bronya. —— He finds her in the office. Not the construction chamber this time — the office, at the desk, with the IPC data and her own notes and the particular focused stillness that means she has been at this since the session ended and has found something worth the attention. She looks up when he comes in. He closes the door. He sits in the chair from the corner. She slides two pieces of paper across the desk without preamble. He looks at them. The first is Topaz's data on the second Stellaron's baseline output — the holding pattern, the inward direction, the forty-three-year stability punctuated by the single spike event. The second is Pela's analysis of the first Stellaron's broadcast escalation timeline, the one she produced during the feedback loop crisis. Bronya has drawn a single line between them — not annotating, not concluding, just connecting two data points with the physical line that means: look at these together. He looks at them together. The first Stellaron's broadcast escalation began — not at Everwinter Hill, which was when it became critical, but the first detectable increase — approximately three months ago. The second Stellaron began its recent behavioral shift — the clarifying, the move toward stability rather than anomalous erratic output — approximately six weeks ago. He looks at the line between them. "They're not synchronized," he says. "No," she says. "There's a gap. The first Stellaron started escalating three months ago. The second one started changing six weeks ago." She looks at the papers. "Something happened six weeks ago that affected the second Stellaron's behavior. Not the array — the array was day eleven, and the behavioral shift predates the array." She pauses. "Something changed six weeks ago that began the clarifying process." He thinks about six weeks ago. Six weeks ago, Sampo gave Luka a job and Luka said he'd think about it. Six weeks ago, Luka started going to the tunnels. Six weeks ago, the conversations at the junction started. The branch going left. The route walked from memory. He does not have a conclusion. He has the shape of a question that is getting more specific. "I don't know what it was," Bronya says, which means she has the same shape and the same absence of conclusion. "But I want to know." She looks at him. "Pela is going to run a full correlation analysis on the second Stellaron's output changes and every documented event in Belobog over the past six weeks." She pauses. "It's going to take a few days." "And in the meantime," he says. "In the meantime, we let the IPC assessment proceed, we don't show our working on this until we have something to show, and we keep the second Stellaron's location as tightly held as possible." She looks at him steadily. "Aventurine already knows it's in the outer machine district from their external readings. But he doesn't know which tunnel network. He doesn't know the depth. And right now, neither do we — not precisely." "You said corridor nine-beta in the construction chamber," he says. She pauses. "You said it was under corridor nine-beta," he says. She is quiet for a moment. "I said I could feel the direction of it," she says carefully. "Corridor nine-beta is the closest surface-accessible point to where I feel the signal originating." She looks at the papers. "Which means if we're going to get better location data, someone needs to be in those tunnels with the right equipment." He looks at her. She looks at him. "Luka has been in those tunnels twenty-two times," he says. "Yes," she says. "And Seele has been in them twice," he says, "and keeps coming back." "Yes," she says, and her voice is even and careful and does not carry any conclusion, because she does not have one, and she is not going to pretend she does. They sit with the absence of the conclusion for a moment. "I'll talk to Pela about the correlation analysis," he says. "Good." She slides the papers back. She picks up her pen. She does not put it down this time — she starts writing, which means she has moved from thinking-at-the-desk to working-at-the-desk, which means the decision is made. "And Gepard." "Yes." "The outer machine district." She is writing. "The people who live there. The people who have always lived there." She pauses, pen still moving. "We should know more about that community before the IPC's assessment goes further." She looks up. "Natasha would know. She's been the district's doctor for years." "I'll go to the ward tomorrow," he says. "Good," she says. He watches her write for a moment. He picks up his patrol folder. He does not attempt to read it. He sits in the chair from the corner and lets the office settle into its working quiet around them — the two of them with the papers and the questions without conclusions and the second Stellaron somewhere below the outer machine district, holding what it holds, counting toward whatever it is counting toward. "Bronya," he says. "Yes." "How long have you been hearing the secondary signal." "Three weeks and two days," she says, without looking up. The precision of someone who started counting from the first moment. "What does it sound like." She is quiet for a moment. The pen stops. "The first Stellaron's signal is like a note held too long — present, constant, slightly wrong, the kind of sound that lives in the back of the teeth." She pauses. "The second one is nothing like that." She looks up. "The second one sounds like something remembering itself." He looks at her. She looks at him. Neither of them knows what this means yet. Neither of them pretends to. The office holds them in its working quiet, and outside the city moves through its evening, and below the outer machine district something old and patient and wholly inexplicable goes about the work it has been doing for twenty-two years, in the dark, in the silence of held things. —— That night, Luka cannot sleep. This is not unusual — he has not been sleeping well for several weeks, since the junction and the branch going left. The not-sleeping has been a consistent presence, ambient, the body knowing something the mind is still catching up to. Tonight is the same, mostly. He lies in his quarters in the Underworld building adjacent to Svarog's workshop, looking at the ceiling in the dark, and he does not think about anything specific. He thinks about the council session. He thinks about the outer machine district named in the context of a Fragmentum event twenty-two years ago. He thinks about the gap in the administrative records, which Pela called notable and which Sampo did not react to visibly and which Luka has been thinking about since the session ended. He grew up in the outer machine district. He has not said this in the council session because it is not data and the council session was about data and there was nothing to contribute beyond the fact of geography. He grew up here. He has lived here his whole life, in this district, in these buildings adjacent to this workshop, moving through these tunnels since he was old enough to go where he wanted. He knows the outer machine district the way he knows the route in the tunnels — not because he learned it, but because it is in him, already there, pre-existing the learning. He closes his eyes. He does not dream, exactly. But in the half-space before sleep, in the way that the body processes things the mind has not yet organized — there is something. Not an image. More like a feeling. The specific quality of a particular kind of cold, a particular quality of darkness, and a sound — not the second heartbeat, something different, something human-scale and brief — a voice. A word, or part of one. The consonants and not the vowel. The shape of a name without the name itself. He stays very still. The feeling does not develop. It does not become more specific. It sits at the edge of access and then retreats, the way something retreats when the attention moves toward it too directly, and he has learned, in the weeks of this, not to move toward these things too directly. He breathes. He lets it be. He falls asleep, eventually, in the deep way he has been falling asleep since the first fragment — the sleep of a body that has put something down it has been carrying for a long time without knowing it, and which finds rest in the putting-down even before it understands what it was carrying. Below him, further below than sleep, the second Stellaron holds what it holds. It sounds, to the one person in Belobog who can hear it, like something remembering itself. —— Notes: 'It sounds like something remembering itself.' She said this. I wrote it and then had to stop for a while. The distinction that matters in this chapter: nobody in that room has a conclusion yet. Pela is running a correlation analysis that will take days. Bronya has noticed the six-week gap between the first and second Stellaron's behavioral shift but does not know what caused it. Gepard has the shape of a question that is getting more specific but not specific enough to say out loud. Nobody is pointing at Seele and Luka. Nobody knows there is anything to point at. The outer machine district was named in the context of a twenty-two-year-old Fragmentum event and a gap in the administrative records. Seele said 'it's just geography' and made herself believe it for the walk home. Luka grew up there and didn't say so because it wasn't data. These are the things people do with the things that are too large to look at directly. Aventurine wanted to walk the city. Gepard sent Chen with him. Aventurine accepted. This will matter. Sampo left fast and quiet. This will also matter. Natasha knows the outer machine district. She has been its doctor for years. Gepard is going to the ward tomorrow, and he doesn't know yet what he's going to find there, but Natasha has been in that community for a long time and she carries what the community carries and some of what she carries is going to be relevant. The second Stellaron is holding something. Nobody knows what yet. The question 'what is it holding' is going to take three more chapters to begin to answer properly. Good. Luka heard the shape of a name without the name. He did not chase it. He is learning. Chapter 15 is the tunnels. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 15: "Nine-Beta" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text The morning session on day three of the IPC visit is Topaz's domain entirely. She wants the array. She wants every specification, every calibration log, every diagnostic reading from the construction and maintenance period. She wants Serval's technical files read back to her with annotation, and she wants Pela present for all of it, and she wants four hours to do it properly. Bronya gives her all of this without hesitation, because giving Topaz the array documentation costs nothing — it is, in fact, exactly what they want the IPC to see. The array is Belobog's argument made physical. Every hour Topaz spends in the construction chamber reading Serval's calibration notes is an hour being convinced by the quality of the work. Aventurine does not attend this session. He sends his apologies through Topaz with the ease of a man who knows he is not going to be missed in a four-hour technical review and does not perform the obligation of pretending otherwise. He takes Chen and walks the city again — the second morning in a row, which means he is being thorough, which means the first walk was reconnaissance and the second is verification. Gepard notes this and goes to Natasha's ward. —— The ward is its usual morning self — the particular organized hum of a medical facility that is always in motion but never chaotic, because Natasha does not permit chaos. She is at the front desk when he arrives, which she always is, but today there is someone else at the desk with her — a woman he does not recognize, older, with the specific physical quality of someone who has spent decades doing manual work in cold conditions, sitting in the chair beside the desk and speaking with Natasha in the low, familiar register of a long acquaintance. The woman looks up when he enters. She looks at his armor. She looks at his face. Her expression does the calculation that the outer district residents do when they see Silvermane Guard in the ward — not hostile, but watchful, the watchfulness of people who have learned that official attention is a variable with outcomes in both directions. "Commander Gepard," Natasha says, which is both a greeting and a contextualizing of his presence for the woman beside her. "Natasha." He nods. "I'm sorry to interrupt." "You're not interrupting." She stands, the smooth transition of someone who manages multiple conversations simultaneously as a matter of course. She looks at the woman beside her. "This is Marta. She's been in the outer machine district for thirty years. She coordinates the community supply distribution." The woman — Marta — looks at him with the watchfulness still present but slightly adjusted. Thirty years in the outer machine district. He does what he does in every room, which is make a note. "I was hoping to speak with you about the outer district," he says to Natasha, "when you have time." "I have time now." She looks at Marta. "Excuse me for a moment." She leads him to the small office at the back of the ward — the one with the chart stacks and the single window that looks at the corridor wall outside, and which has the quality of a space that has absorbed a great deal of things over the years and holds them quietly. She closes the door. She looks at him with the directness that is simply her register, the way she has always looked at him. "The IPC assessment," she says. "The second Stellaron." He looks at her. "Sampo told me," she says. Not defensive. Simply factual. "He came last night." She pauses. "He was—" She selects the word carefully. "Unsettled." He thinks about Sampo leaving the council session fast and quiet. "What did he tell you." "That the IPC has monitoring data on a second Stellaron beneath the outer machine district going back forty-three years. That there was a spike event twenty-two years ago. That the administrative records from that period are missing." She looks at her hands on the desk. "And that he grew up in the outer machine district." Gepard holds this. "Sampo grew up here." "In the outer machine district, yes. Until he was approximately twelve, and then—" She pauses. "And then he didn't. He has not spoken much about that period." A pause that has something careful in it. "He never speaks about it. But he came last night and he was unsettled and he told me those things, which means they connected to something." "To what," Gepard says. She is quiet for a moment. She is doing the thing she does — the measurement, the decision about how much to say. "When Sampo was young," she says, finally, "there was a period in the outer machine district that the older residents refer to as the dark winter. Not an official designation — just what the community calls it. A period of approximately three months, twenty-two years ago, when a number of things happened in quick succession." She pauses. "A Fragmentum surge in the tunnel networks. Several deaths. A community-wide — Natasha's voice is very careful. "Disruption. Of memory." She pauses. "People forgetting things. Not everything, not dramatically — just specific things. Specific periods. Specific people, in some cases." She looks at him. "The community attributed it to the Fragmentum surge at the time. Fragmentum exposure can cause cognitive effects. It was a reasonable explanation." He looks at her. "But," he says. "But the forgetting was unusually specific," she says. "Not random, not generalized. Targeted, almost. Particular relationships, particular events from that period." She looks at the wall. "The older residents talk about it rarely and obliquely. There is a quality to how they discuss it—" She pauses. "Like something was taken rather than lost." He sits with this. "How do you know this," he says. "I've been the district's doctor for six years," she says. "People tell their doctors things they don't tell anyone else." She pauses. "And when I first came here, there was an old woman who had been in the outer machine district through the dark winter. She was — not well, by then. But she told me something before she died that I have been thinking about since Sampo came last night." She looks at him directly. "She said: whatever was taken, it was taken gently. She said it didn't feel like loss. It felt like being put to sleep." The ward is quiet around them. Through the small window, the corridor wall is grey and unmoving. "Natasha," he says. "The administrative records that were lost twenty years ago. The system failure." "I know about the system failure," she says. "I noticed the gap in the records when I first set up the ward. Several years of Underworld health records, community census data, incident reports — all gone." She pauses. "I filed a request through official channels for restoration or explanation. I never received a response." Another pause. "I filed it again two years later. Also no response." She looks at him. "When Sampo told me about the IPC data last night, I pulled my copy of the filing request. The dates correlate with a period about a year after the surface Authority's administrative restructuring." She pauses. "The records were not lost in a system failure. The surface Authority conducted a records purge approximately two years after the dark winter. Classified as routine data management." He looks at her. "It was not routine," he says. "No," she says. "It was not routine." He breathes. He thinks about the outer machine district twenty-two years ago. The Fragmentum surge. The deaths. The specific, targeted forgetting. The records purge by an authority that knew, or suspected, or had been told, that something had happened down there that should not be accessible to the people it happened to. He thinks about a Stellaron that holds rather than broadcasts. That is inexplicably stable. That absorbed something in a spike event twenty-two years ago and has been holding it ever since. He does not have a conclusion. He is much closer to the shape of one than he was an hour ago. "Thank you," he says, and stands. "Gepard." He stops. She is looking at him with the expression she had when she told Sampo she wasn't alright — the one she only gives to people she trusts with the full version. "Sampo has been in this district his whole adult life. He came back here after whatever happened when he was twelve. He built his life here, on top of whatever the dark winter was." She pauses. "Whatever you find when you investigate this — I need him to know what it is. Not at the end. As you find it." She holds his gaze. "He deserves that." "Yes," he says. "He does." He goes. —— Seele is at the entrance to corridor nine-beta at 1100 hours. Luka is not. She waits. She is not good at waiting — it has never been in her constitution, patience in static form, the kind that requires standing still while the seconds accumulate. She is good at waiting in motion, waiting while moving toward something, waiting while her hands are occupied. Standing at a tunnel entrance in the Underworld's outer machine district with nothing to do but be early is a specific variety of uncomfortable. She checks the time. 1102. She looks at the tunnel entrance. She looks at the ambient crystallization on the walls inside — the same dulled amber-black it has been for weeks, inert, reading dormant on any equipment. She has been reading Luka's walkthrough logs since Pela forwarded them ten days ago. She knows what the readings should look like at every junction from one through eight. She is aware that she has been reading walkthrough logs that she was not asked to read, and she is not examining why. 1104. Footsteps from the main corridor. She turns. Luka rounds the corner at a pace that is not quite a jog but is faster than his usual stride, and he is carrying something — a small equipment case in addition to the sensor, which is new. He slows when he sees her. He sees her expression. "I'm four minutes late," he says. "You're four minutes late," she says. "The secondary module needed an adjustment before I left." He holds up the case. "I brought additional equipment. Deeper range sensors." He pauses. "Svarog has been recalibrating the primary since last week. I thought today we might go further than junction eight." She looks at him. She looks at the case. "You planned that without mentioning it." "I'm mentioning it now." "You're mentioning it now that you've already built the plan and brought the equipment." She turns back to the corridor entrance. "What if I had other plans for what we were doing today." "Did you." A pause. "No," she says, because she didn't, because she has been thinking about going further than junction eight since the day they stood at junction seven-alpha-four and the wall pulsed and she heard the thing below more clearly than she had before. "But that's not the point." "What is the point." She turns and looks at him. He is looking back at her with the expression he has been wearing more frequently over the past few weeks — not the easy straightforwardness exactly, something slightly sharper than that, something that has been developing in the specific way that a person's expression develops when they have stopped performing neutrality and are simply being attentive. She has noticed this development in the way she notices things she has not decided to notice yet — sideways, without looking directly. "The point," she says, "is that we're both going into that tunnel and you planned further than the agreed scope without consulting me, which means you made assumptions about what I would agree to." "You agreed on the walk over here," he says. "I haven't agreed to anything." "You didn't say no." "I haven't said yes either." He looks at her. She looks at him. The tunnel entrance is between them and the corridor lighting is the same as it always is and the second heartbeat is there the way it is always there — present, below, the thing neither of them mentions directly but which both of them are aware of the moment they step within range of nine-beta. "Are you coming," he says. She makes a sound that is not quite contempt and not quite anything else. She turns and walks into the tunnel. He follows. —— The first five junctions are the same as they always are. Luka reads the sensor — the recalibrated primary, its readings marginally more precise than before, the secondary module adjusted to a wider range that Svarog specified but has not explained the reasoning for. Seele walks. She does what she always does in these tunnels — observes, asks questions occasionally that are precise enough to reveal she has been thinking about the crystallization behavior between visits, and says nothing about why she is thinking about it between visits. At junction three she says: "The IPC data from yesterday. The correlation Pela is running." "She won't have results for a few days," Luka says. "I know. I'm not asking about the results." She looks at the junction marker. "I'm asking what you think caused the behavioral shift in the second Stellaron six weeks ago." He logs the reading. "I don't know." "Guess." He glances at her. She is looking at the ceiling the way she does when she is working through something — tracking the crystallization threading through the seams, but her attention is not really on the ceiling. "Something changed in the outer machine district six weeks ago," he says. "Something the second Stellaron responded to." "Something or someone," she says. He does not answer. He logs junction four. "You started going to these tunnels six weeks ago," she says, to the ceiling. "Yes," he says. "Sampo gave you the job six weeks ago." "Yes." She looks at him then — directly, no sideways. "Do you think that's a coincidence." He meets her gaze. "No," he says. "I haven't thought it was a coincidence for about three weeks." She looks at him for a moment. Then she looks back at the corridor ahead. "Neither have I," she says, which is not the admission it would have been two weeks ago and is a different kind of admission now. She walks. "I didn't start coming to the tunnels until after you were already coming to them. So if the timing of the behavioral shift correlates with someone entering the corridors—" "It correlates with me," he says. "Not you." "Yes." She pauses. "Which means I'm not the variable. You are." She pauses again. "I don't like that." He looks at her. She is not looking at him — she is looking at the corridor with the evaluative expression, the one that processes things by taking them apart. "Why," he says. "Because if you're the variable and I'm not, then my being here is incidental." The words are brisk. Factual. The tone of someone stating something they find logically irritating. "And I am not incidental." He is quiet for a moment. "No," he says. "You're not." She looks at him sharply, as if she expected him to disagree or deflect and he has done neither. He logs junction five. Yellow. They walk. At junction six she says, without preamble: "I grew up near the outer machine district. After my mother died. We moved because it was the closest available housing and the community administration arranged it." She pauses. "I was four. I don't remember the district from before we moved. I only remember after." He looks at her sideways. "Where before." "The second district. Near the central market." She pauses. "I don't remember it. I know we lived there because someone told me, years later. I don't have any memory of it myself." She looks at the wall. "I have almost no memories from before I was seven. I thought that was normal." "It's not abnormal," he says. "Early childhood—" "I know it's not abnormal," she says, sharply. "I'm not saying it's abnormal. I'm saying it's convenient." She looks at him. "For whatever happened here twenty-two years ago, it's very convenient that the person who was in this district as a child has no accessible memories of it." He stops walking. She stops a step later and turns to look at him. "I grew up in the outer machine district," he says. "My whole life. I should have memories going back to — I don't know, four, five years old. Walking these corridors. Playing in this district." He pauses. "I don't. My earliest clear memory from this district is from when I was approximately ten. Before that — impressions. Colors. The smell of the workshop." He looks at the wall. "I told myself that was normal too." She looks at him. He looks at her. The tunnel is quiet. The second heartbeat is present. "We're both missing the same years," she says, very precisely. "Yes," he says. "Approximately the same years." "Yes." She looks at the corridor ahead. Something is moving in her expression — not the hostile wariness, not the evaluation. Something that is working through the implications of a thing and does not like where they lead. "Then the question isn't just what happened here twenty-two years ago," she says. "The question is what happened to us." He holds her gaze. "Yes." She turns back to the corridor. "Junction seven," she says. "Let's keep going." —— Junction seven-alpha-four. They always stop here. They stop here even when they do not intend to, even when the corridor walk has momentum and the stopping is inefficient and neither of them has made a conscious decision to stop. The body stops. The body has been stopping at this junction since the first time Luka walked past it and has never explained why. The north wall. The amber-black sheet, dulled but present. The convexity that shifted toward the palace foundation and has not moved since. Luka's sensor reads yellow. The secondary module reads something else. He looks at the secondary module reading — the wider-range calibration Svarog specified. It is not the out-of-range blinking he got at junction twelve in the previous chapter's version of this walk. It is something more subtle: a directional indicator. The module has a directionality function he did not know it had until Svarog's recalibration, and the directional indicator is pointing — not at the wall in front of them, not at the crystallization sheet. Down. Significantly down. At an angle that suggests the source is deep and slightly to the north. He shows the reading to Seele without speaking. She looks at it. She looks at the wall. She looks back at the reading. "How deep," she says. "The module doesn't have depth calculation. Just direction." "North and down." She looks at the wall. "The pulse is stronger here than at the other junctions." "Yes." He does not ask how she knows this. She knows it the way he knows the route — pre-existing the occasion for it. "It's been stronger each time we come. Incrementally." She looks at him. "Getting stronger each time we come," she says. "Or getting stronger as we get closer to — whatever it is." "The two of us being here is making it stronger," she says flatly. It is not a question and it is not comfortable. She says it the way she says things she does not like — directly, without flinching. "The thing below is responding to us being in this corridor together." "That would be consistent with the timing of the behavioral shift," he says. "Yes." She looks at the wall with the expression that is not afraid and is not quite not-afraid. "That's an interesting problem." "Which part." "All of it." She turns from the wall. "Let's go past eight." "Seele—" "You brought the extended equipment." She looks at him. "You planned to go further today. So did I. Let's go further." He looks at her. The wariness is there — it is always there, structural, the hostility that is not gone and is not going away on any timeline he can see, it is simply coexisting with other things now, the way a load-bearing wall coexists with the rooms on either side of it. She is looking back at him with the evaluative expression and underneath it something that is not trust exactly but is its precursor — the thing that comes before trust in the specific case of two people who are circling something large together. "Past eight," he says. "Not further than twelve. Not today." "Why twelve." "Because twelve is the junction where the readings change significantly and I want to approach it with better equipment than I currently have." She considers this. "That's reasonable," she says, which from Seele is a meaningful concession. "Past eight. Stop at twelve." They go. —— Past junction eight, nine-beta becomes something different. This is not an exaggeration or a poetic framing — it is simply true. The corridor changes character past junction eight in the way that places change when you cross a threshold that was not marked, when the space you are in stops being a modified version of something human-built and starts being something else. The ceiling height drops by approximately a meter, making it a deliberate exercise in not catching a shoulder on exposed piping. The lighting — which in the first eight junctions was sparse but present, the Underworld's standard amber-tinted maintenance strips — becomes absent. Luka switches on the handheld torch from the equipment case. The beam catches the walls and Seele says, flatly: "That's not construction material." She is correct. Past junction eight, the walls are no longer poured concrete. They are carved stone — the foundation bedrock, close to the surface here, close enough that whoever built the outer district's tunnel network ran out of layered construction material and had to work directly into the rock. The carving is old. The tool marks are not modern. "How old," Seele says. "I don't know." He runs the sensor along the wall. Yellow. "The stone itself or the carving?" "Either." "I don't have geology equipment." He logs junction nine. "But the carving style — the angle of the marks — I've seen this in the workshop. Svarog has fragments of this material. He calls it old-world stone." She looks at the wall for a moment with an expression that is not the evaluating one. It is the young one — the uncertain one — and she wears it briefly and then it is gone. "There were people here before the Underworld," she says. "Yes." "Before the Separation." "Yes." They walk. Junction ten. Junction eleven. The carved stone deepens around them, the ceiling dropping further, the torch throwing longer shadows. The sensor reads yellow at every junction. The secondary module's directional indicator does not change — still north, still down, still deeper than any equipment Luka is carrying can reach. The second heartbeat is louder here. Not audible to anything measurable. Not in any sense that Luka could put in a log. But present in the way it is present in the tunnels and nowhere else — the thing below the cold, the thing below the stone, the rhythm that is not his own and which has been in him since before he knew what it was. Here, past junction ten, it is present enough to notice without trying. He does not say this. Seele says: "It's louder." He looks at her. She is walking with her hand trailing the carved stone wall — the unconscious gesture, the one she does not appear to notice herself making, the reaching. "Yes," he says. "The further in we go." She removes her hand from the wall. "The sensor doesn't catch it." "No." "But we do." She looks at the torch beam ahead of them. "Both of us." "Yes." She is quiet for a moment. They reach junction eleven. She stops here — not the involuntary stop of seven-alpha-four, a deliberate one. She turns and looks at him in the torch light, and the specific quality of her expression in this light — the shadows emphasizing the evaluation, the wariness, and underneath them the thing that is not trust but is becoming adjacent to it — is different from how she looks in the main corridor's amber. More present. More unmanaged. "We should go back," she says. He blinks. "You said—" "I know what I said. I'm saying we should go back." She looks past him toward junction twelve, which is visible from here as a darkening in the passage, the branch point where the corridor opens and changes. "Not because of the readings. Because of what I said at junction three." She looks at him directly. "The two of us being here is making it stronger. The thing below is responding to us." She pauses. "We don't know what that means yet. We don't know what it does when it gets stronger. We don't know what happens if we get to twelve and the readings spike the way the secondary module is suggesting they might." She holds his gaze. "We should not be in an unknown corridor in the deep Underworld without knowing what we're walking into." He looks at her. He looks at junction twelve ahead. He looks at her. "You're right," he says. She appears, very briefly, to not have expected this. "I—" She stops. "Yes," she says. "I am." "We come back with better equipment." He looks at the secondary module. "And with someone who knows what the reading means." He pauses. "Svarog." Something shifts in her expression. "You trust him." "Completely," he says, without hesitation, which is not something he says about many people and which she receives with a quality of attention that suggests she is noting it. "Alright," she says. "We go back." She turns toward the way they came. She has taken two steps when the sound happens. Not the second heartbeat. Not below. From junction twelve — from the darkness of the passage ahead of them, from the branch point they can see from junction eleven — a sound that is not the old-world stone and is not the tunnel and is not anything this corridor has produced in any of the twenty-three walkthrough logs Luka has filed. It is a wet, cracking sound. The specific sound of Fragmentum crystallization under pressure. Active crystallization. Not the dormant amber-black of the past weeks. Luka's sensor spikes into orange. Not red. Orange — the warning register, the precursor, the reading that means: something has changed and it is worth paying attention to. He has not seen an orange reading in these tunnels since before the array. He and Seele both turn at the same time. From junction twelve's darkness, something moves. It comes through the passage in a way that does not suggest weight or biology — it slides, the specific sliding movement of Fragmentum matter that has taken a shape without fully committing to it. The shape is roughly humanoid, roughly the height of an adult, composed of dark amber crystallization threaded through with the deep black of corrupted matter — the Fragmentum Chrysalis form, the one the soldiers at Sector C had encountered during the worst of the convergence period, the one that redirects before you can predict it, the one that learns. Seele moves before he does. This is not surprising. Seele moves before everyone. But what happens in the next four seconds is that she moves and then stops — not because she hesitates, but because the Chrysalis does something he has not seen Fragmentum matter do, which is orient directly toward her and stay oriented regardless of which direction she moves. Left, right, back — it tracks her. Specifically her. "Luka," she says, her voice entirely level in the way that means she is managing something that is not entirely manageable. "It's not tracking the torch." "I know," he says, because he can see it. He moves left, deliberately, into the torch beam, directly in its sightline. The Chrysalis does not reorient. It stays aimed at Seele. "It's tracking you specifically." "Yes," she says. "That's interesting." "Seele—" She moves. The specific movement of someone who grew up fighting in the Underworld's confined spaces — close, fast, using the tunnel walls as geometry rather than obstacles, putting her back to carved stone to remove the angle. The Chrysalis follows her trajectory with the fluid reorientation that is the thing that makes Chrysalis forms dangerous, the thing that makes standard suppression patterns fail. It gets between her and the junction nine exit. "Plan," she says. He is already calculating. The tunnel is narrow — the narrowing past junction eight means single-file movement is most practical, which means a Chrysalis form filling the width of the passage is effectively a wall. The secondary module is reading orange and climbing toward the next threshold. The torch in his left hand. The sensor, useless for this, in his equipment case. "I need you to move left," he says. "Hard left. Keep it tracking you. Give me three seconds." She looks at him. One second of looking, the evaluation running fast. "Three seconds," she says, and moves. The Chrysalis tracks her left. Luka moves right, toward the wall, and then forward — past the Chrysalis, between it and the junction twelve darkness, putting himself at its back. It tries to reorient. He does not give it three seconds. He gives it two and a half, which is what the geometry permits, and drives his boot into the center mass of the form from behind with the full force of his body weight behind it. Fragmentum Chrysalis forms under ambient pressure collapse from impact at a specific structural point — Svarog had explained this once, in the workshop, in the way Svarog explains things, which is without wasting words. The point is not the center. It is the junction between the primary crystalline structure and the corrupted matter threading through it, which in a Chrysalis form sits approximately forty percent from the top. He knows where this is because he has been in Svarog's workshop for eight months and Svarog talks about Fragmentum combat mechanics with the same matter-of-fact thoroughness he applies to everything. His boot connects at forty percent from the top. The Chrysalis form cracks. Not collapses — cracks. The primary structure fractures but the corrupted matter holds, and the form reorients toward him now, which is better than it being oriented at Seele but is not comfortable, and he gets his shoulder into the carved stone wall with enough force to briefly lose his footing and catches himself with one hand. Seele hits it from the left with the specific precision of someone who identified the cracked structural point in the two and a half seconds since he hit it and aims for the same place. The Chrysalis collapses. Not dramatically — it does not explode, does not scatter, does not produce any of the amber-light dispersal of the larger bloom events. It simply falls apart. The crystalline structure fragments on the tunnel floor and the corrupted matter dissolves into the stone and in approximately four seconds there is nothing left of it except a warm patch on the carved bedrock and a reading on Luka's sensor that drops from orange back to yellow within ten seconds of the collapse. They stand in the corridor in the aftermath. Seele is breathing carefully, the controlled breath that manages adrenaline. He is doing the same thing, less successfully. "The equipment case," she says. He looks at it. The case had slid off his shoulder during the impact with the wall. It is on the floor at junction ten, apparently intact. "Still have the sensor," he says. "Good." She looks at the junction twelve darkness ahead of them. "The Chrysalis came from twelve." "Yes." "Which means past twelve is not yellow-safe." "No," he says. "It's not." "Which means your plan of stopping at twelve was correct." He looks at her. She is looking at the darkness ahead, not at him, and her expression is the evaluating one — but there is something else running underneath it, something that the past four seconds of moving in coordinated combat in a narrow tunnel has produced, the specific something that happens when two people discover, under pressure, that they move well together. She has not looked at him since the moment of looking that preceded her moving left on his instruction. "You moved on my call," he says. "You had a plan," she says. "I assessed it in the time available. It was the best option." She picks up the equipment case from the floor. She hands it to him. "Next time come up with a better plan." He takes the case. "Next time don't get specifically targeted by a Chrysalis form." She looks at him. "I didn't choose to be specifically targeted." "I know. I'm noting it as something to account for next time." "By doing what." "By not having you as the primary point of contact until we know why it oriented on you." He looks at the darkness of junction twelve. "Svarog needs to know about this. And Gepard.
