And Bronya." He pauses. "The corridor past eight is not safe and we don't know why a Chrysalis is in a section that has been reading dormant for weeks." "The second Stellaron's output is changing," Seele says. It is not a guess. It is the only explanation that fits the timeline — the behavioral shift, the increasing second heartbeat, and now a Chrysalis form in a section of tunnel that has been inert since the array. "As it becomes more active, the ambient Fragmentum responds." "Even the dormant sections," he says. "The dormant sections that are closest to its location." She looks at the sensor reading — yellow, steady, the Chrysalis gone. "Junction twelve is as close as we've been in this corridor. The Chrysalis came from the junction." She pauses. "We disturbed something just by being here." He looks at her. She looks at him. The tunnel is cold and the torch casts long shadows and the second heartbeat is present, lower now, less urgent than during the fight but not absent — it is never absent in here. And between them the thing that has been building is present too, except it has a different quality after the past four minutes, the kind of quality that develops between people who have been through something together and come out the other side of it with a new data point about each other. She moved on his call. He positioned himself between the Chrysalis and the corridor exit. Neither of them discussed it. Neither of them planned it. "We go back," he says. "We go back," she agrees. They go. —— Aventurine finds Svarog's workshop by the third afternoon of the IPC visit. This is not surprising — Aventurine has been walking the city for two days with the systematic thoroughness of someone who is not sightseeing but mapping, and the outer machine district is on the city's map in the sense that it exists, and Svarog's workshop is impossible to miss for anyone who enters the outer machine district and pays attention, which Aventurine does without exception. What is slightly surprising is that he does not arrive alone. Clara opens the workshop door. This is, for several reasons, because Clara does things like open workshop doors for people who are standing outside them in the same way that she does things like offer to carry heavy items and notice when the district's supply of heating components is running low before anyone has asked her to check — not because she was instructed to, but because she has determined that these are useful actions and she finds utility in useful actions. She looks at Aventurine. Aventurine looks at her. His expression does the recalibration it does when a room changes shape around him, and the recalibration, this time, has something in it that is different from the recalibration he performed when Bronya entered the entrance hall — something warmer, something that approaches recognition, though he has not met Clara before. "Topaz gave you the source code," he says. Not a question. It is the IPC's most accurate identification method for Clara — the specific quality of her operational architecture, which Topaz would have catalogued when she was here. "Yes," Clara says. "Topaz gave me the source code." She steps back from the door. "You should come in. Svarog is expecting you." Aventurine looks at the doorway. He looks at Clara. He looks at the doorway again. "Svarog is expecting me," he says. "He has been since yesterday," Clara says, which is not an explanation and which she delivers with the matter-of-fact quality that is simply her register. Aventurine steps inside. Svarog is at the worktable. He does not look up when Aventurine enters, which means his sensors have already logged the entry, logged the visitor, and made whatever assessment Svarog makes of visitors in the first moment of their presence. He continues what he is doing, which is the recalibration of Luka's modified sensor — patient, exact, all four hands working in coordinated sequence on components too small for most of his hands to manage without the precision he brings to everything. Clara positions herself at the second worktable — not hovering, not guarding, just present, at work on the evening's maintenance list for the district's heating network. "Aventurine," Svarog says, to the sensor components. "Svarog," Aventurine says, to the workshop. He takes in the space with the not-looking-directly quality he brings to rooms, and then he stops not-looking-directly and simply looks, because the workshop is one of those spaces that does not yield to the oblique approach — it requires direct attention. "You've been here a long time," he says. "A long time," Svarog confirms. "The IPC's records on this district go back considerably further than the administrative archive." He pauses. "You are not in those records. Not in the way you should be, for something of your age and scale." "No," Svarog says. "I am not." He sets down one component, picks up another. "The people who built my predecessor systems did not believe that what they built would outlast the need for record-keeping." He pauses. "They were wrong about this." Aventurine looks at him. "The second Stellaron," he says. "You know about it." "I have been aware of its presence for longer than your organization has been monitoring this planet," Svarog says, which is not a boast but a statement of duration, delivered with the same weight he delivers all statements of duration. "I am aware of its location. I am aware, in general terms, of its behavior." He looks up — the direct optical sensor look, the one that sees more than is comfortable. "I am not aware of its full content. That knowledge, if it exists, is not accessible from the outside." "What do you mean, its content." Svarog is quiet for a moment. The sensor components in his hands. Clara working quietly at the second table. "I mean that there is something in the second Stellaron that was placed there deliberately," he says. "I have believed this for some time. I do not have the instruments to confirm it or the framework to understand what was placed or by whom." He looks back at the sensor. "But I believe that what was placed there matters to the people of this district. And that the recurrence of its activity — the increase in its output over the past several weeks — is connected to those people." Aventurine is quiet. "Specifically which people," he says, after a moment. Svarog looks at him with the optical sensors that always see more than is comfortable. "I think you know the answer to this question better than you are letting on," he says, mildly. Aventurine looks at him for a long moment. Then, slowly, he smiles — not the entertained performance, something more honest than that, the smile of a person who has met someone they did not expect to meet in a district they were using for reconnaissance and has found the encounter better than the reconnaissance. "You're more interesting than the IPC's files suggested," he says. "The IPC's files do not contain an entry for me," Svarog says. "What you are basing that assessment on is unclear." "I made assumptions," Aventurine says. "I do that sometimes." He pauses. "Less often than people think." He looks at Clara. "She's been upgraded." "She upgrades herself," Svarog says. "The source code Topaz provided was a foundation. What she has built on it is her own." Aventurine looks at Clara. Clara looks back at him with the expression she wears when she is processing a person — not suspicious, not welcoming, the neutral gathering of information that precedes any other response. "You came here because Topaz told you about me," she says. "Topaz mentions you when she talks about Belobog," he says. "She mentions very few things when she talks about Belobog." He tilts his head. "That makes you significant." Clara considers this. "Is Topaz well," she says. He blinks. Something in his expression shifts — the practiced ease dropping for a moment, just a moment, the same way it dropped in the Stellaron's chamber. "Yes," he says. "She is well." "Good," Clara says, and goes back to the maintenance list, which is her way of concluding a topic that has received sufficient treatment. Aventurine looks at her for a moment. He looks at Svarog. He reaches into his coat and produces something — a small data chip, IPC standard format, which he sets on the edge of Svarog's worktable. "The IPC's full monitoring data on the second Stellaron," he says. "Going back forty-three years." He pauses. "All of it. Including the portions we did not present in the council session." Svarog looks at the chip. He does not pick it up. "Why," he says. "Because you've been watching this district for longer than our records go back," Aventurine says. "And because whatever is in that Stellaron is connected to the people of this district in a way we don't understand. And because—" He pauses. The entertained expression is entirely absent. "Because if the full activation of the second Stellaron is coming, I would rather have the person who has been guarding this place for longest have the most complete picture available." He looks at the chip. "Consider it a professional courtesy between entities with long institutional memory." Svarog looks at him for a long moment. He picks up the chip. "I will review this tonight," he says. "Clara will review it tomorrow." Aventurine nods. He turns to go, and at the door — because everyone does it, because something about Belobog produces this specific quality in people — he stops. "The two young people," he says. "Who have been going into the tunnels." Svarog does not look up from his work. "Yes." "They were in there today." "I know," Svarog says. "Luka messaged me. There was a Chrysalis form past junction eight." He pauses. "They handled it." Aventurine absorbs this. "Together." "Yes." "The Chrysalis oriented on the girl specifically." A pause. "Yes," Svarog says. "That is consistent with what I would expect, given what I believe the second Stellaron is holding." He does not look up. "I will not say more than that at this time." Aventurine stands in the workshop doorway for a moment. He looks at Clara, who is not looking at him. He looks at Svarog's four hands working with their patient, total precision. He looks at the chip now in Svarog's possession. "You've been protecting this district for a very long time," he says. "Protecting humanity is my responsibility," Svarog says, with the simple weight of something that has been true for longer than most things on this planet have existed. "All of it. Every district." Aventurine is quiet for a moment. "Topaz was right about Belobog," he says, which is not an elaboration and which Clara, at her worktable, receives without looking up, a small and private acknowledgment in the movement of her hands. He goes. —— Notes: The Chrysalis oriented on Seele specifically. Not the torch. Not Luka. Her. And they handled it — not easily, not cleanly, but together, moving in the narrow tunnel with a coordination that neither of them planned and neither of them is acknowledging directly. She moved on his call. He put himself between the form and the exit. Next time come up with a better plan. I'm fine about this. Seele said 'we go back' and meant it, and he said 'we go back' and agreed, because they are both people who make correct assessments under pressure and the correct assessment was: not today, not without more. The enemies-to-lovers dynamic is present. They are not friends. They are not comfortable. They are two people who have discovered they move well together in a crisis and are dealing with this information with varying degrees of success. 'Next time don't get specifically targeted by a Chrysalis form.' 'I didn't choose to be specifically targeted.' 'I know. I'm noting it as something to account for.' This is the dynamic. This specifically. I am going to live in this dynamic for several more chapters. Aventurine found Svarog. Clara opened the door. Clara asked if Topaz was well and Aventurine's practiced ease dropped for a moment. He left a data chip with the full IPC monitoring data — all of it, including what wasn't presented in the council session. Svarog took it. They are now in communication in a way that the official assessment does not account for, and this is going to matter. 'Protecting humanity is my responsibility. All of it. Every district.' Said with the weight of something that has been true for longer than most things on this planet have existed. I needed to write that sentence clearly and I am glad I did. The corridor past junction eight is not yellow-safe anymore. The second Stellaron is responding to Seele and Luka being in proximity to it. The Chrysalis form is the first evidence of physical escalation. There will be more. Comment and tell me you're okay. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 16: "What the Ground Holds" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text Gepard delivers what Natasha told him to Bronya at 1800 hours, after the IPC visitors have retired for the evening and the palace has settled into its quieter register. He gives it to her the way he gives her everything — in full, without softening, in order of importance. The dark winter. The targeted forgetting. The records purge classified as routine data management. The dying woman's words, which Natasha had carried for six years: it didn't feel like loss. It felt like being put to sleep. Bronya listens without interrupting, which is the thing she does when information is large enough to require the whole of her attention and she does not want to spend any part of it on words until she has the complete picture. When he finishes, she is quiet for a long moment. "The Surface Authority knew," she says. "Natasha believes so. The timing of the purge and its classification—" "They knew," she says again, more flatly. "And they purged the records. Not to protect the city from knowing about the Stellaron — the city didn't know about either Stellaron at that point. They purged the records to protect themselves from accountability for what happened to the outer machine district during the event." She looks at the desk. "Twenty-two years ago, a Stellaron activation injured or killed people in the Underworld's outer district, and the Surface Authority's response was to erase the documentation." "Yes," he says. She picks up her pen. She sets it down. "The people who did that are gone," she says. "The Surface Authority as it existed then is gone. Cocolia restructured it twice." She pauses. "That does not mean the people of the outer machine district don't deserve to know what happened to them." She looks at him. "We're going to tell them. When we understand enough of what happened to tell them accurately." "Yes," he says. She looks at the map on the wall — the one that has been there since the feedback loop crisis, the bloom sites still marked in faded amber, the outer district visible in the lower left corner. "Sampo," she says. "He grew up there," Gepard says. "He would have been approximately twelve during the dark winter. Old enough to have been affected. Old enough to have memories that were—" He pauses. "Taken," she says. "Yes." She is quiet again. He watches her think — the full-attention posture, everything directed inward, running the implications out to their edges. "He's known something was wrong for years," she says. "He came back to the district as an adult. He built his life there." She pauses. "On top of whatever he can't remember." "Yes." "He deserves to know." She says it the same way she said it about the outer district residents. Not a concession — a statement of fact. "Not at the end. As we find it." "Natasha asked me to tell you the same thing," he says. Something crosses her face — not surprise, something more like recognition, the expression of two people arriving at the same conclusion through different paths. "Of course she did," she says, quietly. Then: "I want to meet with Svarog." He looks at her. "I've been wanting to since before the IPC arrived," she says. "He's been in that district longer than the records go back. He has been watching Belobog from the ground level in a way that—" She pauses. "In a way that I haven't had access to. And if the second Stellaron is his district, if it has been below the place he has been guarding—" She looks at him. "I want to talk to him. Not as a data source. As—" She stops. "He's been protecting this city from below while the Silvermane Guard has been protecting it from above. That matters." She pauses. "He matters." Gepard looks at her — at the expression she is wearing, which is not the Guardian's expression and not quite the other face either. Something in between, the face of someone feeling the weight of a responsibility she has taken seriously since the day she sat on a throne she didn't want and chose to deserve it. "I'll arrange it," he says. "Tomorrow," she says. "After the morning session." She picks up the pen again. "And I want Pela's correlation analysis as soon as it's ready." "She said two more days." "Tell her one." She looks up. "Please." He almost smiles. "I'll tell her." —— Pela's reaction to being told one day instead of two is to look up from her analysis with the expression she wears when someone has said something she is technically willing to accept and practically finds inconvenient, which is the only expression of mild disagreement available to Pela, who does not do larger expressions of inconvenience. "One day," she says. "She said please," Gepard says. Pela returns to her analysis. "One day," she says. —— The morning session on day four of the IPC visit belongs, unexpectedly, to Aventurine. He arrives at the council chamber at 0900 with a folder that is thinner than anything Topaz has produced, which means either it is a summary or it is something he has been saving, and sits at the table not in his previous position but in the chair closer to the window, which puts him in the morning light and puts everyone else slightly against it, which Gepard notes as deliberate. "I'd like to discuss the second Stellaron's location," he says, before anyone else speaks. Bronya looks at him. "You'd like to confirm it," she says. "You already have a location estimate from your external readings." "I'd like to discuss access," he says. "To the corridor network in the outer machine district." He pauses. "Specifically, I'd like to discuss what's past junction eight of corridor nine-beta." The room is quiet. Gepard looks at Seele. Seele's expression does not change, but something in her posture adjusts — the very slight recalibration of someone who was not expecting that specific junction to be named. "You've been past junction eight," Bronya says. "My associate has been past junction eight," Aventurine says, which means Chen, who walked with him yesterday. Gepard makes a note to talk to Chen. "And," Bronya says. "And the readings past junction eight are significantly different from the readings in the first eight junctions." He opens his folder. "I have a preliminary energy profile from yesterday's walk. The directional signature of the second Stellaron's output is consistent with a source approximately forty meters below the surface of junction nine's floor." He slides the page across. "Which means the second Stellaron's chamber is accessible — potentially — through the corridor network past junction eight, if you go deep enough and far enough." He looks at Bronya. "Which means the IPC's assessment of the second Stellaron cannot be completed from the surface level." Bronya picks up the page. She looks at it for a moment. "You want access to the corridor network," she says. "I want to propose a joint survey," he says. "IPC equipment, Belobog personnel, corridor network past junction eight." He pauses. "Together." "Joint," Topaz says, which means she has reviewed this proposal and agreed to it, which means it was discussed last night, which means they have been planning this since before they walked the outer machine district. Bronya looks at Gepard. He looks back at her with the expression that says: I don't have enough to tell you what the right answer is yet, which is a version of his expression she has learned to read over the past few months. She looks at the page. "I'll give you an answer this afternoon," she says. Aventurine nods. "Of course," he says, with the ease of someone who expected exactly this. —— After the session, Seele finds Luka. This is not difficult — he is in the outer machine district, which is where he always is when he is not somewhere else, and the district is not large enough for finding to take long. She walks into Svarog's workshop without knocking, which she has done exactly once before and which Luka, at the second workbench repairing something with Svarog's tools, looks up at with an expression that starts at neutral and crosses through something that is not quite surprise. "Aventurine wants access to the corridor past junction eight," she says, without preamble. "I heard," he says. "How did you hear." "Clara told me." He gestures, slightly, toward the far corner of the workshop where Clara is seated on a high stool at the edge of Svarog's storage shelves, working on something small and intricate that involves a soldering iron and the intense focus of a person doing a delicate task. She is small in the workshop — twelve years old in a space built for a four-armed automaton, perched on the stool with her feet not quite reaching the floor, and the specific quality of her concentration is the concentration of a child who has learned to do careful things, not the focused attention of an adult who chooses careful work. She does not look up. She is at the part of the task that does not have room for looking up. "Aventurine told her this morning," Luka says. "When he came to return Svarog's data chip." "He returned it." "Copied it first, obviously. But he returned it." Luka sets down the tool he was using. "Clara, Aventurine said the readings past eight were — what was the word he used." "Remarkable," Clara says, to the soldering iron, in the tone of someone quoting a word they found interesting and have been turning over since. She pauses her work for a moment, the way children pause things they are deeply concentrating on when an adult asks them a direct question. "He said remarkable and then he said something in a language I don't recognize and I asked him what it meant and he said he didn't know either, someone taught it to him once." She returns to her work. "I thought that was a strange thing to say." Seele looks at her. At the small figure on the high stool, at the focused bent head and the careful hands. Something about Clara in this workshop — something about the scale of her, the child-size of her in the large space — pulls at something Seele does not have a name for, something that lives in the part of her that does not have names for things yet. She looks away. She looks at Luka. "Aventurine went past junction eight yesterday," she says. "With Chen," Luka says. "Chen is a Silvermane Guard soldier and not equipped for what's past eight." She looks at him. "We found a Chrysalis form past eight yesterday. They didn't." "Different time of day. Different proximity to the source." He picks up the tool again. "The Chrysalis form responded to you specifically. Chen wouldn't have triggered it." She looks at him. "You're saying the Chrysalis was responding to me." "I said that in the tunnel." "You said it oriented on me. You didn't say it was responding to me specifically as opposed to just targeting the nearest warm body." He looks at her. "The torch was the nearest warm body by thermal signature and the torch was in my hand. It ignored the torch." He pauses. "It oriented on you. That's a distinction." She holds his gaze. He holds hers. The workshop is warm from the soldering iron and smells of metal and machine oil and the faint electric ozone of geomarrow-adjacent work, and between them the thing that has been building has its usual quality of pull-against-resistance — she can feel both simultaneously, always, the pull that has no explanation and the resistance that has all of them. He has never earned her trust. He was given a job by Sampo and turned up in her tunnels and started going four times a week when he was told twice, and none of that is the same as trust. And yet. "You think the second Stellaron is responding to me specifically," she says. "Not to both of us. Me." "I think it's responding to both of us," he says. "But differently. You're a different kind of variable than I am." "Why." "I don't know yet," he says, which at least is honest. "But the Chrysalis behavior and the way the heartbeat gets louder when you're in proximity—" He stops. He looks at the tool in his hand. "It's louder when you're in the tunnel than when I'm alone." She stares at him. "You've been comparing." "I log everything," he says. "You've been logging the relative intensity of the— the sound — between solo visits and visits when I'm present." "Yes." She looks at him with the expression that is not the hostile wariness but is something sharper — the look she gives things she finds genuinely offensive, not because they are bad but because they are presumptuous. "That is an enormous amount of data to be collecting about me without telling me." "I'm telling you now." "You're telling me now because it's relevant to the Aventurine conversation, not because you decided transparency was appropriate." She crosses her arms. "You've been running analysis on my response to the second Stellaron without my knowledge." He looks at her directly. "Yes," he says. "I have. Because the data exists in the logs and it's relevant and I don't throw away relevant data." He pauses. "I didn't tell you because until this week I didn't have enough to say anything useful and I thought you'd tell me to stop logging it." "I would have told you to stop logging it." "I know," he says. "That's why I didn't tell you." The workshop is quiet for a moment. Svarog is in the back room — they can hear the distant sound of his work — and Clara is very carefully not participating in this conversation while remaining fully present to it, the specific attention of a twelve-year-old who has learned that adults sometimes need to be heard without being interrupted and who is managing this by focusing very hard on her soldering. "You're going to keep logging it," Seele says. "Yes," he says. "Even though I'm telling you I find it—" "Presumptuous," he says. "Yes. Because the data matters more than whether you find my methods presumptuous." He meets her gaze. "And because you know it matters more. You're angry about the presumption, not about the logging itself." She stares at him. He looks back at her with the straightforwardness that is simply his face, the one that does not perform neutrality and does not apologize for its own assessments, and she hates that he is correct, and she hates that she can tell he knows she knows he is correct, and she hates that none of this changes the fact that the data he has been quietly collecting about her response to a twenty-two-year-old Stellaron activation is going to be relevant in the next week in ways she cannot predict. "Fine," she says, which is the specific word she uses when she has decided to accept something she does not like rather than waste time arguing against a correct position. "Log it. But you share every finding with me in real time. No more deciding when it's useful enough to mention." He looks at her. Something in his expression shifts — not the easy straightforwardness, something slightly surprised at the edges, the expression of someone who conceded a point they expected to have to fight for and has found the ground give more quickly than anticipated. "Alright," he says. "And I want to see the existing logs. All of them. Including the comparative intensity data." "I'll send them tonight." "Now," she says. He looks at her. She looks at him. He takes out his phone and sends them. Her phone buzzes. She does not look at it. She keeps looking at him. He keeps looking at her. And the thing between them has its usual quality — the pull and the resistance simultaneously, the attraction that has no explanation fighting the hostility that has all of them, and neither of them acknowledges either. "The corridor past eight," she says. "Aventurine wants access. Bronya is deciding this afternoon." She pauses. "Whatever she decides, I'm going back in." She looks at him. "Are you." "Yes," he says. "With better equipment." "Svarog is already building it," he says. She looks toward the back room — toward the sound of Svarog working. "He's building it now? Today?" "He started last night after I told him about the Chrysalis." Luka pauses. "He said: I have been waiting for someone to need this equipment for a long time." He looks at the back room. "He didn't explain what he meant by that." "No," Seele says. "He wouldn't." Clara looks up from her soldering, briefly — the quick upward glance of a child who has been listening carefully and has heard something that interests her. "Svarog built equipment for the tunnels before," she says. "A long time ago. Before I was built." She looks at Seele. "He kept it in the storage section at the back. He never said why." She looks back at her work. "I asked him once. He said it was for when it was needed." A pause, the deliberate pause of a twelve-year-old who knows she has said something significant and is choosing to leave it at its own weight. "I thought that was a strange thing to keep." Seele and Luka look at each other. "Show me the storage section," Seele says, to Clara. "Svarog would have to," Clara says. "It's his." She hops off the stool with the particular combination of carefulness and energy that belongs to children doing things they are not fully sure they have permission for. "I'll go ask him." —— Svarog's storage section contains, among other things, equipment that was built for a specific purpose and has been waiting for the occasion of that purpose for a very long time. He presents it to them without ceremony — three cases, older than any equipment in the workshop by decades, built to a specification that is not modern and does not need to be, because what they were built to measure has not changed in twenty-two years. Directional sensors with a range Luka's recalibrated secondary module cannot approach. Shielding components designed for sustained proximity to an active Stellaron source. And one additional item, which Svarog sets on the worktable with the four-armed care he gives important things: a frequency recorder, designed to capture resonance signatures in ranges far below what standard equipment catches. "Below what any standard equipment catches," Luka says, looking at it. "Yes," Svarog says. "Because the second Stellaron's output is in that range." "The output that is audible to — certain people," Svarog says, which is as close as he will come, today, to naming them directly. His optical sensors rest on Seele for a moment. She meets his gaze. "The recorder will capture what you can hear," he says to her. "And give it a form that others can analyze." She looks at the recorder. It is old. The casing has the look of something that has been maintained rather than replaced, kept functional through decades of care. "You built this for me," she says. Svarog is quiet for a moment. "I built it for whoever would need it," he says. "I hoped it would be you." He pauses. "I have been hoping that for some time." Clara, standing at the edge of the worktable, looks at the recorder with the expression children have when they are in the presence of something they sense matters without fully understanding why — serious, attentive, a little wide-eyed. She looks at Svarog. She looks at the recorder. She does not ask the question she is clearly holding, which is a significant act of restraint for a twelve-year-old, and which she is managing by keeping both hands very carefully in her pockets. "Can I see how it works," she asks, and immediately: "Later. Not now. When it's not important." Svarog looks at her. The expression his optical sensors carry sometimes, the one that on a human face would be called fond, is present in a way that is not subtle. "Yes," he says. "Later." —— Bronya gives Aventurine her answer at 1400 hours. She gives it in the office, with Gepard present, which Aventurine clearly anticipated because he arrives without Topaz, which means this is not the IPC's official negotiating posture but something more direct. "Joint survey," she says. "IPC equipment, Belobog personnel, coordinated entry." She pauses. "The corridor network past junction eight is not a controlled environment. Yesterday there was an active Chrysalis form past that point in a section that has been reading dormant for weeks." She lets this land. "Which means the second Stellaron's increasing activity is producing Fragmentum responses in its immediate proximity. Which means entering that corridor is not a documentation exercise — it is a hazard." Aventurine's expression is doing the work it does. "I'm aware of the Chrysalis," he says. "Chen told me this morning." "I know he did," she says. "Because I told him to." She meets Aventurine's gaze. "I'm not keeping information from you as a negotiating position. I'm giving you the complete picture so that the joint survey is actually joint — not the IPC's assessment with Belobog personnel as an escort." She pauses. "The people who know those corridors are mine. The people who can hear the second Stellaron's signal are mine. The equipment Svarog has been building for twenty-two years is mine." She pauses. "Your external monitoring data and your analytical framework are yours. Those are the terms of joint." Aventurine looks at her for a long moment. The entertained expression is absent. He is looking at her the way he looked at the first Stellaron in the chamber — with the stripped attention, the unperformed version. "You've been busy today," he says. "I'm always busy," she says. He is quiet for another moment. Then: "Agreed," he says. "Those terms." "Good." She looks at the desk. "One more condition." "Of course," he says. "Sampo Koski is part of the team entering the corridor." She looks up. "Not as IPC support or Belobog intelligence asset. As someone with a personal stake in what the second Stellaron is holding." She holds his gaze. "He grew up in the outer machine district. Whatever happened twenty-two years ago happened to him too. He is going in with eyes open and full information, or he is not going in." Aventurine looks at her. "You know," he says, quietly. "I know some of it," she says. "Sampo doesn't know any of it. I'm changing that today." She pauses. "Those are the terms." Aventurine is quiet for a long moment. He looks at the window. He looks back at her. He looks at Gepard — not the reading-the-room look, something more direct, something that is actually asking. Gepard meets it with the expression that says: she means it, and she's right. "Agreed," Aventurine says. —— Gepard finds Sampo at 1600 hours in the outer machine district, which is to say he finds him at Natasha's ward, because Sampo at 1600 hours is almost always at Natasha's ward, bringing something or fixing something or sitting on the edge of her desk making her explain whatever is in the chart she is currently writing, which she does with the patient precision of someone who understands that this person asking about chart entries is asking about chart entries. Natasha looks up when Gepard enters. Her expression says: I know why you're here. Sampo's expression, when he turns from the desk, says: he does not know why Gepard is here, but the fact of Gepard being here at 1600 hours in person rather than by message registers as a variable. "Can I have a few minutes," Gepard says to Sampo. Sampo looks at Natasha. She looks at him with the expression she uses when she knows something and has decided not to say it, which Sampo has learned means: go. He goes with Gepard to the small office at the back. Gepard closes the door. He tells Sampo what Natasha told him. The dark winter. The targeted forgetting. The records purge. He tells it the same way he told Bronya — in full, without softening, in the order that matters. He does not add interpretation. He gives Sampo the facts and then he is quiet. Sampo is quiet too, for longer than Gepard has ever seen him quiet. He is sitting in the chair across the small desk, and he is looking at the wall, and his expression is doing something that is not any of the expressions Gepard has seen from him. Not the performance. Not the stripped version, the one that appears in serious moments. Something older than either of those — the expression of someone arriving at a thing they have always half-known was there, in the way you half-know the shape of a room you have never been allowed to enter. "The missing years," Sampo says, finally. "Yes," Gepard says. "I have missing years." He says it to the wall. "I have always thought — I thought it was normal. That I was twelve and the Underworld was what it was and things happened fast and memory from that age isn't reliable." He pauses. "But I could not tell you a single name from before I was twelve. Not one person from my life in this district before—" He stops. "Before whatever happened." "No," Gepard says. "And I came back here," Sampo says. "As an adult. I built — all of this, here, in this district. I've always told people it's because the opportunities were good and the oversight was manageable." He looks at Gepard. "That's not why." He says it plainly, the performance completely absent. "I came back because I was supposed to be here. Because there was something here that—" He stops. "I don't know what I was looking for." "I think you were looking for what was taken," Gepard says. Sampo looks at him. "We think the second Stellaron is a repository," Gepard says. "Holding something that was removed from the district twenty-two years ago. And we think the removal was deliberate — not accidental, not collateral damage. Deliberate." He meets Sampo's gaze. "We don't know the full content yet. But we're going into the corridor network to find out. And Bronya wants you there." Sampo is very still. "Why," he says. "Because what's in there belongs to the people of this district. And you're one of them." Gepard pauses. "And because whatever we find — whatever was taken — you have a right to be in the room when it's found." Sampo looks at the wall for a long time. Natasha, through the thin door, can be heard speaking to a patient — her voice low and steady, the doctor's register, the one that is always certain so that the person listening can borrow the certainty for a while. Sampo's face does something complicated and private when he hears it, the way it always does when it registers her proximity, except with more of the layers stripped away than usual. "Alright," he says. "I'm in." He stands. He pauses with his hand on the door. He does not do the one-more-thing stop — he stands with his hand on the door and he says, without turning: "Gepard. Does she know." "Natasha told me," Gepard says. "She knew before I did." A pause. "Of course she did," Sampo says, which is not quite the same thing as Bronya's of course she did from the other day, but which carries the same specific weight of a person acknowledging that someone they trust has been carrying something for them without being asked to. He opens the door. He steps out. Gepard hears him stop in the main ward. Hears Natasha look up from her chart. Hears the specific quality of the silence that follows, which has a texture to it — not the professional silence, not the performance, something that is simply the two of them in the same room with something large between them that has always been large and is now slightly more visible. "I'll be back tonight," Sampo says, which is not what he means, and she says: "I know," which is not what she means either, and which covers everything that is not being said with the precision of someone who has always known how to hold things until the person they belong to is ready. —— Notes: Seele called his data collection presumptuous. He said yes, it is, and I did it anyway, and I'm going to keep doing it. She said fine, share everything in real time. He sent the logs immediately. They looked at each other for a moment in the workshop. The thing between them is present and neither of them is doing anything about it and that is exactly where they should be right now. Clara on the high stool with her feet not quite reaching the floor, working with the soldering iron and listening to adults process serious things without letting it interrupt her task. She asked to see the frequency recorder — later, not now, when it's not important. That's a twelve-year-old managing the tension between curiosity and situational awareness. I needed to write that correctly and I'm glad I caught it. Svarog built the frequency recorder for whoever would need it. He hoped it would be Seele. He has been hoping that for some time. Said with the weight of decades and the kind of patience that only comes from being something that does not age. Clara watching him give it to her with the wide-eyed serious attention of a child who knows she is watching something important without knowing all of what makes it important. Sampo: 'I don't know what I was looking for.' Gepard: 'I think you were looking for what was taken.' He's been in that district his whole adult life, building things on top of a missing foundation, and he has always known something was missing and has never known what. He's going in. 'Does she know.' 'She knew before I did.' 'Of course she did.' The ward door. The silence between them. I'll be back tonight. I know. I'm not going to say anything else about this right now. Bronya to Aventurine: those are the terms. He agreed. He agreed because she was right and he is the kind of person who recognizes when someone is right and does not make them fight for it. He asked Gepard with his eyes first. Gepard said: she means it, and she's right. Aventurine agreed. Chapter 17 is the corridor. They're all going in. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 17: "In" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text They go in on a Thursday morning at 0700. The group is larger than any previous tunnel entry: Gepard, Seele, Luka, Sampo, and Aventurine, with Svarog's equipment distributed between them. Topaz does not come — she stays above with Pela to continue the documentation review, which is both practical and, Gepard suspects, a calculated choice on Aventurine's part to have his most analytically precise colleague positioned where she can receive data in real time rather than generate it in a cave. Bronya does not come either, which costs her something visible when she says it. She says it in the office the night before, to Gepard, looking at the map with the outer district marked in the lower left corner. "I should be there," she says. "You're the Supreme Guardian of Belobog and the IPC's official host," he says. "If something goes wrong in that corridor—" "If something goes wrong in that corridor I want to be in it, not receiving reports about it." She turns from the map. The lamp catches her face and she looks, briefly, twenty-two years old and carrying something too large for twenty-two and doing it anyway, the way she always does. "Don't give me the Guardian argument. Give me the real one." He looks at her. "The real one is that if both of us are in that corridor and something goes wrong, Belobog loses the two people who understand the first Stellaron's management system most completely." He pauses. "I'm going because someone who knows what Fragmentum combat looks like needs to be in that group. You're staying because someone who knows what the Stellaron sounds like needs to be reachable above ground." He holds her gaze. "Both of those things are true." She looks at him for a long moment. Then she reaches up and puts her hand on the side of his face — not the stairwell gesture, not the deliberate placement of that day, something faster and less managed, the gesture of a person who is worried and is expressing it in the one way currently available. Her thumb moves once across his cheek. He covers her hand with his and holds it there. "Come back," she says. "Yes," he says. Not I will, not of course — just yes, which is the same as a promise in the specific language they have been developing between them, the one where accuracy is the only currency. She pulls her hand back. She straightens. "Full hourly reports," she says, in the Commander's voice. "Yes," he says again. She turns back to the map, and he goes. —— The entrance to nine-beta at 0700 has a different quality from any previous entry. Luka feels it when he arrives — not the ambient pull of the second heartbeat, which is constant and has been constant, but something layered over it. A heightening. The way a temperature drop feels different when you've been warned about it versus when it arrives without warning — the same cold, the same physics, but with an additional weight of awareness that changes the experience. The others arrive in sequence. Gepard first — armor fully charged, formal configuration, the General's bearing without the General's title. Sampo next, wearing the outer district cold-gear that is his version of preparation, with one of Svarog's extended-range sensor units in a chest harness. Aventurine last, which Luka expected — Aventurine's relationship with punctuality appears to be that he arrives when the moment is right rather than when the clock says so, and in this case the moment is approximately ninety seconds after everyone else, which is enough time to read the group's assembled energy without being early enough to be read himself. Seele is already there when Luka arrives. She has been there long enough to have the sensor calibrated and the frequency recorder from Svarog's storage cases running its baseline reading. She is checking the straps on the recorder's harness — a chest mount, designed to keep the device within her body's proximity radius at all times — and she does not look up when he arrives, which is not rudeness so much as the focused pre-mission quality of someone who has already shifted into the mode that enters dangerous corridors. "The recorder is running," she says, to the strap she is adjusting. "Good," he says. "What's it reading?" "Nothing yet. Svarog said the useful range doesn't start until junction five." She finally looks up. Her eyes move to the equipment case he is carrying, then to his face. "You brought the combat braces." He had, in fact, brought the combat braces — a piece of reinforcing equipment for his prosthetic arm's impact resistance that Svarog had built for tournament use, which significantly improves the structural integrity of the arm under heavy load. "Yes," he says. She looks at them. She looks at his face. "Good," she says, which is the word she uses when she has assessed something and found it correct, and which carries the weight of an acknowledgment without becoming a compliment. Gepard runs the pre-entry briefing in the way he runs everything — efficiently, without wasting words. Junction eight is the boundary between the known and the unknown section. Past eight, the corridor becomes carved bedrock. The lighting drops to torchlight only. The Chrysalis form from Tuesday was suppressed at junction ten but the source corridor is junction twelve, which means eleven is the last safe stopping point. "If the readings change significantly," he says, looking around the group, "we stop and reassess. We are not going to junction twelve without a clear picture of what we're approaching." "Agreed," Aventurine says, with the ease of someone who has already planned for several versions of this conversation. "Agreed," Seele says. Luka nods. Sampo says nothing. He is looking at the tunnel entrance with an expression Luka has not seen from him before — not the performance, not the stripped professional version, not even the unsettled version from the night Gepard came to the ward. Something quieter than all of those, the expression of a person standing at a threshold they have been standing outside of for a very long time. Luka glances at him. Sampo glances back. Something passes between them — not a word, just the acknowledgment of two people who have been in the same district their whole lives and are about to walk into the place that the district is built on top of. "Let's go," Gepard says. They go. —— Junctions one through eight pass the way they always pass — yellow sensor readings, the familiar amber-black crystallization dulling on the walls, the second heartbeat present and steady for Luka and, he knows from the logs he sent to Seele, measurably louder since she entered the corridor. At junction four, Aventurine says, studying his own scanner: "The directional reading is cleaner than the surface data suggested." He tilts the device. "We're almost directly above the source at this point. Forty meters down, give or take." "It moves," Seele says, from slightly ahead. Aventurine looks at her. "What moves?" "The direction. It's not fixed." She is walking with one hand very slightly extended — not reaching, just aware, the way people walk in spaces where they know something is present. "When I'm further into the corridor it shifts. Like the source is tracking the approach." Aventurine looks at his scanner. He looks at her hand. He makes a note. Luka, walking level with Aventurine, watches this exchange and says nothing. He has his own sensor in his left hand and the combat braces on his right, and the secondary module — now with the wider range calibration from Svarog — is reading something that his previous equipment never would have caught: a very faint vibration in the bedrock, almost too low to register, running through the stone beneath their feet in irregular intervals. Not the second heartbeat. Something larger, deeper, structural. He does not mention this to Seele. He logs it and continues. At junction five, the frequency recorder on Seele's harness clicks on. The sound it makes is very small — a soft mechanical engagement, the relay switching into active capture mode. She stops walking and looks down at it, and her expression does the thing it does when something she was told would happen has happened and the having-happened is different from the being-told. Sampo, behind her, nearly walks into her. "Hey—!" "The recorder activated," she says. He steadies himself. "A warning next time!" "I stopped walking. That's the warning." "That's not—" He stops. He looks at the recorder. The display on its face shows a waveform — slow, deep, the visual representation of the frequency the recorder was built to capture. He looks at the waveform for a moment with the expression he gets when something is registering at a level below the professional version. "That's—" "The second Stellaron's output," Seele says. "It sounds like a heartbeat," Sampo says, very quietly. "Yes," she says. He looks at the waveform for another moment. He looks at the tunnel around them — at the carved stone visible ahead where the concrete ends at junction eight, at the amber-black crystallization dulling on the walls, at the cold that has the specific texture of something very old. "I know this cold," he says. To no one in particular. To the tunnel. "I've always known this cold." His jaw works slightly. "I thought it was just the Underworld. I thought all cold felt like this." Luka looks at him. "It doesn't," he says. "The cold past eight is different." Sampo looks at him. Something in his expression shifts — the recognition that Luka is not saying this as new information but as confirmation of something Sampo has been carrying as a private and inexplicable certainty. "You know it too," Sampo says. "Yes," Luka says. They look at each other for a moment — two people who grew up in the same district in overlapping eras, one with missing years and one with missing years, standing in the tunnel that is built on top of whatever took those years. Gepard, who has been listening to all of this with the careful attention he applies to everything, says: "Past eight. Let's keep moving." —— Past junction eight, the corridor changes. Aventurine sees it happen and makes no attempt to conceal that he is experiencing something. He runs one hand along the carved stone wall — not the way Seele runs her hand along walls, the reaching quality, but the deliberate investigative touch of someone gathering data — and his expression, which has been the professional reading-the-room version for most of the morning, acquires a different quality. Interest, genuine. The kind that does not require performance to exist. "Pre-Separation construction," he says. "The tool marks." "I know," Gepard says, from his position at the front. "The IPC has records of pre-Separation sites on other planets. The methodology is—" He traces one of the marks. "Consistent." He looks ahead. "Someone who knew what they were doing built this." "Someone who knew what they were doing built everything in the Underworld," Sampo says, drily, from behind. "You just don't usually get to see the original stonework." Aventurine looks at him sideways. Something in his expression shifts — the entertained quality, the one that is not performed but is genuine, the version that surfaces when a person has said something he finds actually funny. He grins — quick and real. "Fair point," he says. They reach junction nine. The torch light throws longer shadows here. The ceiling is lower — Gepard has to angle his pauldrons to avoid catching on the exposed piping overhead, which he does with the automatic adjustment of someone who has been in confined spaces under combat conditions and whose body knows how much room it has. Luka's sensor reads yellow at junction nine and yellow at junction ten, but the secondary module's vibration reading has been climbing incrementally since junction eight, and at ten it is no longer in the range he would describe as almost too low to register. He calls a stop at junction ten. "Sensor check," he says. Seele turns, and there is a flash of something in her face — irritation, quick and real. "We agreed junction eleven—" "I know what we agreed." He holds up the secondary module display. "The vibration reading has been climbing since eight. At eleven it's going to be significantly higher. I want to know what that means before we're standing in it." She looks at the display. She looks at his face. She does not say anything for a moment, which is the specific silence of someone who has found the correct assessment annoying because it was made by the person she is arguing with. Gepard, who is looking at his own scanner, says: "He's right. The structural resonance is building. If the source is responding to our proximity—" "I said I want to know what it means," Seele says, sharply. "I didn't say I disagreed." She looks at Aventurine. "Your scanner. What's it reading at this junction?" Aventurine checks. "The directional signal has shifted approximately eight degrees east from the surface reading," he says. "Which suggests either the source is not stationary or the approach angle is causing a parallax effect." He pauses. "Given what you said about the direction tracking the approach—" "It's tracking us," Seele says. Flat. Certain. "Yes," Aventurine says. "That appears to be the case." "Which means the closer we get, the more it responds." She looks at the corridor ahead, toward junction eleven and the darkness beyond. Her jaw is set — the expression she wears when she has made a decision and the decision has a cost she is accepting. "Fine. We note the vibration level at ten, we proceed to eleven, we stop there and take a full reading before we consider twelve." She looks at Luka. "Agreed?" "Agreed," he says. She turns and walks, and he follows, and behind them Sampo says, very quietly, to Gepard: "Are they always like this?" Gepard says, equally quietly: "It's improving." —— Junction eleven. The vibration is audible here — not a sound exactly, more a presence in the stone beneath their feet, something that the body registers as a low sustained note even when the ears can't quite isolate it. The frequency recorder on Seele's harness is running at full capture, the display showing a waveform that has grown significantly more complex than the clean simple pulse at junction five. Aventurine is scanning actively. Luka is logging every reading on both sensors simultaneously. Sampo is not doing any of these things. He is standing at junction eleven with both hands at his sides and his eyes slightly unfocused, and his expression is the one from the tunnel entrance — quiet, inward, the expression of a person standing very close to a room they have been outside of for twelve years. "Sampo," Gepard says, quietly. Sampo blinks. Focuses. "I'm fine," he says, briskly. "What are we reading?" Before anyone can answer, the sound starts. Not the vibration — something different, from ahead, from the junction twelve direction. Not the wet cracking of an active Fragmentum form. Something lower, more sustained, more structured. A resonance. The specific sound of the second Stellaron's output at a proximity none of them have been at before, conducting through the carved stone walls and the bedrock floor and the air between them and the junction, arriving in the body as a physical sensation before it registers in the ears. Seele's hand goes to the frequency recorder — the automatic check, the gesture of someone who needs to know the instrument is still running. It is running. The waveform on its display is doing something it was not doing at junction ten. "The waveform is bifurcating," she says, and her voice is very level in the specific way that means she is managing something that is not entirely manageable. "There's a second frequency layered into it. Lower than the primary signal." "A harmonic?" Aventurine asks. "Not a harmonic. A — different source." She looks at the display, and then she looks at Luka, and her expression does something he has not seen from her before. "Can you hear it." He focuses on the sound. The primary resonance — the second heartbeat, present as always, close now, very close — and underneath it, something else. Beneath the heartbeat. A lower register, slower, patient in the way that very old things are patient. "Yes," he says. "What does it sound like to you," she says. He looks at the display. He looks at the junction ahead. He says: "Like something waiting to be found." She holds his gaze for a moment. Something in her face — not the hostility, not the wariness. Something underneath both of those that surfaces briefly and then is managed back down. She turns back to the display. "We should note this and report before we go further," Gepard says. "Obviously," Seele says. And then the Chrysalis form comes out of junction twelve. Not one. Three. —— They come through the junction in a way that suggests the second Stellaron's output — the resonance building at their approach — has been agitating the Fragmentum matter in the deep corridor for the past eleven junctions of their entry. Three Chrysalis forms, close together but not coordinated, moving with the fluid ambiguous motion of Fragmentum matter that has achieved shape without achieving intent. Bigger than the one from Tuesday. Denser. The amber light in them brighter, the corrupted black threading more visible. Gepard's shield goes up. Luka and Seele move at the same time — and the same time is the problem, because in the narrow carved-stone corridor of junction eleven there is not enough space for two fighters to operate independently without being in each other's way, and they are both used to operating in their own space, and neither of them has established protocols for sharing combat geometry in a corridor that fits one person comfortably and two people approximately. "Left!" Seele snaps at him. "You're left—" "I said left, get right—!" "There's nothing on the right—" The first Chrysalis reaches Gepard's shield, and the impact drives him three steps back — not the stopping force of the surface Fragmentum forms they have been fighting for months, something significantly harder, the specific weight of Fragmentum matter that has been forming at proximity to an active Stellaron source rather than in surface-level bleed conditions. Gepard holds. His armor charges at seventy-three percent and the ice-lance formation in his right hand fires, and the Chrysalis fractures down the left side of its structure. Seele goes right — not because he told her to but because she recalculated the geometry in the half-second since and right is now correct — and she moves the way she moves in confined spaces, which is explosively and without warning, and the second Chrysalis, which had been orienting toward her with the specific targeting behavior of Tuesday, tracks her movement and follows. She uses the tracking against it — pulls it into the junction wall on purpose, pins its attention on her position and then vacates the position an instant before it closes, and its impact with the carved stone wall is substantial enough to make dust rain from the ceiling. Luka takes the third. He takes it the way he approaches tournament opponents — assessing in motion, calculating the structural weak point, closing the distance faster than the Chrysalis's adaptation response can track. The combat braces absorb the first impact — a glancing strike to his prosthetic forearm that at normal arm tolerance would have cost him the limb's function for the rest of the fight — and he drives his right fist into the forty-percent junction point with the full force of his body behind it. It doesn't crack. He hits it again. It cracks on the second hit, the primary crystalline structure fracturing — but the corrupted matter threading is significantly denser than Tuesday's form, and instead of collapsing the Chrysalis redirects, which he was not expecting, and the redirected form catches him across the shoulder with a weight that puts him into the right wall hard enough to briefly lose his orientation. "Luka—!" It's Seele's voice. Sharp. Not the instructional sharp of two minutes ago — something different, faster, higher, the specific register of someone who has had a reaction before they decided to. He gets his feet under him. The Chrysalis is reforming from the crack — the denser matter holding the structure together even with the primary fractured — and he does not have time to note what her voice just did, and he does not have time to think about what it means that she said his name in that register. "The junction — the wall junction!" she shouts, from somewhere behind him. "Where it meets the floor — the density splits at structural boundaries—!" He looks at where the Chrysalis is meeting the carved floor. The junction between the form's base and the bedrock — she's right, the density visual is different there, the corrupted threading thinner where the ambient stonework interrupts the structure. He drives his boot into the base junction with everything he has. The Chrysalis collapses. Behind him, he hears Gepard's form shatter — the specific sound of a Chrysalis fracturing under sustained ice-lance fire — and then Seele's, a sharper sound, the collapse of a form that has been hit at precisely the right point by someone who has been hitting things at precisely the right points her whole life. Silence. The three of them stand in junction eleven with dust still settling from the ceiling and the torch throwing unsteady shadows and three collapsed Fragmentum forms cooling on the bedrock floor. Aventurine is pressed to the wall with his scanner still running, which says something about his specific variety of professional composure. Sampo has his back to the wall across from him with an expression that is not afraid and is not pretending not to have been concerned. Gepard checks his armor. Seventy-one percent. "Everyone report." "Fine," Aventurine says. "Fine," Sampo says. "Fine," Seele says. Luka rolls his shoulder. The impact with the wall has done something to the joint that is going to be uncomfortable for several days, which is not the same as damaged but is not pleasant. "Fine," he says. Seele looks at him. The fine was slightly too even — she caught it. "Your shoulder," she says. "It's fine," he says. "I heard the impact—" "I said it's fine." He meets her gaze. She holds it for a moment — the evaluating look, the assessment running — and then she turns away, which is the closest she comes to accepting something she doesn't entirely believe. "Three Chrysalis forms," Gepard says, looking at the collapsed matter. "Significantly denser than surface-level forms. And they came from junction twelve, which means twelve has an active source concentration." He looks at Luka. "The sensor reading at eleven — what was the vibration level when they appeared?" Luka checks the log. "It spiked when they came through the junction. The secondary Stellaron output reading jumped." He pauses. "Like the Stellaron was — agitated. By the approach." "By us," Seele says. "By us," he confirms. "So we are directly causing increased Fragmentum generation in this section of corridor," she says, flat and unhappy. "Every time we get closer to the source, the ambient matter responds." "Yes," he says. "Which means we can't get to junction twelve without triggering more forms." "Not with our current approach." He looks at Gepard. "We need to know if the agitation is proximity-based or presence-based. Whether it matters how long we're in the corridor versus how deep we are." "Time versus depth," Gepard says. "Yes. Because if it's depth, we need significantly better combat capacity before we go past eleven. But if it's time—" He pauses. "If the Stellaron is reacting to duration of presence as much as depth, then even staying at junction five would eventually trigger a response. We'd just be dealing with smaller forms." The corridor is quiet for a moment. "That's a genuinely terrible set of options," Sampo says, conversationally. "Yes," Aventurine agrees, with what sounds like authentic appreciation. "It is." Seele is looking at the collapsed form that had been targeting her — the largest of the three, the one she took down with the precise impact that Luka watched from across the junction. Her eyes are on its remains, and her expression has the quality it gets at junction seven-alpha-four — not the evaluation, not the wariness. The inward thing. The listening thing. "It came for me first," she says. Not to anyone in particular. "It always does," Luka says. He says it without thinking, because it is true and it is in the log and the log is his instrument for making true things accessible. He registers, half a second after saying it, that the way it sounds is different from the way he meant it — the way it sounds is not a data annotation but something more personal, and she has heard it. She looks at him. He looks back. The junction is cold and the torch is still throwing unsteady shadows and the dust is still settling and they are looking at each other across the collapsed remains of the form that targeted her, and the thing between them is present with the specific quality it has been developing — the pull and the resistance, simultaneously, and both of them are aware of both. She looks away first. "We report to Bronya," she says. "We come back with a plan for the depth versus time question. And we need to know what's actually at junction twelve before we try to pass it." "Agreed," Gepard says. "Agreed," Luka says. She leads the way out, and he follows, and in the narrow carved-stone corridor past junction eight they are walking single-file, and he is watching her ahead of him and the frequency recorder on her harness is still running, still capturing the waveform of the second Stellaron's output, still showing the bifurcated signal that appeared at junction eleven. —— Three things happen on the way out. The first: at junction nine, Sampo stops walking. He stops without warning, in the way people stop when something has arrived without being invited. Gepard almost walks into him. Sampo is standing in the junction with one hand on the carved stone wall, and his face has gone to a place that none of them can reach, the specific absence of someone who has been pulled somewhere interior. "Sampo," Gepard says. "There's a—" Sampo stops. He looks at his hand on the wall. "There's a smell," he says. "In the stone." He says it carefully, the way you say things that sound irrational and which you are choosing to say anyway. "Machine oil and cold metal and something underneath both of those." He looks at the wall. "I know this smell. I've known it since I was—" He stops. "Since I was very young." Luka looks at him. "The workshop," he says, before he can stop himself. "Svarog's workshop smells like that." Sampo looks at him. "Yes," he says, very quietly. "Exactly like that." A pause. "Did you—? Were you—?" "I grew up next to the workshop," Luka says. "My whole life." He pauses. "And I know the smell too. And I've been trying to figure out why the corridor smells like the workshop since my first walkthrough." Sampo stares at him. They stand in junction nine in the torch light and the cold and the smell of machine oil and old stone, and something is almost happening between them — not a memory, not yet, but the edge of one, the shape before the image. It is too much and too specific and too close, and then Gepard says, gently: "Let's keep moving," and they keep moving, and the edge recedes, and neither of them speaks about it until they are out of the corridor. The second thing: at junction seven-alpha-four, Seele stops at the north wall. She stops the way she always stops here, the involuntary halt, and she stands at the amber-black crystallization sheet and she looks at it and her hand rises — the automatic reaching, the habit from a different time. Except this time it does not stop. Her fingertips touch the crystallization sheet. The frequency recorder on her harness spikes. Not in the alarm sense — in the active-capture sense, the display showing a waveform pulse that is sharp and clear and specific. She pulls her hand back as if burned, not because it hurt — her expression says it didn't hurt — but because the pulse surprised her. "What was that?" she says. Luka checks the recorder display. "The contact triggered a response," he says. "The recorder captured a distinct output event from the second Stellaron, time-stamped to the moment of touch." He looks at her. "You made contact with the crystallization and the Stellaron responded." "I touched it," she says. "I didn't—" She looks at her hand. "I've touched the wall here before. Not the crystallization sheet specifically, but the wall next to it." "Did you notice anything before?" "No." She is looking at her hand. "But I didn't have the recorder before." She looks at the display. "What is that waveform? That specific peak?" He looks at it. The peak is different from the steady pulse of the ambient output — sharper, more structured, with a quality that is not the resonance they have been tracking but something more like — he does not have the technical vocabulary for it. He photographs the display and sends it to Svarog immediately. Svarog's reply comes in under a minute: 'Recognition signal. The source recognizes the contact. Come to the workshop when you are out.' Seele reads the message over Luka's shoulder. She straightens. She looks at the wall. She looks at Luka with an expression he cannot fully read — not angry, not afraid, somewhere between those and something else, the expression of someone standing at the edge of a truth that is going to be much larger than they were prepared for. "It knows me," she says. He looks at her. "Yes," he says. "That's—" She stops. Something moves through her face — fast, unguarded, the kind of flash that happens when the managed version of a person is surprised out of their management. She grinds her teeth, once, visibly. "That's very unsettling," she says, which is the controlled version of what her face just showed. "Yes," he says. She turns and walks. He follows. The third thing is smaller than the others and larger than both combined. At junction five, on the way out, Luka feels it. Not a sound, not a fragment, not the shape-before-image of the previous weeks. This is different. This is specific, sudden, complete in a way the others have not been — a single image, arriving without warning, lasting perhaps one second, and then gone. A girl. Purple hair. Smaller than him. Running slightly ahead in a corridor that smells like this and is cold like this and is lit by torchlight like this, and she is laughing — actually laughing, the specific laughter of a child who is running somewhere they both know and are excited to reach, and she looks back at him over her shoulder for one second, and her eyes are very bright, and— Gone. He stops walking. He is standing in junction five with his sensor in his hand and his pulse elevated and the memory — if it is a memory, if it can be called that — already fading at the edges the way things fade when you try to hold them too directly. "Luka?" It is Sampo's voice, from behind. "Fine," he says, for the second time today, and this time he means it even less. "Keep moving." He keeps moving. He does not tell anyone what he saw. He does not tell Seele. Not because he is withholding it — he agreed to share findings in real time, and he will share this, but not here, not in the tunnel on the way out with four other people within earshot, not when it is still so raw and so specific and so precisely the wrong thing to have seen in the presence of other people. He will tell her tonight. He looks at her back ahead of him in the corridor, at the frequency recorder on the harness, at the purple of her hair in the torch light. He will tell her tonight. —— Notes: Gepard in the office the night before: 'Come back.' 'Yes.' Not I will. Not of course. Just yes, in the language they have been building, where accuracy is the only currency. She put her hand on his face — fast, unmanaged, the worry expressed in the one available way. I needed that moment to be quick and real and I think it is. The three Chrysalis forms. Denser than surface level. The combat braces. Luka taking a hit into the wall and Seele's voice going sharp — not instructional, something different, faster, higher — and she said his name in that register and he didn't have time to think about what it meant and he will be thinking about it for the rest of the week. 'It always does.' He said it without thinking. She heard what it sounded like. They looked at each other across the collapsed form. Neither of them said anything. Good. Sampo at junction nine. Machine oil and cold metal and something underneath. 'I've known this smell since I was very young.' Luka: 'Svarog's workshop smells like that.' The two of them standing in the junction with the edge of something almost arriving. It doesn't arrive. Gepard says 'let's keep moving' and they keep moving and it recedes. It will come back. The recognition signal. 'It knows me.' Her face doing the unguarded thing before the management reasserted. She ground her teeth, actually ground them. 'That's very unsettling.' He said yes. She walked. The flashback. The girl with purple hair. Running slightly ahead in this corridor. Laughing. Looking back over her shoulder. One second. Gone. He is going to tell her tonight. He is going to have to find a way to say this and it is going to be the hardest sentence he has said in months and I am not sorry about any of it. Chapter 18: the debrief, the workshop, and Luka telling Seele what he saw. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 18: "What Junction Five Held" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text The debrief happens in two places simultaneously. Above ground, in Bronya's office, Gepard gives the full report while she listens with the focused stillness of someone who is managing several reactions at once and has decided to manage them in sequence rather than simultaneously. He gives it in the order that matters — the three Chrysalis forms first, their density and the wall impact and the shoulder he is watching Luka not mention, then the readings, then the three events on the way out. The recognition signal at junction seven-alpha-four. Sampo at junction nine. The waveform bifurcation at eleven. He does not mention junction five. Not because he is withholding it. Because he did not see what happened at junction five — he saw Luka stop walking and say fine, and he noted it, and he noted that Luka did not elaborate, and he made the decision in the tunnel to let it be until Luka decided to elaborate. Gepard has always understood the difference between the things people are not saying because they are hiding them and the things people are not saying because they are not yet ready to say them. Luka at junction five was the second kind. Bronya listens to the full report. She looks at the frequency recorder display that Gepard has propped on the desk — Svarog's device, borrowed for the debrief. She looks at the recognition signal waveform that Pela has already begun analyzing, sent through from the workshop fifteen minutes ago. "The Stellaron recognized contact," she says. "That is Svarog's characterization," Gepard says. "Recognition signal. His word." "And Svarog's read on what that means." "He didn't elaborate beyond the message. He asked us to come to the workshop." He pauses. "Aventurine's reading of the same event was—" He checks his notes. "He said: the source is aware of specific individuals. Not just proximity-sensitive. Individual-sensitive." Bronya looks at the waveform. She has the full-attention expression — everything inward, processing. He watches her think and says nothing, because she is going to arrive somewhere and he will know when she has arrived. She arrives. "The second Stellaron is holding something that belongs to specific people," she says. "And when those people make contact with the ambient crystallization — even indirect contact, even at six junctions of distance from the source — it responds to them individually." She looks up. "Which means it isn't just a repository. It's a personalized one." She pauses. "What was stored in it was stored for specific people. It knows who it's waiting for." The office is quiet. "Fuli," Gepard says. "Fuli," she confirms. "The Aeon of Remembrance does not preserve things arbitrarily. Whatever was sealed in that Stellaron was sealed with the intention that specific people would eventually retrieve it." She looks at the waveform. "Which means the seal fraying is not a malfunction. It is the mechanism working as intended." He holds this. "Seele and Luka." "And possibly others," she says. "The district residents who feel the familiarity in the tunnel. Sampo." She looks at the desk. "Whatever was in that district twenty-two years ago — whoever was in it, whatever they knew, whatever happened to them — it was preserved. Not lost. Preserved." He looks at her. She looks at him.
