They are sitting across from each other at the desk, in the chair arrangement that has been theirs for months, and the lamp between them and the city outside the north-facing windows and the frequency recorder showing its waveform, and the thing between them has the specific quality it gets in these moments — not the stairwell quality, not the construction chamber quality, but the quality of two people who are jointly holding something very large and finding that the joint-holding is, itself, a kind of warmth. "I want to go to Svarog," she says. He nods. "Today." She is already standing. "Now." He stands with her. —— Below ground, in Svarog's workshop, the other debrief is considerably less orderly. Aventurine is at the secondary worktable with his scanner and Svarog's data comparison materials, running his own analysis of the tunnel readings in a way that is systematic and entirely self-directed, which is how Aventurine apparently works when he is not in a room where performance is required — quietly, thoroughly, without announcing what he is doing. Sampo is sitting on the floor with his back against the wall under the storage shelves, which is not a dignified position and which he has taken because he did not want to sit at a table and did not want to stand and the floor was the remaining option. Clara is at her stool, doing something with the frequency recorder's display data that Luka did not authorize and does not object to, because Clara applying herself to a problem is not something that benefits from objection. Seele is standing at the worktable where Svarog is reviewing the recording data. Luka is across the workshop. This is not accidental. The space between them is more deliberate than it looks — not angry, not cold, but the specific distance of two people who have a conversation to have and have not had it yet and are both aware of this, and the awareness is creating a kind of managed radius that neither of them is ready to close. "The recognition signal," Svarog says, to Seele, who is watching the waveform on the recorder's display. "When you made contact with the crystallization at junction seven-alpha-four." He adjusts the display so the signal peak is centered. "This is a response pattern I have not seen from the second Stellaron before. In forty-three years of monitoring its ambient output, this specific waveform has not appeared." He pauses. "It appeared when you touched the wall." "You've been monitoring it for forty-three years?" Seele says, with a sharpness that is not quite accusatory but is definitely pointed. "Forty-three years and you didn't think to mention to anyone that there was a second Stellaron underneath the outer machine district?!" "I mentioned it to the administrative board," Svarog says, with the immovable patience of something that has been waiting to say this for approximately twenty years. "The administrative board!" She stares at him. "The administrative board that lost the records?!" "The same," Svarog says, entirely unruffled. She makes a sound that is not quite a word — exasperated, sharp, genuine, the sound of someone who has been presented with institutional failure of such spectacular proportion that language becomes briefly inadequate. Clara looks up from the recorder display, assesses the temperature of the room, and looks back down at the display with the specific focus of a twelve-year-old who has learned when to be invisible. "I also mentioned it to the Trailblazers," Svarog adds, which is new information, and which lands in the room with a specific quality. Seele stares at him. Aventurine looks up from his work. Sampo, from the floor, says: "The Trailblazers were here?" "Several months ago," Svarog says. "They were investigating the first Stellaron. I made them aware of my observations regarding a possible secondary source. They did not have time to investigate further before leaving the planet." He pauses. "I gave them what data I had. I hoped they would return it to relevant authorities." Another pause. "They appear to have done so through indirect channels." Aventurine looks at the scanner in his hands. He looks at Svarog. "The IPC received a fragmentary field report from a Trailblazer contact approximately six weeks ago," he says. "Unverified. Secondary Stellaron possible, outer machine district, no location data, no readings." He pauses. "It was one of the reasons for the letter." "Yes," Svarog says. "I suspected as much." The workshop is quiet for a moment while everyone processes the specific topology of how this information traveled — from Svarog to the Trailblazers to an IPC field report to a diplomatic letter to a palace war council — and the number of hands it passed through before arriving back in the district where it started. "Efficient," Sampo says, from the floor, drily. "Extraordinarily efficient," Aventurine agrees, with the genuine version of the entertained expression. Seele looks at Svarog. The sharp edge of her voice has not left — it has just been redirected, the way a blade is redirected by something it cannot cut through. "The recognition signal," she says, returning to the point with the focus of someone who does not let tangents displace what matters. "What does it mean that the Stellaron recognized me specifically?" "It means," Svarog says, carefully, "that what the second Stellaron is holding has your signature in it. The way a letter has the handwriting of the person who wrote it." He pauses. "The source recognizes the hand." She stares at him. "My signature." "Yes." "Something that belonged to me." Her voice has changed — not the sharp edge, not the wariness, something quieter. "Something the Stellaron was given that was mine." "Something that was yours," Svarog says. "Yes." She looks at the waveform for a long time. And then, very quietly, she says: "Then why is the second frequency there? In the bifurcated signal at junction eleven?" She looks at Svarog. "The recorder caught two frequencies at eleven. Not one. The second one was lower, slower. Different from the primary signal." She looks across the workshop — directly at Luka, who is standing at the far end. "He heard it too." Everyone in the workshop looks at Luka. Luka looks at the waveform display. "Yes," he says. "I heard it." "The second frequency has your signature," Svarog says. "As the first has hers." The workshop is very quiet. "Two signatures," Aventurine says, slowly, as if he is testing the weight of the conclusion before committing to it. "Two people's — what — memories? Identity markers? — stored in a single Stellaron repository." He looks at Svarog. "Together." "Together," Svarog confirms. The word lands differently than any other word in the workshop has landed today. Seele is looking at Luka. Luka is looking at Seele. The distance between them — the managed radius, the deliberate space — is the same distance it was five minutes ago, but it has a different quality now, the quality of a space that has been named rather than simply maintained. "Svarog," Luka says, from across the workshop. His voice is even. "The recognition signal. When it fired — when she touched the crystallization — what exactly did it recognize?" "The signal pattern suggests recognition of origin," Svarog says. "The source recognizing the point of origin of what it holds." He pauses. "Like a river recognizing the rain it came from." Luka looks at Svarog for a long moment. Then he looks at Seele. "I need to tell you something," he says. "About junction five." —— He tells her outside. Not because the workshop doesn't have space for a private conversation — it doesn't, given its current population, but that is not the reason. The reason is that what he saw at junction five is not a data point, is not a log entry, is not something that belongs to a room full of people and scanners and waveform displays. It is small and specific and not yet fully his to give and he wants to give it in the cold of the outer district where the second heartbeat is ambient and the corridor is three minutes' walk away and there is no performance requirement. They stand at the side of the workshop building, in the narrow gap between the workshop wall and the neighboring generator housing, which is not scenic but is private. The cold of the outer machine district is specific here — the textural cold, the one that lives in the corridors — and the district hum is ambient, the generators cycling through their afternoon pressure checks. She is looking at him. He is looking at the generator housing. "At junction five," he says. "On the way out." He pauses. "I had a flash." She is very still. "What kind of flash." "Visual. Specific." He looks at the generator housing. "A girl. Purple hair. Running slightly ahead of me in the corridor. Laughing." He pauses. "Looking back over her shoulder." He looks at her. "Her eyes were very bright. And then it was gone." Seele is not breathing the way she was thirty seconds ago. "How old," she says. "Young. Eight, nine. Maybe ten." He meets her gaze. "Younger than she is now." She stares at him. Her jaw works slightly. Something in her face is doing what it does when she is managing something that is significantly larger than the management available to her — not the hostile wariness, not the evaluation, the raw thing, the young thing, the version that surfaces when Seele runs out of surface. "You saw me," she says. "I think so," he says. "I don't know for certain. But—" He pauses. "She was running somewhere we both knew. In the corridor. And she was excited to get there." He looks at her. "And she looked back at me. The way you look back at someone you're running ahead of because you know they're going to keep up." She looks at him. He looks at her. The generator housing hums. The outer district is cold and specific and the second heartbeat is below them, steady, patient, waiting with the particular patience of something that has been waiting for twenty-two years and has calibrated itself to the duration. "I looked back," she says. Not a question. Just — placing the image in something. "Yes," he says. She breathes. In and out, the controlled breath, the one that manages something. "I used to do that," she says. "As a child. Running ahead." She says it to the workshop wall. "I was always faster than the people I was running with. I always got ahead." A pause. "And I always looked back. To make sure they were still there." She looks at him. "I stopped doing it when I was — approximately ten." She says it with the precision of someone who has located a data point they did not know was data. "When I stopped running ahead of people." She pauses. "When there was no one to run ahead of." Luka says nothing. "You were there," she says. "In the corridor. That's what you're saying." "I was there," he says. "I think I was always there." She looks at him for a long moment. The hostile wariness is present — it is structural, it is always present — but it is doing significantly less work than it usually does, because the thing it is defending against is not him, is the size of what is being assembled between them, the shape of the photograph that is becoming more visible with every fragment. She is not afraid of him. She is afraid of the scope of what they are finding. He understands this. He is also afraid of the scope of what they are finding. "Luka," she says. "Yes." "Why didn't you tell me in the tunnel." "Because there were four other people in the tunnel," he says. "And because it was — specific. And I wasn't ready to say it in front of an audience." She looks at him. She looks at the workshop wall. She makes a sound that is not quite a laugh — something adjacent to it, wry and a little desperate, the sound of a person who has been managing something alone for a very long time and has just discovered they do not have to manage this particular piece of it alone. "You should have told me immediately," she says. "I know," he says. "Real time. We agreed real time." "We agreed data real time. This was—" He pauses. "This was not data." She looks at him, and her expression does the complicated thing, the layered thing, the one that has the hostile wariness on top and the young uncertainty beneath that and beneath that, deeper, the thing that surfaces at junction seven-alpha-four and at the branch going left and when the frequency recorder captures a bifurcated signal that has two signatures in it, not one. "Fine," she says. The word she uses when she has accepted something she does not want to accept because it is correct. "But next time—" "I know," he says. "I'm serious—" "I know!" he says, with more force than he intended, and it surprises both of them — the volume of it, not angry, just present, genuine, the sound of a person who has stopped managing the level. He pauses. "I know," he says again, quieter. "Next time I'll tell you immediately. Regardless of audience." She stares at him. He stares at her. The force of the I know is still in the air between them — not hostile, not combative, just real, a real thing said in a real voice. "Alright," she says, and something in her posture adjusts — very slightly, the fraction of an adjustment that means something has been established, some piece of ground clarified, the way the terms of a negotiation become legible when both parties stop performing their positions. They stand in the gap between the workshop and the generator housing in the outer machine district's specific cold. "She was laughing," Seele says, after a moment. "Yes," he says. "That's—" She stops. She looks at the generator housing. Her jaw does the small tight movement again, the one he has been cataloguing. "I haven't thought of myself as someone who laughed a lot. When I was young." She pauses. "It was not — a particularly funny time." "You were running," he says. "Toward something you wanted to reach. That's a specific kind of laughing." He looks at the wall. "The kind that doesn't need the rest of your life to be funny." She looks at him. The expression does the thing — the flash of something unguarded, fast, present for one second. She looks away before it can fully surface. "Go back inside," she says. "Svarog will want to recalibrate the secondary module for the depth-versus-time test." "I know," he says. She walks. He follows, and the workshop door opens, and inside Clara looks up from the recorder display with the quick assessing glance of a twelve-year-old who has been listening to everything through a very thin workshop wall and has questions she has decided not to ask, and Aventurine looks up from his scanner with the not-looking-directly look that confirms he also heard everything through the very thin workshop wall and has drawn several conclusions he is also not announcing. Sampo is still on the floor. He looks up when they come in, and his expression has the specific quality it has had since junction nine — the inward thing, the adjacent-to-grief thing, the thing that has been developing since Gepard told him what Natasha told him. "The two signatures," he says. "In the Stellaron." "Yes," Luka says. "Is there — is there a third?" He says it carefully, the way you ask something when you are afraid of both possible answers. "In the recording. Could there be other signatures in the Stellaron? From other people?" The workshop is quiet. Svarog looks at the recorder display. He looks at Sampo. His optical sensors carry the expression that on his face serves for several human expressions simultaneously — careful, honest, the decision to say a true thing. "The recording captures what the Stellaron projects toward the surface," he says. "What the Stellaron holds internally is considerably more than what projects." He pauses. "The two signatures are the loudest. The ones nearest the surface of what is held." Another pause. "Below them, there may be others. The recorder cannot reach that depth." He looks at Sampo. "But I believe there are others." Sampo nods. Very slowly. "People from the district," he says. "People who were in the outer machine district twenty-two years ago," Svarog says. "Yes." "And their—" He stops. "Their memories. Their relationships. The things they lost." "Yes," Svarog says. Sampo is quiet for a long moment. He is looking at the workshop floor. The district hum is ambient outside and the workshop is warm with the heat of machines and the afternoon is fading toward evening, and Sampo Koski is sitting on the floor of Svarog's workshop looking at the ground with the expression of a person standing at the edge of something enormous and taking the time to feel the edge before stepping toward it. "I want to go back in," he says. "To the corridor." "Yes," Svarog says. "You will." "Not today," Sampo says. "No," Svarog says. "Not today." —— Bronya and Gepard arrive at the workshop at 1700 hours. Bronya has not been to Svarog's workshop before. She comes through the door and the first thing she does — before the greeting, before looking at the equipment, before any of the things she would do in any other professional context — is look at Svarog. She looks at him the way she looks at things she is reassessing in real time, the full-attention look, and what she is reassessing is the question of what this is, this machine in this workshop in this district, this entity that has been here longer than the records go back, that filed a report the administrative board ignored, that built equipment twenty-two years ago for the moment when it would be needed. Svarog looks back at her with the optical sensors that see considerably more than is comfortable. Clara, on her stool, watches this happen with the serious wide-eyed attention she brings to significant things, her feet not quite reaching the floor, her hands in her lap. "Guardian Bronya," Svarog says. "Svarog," she says. And then, simply, honestly: "I owe you an apology." The workshop is very quiet. "You owe me nothing," Svarog says. "You were not Guardian when the records were purged. You were not Guardian when the report was ignored. You were not Guardian when the second Stellaron activated and the district carried what happened to it alone." He pauses. "None of that was yours." "The city is mine now," she says. "All of it. Including what it has not been told." She looks at him. "I want to fix that." Svarog regards her for a long moment. His four hands are still — the sharp-intake-of-breath equivalent. "Yes," he says. "I believe you do." Clara is looking at Bronya with an expression that is not the wide-eyed seriousness anymore. Something more evaluative — the child's version of Seele's assessment look, quick and instinctive and already filing conclusions. Bronya notices, and she turns to look at Clara, and something in her expression adjusts — not the Guardian's posture, the other one, the slightly-younger one that comes out in lamplight and in private. "You're Clara," she says. "Yes," Clara says. "Topaz mentioned you," Bronya says. Clara looks at the floor. Then back up. "Topaz is kind," she says, carefully, in the tone of a child choosing an accurate word for a complicated thing. "She does not always show it but she is." She pauses. "I hope she comes back." Bronya looks at her for a moment. "I think she will," she says. Clara nods, accepting this, and hops off the stool to make room at the worktable for the adults who need it, which is a very Clara way of concluding an interaction that has been sufficient — not withdrawn, just complete. Gepard, who has been watching all of this from the doorway, closes the door behind him and crosses to the worktable. He looks at the recorder data. He looks at the waveform. He looks at Luka, who is at the secondary worktable, and at Seele, who is at the other end of the same table, and at the distance between them, and he files the distance in the part of his mind that tracks the human weather in rooms. "The depth versus time question," he says. "Where are we on that?" "Working on it," Luka says. He pulls up the secondary module data. "The vibration reading climbed consistently with depth, but I cross-referenced the time-stamp log against the formation event — the three Chrysalis forms appeared at junction eleven approximately twenty-two minutes after our entry at junction one. Which is consistent with a time-based agitation mechanism rather than purely depth-based." "Meaning the longer we're in the corridor, the more the Stellaron responds," Gepard says. "Regardless of depth. Yes." He looks at the data. "The depth makes it faster — closer proximity accelerates the agitation. But time is also a factor." He pauses. "Which means a rapid entry to junction twelve — moving through at speed rather than stopping to take readings at every junction — might produce a different result than our current approach." "More dangerous," Seele says, from the other end of the table. "More dangerous," he agrees. "But possibly more successful." "Those aren't mutually exclusive," she says, with the flat precision of someone noting an obvious thing. "No," he says. "They're not." She looks at him across the table. He looks back. The distance is still maintained, the managed radius, but it has the quality from outside the workshop — the named quality, the clarified quality, the piece of ground that has been established. "I want to run the rapid approach," she says. "With combat formation pre-established. We know what the forms look like past eight now. We know the wall junction weakness." She looks at Gepard. "If we move at speed and we know what we're fighting—" "The forms past twelve will be worse than eleven," Gepard says. "I know that." "Significantly worse." "I know that too." She meets his gaze with the patience of someone who has already factored this in and resents being told it. "I'm not suggesting we go tomorrow. I'm suggesting we work toward a rapid approach as the methodology for the next entry." She pauses. "Which requires knowing the enemy formations at twelve and beyond before we go in." She looks at Aventurine. "Your scanner data from yesterday — you went as close as twelve with Chen. What were the readings?" Aventurine, who has been very professionally not participating in the Luka-and-Seele portion of the afternoon and is now being directly addressed, looks up. "The readings at junction twelve were significantly elevated above eleven," he says. "The directional signal was clear enough to give a depth estimate of approximately thirty-five meters from junction twelve's floor — so fifteen meters closer than from junction eleven." He pauses. "I did not have Svarog's equipment. With the proper instrumentation, the reading at twelve could give us a formation estimate." "A scout run," Luka says. "Fast entry, junction twelve only, readings and retreat before the time-based agitation reaches formation threshold." "Twenty-two minutes was today's formation threshold," Seele says. "With a five-person group. The agitation may scale with the number of people present." "It scales with our presence specifically," Luka says, and they both know what our means, and neither of them says it out loud in this room full of people, and the not-saying-it is louder than saying it would have been. Gepard watches this exchange with the same expression he has been wearing since he walked into the workshop — the one that is not quite the almost-smile and is not the professional attention, something in between, the expression of a man who has been cataloguing a particular kind of human weather for several months and has developed a finely calibrated instrument for reading it. He says nothing. "A scout run at junction twelve," he says. "Two people. Fast. Full combat readiness." He looks at the room. "Who and when." The room's answer is obvious to everyone in it. Nobody says it, because nobody needs to. Sampo says, from the floor, because he has been on the floor this entire time and nobody has suggested he move: "You know, for a group of people who are very good at not saying things, you're all remarkably easy to read." Clara makes a sound that is very brief and very quiet and is absolutely a suppressed laugh. She does not look up from whatever she is doing. Aventurine's mouth does something that is also not quite a laugh but is adjacent. Seele looks at Luka. Luka looks at Seele. "Tomorrow," she says. "Tomorrow," he agrees. —— Later that evening — well after the workshop has emptied, after Bronya and Gepard have returned to the palace, after Aventurine has gone back to the guest quarters with a significant quantity of new data, after Clara has gone home and Sampo has gone to the ward — Svarog is alone in the workshop. He does not experience alone the way humans do. Aloneness for Svarog is simply the state of the workshop when it does not contain other people — the machines still running, the sensors still recording, the data still accumulating, the second heartbeat still present below in its patient ongoing way. He looks at the frequency recorder's display. The bifurcated waveform from junction eleven. Two signatures. The one that responded to Seele's touch at junction seven-alpha-four. The scout run tomorrow that will bring both of them to junction twelve, which is as close as any living person has been to the second Stellaron's chamber. He has been watching this approach for a long time. He does not rush it. Rushing is not in his operational vocabulary. He has been here longer than the records go back, longer than the district's oldest residents can remember, long enough to have seen what happens when things that should unfold slowly are forced to unfold fast. He has filed reports. He has built equipment. He has maintained the workshop and the sensors and the second heartbeat's monitoring and the knowledge of what is below — carrying it carefully, the way you carry something fragile, not tightly, with the attention that allows it to exist intact until it is needed. He looks at Clara's workstation. At the tools she left neatly arranged, the small hands' version of the workshop's organization. She has been with him for eight months and in that time she has learned the workshop the way Luka has learned the tunnels — not because she was taught, because she absorbed it, because the knowledge was already somewhere in her and the workshop gave it occasion. He has a theory about this that he is not yet ready to articulate. He looks at the recorder waveform. He looks at the storage cases where the equipment had been waiting. He looks at the workshop door, through which tomorrow morning two young people are going to walk toward something that has been waiting for them for twenty-two years. He continues his work. Outside, in the district, the generators cycle through their evening pressure check. The corridors are cold and specific and the second heartbeat is below, patient, counting whatever it counts toward the moment the seal comes fully apart. It is counting faster now. —— Notes: 'She was laughing.' 'Yes.' 'I haven't thought of myself as someone who laughed a lot. When I was young.' The thing he said about the specific kind of laughing that doesn't need the rest of your life to be funny — I wrote that sentence and then sat with it for a while. He said I know! with too much force and surprised both of them. The volume was not anger, just presence, a real thing said in a real voice. She stared at him. He stared at her. Something was established. I am not going to be coy about how much I enjoy writing these two. 'Fine.' The word she uses when something is correct and she doesn't want to accept it. He said it first at junction eleven and now she's saying it. They are picking up each other's vocabulary without noticing. That's going to get louder. Sampo on the floor of the workshop for the entire scene. 'Is there a third signature?' Said carefully, the way you ask something when you're afraid of both possible answers. Svarog: 'I believe there are others.' The way Sampo nodded, very slowly. He is going back in. Not today. Clara on the stool with her feet not reaching the floor, suppressing the laugh at Sampo's comment, not looking up. She heard everything through the workshop wall. She has questions she is not asking. She will ask them eventually, at a time of her choosing. Bronya in the workshop: 'I owe you an apology.' Svarog: 'You owe me nothing.' She said the city is mine now, all of it, including what it has not been told. He said I believe you do. That exchange is the beginning of something that will take a long time to fully become what it is. Svarog alone in the workshop at the end, looking at the recorder waveform. He has been here longer than the records go back. He does not rush. He has a theory about Clara he is not yet ready to articulate. This sentence will matter later. Not soon. Later. Tomorrow they go to junction twelve. Two people. Fast. Comment and tell me you're ready. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 19: "Junction Twelve" Chapter Text They go in at 0600, before the district wakes up properly. This is Seele's call, made the night before via a text message that arrived at 2217 hours: 'Earlier than yesterday. Before the district activity increases. Less ambient variable.' Luka had read it, looked at the clock, and sent back: 'Agreed. 0600.' She had not replied, which meant she had already known he would agree and had moved on to the next preparation. He was at the corridor entrance at 0547. She was already there. They do not speak for the first minute. She is running the frequency recorder through its baseline calibration — a process she has done enough times now that her hands move through it with the automatic confidence of something learned by repetition — and he is checking the combat braces and the sensor array and the secondary module's range settings, which Svarog recalibrated at 0530 this morning when Luka knocked on the workshop door and Svarog opened it without apparent surprise, as if he had been expecting the knock. The new equipment is different from anything Luka has carried into the tunnel before. Svarog's extended-range units are heavier than the modified sensor but significantly more capable — directional to within two degrees at close range, capable of capturing the second Stellaron's output at a depth the previous equipment could not approach, and shielded against the ambient Fragmentum resonance that has been interfering with standard readings past junction eight. The frequency recorder on Seele's harness has been upgraded too — Svarog's work from this morning, calibrated to the bifurcated signal, capable of distinguishing between the two signatures independently rather than recording them as a single composite. This matters. Luka is not entirely sure yet why it matters, but he knows it does. "Ready," Seele says, to the recorder display. "Ready," he says. She turns and looks at him. The assessment — fast, complete, the eyes moving over the equipment and then his face and then back to the corridor entrance. "We move fast," she says. "We don't stop at junctions unless something changes on the sensor. We log in motion. We stop at twelve, we take the readings, we assess the formation threat, and we retreat before the twenty-minute mark." "Nineteen," he says. "The formation threshold was twenty-two yesterday with a five-person group. Smaller group, potentially faster agitation. We leave at nineteen." She looks at him. "You think the agitation scales with group size?" "I think it scales with us," he says, which is the same thing he said yesterday and which they both know means something more specific than group size, and which neither of them is going to address before entering a corridor. "With both of us in proximity to the source. Yesterday was five people and twenty-two minutes. Today is two people who are — more specifically relevant — and I don't want to assume the threshold is the same." She holds his gaze. "Nineteen," she says. "Nineteen." She turns to the corridor. They go in. —— The first eight junctions at speed feel entirely different from the first eight junctions at the deliberate pace of previous entries. Speed strips the ambient quality from the tunnel — the way fast movement strips atmosphere from any space, replacing the texture of a place with the immediate demands of moving through it. Luka logs in motion, which he practiced last night against a timer until the motion and the logging were one action rather than two. The sensor readings scroll past the display at a rate that requires peripheral attention rather than focused reading, and he has calibrated what yellow-at-speed looks like so that any deviation will register as wrong before he consciously processes it. The second heartbeat is present from junction one. It is louder than yesterday. He notes this. He does not say it. They are moving too fast for conversation and the note is in the log and Seele's recorder is running and they will look at everything later. At junction five he does not stop. He does not feel a flash. He does not feel anything at junction five that is different from junctions four or six, and the absence of the flash from yesterday is itself information — the flash was not a property of the junction, it was a property of the pace, the slowing-down, the moment of stillness that gave the fragment room to arrive. At speed, there is no room. He files this. Junction eight. The carved stone begins. The lighting drops. Seele switches on her torch without breaking stride — the automatic motion, practiced, and he switches on his one second later and their combined beams make the corridor ahead navigable. The ceiling drops. His pauldrons catch nothing because he angled them at junction seven-point-five, the adjustment already built into the motion. He is watching the sensor display. At junction nine the secondary module vibration reading is already higher than it was at eleven yesterday. He does not stop. He logs it. "Vibration at nine is above yesterday's eleven reading," he says, because this is information and the moving-fast compact includes saying information when it is time-sensitive. "Recorder confirms elevated output from source," she says, in the same register — brisk, informational, moving. "The second signature is louder today." "Mine or yours?" "Yours," she says, and there is something in the way she says it — not emotional, just the fact of it, the way you state a fact you have not fully processed yet but which is clearly a fact. "The lower frequency. It's significantly stronger than yesterday's baseline." He processes this at junction ten, which they pass without stopping, the sensor reading not red, not even orange, holding at a high yellow that is technically acceptable and which feels wrong in the way that technically acceptable sometimes does. "Ten is high yellow," he says. "I see it," she says. "We're moving faster than yesterday so the time-based agitation hasn't built yet, but the depth-based agitation is compensating." He looks at the display. "The formation threshold might be different if the source responds to depth faster when we're moving at speed." "How much faster?" "Unknown." He looks at the display. "But I'd rather know it before we're standing in a Chrysalis form." "We're at ten," she says. "Eleven is thirty meters ahead. Twelve is thirty meters past eleven." She looks at him over her shoulder — the quick backward glance, the assessment glance, and something in the geometry of it makes the air in the corridor slightly different, the way a corridor is slightly different when something from a fragment maps onto the present moment. She does not know she did it. He knows she did it and keeps moving. "Rapid pass through eleven," she says. "We stop at twelve." "Yes," he says. They run. —— Junction eleven at speed is a different experience from junction eleven at a halt. At a halt, the vibration in the bedrock floor is a sensation. At speed, it is a sound — transmitted upward through the running footfall, conducted through the body in a way that is not audible to the ears but which the chest and the soles of the feet register as a persistent resonant note. Luka feels it climb from the moment they enter eleven's range and it does not stop climbing. The secondary module is reading above the highest previous entry in his log. The sensor goes orange at junction eleven. Not red. Orange — the warning register — but it reaches orange faster than yesterday, faster than the depth-versus-time analysis predicted, which means either the analysis was wrong or something about today's entry is different from yesterday's. He knows what is different about today's entry. "Orange at eleven," he says. "I know," she says. "Keep moving." They keep moving. Junction twelve. They stop. The corridor at junction twelve is wider than eleven — a natural widening in the carved stone, the bedrock pulling back on both sides to create a chamber-like space that is approximately three meters across and two meters high, which is significantly more room than any junction they have passed through. The torch light catches the walls and the ceiling and the specific quality of the stone here, which is different from the stone at eleven or ten — older, or worked differently, the tool marks running in a pattern that Aventurine would probably find significant and which Luka is logging for him without having time to interpret it. The floor of junction twelve is not flat. It slopes. Gently, almost imperceptibly, but the slope is there — downward, toward the branch that goes right. The branch that on their previous entry they turned back from. The branch that is now visible from where they stand, the carved stone curving away into a darkness their torches do not reach, and from the darkness— Light. Not the faint light from a previous sighting. Not the ambient glow through a fissure in bedrock. Something more direct, more present, the light of a source that is close enough to be primary rather than ambient. Old gold and deep water, the color of a geomarrow lantern that has been burning for twenty-two years in a place where nobody has maintained it, coming from somewhere around the curve of the right branch, somewhere between thirty and fifty meters away. Seele has stopped moving. She is standing at the entrance to the right branch, not in it, and she is looking at the light with an expression he has not seen from her before. Not the hostile wariness. Not the young uncertainty. Not the specific evaluating expression she uses for problems. Something older than any of those — the expression of a person standing in front of something so specifically theirs that no external processing is available. "Seele," he says. "I know," she says. Very quietly. "I know what it is." "The readings—" "I know what it is." She turns to look at him. Her face in the torchlight has the quality it has at junction seven-alpha-four and at the bifurcated signal and in the gap between the workshop and the generator housing — raw and present and without the management. "I've been hearing it since I was four years old," she says. "I thought it was the Underworld. I thought it was just how the deep cold sounded, underneath everything, the sound the machine district makes when everything else is quiet." She looks at the light. "It's not the district. It's that." She gestures toward the branch. "It's always been that." He is quiet. "The second heartbeat," he says. "Yes." She looks at him. "You hear it too." "Since I can remember," he says. "Probably before I can remember." She holds his gaze for a moment. Then she turns back to the branch, and her jaw sets — the decision being made, visible — and she says: "I'm going to the entrance of the branch. Not in it. I want to see the source of the light." "Seele—" "Not in it," she says, firmly. "Three meters. The branch entrance." He looks at the sensor. Orange, holding. The vibration in the floor is stronger here than at eleven — significantly stronger — the resonant note through the soles of his feet almost audible now, almost crossing from physical sensation into something the ears can locate. "The time," he says. "We're at—" He checks. Eleven minutes forty seconds from entry. "We have seven minutes before the nineteen-minute mark," she says, which means she has been counting too, which means she has always been counting, which means they are doing the same calculation from the same starting point. "Seven minutes," he says. "Three meters into the branch. We look at the light source. We take the readings. We leave." She meets his gaze. "That's all." He looks at the branch entrance. He looks at her. "Three meters," he says. "Together." She turns into the branch. Together. —— Three meters into the branch the light is considerably stronger. The right branch is narrower than the main corridor — barely wide enough for two people to walk abreast, which means they are walking side by side in the specific way the tunnel geometry requires, shoulders almost touching, and the light from the source ahead is hitting both of their faces and throwing their shadows back down the branch toward junction twelve behind them. The source is visible from here. Not directly — the branch curves again, a gentle rightward arc, and the light is coming from around the curve, approximately twenty to twenty-five meters further. But the curve is gentle enough that Luka can see the edge of the source, the place where the light originates, and what he sees is bedrock. Exposed bedrock, uncarved, the deep foundation stone of the Underworld, and in the bedrock a formation. Not a fissure. Not a crack. A formation — the specific geometric quality of crystalline material that has grown from the stone rather than been cut into it, the way geomarrow grows in high-concentration deposits, except this is not geomarrow. The crystalline structure is different — denser, the geometry more complex, and the color is the old gold and deep water that they have been tracking since the first time the light became visible. "That's it," Seele says, very quietly. "Yes," he says. The frequency recorder on her harness is doing something it has not done in any previous entry. The display is not showing a waveform — it is showing two waveforms, the bifurcated signal from yesterday, but at this proximity the two signatures are no longer intertwined. They are distinct. Side by side on the display, clearly differentiated, the lower one — his — and the higher one — hers — running parallel with the specific quality of two things that belong together and have been held apart. Seele is looking at the display. He is looking at the display. Neither of them says anything for a moment that is longer than the nineteen-minute constraint comfortably permits. "That's us," she says. "Yes," he says. "In there." She looks at the light around the curve. "Whatever we were to each other. Whatever we knew. Whatever happened." She looks at the display. "It's in there." He is aware of several things simultaneously: the sensor reading, which has gone from orange to a deep persistent orange that is one degree of saturation from red. The vibration in the floor, which is now something between sensation and sound, a note that is in his feet and his chest and the back of his jaw. The time — thirteen minutes fifty seconds from entry, which means five minutes and ten seconds until they need to leave. And the specific quality of standing this close to Seele in a narrow branch in the light of the thing that holds their memories, shoulder to shoulder, breathing the same cold air, and the second heartbeat audible to both of them and at this proximity doing something it does not do at surface level, which is sounding, very faintly, like two heartbeats instead of one. "Seele," he says, because her name is the thing that is available when nothing else is. "I know," she says. "The time." "Yes." She is still looking at the light around the curve. "Five minutes," she says. "Four minutes fifty." "We should get the full scanner reading before we go." She does not look away from the light. "Svarog's extended-range unit — point it at the formation." He raises the unit. He takes the reading. The display outputs a data string that is going to take Pela and Aventurine several hours to fully parse — depth, energy density, crystalline structure analysis, frequency profile. He captures everything and logs it simultaneously. "Got it," he says. She nods. She reaches out her hand — not toward the light, not toward the branch, toward the wall. The carved stone wall of the right branch, which has the same construction as the main corridor but older, the tool marks deeper and more deliberate. Her fingertips touch the wall. The recorder spikes. Not the same spike as junction seven-alpha-four. Bigger. The recognition signal, the waveform peak that means the source recognizes the contact, but here the peak is not a spike — it is a sustained elevation, the display holding at the peak level rather than returning to baseline, as if the proximity to the source means the recognition cannot be brief. As if this close, recognition is not an event but a state. And then something else happens. The second waveform — his — elevates too. Not as high. Not the sustained recognition peak. But a distinct and specific rise from baseline, occurring at the same time as her recognition signal, and then — very briefly, for perhaps three seconds — the two waveforms on the display align. The same frequency. The same amplitude. Running exactly together, parallel, the way they ran parallel when they were side by side on the display but closer now, closer than parallel, almost— "Luka," she says. Her voice is different. Not the brisk informational register, not the sharply hostile register, not any register he has catalogued in the weeks of knowing her. Something rawer than all of those, something that has come from underneath the management because the management has encountered something too large to process in real time. He looks at her. She is looking at the display. The two waveforms are still aligned. And then — the fragment arrives. Not his. Not a fragment he has been carrying. This one arrives from outside him, from the recorder's display, conducted through the proximity and the recognition signal and the specific resonance of being this close to the source with her beside him, and what arrives is not visual but auditory. A voice. A child's voice. His voice, from a long time ago, saying something he cannot fully resolve — the consonants and not the vowel, the shape of a word — and then another voice, also a child's, also from a long time ago, responding. The second voice is hers. Both of them hear it. He knows they both hear it because she goes absolutely still beside him in the way she went still at the recognition signal, the stillness of someone receiving something too specifically theirs to react to with anything but the body's most fundamental response, which is to stop moving and hold. The voices are gone in three seconds. The waveforms diverge back to parallel. The recorder resumes its normal capture mode. The sensor reads deep orange. "Time," he says, and his voice comes out even because he is using every piece of discipline he has to keep it even. She pulls her hand from the wall. "Time," she agrees, and her voice is also even, and they are both using discipline they should not have to use in order to say words that are functionally just a coordinate check, and they both know this about each other, and neither of them says anything about it. They turn back toward junction twelve. —— The Chrysalis form at junction eleven comes from the right wall. Not from ahead, not from the direction they have been tracking. From the right wall — the stone itself, the carved bedrock, the amber-black crystallization that has been dormant for weeks erupting in the specific wet cracking sound of Fragmentum matter under activation pressure, and the form that emerges is not like yesterday's forms. It is larger. Significantly larger — a meter taller than any Chrysalis form Luka has encountered at surface level, the crystalline structure denser and the corrupted threading more saturated, the amber light in it brighter and more active. And it does not come out of the wall at corridor speed. It comes out of the wall fast, the speed of something that was ready before they were. Seele moves. She moves the way she moves in confined spaces — explosively, without telegraphing, using the narrow corridor geometry to limit the form's angles of approach. But the form is faster than yesterday's forms and it knows the geometry too, and it cuts off her primary angle before she can establish it, which means she has to improvise the secondary angle, which costs her a half-second, and in that half-second the form gets inside her reach. The impact catches her left shoulder. Not a full impact — she rotated in the half-second, so it is a glancing strike rather than a direct hit, but a glancing strike from something this dense at this speed is not nothing, and the force puts her into the corridor wall on the left side with a crack that is too loud to be just the torch she drops. "Seele—!" He is moving before her name finishes leaving his mouth. The form reorients toward her immediately — the specific targeting behavior, the one that has been consistent since Tuesday, the Stellaron's proximity and her recognition signal making her the primary target in every encounter — and he drives into its right flank with everything he has. The combat braces take the impact loading. The prosthetic arm, reinforced for this encounter, does not buckle under the force of driving into a dense crystalline mass at full speed. He hits it at the forty-percent structural junction, both fists, the braces conducting the impact force into the form's primary structure. It cracks. It does not collapse. He hits it again. The crack propagates — he can hear it spreading through the crystalline structure, the specific sound of material failing under repeated stress — and the form loses coherence in its upper half, the top section fragmenting away, but the corrupted threading in the lower half holds the base structure together and the base structure reorients toward him. He takes the hit. It is not a glancing hit. The full force of the form's lower section catching his right side picks him up and puts him into the right wall at a height that his feet leave the floor for a moment, the impact conducted through the combat braces and the armor and into his shoulder and ribs with the specific quality of something that has exceeded the tolerance of what it is hitting. He goes into the wall and down — his feet find the floor, he catches himself on one knee — and the form is already reforming from the corrupted threading, the damaged upper section rebuilding from the intact lower, the amber light pulsing as it does. "The base—!" Seele's voice, from the left wall. Her voice is sharp and strained and present. "Drive the left side of the base — the threading is thinner there—!" He can see it — she is right, the left side of the base structure has less corrupted threading density than the right, the impact he took earlier disrupted the distribution. He drives his prosthetic fist into the left base with the full force of the braces behind it, and the structural failure propagates from the crack in the upper section down through the left base simultaneously, and the form collapses — not cleanly, not all at once, in sections, the corrupted threading trying to hold the structure together and failing junction by junction, until the last section crumbles and the amber light dies and the floor is covered in cooling Fragmentum debris. Luka is on one knee in the corridor. Seele is against the left wall. They are both breathing hard in the specific way of people who have spent everything they have in a short period of time and whose bodies are now doing the accounting. "Report," he says, which is the word that means: tell me where you are damaged. "Left shoulder," she says. "Impact bruising, possibly a cracked rib from the wall. Functional." She pauses. "You." "Right side," he says. "The impact was above the brace loading threshold. Something in the shoulder joint." He pushes to standing. The shoulder sends a signal that is specific and informative and which he is going to have to have Natasha look at. "Functional." He picks up the extended-range scanner from the floor, where it landed when the form hit him. He checks it. "Scanner is intact." "Recorder is intact," she says, checking the harness. "Time," he says. "Seventeen minutes twelve seconds." "We need to move." "I know." She pushes off the wall. He can see her managing the left side — not favoring it dramatically, but the specific controlled movement of someone keeping a damaged area out of the motion path. "The torch." He looks down. Her torch is on the floor, the handle cracked where it hit the wall, but the beam still on. He picks it up, hands it to her. She takes it. They look at each other in the two torch beams — the cold of junction eleven, the residual amber of the collapsed form, the carved stone walls with the tool marks that Aventurine finds significant. Her hair has come loose from whatever held it — the impact with the wall — and it is around her face in a way that is entirely unmanaged and which he is not thinking about. She has a cut above her left eyebrow from something in the wall contact, the specific kind of small cut that bleeds more than its size justifies, and the blood is already at her cheekbone. He does not say anything about her hair or the cut. He says: "Eleven was not the last formation." "No," she says. "It wasn't." She looks at the collapsed debris. "The forms at twelve and beyond are going to be worse than this." She looks at him. "We know that." "Yes," he says. "We know that." They look at each other for a moment that is longer than the seventeen-and-something-minutes-from-entry constraint recommends. "Nineteen minutes," she says. "We're past it," he says. "Then let's move," she says. They move. —— They exit the corridor at eighteen minutes forty-one seconds, which is technically within the nineteen-minute window if you do not count the time inside the branch at junction twelve, which Luka is logging as a separate entry because the time-based agitation mechanism appears to function differently in the branch than in the main corridor, and the distinction matters for the next entry's planning. He is logging this while walking, which is how he logs everything now, and his right shoulder is doing something that requires Natasha's opinion, and the secondary module is showing the highest post-entry readings he has recorded since beginning the walkthrough logs. Seele is walking beside him. She is managing the left side with the controlled quality he noted in junction eleven. The cut above her eyebrow has been pressed with the cuff of her sleeve — not elegantly, not with particular attention to appearance, the purely functional intervention of someone who knows a cut is bleeding and applies pressure without thinking about how it looks. He does not say anything about the cut. He says: "The two waveforms aligning. In the branch. What did you make of that?" She is quiet for a moment. "I don't know yet," she says. "I don't have enough to make something of it." "The voices," he says. She is quiet for longer. The corridor passes — junction nine, junction eight, the carved stone transitioning back to concrete, the lighting resuming. "I heard them," she says. "Yes." "I don't know whose they were." "I think you do," he says. She is quiet again. "I think I do too," she says, finally. "But I'm not going to say it until I have more than three seconds of something that came from a Stellaron resonance field in a confined corridor." She looks at him. "I require more evidence than that." "That's reasonable," he says. "I know it's reasonable." She pauses. "Don't agree with me so readily." He looks at her sideways. "You want me to argue against the reasonable position?" "I want you to stop being—" She stops. She makes the sound — not quite a word, the exasperation sound — and looks ahead. "You've been agreeing with me more than you disagree and it is unsettling." He blinks. "You want me to disagree with you more." "I want you to stop performing agreement when you actually have a different position," she says. "It's—" She pauses, selecting the word. "Condescending." He stares at her. "I'm not performing agreement. I genuinely agree." "You genuinely agreed at junction eleven that we should move, which is the thing you were already doing anyway. You genuinely agreed about nineteen minutes, which was your suggestion. You genuinely agreed about the branch, which was my plan but is also plainly correct." She looks at him. "You've been agreeing with everything I say for two entries now." "Because you've been correct," he says. "Nobody is correct all the time!" "You've been correct all the time today!" She opens her mouth. She closes it. He can see her working through whether this is a valid complaint given that the substance of it is that he has been agreeing because she has been right, and the logical problem with complaining about that. Her jaw does the tight small movement. "Fine," she says. "That's my word," he says, before he can stop himself. She turns and looks at him with an expression he has not seen before — not the hostile wariness, not the young uncertainty, not the flash of something unguarded. Something between irritation and something else entirely, the expression of a person who has been caught off guard in a way they did not anticipate and are not sure how to categorize. She looks ahead. "Your shoulder needs Natasha," she says. "Your rib needs Natasha," he says. "I said left shoulder originally." "And a possibly cracked rib." "I said possibly." "You hit a carved stone wall at speed." "I know what I hit." "Then you know Natasha needs to look at it." She is quiet. "Fine," she says. He does not say it's my word again. He files the victory, which is not a victory because this is not a contest, except that it is absolutely a contest and they both know it, and the fact that they both know it is doing something to the temperature of the corridor that has nothing to do with the bedrock or the second heartbeat or the twenty-two years of held memory thirty meters below junction twelve. They exit nine-beta at 0619. The outer machine district is waking up around them — the sound of the district's generators cycling through the morning pressure check, a distant mechanical rhythm that has been ambient all of Luka's life and which today, for the first time since he began the walkthrough logs, he hears as something separate from the second heartbeat rather than part of it. They stop at the corridor entrance. She is looking at the district. He is looking at the scanner data on the extended-range unit's display. They are standing in the specific arrangement of the past twenty-three walkthrough entries — close enough that the proximity is a fact, far enough that it is not a statement — and the morning cold of the outer machine district is specific and textural and familiar. "The data," he says. "I'll have it formatted and sent to Pela and Aventurine by 0800." "I'll send the recorder output at the same time," she says. "The bifurcated signal data from the branch." "And the alignment event," he says. A pause. "And the alignment event," she says. "Yes." He nods. He turns to go — toward the workshop, toward Svarog, toward the recalibration and the debrief and the Natasha visit that he is going to have to have. He takes three steps. "Luka." He stops. He turns. She is still standing at the corridor entrance, looking at him, and the morning light is starting to come into the outer district — the Underworld's approximation of morning light, the warm amber of the district's main lamps cycling up to their daytime setting — and in that light her hair is still loose and the cut above her eyebrow has stopped bleeding but dried in a thin dark line and she looks like what she is, which is someone who just fought their way out of a deep tunnel and got hit by something larger than she was and is going to have a very sore rib for a week and is standing at the corridor entrance watching him go with an expression he has not catalogued before. "The branch," she says. "The voices." "Yes," he says. "Next entry," she says. "When we go back in." She pauses. "I want to go to the branch. I want to hear them clearly." She holds his gaze. "Together." He looks at her. "Together," he says. She nods. She turns toward the district. He turns toward the workshop. They go in opposite directions in the morning light of the outer machine district, and the second heartbeat is below them, steady, patient, and it is counting faster than it was yesterday. —— In the palace, at 0620, Bronya wakes from a dream she does not remember to a message on her phone. The message is from Gepard, who was apparently awake at 0619, which means he was awake before 0619, which means he did not sleep last night either, or not much. The message says: 'They're out. Both functional. Data incoming.' She reads it twice. She sets the phone on the bedside table and looks at the ceiling of the palace room, which is high and ornate and cold in the pre-dawn dark, and she breathes once. Twice. She picks up the phone again and types: 'Good.' She pauses. She types: 'Come to the office when you're ready. I'll have something from the right side of the cabinet.' She sends it before she decides not to. His reply comes in forty-five seconds, which means he smiled before he answered, probably: 'I'll be there at seven.' She puts the phone down. She looks at the ceiling. Outside the palace, Belobog is waking up — the inhabited districts cycling through their morning in the amber warmth that is the city's particular quality of alive, ten thousand people beginning their day without knowing that two people came out of a deep tunnel this morning with data that is going to change what Belobog knows about itself. She will tell them. When she understands enough to tell them accurately. She is going to tell them. She gets up. She goes to the office, and she puts the kettle on, and she opens the right side of the cabinet, and she waits. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 20: "What the Data Says" Chapter Text Gepard arrives at the office at 0658. He is two minutes early, which is not unusual, but the two minutes have a different quality this morning — not the professional early of a man who runs on precise timing, but the earlier quality of someone who has been awake since before 0600 and for whom arrival is simply the moment the waiting became possible to do somewhere specific. He comes through the door and Bronya is already at the desk, which means she has been there long enough for the kettle to have gone and come back, and the right side of the cabinet is open, and there are two cups on the desk. She looks up when he comes in. Her expression does the thing — the less-sealed version, the one that has been the default in private since the stairwell — and then she looks at the cups. "Sit down," she says. He sits in the chair from the corner. He picks up the cup. It is warm. He looks at the desk and at her and at the morning light coming through the north-facing windows, which is pale and early and entirely honest, and he thinks about Seele at the corridor entrance with the dried blood above her eyebrow and Luka going toward the workshop in the opposite direction, and the data incoming, and the voices in the branch. "They're both functional," he says. "The shoulder is going to need Natasha. Seele took a rib impact." Bronya looks at him. "How bad." "Functional. Not critical." He pauses. "The Chrysalis form at eleven was significantly larger and faster than the ones from yesterday's group entry. The source is escalating." "Because they were closer," she says. "And because they were the two of them alone," he says. "The agitation mechanism — whatever the Stellaron is responding to — it responds differently to the two of them together without the buffering effect of other people in the group." He pauses. "More specifically." She holds her cup. She is thinking — the full-attention posture, everything directed inward. He watches her and does not hurry her, because she is going to arrive somewhere and he will know when she does. She arrives. "They went into the branch," she says. He looks at her. "How did you know?" "Because if I had been in that corridor and the light was visible and there were seven minutes on the clock, I would have gone three meters in to look." She meets his gaze. "And because the data Pela forwarded at 0745 includes a branch entry log." She picks up a page from the desk. "The bifurcated signal alignment event." She slides it across. "Two waveforms running at the same frequency for approximately three seconds." He looks at the page. "What does that mean." "Pela thinks it's a resonance synchronization — the proximity to the source causing the two signatures to briefly align. Like two tuning forks at the same pitch." She pauses. "But Pela is working from the theoretical framework of the first Stellaron's behavior, which broadcasts. This one holds." She looks at the page. "I think the alignment means something different from a resonance synchronization." She pauses. "I think it means the two things being held were held together. Not separately. Together, and the proximity is making the Stellaron show the shape of how they were held." He is quiet. "Together," he says. "Together," she says. "Whatever was sealed — whatever was stored — it was not two separate deposits. It was one." She looks at the desk. "Which means whatever happened to Seele and Luka twenty-two years ago happened to them together. The memory, the relationship, whatever it was — it was stored as a single thing. Not two halves of different things." He holds this. "And the voices," he says. She looks at him. "The voices in the branch. Pela forwarded the recorder's audio capture alongside the waveform data." She pauses. "I listened to it." "And." "Three seconds of two children's voices," she says, very carefully. "The content is not fully audible at the recorder's capture quality — it needs Svarog's analysis tools to resolve the words." She pauses. "But the timbre. The age. The way they are speaking to each other." She looks at the desk. "I don't need the words. I can hear what it is." The office is quiet. Outside, Belobog goes through its morning. The pale northern light sits on the city's rooftops and the inhabited districts glow with the amber warmth of people going about their lives, and two floors below this office there is a Stellaron sitting at 1.3 and holding, and somewhere below the outer machine district there is another one counting toward the moment the seal comes apart. "They need to go back in," he says. "Yes," she says. "They do." She picks up her cup. "Which means we need to know what is at junction twelve and beyond before they do." She looks at him. "The formation at eleven was larger and faster than the day-one group entry forms. The forms at twelve are going to be worse. We don't send them in past eleven without knowing what they're going to face." "Aventurine's scanner data from the scout run gives a partial picture," he says. "Pela is running it now." "Pela is sixteen years old and has been running continuous analysis for three days on three hours of sleep," Bronya says, with the specific flat quality she uses when she is stating a fact that she finds unacceptable and is in the process of deciding what to do about it. "I'm going to tell her to sleep before she looks at the junction twelve data." He looks at her. "She won't," she says. "But I'm going to tell her." "She'll tell you she's fine," he says. "She'll tell me she's fine and she'll mean it and she'll also be wrong," Bronya says. She sets her cup down. "She is extraordinarily capable and she is a teenager who has been carrying the analytical weight of a crisis for weeks, and both of those things are true simultaneously and I am responsible for the second one even if I cannot change the first." She pauses. "I'm going to make sure she has actual food and somewhere to sleep that isn't her office floor." She looks at him. "That's not a bureaucratic concern, it's—" "I know," he says. She looks at him. The corner of her mouth. "I told you to stop saying that." "I know," he says again, and she makes the sound — the nearly-laugh, the one that surfaces when she is tired and has stopped suppressing it — and he drinks his cup and watches the morning come through the windows and does not say anything else for a while. This is also something they have, now — the morning quiet. The office at 0700 before the day requires them to be versions of themselves that have responsibilities and titles. He has been coming at seven for three weeks and she has been there before him every time and the right side of the cabinet is always open and neither of them has named what this is, because naming it would require deciding whether it is allowed, and they have both silently agreed that some things are better decided by the fact of them than by the naming. She breaks the quiet first. "The IPC assessment," she says. "Aventurine told me last night that he and Topaz are preparing their preliminary findings." She looks at the desk. "He was not specific about the content but he said—" She pauses. "He said he thinks Belobog is managing its situation adequately." She pauses again. "He said it in the way that means there is a 'but' attached to it." "The second Stellaron," he says. "The second Stellaron is not something Belobog has managed yet," she says. "It's something Belobog is currently investigating. Which is different." She meets his gaze. "I don't think the IPC is going to classify Belobog as under-managed. But I think they are going to classify the second Stellaron as an ongoing concern requiring monitoring." She pauses. "Which means a continued IPC presence." He considers this. "Aventurine specifically." "Possibly. Or a designated contact." She looks at the window. "I find I don't object to Aventurine having a continued presence as much as I expected to." "He gave Svarog the full monitoring data," Gepard says. "Yes," she says. "That matters." A pause. "And he asked the right question about the tunnel logs." She looks at the desk. "He's been trying to understand rather than assess, which is not what I expected from the IPC." "Topaz is assessing," he says. "Topaz is doing both," she says, with the precision of someone who has been watching Topaz carefully for four days and has a complete picture. "She is assessing with genuine curiosity rather than predetermined conclusions. That is rarer than it should be." She pauses. "I want them both to see what Pela's analysis finds in the junction twelve data before they finalize the assessment report." "I'll arrange it," he says. She nods. She is quiet for a moment. Then she says, without quite looking at him: "Gepard." "Yes." "The voices in the branch." She is looking at the page with the waveform data. "The children's voices." She pauses. "Does it—" She stops. She starts again. "Does it change anything for you. Knowing that what Seele and Luka had was something specific enough to be stored as a single thing. That they were — close enough, whatever they were to each other, for the seal to hold them together rather than separately." He looks at her. "No," he says. She looks up. "It doesn't change anything?" "I mean it doesn't change anything about what needs to happen," he says. "They need to go back in. They need to remember. The seal needs to come apart on its own terms." He pauses. "It changes what I understand about what was lost." He meets her gaze. "Something very significant was taken from two children who were apparently very significant to each other. And they've been living in the same city for twenty-two years without knowing it." He pauses. "That is—" He stops. He looks at the window. "Difficult to sit with," he says, which is the understatement of the year, and she knows it, and her expression says she knows it. "Yes," she says, very quietly. They sit with it for a moment, together, in the office with the morning light and the two cups and the page with the waveform data and the city going about its morning outside. "The right side was better today," he says, eventually. She looks at him. "Was it?" "Yes." She almost smiles. "Good," she says. —— Natasha's ward at 0830 has the specific quality of a medical facility that has been open since before most of its patients were awake, which is to say it has the quality of Natasha. She is at the front desk, as she always is, except today Marta from the outer machine district is not in the chair beside her — Marta is in the examination room, which Natasha stepped out of to get supplies, and which she is clearly about to return to. She looks up when Seele and Luka come through the door, and her expression takes in the information with the clinical efficiency of a doctor who has developed the specific skill of assessing an entire room's worth of injury in a single glance. "Sit down," she says, to both of them. "I'll be with you in three minutes." Seele sits. Luka sits. They sit in the ward's waiting area in the two chairs that are closest to the examination room door — not next to each other, one chair of gap between them, the arrangement of two people who have been in close proximity for an intense two hours and are now doing the thing people do after intense proximity, which is recalibrate the normal distance. The ward is quiet in the morning way. Someone in the back is coughing. The sound of Natasha's voice from the examination room is low and steady and has the specific quality she brings to every patient interaction, which is the quality of someone whose certainty can be borrowed. "Your shoulder," Seele says, to the wall ahead of her. "Natasha will look at it," he says. "I'm aware she will look at it. I'm asking how it feels now." She is also looking at the wall ahead. "It feels like I hit a Chrysalis form that exceeded the brace loading threshold," he says. "That's descriptive, not informative." "It's sore," he says. "The joint moved in a direction it shouldn't have when the impact went above tolerance." She turns and looks at him. He turns and looks at her. The cut above her eyebrow is clean now — she washed it before they came in, he noticed when they were outside the workshop — and the bruising at her jaw from the wall is starting to color, a faint blue-purple that is going to be more visible by tonight. "The rib," he says. "It's sore," she says, in exactly the register he used. He looks at her. She looks at him. The gap between them has a specific quality this morning that is different from the gap in the tunnel or the gap in the workshop. It is the gap of two people who have been through something and are sitting with the aftermath of it in a quiet ward waiting room, and the aftermath has a texture — not comfortable, not uncomfortable, just present. "The voices," she says, quietly. The ward is not empty but it is quiet enough for quiet conversations. "Yes," he says. "I've been trying to—" She stops. She looks at her hands. "Reconstruct it. Since we exited the corridor. What I heard. The specific quality of it." She pauses. "I know what a memory sounds like. I've had fragments before — the shape before the image, the smell of the corridor. This was different from those." "How," he says. "Those are mine," she says. "They come from inside me — from something I'm carrying and haven't accessed yet. This one—" She pauses. "This one came from outside. From the Stellaron. It was — returned to me, rather than surfacing from where it was stored in me." She looks at him. "Do you understand the distinction?" "Yes," he says. "I felt the same thing." He looks at his hands. "The fragments I've had before — the girl running, the shape of a name — those came from somewhere in me. From the edge of something I'm carrying. The voices were different. They came from the recorder. From the branch. From outside." "Which means the memories aren't only stored in the Stellaron," she says. "They're also in us. Both places." He is quiet. "What does that mean," she says. "I don't know yet," he says. Which is honest, and which she accepts without the sharp edge that would usually accompany his not-knowing something. The examination room door opens. Natasha comes out, looks at them, and says: "You first," to Luka, which is the professional triage of someone who has already assessed which injury is more urgent and is not going to discuss the ranking. He goes. Seele stays in the waiting area. She looks at her hands. She looks at the ward. She looks at the chair between her and the chair Luka was sitting in, and she thinks about voices in a branch in a deep tunnel and a frequency recorder capturing the shape of something that was stored as a single thing rather than two halves of separate things, and she thinks about a girl with purple hair running ahead and laughing and looking back. She looked back, Luka had said. The way you look back at someone you know will keep up. She presses her fingers together in her lap, very carefully, and does not think about what it means that she was running in those corridors and laughing. She does not think about this specifically and deliberately, the way she does not think about junction seven-alpha-four or the branch going left or the second heartbeat that has been the sound of the Underworld's deep cold her entire life. She is extremely good at not thinking about things. She sits in the ward and not-thinks about them with considerable skill. —— In the palace's secondary analysis room, Pela has been awake since 0400. This is not unusual. What is slightly unusual is that at 0900, Bronya appears in the doorway of the analysis room with a plate of food and the expression of someone who has made a decision and is implementing it with the specific quality of a decision that is not open for discussion. "Eat this," Bronya says. "Then sleep for four hours." Pela looks up from the junction twelve data. She looks at the food. She looks at Bronya. "The waveform alignment event needs—" "It needs you to have slept," Bronya says, setting the plate on the desk in the available space, which is small. "The alignment event will still be there in four hours. The second Stellaron has been there for twenty-two years. It can wait four more hours." She pauses. "Eat. Then sleep. The data will be here when you wake up and you will be more useful to it." Pela looks at the plate. She looks at the junction twelve data. She looks at Bronya with the expression of someone who knows the correct answer and is waiting for their objection reflex to catch up to it. "The energy output profile at junction twelve needs cross-referencing with the ambient Fragmentum response data from the surface blooms," she says, "to determine whether the second Stellaron's escalation is connected to the first Stellaron's historical broadcast behavior, or whether they're operating on independent—" "Four hours," Bronya says. "Then you can cross-reference whatever you need." Pela is quiet for a moment. "You're going to wait until I start eating before you leave," she says. "Yes," Bronya says. "That's—" Pela looks at the plate. Something in her expression does the thing that Pela's expressions very rarely do, which is become briefly and entirely unguarded.
