She is sixteen years old and she has been the administrative analysis backbone of a three-month crisis and she is sitting in a secondary analysis room at 0900 having been awake since 0400, and someone has brought her a plate of food and is standing in the doorway waiting to make sure she eats it. "Thank you," she says, very quietly. "Eat," Bronya says, gently. Pela eats. Bronya stays in the doorway until the plate is half empty, which is enough, and then she goes. —— Sampo comes to the workshop at noon. He comes with a delivery that is legitimate — a component order for one of the district's generator units — and he comes in the way he always comes into the workshop, which is through the door, at a normal pace, wearing the performance of a man who is here for professional reasons. He sets the delivery on the secondary worktable. He nods at Svarog. He nods at Clara, who is at her stool sorting the workshop's cable inventory with the methodical attention of someone who has decided that the cable inventory has been in an unacceptable state for too long. "The scout run data," Sampo says, to Svarog's back. "I have reviewed it," Svarog says, without turning from his work. "And." Svarog is quiet for a moment. He sets down what he is doing. He turns, which is an event of some significance given his size, and looks at Sampo with the optical sensors that see considerably more than is comfortable. "The second Stellaron's output increased by thirty-seven percent during the scout run," he says. "The largest single-entry increase I have recorded since the behavioral shift began six weeks ago." Sampo looks at him. "Because they went into the branch." "Because they approached the source," Svarog says. "The branch entry was the final proximity. The increase began at junction eleven." He pauses. "The rate of increase is accelerating." He pauses again. "The threshold for full activation — the point at which the seal comes fully apart — is approaching faster than the current rate of the remembering." Sampo is quiet for a moment. "Meaning." "Meaning the Stellaron's activation timeline and the natural pace of their remembering may not be synchronized," Svarog says. "If the Stellaron activates fully before they have remembered enough to understand what is happening—" He pauses. "The activation will be significantly harder to manage." The workshop is quiet. The generators cycle outside. Clara, at her stool, has stopped sorting cables and is very carefully not looking at either of them, which is her method of listening without appearing to. "Can the activation be slowed," Sampo says. "Not from the outside," Svarog says. "The seal is internal to the Stellaron's mechanism. What accelerates the activation is their proximity to the source. What decelerates it—" He pauses. "Is distance. If they stopped going into the corridor." "They're not going to stop," Sampo says. "No," Svarog says. "They are not." Sampo looks at the workshop floor. He looks at the secondary worktable with his legitimate delivery sitting on it. He looks at the door through which, this morning, two people walked into a deep tunnel and came back with a cut above an eyebrow and a shoulder that needs medical attention and data that is going to change what they know about the city they grew up in. "Then we make sure they're ready for what they find," he says. "Yes," Svarog says. "That is the correct approach." Clara looks up from the cable inventory. She looks at Sampo, and then at Svarog, and then at the door. "When are they going back in?" she asks, in the tone of a twelve-year-old who has been following the thread of a conversation and has arrived at the question everyone else in the room was not saying. "Soon," Svarog says. "Will they be okay?" she asks. The workshop is quiet for a moment. "They are going to have to be very strong," Svarog says, which is not the same as yes and is not the same as no and is the most honest answer available. "Both of them." Clara looks at the cable inventory. She picks up a cable. She starts sorting again, carefully, the small hands doing the careful work, her feet not quite reaching the floor. "Okay," she says, which is a twelve-year-old's version of I accept this and I'm afraid of it and I trust that it's the truth, all in one syllable. Sampo watches her for a moment. Something in his face does the complicated private thing — the thing that has been happening more frequently since Gepard told him about the dark winter, the thing that is the missing years making themselves known in the present. He looks at the workshop door. And then it happens. It does not announce itself. It arrives the way the corridor smell arrived at junction nine — without permission, without context, preceding any framework for understanding it. One moment Sampo is standing in the workshop looking at the door, and the next he is somewhere else that is also here, the two times occupying the same space for a duration that is not measurable in seconds. He is twelve years old. He is in the outer machine district. Not the workshop — somewhere adjacent to it, in one of the generator corridors that branch off the main district path, the kind of corridor that children use as shortcuts and that adults mostly ignore. The corridor smells like machine oil and cold metal and the specific electrical ozone of geomarrow-adjacent equipment, and he is moving fast because something has happened and he can hear it — not with his ears, with his chest, the low resonant note that in the present day has a name but in this moment is simply a thing that is wrong, a thing that is pulling at the ground underneath him. He is running. He does not know where he is running to. He is running because someone told him to run — a voice, older than him, the voice of someone who was important and whose name is — whose name is — The workshop is back. He is standing in front of Svarog's secondary worktable with his hand on the delivery component and his heart going at a rate that is significantly above his resting rate, and Clara is looking at him with the wide-eyed serious expression and Svarog has turned again and is looking at him with the optical sensors. "Sampo," Svarog says. "I'm—" Sampo looks at his hand on the delivery component. "I was running," he says. "I was twelve. I was in the generator corridors." He looks at Svarog. "Someone told me to run." Svarog is very still. "Yes," he says. "Who," Sampo says. A pause. A long one. Svarog looks at him with the expression his optical sensors carry when he is about to say something that has been waiting a long time to be said. "Someone who was trying to protect you," Svarog says. "Someone who knew what was coming and made a decision." He pauses. "I cannot tell you more than that. Not yet. Not until—" He pauses again. "Not until the people it concerns most have remembered enough to hear it." Sampo stares at him. "You know who it was." "Yes," Svarog says. "You've known this whole time." "Yes," Svarog says. The workshop is very quiet. The generators cycle outside. Clara has put the cable down and is looking at Sampo with the expression of a child who knows she is in the presence of something very large and is managing the size of it by keeping very still. Sampo looks at Svarog. The performance is completely absent — all of it, every layer of Sampo Koski that has been built over twenty-two years of living in this district on top of the missing foundation — and what is underneath it is someone who was twelve years old and running in a generator corridor and heard a voice that told him to run and has been here, in this district, for his entire adult life looking for the person who said it. "Tell me," he says. "I cannot," Svarog says. "Not yet." "Svarog—" "Not yet," Svarog says, with the immovable patience of something that has been waiting twenty-two years and can wait a little longer. "When it is time to tell you — when the people it concerns most have what they need — I will tell you everything I know." He holds Sampo's gaze with the optical sensors that see more than is comfortable. "I promise you this." Sampo looks at him for a long time. Then he looks at the workshop floor. At the cable Clara has put down. At the secondary worktable. At the delivery component his hand is still resting on. "Alright," he says. He picks up the delivery component. He sets it properly on the worktable. He turns to the door. "Sampo," Svarog says. He stops. "You have been returning to this district for twenty-two years," Svarog says. "You have been coming back to the place where the voice was. Looking for it." He pauses. "You were always going to find your way back. It was never a question of whether." Another pause. "I am glad you are still here." Sampo stands with his back to the workshop for a moment. "Yeah," he says. His voice is even. Too even, the specific evenness of something being maintained. "Me too." He goes. —— The ward at 1400 hours is quieter than the morning. Natasha's verdict on Luka's shoulder: the joint moved beyond its natural range of motion during the impact, producing what she calls a minor subluxation that has self-reduced but will be sore for a week and should not be loaded with the combat braces again until it is. The brace loading tolerance needs to be recalibrated for the specific force the Chrysalis forms are now producing — something she says to Luka directly, with the precision of someone who is also saying: the next time you go in you are going to face something worse and your equipment needs to account for this. Her verdict on Seele's rib: hairline fracture, lower left, which is exactly what Seele said was possibly and which Natasha confirms with the flat certainty of someone who has seen enough of this injury to identify it on sight. Wrapped. Rest recommended. Not mandatory, she says, with the dry emphasis of someone who is aware that rest is not going to be what happens. Luka hears this from the waiting area because the ward does not have thick walls. He is still in the waiting area when Seele comes out. She comes through the examination room door with the specific controlled quality of someone who is managing the left side and has been told not to move it more than necessary, and she stops when she sees him still in the chair. "You waited," she says. "I was going to send the data to Pela while you were in there," he says. "I stayed to confirm you received the same injury report." She looks at him. "The rib," he says. "Hairline fracture," she says. "I heard," he says. "The ward walls are thin," she says, which is not an objection. "Yes," he says. She sits. Not in the chair that was hers before — in the chair that was his, the one immediately next to the gap. One chair closer. She does not appear to notice she has done this. He does not mention it. "The data," she says. "Did you send it?" "Waiting for the recorder output you said you'd send this morning," he says. "You haven't sent it." She looks at her phone. She makes a sound — the exasperation sound, brief, genuine — and pulls up the file. "I was going to send it before the ward and then the ward happened—" She types. Sends. "There." His phone buzzes. "Got it," he says. She puts her phone away. She looks at the ward's front desk, where Natasha is writing in a chart with the focused quiet she brings to chart-writing. The ward has its morning sounds — a cough from the back, a door opening and closing, the ambient hum of medical equipment. Normal, ordinary, the sounds of a place doing its constant work. "The data," Seele says, and then stops. "What about it," he says. "The alignment event. The two waveforms running together." She looks at the desk. "Pela is going to find what it means." She pauses. "And when she does, we're going to know the shape of — whatever we were to each other." She is looking at the desk and not at him. "Before." He is quiet. "How do you feel about that," she says. He looks at her. The question is surprising from her — not the content of it but the directness, the asking about feeling rather than about data or strategy or the correct next step. She is looking at the desk and waiting for an answer, and her jaw has the slight tightness that it has when she has said something that cost her something to say. "I don't know yet," he says. "I know it matters. I know that the shape of it is going to change how I understand certain things about my own life." He pauses. "I know that the idea of it being a single thing — that whatever we had was held together rather than separately — is—" He stops. He looks at the ward ceiling. "I don't have a word for that yet." "No," she says. "Neither do I." They sit in the ward in the quiet of the early afternoon, in the chairs that are one closer together than the previous arrangement, and Natasha writes in her chart and the ward does its work and the second heartbeat is below them, always below them, steady and patient and counting faster than it was yesterday. "The next entry," he says. "Yes," she says. "We need the junction twelve formation data from Aventurine's scanner before we plan it," he says. "And we need the brace loading recalibrated. And you need the rib to be stable." "How long for the rib," she says. "Natasha said—" "I know what Natasha said," she says, sharply. "How long do you think." He looks at her. "Three days minimum before you should be taking impacts," he says. "The fracture needs to set." She looks at the desk. "Three days," she says. "Yes." "That's three days of the Stellaron's activation increasing," she says. "Three days of the seal fraying without progress from our end." "Three days of you not getting hit in a fractured rib by a Chrysalis form that is larger and faster than the previous entry's forms," he says, with more sharpness than he planned. She looks at him. He looks at her. The ward is quiet around them. "You're worried," she says. "I'm noting a constraint," he says. "You're worried," she says, again, and this time there is no hostility in it — she is not using it as a weapon, she is simply stating what she sees, with the directness she applies to all observations. "You've been worried since the impact at junction eleven." He holds her gaze. "Yes," he says, because the honest version is always the correct version and she has asked him directly. Something in her face does the thing — the flash, the unguarded moment, gone before it surfaces fully. She looks away. She looks at the desk. "Three days," she says. "Fine." He exhales. "Thank you." "Don't thank me," she says. "I'm accepting a correct constraint, not doing you a favor." She pauses. "But—" She stops. She starts again, and this time the sentence clearly costs her something in the starting. "But — I appreciate that you said it. Instead of just noting the constraint without the worry behind it." He looks at her. She is looking at the desk. "Seele," he says. "Don't make it a thing," she says, immediately, which is Pela's line delivered in Seele's voice, and it lands strangely and correctly simultaneously, and the corner of his mouth does something it shouldn't. She sees it. "Don't," she says. "I wasn't—" "You were about to," she says, with a certainty that is annoying because it is accurate. "Whatever expression that was, don't." He schools his expression. He looks at the desk. He is extremely disciplined about the expression for the remainder of their time in the ward, which is approximately eight more minutes, and which Natasha spends at the front desk doing her chart-writing with the focused serenity of someone who has heard everything through the thin ward walls and has chosen to be professionally uninvolved. They leave the ward at 1424 hours. Outside, the outer machine district goes about its afternoon. The generators cycle. The amber lamp light has reached its daytime peak. Somewhere in the main corridor a group of district children are playing something that involves running and what sounds like rules that are being contested, the specific contested-rules quality of children's games that exist at the boundary between game and argument. They stop at the district's main junction — the place where the outer machine district's central path branches toward the workshop on one side and toward the broader Underworld on the other. The place where their routes diverge. She looks at the workshop direction. He looks at the Underworld direction. "Three days," she says. "Three days," he says. "You'll have the recalibration data from Svarog by tomorrow." "And the Aventurine scanner analysis from Pela by tonight if she's slept." "She probably hasn't slept," Seele says. "Bronya was going to make her," he says. "Nobody makes Pela do anything," Seele says, which is accurate and which they both know, and the corner of her mouth does a very small, very brief movement that is the first time he has seen that specific movement from her face outside of the context of the hostile wariness dissolving momentarily. He looks at it for the half-second it exists. She looks toward the workshop direction. "Three days," she says. "Three days," he says. She goes. He watches her go for one second, two, and then he turns toward the workshop direction too, and below them the second heartbeat counts on, and the outer machine district does its afternoon, and somewhere in the deep tunnel past junction twelve the light that is old gold and deep water pulses slow and patient and entirely certain that what it is waiting for is coming. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 21: "Three Days" Chapter Text The first of the three days belongs mostly to Pela. She sleeps for four hours — not because she accepted Bronya's instruction gracefully, but because she fell asleep at her desk at 1100 hours with the junction twelve waveform data open in front of her and did not wake up until 1500, which Bronya discovers when she comes to check and finds Pela's head on her folded arms on the analysis desk, her pen still in her hand. Bronya takes the pen carefully from her fingers, sets it on the desk, and closes the door. She is awake at 1500 with the specific alertness of someone whose body has collected a minimum debt and considers the account settled. By 1700 she has something. She sends it to Bronya, Gepard, and — because Pela does not make decisions about information access based on anything other than who needs the information — Aventurine and Topaz simultaneously, with a header that reads: Preliminary findings, junction twelve alignment event and bifurcated signal analysis. She does not send it to Seele or Luka, which is the one piece of discretion she exercises, and which she exercises because Bronya asked her to this morning over the plate of food, saying: give me twelve hours with the findings before they see them. Pela had looked at her and said: twelve hours and then they get it regardless. Bronya had said: yes, and meant it. Gepard reads Pela's findings at his desk at the Sector A post while the evening patrol completes its first circuit. He reads it once. He reads it again. He sits with it for a moment. Then he puts on his coat and goes to find Bronya. —— She is in the construction chamber. Of course she is. The construction chamber is where she goes when something is large enough that the desk cannot contain it, and Pela's findings are the largest thing the analysis has produced since the array. He comes through the door and she is standing at the two-meter mark, not looking at the array but at the floor, and when he closes the door she says: "I read it." "Yes," he says. "Three seconds of audio from the branch," she says. "Two children's voices. Pela ran the audio through Svarog's analysis tools — the ones she borrowed this afternoon." She pauses. "The voices are identifiable." "Seele and Luka," he says. "Yes." She looks up from the floor. "Seven years old, Pela estimates, based on vocal timbre analysis. Possibly eight." She pauses. "They were seven or eight years old when whatever happened in the dark winter happened." She looks at him. "Not nine. Not ten. Seven or eight." He holds this. Seven or eight years old. The specific weight of that — not the abstraction of two children who were close, but the specific weight of how young seven or eight is, how much world a person has not yet encountered at seven or eight, how complete and total and foundational a friendship at that age is because at that age a person does not yet have the vocabulary for the complexity of what they feel and so simply feels all of it without management — this lands in the office with the specific quality of something that required the audio analysis to become real, that was known in outline and is now known in fact. "What else," he says, because Pela's findings ran to six pages and he read them twice but some of what they contain he wants to hear from her. "The alignment event," she says. "The two waveforms running at the same frequency for three seconds. Pela's analysis is—" She pauses. "It's not a resonance synchronization. It's a recognition event. The two signatures aligning means the source recognizing that both of them are present simultaneously at close range — not just individually, but together. The Stellaron recognizing the specific configuration that was present at the time of sealing." She looks at the array. "The source recognizes them as a pair. Not as two individuals." A pause. "It's been waiting for the pair. Not for either of them separately." He is quiet. "Which means," he says, "that neither of them alone could have triggered the seal fraying." "No," she says. "Both of them returning to the district — the proximity of the pair in the place where the sealing happened — that's what began it six weeks ago." She looks at him. "Luka went to the tunnels first. But the behavioral shift in the Stellaron began after the first time they were in the tunnel together. Not after Luka's first solo entry." She pauses. "Pela identified this from the output data. The behavioral shift timestamp correlates exactly to the first time they were both in nine-beta." He thinks about the junction. The branch going left. The first time they stopped together at seven-alpha-four. "They found each other before they knew they were looking," he says. She looks at him. Her expression does the thing — the less-sealed version, warmer, the one that surfaces when something has moved her and she is not suppressing it. "Yes," she says. "That's exactly what happened." The construction chamber is quiet. The array hums at its maintenance charge. Below them, twenty-three meters down, the first Stellaron sits at 1.3 and holds. Below the outer machine district, further below than the first Stellaron's chamber, the second one counts toward its moment. "The activation timeline," he says. "Svarog's assessment — that the Stellaron's timeline and the pace of the remembering may not be synchronized." "Yes," she says. "That's the part I've been standing here thinking about." She turns from the array to face him fully. "Pela's energy output analysis shows the second Stellaron's activation rate is currently accelerating at approximately twelve percent per week. At the current rate, the full activation — the seal breaking completely — is approximately five to seven weeks away." She pauses. "The remembering is not moving at twelve percent per week. It's moving at whatever pace the proximity and the fragments allow." She looks at him. "If the seal breaks before they understand what they're remembering — before they have the context to hold the full weight of what the Stellaron is going to give back to them—" "The activation without context," he says. "The activation without context," she confirms. "Twenty-two years of sealed memory returning without the framework to receive it." She looks at the array. "For both of them simultaneously." She pauses. "I don't know what that does to a person." He does not know either. Nobody in Belobog has the vocabulary for it, because nobody in Belobog has done this before. "They need to go back in," he says. "Yes. And they need to go in knowing that the pace matters now." She meets his gaze. "Not so fast that they face what's past twelve before they're ready. Not so slow that the activation outpaces them." She pauses. "It's a narrow window." "How narrow." "Pela's estimate is that they have approximately three to four more corridor entries before the activation rate reaches a threshold where the seal's integrity becomes unpredictable." She pauses. "Three to four entries to get far enough that they're remembering at a rate that keeps pace with the Stellaron." He is quiet for a moment. He looks at the array. He looks at her. In the lamplight of the construction chamber she has the quality she has had since the array ran — slightly lighter, slightly less sealed at the edges — but there is also something new in it tonight, the quality of someone who has read a six-page analysis document and arrived at the weight of what it contains and is deciding how to carry it. He crosses the room. He stops in front of her — close, the stairwell distance, the distance that requires both of them to choose it — and he puts his hand on the side of her face, slowly, the deliberate placement that she gave him first and which he has learned to give back. She tilts her face up. Her hands come to rest on his chest, over the armor, the same placement as the construction chamber three weeks ago. "We'll figure it out," he says. "I know," she says. And then: "I hate that I don't know how." "You don't have to know how right now," he says. "Right now you know what the problem is. That's enough for tonight." She looks at him. "You always say that," she says, which is not a complaint. "Because it's always been true," he says. She breathes — in and out, the controlled breath, except at this range and with his hand on her face the controlled breath is doing less controlling than usual, the edges of it more present than she probably intends. He watches her decide something. He watches the decision arrive. She turns her head slightly and presses her lips to his palm. Which is what he did to her hand in the stairwell, the confirmation that arrived after everything else had already done the work, and she is doing it now, deliberately, with the full weight of choosing it. He is very still. She pulls back, just slightly, to look at him. Her eyes are very grey in the lamplight and entirely unmanaged. "Okay," she says, which is the word they use when something has been established and both of them know it. "Okay," he says. He leans down and presses his lips to her forehead — not a grand gesture, not the culmination of something, just the placing of something real in a real place — and she closes her eyes. They stand like that in the construction chamber for a moment, his lips on her forehead and her hands on his chest and the array humming and the city above them and the Stellaron below and the five to seven weeks on Pela's timeline, and none of the large things go away, and the warmth is there anyway, real and specific and entirely theirs. —— The second day belongs to Aventurine. He delivers the IPC's preliminary assessment at 1000 hours in the council chamber, with Topaz beside him and the full documentation — Pela's analysis, the tunnel data, the scanner readings, the recorder output, Svarog's monitoring records — spread across the table. The delivery is different from what Gepard expected, which is to say it is better. Aventurine presents without the performance. He sits at the table rather than positioning himself strategically, he does not arrange the light, he does not deploy the entertained expression. He looks like someone who has been reading data for four days and has arrived at conclusions he takes seriously, and he delivers those conclusions in the register of those conclusions. "The IPC's preliminary finding," he says, "is that Belobog is managing its primary Stellaron situation adequately and with a level of analytical sophistication that significantly exceeds what our monitoring data predicted." He pauses. "The fractured resonance solution, the array, the feedback loop disruption — this is not improvised crisis management. This is genuine understanding applied to a novel problem." He looks at Bronya. "The documentation supports this. The array supports this. The people in this room support this." Bronya holds his gaze. "And the second Stellaron." "The second Stellaron is a different category," he says. "Not because Belobog is handling it inadequately — the investigation has been methodical and the findings are significant. But because the second Stellaron's situation is genuinely unprecedented in the IPC's records." He pauses. "A repository Stellaron. A deliberate sealing event by an Aeon. A stored content of this specificity and personal scale." He pauses again. "The IPC does not have a framework for this. We have models for Stellaron activation, containment, disruption. We do not have models for Stellaron retrieval — for the deliberate unsealing of a Stellaron that was sealed with intent." "Because it hasn't been done before," Topaz says, which is the clarification she provides when Aventurine's delivery requires it. "Because it hasn't been done before," he confirms. "Which means the IPC's formal position is that the second Stellaron requires ongoing monitoring and collaborative investigation — not intervention, not external management, but partnership." He looks at Bronya. "We want to be in the room. Not running the room." A pause. "Those are different things." Bronya looks at him. She holds the look for a long moment — the full-attention assessment, reading him the way she read him the first day, except now with four days of data to inform the reading. "I accept those terms," she says. Something in Aventurine's expression relaxes fractionally — not relief exactly, more the specific easing of a person who has been making a case they believe in and has found the ground give in the right direction. "Good," he says. "I have a condition," she says. "Of course you do," he says, with the first appearance of the genuine entertained quality since he began the formal delivery. "The outer machine district," she says. "The people who live there. The dark winter, the records purge, the twenty-two years of carrying something that was taken from them." She looks at him steadily. "The IPC's monitoring data goes back forty-three years. You have records that the Surface Authority purged. Records that belong to those people." She pauses. "I want them returned. Not to the IPC. Not to the palace. To the community." She pauses. "Whatever those records contain — whatever was in the outer machine district twenty-two years ago — the people it happened to deserve to know." Aventurine looks at her. Topaz looks at him. "The records are in our archive," Topaz says, carefully. "Fragmentary — the IPC's monitoring at that time was limited to energy output data. But there are incident logs. Field observations from the monitoring team present at the time of the spike event." She pauses. "Including some that were not included in what we shared in the council session." "I know," Bronya says, which means she suspected, which means she has been waiting for this moment to say so. "I want those too." Aventurine is quiet for a moment. He looks at Topaz. She looks at him. The warm watchful private quality of her attention on him in the Stellaron's chamber is present now too — she is watching him make a decision, and the watching is not pressure, it is something more like support. "Agreed," he says. "We'll provide the full archive on the outer machine district incident. Everything we have." He pauses. "It's going to be incomplete. The monitoring at that time was not comprehensive." "Incomplete is better than nothing," Bronya says. "It's significantly better than what they have now, which is a system failure that wasn't a system failure." Aventurine nods. "We'll have it transferred by end of week." The council chamber is quiet for a moment. Gepard, who has been watching this exchange with the careful attention he brings to every room, looks at Bronya. She is looking at the table with an expression that is not the Guardian's face and not entirely the other face — something between them, the face of someone who has just done something they consider important and is taking a moment to acknowledge that it is done. "There's one more thing," Aventurine says. She looks up. "The two people in the tunnels," he says. "The scout run. The waveform alignment." He looks at the table. "I want to propose something that is outside the formal assessment parameters and which Topaz is going to tell me is not standard IPC methodology." "It is not standard IPC methodology," Topaz says. "No," he agrees, without apology. "But I think it's the right thing." He looks at Bronya. "When the time comes — when they go back in for the entries that matter — I want to be in the corridor." He meets her gaze. "Not to assess. Not to document for the IPC." He pauses. "Because whatever is in that Stellaron belongs to people who are going to need someone with external analytical capacity in that corridor. And because—" He stops. He starts again, in the stripped version. "Because I've been reading Svarog's records. I've been reading the monitoring data from twenty-two years ago. And what happened to those children in that district was—" He stops again. His jaw does a very small tight movement that is not unlike Seele's jaw movement, and which on him is even more surprising. "It mattered," he says. "What was done. And what it took from them." He looks at the table. "I don't want to read the data on this and then stand outside when the answer arrives." The council chamber is very quiet. Bronya looks at him for a long moment. "I'll consider it," she says. Which, from Bronya, is not a deflection — it is the answer she gives when she means I am taking this seriously and will give you the honest version when I have it. Aventurine nods. Topaz is looking at him. The warm watchful private quality is fully present, and this time it is not watching him make a decision — it is watching him after a decision, the watching that comes after, the one that is simply: I see you. He does not look at her for a moment. Then he does. Something passes between them that the council chamber and everyone in it receives without comment, in the way that people receive things that are not theirs to receive and choose to be appropriately invisible about. Gepard looks at the table. He looks at his patrol folder. He reads a line of it. —— The second day's afternoon belongs to Svarog and Bronya. She comes to the workshop at 1500. Not with Gepard — alone, which is its own statement, the statement of someone who is coming to a conversation as herself rather than as the Guardian with her General. Clara is there, at her stool, doing something with a set of frequency calibration components that Svarog has apparently decided she is ready to work with, given the level of focus she is applying. She looks up when Bronya comes in. The evaluative glance — the quick child's version of assessment — and then back to the components, with the specific awareness of a twelve-year-old who understands that adults sometimes need space to talk without her. Svarog looks at Bronya. Bronya looks at Svarog. "I want to understand you," she says, directly, because Bronya does not circle things when she has decided to approach them. "Not as an asset or a resource or a historical record. As someone who has been in this city — in this district — for longer than anyone else alive." She pauses. "As someone who chose to stay." Svarog is quiet for a moment. His four hands, which are always doing something, are still. "I did not choose to stay in the way you mean," he says. "I was not offered departure. I was built here. I have always been here." He pauses. "But I have chosen, many times, to continue to exist in ways that serve this district rather than ways that do not." He looks at her with the optical sensors. "The choosing has been ongoing. It continues." "Why," she says. "Because the people here are worth the choosing," he says, simply. "Every one of them. Including the ones the surface forgot about. Including the ones whose records were purged." He pauses. "Especially those." She looks at him. "Protecting humanity is your responsibility," she says, echoing what Aventurine reported, Svarog's words from the workshop. "Yes," he says. "All of it," she says. "Every district." "Yes." She is quiet for a moment. "I want to be someone you can come to," she says. "When you find something in this district that needs to reach the surface. Not the administrative board. Not an official channel." She meets his optical sensor gaze directly, the way you meet the gaze of something that sees more than is comfortable. "Me." Svarog is very still. "I filed a report twenty-two years ago," he says. "The board did not respond." "I know," she says. "And I cannot undo that. I can only make sure it doesn't happen again." She pauses. "I'm asking you to trust that." Another pause. "I understand if you need more than asking." Svarog looks at her for a long time. The optical sensors do what they always do — see more than is comfortable — and what they see in her, apparently, is sufficient, because he says: "I will come to you," he says. "When something requires it." "Thank you," she says. Clara, at her stool, has gone very still with the calibration components in her small hands, the focused not-looking that is her method of being fully present without being obtrusive. She is listening to every word. She is filing it in the same place she files everything — the careful internal archive of a twelve-year-old who has learned that paying attention is the most important skill available to someone her size in a world that does not always notice her. "Clara," Bronya says. Clara looks up. The wide eyes. The serious expression. The feet not quite reaching the floor. "Topaz mentioned you specifically when she talked about Belobog," Bronya says. "Not many things made it into those conversations. You did." Clara is quiet for a moment. "I know," she says, carefully. "She said something similar when she was here." She looks at the calibration components. "Topaz is—" She pauses, selecting the word with the same care she always uses. "She is careful about what she says. So when she says something, it is because she means it." She looks up. "I hope she comes back. I have questions for her." "What kind of questions?" Bronya says. "About the source code," Clara says. "What it is and why she had it and what she intended for it." She looks at the components. "Svarog says the source code was a foundation and what I have built on it is my own. I believe him. But I want to understand where the foundation came from." She pauses. "It seems important to understand where things come from." Bronya looks at her for a moment. She looks at Svarog. She looks back at Clara. "Yes," she says. "It does." Clara nods, accepting this, and returns to the calibration components. Her hands are small and careful and they do the work with the precision of someone who has been learning from the best available teacher. Svarog watches her work with the optical sensor expression that serves for fond. Bronya watches them both. "I'll come back," she says. "When there's more to discuss." "I will be here," Svarog says. Which is, for Svarog, the most complete promise available. —— The third day. Seele spends it in motion, because stillness and three days of enforced tunnel abstinence are not compatible with her constitution. She does the civilian alert coordination for the outer district relocation — the residents who declined the first offer are being approached again, gently, with more complete information now that Bronya has decided the community deserves the accurate picture. She coordinates with Natasha on the medical capacity in the ward. She reads Pela's analysis three times and argues with several of its conclusions in the margins of her copy in handwriting that is precise and angular and leaves no room for ambiguity. At 1700 she is at the junction of the main Underworld corridor and the path toward the outer machine district, standing in the specific way she stands when she is waiting for something she has decided not to say she is waiting for. Luka comes from the workshop direction at 1703. He sees her. She sees him. "The brace recalibration," he says. "Svarog finished it this afternoon. The loading tolerance is now rated for the force output we measured from the junction eleven form." He pauses. "It'll hold the next entry." "Good," she says. "The rib is stable." She says it in the tone of someone reporting a fact they have confirmed with Natasha this morning and are not going to elaborate on. "Good," he says. They stand at the junction in the amber lamp light of the Underworld's late afternoon. The district is at its quietest hour — the generators cycling through their pre-evening check, the specific rhythm of the place doing what it has always done, patient and mechanical and steady. "Pela's analysis," she says. "I read it," he says. "Your conclusions?" she says, which is her version of asking what he thinks — never asking what he thinks directly, always asking what his conclusions are, as if the framing of it as analysis rather than opinion makes it more acceptable to request. He looks at her. "Seven or eight years old," he says. She is quiet. "We were seven or eight," he says. "Whatever we were to each other — whatever happened in the dark winter — we were seven or eight years old when it was taken from us." He pauses. "That's—" He stops. He looks at the junction wall. "That's a long time to have been missing something from that age." She is quiet for longer. "Yes," she says, finally. "It is." She looks at the wall too. "The alignment event. The pair recognition." "The Stellaron has been waiting for both of us," he says. "Not separately. Together." "Yes," she says. And then, carefully, with the precision she applies to things that cost something to say: "Which means the six weeks. Since you started coming to the tunnels." She pauses. "I started coming because you were here. I came to do supply checks in corridors where you were doing walkthroughs, and I—" She pauses. "I didn't know why I kept coming back. I thought it was the supply checks. I thought it was the crystallization data." She looks at her hands. "It wasn't the crystallization data." He looks at her. He does not say anything. "I was coming back because you were there," she says. Flat. Factual. The way she states things that are true and uncomfortable. "And I don't—" She stops. She starts again. "I don't have an explanation for that that isn't connected to all of this." She gestures, slightly, in the direction of the tunnel. "The Stellaron. The pair recognition. The seal fraying because we're in proximity." She looks at him. "Which means I can't tell how much of what I—" She stops very deliberately, cutting off the end of the sentence before it arrives. He waits. She does not finish the sentence. "Seele," he says. "Don't," she says, immediately, and this time the hostility is fully back — sharp, real, the edge of it. "Don't ask me to finish that." "I wasn't going to ask you to finish it," he says. She looks at him. Her jaw is set. Her eyes are very purple in the lamp light. "Then what were you going to say." "I was going to say that I have the same problem," he says. "The same question about how much of what I—" He mirrors her deliberate stop. "Is mine and how much is the Stellaron's proximity." He meets her gaze. "And I don't have an answer either." She holds his gaze for a long moment. "It's an unanswerable question right now," she says. "Yes," he says. "We don't have enough context." "No," he says. "We won't have context until the seal breaks," she says. "Until we remember." "No," he says. "We won't." She breathes. The controlled breath — in and out. "Then we don't answer it," she says. "Not yet." She holds his gaze with the expression that is not the hostile wariness and is not the young uncertainty and is not the unguarded flash. Something new. Something that is what comes after you've admitted to someone that you have an unanswerable question and they've said they have it too — a kind of shared standing in the unknown, which is less comfortable than standing in it alone and also, somehow, less heavy. "We go back in tomorrow. We find what's past twelve. We remember what we need to remember." She pauses. "And then we answer the question." He looks at her. "Agreed," he says. She nods. She turns toward the outer district. "Seele," he says. She turns back. Waits. "The laughing," he says. "In the fragment." He holds her gaze. "Whatever the Stellaron gives back — I think that's in there too. The laughing." He pauses. "I think we were — whoever we were to each other — I think we were also that." She looks at him. The expression does the thing — all the way this time, not cut short, the full unguarded version. It is not happy exactly. It is something more complicated than happy, something that is aware of twenty-two years of the absence of it and is feeling the weight of that alongside the warmth of the idea of it. She looks at the tunnel direction. "Tomorrow," she says. "Tomorrow," he says. She goes toward the outer district. He watches her go for two seconds, three, and then he turns back toward the workshop. The second heartbeat is below, steady, patient. It is counting faster. —— At 2300 hours, Gepard is at Outpost B with the patrol report he is actually reading this time — or was, until his phone lit up twenty minutes ago and has now been face-down on the desk for nineteen minutes and forty seconds while he does not read the patrol report. He picks up the phone. Lady Bronya: 'Are you at the post.' -'Yes.' Lady Bronya: 'I've been thinking about what Aventurine said. In the council chamber.' -'Which part.' Lady Bronya: 'That he wants to be in the corridor when it matters. That reading the data and standing outside when the answer arrives are different things.' He reads this. -'Yes.' Lady Bronya: 'I think he should be there. When they go for the deep entries.' -'You trust him.' A pause. Longer than her usual pauses. Lady Bronya: 'I trust that he means what he said. About it mattering.' Another pause. 'I think he has seen things in the IPC's records about what happened in that district twenty-two years ago that affect him. I don't know what. But I think the affect is genuine.' -'Topaz watches him carefully,' he sends. 'The way you watch someone you know well in the presence of something that costs them something.' Lady Bronya: 'I noticed that too.' -'She knew about the second Stellaron before the letter,' he sends. 'He confirmed it in the Stellaron's chamber. And she was watching him look at the first Stellaron like someone looking at something they have a history with.' He pauses. 'I don't know what the history is.' Lady Bronya: 'Neither do I. Yet.' He reads this and thinks about the yet, which is a very Bronya word in a very Bronya sentence, the word that means I don't know this thing and I intend to know it eventually and both of those facts are present simultaneously. -'Pela's timeline,' he sends. 'Five to seven weeks for the activation threshold.' Lady Bronya: 'I know.' -'Three to four entries before the seal's integrity becomes unpredictable.' Lady Bronya: 'I know, Gepard.' -'I'm going to be in the corridor for the deep entries,' he sends. 'Not just as coordination. In it.' A pause. Lady Bronya: 'I know that too.' -'You're going to tell me the same thing I told you. About not both of us being in the corridor.' Lady Bronya: 'Yes.' -'And you're going to tell me the array argument. That someone who understands the Stellaron's frequency needs to be reachable above ground.' Lady Bronya: 'Yes.' -'And you're going to tell me this even though you want to be in the corridor yourself.' A longer pause. Lady Bronya: 'I'm going to tell you these things because they're true, Gepard. Not because I'm not afraid for you.' He sits with this. He sits with it in the outpost at 2300 hours with the patrol report face-up on the desk and the lamp humming and the outer district somewhere below the city's surface doing what it has always done, and the phone in his hand carrying the most honest thing she has said to him today. -'I know,' he sends. Lady Bronya: 'Stop saying that.' -'I know,' he sends again, because he cannot help it, and because she has told him to stop and he is saying it anyway, and they both understand what that means. Lady Bronya: 'Impossible man.' -'Goodnight, Bronya.' A pause. Shorter than usual. Lady Bronya: 'Goodnight, Gepard.' He puts the phone face-down on the desk. He picks up the patrol report. He reads it — actually reads it, all the way through, retaining every line — because tomorrow they go back into the tunnel and he needs to know the perimeter status before they do. He reads. Outside, the snow falls softly on Belobog. The wall stands. The first Stellaron holds at 1.3. The second one counts in the dark below the outer machine district, patient and very certain, toward the moment the seal comes apart. Three to four entries. He reads. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 22: "Past Twelve" Chapter Text The Moles find Clara on a Tuesday. This is not remarkable in itself — the Moles find everyone eventually, because finding people and inserting themselves into situations is what the Moles do, and the outer machine district is not a large place, and a twelve-year-old girl who spends most of her time in a workshop with a four-armed automaton is, to three children who run salvage operations through the Underworld's secondary tunnel networks, a person of obvious interest. What is slightly remarkable is that they find her at exactly the wrong moment, which is to say the moment when the workshop door is open and Clara is carrying a set of frequency calibration components from Svarog's storage shelves to the secondary worktable and drops half of them when three children appear in the doorway without knocking. "Oh!" she says, which is the most expressive thing she has said in days and is directed at the floor where the components are now scattered. The three children in the doorway look at the components on the floor. They look at each other. The tallest one — a girl with red hair and the specific wiry build of someone who climbs things regularly — goes to her knees and starts collecting components without being asked. "Sorry," she says, to the floor. "We didn't know anyone was in here." "The door was open," Clara says, also collecting components. "Open doors are usually—" the red-haired girl starts. "An invitation, yes," Clara says. "I know. I left it open because I was carrying things and couldn't close it." She straightens with her half of the components. "It wasn't an invitation." The three children look at her. She looks at them. This exchange has the specific quality of children assessing each other — not hostile, not friendly, the calibration that precedes both. "I'm Toma," the red-haired girl says. She gestures. "That's Pino." The smaller boy with the mismatched jacket gives a short nod. "And that's Oleg." The third, stocky, with the expression of someone who has been in enough situations to develop a default neutrality, raises a hand briefly. "Clara," Clara says. "We know," Pino says. "You're the one who lives in Svarog's workshop." "I don't live here," Clara says. "I work here." "Same thing, basically," Oleg says. "It's not the same thing," Clara says, with the precision of someone for whom distinctions matter. Toma looks at the calibration components in Clara's hands. "What are those?" "Frequency calibration components," Clara says. "For a resonance recorder." Toma looks at them. Looks at Clara. "What's a resonance recorder?" "A device that captures frequency output from a Stellaron source," Clara says, and then stops, because Pino has made a face that is the specific face of someone who understood less than half of what was just said and is deciding whether to admit it. "Cool," Oleg says, which is not a response to the content but to the general category of the thing, the way children say cool when something is clearly complicated and complicated is impressive regardless of whether you understand it. Clara looks at them for a moment. She looks at the components. She looks at the workshop, which is empty — Svarog is in the back room, which means she is technically alone in the front, which means she has been alone in the front for the past forty minutes doing careful calibration work and talking to nobody, which is a thing she does frequently and which, she is becoming aware in the way children become aware of things, she does not entirely prefer to the alternative. "Do you want to see how the calibration works?" she says. Toma's face does the thing. The face of a twelve-year-old being offered something genuinely interesting. "Yes," she says, immediately. "You have to not touch anything I don't say you can touch," Clara says. "Svarog's workshop has components that are — Svarog's components." "Obviously," Oleg says, in the tone of someone who considers himself a professional. They come in. —— The entry into the corridor on the fourth day happens at 0700, and the group is larger than the scout run but smaller than the first joint entry. Gepard. Seele. Luka. Aventurine. Topaz does not come — she is above ground coordinating the transfer of the IPC's outer machine district archive with Pela, which is work that matters and which Topaz is the correct person for, and which also means that Aventurine is in the corridor without his usual point of reference, which Gepard has been watching him calibrate to since the group assembled at the entrance. He is calibrating well. Aventurine without Topaz is not a diminished version of Aventurine — he is a different configuration of the same thing, the reading-rooms quality more outward, less filtered, the lack of the usual anchor making him fractionally more attentive to the group he is with. He is wearing the outer district cold-gear rather than his usual coat, which means he prepared, and he has his IPC scanner in a chest mount alongside a second piece of equipment that Gepard does not recognize. "What is that?" Gepard asks, gesturing at the second device. "Cornerstone," Aventurine says, which is not an explanation. "That doesn't tell me what it does," Gepard says. Aventurine looks at him with the entertained expression — genuine, brief. "It generates a defense field," he says. "Probability-weighted. Under high-risk conditions." He pauses. "If something in that corridor tries to kill one of you, the Cornerstone improves your odds of survival. Not your odds of avoiding the hit — your odds of surviving it." Gepard looks at the device. He looks at Aventurine. "You've been in worse corridors than this," he says, which is not quite a question. "Much worse," Aventurine says, pleasantly, in the tone of someone who has files of experience he is choosing not to open right now. Seele is running the frequency recorder calibration at the corridor entrance. She does it without looking at the display, which means she has done it enough times to have memorized the sequence. The cut above her eyebrow has faded to a thin pale line. The rib is wrapped and stable — Natasha confirmed it this morning at 0630, the earliest appointment available, which Seele had booked the night before. Luka is checking the recalibrated combat braces. He runs a loading test on the prosthetic arm — a specific rotation sequence that tests the joint tolerance under the new brace parameters — and the test returns clean. He looks at Seele. She is still looking at the recorder, but she was looking at him a half-second ago, which he knows because the quality of her looking-at-the-recorder is slightly different from her looking-at-the-recorder when she was not just looking at him. He says nothing about this. "Ready," she says. "Ready," he says. Aventurine looks at the corridor entrance with the expression of someone who has been reading about a place for several days and is now looking at it directly. He steps forward. "After you," Gepard says. "After me," Aventurine agrees, and goes in. —— The second heartbeat at junction one is louder than the scout run. Luka logs this without breaking stride and sends the notation to Seele's recorder display via the paired connection Svarog set up between the devices this morning. The recorder confirms it on the bifurcated signal: both signatures elevated above scout run baseline. The Stellaron has been active in the three days since the scout run, and whatever it has been doing in that time has produced a measurable increase in output. Aventurine's scanner is registering something at junction three. He does not slow down — he logs it in motion, which means he has also been practicing the in-motion logging, which means he prepared for this entry more thoroughly than he has been indicating. "Directional signal is significantly cleaner than the surface data," he says, at junction four. "Three days ago the source read at forty meters from the surface. Current reading puts it at thirty-seven." He pauses. "The source is closer." "It moved?" Gepard says, from the front. "The source is expanding," Aventurine says. "The Stellaron's physical footprint is growing as the activation increases." He pauses. "Slowly. But measurably." He looks at the scanner. "At the current expansion rate, it will reach the tunnel floor at junction twelve in approximately—" He does a calculation that appears to be internal and rapid. "Eleven days." Seele, who is walking level with Luka, says: "When it reaches the tunnel floor it becomes accessible." "Yes," Aventurine says. "Directly accessible. Not through the bedrock fissure — actually accessible. The chamber will be enterable." He pauses. "Which is either the best possible development or the most dangerous one, depending on what the full activation looks like." "Probably both," Luka says. "Probably both," Aventurine agrees. Junction eight. The carved stone begins. The torch light. The ceiling dropping. Gepard's automatic pauldron adjustment. Aventurine, who is taller than Gepard by several centimeters, makes the same adjustment half a second later with the unconscious ease of someone who has navigated confined spaces under worse conditions than a ceiling drop. He does not comment on the carved stone the way he did during the first group entry — he has already processed the pre-Separation construction, already integrated it, his attention focused on the scanner readings rather than the architecture. The secondary module vibration reading at junction eight is already higher than the scout run's junction nine reading. "Elevated at eight," Luka says. "I see it," Seele says. "The formation threshold may be earlier than nineteen minutes," he says. "Then we move faster," she says. They move faster. Junction nine. The smell of the corridor — machine oil and cold metal and the specific electrical ozone, the smell that stopped being atmospheric and became something more specific since the fragment at junction five, since Sampo's moment in the workshop. Junction ten. The vibration note audible now — that almost-sound in the chest and the soles of the feet. The recorder's display showing the waveforms climbing. The secondary module reading deep orange at junction ten, which is where the scout run hit orange at eleven. "We're running hot," Luka says. "Keep moving," Seele says, and her voice has the brisk forward quality it has in motion — not cold, just directed, all of her pointing at the same thing. Junction eleven. No Chrysalis form. They pass through without stopping. The vibration at eleven is significantly above the scout run's reading here — above anything logged in any previous entry. The recorder is showing a waveform that has grown more complex at the bifurcation point, the two signatures more distinct, pulling slightly further apart before re-aligning, the way something does when it is oscillating under increasing pressure. Luka watches the display. The signatures pull further apart. Re-align. Further. Re-align. He has seen this pattern before in Svarog's frequency data — not from the Stellaron, from geomarrow under resonance load. It is what geomarrow does before a significant output event. The period of oscillation increasing before— "Junction twelve," Seele says. They stop. The chamber-like widening. The slope of the floor toward the right branch. And the light from the right branch, which is not the faint ambient glow from the scout run. Significantly brighter. The old gold and deep water now genuinely illuminating the carved stone walls of the junction, throwing their shadows long behind them. Aventurine stops beside Gepard and says nothing for a moment. He is looking at the light. His expression is doing the thing it did in the Stellaron's chamber — the stripped attention, the unperformed version, the face of someone in the presence of something that costs them something to be near. "The source expanded toward junction twelve since the scout run," he says, very quietly. "The light was not this strong three days ago." "No," Luka says. "It wasn't." "The branch," Seele says. They look at the right branch entrance. "Six minutes from entry," Gepard says. "The formation at eleven appeared at approximately sixteen minutes in the scout run. We have time." "The threshold may be different today," Luka says. "The output is higher." "Best estimate," Gepard says. Luka looks at the secondary module. "Twelve minutes from now, possibly fourteen," he says. "The oscillation pattern in the bifurcated signal—" He shows the display to Gepard. "When this closes completely — when the two signatures stop pulling apart and lock into alignment — I think that's the formation trigger. The Stellaron's output peak." Gepard looks at the display. He looks at the branch. He looks at Aventurine, who is also looking at the display. "Agreed?" he says. "The oscillation pattern is consistent with what I've seen in IPC data from other Stellaron activation events," Aventurine says. "The alignment event is likely the trigger. Your estimate is sound." "Twelve minutes," Gepard says. "We go in. We go to the branch source — not twenty meters in, all the way to the curve, to the light source. We take the full readings. We note everything. We leave before the alignment locks." He looks at Seele and Luka. "You two take the branch. Aventurine and I hold junction twelve." Seele looks at him. "The Chrysalis forms come from the branch direction." "I know," Gepard says. "Aventurine and I hold junction twelve." Aventurine has his hand on the Cornerstone device at his chest. The probability field has not activated — it sits dormant, waiting — but the hand on it is the hand of someone who knows exactly when to use what they are carrying. "We hold junction twelve," he says, to Seele, in a way that is not reassurance but is the statement of a fact about his own capacity. "Go." She looks at him for a moment. Then she turns to the branch. She and Luka go in. —— The right branch at full approach is not the branch from the scout run. The branch from the scout run was a narrow carved corridor with a curve at the end and a faint light around the curve and a secondary module reading they were not equipped to fully capture. The branch now is a space that is alive in the specific way that proximity to an active source makes a space alive — the old gold and deep water light conducting through the carved stone walls, giving them a quality that is not illumination from an external source but illumination from within, the stone itself carrying the light. They move fast. They have twelve minutes, possibly, and seven of them are already spent on the junction twelve entry. At the curve, they stop. Around the curve, the branch opens. Not the narrow corridor widening — the branch ends in a chamber. Small — three meters across, four meters deep, the ceiling low but not confining — and the chamber is the source of the light, because in the chamber's far wall the crystalline formation that was a glimpse around a curve in the scout run is now fully visible, and it is larger than either of them anticipated. It is approximately a meter and a half tall and a meter wide, growing from the bedrock with the geometric density of something that has had twenty-two years to develop. The old gold and deep water color is not the surface color — it is the color throughout, the crystalline structure lit from within, the light pulsing with the rhythm of the second heartbeat but closer now, much closer, the heartbeat not below them but here, in this room, in the formation on the wall. Seele goes very still. Luka goes very still. The frequency recorder on Seele's harness is running at full capture. The secondary module in Luka's hand is showing readings he has not seen before — not out of range, into a new range entirely, the recalibration Svarog performed giving the display the vocabulary for what is here. "Take the readings," she says, very quietly. He takes the readings. Full directional scan. Energy density profile. Crystalline structure analysis. Frequency profile at this proximity. He captures everything in the time available and sends it immediately to the paired connection with Pela's analysis terminal above ground. The formation pulses. The recorder on Seele's harness spikes. Not the recognition signal from junction seven-alpha-four — this is something larger. The display shows the bifurcated signal, both signatures, and they are not oscillating anymore. They are locked. Locked together, running at exactly the same frequency, aligned fully for the first time since the scout run's three-second event, except this time the alignment is not three seconds. It holds. It holds and holds and Luka is watching the display and the alignment is holding and the formation is pulsing and the light is everywhere in the small chamber and Seele is— He looks at her. She is looking at the formation. Her expression is the one from the stairwell debrief — not the hostile wariness, not the young uncertainty, something older than both. Something that has been waiting behind those for a long time. She is looking at the formation the way you look at something you have been looking for without knowing you were looking for it. Her hand is moving. Not the automatic reach — not the unconscious gesture. A deliberate reach, slow, her arm extending toward the formation, and he knows what will happen when she makes contact at this proximity and alignment, and he knows it is going to be significant, and he does not stop her. Her fingertips touch the formation. The chamber does something. The light doubles — not blindingly, but fully, the old gold and deep water going from the walls and ceiling and floor simultaneously, the entire chamber becoming the source, and the recorder display whites out for a moment and then comes back showing a waveform that Luka has no previous data point to compare to, and the second heartbeat is not a heartbeat anymore— It is two heartbeats. Running together. In exact synchronization. And the fragment arrives. Not his. Not hers. From the formation — from the contact — returned, the way something is returned rather than surfaced, from outside rather than inside, and what arrives is not a fragment. It is the beginning of a memory. A real one, with context, with color, with the specific complete texture of something that actually happened rather than the edge-of-image quality of the fragments they have been carrying. They are children. The tunnel smells like this. The cold is like this. There is no torch — there is the formation, which is smaller, significantly smaller, the way things are smaller when they are new. And they are children, and they know this tunnel the way they know their names, the way they know the route, and they are standing in this chamber together and the formation is new and the heartbeat is new and— The memory cuts. Not because it ends. Because something outside the chamber ends it. The sound from junction twelve. Wet. Cracking. Structural. Fragmentum matter under activation pressure. Multiple points. "Seele!" He grabs her arm — her contact hand, pulling it back from the formation — and she comes back into the present with the specific gasping quality of someone surfacing from depth. "The—" she starts. "Junction twelve," he says. "Multiple forms. We need to go." She looks at the formation. She looks at him. Her eyes are — he does not have time to catalog what her eyes are doing. He pulls on her arm and she moves and they run. —— Junction twelve has four Chrysalis forms. Four. Not three. Significantly larger than anything from the scout run — closer to the source, higher activation level, the forms denser and more saturated than any Fragmentum matter Gepard has encountered in eight years of Restricted Zone service. He has his shield up and his geomarrow charge at ninety-one percent and the ice-lance formation deployed and he is already in contact with the nearest form when Seele and Luka come out of the right branch. Aventurine is not where Gepard left him. Aventurine is between two Chrysalis forms. This is initially alarming until it becomes apparent that being between the two forms is deliberate — the Cornerstone device at his chest is active, a visible golden shimmer extending approximately a meter in every direction from him, and the forms that are hitting it are hitting probability. Not his body. The probability field absorbing the impact loading, redistributing the force across calculated outcomes, the forms striking it and finding their impact somehow less effective than physics should permit. Aventurine himself is not fighting in the conventional sense — he is moving in small precise steps within the field's radius, maintaining the exact positioning that keeps the probability advantage maximized, one hand on the Cornerstone, the other holding his scanner like it is the thing that matters, which for him it probably is. He is also, Gepard observes in the half-second available for observation, taking readings off every form that hits the probability field, which is the most Aventurine possible thing to do in this specific situation. "Left flank!" Seele snaps at Gepard, because she has assessed the formation geometry in the two seconds since leaving the branch and has the tactical picture complete. She is already moving toward the right flank, pulling the two forms on that side into her angle, away from Gepard's left. She moves the way she moves — explosively, using the junction's wider geometry better than the narrow corridor of previous entries, her reach longer in three meters of space than in one. The forms track her with the specific targeting behavior. She uses this. She pulls the tracking, one form following her left, and she drives her elbow into its structural junction with a force that cracks the upper section simultaneously with a kick to the base, and the form collapses in a single sequence. The second right-flank form reorients toward her. "Behind—!" Luka, from her right, already moving. He takes the second form off her from the right side, both combat braces engaged, hitting the base junction at the precise point Svarog's recalibration was designed for, and the form doesn't crack — it shatters, the new brace loading tolerance handling the impact distribution correctly, the crystalline structure failing completely rather than partially. He grins. Briefly. One second. It is the grin of someone whose equipment just worked exactly as designed and who finds this specifically satisfying. Seele catches it from the corner of her eye. She says nothing about it. She turns to the left flank. Gepard has one of the two left-flank forms suppressed — not collapsed, suppressed, the sustained ice-lance formation driving it back against the carved stone wall and holding it there, his charge depleting faster than he would like. The fourth form is oriented toward Aventurine's probability field, hitting it repeatedly with the specific persistence of Fragmentum matter that has not encountered probability-weighted defense before and is attempting to recalculate. Aventurine, inside the field, looks at the fourth form with the expression he wears when he is doing something. "The base junction," he says, to no one in particular, or possibly to the form itself. "Left side, thirty percent from the bottom. The threading is thinner there — I've been reading the failure points from the other forms." "Then hit it," Seele says, from across the junction. "I don't hit things," Aventurine says. "Then tell someone who does," she says. "Luka," Aventurine says, immediately, which means he has already decided who is in the best position, which means he has been tracking everyone's position in the junction during the entire engagement, which is not a surprise but is notable. Luka is already moving toward the fourth form. He takes Aventurine's structural read — left side, thirty percent from the bottom — and drives into it from the right, the combat braces absorbing the approach impact, the targeting exact. The form cracks at the threading-thin point. The Cornerstone's probability field, with the structural integrity now compromised, pushes the odds further in the same direction, and the form collapses against the calculated weight of its own failure. Gepard releases the sustained suppression on the first left-flank form and fires a concentrated ice-lance burst at its upper structure. It shatters. Four forms down. The junction is silent except for their breathing and the cooling sound of Fragmentum debris and the second heartbeat, which has not stopped and has not slowed and is if anything more present in the immediate aftermath of the chamber contact than it was going in. "Time," Gepard says. Luka checks. "Eleven minutes forty seconds." "We're under the formation threshold," Seele says. "The threshold moved," Luka says. "Four forms at eleven forty. The scout run had three forms at sixteen twelve." He looks at the corridor ahead — the way out, junctions eleven through one. "The activation rate is accelerating the formation response. We need to recalculate every entry's threshold based on current output data, not previous entry data." Gepard looks at the junction. At the four collapsed form sites. At Aventurine, who is still inside the probability field's radius, looking at the readings his scanner captured during the engagement with the focused attention of someone who has just collected a great deal of data and is beginning to sort it. "Are you injured," Gepard says. "No," Aventurine says, without looking up. "The field held through four direct impacts," Gepard says. Not a question. "It held through six," Aventurine says. "Two of them hit twice." He pauses. "The probability field degrades with sustained use. By the end of that sequence it was operating at approximately seventy-two percent efficiency." He looks up. "It won't hold a seventh impact at the same formation density as those four." Gepard looks at him. Aventurine looks back. "I told you I've been in worse corridors," he says. "You told me worse corridors," Gepard says. "You didn't tell me what you brought to those corridors." "You didn't ask," Aventurine says, pleasantly. Seele is looking at the branch entrance. She is not moving toward it. She is standing at the junction with the expression she had in the chamber — the older thing, the thing that was below the wariness and the uncertainty — and it is still present, still close to the surface, the memory that began and was cut short still working its way through whatever in her is processing it. Luka stands beside her. Not speaking. Not asking. Just beside her, at junction twelve, with the second heartbeat louder than it has been in any previous entry and the recorder still running and the light from the right branch still strong enough to reach into the junction. "The memory," she says, very quietly. To him. Not to the junction or the branch or the room — to him. "Yes," he says, equally quiet. "It started." "Yes." She breathes. In and out. "We were children," she says. "Standing in the chamber. The formation was smaller." She pauses. "You were there." He is quiet. "We were both there," she says. "In that chamber. Together. As children." She looks at the branch entrance. "That's what we're getting back." "Yes," he says. "I think that's what we're getting back." She nods. Very slowly. The nod of someone integrating something that has weight — not accepting it, not resolving it, just integrating it into what is known, adding it to the known things and seeing how the known things rearrange around it. "Gepard," she says, louder. "We need to go." "Yes," Gepard says. They go. —— Clara is in the workshop when they return. She is not alone. Toma is sitting on the floor with her back against the storage shelves, sorting a collection of salvage components that she has apparently brought in her bag, holding each one up to the workshop light and examining it with the critical eye of someone who values things by how fixable they are rather than how intact they are. Pino is on the stool — Clara's stool, though he doesn't know that, and Clara is choosing not to tell him specifically because it will produce the negotiation about the stool and the negotiation about the stool will be loud — reading something on his phone with his legs dangling. Oleg is asleep. Specifically asleep, back against the secondary worktable leg, knees up, entirely comfortable in the sleep of someone who can fall asleep anywhere and does so whenever the opportunity presents. Svarog is in the back room. Clara looks up when the corridor group comes through the door. Her eyes do the quick inventory — Gepard, Seele, Luka, Aventurine — and then the secondary inventory: who is injured, who is not, what the energy in the room is. She is twelve years old and she has been learning from Svarog for eight months and she is very good at this specific skill. "The brace held," she says to Luka, which is not a question. "Yes," he says. She nods. "Svarog will want to see the contact point data." She looks at the secondary module in his hand. "The readings." "I know," he says. Toma has looked up from the salvage components. She is looking at Seele with the specific expression of a child encountering an adult who has the particular quality of having recently been somewhere significant and brought some of it back with them. Seele's hair is still partly loose from the tunnel. The old cut above her eyebrow is a thin pale line. The expression on her face is the integrated-something expression. "Are you okay?" Toma says, to Seele, with the directness that belongs entirely to children, who have not yet learned to ask the indirect version. Seele looks at her. Something in Seele's face adjusts — the adult in a room with a child adjustment, the slight recalibration of register. "Yes," she says. "Thank you for asking." "You look like something happened," Toma says. "Something did," Seele says. "But it's alright." Toma considers this. She looks at the salvage component in her hand. "Okay," she says, and goes back to sorting, with the specific trust of a child who has been given an honest answer and chooses to believe it. Pino has not looked up from his phone. Oleg has not woken up.
