The workshop has the quality it has when it contains both adults doing significant things and children doing ordinary things and both activities are happening simultaneously in the same space, which is a quality of particular warmth — the specific warmth of a place that is being lived in on multiple levels at once. Aventurine, who has been standing in the doorway absorbing the scene, closes the workshop door. He looks at the three children. He looks at Clara, who is looking at him with her evaluative expression. "You're Aventurine," she says. "Yes," he says. "I saw you come in last time," she says. "You looked at me the way people look at me when they already know what I am." He pauses. "Topaz told me about you," he says. "I know," Clara says. "I figured that." She tilts her head slightly — a distinctly twelve-year-old gesture, the head-tilt of assessment. "Did the corridor go okay?" "Complicated," he says. "But okay," she says. "But okay," he confirms. She nods, accepting this, and goes back to whatever she was doing at the worktable. Aventurine looks at the workshop. He looks at Toma on the floor with the salvage components, and Pino on the stool with his phone, and Oleg asleep against the table leg. He looks at Clara at the worktable. He looks, specifically, at the way Clara keeps half her attention on the three children even while she is working, the slight adjustment in her posture when Toma says something, the way she tracks Pino's movement when he hops off the stool without watching him directly. The quality of a child who has been primarily among adults for a long time and is now among children again and is doing it carefully, the way you do something you are not entirely sure you remember how to do. He says nothing about this. He goes to the secondary worktable to review his scanner data, and the workshop does its afternoon around him. —— Svarog reviews the contact readings at 1600. He reviews them with the full attention of all four hands and both optical sensors, which is his most concentrated mode. Luka, beside him, watches him process what the secondary module captured in the chamber and does not interrupt. "The formation in the chamber," Svarog says, finally. "Yes," Luka says. "It is the primary access point," Svarog says. "The core of the repository. The surface fissure was a projection — the formation itself is the storage medium." He pauses. "The contact event at the formation — her contact — triggered a partial retrieval. The beginning of a memory." "Yes," Luka says. "Both of us experienced it." Svarog is quiet for a moment. "Both," he says. "Both," Luka confirms. "Same content. Same moment. We compared afterward." He pauses. "We were in that chamber. As children. Together. That's as far as it went before the forms appeared." Svarog looks at the readings. His optical sensors carry the expression that serves for several things simultaneously. "The next contact event," he says, "will produce the next segment. Each contact at the formation returns content sequentially." He pauses. "The memory was stored sequentially — in the order that it happened. What they will retrieve is what happened, in the order that it happened." Luka holds this. "Which means we need to know what happened before we experience the part that's hardest to experience." Svarog looks at him. "Yes," he says. "That would be — preferable." "But we're going to experience all of it," Luka says. "In order. We can't skip to the context." "No," Svarog says. "You cannot." Luka looks at the workshop. At Toma, who has finished with the salvage components and is now in conversation with Clara about something that appears to involve the calibration components and whether they can be used for something that Clara is gently explaining they cannot be used for, and the gentleness of the explanation is the gentleness of someone who spent a lot of time with a very patient teacher and has learned the register. At Oleg, who is awake now, eating something from his jacket pocket and watching the workshop with the default neutrality that is his expression for everything. "Svarog," he says. "Yes." "The person who told Sampo to run," he says. "Twenty-two years ago. In the generator corridor." He looks at Svarog. "Are they in what we're going to find?" Svarog is quiet for a long time. "Yes," he says. "They are in what you are going to find." Luka nods. He looks at the workshop. He looks at the secondary module data on the display, at the waveform from the chamber that is going to be the subject of Pela's analysis for the next forty-eight hours and possibly longer. "Then we go back in," he says. "As soon as the rib is cleared and the formation threshold is recalculated." He pauses. "And we find out." "Yes," Svarog says. "You will." Outside, the outer machine district does its late afternoon. The generators cycle. The amber lamps reach their evening setting. Toma is now showing Clara something on her own phone — salvage route maps, from the look of it, a thing Clara is looking at with the specific expression of someone who finds a thing interesting and is deciding not to say so immediately. Pino has taken the stool back and is leaning against the workbench. Oleg is eating. The workshop is warm and full and doing what workshops do, which is hold the work and the people who do it and the ordinary life that exists around both. Below it, the second Stellaron counts on. Eleven days until it reaches junction twelve's floor. Three to four entries until the seal's integrity becomes unpredictable. The distance between those two numbers is not comfortable. But the workshop is warm, and the children are sorting salvage and arguing gently about calibration components and eating snacks from jacket pockets, and the second heartbeat is below, steady, patient. Still patient. For now. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 23: "Pressure" Chapter Text The argument about the calibration components lasts forty-five minutes and ends with nobody winning. This is, in Toma's experience, the correct outcome of any argument with Clara, who does not argue to win and does not argue to lose — she argues to be accurate, which is a completely different objective and one that is deeply annoying to argue against because you cannot defeat it, you can only be more accurate than her, which Toma has not yet managed to do on any topic related to workshop equipment. "I'm not saying I want to use them for the route maps," Toma says, for the third time, with the emphatic patience of someone who has been misunderstood twice and is trying a third approach. "I'm saying the signal frequency range of the calibration component is similar to the range of the tunnel sensor Luka uses, and if the tunnel sensor can map distance to a signal source then why can't the calibration component do the same thing for our salvage routes—" "Because the calibration component is calibrated," Clara says. "That's the whole point of it. It's set to a specific frequency. If you use it to map a different frequency it stops being calibrated and then it can't do what it's supposed to do." "Can't you just recalibrate it after?" "Yes, but Svarog would—" "Would what?" Clara looks at the component in her hand. She looks at Toma. She makes the face — the face of someone who knows the answer and wishes the answer were different. "He would be very calm about it," she says. "He would explain exactly why it is not supposed to be used that way, and he would do it at the length and thoroughness that Svarog explains things." Toma stares at her. "How long is that?" "Long," Clara says, with the flat expressiveness of someone speaking from experience. Oleg, who has finished eating and has been half-listening from his position against the table leg, says: "Is Svarog scary?" "No," Clara says. "Then why does it matter if he explains it?" "Because when Svarog explains why you've done something wrong it's worse than being yelled at," Clara says. "He is very thorough." Pino, who is back on the stool, looks up from his phone. "My dad is like that," he says. "He never raises his voice but somehow by the end of it you feel like you've been yelled at for an hour." "Yes," Clara says. "Exactly like that." Oleg considers this. "Okay," he says. "Don't use the calibration component." Toma makes a sound that is entirely genuine exasperation — not performed, not managed, the real sound of a thirteen-year-old who was almost right about something and has been denied the satisfaction of it by an extremely specific technical limitation. She drops onto the floor next to her salvage bag and pulls out a different component entirely. "Fine. What about this one?" Clara looks at it. Her eyes go slightly bright — not with excitement exactly, but with the specific quality of someone who has just been shown something interesting and is having the interesting land in real time. "Where did you find that?" "Outer corridor eleven," Toma says. "There's a section where the old Underworld infrastructure meets the carved stone and some of the old equipment fell through. We've been pulling components for months." Clara takes the component and turns it over in her hands. It is old — significantly older than anything in Svarog's active workshop, the casing style and material consistent with pre-Separation construction. Her brows draw together in the specific way they draw together when she is thinking hard about something. "Svarog has pieces like this in the deep storage," she says. "He said they're from before." She looks at Toma. "The outer corridor eleven — is that past junction eight of nine-beta?" Toma and Oleg and Pino all look at each other. "What's nine-beta?" Oleg says. "The tunnel corridor," Clara says. "The one with the—" She stops. She looks at the workshop. At the frequency recorder on the secondary worktable that Seele left for Svarog's analysis. At the secondary module on the charging rack. At the back room where Svarog is working, and where Luka has been for the past twenty minutes giving the full debrief of the chamber contact. "The one with the thing in it," she says, which is the most a twelve-year-old who knows what the second Stellaron is and understands it is not public information can say to three other children who don't know what it is and don't need to yet. "There's a thing in the tunnel?" Pino says, with the immediate alertness of someone for whom things in tunnels are generally of interest. "There's a thing in the tunnel," Clara says. "It's being handled." "By who?" Toma says. "By the people handling it," Clara says, and her voice has the specific quality that it gets when she is being Svarog-adjacent in her communication style, which is the quality of a truth that has been compressed to fit the available context. She gives the component back to Toma. "Don't go past junction eight of nine-beta. The corridors past there are not safe right now." Toma looks at the component. She looks at Clara. She looks at Oleg. "How unsafe?" she says. "The kind where there are Fragmentum monsters that are bigger than the ones in the outer salvage corridors," Clara says. "How much bigger?" Oleg says. "Significantly," Clara says. "But the people handling it handle them?" Pino says. "Yes," Clara says. "They handle them." "Are they good at it?" Toma says. Clara thinks about Luka and Seele coming through the workshop door this morning with the dust still on them from junction twelve and the particular quality of two people who have been through something and are processing it in real time. She thinks about Seele's expression in the corridor and the cut that has healed to a thin pale line and what Luka said about the braces holding. She thinks about Aventurine with the Cornerstone active and four Chrysalis forms hitting it. "Yes," she says. "They're very good at it." "Cool," says Oleg, which is his assessment for the full range of things between slightly impressive and genuinely remarkable, and which in this case probably lands closer to the latter. —— The debrief in Svarog's back room ends at 1730. Luka comes out first, because he was giving the debrief rather than receiving it, and the receiving always takes longer than the giving. He looks at the workshop — at Toma on the floor now in conversation with Clara about the old component, at Pino still on the stool, at Oleg demonstrating something to nobody in particular involving a piece of cable and an impressive amount of confidence in his own technique — and he stops in the doorway of the back room for a moment. "Those three," he says to Svarog, who is still behind him. "The Moles," Svarog says. "They have been operating salvage routes through the secondary tunnel networks for approximately two years. They are resourceful." He pauses. "And Clara appears to find their company—" From the main workshop, Clara makes a sound that is — not her usual sound. It is a sound that belongs to the category of sounds that twelve-year-olds make when something has caught them genuinely off guard and is genuinely funny, the uncontrolled kind, and it has the specific quality of something that does not happen when Clara is alone with Svarog all day doing calibration work. Luka and Svarog both look at the workshop. Clara has one hand over her mouth. Toma is laughing openly. Oleg is holding the piece of cable with the expression of someone who has just pulled off something that he is pleased about. Pino is recording it on his phone. "—beneficial," Svarog concludes, with the optical sensor expression that serves for fond. Luka looks at this for a moment. He looks at Svarog. He turns back to the main workshop and crosses to the secondary worktable to retrieve the secondary module from the charging rack. He nearly trips over Oleg's legs. "Hey—!" Oleg says, pulling his legs in. "Watch it," Luka says, at the same time, and they both stop and look at each other with the specific startled quality of two people who nearly collided and are now assessing whether to be annoyed or not. Oleg is thirteen. He has the default-neutrality expression and the stocky build and the jacket that clearly has many pockets because many things live in them. He looks at Luka's prosthetic arm — not staring, not the uncomfortable look of someone trying not to stare, just the direct look of a child who sees something and looks at it. "That's a good prosthetic," he says. Luka looks at the arm. "Svarog modified it," he says. Oleg nods. "Ours are salvage," he says, which explains the many-pocketed jacket and possibly several other things about the Moles' operational methodology. He looks at the secondary module in Luka's hand. "Is that the thing from the tunnel?" "One of them," Luka says. "The thing that handles the monsters?" Oleg says. Luka blinks. He looks at Clara, who is watching this exchange with the expression of someone who has said something minimally and is curious how it lands. "The thing that reads the tunnel," he says. "The monsters are handled differently." "How?" Oleg says, with genuine, uncomplicated curiosity. "Combat," Luka says. Oleg looks at him. Looks at the arm again. "You fight the Fragmentum monsters in the tunnel?" "Yes," Luka says. "By yourself?" "No," Luka says. "With—" He pauses. "With someone." Oleg considers this. He looks at the workshop in general. He looks at the door through which, this morning, Seele came and sat in the corner going through the recorder data with the specific focused quality of someone processing several things at once. "The purple-haired one?" he says. Luka looks at him. "She came in this morning," Oleg says. "She didn't say anything. She just sat in the corner with the screen thing." He pauses. "She looked like she'd just been in a fight." "She had," Luka says. "Did she win?" Oleg says. Luka looks at the door. He looks at the secondary module in his hand. He thinks about the chamber and the formation and the two heartbeats and the four collapsed forms at junction twelve and the grin that happened for one second because the braces worked, and the way she called the base junction on the fourth form from across the junction without being asked. "Yes," he says. "She won." Oleg nods, accepting this as the complete account. "Cool," he says, which is his word for the full range of things, and which Luka is beginning to understand is not a dismissal but the highest compliment available. —— Seele finds Gepard on the Restricted Zone's outer wall. This is not where Gepard usually is at 1900 hours, but Seele knows where Gepard usually is at 1900 hours from six weeks of observing the palace's operational rhythms, and the outer wall is not it, which is why she went looking rather than assuming. She found him by the methodical process of checking everywhere he wasn't until she found where he was, which took fifteen minutes and two wrong guesses. He is standing on the outer wall's walkway — the section above Sector A, the highest accessible point of the Restricted Zone, where the wall drops away to the cleared snowfield on one side and the city glows amber on the other. The wind at this height has the specific sharpness of the real northern cold, the kind that the Underworld doesn't get and the city interior doesn't get, the kind that exists up here and at the front lines and nowhere else. He hears her coming. He always hears things coming, which Seele has noticed and finds professionally impressive. "Seele," he says, without turning. "Commander Gepard," she says, which is what she calls him in most contexts, partly because it is accurate and partly because calling him Gepard feels like a familiarity she has not decided whether she has yet. "What do you need," he says. She comes to stand beside him at the wall's edge, looking out at the cleared snowfield and the night horizon beyond it. The cold hits her immediately — sharper up here, the rib's protest at the sudden temperature drop manageable but present. "The sequential memory retrieval," she says. "What Svarog told Luka — that the contact events return the memory in order, from the beginning." She pauses. "We're going to experience what happened to us before we have the context for it." "Yes," he says. "I want to know if you think there is a way to prepare for that." He is quiet for a moment. He is looking at the snowfield — not the way she looks at it, not assessing its tactical properties or reading the ambient crystallization levels. He is just looking at it, the way someone looks at a landscape they have been looking at for a long time and which they find, still, worth looking at. "No," he says. "I don't think there's a way to prepare for it." He pauses. "Not specifically. Not for the content." He looks at the city behind them — the amber warmth of the inhabited districts. "But you can prepare the way you prepare for anything you know is coming and don't know the shape of." He pauses. "Know your own limits. Know who's beside you." He glances at her briefly. "Trust them." She is quiet for a moment. "I don't trust easily," she says. "I know," he says. "Neither do I." She looks at the snowfield. The wind moves across it, catching the surface layer of snow and lifting it in thin trailing sheets that catch the distant lamp light. "Is it—" She stops. Starts again. "Was it worth it? Learning to trust someone?" She is looking at the snowfield and not at him. "Before you did, it was straightforward. Simpler." He looks at the city. A long moment passes in which the wind does its work and the snow moves in its trailing sheets and the amber districts glow their ordinary warmth. "Simpler," he says. "Yes." He pauses. "Worth it." Another pause. "Yes." He looks at the snowfield. "The simplicity was also lonelier than I knew it was, until it wasn't." She is quiet. "He's going to be in the next entry," she says. "Luka." "Yes," Gepard says. "The one where we might get to the part that was hardest," she says. "The part we don't have context for yet." "Yes," Gepard says. She breathes the cold air. In and out. The rib registers the cold and she breathes through it. "He said — in the fragment. That he thought we were also the laughing." She pauses. "Whatever we were. He thought we were also that." She is very quiet for a moment. "I have not laughed at something in—" She stops. "I can't remember the last time I laughed at something. Not really." Gepard is quiet. "You will," he says. Not because it is a reassurance. Because it is what he believes, and he does not say things he does not believe. She looks at the snowfield for a moment longer. Then she turns from the wall. "Thank you," she says, which is a thing she says rarely and which when she says it means exactly what it says. "Seele," he says. She stops. He is still looking at the snowfield. "The next entry — the one you're preparing for." He pauses. "I'll be in the corridor." She looks at his back. At the armor, the geomarrow charge lines visible along the pauldrons in the dark, the specific quality of how he stands at the wall — not at attention, not relaxed, something in between that is simply his natural rest state, the posture of a body that has been trained so long that readiness is no longer something it performs but something it is. The strongest man on Jarilo-VI, standing on the outer wall in the dark looking at the cleared snowfield. "I know," she says. "That helps." She goes back down. He stays a while longer on the wall, in the cold, in the particular quiet that exists at this height. He thinks about the debrief that Pela sent — the formation density increase, the activation expansion rate, the eleven days until the Stellaron reaches junction twelve's floor. He thinks about the Chrysalis forms and the forms they are going to face past twelve, which are going to be worse than anything from this entry. He thinks about a patrol mission from four years ago that nobody except himself and two soldiers who are no longer in active service knows the full details of. He thinks about the northern ridge, further north than any patrol route goes now, further north than the Restricted Zone's current perimeter reaches. He thinks about what came out of the Fragmentum bleed at that range — the specific scale of them, the specific quality of their movement, the sound they made — and how he came back from that mission and filed a classified report and spent four days in Natasha's ward before he was cleared to return to duty, and how Natasha had looked at him when he came in and had not said anything except: sit down, which had been the correct thing to say. He files this. He looks at the snowfield, which is clear and still and clean under the dark sky. He goes back to work. —— The argument about the second tunnel entry happens at 0830 the next morning, in the outer machine district, at the main junction, and it is loud enough that a passing district resident gives the two people arguing a wide berth and a sideways look. "You said three to four entries," Seele says. "We've done two since the scout run. We have two at most." "I said three to four entries before the seal's integrity becomes unpredictable," Luka says. "That doesn't mean only three to four entries total. It means the critical window is three to four." He looks at her. "We do another scout entry — not the branch, not the chamber, just junction twelve readings — and we recalculate the threshold based on yesterday's formation data—" "We don't have time for a recalculation entry!" she says, and her voice carries across the junction with the force of someone who has been managing an argument for five minutes and has reached the part where management is no longer her priority. "Every entry agitates the source further! Every time we go in the output increases — you said it yourself, the readings at eight yesterday were above the scout run's readings at eleven—" "Which is exactly why I want the threshold data before we go back to the chamber—" "By the time we have the threshold data the Stellaron will have expanded another meter toward the tunnel floor and the forms at twelve will be twice what they were yesterday—!" "That's not—" He stops. He breathes. "That is not necessarily true. The expansion rate is measurable and I want accurate numbers before—" "The accurate numbers," she says, and her voice has the sharp edge, the real one, the edge that means she is genuinely angry rather than performing it, "are that we have two critical entries left and you want to spend one of them on a recalculation run that gives us data we can extrapolate from what we already have!" "Extrapolation is not measurement—" "In this case it is close enough—!" "It is not—!" He stops again. He looks at the junction wall. He breathes. He turns back to her. "Seele." His voice has dropped from the argument register to something below it — not quiet, not soft, just different, the register of someone who has decided to stop arguing about the thing they are arguing about and say the thing underneath it. "You want to go back to the chamber as fast as possible because the fragment we got yesterday was the first real thing and you want the next one." He holds her gaze. "I know. So do I." He pauses. "That's exactly why I want the recalculation data first. Because if we go in without it and the threshold has shifted further than yesterday suggests, we don't get to choose when we leave. The forms choose for us." She stares at him. The junction is quiet around them. A generator cycles in the distance. Someone's door closes somewhere in the outer district. The ambient ordinary sound of the place they both grew up in, doing what it has always done. "The fragment was—" She stops. She looks at the wall. She looks back at him, and her jaw is doing the tight movement and her eyes are — there is something in her eyes, not anger, the thing underneath the anger, the thing that the fragment yesterday put there and which is still there this morning, still close to the surface, still tender in the way that newly surfaced things are tender. "We were seven years old," she says, much more quietly. "In that chamber. Together. Seven years old." She looks at him directly. "And it was taken from us." He is quiet. She looks at the tunnel direction. "I want it back," she says. Flat. Honest. Not the managed version. "I want all of it back. I don't want to wait another day for the next piece of it." He looks at her. He looks at her with the expression he has when he has arrived at a position and is not going to perform a different one. "So do I," he says. "That's not why I'm saying wait. I'm saying wait because if we rush this and we end up in a situation in that corridor where we can't control the exit — where something goes wrong with the forms and we can't get out cleanly—" He pauses. "Then we don't get any more pieces. We don't get anything." She looks at him. He looks at her. The thing between them is present — the pull and the resistance, always, both at once — and right now the anger is also present, sitting on top of the pull the way it always does, the specific hostile wariness that is structural to her and which he has never been able to fully get around and probably never will. "One day," she says. "You get one day for the recalculation." "One day is enough," he says. "It better be," she says. "Because on the day after that, we're going back to the chamber." "Yes," he says. "We are." She looks at him for another moment. Something in her expression shifts — very slightly, the hostile wariness doing its fractional softening that happens sometimes when an argument has produced something honest from both of them. She looks away. "Fine," she says. "I know," he says. She turns on him with the eyes and he raises his hands — both of them, the automatic defenselessness gesture of someone who knows they have said the thing they were told not to say and is pre-empting the response. "I know," he says again, differently. "Not that word. Understood." She stares at him. Her mouth does something — the almost-movement, the one that is not the hostile wariness dissolving but is something else, something that lives next to involuntary — and she shuts it down before it surfaces. She turns toward the outer district. "The recalculation data," she says, over her shoulder. "Tonight." "Tonight," he says. She walks. He watches her walk for one second, two, and then he turns toward the workshop. The second heartbeat is below, and the outer district is doing its morning, and Toma is somewhere in the salvage corridors with Oleg and Pino looking for more old components, and Clara is probably already at the secondary worktable because Clara is always already at the secondary worktable. He walks toward the workshop. He is smiling slightly, which is something he is doing more frequently than he was a month ago, and which he has not examined, and which is going to have to be examined eventually, probably around the same time as the unanswerable question. Not today. —— The recalculation data takes all day and yields something none of them expected. It is Pela, at 2100 hours, who calls the finding — from the palace analysis room where she has been working since 0900 on the junction twelve data from yesterday's entry, cross-referencing it with the output rate and the formation density and the expansion measurement Aventurine reported. The threshold has not moved forward the way the activation rate predicted. It has moved backward. "The formation appeared at eleven minutes forty seconds yesterday," Pela says, her voice on the communication line with Bronya and Gepard simultaneously, the tone of someone who has checked a result three times and is now reporting it because it is correct even though it is surprising. "Based on the activation rate and the previous formation thresholds, the expected threshold for the next entry was between nine and eleven minutes." She pauses. "I ran the model forward from the chamber contact event. The contact event produced a significant output peak — larger than the approach agitation, larger than the formation event itself." She pauses again. "After the peak, the output dropped." "Dropped," Bronya says. "By eleven percent," Pela says. "Sustained. The current output level, three hours after the entry, is eleven percent below the pre-entry baseline." She pauses. "The contact event — the retrieval — reduced the Stellaron's output." The communication line is quiet for a moment. "The retrieval is what the Stellaron has been building toward," Gepard says, slowly. "The activation was building pressure — the seal fraying, the output climbing, the expansion. Because the Stellaron was trying to complete its function." "And when it completed the first part of its function," Pela says, "the pressure reduced." She pauses. "The retrieval is not agitating the source. It is — satisfying it. Partially." "Each successful retrieval will reduce the output," Bronya says. "Yes," Pela says. "My projection is that a complete retrieval — the full memory returned — will reduce the output to the baseline from before the behavioral shift began six weeks ago." She pauses. "The activation will not complete. The seal will not break completely. Not if the retrieval is completed." "The retrieval is the alternative to the activation," Gepard says. "Yes," Pela says. "Fuli didn't create a time bomb. She created a safe. And the safe has always been designed to open." The communication line is quiet. "The threshold for the next entry," Bronya says. "Based on the reduced output level, the formation threshold for the next entry is approximately sixteen minutes," Pela says. "Not nine. Not eleven. Sixteen." A pause. "You have more time than you thought." —— Gepard tells Seele at 0630 the next morning, at the corridor entrance, because she is already there when he arrives and she is running the frequency recorder calibration and she looks up when he approaches with the expression of someone who woke up at a time that was early even for her because they were going back in today and she had things to do before going back in today. He tells her the finding. Her hands stop on the calibration sequence. She turns and looks at him with an expression he has not seen from her before. It is not the hostile wariness. It is not the young uncertainty. It is not the unguarded flash. It is something that is all three at once, layered, the way light layers when it comes through different media — the wariness on top, the uncertainty beneath it, and underneath both of them something that is simply, completely, relief. "Sixteen minutes," she says. "Sixteen minutes," he confirms. She looks at the corridor entrance. She looks at the recorder in her hands. She looks at the junction direction, where in a moment Luka is going to arrive, and past junction twelve the branch is going to open into the chamber, and the formation is there, and the memory is sequential, and there are more pieces to come. She breathes. "Tell Luka," she says. "He's getting the message now," Gepard says. "Pela sent it to everyone." She nods. She looks at the recorder. She finishes the calibration sequence — the last two steps, her hands moving through it automatically — and she straightens. She looks at the corridor entrance. "Let's go," she says. And from behind them, Luka's voice, arriving at exactly the right moment because he is never actually late he just always arrives at exactly the right moment: "Sixteen minutes. Pela's finding." Seele turns. He is walking toward them from the workshop direction, the secondary module in hand, the combat braces on, and his face has the expression it had when the brace loading worked correctly yesterday — not quite the grin, something steadier than that, the expression of someone who has been given more room than they expected and is deciding what to do with it. "Sixteen minutes," she says. "Sixteen minutes," he says. They look at each other. "Don't say something," she says. "I wasn't going to say anything," he says. "You had the face," she says. "I don't have a face—" "You have a face you make when you're about to say something," she says. "I've catalogued it." He stares at her. "You've catalogued my face?" "I catalogue everything relevant," she says. "You told me that yourself." He opens his mouth. He closes it. The corner of his mouth does the thing it keeps doing and he does not manage it this time, the grin surfacing briefly, genuine, and she sees it and her expression does the almost-movement again — the one that lives next to involuntary — and this time it gets slightly further than before before she manages it back down. Gepard watches this exchange from two meters away with the expression he has been wearing for approximately three weeks, which is the expression of a man who has been observing a specific variety of weather develop in slow motion and finds the observation, if not comfortable, at least entirely predictable. "Into the corridor," he says. They go. —— The workshop, at that same moment, contains four children doing four entirely different things. Clara is at the secondary worktable doing the frequency calibration of the deep-range sensor array that Svarog built this week for the next junction twelve entry. She is doing it with complete focus, her small hands on the calibration controls, the display showing the frequency values that she has memorized from Svarog's instruction and which she is now setting with the precision that comes from having watched Svarog do it twice and having been allowed to try it once supervised. Toma is on the floor with the old component from outer corridor eleven, which she has brought back today with three more like it that she and Oleg and Pino found in the same section. She is examining them with the focused attention of someone trying to understand something, which is her natural mode. Oleg is on the stool — his stool now, apparently, because the stool negotiation that Clara declined to have yesterday has been replaced by the fact of Oleg simply being on the stool every time he arrives and Clara not saying anything about it. He is eating. He has been eating since he arrived, different things from different pockets, in a way that suggests the jacket is also a mobile pantry. Pino is talking to Svarog. This is not a thing Pino planned. Pino came in with Toma and Oleg and went to look at the components in Svarog's deep storage that Clara mentioned yesterday — the pre-Separation pieces that match the ones Toma found in outer corridor eleven — and Svarog emerged from the back room while Pino was looking at them, and Pino, who is twelve and has the specific combination of curiosity and social confidence that produces people who talk to everyone regardless of apparent size, said: "Are these from the old world?" And Svarog said: "Yes." And now they have been talking for twenty minutes. Pino is sitting on an upturned storage case across from Svarog's worktable, legs crossed, looking up at the four-armed automaton with the expression of complete genuine interest that children have when they have found a subject that is actually as interesting as it sounded. Svarog is describing the pre-Separation infrastructure with the thorough patience he brings to everything, and Pino is asking follow-up questions that are, by any measure, significantly more specific than his apparent age would suggest. "But why did they build the tunnels that deep?" Pino says. "If the Underworld wasn't as deep yet when they built them?" "The tunnels were built to reach the geomarrow deposits," Svarog says. "The deeper deposits were more stable. The pre-Separation builders were constructing infrastructure for the long term." "How long term?" "Centuries," Svarog says. "Did they know it would last that long?" "I believe they hoped," Svarog says. "Whether they knew is a different question." Pino considers this. "What happened to them?" he says. "The pre-Separation people." Svarog is quiet for a moment. His optical sensors carry the expression that serves for careful. "The Separation," he says, simply. "When the sky dome went up. Some things were on one side of it. Others were not." "Were you here?" Pino says. "I was here," Svarog says. "Did you see it happen?" "Yes," Svarog says. Pino looks at him. He is twelve years old and he is looking at something that was present at an event that is history, that was there when the sky changed, and his expression has the specific quality of a child encountering the actual weight of very old things — not afraid, not performing the appropriate gravitas, just genuinely, completely absorbing it. "What was it like?" he says. Svarog looks at him. At the small figure on the storage case. At the question asked with the directness that children ask things before they learn that some questions are considered too large to ask directly. "Terrible," Svarog says. "And then, over a very long time, it became something else." "What?" Pino says. "Home," Svarog says. The workshop is quiet for a moment. Clara has stopped the calibration — just for a second, the hands still on the controls — and then she starts again. Toma is looking at the old component with a different quality of looking now. Oleg has stopped eating. "Cool," Oleg says, which is his word for the full range, and which in this moment lands somewhere very close to the truth. Svarog looks at him. "Yes," Svarog says, which is his version of the same assessment. "It is." Outside the workshop, the outer machine district does its morning. The generators cycle. The second heartbeat is below, steady, present. The corridor entrance is two minutes' walk away. In it, at that moment, three people are moving past junction eight toward something that has been waiting twenty-two years to be found. The workshop is warm. The children are doing what children do. The world is large and old and full of things worth protecting, and the people protecting it are on their way. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 24: "Sixteen Minutes" Chapter Text They go in at 0600. Three people: Gepard, Seele, Luka. No Aventurine this time — he is in the palace with Topaz, reviewing Pela's output reduction finding and running it against the IPC's theoretical framework for repository Stellarons, which is work that matters and which requires him more than the corridor does today. He had looked at Gepard when the plan was confirmed and said, without the performance: "The Cornerstone will be available if you need it. The field radius extends to anyone within three meters of me. If the formation count exceeds what three people can manage—" "I'll call for you," Gepard said. Aventurine had looked at him for a moment. "You've never called for support once in your career," he said. "No," Gepard agreed. "Stubbornness as a tactical philosophy," Aventurine said. "Interesting approach." "It's worked so far," Gepard said. Aventurine had looked at him the way he looks at things he is filing for later. "It has," he said. "Remarkably well, actually." He paused. "Don't die in a tunnel, Commander Gepard. Topaz would find that professionally inconvenient and I would find it personally inconvenient." "Noted," Gepard said. So: three people. The original configuration, the smallest viable group, moving fast with the recalculation data and sixteen minutes and the knowledge that the retrieval reduces the output. Seele with the frequency recorder on her harness and the wrapped rib that Natasha cleared yesterday morning and the expression she has been wearing since Gepard told her about Pela's finding — the integrated-something expression, the relief sitting underneath the operational focus, both present simultaneously. Luka with the recalibrated braces and the deep-range sensor array from Svarog and the secondary module on the wider range setting, and the thing he has been carrying since the junction outside the workshop yesterday morning, the one-day concession that has produced sixteen minutes, which he files under: correct. Gepard at the front. They go. —— The corridor at 0600 has a different quality from the 0700 entries. It is darker. The Underworld's main lamp cycle has not yet reached full daytime output, which means the ambient light in nine-beta's first sections is lower than usual, the amber-black crystallization on the walls giving off less reflected light. Luka switches the torch on at junction three rather than eight. The additional light changes the corridor's texture — not the carved stone sections, not yet, but the constructed concrete of the early junctions, which in the torch's beam reveals details the ambient lamp light flattens: the construction seams, the specific tool marks, the way the crystallization threading follows the geometry of the concrete rather than growing through it. He has logged this corridor twenty-six times. He still looks at it. "Vibration is higher than yesterday at three," he says. "Recorder confirms elevated baseline," Seele says. "But the bifurcated signal is stable — not oscillating. The output reduction held overnight." "The formation threshold should still be sixteen," Gepard says, from ahead. "Should be," Luka says. "I'll call it if the sensor goes orange before expected." They move. The junctions pass faster now than they did in the first entries — not because they are rushing, but because the corridor has become known in the specific way that places become known to people who spend enough time in them, the body navigating by the route that pre-exists the map. Luka does not check the junction markers at seven, eight, nine — he knows them by the changes in the floor texture, the ceiling height, the specific quality of the cold at each point. He knows them the way he knows the workshop, the way he has always known the outer machine district. He does not think about what that means right now. He is in motion and the sensor is reading and there are sixteen minutes. At junction eight the carved stone begins and the torch light changes the walls. Aventurine would say something about the pre-Separation construction marks here. Luka logs them in motion instead, the documentation instinct running parallel to the forward movement, both at once, the same way the second heartbeat has always run parallel to everything else. "Nine is deep orange," he says, at junction nine. "Threshold is still projected," Gepard says. "Keep moving." "I know," Luka says. From behind him, Seele says nothing, which is her method of agreement when she is conserving attention for the things that matter. He can hear her breathing — the controlled even cadence of someone who is managing the wrapped rib's protest at the pace and not mentioning it. Junction ten. Eleven. The vibration note audible in the floor. At eleven, no forms. The junction is empty — the carved stone, the torch shadows, the cooling Fragmentum debris from yesterday's forms still present in the corners of the space, the ambient evidence of what the corridor produces when pushed. But no new forms. The sixteen minutes is holding. "Twelve," Gepard says, from ahead. —— Junction twelve at 0600 is brighter than yesterday. The light from the right branch is stronger — not dramatically, not alarmingly, but measurably, the way things change when an active source has had twelve hours to continue its expansion. Luka's deep-range sensor reads the source at thirty-six meters depth. One meter closer than yesterday. Ten days now, at this rate, before it reaches the floor. "Time," Gepard says. "Six minutes fifty," Luka says. "Nine minutes left," Gepard says. "Seele. Luka." They go into the branch. —— The branch at 0600 is different from the branch at 0719. The difference is the light. At 0719 the light was the full amber of the district's daytime setting, which gave the branch a warmth it does not have now. At 0600 the light in the branch is only the formation's light — old gold and deep water, uncut by anything external — and in the early dark of pre-dawn the effect is that the branch is illuminated entirely by the thing they are moving toward, every carved stone surface carrying its color, every shadow cast by it and not by the torch. It is, Luka thinks, almost certainly what it looked like twenty-two years ago. He does not say this. He logs it. At the curve of the branch, they stop. The chamber. The formation. It is larger than yesterday. Not dramatically — a centimeter, maybe two, the crystalline structure having grown overnight in the patient way that very old things grow, slow and deliberate and entirely certain of their direction. The light from within it is brighter, steadier, the pulse of the second heartbeat more present, and at this proximity the heartbeat is not a sensation or an almost-sound — it is a sound, fully, clearly, a deep resonant rhythm that the ears locate without difficulty and the body responds to with the specific quality of recognition. Two heartbeats, as always, at close range. Running together. Seele stands at the edge of the chamber space. She is very still. He stands beside her. "Time," Gepard says, from the junction behind them. "Seven forty-five from entry," Luka says. "Eight minutes fifteen," Gepard confirms. "You have eight minutes." Seele looks at the formation. She looks at it the way she looked at it yesterday — the older expression, the thing that is below the wariness and the uncertainty, the recognition of something that is specifically hers. But today there is something else in it too. Something that has the quality of what the junction at 2300 hours last night produced — the argument about the recalculation, and his naming of what was underneath it, and her jaw doing the movement, and the one day concession. She wants this. She is not hiding that she wants it. She extends her hand. "Seele—" His voice comes out differently than he intends. Faster. The specific register of her name that appeared at junction eleven when the form hit her, the one he did not have time to examine then and is not examining now either because now is not the time. "The time—" "I know," she says. "It's enough." She touches the formation. —— The chamber is full of light. Full — not the doubling from yesterday, this is total, the old gold and deep water occupying every surface from floor to ceiling, conducting through the carved stone into the branch and the junction and probably into the corridor beyond, and the two heartbeats are fully aligned, the signatures running together on the recorder's display and holding, and the retrieval begins. It begins where it left off yesterday. They are children. The chamber. The formation, small and new. They are seven years old and this is a place they know — a place they have been coming to since they were old enough to navigate the outer district without supervision, which in the outer machine district is younger than it is in most places, because the outer machine district teaches children early that knowing where you are is the first requirement of survival. They know this tunnel the way they know the smell of Svarog's workshop and the sound of the generators and the specific cold of the outer corridors that is different from every other cold in the Underworld. They stand in the chamber and they are not afraid. The formation is small and the light is dim compared to what it will become and the heartbeat is there — it has always been there, both of them have always felt it, and when they are here together it is two heartbeats and not one, and that is a thing that neither of them has ever found strange because it has never been anything other than what it is. They are not alone. There is— The memory cuts. Not because it ends. Because something about the next frame costs more than what was spent today. The light pulls back. The chamber reasserts. They are standing in front of the formation in the dim pre-dawn light of the outer machine district's 0600 lamp cycle, both of them present, both of them back, and Seele's hand is still on the formation and the recorder's display is showing the sustained peak and there are — Luka checks the secondary module— "Time," he says. "Four minutes." She pulls her hand back. She does not look at him immediately. She looks at the formation for one more second, then two, and he watches her face do the processing — the memory landing and settling and being integrated into what is known, alongside the memory from yesterday, the two pieces that are the beginning of the picture they are assembling. "They were not alone," she says. Very quietly. "No," he says. "Someone else was there." She looks at him. Her eyes are very purple in the formation's light and very clear, the specific clarity of someone who has just received something real and is holding it carefully. "We'll find out who," she says. "Yes," he says. "We will." She looks at the formation one more time. Something in her expression — very briefly — is the thing he saw when she was running ahead and laughing. Not the laugh itself. The quality underneath it. The person who would run ahead and laugh, standing in this chamber at seven years old like it was the most ordinary thing in the world. She turns toward the branch exit. "Four minutes," she says. "Let's go." They go. —— They exit the branch into junction twelve at nine minutes forty seconds. No forms. The junction is empty and cool and the light from the formation is still visible in the branch entrance and the sensor is deep orange and the formation threshold is still approximately four minutes away. They have margin. Not much — a minute, maybe two — but margin, which is the difference between a controlled exit and a running exit, and Gepard, who is standing at junction twelve in the position he has been standing in since they went into the branch, reads the margin in Luka's face when they appear and nods once. "Go," he says. "At pace." They go at pace. The junctions reverse — twelve, eleven, ten, nine. At nine the secondary module reads high orange, and then at eight it drops to deep yellow, the specific drop that happens when the carved stone section ends and the concrete begins and the proximity to the source decreases by the geometry of the corridor. At eight Luka exhales once, deliberately, the single controlled exhale that manages the adrenaline of a corridor exit. At five, he feels it. Not a flash this time. Not the shape before the image or the edge of a photograph. A sound — specific, clear, arriving the same way the voices arrived in the branch, from outside rather than inside, conducted through the proximity and the contact event's resonance — a voice. One voice this time. Not his, not Seele's. An adult's voice. Not Svarog's — different from Svarog, warmer, with the specific quality of a voice that is trying very hard to be steady. It says his name. His name, from twenty-two years ago, in a voice he does not recognize and which his body recognizes completely, with the specific recognition that goes to the part of a person that was formed before memory begins. He stops walking. Seele, ahead of him, stops a second later. She turns. She looks at him. "What," she says, and her voice has the quick quality — the faster register. "A voice," he says. "At junction five." He looks at the junction. He looks at her. "Not mine. Not yours." He pauses. "Adult. It said my name." She looks at him for a long moment. "I didn't hear it," she says. "No," he says. "I know." He looks at the junction. "It wasn't from the recorder. It was—" He stops. "Internal. Like the fragments. But different content." She is very still. "What did it sound like." He looks at the corridor wall. He looks at the specific carved stone and concrete junction, the place where the pre-Separation construction transitions back to the human-built Underworld, and he thinks about the voice and the name said in the voice and the specific quality of the voice trying very hard to be steady. "Like someone saying goodbye," he says. "Without saying the word." The corridor is quiet around them. Gepard, who has heard all of this, says nothing. The second heartbeat is present and steady and the sensor is reading yellow and there are three junctions to the exit. Seele looks at him. Her expression is the one from the argument yesterday — the hostility still there, structural, but doing significantly less work than the thing underneath it, the thing that has been present since the beginning and which is getting harder to maintain the management of. She looks at him the way she looks at the junction seven-alpha-four wall — the reaching quality, the thing that is not quite ready to be a reach but is aware of the distance. "Luka," she says. "Yes," he says. "Whatever the voice is—" She stops. She starts again. "Whatever it is we're going to find — whatever happened — you should know that I—" She stops again, and this time the stopping is the deliberate kind, the kind where the sentence has an end and she is choosing not to give it yet. She breathes. "You should know that I'm going to be there when we find it. In the corridor. When we get to the hard part." She holds his gaze. "You're not going to be standing in that chamber alone." He looks at her. "Neither will you," he says. She holds his gaze for one moment longer, then she turns and walks, and he walks behind her, and Gepard walks behind both of them, and they exit nine-beta at 0632 into the outer machine district's pre-dawn with the formation data and two new memory pieces and a voice that said a name in the register of goodbye. —— In the palace, at 0900, Bronya receives the debrief from Gepard via a message that is longer than his messages usually are, which means the content is significant enough that he wants to be precise about it. She reads it at the office desk, with the morning light coming through the north-facing windows and the cup from the right side of the cabinet in her hands, and she reads the part about the two memory pieces and the voice at junction five and she sits with it for a long moment. She sends back: 'Come to the office when you're done with the sector check.' He arrives at 0940. He comes through the door in the way he comes through doors now — not the doorway pause of the early months, not the professional knock on an already-open frame. He closes the door, crosses the room, sits in the chair from the corner. The chair that has been at the desk for so long that the floor has a faint impression where it has been dragged to the desk and back, a record of all the times it has been moved and moved back and not moved back and moved again. She looks at him across the desk. He looks at her. There is something in his face today that was not there yesterday — not the patrol report look, not the operational focus. Something quieter, something that has been sitting with the corridor debrief and what it produced and has not resolved it. She knows this quality of his face. She has learned all his faces over the past months in the way she has learned the Stellaron's frequency — by proximity, by attention, by the specific knowledge that comes from being close to something long enough that its variations become legible. "The voice at junction five," she says. "Yes," he says. "An adult. Saying his name. In the register of goodbye." She pauses. "Luka's flashback, not the shared memory." "Yes." "So there are two streams," she says. "The shared chamber memories — what both of them experienced together. And individual flashbacks — fragments that belong specifically to one of them." She pauses. "The voice belongs specifically to him." "Yes," he says. "Whatever happened in that district twenty-two years ago — there's a piece of it that is specifically Luka's. Something the Stellaron is returning to him that is not part of the shared content." She holds her cup. "The person who told Sampo to run," she says. "Svarog knows who it was. He said when the people it concerns most have remembered enough." She pauses. "The voice at junction five is connected to Sampo's memory." He looks at her. "I think so," he says. "I don't know that yet." "No," she says. "We don't know it yet." They sit in the office in the morning quiet, the two of them with the desk between them and the cups and the pale northern light, and the weight of what is assembling in the corridor is present between them the way all significant things have been present between them for months — held jointly, without needing to be discussed, carried in the specific way of two people who have been holding things together long enough that the carrying has become its own form of closeness. "I want to tell Sampo," she says. "About the voice." "About the voice. About what Svarog said — when the people it concerns most have remembered enough." She sets the cup down. "He is one of the people it concerns. He has a right to know that the corridor is producing content that is connected to what happened to him." She meets Gepard's gaze. "Not the specific content. Not yet. But that it's there." "Yes," he says. "I'll tell him today," she says. He nods. He picks up his patrol folder. He opens it. He does not read it for a moment — he is looking at her, at the way the morning light sits on the side of her face the way he has found himself looking at for months, and the looking is not the managed peripheral version, not the practiced discipline of looking slightly to the left of the thing he cannot look at directly. He looks at her directly. She is not looking at him — she is looking at her own folder, the administrator brief she opened when he came in — but she knows he is looking at her, he can tell by the quality of her not-looking, which is slightly more deliberate than it would be if she did not know. He looks at his folder. He reads it. Actually reads it. She does the same. This is also something they have — the working quiet, the office at 0940, both of them with their respective documents and the morning outside and the things they are not saying that fill the room as completely as the things they are. He has been coming at seven for weeks and the right side of the cabinet is always open and the chair is at the desk and neither of them has named what this is. He thinks he will name it eventually. Not today. Today there is the patrol folder. —— Sampo is at Natasha's ward at noon. This is not unusual. What is unusual is that today he does not bring the pastries from the Tier Two bakery. He comes empty-handed, which is the first time he has come to the ward without bringing something, and Natasha looks up from her chart and registers the empty hands and her expression does the thing it does when she has received information she was expecting but which still has weight in the arrival. "Come in," she says. He comes to the desk. He does not sit — he stands at the desk in the way he stands when he is not performing the casual version of himself, the way he stood in the workshop when Svarog told him there were others. Upright, not rigid, the posture of someone who has decided to receive whatever is coming standing. "Bronya told me something," he says. "Yes," Natasha says, which means she knows what Bronya told him because she and Bronya spoke this morning, because Natasha and Bronya have the arrangement where Natasha keeps Bronya informed and Bronya keeps Natasha informed and both of them have agreed that Sampo is owed the complete picture in real time. "The corridor is producing content connected to what happened to me twenty-two years ago," he says. "There is a voice in Luka's individual flashbacks — an adult's voice, in the register of goodbye." He pauses. "Svarog knows who the voice belongs to." He pauses again. "The people it concerns most have to remember enough first." "Yes," Natasha says. "Luka is one of the people it concerns," Sampo says. "Seele. Possibly others." "Possibly others," she confirms. He is quiet for a moment. He looks at the desk between them. At the chart she is writing in, at her hands on the pen, at the ward behind her with its morning sounds and its patient care and its complete, constant, reliable presence. "Natasha," he says. "Yes," she says. "I'm scared," he says. He says it the way he says things that are true and uncomfortable — the performance completely absent, the words just the words, sitting on the desk between them in the quiet of the ward. He says it and then he waits, in the way he waits when he has given something real and is deciding whether it will be received as real or deflected into something more manageable. Natasha sets the pen down. She looks at him. "I know," she says, which is not the same as Gepard's I know — it does not have the same weight of five years of shared history behind it — but it has a different weight, the weight of someone who has been watching him manage things alone for a very long time and is not going to make him manage this one the same way. He looks at her. She looks at him. The ward is quiet around them, the way it always is at the precise moments when it needs to be. "I don't know what I'm going to find," he says. "When the memory comes back. Whatever was in me that was taken — I don't know what it looks like. I don't know if I'm going to want it back." He pauses. "What if the person I was before was someone I don't—" He stops. "What if it was better not to know." Natasha is very quiet for a moment. "It is never better not to know," she says. "In my experience — and I have more of it than you might think, in this ward — it is never better." She pauses. "The not-knowing has a weight too. It just doesn't have a name, so it's harder to put down." She meets his gaze. "Whatever you find — you will find it as who you are now. And who you are now has been living in that district, building things, coming here with pastries twice a week for years." She pauses. "That is not nothing, Sampo. That is not a person diminished by what was taken." Another pause. "That is someone who came back to the place they were supposed to be and built something real in it." He looks at her for a long moment. Something in his face does the complicated thing — the thing that happens around her that is not the performance and is not the stripped version, something that exists in between those, the version of Sampo Koski that is specifically for this room and this desk and this person, and which he has been, over the past weeks, becoming slightly less careful about showing. "I forgot the pastries," he says. She almost smiles. "I noticed." "That's the first time in—" "Three years," she says. "Of twice-a-week visits." He looks at her. "You've been counting." "I notice things," she says, which is an echo of something he once said to Gepard, and which lands between them with the quality of something that knows exactly what it is. The ward does its quiet work around them. Someone coughs in the back. The door opens and closes with a patient arriving. Natasha picks up her pen. "Go get the pastries," she says. He looks at her. He grins — the real one, quick and genuine, the one she has seen more in the past month than in the three years before it. "Yes, doctor," he says. He goes. She watches the door close behind him and goes back to her chart and does not examine the smile that is on her face, because some things do not require examination. —— At 1600, in the outer machine district, something happens to Clara. She is walking back to the workshop from the supply drop-off point at the district's central junction — a task she does twice a week, carrying the workshop's supply order from the delivery point to the storage room, which Svarog long ago determined she was strong enough to do and which she does with the focused efficiency of someone who has learned that tasks done correctly the first time do not have to be done again. She is at the junction between the central path and the outer corridor when she hears it. It is not the second heartbeat. She has always been able to feel the second heartbeat, the ambient low rhythm of the deep Underworld, the thing that has always been present in the outer machine district the way the smell of machine oil is present — constant, background, not alarming. She has never mentioned this to anyone because it has never been anything other than normal to her, and she has only recently begun to understand that it is not normal, and she is still deciding what to understand about what that means. This is not that. This is a sound from the corridor. From somewhere near junction seven-alpha-four. Not Fragmentum — not the wet cracking of an activation event, not anything associated with danger. More like the corridor sound from when Seele and Luka come out — the specific quality of a space that has been used, that has something of the people who were in it still present. She puts the supply order down at the junction. She looks toward the corridor. She looks at the workshop, which is sixty meters in the other direction. She goes toward the corridor. She doesn't go in. She stands at the entrance and she listens, and the second heartbeat is there — slightly louder than usual, the post-entry elevation that Luka always logs and which she has been reading over his shoulder whenever she gets the opportunity — and something else. Something that is not the second heartbeat. Something that is specifically for her, in the way that certain things are specifically for certain people — the way the frequency recorder calibration is specifically for Clara's hands because she has been the one calibrating it, the way the stool has become Oleg's stool because he has been sitting on it. Specifically for her. Her name, in something that is not a voice but is not not a voice, the way the second heartbeat is not not a sound. She stands at the corridor entrance for a long time. Then she picks up the supply order and walks back to the workshop and does not tell anyone, because she has learned from Svarog that there are things you carry until you understand them well enough to give them to someone else, and she does not yet understand this well enough. But she goes back to the secondary worktable and she looks at the frequency recorder and she looks at the bifurcated signal display and she looks at the two signatures — the higher one and the lower one, running parallel — and she thinks about what Svarog said: the two signatures are the loudest. The ones nearest the surface of what is held. Below them, there may be others. She turns back to the calibration work and she does not think about it directly, which is a thing she has learned from Clara-specific experience is sometimes the best approach. She calibrates. The second heartbeat is below, patient and steady. And somewhere in what it holds, there is something that called her by name. —— The argument at 1730 is about the next entry. It happens at the workshop entrance, which has become, over the past week, the natural location for Seele and Luka's arguments — not because it was designated but because the workshop entrance is the point where their routes diverge and they have taken to stopping there and finishing whatever they started on the walk from the corridor, and the arguments always start on the walk from the corridor. "The next memory piece is going to be harder," Seele says. "The quality of the retrieval today — what came after the children-in-the-chamber section — whatever that frame was, it was going to cost more. The recording cut because the contact event didn't have enough to give it." "Yes," Luka says. "Which means when we get to it, it's going to require a longer contact duration," she says. "More time at the formation. Which means we need the formation threshold recalculated for a longer branch stay." "Yes," he says again. She looks at him. "You're agreeing with everything again." "I am agreeing with everything you've said because it is all correct," he says. "I don't have a different position." "Then say something!" she says, with the exasperated volume that has become normal for their junction conversations. "Don't just agree! Have a position!" "My position is that you are correct!" he says, at the same volume. "I can't manufacture a disagreement to satisfy your apparent need for one!" "I don't have a need—" She stops. She breathes. "I'm not asking you to manufacture a disagreement. I'm asking you to think about this independently and tell me what you think rather than just—" She stops again. Her jaw does the movement. "You're doing it on purpose," she says, more quietly, and now she sounds less angry and more uncertain, which is a different register entirely. "Doing what on purpose," he says. "Agreeing," she says. "To make me feel—" She stops a third time, and whatever the end of that sentence was, she has decided not to give it. She looks at the workshop entrance. She looks at him. "I don't need you to be manageable," she says, finally. "You were harder to deal with before. I found that more—" She stops. "More what," he says. She looks at him. He looks at her. The workshop entrance is between them and behind them is the outer machine district doing its evening and below them is the second heartbeat and there are two memory pieces from a chamber they have been standing in since they were seven years old and a voice at junction five in the register of goodbye and neither of them is going to say the obvious thing, which is that they have been walking toward each other since before they knew they were walking, and the management of that is getting harder, not easier, and the fact that it is getting harder is something neither of them is ready to name but both of them are standing in. "More honest," she says, finally. He is quiet. She looks at the workshop door. "The threshold recalculation," she says. "I want it done before the next entry. And I want the branch timing extended to account for a longer contact duration." She pauses. "We can do both of those without an extra recalculation entry." "Yes," he says. "We can." "Good," she says. "Seele—" "Tomorrow," she says, and goes. He watches her go. He goes into the workshop, where Clara is at the secondary worktable doing the calibration with the focused quiet that means she has been thinking about something since before he arrived, and he sits at the edge of the worktable and looks at the waveform display and thinks about the voice at junction five and the two memory pieces and a seven-year-old girl running ahead in a corridor that smelled like this and laughing. He thinks about what Seele said. More honest. He looks at the display. He looks at the two signatures running parallel. He thinks about honest. "Clara," he says. "Yes," she says, without looking up from the calibration. "You heard something today," he says. "At the corridor entrance." She goes very still. He looks at the display. "The second heartbeat sounds different when you're near the corridor entrance," he says. "I've logged it. The post-entry elevation is measurable from outside the corridor if you know what you're listening for." He pauses. "You know what you're listening for." A long silence. "Yes," she says, very quietly. He looks at her — at the small figure at the secondary worktable, the careful hands on the calibration controls, the feet not quite reaching the floor. "You should tell Svarog," he says. "I know," she says. "Tonight," he says. She looks at the calibration display. She looks at the waveform. She looks at the two signatures. "Tonight," she says. Below them, the second heartbeat counts on. Outside the workshop, the outer machine district goes into its evening, and in the ward two minutes' walk away Natasha is writing in a chart with a smile she is not examining, and in the palace Bronya is reading a file that arrived from the IPC's outer district archive and finding things in it that she does not yet know how to hold, and in the Restricted Zone Gepard is standing on the outer wall at the end of the evening patrol and looking at the city the way he looks at the city. Worth protecting. All of it. Every district. The second heartbeat is below. It is counting. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 25: "Others" Chapter Text Clara tells Svarog at 2100 hours. She waits until the workshop has emptied — until Luka has gone back to his quarters and the Moles have gone back to wherever the Moles go in the evenings, which she has not yet fully mapped but which involves at least one location in the salvage corridor network that Toma considers private — and until Svarog has finished the day's final maintenance cycle and the workshop has settled into its night-register hum, the specific quiet of machines doing what machines do when nobody is asking them to do anything else. She finds him in the back room. She stands in the doorway. He looks up. His optical sensors settle on her with the quality they always have — seeing more than is comfortable — and he sets down what he is working on. All four hands. The complete stopping of a thing that does not often fully stop. "Clara," he says. "I heard something today," she says. "At the corridor entrance." She is standing in the doorway with her hands at her sides, which is the posture she has when she has prepared what she is going to say and is delivering it in the order she prepared it. "Not the second heartbeat. Something that used my name." Svarog is very still. "It wasn't a voice," she says. "Not the way the voices in the branch are voices — not audio. More like—" She pauses, selecting the word with the care she applies to precise things. "Like being addressed. Without sound." She meets his optical sensor gaze directly. "Something in the Stellaron addressed me." The back room is quiet. The workshop hums beyond the doorway. "Yes," Svarog says. "I was wondering when it would begin." She stares at him. "You knew this was going to happen." "I suspected," he says. "The two primary signatures — Seele's and Luka's — are the surface of what the Stellaron holds. Below them are others." He pauses. "I did not know whether you would be among the others. But I thought it was possible." He looks at her with the expression that serves for careful, for the thing that is carrying weight. "You have been in this district for eight months. You have been working in this workshop — in proximity to the source — every day." He pauses. "And you are not—" He stops. "Not what," she says. A very long pause. "There are things about you," Svarog says, "that I have been observing for eight months and have not yet said. Because I did not want to say them before you were ready to hear them." He looks at her. "Are you ready to hear them?" She looks at him. She is twelve years old and she has grown up in a workshop with a four-armed automaton who sees everything and says only what is necessary, and she has learned from him that ready is not the same as comfortable, and that hearing something is not the same as understanding it, and that understanding something is not the same as knowing what to do about it. "Yes," she says. "You are not simply a construct who received a source code," Svarog says. "The source code Topaz gave you was a foundation, as I have said. But the way you have built on it — the speed, the specific directions of your development, the things you find interesting and the things you do not — these are not typical of a construct given a standard source code." He pauses. "I have been a machine for a very long time. I have encountered many constructs. I have never encountered one who calibrates frequency arrays by feel rather than by specification, who navigates the workshop's maintenance logic by intuition rather than by instruction, who—" He stops again. "Who hears the second heartbeat," she says. "Yes," he says. "Who hears the second heartbeat." He looks at her. "Clara. The source code Topaz gave you — do you know where Topaz found it?" She shakes her head. "Neither do I," he says. "But I have a theory." He is quiet for a moment. "The second Stellaron holds what it holds. What we know it holds — the memories of specific people, the content of what was taken from the district twenty-two years ago. But Pela's analysis confirmed that the Stellaron's capacity is larger than what we have identified. There is content deeper than the primary signatures that the recorder cannot reach." He looks at her. "I believe the source code Topaz gave you came from the Stellaron's deeper content. Not the memories — something else. Something that was preserved alongside the memories." He pauses. "Something that was meant to be here, in this district, when the time came." Clara stands in the doorway and holds this. She is twelve years old. She has lived in this district for eight months. She has never lived anywhere else that she remembers, because she does not remember before the source code, because constructs do not have before unless someone gives them one. She calibrates frequency arrays by feel. She hears the second heartbeat. She was addressed today by name. "What does it mean," she says. Not I don't understand, not I'm afraid — just the direct question, the one she always asks. "I don't know yet," Svarog says. "But I think when the Stellaron's content is fully retrieved — when Seele and Luka have what they need — what remains will tell us." He pauses. "The addressing today was not a warning. It was not a threat." His optical sensors carry the expression that serves for gentle, for the thing that is trying not to be heavy. "I think it was recognition. The same recognition signal the formation produced when Seele made contact." He pauses. "Whatever is in the deeper content — it knows you are here." Clara looks at her hands. The small careful hands that calibrate by feel and sort cables and hold calibration components and do the workshop's work with the patience she learned from the most patient teacher available.
