Genuinely asking — not preempting, not predicting. "To sit in the office," she says. "At seven. With both sides of the cabinet open." She holds his gaze. "To not be the Guardian for an hour. To be—" She stops. She starts again, with the specific quality she has when she says things that cost something to say. "To be the person who has been meaning to tell you something since the construction chamber and has been waiting for there to be nothing pressing in on it." She pauses. "There will be moments when nothing is pressing. I want to use them." He looks at her. He looks at her with the expression that has been developing since the stairwell — the face that is not the Commander's and is not the almost-smile but the thing between and under both of those. "The office at seven," he says. "The office at seven," she says. "Both sides of the cabinet." "Both sides," she says. He picks up the patrol folder. He reads it. —— The nineteen days begin. —— On day one, at 0800, Seele and Luka go back into the corridor. Not sub-level three — not the secondary shaft, not the primary structure, not anything that requires the specific preparation and specific risk of what came before. The corridor. Nine-beta, the familiar path, the junctions they have walked thirty-plus times between them, the junction seven-alpha-four where they always stop without deciding to, the formation chamber at the end of the right branch. They go because the formation is still there and the retrieval is still incomplete and the contact events at the formation level — the interface level, not the primary structure — still have content to give. The most personal content. The content that is specifically them. They go without Gepard this morning. Not without permission — without need. The formation entries at the interface level are not the combat event of the sub-level approaches. The formations in the corridor have continued their usual behavior, the generator rate manageable, the familiar orange thresholds. They know this corridor. They know it the way Svarog knows the workshop — by feel, by the smell of it, by the specific cold that is different from every other cold. Seele runs the recorder calibration at the corridor entrance in the pre-dawn. The display shows the bifurcated signal at its current baseline — both signatures, the second Stellaron's output at the resting level that it has maintained since the sub-level three contact event. Slightly elevated from the original baseline. Settled. "Ready," she says. "Ready," he says. They go in. —— The corridor at 0600 on day one of the nineteen is different from the corridor in the early entries. Not in any measurable way — the sensor reads the same junctions, the temperature is the same, the crystallization on the walls maintains its dormant amber-black state. But the difference is in the quality of walking it. He has walked this corridor thirty-plus times. She has walked it almost as many. They know every junction. They know seven-alpha-four stops them both without a signal. They know the carved stone past junction eight and the ceiling drop and the torch light throwing its specific shadows. They know it together, now. Not just in parallel. At junction five, he does not feel a fragment. He has not felt a fragment at junction five since the first one — the girl with purple hair running ahead, looking back over her shoulder, laughing. That fragment was the beginning. The beginning has been given back. The primary structure gave them the name. What the formation still holds is something different from the fragments — something more complete, more personal, more specifically theirs. He walks and he does not reach for it. The reaching is not what produces the contact events — the contact events arrive when the conditions are right and he has been in this corridor long enough to know the conditions. She says, at junction seven: "Tell me something I don't know about you." He glances at her. "What?" "Something I don't know," she says. "We have been in these tunnels for months and I know your sensor data preferences and your combat brace calibration tolerances and your specific method of hitting a base junction at forty percent from the bottom — I know all the things that are observable." She looks ahead. "Tell me something that isn't." He looks at the corridor. He thinks about this. "I used to practice in the outer corridor network," he says. "Before the walkthrough job. Before Sampo came to me. The corridor past junction twelve — I found it when I was sixteen. By myself. I used to go in there with a training weight and practice the brace loading." He pauses. "I never told anyone. I just — went. Because the corridor was far enough from the inhabited sections that nobody would see." She looks at him sideways. "You were training in the tunnel above the second Stellaron since you were sixteen." "I didn't know what was there," he says. "I just knew the corridor was deep enough to be private." He pauses. "Although—" He stops. "Although," she says. "Although," he says, "I always went further than I needed to for privacy. I could have stopped at junction four and been private enough. I always went to the deeper junctions. As far as seven or eight." He pauses. "I told myself it was because the temperature change made the training harder. Better conditions." She looks at the corridor. "But." "But I don't think that was why," he says. "I think I was going because there was something there. Something I was going toward without knowing it was a thing to go toward." He pauses. "The second heartbeat was always louder past junction five." She is quiet for a moment. They walk. Junction eight. The carved stone. The torch on. "My mother used to bring me to the edge of the outer district before she died," she says. "Not into the tunnels — just to the entrance. She would bring me there at night when the district was quiet and she would hold my hand and we would stand at the entrance and listen." She pauses. "She never told me what she was listening for. I thought she just liked the cold." She pauses again. "She was listening for the second heartbeat." He is quiet. "She knew about it," Seele says. "She never told me what it was. But she knew about it and she brought me to listen to it and she held my hand." She looks at the corridor walls. "And then she died in the outer corridor collapse and we moved to the outer machine district because it was the closest available housing and—" She stops. "I had been to the entrance before. I knew the cold. I knew the second heartbeat. Moving to the district felt—" She pauses. "It felt like coming back to something." He looks at her. "Like you were supposed to be there." "Like I was supposed to be there," she says. They reach junction twelve. The branch. The chamber. The formation at two and a half meters, the old gold and deep water light continuous and primary, the two heartbeats running together already aligned from the moment they step into the space. She reaches out. Her hand makes contact. —— The memory that comes on day one is not the horror of the spike event or the weight of Vera's decision or the goodbye at junction twelve. It is before all of those. It is early — the formation small and new, the three of them in the chamber with the quality of an ordinary afternoon in a place that has become ordinary through repetition. They are eight years old. The formation is half the size it will be in the year of the dark winter. The light is dim — the source still small, still early. Vera is at the formation with her careful hands, making her readings, the patient work of someone who comes here often enough that the work is routine. And the two of them are — doing what they apparently did when they came to this chamber. Which is: arguing. The eight-year-old Seele and the eight-year-old Luka, sitting on the chamber floor with approximately two feet of space between them, are in an argument that has the specific quality of an argument between two children who have been having this argument for long enough that it has developed its own vocabulary. "You always go further than me," the eight-year-old Seele says. "I go at my own pace," the eight-year-old Luka says. "Your pace is faster than my pace!" "That's not a problem with my pace—" "It is a problem with your pace because your pace means I have to run to keep up and I don't like running when I don't have to!" "You like running!" "I like running when I choose to run! Not when you walk faster than me and I have to chase you!" Vera, at the formation, makes the not-quite-a-laugh sound. The eight-year-old Seele looks at her immediately. "Don't laugh," she says, with the authority of a child who has been told this is not funny and remains confident that it is not funny. "I'm not laughing," Vera says. "You're thinking about laughing," Seele says. "I would never," Vera says, and the edge of laughter is very present in the words. "Vera," the eight-year-old Luka says, "tell her she can keep up if she just—" "I don't need keeping up told to me—" "You just said you can't keep up—" "I said I shouldn't HAVE to keep up!" Vera turns from the formation. She looks at the two of them. Her expression is the warm one — the specific warmth that exists when a person is in the presence of something they love and the love is very visible because it has no reason to be hidden. "You argue about this every time," she says. "Because he does it every time!" the eight-year-old Seele says. "She does not have to run," the eight-year-old Luka says, with the specific stubbornness of a child who has made his point and is going to keep making it. "She just thinks she does because she assumes I am going faster than I am." "You ARE going faster—" "If you walk the same speed you always walk—" "I WALK AT A NORMAL SPEED—" "—and I walk the speed I always walk—" "—which is FASTER than normal—" Vera says: "Both of you." They both stop. She looks at them. "Luka," she says, "do you walk faster than Seele?" The eight-year-old Luka considers this. He opens his mouth. He considers it more carefully. "Yes," he says, finally, with the specific reluctance of a correct answer that is not the answer you wanted to give. "Seele," Vera says, "does it bother you that he walks faster?" "Yes," the eight-year-old Seele says, immediately, with zero reluctance. "Then the solution," Vera says, "is for Luka to walk slower." The eight-year-old Luka looks at her. "But—" "You are not in a race," Vera says. "You are both going to the same place. Walk at the same speed." She pauses. "It is not complicated." The eight-year-old Luka looks at Seele. The eight-year-old Seele looks at him. There is a moment of mutual consideration — the specific mutual consideration of two children who have been given a reasonable resolution and are deciding whether they want a reasonable resolution or whether they want to continue the argument. "Fine," the eight-year-old Luka says. "Fine," the eight-year-old Seele says. The memory holds this for a moment. Then the formation's light shifts — the contact event giving more, the next scene, the specific thing that was most theirs. The two children, side by side, walking out of the formation chamber together, and the eight-year-old Seele is not running and the eight-year-old Luka is not going ahead and they are walking at the same pace and the pace is — easy. The ease of two children who have figured out the correct speed, which is the speed that keeps them together, and who have immediately adjusted to it without needing to discuss it further because they are eight years old and when eight-year-olds solve a problem they solve it and move on. The memory gives them this. And then it holds on one specific thing. The eight-year-old Luka, walking beside the eight-year-old Seele in the corridor, looks at her. He looks at her the way an eight-year-old looks at the person who is most important to them — completely, without the managed peripheral of adulthood, the full direct look of someone for whom the person beside them is simply the most significant thing in their immediate world. He looks at her and she looks ahead and the second heartbeat is below them and the formation's light is behind them and they are walking at the correct speed. And the eight-year-old Luka thinks — the memory gives the thought with the specific texture of an eight-year-old's unfiltered internal experience — I don't want to go faster than her. I never want to go faster than her. I want us to go at the same speed and arrive at the same time and always be here. The contact ends. —— Seele pulls her hand from the formation. She turns to look at him. He is looking at the chamber wall — the specific quality of somewhere else, which means the memory is still arriving in him, landing. "Luka," she says. He looks at her. His face has the stripped quality and something additional — something that is the receiving of an eight-year-old's unfiltered thought about her, received from the memory at full volume, without the framework of adulthood to manage the reception. "We argued about your walking speed," she says. He makes a sound — not quite a laugh, the edge of one. "Every time," he says. "The memory gave us every time as a single memory because it was so consistent it is basically one event." "I was right," she says. "You were walking faster than me." He looks at her. "I have been informed of this," he says. "You admitted it to Vera," she says. "I admitted it to Vera," he says. She looks at the formation. She looks at the old gold and deep water light, the two heartbeats running together, the chamber that has been hers and his and Vera's since they were seven years old. "The thought," she says. "At the end. The eight-year-old thought." He is quiet. "Did you feel it?" she says. "Yes," he says. "The memory gave the thought with the full content of being eight years old and thinking it." He pauses. "No framework." "No framework," she agrees. "Just the thought." They stand in the formation chamber on day one of the nineteen days and the thought is in both of them — the eight-year-old certainty that is not complicated by adulthood or by what happened in the dark winter or by twenty-two years of not knowing, that is simply the most direct expression of what was true then and is presumably still true now. I don't want to go faster than her. I want us to go at the same speed and arrive at the same time and always be here. "We should go," Seele says. "Before the formation threshold." "Yes," he says. She turns toward the branch exit. She stops. She turns back. She walks up to him and she looks at him and he looks at her and she says: "We go at the same speed." He looks at her. The corner of his mouth does the thing. "Yes," he says. "We do." She goes. He follows. At the same speed. —— On day three, the Moles go to Boulder Town. This is not related to the Stellarons or the notation sections or the corridor mapping. It is related to the fact that it is day three of the nineteen and it is a rest day by the specific logic of Oleg, who announced at 0800 that there was a Fighter's Circuit event in Boulder Town that afternoon and that he had tickets for all of them including Clara, and that the Fighter's Circuit was non-negotiable. "What's the Fighter's Circuit?" Clara says. "It's the fights," Oleg says. "What fights?" "The Boulder Town Super League preliminary rounds," Pino says. "They run every season. The final is the Super League Championship but the preliminary rounds are what you go to if you want to see the fights without the championship crowd." Clara looks at Toma. "Did you know about this?" "I've been twice," Toma says. "It's loud and it's very good." Clara looks at Oleg. "Do you have actual tickets or are you saying tickets as a figure of speech?" Oleg produces tickets from his jacket. Clara stares at them. She looks at Oleg. She looks at the tickets. "How does the jacket—" "The jacket is a system," Oleg says. "I know the jacket is a system," Clara says. "That doesn't explain how tickets for today's fight were in it." "I got them yesterday," Oleg says. "They fit in the pocket." "Everything fits in the pocket," Clara says. "Yes," Oleg says. "That's not an explanation," Clara says. "No," Oleg says. "But the fights are in three hours so if we want to get good spots we should go now." Clara looks at the tickets. She looks at the secondary worktable where the correlation analysis from the outer corridor seven fourth notation block is waiting for her attention. She looks at Toma, who is wearing the expression of someone who has already decided they are going to Boulder Town and is simply waiting for Clara to arrive at the same conclusion. She looks at Svarog's back room. "Svarog," she says. "Go," Svarog says, from the back room, without looking up from whatever he is doing. "The fourth notation block analysis—" "Will be there when you come back," Svarog says. "Go." Clara looks at the tickets. She looks at the Moles. "We should eat something first," she says. "If we're going to be standing at a fight ring for three hours we should eat first." "The jacket has snacks," Oleg says. "The jacket cannot possibly have enough snacks for three hours of a fight," Clara says. "The jacket has significant snack capacity," Oleg says. "I'll buy something at the district bakery on the way," Clara says, and she is already getting her coat, which means the decision has been made, which Toma and Pino and Oleg all know because they know Clara and they know that Clara getting her coat is the operative conclusion regardless of whatever verbal processing is still ongoing. They go to Boulder Town. —— Boulder Town is louder than Clara expected. She knew it would be loud — she has been to the outer machine district's generator maintenance events and those are loud in the specific way of mechanical things. This is different. This is the specific loud of a large number of people watching something that is producing genuine excitement, the kind of crowd noise that has different qualities at different moments — the anticipatory hum, the eruption, the specific sound of an audience that knows what it is looking at and is responding to it with the full force of knowing. The fight ring is in Boulder Town's central square, which has been cleared for the occasion and ringed with the temporary seating that the Super League preliminaries use. The Moles have maneuvered — with the specific spatial intelligence of children who have been navigating the Underworld's secondary tunnels for two years — to a position that is close enough to the ring to see the fighters clearly but far enough back that the crowd surge does not push them into each other. Clara stands between Toma and Oleg and she looks at the ring. "Who's fighting first?" she says. "The preliminaries are open draws," Pino says. He has the fight card on his phone already. "But the headliner is—" He stops. He looks at the fight card. He looks at Clara. He looks at Toma. "The headliner is Luka." Clara stares at him. "What?" "Luka," Pino says. "He's in the Boulder Town Super League." He shows her the fight card. The Boulder Town Super League Championship preliminary draw. And there, in the headliner slot for the afternoon session: LUKA. "He's defending champion from last season." Clara looks at the fight card. She looks at the ring. She looks at Toma, who is looking at the fight card with the expression of someone who has just realized the afternoon got significantly more interesting. She looks at Oleg, who is eating from the jacket and looks unsurprised. "Did you know about this?" Clara says to Oleg. "I knew he was in the Super League," Oleg says. "That's part of why I got the tickets." "You didn't tell us!" Clara says. "I thought it would be better as a surprise," Oleg says. "It's—" Clara looks at the ring. "It's a very big surprise!" She pauses. "How long has he been in the Super League?" "Since before we knew him," Toma says. She is reading the fight card over Pino's shoulder. "He's been Boulder Town champion for two seasons. The Super League is the Underworld-wide circuit." She pauses. "So he is the best fighter in Boulder Town and he has been this whole time." "While also going into sub-level three," Pino says. "While also going into sub-level three," Toma confirms. Clara looks at the ring. She thinks about the combat braces and the base junction targeting and the platform lift in sub-level three and the way Luka moves in a fight — the economy of motion, the reading of structural weak points, the specific precision of someone who has been doing this for a long time and has the body knowledge of long practice. "He's very good," she says. "Isn't he." "He's defending champion of the Boulder Town Super League," Oleg says. "Which means he's very good," Clara says. "Yes," Oleg says. The first preliminary bout begins in the ring below them. The crowd noise goes up. Toma goes immediately to the railing with her phone out — not because she is documenting for the notation project, because she wants to see the fight up close and is photographing it for the same reason she photographs everything, which is because looking at things closely tells you things about them that you do not get from looking at them from a distance. Pino is already watching with the open-direct interest expression, the one he brought to his conversation with Svarog about the Separation. Oleg has found a position against a structural support column that gives him a clear view of the ring and a comfortable back surface simultaneously. Clara watches the preliminary bout. She watches it with the specific attention she brings to things that have mechanics — reading the structure of the fight, the way the fighters are assessing each other, the specific moments where one fighter reads the other's approach and adjusts. She is twelve years old and she has been in Svarog's workshop for eight months and Svarog's workshop has made her someone who looks at how things work. After the first bout she says to nobody in particular: "The fighter in blue made a mistake at the start of the third exchange. He telegraphed the direction of his shift before executing it." Toma looks at her. "Did he?" "He moved his weight right before he actually shifted right," Clara says. "By about a full second. If the other fighter had been watching for it—" "He would have anticipated the shift," Pino says, looking at the replay on his phone. "And counter-positioned before it completed." He looks at the replay. "You're right. The weight transfer was visible." Clara looks at the ring, where the second preliminary bout is being set up. "Luka doesn't do that," she says. "Does what?" Toma says. "Telegraph," Clara says. "His weight transfers are minimal. He moves from a position of equilibrium rather than a position of preparation. The preparation is not visible." She pauses. "That's why the base junction strikes are effective. The target doesn't know the strike is coming until the weight is already behind it." Oleg says: "How do you know that?" "Sub-level three," Clara says. "I watched the sensor data from the sub-level entries. The timing of the brace loading events against the formation response times." She pauses. "He hits things before they know they are going to be hit." The Moles look at each other. "That's how he wins," Toma says. "That's one way he wins," Clara says. The afternoon progresses through the preliminary bouts and the crowd noise builds and the Moles eat from the jacket and Clara eats the bakery roll she brought and watches with the calibration-by-feel attention that is her version of watching things, and at the end of the preliminaries the headliner bout is announced and the crowd's hum shifts quality — the anticipatory version, the version that knows what it is about to see. Luka comes out. Clara has seen Luka in the workshop and in the outer corridors and in sub-level three. She has seen the combat braces on and the sensor in his left hand and the specific economy of motion in the cave. She has not seen him in this context — in the fight ring, in the Boulder Town Super League, in the headliner slot of the preliminary round. He looks different here. Not a different person — a more specific version of the same person, the way things look when they are in the context they were built for. The ease of the way he carries himself in the ring — not the performing ease of someone who wants to look comfortable but the actual ease of someone who is. He knows this space. He knows it the way he knows the corridor past junction eight. The opponent is large. Significantly larger than Luka — one of the outer district's established fighters, someone the fight card identifies as having placed in the Super League's upper bracket for three consecutive seasons. The bout starts. Clara watches. The first exchange is probing — both fighters reading each other, the specific reading phase that comes before the commitment phase. Luka is doing the reading with the open-direct quality he has when he is assessing a formation's structural weak points, except the formation is a person and the structural weak points are the specific patterns of how the opponent moves. Three exchanges and he has what he needs. The fourth exchange is not a probe. The fourth exchange is Luka having completed the read and implementing the conclusion. The movement is exactly what Clara said — minimal telegraph, equilibrium position, and the weight behind the strike is full and committed and it arrives before the opponent has anticipated it. The bout ends in the seventh exchange. Boulder Town makes a very specific noise that Clara has not heard a crowd make before — the noise of people watching someone do something they came specifically to see and seeing it as well as they hoped. "Oh!" Toma says — the actual, full-volume, unmanaged oh of genuine surprise and delight, even though she presumably knew he would win, because knowing someone will win is different from watching them win and the watching is its own thing. "YES!" Pino says, with an enthusiasm that is entirely twelve years old and has absolutely zero relationship to the professional interest he was applying to the fight analysis three seconds ago. Oleg says nothing. But he is smiling — the real one, the one that is not the default neutrality — and he is looking at the ring with the expression of someone who is pleased in a way that they did not expect to be as pleased as they are. Clara is looking at the ring. She is looking at Luka taking the bout result from the referee, the easy acknowledgment of the crowd, the specific way he carries the win — not performing the victory, simply present in it. She is thinking about the platform lift in sub-level three and the combat braces and the reformed fifth form and the inscription on the tablet Svarog has in the deep storage that she has never been allowed to fully read that she suspects is a record of the specific individual to whom the artifact was always going to belong. She is thinking: he is the champion of Boulder Town and he has been this whole time and she did not know. She is thinking: there are always more dimensions than the ones you are looking at. "Clara?" Toma says. Clara looks at her. She looks at the ring. She looks at the Moles. She grins. Full — uncontrolled, the twelve-year-old version, the one that surfaces when something is genuinely good and you are not suppressing it. "He's going to be so surprised that we know," she says. "Should we tell him we were here?" Pino says. "Yes!" Clara says. "Immediately! He should know we saw it!" "He might be embarrassed," Toma says. "Why would he be embarrassed?!" Clara says. "He just won the headliner bout of the Boulder Town Super League preliminary! That's not embarrassing, that's—" She pauses. "That's extremely impressive and he should know we think so!" "Agreed," Oleg says. "We tell him tonight," Toma says. "Tonight," Clara confirms. They go back to the outer machine district with the afternoon in them and the noise of Boulder Town still in their ears, and Clara messages Luka on the walk: 'We went to the Boulder Town Super League preliminary today. We saw your bout. You hit the seventh exchange before he had finished reading the fourth.' His response comes in four minutes: 'How did you end up at the Super League??' Clara: 'Oleg had tickets.' Luka: 'Of course Oleg had tickets.' Clara: 'The jacket is a system.' Luka: 'I am aware.' Clara: 'You are defending champion of the Boulder Town Super League and you have been in our workshop every day for eight months and you never mentioned this.' A pause. Then: 'It didn't seem relevant to the corridor entries.' Clara: 'It is relevant! We want to know things about you that are not relevant to the corridor entries!' Another pause. Then: 'I'll be at the workshop at 0600 tomorrow.' Clara: 'We will ask you about the Super League at 0600 tomorrow.' Luka: 'I look forward to it.' Clara: 'No you don't.' Luka: 'No I don't. But I'll answer the questions.' Clara looks at this exchange and then shows it to Toma, who reads it and does the Toma-version of the full grin, which is a quick real thing that appears before she can manage it and is gone again fast but was genuinely there. "He's going to answer the questions," Toma says. "Yes," Clara says. "He's dreading the questions," Toma says. "Obviously," Clara says. They walk back through the Underworld's main corridor in the afternoon light and Clara is twelve years old and the Stellarons are what they are and the first Stellaron is at 2.4 and the nineteen-day window is running and in eighteen days something significant is going to happen and right now Clara is walking back from Boulder Town with the Moles and she won a morning roll from Oleg because she correctly predicted the duration of the seventh exchange and it is a good afternoon. —— On day five, Gepard comes to the office at seven. Both sides of the cabinet are open. The lamp is on. Bronya is at the desk not reading the briefing in front of her, which she has not been reading since approximately 0640 when she arrived and opened both sides of the cabinet and put the kettle on. He closes the door. He sits in the chair from the corner. She looks at him. She looks at the cabinet — both sides, the two cups. She looks at the desk between them. "I want to tell you something," she says. "That I have been carrying since before the IPC visit. Since before the array. Since—" She stops. She starts again, and this time the starting has the quality of something she has decided to say and is saying regardless of the cost of saying it. "Since the first time I understood that what was happening between us was not a professional relationship with a warm quality. That it was the other thing." He looks at her. He does not say I know. He waits. "I was seventeen years old when you stood outside the briefing room for six minutes," she says. "I told you I had been counting since then. That is true. What I did not tell you is what the counting meant. I was counting because I wanted the information specifically. I wanted to know the specific number of minutes because the specific number of minutes was yours and yours specifically interested me." She looks at her hands. "And when you came through the door and gave the briefing and it was clear that you were — you — I understood that the counting was not a neutral behavior." She pauses. "I was seventeen years old and I understood it and I spent five years doing the correct thing about it. And the correct thing was: nothing. Because I was your Commander and then your Supreme Guardian and the correct thing was always nothing." He is looking at her. He has not moved. He is looking at her with the face that is not the Commander's and not the almost-smile and is the specific face of someone who is receiving something that matters. "I have been the Supreme Guardian for four months," she says. "And in those four months I have been — I have been learning what it means to hold a country and also hold—" She stops. "To hold this. Simultaneously. Not one or the other. Both." She looks at him. "I don't know if I have learned it properly yet. I don't know if there is a properly. But I know that both are real and I know that I don't want to choose." She pauses. "I am not asking you to solve that for me. I'm telling you what is true." He is quiet for a moment. "You have been doing the correct thing for five years," he says. "And the correct thing has been: nothing official, nothing that created a difficulty for the structure. And you have also been—" He pauses. He is finding the words the way he finds everything — by looking at what is true and stating it accurately. "You have been present," he says. "At seven. With both sides of the cabinet. With the thing in the stairwell. With the palm pressed to your jaw. With I know said in the language we have been building." He looks at her. "The correct thing has been nothing official. You have been doing the correct thing. And you have also been doing the real thing." He holds her gaze. "Both." She looks at him. "Yes," she says. "I know," he says, and this time it is the full version, the version that carries five years of it. "I have always known." He pauses. "I have also been doing the correct thing for five years. And the real thing simultaneously." He pauses again. "The stairwell. The construction chamber. The forehead. The patrol folder." He holds her gaze. "Both. Always both." She is quiet for a moment. "The thing I have been meaning to tell you since the construction chamber," she says. "Tell me," he says. Genuinely — not predicting, not preempting, waiting for the specific thing in the specific words she has found for it. She looks at the desk. She looks at him. "You make this bearable," she says. "Not manageable — bearable. The Stellarons and the IPC and the dark winter and the district and the signal at 2.4 and everything that is coming. The weight of it would be what it is regardless of you. But there is a difference between carrying something alone and carrying it in the same direction as someone else." She pauses. "You are the someone else. Specifically. Irreducibly. The same word she used." She pauses again. "And I have been wanting to tell you that clearly, without the professional architecture, without the language of the Guardian, for longer than the construction chamber." She looks at him. "That is what I have been not-saying." He looks at her. He looks at her with the expression that has been building since Everwinter Hill and the stairwell and the construction chamber and the forehead and the palm and all of it, all of it, and the expression now is not any of its previous versions but the thing all of them were building toward, which is the version with no remaining distance between what is true and what is visible. "Bronya," he says. "Yes," she says. He stands from the chair. He crosses the room. He sits on the edge of the desk — not across it, on it, the specific closing of the distance that the desk has been for four months, and he is close enough now that the professional architecture is simply not available as a framework. He puts his hand on the side of her face. She closes her eyes. He leans forward and presses his lips to her forehead — the deliberate placement, the real thing, the specific warmth of it in the office at seven with both sides of the cabinet open and the morning light coming through the north-facing windows. She opens her eyes. She looks at him — close range, the grey eyes, the lamp's warmth on his face, the Commander's face with the Commander's expression absent and the other face entirely present. "Both," she says. "Both," he says. "Always." She puts her hand over his on her face. They stay like that — his hand on her face and her hand over his and the desk between his position and hers bridged by the closing of it — in the office at seven with Belobog outside the windows doing its morning. After a while she says: "The patrol folder." "I'll read it," he says. "When?" she says. "After this," he says. She looks at him. "After what exactly," she says. "After however long this takes," he says. She looks at him. She looks at the patrol folder on the corner of the desk. She looks at him again. "Acceptable," she says. —— On day eight, the second memory piece of the personal retrieval arrives. Not in the formation — at junction seven-alpha-four. They are doing the morning entry, moving through the corridor at the established pace, the sensor reading and the recorder running and the familiar light of the formation visible from junction ten onward. They reach junction seven-alpha-four. They stop — the usual involuntary stop, the both-of-them-without-a-signal stop that has been happening since the first joint entry. The north wall. The amber-black crystallization, the convexity pointing toward the palace foundation, the dormant formation. Seele puts her hand on the crystallization sheet. She has done this once before — the first recognition signal, the spike on the recorder, the source recognizing the contact at junction seven-alpha-four. This time the spike is different. Not the recognition signal quality — something more. The formation responding not just to her presence but to the specific thing that the memory fragment produces, the recognition quality having deepened since the primary structure contact, the source knowing her more completely now. The memory does not arrive as a fragment. It arrives as understanding. Not an image, not a sound, not the textural quality of a specific moment. Something more diffuse — the understanding of what the two children meant to each other, not in any one moment but in the accumulated weight of every moment, the understanding that you receive when you have been given enough pieces that the picture between the pieces is present. What they understand is this: There has never been a version of either of them that did not include the other. Not since they were seven years old in the formation chamber for the first time. Not in any memory the source holds. Not in any piece that the retrieval has given. In every fragment, every memory, every flash and recognition signal and primary structure contact event — in all of it — they are together. Not because they were always in the same place at the same time, but because the presence of one of them in any memory implies the other. The absence of one of them is the most notable thing about any memory in which they are apart. The weight of the absence. This is what it means to be the most important person to someone — not the largest or the most dramatic or the most formally declared. The most necessary. The one whose absence is the loudest thing in the room. Seele takes her hand off the wall. She looks at Luka. He is looking at the wall. He has the quality of someone who has received the same thing she received — not from the contact, from the proximity. The formation's ambient field at junction seven-alpha-four, at the output level it is currently at, is sufficient to produce the understanding without the contact event. They both received it. Standing at their junction, at the wall that has stopped them without a signal since the first joint entry. "Oh," Seele says. He looks at her. "Oh," she says again. Not the standing-in-it okay. The genuine response to arriving at a thing — the specific oh of someone who has just understood something that was always true and is only now understanding it as true, the understanding being different from the fact. "Yes," he says. Not the vocabulary — the full version. He has arrived at the same oh and they are standing in the same understanding at the same junction that has always been the junction where things arrive without being invited. She looks at the wall. She looks at him. She looks at the formation visible further down the corridor, the light more present at the outer ranges than it was three weeks ago. "We should keep moving," she says. "Yes," he says. She walks. He walks beside her. At the same speed. The established speed, the one they found eight months of tunnels ago, the speed that keeps them together and arrives at the same time. The second heartbeat is below, settled. The formation is ahead. The retrieval is still in progress. —— On day twelve, Serval comes to the workshop with a finding that produces a silence of approximately ninety seconds. She comes in at 1500 with her portable analyzer and the specific quality of someone who has been in the Energy Hub since 0700 and has found something that she needed to leave the Energy Hub to say in person. She puts the analyzer on the primary worktable. She looks at Svarog. She looks at Clara. She looks at Luka, who is at the secondary worktable reviewing the fourth notation block documentation. "The array's sustained activation architecture," she says. "I finished the restructuring. Eleven minutes of sustained output is achievable." She pauses. "I also ran a simulation of what an eleven-minute array activation does to the first Stellaron's signal in the context of the current calibration progression." "And?" Luka says. "An eleven-minute array activation will not suppress the first Stellaron's signal to 1.8," she says. "Not at the current level. Not with the current calibration state." She pauses. "At the current signal level and current calibration, an eleven-minute activation suppresses the first Stellaron's signal by approximately 0.3 kilocycles. It goes from 2.4 to 2.1." "That's not suppression," Luka says. "That's a minor interruption." "No," Serval says. "It's not suppression. And more importantly—" She pauses. "The calibration is not static. The calibration is being built by each contact event. Each retrieval from the second Stellaron adjusts the calibration. The array's disruption frequency was designed for the first Stellaron at 1.3 before the calibration progressed." She looks at Svarog. "The array becomes less effective with each passing day because the first Stellaron's state is changing as the calibration builds." "The array was a tool for a previous version of the problem," Svarog says. "Yes," Serval says. "We fractured the feedback loop. The array did what it was built to do. But the problem is no longer the feedback loop." She pauses. "The problem is the calibration. And the array cannot address calibration." "Then the array is not the solution for what's coming," Luka says. "The array is not the solution for what's coming," Serval confirms. The ninety seconds of silence. Then Clara says, from her stool, with the flat directness of someone who has been listening and has arrived at the next question: "What is?" Serval looks at her. "I don't know yet," Serval says. Clara nods. "Okay," she says. The standing-in-it version. "But we should probably start finding out." "Yes," Serval says. "Bronya should know this today," Luka says. "I came here first," Serval says. "I am going to the palace in thirty minutes. I wanted Svarog to have this information before the briefing." Svarog looks at the analyzer. He looks at the data. He looks at the ninety-second silence that has just been filled with Clara's question and Serval's I don't know yet and Luka's Bronya should know this today, which is the outer machine district's specific method of processing significant information — immediately, practically, the next step first. "The lower notation section," he says. "The pre-Separation notation. The connection to the first Stellaron that predates my presence in this district." He pauses. "The person who built that connection knew the first Stellaron's broadcast frequency and built a conducting interface for it in the outer corridor wall before the district as we know it existed." He looks at Serval. "I believe they also left an answer for this question. I believe the answer is in the outer corridor network. In the sections we have not yet mapped." "We mapped seven sections this week," Clara says. "There are—" She consults her notes. "Fourteen sections we haven't reached yet in the outer corridor network." "We map all fourteen," Svarog says. "Starting tomorrow?" Clara says. "Starting tomorrow," Svarog says. Clara looks at the Moles — or rather, looks at where the Moles would be if they were currently in the workshop, which they are not because it is 1500 and they are in the Rivet Town salvage corridors doing the afternoon run. She picks up her phone. She messages the Moles group: 'Outer corridor mapping tomorrow 0700. All remaining sections. Svarog thinks the answer to the first Stellaron problem might be in the notation network.' Toma responds immediately: 'We'll be there. Oleg has the thermos ready already.' Oleg responds: 'The jacket is prepared.' Pino responds: 'I recharged the sensor last night. We're ready.' Clara looks at the messages. She looks at the workshop. She looks at the warm space — Svarog with his four hands and the secondary worktable and the fragment's glow and the conducted warmth from Luka at the secondary worktable with the artifact in his spine and the finding that needs to go to Bronya in thirty minutes. She looks at her own hands on the stool surface. She is twelve years old. She calibrates by feel. The second heartbeat called her by name. The notation that is older than Svarog is in the outer corridors and they are going to map all fourteen remaining sections tomorrow. The answer might be there. Or it might not be. But they are going to find out. She picks up the next calibration component. She goes back to work. —— On day fifteen, the third personal memory piece arrives. Not in the formation and not at junction seven-alpha-four. In the outer machine district, at the main junction, at 1400 hours, when they are walking back from the ward where Natasha has done the monitoring check on the rib resolution and the spinal integration. They are walking back through the mid-afternoon district — the generators doing the midday cycle, the amber lamps at full daytime output, the outer district's specific warmth that is different from the other warmth — and the memory arrives the way certain things arrive when you have been building toward them long enough that they can arrive anywhere, at any moment, in the middle of any ordinary thing. They are nine years old. The outer machine district. The main junction. Mid-afternoon — the specific time of day when the district children have finished the morning's work-adjacent activities and have the afternoon hours for the things that are their own. They are standing at the main junction with the quality of two children who have just arrived somewhere and are deciding what to come next. The nine-year-old Luka says: "We could go to the formation." The nine-year-old Seele says: "We went yesterday." "We could go again." "We can go anytime," she says. "The formation is always there. What else is there?" The nine-year-old Luka looks at the junction. He looks at the district around them — the generator housings and the outer corridor entrances and the workshop direction and the main path. He looks like someone who has approximately eight ideas about what to do next and is trying to arrange them by preference. "There's the generator corridor past junction six," he says. "Toma found a section last week with the old pipes that you can climb." "I know about the pipes," she says. "I've been to the pipes." "With Toma?" "With Toma." "When?" "Last week, when you were at the workshop with Vera and Sampo learning the geomarrow calibration." "I was not learning geomarrow calibration," he says. "Vera was explaining geomarrow calibration and I was listening because it is polite to listen." "You were learning it," she says, with the flat certainty of someone who was told about this afterward and has made a judgment. "You came home and you explained geomarrow calibration to me." "I was explaining what I had heard," he says. "That is different." "Is it?" He opens his mouth. He closes it. He does the thing he does at eight and nine years old when he has been presented with an argument that is technically valid and is deciding whether to accept it or continue the original position. "The pipes are good even the second time," he says, which is the eight-year-old version of subject change. "The pipes are good every time," she says, and the hostile wariness is not there because at nine years old she has not built it yet, she is simply Seele without the architecture, and Seele without the architecture says things like the pipes are good every time with the flat certainty of someone who has been to the pipes and found them worth going back to. "Then we go to the pipes," he says. "Yes," she says. They go to the pipes. The memory gives them this — not the pipe-climbing specifically, the walking to it. Two nine-year-olds walking through the outer machine district at mid-afternoon with the quality of children who have nowhere specific to be and the afternoon ahead and a destination that is specific enough to walk toward. The specific ease of it. The specific ordinary warmth of it. And then what the memory gives that the walking contains: the implicit fact of them. The fact that is not stated and does not need to be stated, that is simply present in the quality of the walking — the nine-year-old Luka walking beside the nine-year-old Seele, going to the pipes instead of the formation, the afternoon opening ahead of them, and the quality of the afternoon as something shared rather than parallel. The implicit fact of: I would rather be here, wherever here is, than anywhere without you. Not said. Not decided. Not even thought as a thought, at nine years old. Simply: the quality of the walking. The memory holds this. Then it releases. They are at the main junction in the outer machine district at 1400 hours on day fifteen of the nineteen days, and they are standing where two nine-year-olds stood at 1400 on an afternoon that the second Stellaron preserved for twenty-two years. "The pipes," Seele says. Luka looks at her. "The generator corridor past junction six," she says. "Toma's pipes. The ones you can climb." She looks at the junction direction. "Do they still—" "The pipes are still there," he says. He knows because he was in that corridor six months ago on a walkthrough. "Sector C maintenance cleared the section but the old pipe structure is in the wall. Fixed to the stone." She looks at the junction direction. He looks at her. "The formation is always there," she says. It is the nine-year-old's words and it is also true now, still true, the formation is always there and always will be and there are four days left in the nineteen and the retrieval is still in progress and the formation will give what it gives when it gives it. "What else is there," he says. She looks at him. She holds out her hand. He takes it — the deliberate version, the adult version, the version that knows exactly what it is doing and has chosen it. They are standing at the main junction of the outer machine district at 1400 hours on day fifteen, and the district is doing its mid-afternoon, and the generator corridor past junction six is a seven-minute walk. "Let's go," she says. They go. They climb the pipes. Toma was right — the pipes are good every time. —— On day seventeen, Gepard reads the Sector A report and finds something that requires immediate conversation. He goes to the office at 0900, which is not seven but is not so far from seven that the both-sides-of-the-cabinet context has fully dissolved. Bronya is at the desk reading the archive files. She looks up when he comes in. "The morning report," he says, and puts it on the desk. She reads it. She looks at the specific numbers — the crystallization density in northern Sector A, the vertical component reading, the specific rate of change over the past forty-eight hours. "The rate has changed," she says. "In the past forty-eight hours," he says, "the acceleration has — stopped." She looks at him. "The signal is still climbing." "The signal is still climbing," he says. "But the acceleration — the rate at which the rate is increasing — has plateaued. As of two days ago." He looks at the report. "The crystallization density increment per hour has been constant at 0.11 for forty-eight hours. Not increasing." She looks at the report. "What changed two days ago?" He looks at her. "Day fifteen," he says. "The contact event at junction seven-alpha-four." She is quiet for a moment. "The third personal memory piece," she says. "Yes," he says. "Whatever the contact event gave them — whatever the formation released on day fifteen — the first Stellaron's acceleration plateau began that night." She looks at the signal data. "The first Stellaron responded to the retrieval," she says. "The first Stellaron has been responding to every retrieval," he says. "But this one produced a different response. Not a signal increase. A stabilization of the rate." He looks at the data. "I don't know what that means. But it is a change." She looks at the data. She looks at the window. "When the retrieval is complete," she says. "When Seele and Luka have what they need — the most personal piece, the sacrifice, the full weight of what they were to each other — the second Stellaron will manifest." She pauses. "And when that happens, the first Stellaron will respond. We know the first Stellaron will jump to approximately 3.1." She pauses. "But we don't know if the plateau in the acceleration rate is a sign that the response will be different from what the model predicts." "We don't know," he says. "We continue preparing," she says. "The Silvermane Guard. Wildfire. Svarog and Clara and Sampo — they need to know their roles when the second Stellaron manifests." "I'll brief Dunn today," he says. "And I'll speak to Svarog," she says. "And Sampo." She looks at the report. She looks at him. "Gepard," she says. "Yes," he says. "We have two days," she says. "Two days before the retrieval reaches its final piece. Two days before the second Stellaron's manifestation." She holds his gaze. "I want you to know that whatever happens after those two days — whatever the second Stellaron manifests as, whatever the first Stellaron does in response — I am going to be present. For all of it." She pauses. "And I want you to be present for all of it too." He looks at her. He looks at the patrol folder. He looks at the office — the cabinet with both sides open, the lamp, the north-facing windows, the desk between them with the signal data and the morning report and the monitor showing 2.6 as of this morning. "I'll be present," he says. "Good," she says. He picks up the patrol folder. He goes to brief Dunn. —— That evening, in the outer machine district, Svarog calls Clara into the back room. He calls her in the way Svarog does things — not a summons, not an instruction. He says: "Clara. Come in." And the quality of come in is the quality it has when there is something to be said. She comes in. She sits on the storage case she has been sitting on for eight months when they have conversations in the back room. Her feet do not quite reach the floor. Svarog looks at her with the optical sensors. "I have enough to say the thing I told you I would say when I had enough," he says. She looks at him. "The hypothesis," she says. "The one that is partly about me." "Yes," he says. "The lower notation sections in the outer corridor network. The fourteen sections you and the Moles have mapped. The frequency at 4.7 kilocycles. The pre-Svarog notation." He pauses. "I have been comparing the notation system to every system I have documentation for. I have been doing this for two weeks." He pauses again. "The notation system of the lower sections does not match any human system I have record of. Not the pre-Separation builders. Not any iteration of the Underworld's construction periods. Not any system from any other planet in the IPC's documentation that Serval shared with me." Clara looks at him. "Then who wrote it?" Svarog looks at her with all four hands still. "I believe," he says, "that the lower notation sections were written by something that is not human. Something that was in this district before the pre-Separation builders arrived, before the Separation, before any of the historical periods I have access to." He pauses. "Something that knew about both Stellarons. That built the connection between them. That designed the system that the pre-Separation builders later built upon when they came and found the notation and understood — partially — what it was for." Clara is very still. "Something that knew about both Stellarons," she says. "Yes," he says. "And built a system," she says. "Yes," he says. "And then was gone," she says. "Before the pre-Separation builders came." "Apparently," he says. She looks at her hands. She looks at the workshop through the doorway. She looks at the fragment in the equipment tray, the old gold and deep water light. "Svarog," she says. "The part of this that is about me." "Yes," he says. "The source code Topaz gave me," she says. "You said you thought it came from the Stellaron's deeper content. From something preserved alongside the memories." "Yes," he says. "Do you think—" She stops. She starts again with the careful Clara precision of someone who does not want to say something that is less accurate than the reality. "Do you think what preserved the source code in the Stellaron was the same thing that wrote the lower notation sections?" Svarog looks at her. "Yes," he says. "I think it is possible that what wrote the notation sections placed something in the second Stellaron's content — something that was not a memory and was not Vera's endurance but was a different kind of preservation. Something intended for a specific occasion." He pauses. "And I think the specific occasion it was intended for is the one we are currently in." Clara is quiet for a long moment. She looks at the workshop. At the secondary worktable with its calibration components. At the fragment. At the conducted warmth from the workshop's walls that has increased since Luka's integration added its own warmth to the space. "That's a very large thing to be," she says. "Yes," he says. "I don't understand it yet," she says. "No," he says. "You don't need to understand it yet. You only need to know that it is possible and that when the understanding arrives, you will be ready for it." She looks at him. "How do you know I'll be ready?" Svarog looks at her with the optical sensor expression that serves for fond, that has been present since she arrived eight months ago and has never not been present when he looks at her. "Because," he says, "you have been getting ready since the day you arrived." Clara looks at the workshop. She looks at the Moles' empty stool — Oleg's stool, that was hers before it became Oleg's by right of occupation. She looks at the calibration components and the cable inventory she organized and the recorder on its stand and the fragment in the tray. She looks at the two warm things in the workshop — the fragment and the conducted warmth from Luka's integration. And she looks at her own hands. And she thinks about calibrating by feel. And the second heartbeat calling her name. And the notation older than Svarog. "Okay," she says. Not the standing-in-it version this time. Something past that. The version that means: I receive this. I am going to carry it. I don't understand it yet and I am going to carry it until I do. "Okay," Svarog says. She goes back to the secondary worktable. She goes back to work. Outside, the outer machine district does its evening. The generators cycle through the last pressure check. The amber lamps shift to their overnight minimum. The second heartbeat is below, patient and warm, holding what it holds for two more days. Then it will give the last piece. Then the second Stellaron will manifest. And the outer machine district will face what it faces with Svarog and Clara and Sampo and the Silvermane Guard and Wildfire and all the people who have been preparing, quietly and specifically and for the occasion, for as long as each of them has been preparing — which is, in different ways, their whole lives. The second heartbeat counts. Two days. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 39: "Day Eighteen" (Rewrite) Chapter Text Lynx Landau arrives in the outer machine district on day sixteen of the nineteen, which is not where anyone expected her to arrive. She is supposed to be at the palace. She had a meeting scheduled with Pela at 1400 in the analysis room — the specific meeting they have every two weeks since Lynx joined the Wildfire medical response units, the one that looks like a briefing update but which both of them understand is primarily an occasion for Pela to be around someone her own age who does not require Pela to be competent at everything. She is not at the palace at 1400. She is in the outer machine district at 1200, which means she left early, which means the outer machine district was the destination rather than the palace being the destination with the outer machine district as an alternative. Gepard gets a message from her at 1215: 'I went to the outer district. Don't ask. Back before dinner.' He reads this. He reads it twice. He puts the phone down. He picks up his patrol folder. He reads the patrol folder for approximately forty seconds. Then he picks up his phone again. He messages Pela: 'Lynx is in the outer district. She messaged me don't ask which means there is something she wanted to see without having to explain it first.' Pela responds in thirty seconds: 'She has been wanting to see the workshop since I told her about Clara three weeks ago.' Gepard: 'You told her about Clara.' Pela: 'I tell Lynx things. She's my friend. She wanted to know about the district.' Gepard: 'She is sixteen years old and she went to the outer machine district alone—' Pela: 'She is sixteen years old and she has been working with Wildfire medical response units for a year. She knows the Underworld better than most of the Silvermane Guard. She is completely fine.' Gepard looks at this message for a moment. He is not specifically worried about Lynx's safety — Lynx has the quality of someone who has been navigating the Underworld's secondary networks since she was twelve and who finds most situations manageable if she thinks about them clearly. He messages Pela: 'Tell me if she messages you anything alarming.' Pela: 'She won't. She's going to the workshop and she'll come back with opinions about Clara.' Gepard: 'What kind of opinions?' Pela: 'Enthusiastic ones. She likes people who are exceptional at things.' He puts the phone down. He messages Luka: 'My sister is in the outer district. Heading to your workshop. Not dangerous but she is sixteen and she will have questions. I apologize in advance.' Luka responds in forty-five seconds: 'Understood. She'll be fine. The Moles will find her before she reaches the workshop anyway.' Gepard: 'Why will the Moles find her?' Luka: 'The Moles find everyone. It's what they do.' —— The Moles find Lynx at the junction between the outer corridor network and the district's main path. She is standing at the junction looking at the corridor entrance with the quality of someone who has been told about a place and is now facing it directly and finding the confrontation more significant than the description. She has a Wildfire medical kit at her hip and the outer district cold-gear and the Landau quality in her face — warmer and rounder than Gepard's version, the same bones arranged with different emphasis, unmistakably the same family. Toma appears first, because Toma always does. She looks at Lynx with the quick assessment. "You're Gepard's sister," she says. Lynx looks at her. She looks at the three children who have materialized around her with the efficiency of people who know every meter of these corridors. "I'm Lynx," she says. "Toma," Toma says. "That's Pino. That's Oleg." A nod for each. "We're the Moles. You're looking for the workshop?" "Yes," Lynx says. "We'll take you," Toma says. "Seven minutes." "I know where the workshop is," Lynx says. "You know where it is on a map," Toma says, with the certainty of someone who understands the difference between map-knowledge and district-knowledge. "The workshop is past the generator housing junction, left at the secondary maintenance corridor entrance, right at the supply depot — but the depot moved last month. The map still shows the old location. You'd end up in Sector C equipment storage." Lynx looks at her for a moment. "Seven minutes," she says. "Seven minutes," Toma confirms. They walk Lynx to the workshop. She asks questions on the way — about the salvage routes, the outer corridor network, the notation mapping — and the Moles answer with the mixture of specific knowledge and twelve-year-old energy that is their characteristic mode. Pino shows her the sensor readings on his phone. Oleg offers something from the jacket, which Lynx accepts without asking what it is, immediately placing her in the category of acceptable people. "What was it?" Toma says. "It was good," Lynx says. "What was it?" "The jacket has several things," Oleg says. Lynx looks at the jacket with the evaluating precision of someone in medical logistics. "Could I study the internal—" "The jacket is not available," Oleg says, with the specific firmness of a final answer. "I just want to understand the—" "Not available," Oleg says. Lynx looks at Toma. "Is he always like this about the jacket?" "Especially with people he likes," Pino says. Lynx files this with the expression of someone who intends to return to this conversation at a better-prepared moment. Then they reach the workshop. —— Clara looks up when Lynx comes through the door. Inventory — Wildfire kit, cold-gear, Landau quality, sixteen, the expression of someone who came specifically to see a specific thing and is now seeing it. "You're Gepard's sister," Clara says. "Lynx," Lynx says. "Pela told me about you. She said you calibrate frequency arrays by feel, you mapped the pre-Separation notation network, and you might be connected to whoever built the first notation sections." She pauses. "She said you were the most interesting twelve-year-old she had encountered." Clara looks at her own hands for a moment. "That's a lot to be told about someone," she says. "Pela is thorough," Lynx says. "Nobody is as thorough as Pela," Clara says. "True," Lynx says. "Can I sit down?" "The stool," Clara says, gesturing at Oleg's stool, currently unoccupied because Oleg has positioned himself in the workshop's corner with the thermos from his jacket, which he pours from at intervals with the calm of someone who has a self-contained supply system. Lynx sits. The comfortable ease of someone used to field conditions. She looks at the workshop — the equipment cases, the fragment in the tray, Clara's calibration work on the secondary worktable, the back room where Svarog's presence is audible. "How did you end up here?" she says. "Svarog needed calibration help," Clara says. "I needed somewhere to be. The district was where the second heartbeat was." She pauses. "It felt right." "You can hear the Stellaron," Lynx says. "The second heartbeat version of its ambient output," Clara says. "Most people in the outer district feel it as background awareness. I hear it specifically." She pauses. "Louder since Luka has the artifact integrated. The workshop is warmer." Lynx looks at the workshop. At the warmth that is specific to this space — not just mechanical warmth but something else. The old gold and deep water quality. "I felt that when I came in," she says. "I thought it was the generators." "Partly the generators," Clara says. "Also the conducting network, the fragment, and—" She gestures at the back room. "Svarog. He has been in this district long enough that the district's frequency is part of him." "Can I meet him?" Lynx says. "You can try," Clara says. "He meets people when he decides they are worth his full attention. He decides by listening to how they talk to other people." She pauses. "He has been listening since you came in." Lynx looks at the back room. "Svarog," Clara says. "Yes," Svarog says. "Lynx Landau," Clara says. "Gepard's sister. Wildfire medical. Pela's friend. She felt the workshop's warmth when she came in." A pause. Then Svarog comes through the doorway — the four-armed automaton who is significantly larger than the doorway permits, the quality of something very old and very present. Lynx looks at him with genuine composure, the composure of someone who works Wildfire response and has learned that accurate assessment is more useful than performed reaction. "Lynx Landau," Svarog says. "Svarog," Lynx says. "You felt the warmth," he says. "It feels different from anywhere else I've been in the Underworld," she says. "More present. Specific." She pauses. "I didn't have a word for it until Clara said source material." Svarog looks at her for a long moment. "Clara," he says. "Show her the workshop's ambient frequency spectrum." Clara pulls up the display on the recorder. Lynx looks at it without full technical comprehension but reads the pattern rather than the labels. "The warmth I felt," she says. "It's in this." "Yes," Clara says. "How do you calibrate something you can't directly measure with instruments?" Lynx says. And then they are talking. Three hours. Lynx and Clara at the secondary worktable, the Moles moving in and out with the afternoon's findings, Svarog present without interfering. At one point Toma says: "Clara, show her the outer corridor seven photographs." Clara shows her from the beginning — the first coordinate notation find, the frequency profile, the 4.7 kilocycles, the older tool marks below the pre-Separation section, the hypothesis about what wrote them. Lynx asks questions that are sometimes technical, sometimes practical, and occasionally the simple ones that Clara never suppresses because Svarog taught her that simple questions are often the important ones. "If whoever built the notation sections knew about both Stellarons and designed the entire connecting system," Lynx says, "and placed what became Clara's source code in the second Stellaron—" She pauses. "They planned for everything. For Seele and Luka. For the retrieval. For Clara. For right now." "Yes," Clara says. "You're part of the plan," Lynx says. "Apparently," Clara says. Lynx is quiet for a moment. She looks at the fragment in the equipment tray — the warm glow of it. "Is that scary?" "A little," Clara says. "But mostly it means I was put here specifically. For this." She pauses, finding the right word rather than the first one. "I have never had that before. Having been put somewhere for a reason." Something in Lynx's expression does a thing — not the medical professional's warmth, something more direct. The specific directness of someone who has heard something that lands personally. "Good," she says. "Everyone should have a reason." Clara looks at her. Twelve years old. Three hours of talking. The fragment warm in the tray. Two days left. "You should come back," Clara says. "Bring Pela." "Pela doesn't come to the outer district," Lynx says. "Pela should," Clara says. "The frequency data from the outer corridor seven network alone is worth a full day of her attention. And the analysis room is not the only place to do analysis." Lynx looks at the workshop. At all of it. "I'll tell her," she says. "She'll come." "I know," Clara says. —— On day seventeen, at 2000 hours, Sampo is at the main junction of the outer machine district. Not the ward — for once. He has been at the ward through the afternoon, the usual visit, the pastries, the desk, Natasha. But at 2000 he left and came here and stood at the junction the way he stood here on day one when Seele and Luka came to him with Vera's name. He is standing here because the final missing piece has been arriving for three days — not from the Stellaron, from himself. The Stellaron's retrieval is for Seele and Luka. What has been arriving in Sampo is something different: the specific quality of a memory sealed not by Fuli but by the person it belongs to. Some things you seal yourself. You seal them because they are too large to carry while running, and he has been running for twenty-two years, and the running has been very successful and the sealing has been very effective. Until Vera's name. Until the dark filling pastries and Natasha's hand over his and I'll be here. The seal on the memory of his last day in the outer machine district — the day Vera sent him away, the day before the dark winter — has been loosening since the first flash. Not breaking. Loosening. The way ice loosens before it gives. He is standing at the junction because the ice is giving. He knows the quality of a memory about to arrive. He has been developing this knowledge for weeks. He knows the way the district goes very slightly brighter and the second heartbeat goes very slightly louder and the particular cold of the outer machine district takes on the texture of a different cold — the cold of twenty-two years ago, the cold of a twelve-year-old's last night. The memory arrives. —— He is twelve years old. The outer machine district. Night — the specific night before everything, the night after Vera said: you should go to your quarters now, Sam. Stay. And he said okay. And did not go. Because he was twelve years old and he knew Vera well enough to know that okay-but-not-going was sometimes the more loving response than going. He is in outer corridor, junction nine, watching through the gap in the stone. He found the gap three months earlier — a narrow architectural gap where the secondary maintenance corridor's construction left a sightline into the formation chamber, the kind of gap that only exists because the pre-Separation builders were not thinking about twelve-year-olds who would want to watch something they had been told not to watch. He and Pino from his class found it on a dare. You have to be twelve and narrow to get the right angle. Through the gap, he can see Vera. She is at the formation. Her hands on it. The formation is at its maximum — the light in it at a level he has never seen, old gold and deep water at full saturation, the pressure of it visible in the quality of the air around her. She is not afraid. That is the thing he could not have predicted — the specific thing he is twelve years old and watching and does not have a framework for. She is not afraid. She is the most Vera he has ever seen her. The concentrated version. The full expression of the person she is. Her hands on the formation and her face at the specific peace of someone who has made a decision and is inside the execution of it and is not afraid. He counts. He counts because he cannot not count. Because he is twelve years old and Vera is his best friend and she is in the formation chamber and he is outside it and counting is the only action available to him. At three minutes and forty-two seconds, he tries to go through the gap. He cannot. The gap is tight for someone twelve and narrow and it is — it is somehow tighter than it was when he found it. Not physically tighter. The gap has not changed. But something in the air around the formation chamber has changed. A pressure that is not temperature and is not sound but which resists his movement the way water resists you when you move against its current. He cannot go through. He puts his hands on the stone on either side of the gap and he watches and he counts. Four minutes and eleven seconds. At four minutes and eleven seconds, the light in the formation chamber — which has been at full saturation, which has been at the level that is not comfortable to look at directly through the gap — changes. Not goes out. Changes. The old gold and deep water shifts from the directed concentrated quality of a source broadcasting outward to the settled ambient quality of a source that has been given something and is now containing it. The formation is not empty — it is full in a different way. Full of what Vera gave it rather than full of its own activation pressure. And Vera's hands are no longer on the formation. Vera is on the floor of the chamber. He screams her name through the gap. She does not move. He tries the gap again. The pressure that resisted him before is gone — whatever maintained it was the activation, and the activation is done, and the gap is just a gap again. He goes through it. He is twelve and narrow and he goes through and he crosses the chamber floor in three steps and he kneels beside Vera and she is— She is breathing. She is alive. But the quality of the alive is wrong in a way he cannot name at twelve. She is there but not fully there — the way a lamp is on but the flame is lower than it should be. She is alive and she is breathing and she is looking at him. Looking at him with the warm version of her expression. The specific warmth of a person who is not surprised by what they are seeing. Who expected this specific face to come through the gap. "Sam," she says. "Vera," he says. His voice is twelve years old and has been counting to four minutes and eleven seconds and has screamed her name through a gap in the stone and is doing the thing that twelve-year-old voices do when they are past their capacity. "Vera, what did you—" "Sam," she says again. "Listen to me." "I'm listening," he says. "I'm—" "You're going to need to run," she says. "Not away. You hear me? Not away. You are going to run and it is going to feel like running away but you are running toward. Do you understand?" "No," he says. Because he is twelve and he does not understand. "You will," she says. "Eventually." She looks at him. The warm version — fully the warm version, the complete expression of it, as if she is giving him everything she has of the warm version right now because it is what she can still give. "You are going to come back to this district," she says. "Many times. You are going to come back because you are the kind of person who comes back. And when you come back—" She pauses. "There will be things here that are worth coming back for. Things that grow while you are away." Another pause. "You are going to be so happy, Sam. Not immediately. But eventually. Extraordinarily happy." He is twelve and his best friend is on the chamber floor and the word happy does not feel like the right word for what is happening right now. But he is listening because she told him to. "Vera," he says. "Take care of yourself," she says. "Until you come back." She looks at the formation — the settled full light. "It's going to be alright," she says. "The city is alright. The people are alright." She looks at him. "You're alright." He does not feel alright. But she says it the way she says things that are true. And he has known her long enough to trust the way she says things that are true. "I need to go," she says. "In a moment. Not away. Just—" She looks at the formation. "I need to go in. You need to go out." She looks at him. "Run, Sam." He runs. —— That is the memory. Sampo is standing at the main junction of the outer machine district at 2000 hours on day seventeen and the memory is in him — complete, the seal broken, every second of four minutes and eleven seconds present in the specific quality of a thing that has been contained and is now received. He stands with it. He stands with the twelve-year-old counting and the gap in the stone and Vera on the chamber floor and run Sam and the promise that it would be alright. He looks at the ward direction. He goes. —— Natasha puts the pen down before he has finished opening the door.
