He comes through and she reads him immediately — the specific quality of someone who has received something enormous and has come to the right place because here is the right place. She comes around the desk without asking anything. She stands in front of him and she is simply present, which is what he needs more than anything she could say. "I remember," he says. "Yes," she says. "I was there," he says. "Through the gap in the stone. I counted. Four minutes and eleven seconds. Every one." He looks at his hands. "And she told me to run. And I ran. And she said I would come back." He looks at Natasha. "She knew I would come back." "Yes," Natasha says. "She knew." "She said I would be happy," he says. "Eventually. Extraordinarily happy." He looks at Natasha with the expression that is the most unmanaged thing she has ever seen from him — the performance completely absent, everything present. "I was twelve. I did not understand that yet." He pauses. "I think I understand it now." Natasha looks at him. She steps forward and she puts her arms around him. Not the ward's gesture. Not the medical professional's gesture. The human one — the specific act of putting your arms around a person because they are a person and they are in front of you and this is what the moment requires. He stands completely still. After a moment his arms come up. He holds on. They stand in the ward at 2000 hours and the second heartbeat is below, settled, and the ward is warm. After a while Sampo says, quietly: "She said to come back." "You came back," Natasha says. "She said there would be things worth coming back for," he says. Natasha does not say anything. She holds on. "She was right," he says. —— Three hours later, at 2300, Sampo is at the front desk with Natasha across from him. Tea from the ward's electric kettle. The lamp on. The district at its overnight quiet. They have been talking for three hours. Not about the Stellaron or the retrieval or any of the large things — about Vera. The specific Vera who sat on the workshop floor and laughed. The twelve-year-old Sam who found the gap in the stone and could not go through it during the four minutes and eleven seconds. The promise that it would be alright. What alright looks like. At 2314, the ward door opens. Sampo looks up. A woman comes through the door. She is not a patient. She has the quality of someone who came to this specific ward at this specific hour for a reason that is not medical, and she knows it, and she is walking with the comfortable ease of someone who knows where they are going and is not treating the hour as an obstacle. She is — striking is not quite the right word. The word for her is specific. Specific in the way of someone who is very precisely themselves in every detail of how they look and move, from the white-and-black of her hair and clothing to the way she is already smiling with the full warmth of someone who arrived for something entertaining. She looks at Sampo. The smile gets wider. "Oh," she says. "This is better than I expected." Sampo looks at her. He is twenty-two years of running and reading rooms and the specific intelligence of someone who has spent a significant portion of their life in the spaces between official and unofficial. He is looking at this woman and she is not fully legible, which is — unusual. "I don't know you," he says. "You don't," she agrees cheerfully. She sits in the patient's chair across from him with the ease of someone who has decided it is her chair. "But you're going to. I've been watching you for a while, Sampo Koski. From a comfortable distance. I find you very interesting." "Why," he says. "Because," she says, "you have been performing one version of yourself for twenty-two years on top of the version that is actually yours. And I appreciate a good performance. I really do. But what is happening to you right now—" She looks at him with the specific quality of someone who finds things genuinely delightful. "Watching someone arrive at themselves. I don't get to see that very often from this close." Sampo looks at her. "Who are you." "Sparkle," she says. She says it the way some people say their names — with the awareness that the name is already information, that the name does something when it lands. Natasha looks at her carefully. "Sparkle," she says. "Is that a name or a description?" "Both," Sparkle says. She looks at Natasha with the warm evaluating look — the look of someone reading a room they find genuinely interesting. "You're the doctor. She puts the pen down when he comes through the door." She looks at Sampo. "Before he finishes his first sentence. I heard about that." Her expression does the thing — the genuine warmth under the performance, the actual delight rather than the performed version. "She puts the pen down. That's lovely." Sampo looks at Natasha. Natasha looks at Sampo. "Who told you about the pen," Sampo says. "No one specific," Sparkle says. "I have a tendency to know things." She pauses. "Specifically about people who are connected to a particular path. People who have a specific relationship with joy." She looks at Sampo with the full warm attention. "People who have been running toward something for twenty-two years and have arrived." Sampo is very quiet for a moment. "The path of Elation," he says. Slowly. The way you say something when you are hearing yourself say it and the hearing is new information. Sparkle looks at him with the specific expression of someone who arrived at exactly the moment they intended to. "You knew it before I said it," she says. "You have always known it. You just didn't have the framework." "The night Vera told me to run," he says. "Tell me," she says, and she means it — the specific genuine interest of someone who wants the story because the story matters, not because it is information to collect. He looks at her. He looks at Natasha. He looks at the ward and the lamp and the tea from the electric kettle. He tells her. The full version — the gap in the stone and the counting and Vera on the floor and run Sam and the promise. The running for twenty-two years. The district and the pastries and the pen going down before he finishes his sentence. Sparkle listens with the full attention of someone who is always performing and who is, right now, not. When he finishes she is quiet for a moment. "That is the beginning," she says. "The running. That is when it started. Not the path of Elation as a doctrine or an institution or an affiliation." She pauses. "The thing the path is named for — the specific experience of continuing to move toward joy even when joy is not the condition you are currently in. Even when the condition you are currently in is watching your best friend give herself to a Stellaron through a gap in the stone and knowing you cannot stop it and running anyway, not away — toward." She pauses. "That experience. That is when you started walking this path." Sampo looks at the ward. "She said I would be extraordinarily happy," he says. "You will be," Sparkle says. "You already are, in the specific way of someone who has found their way back." She looks at him with the warmth — the real kind that does not require performance because it does not need to be managed. "And there is significantly more where this came from." She pauses. "The world is large, Sampo Koski. Belobog is one city on one planet. The path of Elation is not confined to a city." She holds his gaze. "When this is over — when whatever Belobog is facing has been faced and the city is standing and the people you care about are alright — I think you and I are going to have a conversation about what comes next." He looks at her. "You're asking me to leave Belobog," he says. "Not yet," she says. "Not now. Not while the Stellarons are what they are and the people here need you." She pauses. "But after. When the city is standing. When Vera's promise has been kept and the extraordinary happiness is fully present." She looks at him steadily. "I am asking you to consider that the path you have been walking for twenty-two years continues beyond this city. And that I would like to walk some of it with you." Sampo looks at her. He looks at Natasha. Natasha looks at him with the warm reading quality — the full version, the version that has been present every time he came through the door. She is reading him now and what she reads is that this is real, that the woman across from him is offering something real, and she is not afraid of the offering. She is the person who has been here for three years with the pen going down before he finishes his sentence and who has held him tonight when the ice gave and who is looking at him now with the expression that means: you are allowed to want things. "Natasha," he says. "Yes," she says. "She is asking me to leave," he says. "Eventually." "I heard," Natasha says. "Belobog," he says. "I know where she is asking you to leave," Natasha says, with the dry warmth that is specifically hers. "What do you—" He stops. He starts again. "What do you think about that." Natasha looks at him. She looks at the ward. She looks at the lamp and the desk and the pen and the three years and the tonight and all of it. "I think," she says, "that Vera told you to run toward. Not away." She pauses. "And that after everything is done and the city is standing — if the path is toward, then it is the right direction." She pauses again. "And I think—" She stops. She looks at him with the full reading quality and the full warmth under it. "I think I would not ask you to stay still for my sake. That is not what this is." Sampo looks at her. "Natasha," he says. "Yes," she says. "I'm not going anywhere right now," he says. "I know," she says. "The Stellarons," he says. "I know," she says. "The people here," he says. "Sampo," she says, with the specific quiet of someone who does not need the list because she already understands everything on it. "I know." He looks at her. At the lamp and the pen and the desk and three years of dark filling pastries and the arms around him tonight and I'll be here. "When it's done," he says. "When both of them are resolved and the city is standing—" He pauses. "I want to talk to you. About what comes next. For me. For—" He stops. "For us. Whatever that means." Natasha looks at him. The warm reading quality. The full version. "Yes," she says. "We'll talk." Sparkle is looking at both of them with the specific expression of someone who finds this genuinely delightful and is doing a respectable job of not announcing the delight. She stands. "I came for exactly this," she says. "And exactly this has happened." She looks at Sampo. "Some paths need a witness. Someone to confirm that what you are walking is real." She holds his gaze. "I will come back, Sampo Koski. When the time is right. And we will have the conversation about what comes next." She pauses. "After." She goes. The ward door closes. Sampo sits at the front desk with Natasha across from him and the lamp on and the ward's overnight quiet around both of them. After a long moment Natasha says: "She knew about the pen." "Yes," Sampo says. "Nobody told her," Natasha says. "No," Sampo says. A pause. "She said the world is large," Natasha says. "Yes," Sampo says. "She was right," Natasha says. Sampo looks at the tea in his hands. He looks at Natasha across the desk. He looks at the ward — the lamp, the charts, the pen, the door. At the specific quality of the place he has been coming to for three years because Vera told him to run toward and this ward was toward and Natasha was toward and the district was toward. "She said I would be extraordinarily happy," he says. Natasha looks at him. "She was right about that too," she says. He looks at her. He almost smiles — the real one, the quick vulnerable one that the performance never manages. "Yes," he says. "She was." —— On day eighteen, at 0900, Seele cuts Luka's hair. This is not planned. It begins because they are in the workshop — he at the secondary worktable reviewing the outer corridor nine frequency data, she at the equipment tray calibrating the recorder for the morning formation entry — and she is standing behind him while he reads and she is looking at the back of his head with the specific evaluating look she brings to things she is assessing for accuracy. "Luka," she says. He does not look up from the display. "Yes," he says. "Your hair," she says. He looks up now. He turns enough to see her peripheral. "What about it." "It's—" She considers this with the careful Seele vocabulary of someone finding the precise word. "Inexact," she says. He turns fully. "Inexact," he says. "The length at the sides is not consistent," she says. "It is longer on the left than the right. And the top—" She looks at the top. "The top has no specific direction. It is simply present." He has been cutting his own hair since he was thirteen in the outer machine district. In a mirror. Alone. The sides being precisely consistent is something that requires two functioning hands and full range of shoulder motion, both of which have had complications in the recent months. She is not wrong about the inconsistency. "Are you offering to fix this," he says. "I am noting that it could be fixed," she says. "That is not an offer," he says. "No," she agrees. He looks at her. She looks at him. "Seele," he says. "Yes," she says. "Do you want to cut my hair," he says. She considers this with the same evaluating precision she brings to everything. "Yes," she says. "I think I do." He looks at the workshop. He looks at her. "There are shears in the back room," he says. "Svarog uses them for geomarrow component maintenance." "I know," she says. "I've seen where they are." She gets the shears. He sits on the stool. She stands behind him. The workshop is warm and specific around them — the fragment in the tray, Svarog in the back room making the specific sounds of precise work, the district doing its morning outside. Clara and the Moles are in the outer corridor network for the morning mapping run. It is just the two of them and Svarog's presence in the back room and the warmth. She looks at his hair. She looks at it with the full assessment — not the casual look of someone who is going to trim without intention, the specific look of someone who is deciding what it should be rather than correcting what it is. She runs her hand through it. He goes still under the contact. "Short," she says. "Close on the sides. The top — textured, layered, but with intention. Not just present." She pauses. "It will fit your face better." "You have a specific vision," he says. "I always have a specific vision," she says. "That is not new." "Fair," he says. She begins. She works with the focused attention she brings to things she has decided to do properly — not the speed of someone in a hurry, the pace of someone doing something specific and unwilling to do it faster than the specific requires. The shears are precise and she is precise and the workshop is quiet except for the work and Svarog's back room sounds and the second heartbeat below. Luka sits. He does not have a mirror. He is simply here, with her hands in his hair, in the workshop at 0900 on day eighteen of the nineteen. The words he has for this specific quality are not the combat vocabulary or the sensor vocabulary or any of the catalogued frameworks. The word is good. This is specifically and completely good. "Tell me something," she says. The version that means: give me something that is yours. He thinks. "The first time I entered the corridor," he says. "The first solo walkthrough, before Sampo found me. I went to junction nine and there was a wall that was not on any map. It had not been there the previous month. I stood in front of it for twenty minutes." "What was on the other side?" she says. "I don't know," he says. "I didn't go through. The brace calibration I had at the time was not adequate for the formation density I was reading on the sensor." He pauses. "I went back four months later with the recalibrated setup and the wall was gone." She pauses in the cutting. "Gone," she says. "The wall-form had fully integrated into the corridor architecture," he says. "Looked like natural stone. Only visible as a formation if you knew the frequency signature of that specific class." He pauses. "I wrote it in the log as a corridor morphology event. I didn't tell anyone because at the time I didn't know who would care." She resumes the cutting. "I would have cared," she says. "I know that now," he says. She works for a moment in the quiet. "Your turn," he says. She does not pause. "Day one," she says. "First tunnel entry. When you came down the outer corridor main path toward the junction and I was already at the corridor entrance. You saw me before I saw you — I know because when I turned you already had the sensor in your hand. You had been reading me on the sensor before I turned." "Yes," he says. "Why," she says. "Because you were already at the corridor entrance at 0530," he says. "And your frequency signature on the sensor was—" He pauses. "Not what I expected. The reading for a standard Stellaron-proximate resident is a specific signature. Yours was that signature and something additional. A quality I did not have a framework for." He pauses. "I wanted to understand what I was reading before I spoke to you." "And?" she says. "I did not understand it," he says. "For several months." She is quiet. The shears working. "Do you understand it now?" she says. "Better than I did," he says. "Not completely." He pauses. "I think the complete understanding is still becoming available." She makes a sound — not the operational voice, the quiet version of the real one, the one that means she is pleased with an answer. She finishes the sides. She moves to the top. She works the layering with intention — the direction she described, the texture that is not just present. The workshop is warm. Svarog in the back room. The fragment glowing. When she is done she steps back. She looks at the result. "Better," she says. He turns to look at her. "You haven't told me what it looks like," he says. "Short on the sides," she says. "Textured on top. There is a direction to it now." She pauses. "It fits your face." He looks at her with the expression — the stripped face, the one that has no management. "Should I find a mirror," he says. "If you want," she says. He does not move toward a mirror. He looks at her instead. "Tell me honestly," he says. She looks at him. At the result. At the work she did. At the specific person in front of her with the morning light in the workshop and the hair that has a direction now instead of being simply present. "It looks like you," she says. "The version of you that was always going to exist when you became more of what you are." He looks at her. She crosses to him and she stands close and she looks at him and her expression is the whole-face version with no architecture and she says: "I love you." Not performed. Not exchanged as a reflex. Said toward him — the specific weight of two months of corridors and the outer corridor nine and the moon and the spinal integration and the hair she just cut and the workshop at 0900 on day eighteen of the nineteen days, all of it in those three words and the three words being the correct words for all of it. He looks at her. He looks at her with everything. He cups her face in his left hand — the steady one, the one that has carried her and held her and been there since junction twelve at nine years old — and he says: "I love you." Not the same words said back. The same words said forward, with the full weight of twenty-one years and the second heartbeat and the will that does not stop and the moon promised and all of it. She steps forward. She kisses him. Not the brief version — not the ward-chair version or the lamp-lit room's first version. The full version. The one with the weight of the whole story in it. His hand on her face and her hands on him and the workshop around them and the fragment glowing and Svarog in the back room making the quiet sounds of precise work and neither of them going anywhere. After a long time she pulls back. She looks at him. "The formation entry is at 1000," she says. "I know," he says. "We have an hour," she says. He looks at her. At the workshop. At the hour between now and the formation entry. "Yes," he says. "We do." The hour is theirs. —— At 0730, before any of this, Gepard goes to the Landau house. Not the palace — the Landau house, in the specific part of Belobog's inhabited district where the older families have been since before the Separation. It is not large. It has the quality of a place that has been lived in by the same family for generations — different from old, different from historic, simply: theirs. He goes because Lynx sent him a message at 0700 that said: 'Come for breakfast. I have things to tell you about the workshop.' And he goes because it is day eighteen and in thirty-six hours the second Stellaron will manifest and the patrol folder will be the only thing in the room, but today is not thirty-six hours from now. Bronya is with him. He asked her — 'come' was the specific word, no framework around it — and she received it correctly and came. She has not been to the Landau house before. She walks through its rooms with the quality of someone receiving new information about a person they know well, the specific experience of seeing someone in their specific space. Lynx is at the kitchen. She looks up when they come through the door and she reads Bronya — the quick intelligent read — and her expression does the thing that Gepard's face does when something registers as more significant than expected. "You brought the Supreme Guardian," Lynx says. "Bronya," Gepard says. "I know who she is," Lynx says, to Bronya directly. "He has never brought anyone for breakfast before." "Lynx," Gepard says. "I'm just noting it," Lynx says. She looks at Bronya. "Do you want tea?" "Yes," Bronya says. "Please." Lynx makes tea. They sit at the Landau kitchen table — the specific surface of a family home, different from the palace table and the council chamber table and every other table the Supreme Guardian sits at. Bronya sits at it with the quality of someone who is simply sitting, without the professional architecture, the person underneath it present. "The workshop," Gepard says to Lynx. "Clara," Lynx says. "I need to tell you about Clara." She looks at Bronya. "You should know this too. What she told me about the notation sections and the source code." She pauses. "She thinks the entity that built the notation sections placed the source code in the second Stellaron specifically for Clara. That Clara is a descendant of whatever that entity was." "Svarog mentioned the possibility," Bronya says. "He confirmed it to Clara on day fifteen," Lynx says. "Or as close to confirmed as Svarog gets when he is being careful." She looks at Gepard. "She is twelve years old and she is handling the information that she was placed here for a specific purpose with the specific equanimity of someone who has always known she was in the right place and now has the framework for why." She pauses. "She's extraordinary." Gepard looks at his tea. "Pela told me she was the most interesting twelve-year-old she had encountered," he says. "Pela was being restrained," Lynx says. Bronya looks at the tea in her hands. She looks at the kitchen table. She looks at the specific morning light in the Landau house, which is different from the palace morning light because it comes through different windows and falls on a different surface and is simply morning light in a house where people have lived for generations. "What Pela told you," Bronya says to Lynx, "about Clara and the notation sections. How did Pela describe what it means? What did she think the endpoint was?" Lynx looks at her. "She said she didn't know the endpoint," she says. "But she said the endpoint is what both Stellarons are building toward. Together." She pauses. "She said the first Stellaron's plot is the larger one. The second Stellaron's resolution is the preparation for something that hasn't happened yet." Bronya is quiet. "What the first Stellaron has been holding," she says. Not to anyone specifically. To the kitchen table. "Yes," Lynx says. "What has it been holding?" Bronya looks at the morning light. She looks at Gepard. He looks at her with the expression — the face that has been building since Everwinter Hill and is fully present now in the Landau kitchen with the tea in his hands. "I am close to an answer," she says. "Not yet. But close." "When you have it," he says. "You'll know before anyone," she says. Lynx looks at the two of them across the kitchen table. She looks at them with the specific intelligence of a sixteen-year-old who understands what she is looking at and does not say anything about it. She picks up her tea. "After breakfast," she says, "I want to show you something. The outer wall section above Sector A has a specific view of the northern perimeter. The crystallization is visible from there on a clear morning." "I know," Gepard says. "Bronya hasn't seen it," Lynx says. He looks at Bronya. She looks at him. "After breakfast," she says. They eat breakfast. —— The outer wall section above Sector A at 0900 has the quality of Gepard's familiar ground — the high walkway, the drop to the cleared snowfield on one side, the city amber on the other, the northern sector visible in the specific way it is visible to him every morning. Bronya stands on the walkway and looks at the northern sector. The crystallization pattern in the Restricted Zone's northern boundary — the amber-black of the Fragmentum response, the inward-and-upward orientation that Gepard has been reading for three weeks. She looks at it with the full-attention posture. Everything inward. All of it directed at the thing she has been building toward. "What do you hear from here?" Gepard says. She looks at the Restricted Zone. At the first Stellaron's direction — the palace foundation, the sub-surface source, the signal at 2.5 and holding since day fifteen. "Permission," she says. And the second time the word has more specific content than the first — she has been building the framework for three days. "Permission for what it has been waiting to begin." "What has it been waiting to begin?" he says. Not the same question — the same words, different weight. He is asking because she is closer to the answer. She looks at the Restricted Zone. "The first Stellaron has been here since before the Separation," she says. "In the foundation of this city for hundreds of years. It has been listening. Watching." She pauses. "Everything that has happened in Belobog — the Separation, the Cocolia years, the Trailblazers, the array — the first Stellaron has been present for all of it. Below the palace. Below the wall." She pauses. "And the second Stellaron's retrieval — the return of what was sealed, the completion of Vera's function — that is the signal the first Stellaron has been waiting for. The confirmation that the thing it was waiting for is done." She pauses. "And now—" She looks at the Restricted Zone. "It is going to wake up completely," she says. "Not the way Stellarons usually wake up. Not the way the second Stellaron will manifest — a battle, a Fragmentum event, something to face and defeat. The first Stellaron's waking is—" She finds the word. "Different. Categorically different." She looks at the crystallization. "I think the first Stellaron is not an adversary. I think it has never been an adversary. I think it has been—" She stops. "Waiting for us to be ready." Gepard is very still beside her. "Ready for what," he says. She looks at the Restricted Zone. She looks at the first Stellaron's direction. She looks at Belobog behind the wall — the amber warmth, the ten thousand people going about their morning. "I don't know yet," she says. "But I am close." He looks at her. She looks at the Restricted Zone for a moment longer. Then she turns to look at him — close range on the walkway, the morning cold around them, the city behind him. "Gepard," she says. "Yes," he says. "Everwinter Hill," she says. He looks at her. "I promised," she says. "Before the continuation. I said I would tell you the truth about what happened there. Not in the briefing. Not in the report. You have not asked. I know you have been waiting for me to say it when it was mine to say." "Yes," he says. She looks at the city. She looks at the wall. She looks at him. "I was not going to make it," she says. "That is the truth. Without the Stellaron — without the specific activation that happened when Cocolia's plan reached its peak and the Stellaron responded — I was not going to make it off Everwinter Hill." She pauses. "I did not understand what happened at the time. I understood that something changed. That something reached down — I cannot describe it as anything other than reaching down — and pulled me back from the edge of something I had no business being on the edge of." She pauses. "I have spent the months since trying to understand what that was. And I think—" She pauses a final time. "I think it was the first Stellaron. Below the palace. Below Belobog. It has been listening to everything in this city for hundreds of years." She looks at him. "And on Everwinter Hill it decided that I was not done yet." Gepard looks at her. He receives the full weight of what she has told him — the truth about Everwinter Hill, held for months, given to him on the outer wall on day eighteen of the nineteen with the crystallization visible in the Restricted Zone and the second heartbeat still below. "Bronya," he says. "Yes," she says. "I'm glad it decided that," he says. She looks at him. The full-attention expression, all of it. "So am I," she says. He reaches out and takes her hand. Not the formal version or the managed version. The simple direct version — his hand around hers on the outer wall walkway with the morning cold and the city behind them and both of them present, specifically, irreducibly, in this moment. She holds it. Lynx, who has been standing six meters to their left looking at the northern sector crystallization with the appropriate distance and the appropriate not-looking-at-them, says: "The crystallization density at the secondary monitoring post boundary is higher than the primary monitor's reading. You should brief Dunn today." "I will," Gepard says. "Good," Lynx says. She continues looking at the crystallization. She does not look at the two of them holding hands on the outer wall walkway. She is sixteen years old and she is excellent at not-looking when not-looking is the correct thing to do. —— At 1000, Seele and Luka go to the formation. The corridor at 1000 on day eighteen has the familiar quality and the quality under the familiar — the specific sense of a last time, which is not sad but is present, the way you feel the weight of a thing you are going to miss in the last moment before you miss it. The formation at 1000 is at its largest yet. Three meters. The light at full primary saturation — the active broadcasting quality of a source that has reached the end of what it has been building toward. Both heartbeats running in the recorder display. Fully aligned. No oscillation. The chord of two signatures running as one. She reaches out. She puts her hand on the formation. She receives the resting warmth of it. The same warmth as the conducting network. The same warmth as the depression in sub-level three. The same warmth as Vera's endurance in Luka's spine. The formation is simply present. Warm. Patient. It is not yet time for the last piece. She takes her hand away. She looks at Luka. "Not today," she says. He reads her expression. "Tomorrow," he says. "Tomorrow," she says. They stand in the formation chamber on day eighteen with the chord of both heartbeats and the light at full saturation and the sealed letter of the final piece waiting for tomorrow. "The chamber is quiet," she says. "For the first time since we started entering." He listens. She is right. The ambient tension of building activation — the sub-frequency below the second heartbeat that has been present in every entry — is gone. What remains is the formation itself. Warm and full. Ready. He reaches out. He takes her hand — his left hand, the steady one, the one that has been there since junction twelve when they were nine years old. She closes her fingers around it. "Tomorrow," she says. "Tomorrow," he agrees. They leave the chamber. At the same speed. —— That night, at midnight, Svarog calls Luka into the back room. Luka comes in. He sits on the surface. He looks at Svarog with the fully-present face — the stripped face, no management, the face he has been wearing since sub-level three when the management ran out. Svarog looks at him. "Tomorrow," Svarog says. "Yes," Luka says. "The second Stellaron's manifestation," Svarog says. "I do not know what it will look like. No one does. But I know the scale of it." He pauses. "I have been in this district for longer than the records go back. I have seen Fragmentum events. I have faced formations. I was present at the Separation." He pauses again. "I have never been present at a Stellaron's full manifestation." "No one alive has," Luka says. "Correct," Svarog says. "Which means tomorrow is the largest event this district has seen. Possibly the largest this city has seen since the Separation." He looks at Luka with the optical sensors. "I want you to know that I will be in the district. With Clara. With the Wildfire units and the Silvermane Guard." He pauses. "Whatever comes out of the second Stellaron's manifestation that is not the Stellaron itself — the Fragmentum response, the formations, the ambient activation — I will address it." "I know," Luka says. "What comes for you and Seele specifically," Svarog says, "I cannot address. That is yours." "I know," Luka says. Svarog looks at him. The four hands at rest. The optical sensor expression — the one that carries many things simultaneously, and which right now carries the specific thing that exists between a person who has been in a workshop for eight months building toward something and the other person who has been building alongside them. "You are ready," Svarog says. Luka looks at his hands. Both of them — left and right, the biological pair, the right one still the recovering shoulder and the arm that has been through sub-level three and the brace failure and everything. He looks at his hands and thinks about what Svarog has built in the back room over the past days. He knows the shape of what is coming after the last piece. He knows what the back room has been working toward. He does not say it tonight — tonight is for this. "Yes," he says. "Both of you," Svarog says. "Both of us," Luka says. Svarog looks at him. "The road continues," he says. "Yes," Luka says. He looks at the workshop through the doorway — the warm familiar space, the fragment in the tray, the secondary worktable. He looks at it all. "Svarog." "Yes," Svarog says. Luka looks at him. At the four-armed automaton who has been in this district since before the records go back, who built a calibration for a sub-level he had never been to, who maintained an artifact for twenty-two years, who is, in the specific functional sense of the word, the closest thing to a parent Luka has. "Thank you," Luka says. Svarog looks at him. "You are welcome," Svarog says. And it carries everything — the eight months and the calibrations and the midnight procedure and the artifact and the spine and tomorrow and the road that continues. Luka goes to the ward. He sits in the chair beside the examination room door. The chair that has become his specific chair. At 0100, the door opens. Seele comes through it — she has been sleeping on the examination table since 2200, the pragmatic Seele decision to sleep where sleep is available rather than walking home and walking back. She looks at him in the chair. She crosses to him. She sits in the chair beside his. They sit in the ward in the overnight quiet with the second heartbeat below — still, the specific stillness of completion, the first time in the outer machine district's memory that the second heartbeat has been still. Not gone. Still. The specific stillness of something that has reached what it was building toward and is at rest in the having-reached-it. "Tomorrow," she says. "Tomorrow," he says. She reaches out. She puts her hand over his right hand — his hand, his biological hand, the one that has been holding things since before either of them can remember. "Whatever it manifests as," she says. "We are ready." "Yes," he says. "The second Stellaron is Belobog's greatest peril to date," she says. The honest voice, the Seele-without-architecture voice. "I know that." "Yes," he says. "And we are ready anyway," she says. He looks at her. At the butterfly quality present in the warmth around her — more visible now than at the beginning of the nineteen days, more present, closer to the surface. At the change the source made in her that has been growing since. "You," he says. "The source's change. Whatever it is becoming in you." He looks at her. "You are not afraid of it." "No," she says. "I am becoming what I am supposed to become. I feel it." She looks at her free hand. "The returning quality. The refusing-to-end quality. The thing that comes back." She pauses. "I am not afraid of it. I am ready for it." She looks at him. "Whatever the second Stellaron manifests as and faces us with — I am going to face it as what I am becoming." He holds her gaze. "Yes," he says. "You are." The ward is quiet. The district is quiet. The second heartbeat is still. Tomorrow. The last piece. The second Stellaron. Belobog's greatest peril to date. And after that — whatever the first Stellaron has been waiting to begin. But that is after. Right now it is tonight, and they are in the ward, and both of them are here, and her hand is over his, and neither of them is going anywhere. The second heartbeat counts down to morning. Still. Patient. Complete. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 3: "Eleven Minutes" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text The alarm does not stop for eleven minutes. Gepard knows, because he is counting. One hand braced against the concrete wall beside the outpost door, the other still warm —still burning from where her fingers had been laced into his hair— and he is counting every second of that alarm the way a drowning man counts the surface above him, because it might be the only fixed point he has left. Six hundred and sixty seconds of that blaring, zone-wide shriek, methodically dismantling the small world they had built inside this room, until nothing remains of it but the shallow indentations her boots left in the thin crescent of snow that had blown through the door when she arrived. Then silence. Then her voice. "Sector A is reporting a Fragmentum convergence at the North ridge." She is already reading from the tactical display at her wrist, standing at the far end of the room with her back to him, every cable re-secured and tucked neatly behind her pauldrons. Commander Bronya. He watches the shift happen in real time; the line of her shoulders setting themselves with intention, her spine making a decision, every soft and unguarded thing about the last thirty seconds rearranging itself into something armored and elsewhere. "Estimated density: high. They're requesting reinforcements." A pause. He hears her exhale — slow, deliberate, the particular sound of pressing something back to a manageable depth. "It's near the ridge where we were earlier." She looks at him over her shoulder, and her eyes are sharp. Present. Already somewhere he is not. "I need to return to the palace before the patrol units notice my absence." "I know." He says. Because it is the only thing he knows for certain right now, and he has always preferred certainty to the alternative. "I'll take the East corridor back." She pulls her hood up. Not the cloak — that she folds over the chair with a mechanical neatness that makes something in his chest pull sideways — but the isothermal hood, the one that covers everything but her eyes. She picks up her face mask from the desk and holds it for a moment, half-raised, not putting it on yet. "Send word to Sector A that reinforcements are incoming." Brisk. Professional. The Commander's voice, the one that had spent the last twenty minutes being notably absent. "Standard suppression formation. I want hourly status updates until the bloom is confirmed cold." "Understood." "And Gepard." He waits. Something moves through her expression — there and gone, so quickly he cannot name it. She lowers the mask. "Stay inside the perimeter." He nearly says something. He can feel the something sitting at the back of his throat, and he makes the correct and responsible decision to leave it there. "Of course." She puts the mask on. She goes. The door closes behind her with its usual soft thud, and the outpost goes out like a lamp. —— He runs. It is completely unprofessional and he does not slow down once. Three minutes to Sector A at a full sprint through the blizzard, and he does not mind the cold tonight — the cold is useful, actually, shockingly so, and he drags lungfuls of frozen air into his chest and lets it do its work, scouring his thoughts into something flatter and more manageable with every stride. He focuses on the ground under his boots. The sound of packed snow. The orange hum of his pauldrons, their charge still holding strong. The sound of Sector A growing louder around the bend of the North ridge. He hears it before he sees it. A large Fragmentum convergence has a sound unlike anything else in the Restricted Zone — a sound no amount of years on the front line ever fully normalizes. A low-frequency thrum, deep enough to settle in the sternum rather than the ears, layered over a wet cracking noise like ice fracturing from below, and underneath both of those, if you are close enough, and he always ends up close enough: something that is not quite breathing, but that your body insists on treating as such anyway. He clears the ridge. The bloom is roughly forty meters wide. A mass of amber and black erupting from the snowfield ahead, crystalline spires jutting upward at angles that suggest no awareness of how angles are supposed to work, the ground around their base heaving very slowly and very steadily, like a set of lungs. Eight soldiers already in perimeter formation, geomarrow shields up and lances deployed, their breath rising in short controlled bursts. Two more at the portable charge station on the ridge's lip, the meter spinning urgently. One soldier — Mira, he recognizes her by the dent in her left pauldron from a Fragmentum impact three weeks ago that he has been after her to get repaired — is managing to hold the southern flank alone despite the bloom pushing steadily in her direction, and he makes a note to tell her so later. "Commander on site!" "Report," He says, and draws his weapon without breaking stride. "Convergence started twenty minutes ago, sir. Growth rate is slow but the density keeps climbing — we've pushed it back twice and it just redirects." The soldier speaking is young; Gepard doesn't know his name yet, which means he's newer than two months, which means he has never seen a convergence this size before, which explains the very specific quality of controlled terror in his voice. "It's different from the ones last week, sir. The ones last week felt random. This one feels like it knows where we are." Gepard looks at the bloom for exactly three seconds. Behavioral. Pela's word from the report surfaces in his mind, and he files it and moves on. "Yuna — south flank with Mira, don't let it push toward the Energy Hub." The soldier is already moving. "Chen, you're with me on the front line. Everyone else — suppression formation, maintain the perimeter, nothing crosses the line." He locks eyes with the young soldier briefly. "What's your name?" "Rikov, sir." "Rikov. You're doing fine. Stay in formation." The boy straightens. And then they push. Fragmentum combat at this scale is nothing like the clean, decisive engagements in Belobog's training manuals. It is grinding, loud, relentless, deeply inelegant work — the kind that leaves bruises in places you don't notice until the next morning and a particular ringing in the ears that takes two days to fully clear. His geomarrow shield takes the first impact and he feels it in both elbows, driven three steps back, boots carving furrows in the packed snow. He drives forward again, leans his whole body into it, and fires a concentrated burst of ice-lance energy at the nearest spire formation. It shatters. Three more grow back from the fracture points within seconds. He fires again. He does not stop. This is the part they don't depict in the palace murals. The murals show the General standing at the head of a formation, shield raised, the Fragmentum retreating before him in dramatic amber-lit defeat. The murals do not show two hours of brutal, grinding attrition in the teeth of a blizzard, while the Fragmentum grows and redirects and grows again with a patience that genuinely feels like intent, and the only real strategy available is simply to outlast it. They outlast it. The bloom collapses at the third hour. Not dramatically — it doesn't shatter or retreat so much as it simply exhales. The amber light dims. The ground stops moving. The crystalline structures crack from the base and crumble inward, and the sound drops from that deep unsettling thrum to nothing, and the nothing is almost louder. Gepard stands in the center of the cleared snowfield in the silence that follows, his charge at twenty-three percent, one hand on his knee, and breathes. His soldiers are quiet around him in the particular way that follows something that required everything — not unhappy, not relieved exactly, just hollow in a way that only sleep can refill. "Good work." He says. And then, because the young soldier is still standing nearby with the expression of someone who narrowly escaped something and hasn't fully processed it yet: "All of you. Solid formation tonight." Rikov exhales like he'd been holding it for three hours, which he probably had been. Gepard straightens. He looks north, toward the ridge, toward the distant dark mass of Qlipoth palace with its upper spires just catching the first suggestion of coming dawn. She'll be back by now. Inside. Safe behind the walls. He tells himself this is all he needs to know, and he is mostly successful. "Sir." Chen appears at his shoulder, holding out a small sealed report cylinder. "The charge station team documented the bloom's growth patterns. Pela's people asked us to collect real-time data whenever we have a large convergence." "Good thinking." He takes it. "Get everyone back to the main post for debrief. I'll be there in ten minutes." Chen nods and moves off. Gepard stands alone in the scorched snowfield for a moment longer, and in the quiet, he notices something he didn't have time to notice during the fight. The residual Fragmentum crystallization on the ground — the fragments too small to pose a threat, the ambient debris left after a bloom collapses — is not dispersed evenly across the site, the way it usually is. It is concentrated on the northern edge of the clearing. Pointing, very deliberately, toward Belobog's outer wall. He stares at this for a long moment. Then he takes out his phone and photographs it. —— He does not sleep before the morning meeting. This is not a decision so much as a thing that simply happens to him, in the way many things have been happening to him lately — he never finds the right moment, and then dawn arrives anyway. The war council chamber on the second floor of the palace's east wing is a room he knows with the familiarity of years. Long table, chairs designed by someone who had apparently never sat in one, maps of Belobog and the surrounding Restricted Zone spread across every surface, standing lamps casting pale yellow light because the north-facing windows offer the sun almost nothing to work with. He arrives first, which is habit and slightly excessive, and spends the waiting time reading a border assessment on his feet rather than sit down and have nothing to do with his hands. The others filter in. Administrator Pela arrives with the specific energy of someone who has been awake since four AM and has made peace with it entirely. She sets down a tower of folders, accepts a cup of coffee from a palace aide with the reverence typically reserved for religious ceremonies, and immediately begins cross-referencing three documents at once. She glances up at Gepard. "You were at the Sector A convergence last night." "Yes." "I heard the bloom pattern was atypical." "I sent you a photograph of the residual distribution at 4 AM." She checks her device, and her expression sharpens the way it does when data confirms something she suspected and wished it hadn't. "I'll have a full analysis ready by end of day." A pause, her finger tapping the edge of the table. "Gepard. How long has the bloom orientation been directional?" "That's the first time I've seen it this clearly. But looking back at the past month's patrol reports—" He opens his folder to a marked page and pushes it across the table. "The growth vectors have been incrementally northward across all three sectors for the last six weeks. I thought it was environmental variance." Pela stares at the page. She stares at it long enough that two other people enter the room and sit down before she speaks. "It's not environmental variance," she says, with the quiet, controlled devastation of someone delivering a diagnosis they had been hoping to avoid. "No," he agrees. "I don't think it is." Seele arrives like weather — fast, slightly disorganized in the way that is somehow always purposeful, her silver hair loose in a way that suggests she woke up twenty minutes ago and considered this a reasonable preparation time. She drops into the seat beside Pela, glances at the report, and looks up at Gepard. "Is this the bloom data from last night?" "Pela's running analysis—" "The Fragmentum is moving toward the populated sectors." Flat. Not a question. Something in her jaw sets. She spent her formative years in the Underworld — navigating a city the surface considered expendable — and Gepard has noticed that threats to civilians draw from Seele a specific quality of cold fury, very different from the hot kind, the kind that moves precisely and does not miss. "How long have we known." "We're establishing the timeline now," Pela says carefully. "How long have we suspected." A pause. "Possibly six weeks," Gepard says. Seele's eyes move to him, and he receives the full weight of that, and then she gives one sharp, accepting, no-time-to-waste nod, and opens her folder. Sampo Koski arrives last, because Sampo Koski always arrives last, and always arrives in a way that makes it feel like a choice rather than a failure of punctuality. He comes through the door without knocking — which, Gepard has gathered, is not rudeness so much as a deeply held personal philosophy about the nature of doors — spots the three of them already arranged in various stages of grim professional readiness, and pauses. "Hm," he says, reading the room with the practiced efficiency of a man whose survival has always depended on reading rooms quickly. "So it's that kind of morning." "Sit down, Sampo," Pela says. "Gladly." He takes the remaining chair, drops into it with the easy confidence of someone who belongs wherever he happens to be sitting, and sets a small folded piece of paper on the table in front of him. "I brought notes." Seele stares at the folded paper. "Are those written on the back of a playing card." "I work with available materials." He unfolds it. "Don't worry, it's all very professional. I used complete sentences and everything." Seele's expression does the thing it does in Sampo's presence, which is cycle visibly through several emotional weather systems before settling on a cold front. Gepard has always found Seele and Sampo's dynamic instructive — the specific texture of an old acquaintance relationship forged in the Underworld's particular school of hard lessons, which produces a familiarity that looks, to the uninformed observer, a lot like hostility. He suspects there is genuine warmth underneath it. He also suspects neither of them would appreciate him saying so. Bronya enters. The room does what it always does when she enters — a subtle collective reorganization, nothing dramatic, nothing anyone would admit to if asked. Just the slight shift that happens around a gravitational center. She is wearing the formal Guardian's coat over her armor today, the deep navy one with the gold shoulder trim, her silver hair pinned at the base of her neck. She looks exactly like what she is: twenty-two years old and carrying a country on the same shoulders that were pressed against a concrete wall three nights ago, whispering things she doesn't say in daylight. She sits at the head of the table, and she looks at Gepard first. The look lasts one second and communicates several things at once: you were at the convergence last night, you have not slept, and we are not going to discuss either of these things in this room. He responds with a look that says: understood. She looks briefly at Sampo in a way that communicates something else, something that has the shape of of course you're here, and Sampo responds to this with a small, precise nod that has none of his usual theater in it — the gesture of a man in a professional context he takes seriously despite every reasonable expectation to the contrary. "I'll keep this brief," she says, and opens her briefing folder. "The Sector A convergence last night has yielded data consistent with patterns we've been observing across all three sectors for the past several weeks. Pela, your analysis." Pela presents for eight minutes. She is extraordinary at this — at taking deeply alarming information and delivering it in a register calibrated to prevent panic without verging into dishonesty, threading that particular needle with the precision of long practice. Behavioral clustering. Directional growth. Consistent orientation toward population centers across all observed sites. The word intelligence continues to go unspoken. Everyone in the room hears it anyway. "There is one additional finding," Pela says, and her voice undergoes a very slight flattening — the specific register she uses when she is about to say something she would very much prefer not to. "The convergence last night reached a density level we haven't recorded since the event at Everwinter Hill." She pauses. "And the Everwinter Hill event was not a naturally occurring convergence." The room is quiet. "Something triggered it," Sampo says. He is not performing anything right now. His voice is even, his expression unreadable, the playing card notes folded neatly under two fingers on the table. This is, Gepard reflects, what Sampo Koski looks like when something has actually gotten his attention — stripped of every affectation, just a very sharp mind turning a problem over. "The energy signatures are similar. I am not drawing conclusions beyond that until the analysis is complete," Pela says. Bronya has not moved. She is looking at the map in the center of the table, her fingertip resting on the paper's edge with a faint, continuous pressure. "Thank you, Pela." Her voice is completely level. "Gepard — increase patrol frequency across all three sectors. Stagger the rotations so the schedule remains unpredictable." Her eyes move to him. "I want a second observation post established on the North ridge by end of week." "Already started the requisition." The almost-smile. There and gone. "Of course you have." She looks around the table. "Seele — coordinate with the Underworld district administrators. We'll increase civilian alert capacity without increasing visible military presence in the streets. I don't want people frightened before we understand what we're dealing with." "Understood," Seele says. No hesitation. "Sampo." The way Bronya says his name is different from how she says everyone else's — not unkind, not quite formal, the tone of someone who has decided to be pragmatic about the fact that a useful person is a useful person regardless of their relationship with convention. "The outer tunnel networks beneath the Restricted Zone's northern perimeter. You have people down there." It is not a question. Sampo looks at her for a moment. Something passes between them — a brief, mutual acknowledgment of a particular kind of transaction, the kind where both parties understand exactly what is being asked and exactly what it costs. "I might know some individuals with eyes in those corridors, yes." "If the Fragmentum is moving toward population centers through the surface, it may be using the tunnels as a secondary vector. I need to know if the crystallization has reached the tunnel walls. And I need to know without triggering a public report that goes through twelve administrative desks before it reaches mine." She holds his gaze. "Quietly." "Quietly," he repeats, with the air of a man who has built an entire career on that word. "I can do quietly." "Good." A pause. The faintest shift in her expression — not quite gratitude, not quite warning, something precisely between them. "Whatever it costs." "Consider it a community service," Sampo says, pleasantly, and picks up his folded notes. "I do those sometimes. It surprises people." "Good. Reports from all stations by 1800 hours. That's all." The room begins to move. Gepard stays seated a moment longer than the others, which he does not bother pretending is accidental, and when the chairs have cleared and the door has closed, it is the two of them and the north-facing windows and the pale morning light. "The signature Pela mentioned," he says. "Yes." "You already knew." She is quiet for a moment. "I suspected." "And the thing you weren't ready to say." She looks at him fully. Her eyes are very grey in the colorless northern light. "Not yet," she says. "But soon." He nods. He does not push. He never pushes — this has been the compact between them for years, unspoken and unbroken, the agreement that has survived more than most things in both their lives. She will tell him when she can. He will still be there when she does. "Gepard." He stops in the act of standing. "You look terrible." "Thank you, Commander." "Sleep before the evening patrol. That is not a suggestion." "Noted." "It's an order." He looks at her. She looks at him, and does not flinch, and does not soften, and does not look away. "...Understood," he says. —— He goes first to the Underworld's medical ward, because he promised Serval three days ago that he would check on a soldier recovering there and he is already twenty minutes late on that promise and feels appropriately guilty about it. The ward occupies a converted warehouse on the border of the second district. Natasha runs it the way she runs everything — with a quiet, total, slightly alarming competence, and an organizational logic that is not immediately legible but turns out, once you understand it, to be extremely efficient. The place is clean with the thoroughness of a personal philosophy. He has been here four times in two months and has yet to find a single thing out of place. Natasha is at the front desk when he enters, writing in a patient chart with the focused serenity of a person who has long made peace with the fact that there will always be more to do, and the only reasonable response to this is to keep doing it. She looks up. "Commander Gepard. You're late." "I know. I apologize." "Don't apologize to me." She closes the chart. "Private Shen has been awake since six AM and will absolutely not admit he's been listening for the door. He's doing well — Fragmentum crystallization has retreated to under four percent on the affected tissue. Another week and he'll be cleared for light duty." "That's good news." "It is." She comes around the desk and looks at him the way doctors look at people who present at a medical facility to visit a patient and are themselves in more obvious need of attention. "When did you last eat a full meal." "That's—" "I'm not accusing you of anything. The answer is on your face whether you give it to me verbally or not." She folds her hands — a gesture that reminds him, for a split second, of Bronya, though the warmth behind it is entirely different. Bronya's folded hands are a fortress. Natasha's are an open door. "The Sector A convergence was last night." "Yes." "You were there." "Yes." "And the Supreme Guardian has, I assume, already issued the same observation I'm currently making." He looks at her. "You speak with her often." "She sends reports. I send recommendations she mostly ignores." A brief, dry smile. "We have an arrangement." She turns and gestures for him to follow her. "Come. He's been asking about you." Private Shen is twenty years old and embarrassingly grateful to see him, in the way young soldiers get when a superior takes time to come in person, and Gepard spends twenty minutes at his bedside doing the part of the job that no mural ever depicts, which is simply being present. He tells him about the convergence last night, about Rikov holding the southern formation, about the bloom's collapse at the third hour. Shen laughs at the description of Rikov's face after it was over, and the laugh sounds like something releasing, like a held breath, and Gepard considers twenty minutes well spent. He is almost to the exit when the front door opens. Sampo Koski walks in carrying a paper bag that smells excellent. He is still wearing what he was wearing in the war council chamber three hours ago, which means he came here directly after the meeting, which means this is not the coincidence he is about to claim it is. He stops when he sees Gepard. His face does several interesting things in quick succession. "Commander." Not brightly this time — a genuine acknowledgment, the meeting still sitting somewhere behind his eyes. Then, recovering: "Another coincidence." "You followed me here from the palace." "I was heading this way regardless." He holds up the bag. "I bring food to the ward twice a week. Ask anyone." "Sampo." They both turn. Natasha is standing in the hallway behind them, chart in hand, with the expression of a person who understands something completely and has decided, as a matter of ongoing policy, not to make an issue of it. "Twice a week," she confirms, to Gepard, in a tone of absolute neutrality. Then, to Sampo: "If those are from the Tier Two bakery, tell me you got the ones with the dark filling." Sampo opens the bag, checks, looks up. "Dark filling." "Good." She extends her hand. He crosses the room and gives her the bag, and for a moment, as the handoff happens, neither of them moves particularly quickly about it. Natasha looks at the bag. Sampo looks at Natasha, and something in his face goes quiet in the specific way it had gone quiet in the council chamber when Pela said energy signature similar to Everwinter Hill — that stripped, unexpectedly sincere thing — except this version is warmer and considerably more difficult to look at directly. It slides back into place before it fully surfaces. "Break room," she says. "Of course," he says. She walks away down the hall. He watches her go with the practiced patience of a man who has been watching someone walk away for long enough that he's gotten very good at making it look casual. Then he seems to remember Gepard exists, and turns back. "So." He glances toward the hall once more, a flicker. "She works too much, you know." "I'm aware of the irony of you saying that to me." Sampo grins, and this one is entirely genuine. "That's what I like about you, Gepard. You're funny for someone who never laughs." "I laugh." "Name a time." He considers this with genuine effort. He cannot, at present, produce a specific example. "The meeting," Sampo says, and the grin fades to something more even. "The Stellaron data Pela held back. She held some back — you could tell from how she phrased the bit about the analysis not being complete. There's something she knows and isn't ready to say in a room with that many people in it." He is not asking. He is reporting, in his particular offhand way, that he noticed. "The Supreme Guardian knows what it is already." Gepard looks at him. Sampo shrugs lightly, folding his hands behind his head. "Just an observation. I notice things. It's a habit, mostly keeps me alive." He pauses. "I'll get the tunnel reports to you by end of week. Quietly, like she asked." A beat. "She meant what she said, by the way. About the distinction mattering." "I know she did." "Good." He tilts his head toward the door. "Get some sleep. You look like the Fragmentum won." —— Meanwhile, in the Underworld's outer machine district, a different kind of meeting is happening. The workshop belongs to Svarog, which means it is enormous, obsessively organized, and smells of machine oil and scorched metal in a ratio that Luka has always found oddly comforting. He grew up around this smell. It means work is getting done, and work getting done means problems getting solved, and Luka has always had more faith in solved problems than in most other things. He drops into the chair across the worktable from the massive automaton, who is currently disassembling something complicated and doing it the way Svarog does everything — with a focused, unhurried precision that makes Luka feel slightly chaotic by comparison, which is unfair because Svarog is twelve feet tall and has four arms. "You look like you haven't slept," Svarog observes, without looking up. "Everyone keeps saying that today." "Because it is true today." A pause, the clean sound of metal components separating. "You came from the surface." "Had a meeting." Luka stretches one arm over the back of the chair, his prosthetic resting on the table. "With the new Supreme Guardian. Up in the palace war room." Svarog is quiet for a moment. His optical sensors flicker, the subtle equivalent of a raised eyebrow. "The Silvermane Guard invited you to a war council." "The Supreme Guardian invited me to a war council. There's a distinction." He pauses, hearing Bronya's voice in his head as he says it, and finds it annoying that the distinction landed. "She wanted to know about surveillance in the outer tunnel networks. Whether we've got eyes down there." "Mmm." Svarog sets down the component he was working on and looks at Luka properly. His gaze, as always, has the quality of something that sees considerably more than is comfortable. "And you told her." "I told her I'd look into it." A pause. "Which means I'm asking you. Because you've got sensors in every corridor in the outer district that anyone with sense installed them in." "I installed them." "I know you installed them." "They are for monitoring structural integrity and atmosphere toxicity levels. Not surveillance." "Svarog." The automaton regards him for a long, deliberate moment. "The Fragmentum crystallization reached the outer tunnel wall in corridor seven-alpha four days ago," he says, with the calm of someone reporting something he has been waiting for someone to ask about. "Corridor nine-beta, six days ago. The growth rate is consistent with surface-level convergence patterns, which means it is not growing from inside the tunnels outward. It is growing from outside inward." A pause. "Something on the surface is pulling it." Luka stares at him. "You've known for six days." "I filed a structural integrity report with the Underworld administrative board." "The administrative board takes three weeks to process a structural integrity report." "Yes," Svarog says. "I am aware of this." And there is something in his voice — very faint, barely there — that sounds like frustration, which on Svarog is remarkable. He picks up the component again. "I was going to bring it to Natasha next. She has the Supreme Guardian's contact information." "I have the Supreme Guardian's contact information now," Luka says, and takes out his phone. "More specifically, I have Gepard's. Which is functionally the same thing apparently." He sends the message and puts the phone in his pocket, and they sit in the companionable near-silence of the workshop for a moment, the only sounds being the soft metallic work of Svarog's hands and the distant hum of the Underworld's generators. "You went to the surface for this meeting," Svarog says eventually. "Yeah." "Voluntarily." "She asked. It seemed important." He looks at his prosthetic, turning the wrist joint slowly. "It is important." "Mmm." Svarog works. "The silver-haired girl was also in the meeting." Luka goes very still. "How did you—" "You have a specific expression when you are trying not to think about something." Svarog does not look up. "You are currently making it." "I'm not—" He stops. Luka has known Svarog long enough to understand that arguing with him about observable data is a waste of both their time. "Seele is Bronya's sister. Obviously she was there." "Obviously," Svarog agrees, with the serenity of someone who has made his point and has no need to labor it. "She doesn't like me," Luka says, which comes out with slightly more feeling than intended. "She does not know you." "She's decided she doesn't like me, which in practice is the same thing." Svarog finally sets the component down and looks at him. His optical sensors carry, as they occasionally do, an expression that on a human face would be something like patience — the specific patience of someone watching a person be considerably more dense than they are capable of being about everything else. "You grew up on opposite sides of a wall that everyone told you was permanent," he says. "She is protective of what she nearly lost, and suspicious of what she does not yet understand. This is not the same as disliking you." Luka opens his mouth. Closes it. "The surveillance data," Svarog continues, returning to his work with perfect equanimity. "I will compile a full corridor report tonight. You can bring it to Commander Gepard in the morning." "Right." Luka looks at his phone. "Right. Yeah." The workshop hums. Somewhere deep in the outer district, a generator cycles through its nightly pressure check, the sound of it rolling through the tunnel walls like a slow mechanical tide. "Svarog," Luka says. "Yes." "Do you think it's going to get bad." Svarog is quiet for a moment. His four hands are still. "I think," he says carefully, "that it is already getting bad, and that the people on the surface are only now beginning to have the vocabulary for what they are looking at." He resumes his work. "Which means there is still time. But not, perhaps, as much as one would prefer." Luka nods. He looks at the corridor seven-alpha notation on his phone screen, at the date beside it — six days ago — and thinks about ten thousand people going about their lives in the amber warmth above him, and the thing moving quietly through the walls toward them, and the young woman in the palace war room with her silver hair and her too-careful posture who already knows and is trying to hold the line alone. He stays until Svarog finishes the report. Then he takes it, and heads toward the lift back to the surface. —— He does sleep. Four hours, total and dreamless, the kind of sleep that comes from a body collecting a debt owed in full. He wakes seven minutes before the evening patrol with the bone-deep punctuality that has lived in him since his first year in the Restricted Zone, takes a moment to lie in the dark and let the ceiling be the ceiling, and then gets up. The patrol takes four hours and is quiet. He is grateful for the quiet. He is less grateful for what the quiet does to his thoughts, which is give them room to move in directions he would prefer they didn't. He thinks about the residual Fragmentum distribution. The way it pointed, clearly and without ambiguity, toward the inhabited districts. The way Pela had said similar energy signature to Everwinter Hill and then stopped talking, and the way Bronya had not reacted to this like someone receiving new information. He thinks about what she said. I have a guess. But I don't want to say it until I'm sure. He has known Bronya since they were seventeen years old. He knows how she sounds when she is genuinely uncertain, and he knows how she sounds when she is certain and afraid of being right. In the outpost three nights ago, and in the council chamber this morning, and in the fragments she has been sending him at irregular hours — that was the second kind. He stands alone on the North ridge while his squad checks the convergence site below, and looks out at Belobog's outer wall. Amber warmth leaks from behind it, thousands of people living their lives inside the shell of a city being held together with geomarrow and collective stubbornness and the quiet ongoing work of the people posted out here every night in the snow, none of whom know yet that the thing at the center of their world might be calling the dark toward them. Something is drawing the Fragmentum. Something that wasn't there six months ago. He breathes the frozen air and does not finish the thought, because finishing it means confirming what Bronya isn't ready to say yet, and he has never taken from her something she wasn't ready to give. He turns away from the wall. He goes back to his squad. —— The message comes at 1:47 AM. He is at Outpost B, same desk, same ceiling with its fifteen cable spools. Halfway through a patrol report stack, his phone lights up on the table. Lady Bronya: 'Are you awake.' -'Yes.' Lady Bronya: 'How is the North ridge.' -'Clear. Convergence site is cold. I was there four hours ago.' Lady Bronya: 'Good.' A pause. The outpost hums. Outside, the blizzard has softened to steady snowfall — gentle enough that he can hear the silence between it if he keeps still. His phone lights up again. Lady Bronya: 'Can I ask you something.' Something in him goes very still. -'Of course.' Lady Bronya: 'In the meeting today. When Pela said the energy signature was similar to Everwinter Hill.' He reads this. Waits. Lady Bronya: 'Were you afraid.' He reads it twice. He reads it a third time. Then he picks up the phone. -'No.' He watches the message send, and then types the rest. -'I was concerned. There's a difference. Fear is about what might happen to you. Concern is about what might happen to everyone else.' A longer pause than usual. He sets the phone down. He picks it up again. Lady Bronya: 'That is a very Gepard distinction to make.' His mouth does something it probably shouldn't, given that it is nearly 2 AM and he is alone in an outpost. -'Is that a bad thing.' Lady Bronya: 'No.' A pause. Lady Bronya: 'I was afraid.' He sits with this. Then his thumb moves. -'I know.' Lady Bronya: 'You knew in the meeting.' -'Yes.' Lady Bronya: 'And you didn't say anything.' -'It wasn't the right time. You were running a war council.' Lady Bronya: 'And now?' -'Now you're asking. So now is the right time.' The lamp hums. Snow falls. He watches his phone and waits, in the same way he waited three nights ago counting down the minutes, except this time the waiting is quieter and somehow harder because of it. Lady Bronya: 'I think I know what's drawing the Fragmentum.' He sits up. -'Tell me.' Lady Bronya: 'Not yet. I need to be sure.' -'Bronya.' A pause. Lady Bronya: 'I know. I know, Gepard.' -'You don't have to carry this one alone.' Lady Bronya: '…' The ellipsis sits on his screen for a long time. He watches it and does not type, because this is one of those moments where the wrong word closes a door, and he would rather have no word and an open door than the opposite. Lady Bronya: 'When I'm sure. I'll tell you first.' He breathes. -'Alright.' Lady Bronya: 'You should sleep, Commander.' He exhales quietly. -'Says the person texting at 2 AM.' Lady Bronya: 'I am the Supreme Guardian of Belobog. Different rules apply to me.' -'Two months ago in the throne room you said you wanted people around you who would hold you accountable.' Lady Bronya: 'I did say that.' -'So. Sleep, Bronya.' The pause this time is the longest yet. He watches the ceiling. Counts the cable spools. Fifteen. Seventeen if you include the two broken ones in the corner that he keeps meaning to requisition replacements for and keeps forgetting. Lady Bronya: 'Alright.' And then: Lady Bronya: 'Goodnight, Gepard.' His thumb moves before he decides to let it. -'Goodnight.' He puts the phone face down on the desk. He picks up the patrol report. He reads the same paragraph four times before accepting that he is not going to be reading patrol reports tonight, and stacks the dossiers neatly to one side. He charges his pauldron and watches the orange light bloom across it, slow and steady, and tries to let his thoughts go quiet. They do not go quiet. But the attempt is there, and he has always believed that the attempt counts for something. He does not sleep at the desk. But he does sit in the quiet outpost until the sky outside the small window shifts from black to the dark grey that precedes dawn, and he does not question what it means that he doesn't mind. Outside, the snow falls softly over the North ridge. Over the scorched circle in the snowfield where the bloom was. Over the outer wall of Belobog with its amber-lit windows. Over the palace, with its north-facing council chamber and its geomarrow throne and the room at the top of it where she is supposed to be sleeping. He hopes she is sleeping. He checks his phone. No new messages. Good. —— Three days later, Pela completes her analysis. She sends it to Gepard at 6 AM, before the war council, with a message that reads only: Read this before the meeting. He reads it standing in the corridor outside his quarters in his socks, and then reads it again, and then gets dressed quickly and goes to find Bronya. He finds her instead in the East wing corridor, already walking toward the council chamber, the analysis in her hand. They stop four feet apart. She looks at him. He looks at her. She has clearly already read it — she has the expression she gets when she is in possession of information she would strongly prefer not to be in possession of, and is in the process of deciding what to do about it anyway. "The Stellaron," he says. "Yes." Her voice is completely even. "It's not just communicating with you." "No." A pause. "It's broadcasting." The corridor is empty. The northern light comes pale through the high windows. Somewhere below them in the city, Belobog is waking up — ten thousand people beginning their day in the warm amber glow of the inhabited districts, none of them knowing yet that the thing buried at the center of their world has been singing to the Fragmentum for six weeks, drawing it closer like a tide, patient and enormous and entirely indifferent to the walls between them. Bronya looks at him, and for a moment the Commander's face is not the one she's wearing — it's the other face, the one that only surfaces in outposts and empty rooms and text messages at 2 AM. "I told you I'd tell you first," she says. "You did," he says. She looks down at the report in her hands. Then back at him. "I don't know how to stop it yet." "We'll figure it out."
