The Aeon of Remembrance looks at a twenty-one-year-old in the generator housing perimeter of the outer machine district of Belobog, Jarilo-VI, with a chrome arm and a spinal integration and the will that does not stop and the face of someone who has arrived at the one decision they will never reconsider. The pact completes. The world goes— —— White. —— The world goes white and then the world is a different world. He does not know it is a different world at first. He knows his name is Luka and he knows he is twenty-one years old and he knows there is a thing he needs to do, a thing he has been doing, a reason he is here and moving and going forward. He knows these things the way you know things that are more fundamental than memory. He does not know her name. He does not know why the thing he needs to do is the thing he needs to do. He only knows: keep going. He keeps going. In this world, he is a maintenance worker in the outer machine district. He is twenty-one years old and he lives two corridors from a bakery that opens at 0630 and he has been in this district his whole life and there is a thing he needs to do that he cannot name and he keeps going toward it. He reaches twenty-seven in this world. The corridor collapse takes him at twenty-seven — a maintenance access in outer corridor fourteen, structural failure, the specific loud dark of a geological ceiling coming down. He does not run from it. He has been not-running from things his whole life because the running-from option has never felt like the correct direction and this is consistent with that. The world goes white. —— And then the world is another world. In this world he is a fighter. The Boulder Town Super League. He is twenty-one years old and he has been fighting since he was fourteen and he is very good at it and there is a thing he needs to do that he cannot name and he keeps going. He reaches twenty-seven. The opponent in the championship bout does not fight the way anyone else he has faced fights — specific, deliberate, targeting the right shoulder that has been the right shoulder since a maintenance tunnel in the previous world and carries the memory of that specific weakness in the way that the body carries the accumulated damage of everything it has been through. He goes down. He does not stay down because staying down is not consistent with the thing he needs to do. He gets up. The opponent hits him again. He gets up again. The fourth time he gets up, his body has reached the limit of what it can receive and continue to get up from, and the getting-up becomes the getting-not-quite-up, and then it becomes staying. The world goes white. —— And then the world is another world. He is twenty-one years old. He has the things that make him himself. He has the thing he needs to do. He does not know what it is. He keeps going. Twenty-seven. The world ends. He goes white. Another world. Twenty-one. The things that make him himself. The thing he needs to do. Keep going. Twenty-seven. The ending. The white. Another world. Twenty-one— —— The worlds accumulate. He does not count them. Counting is not available to him in the way that the name of the thing he needs to do is not available to him — sealed, the specific mercy of the pact that allows him to function in each world without the full weight of what he is doing being present in the functioning. If the full weight were present he could not function. He knows this without knowing how he knows it. He keeps going. In each world he is himself — the specific quality of someone whose nature does not change with the world, who is Luka in the outer machine district and Luka in the Boulder Town circuit and Luka on the northern ridge and Luka in the Restricted Zone and Luka in every other configuration that twenty-two years of cycling through worlds produces. The nature is the same. The name is the same. The thing he needs to do is the same, unnamed, present. He keeps going. The worlds are designed to break him. He understands this without knowing it. Each world is the specific world that is most likely to be the one that stops him — the one that finds the specific angle that will make keeping going no longer possible. Some worlds are physically violent. Some worlds are the specific torture of mundane misery, the grinding accumulation of small losses that produces the same endpoint as acute catastrophe through longer and quieter means. Some worlds are close — so close to the right thing, the right direction, the right person, almost-but-not, the worlds where the worst loss is the one that was almost not a loss. He does not stop. He does not stop because the thing he needs to do is below the worlds' ability to reach. It is below the specific layer that each world operates at. The grinding accumulation of small losses does not reach it. The almost-but-not worlds do not reach it. The acute catastrophes of the physically violent worlds do not reach it. The thing he needs to do is: he wants to see her breathe again. He does not know her name. He does not need to know her name. He wants to see her breathe again. She breathes and he knows that she breathes and the wanting-to-see-it is the thing below everything else, the thing the worlds cannot reach, the foundation. He keeps going. —— The worlds accumulate to a number that is very large. He does not know the number. He only knows: many. More than many. The specific quality of accumulated cycling that the pact produces, the worlds compressing into the amber-black of the formation's dormant state, the white becoming the transition rather than the ending, the cycle becoming the condition. He keeps going. He keeps going through worlds that are designed to break him and he does not break and Fuli watches each cycle with the specific attention of an Aeon of Remembrance watching something that should, by any reasonable measure, have stopped well before this point. Fuli does not stop him. Fuli watches him keep going and does not stop him because Fuli is watching something that is not common. Something that has not, in the specific history of Fuli's domain's observation of the universe, been seen in quite this form. The will that does not require survival to continue. Not as a quality given by a source or an artifact or a Stellaron or anything external. As what he is. At cycle nine hundred and seventy-two thousand and some number that does not matter, Fuli makes a decision. The decision is: it is enough. —— The white arrives. The same white that has been arriving at the end of each cycle. But this white does not resolve into another world. This white is different — the specific quality of a white that is not the transition to the next cycle but the transition to the ending of the cycling, the specific quality of completion. In the white, he is twenty-one years old. He does not know where he is. He knows her name. He does not know how he knows it. He knows it the way he has always known the second heartbeat — not a learned fact, a condition of what he is. The name that has been the unnamed thing in every world, the name that is the reason for the thing he needed to do, the name that is the foundation below everything. Seele. He says it. He says it in the white with the full weight of approximately a million cycles of being a person who keeps going because he wants to see her breathe again. "Seele," he says. The white resolves. —— He is in the generator housing perimeter. On his knees. His left hand on her face. The chrome arm at his side. The right shoulder at its accounting. The Stellaron above. Gepard holding. Svarog at the forms. Clara at the wall. The moment he left at 0958. The same moment. Fuli's gift — the specific mercy of the pact — is that it operates outside the standard temporal sequence of the world in which it was made. The cycles happen. They happen completely and genuinely, every world real, every life real, every death real. And they happen in the space between one moment and the next in the world where they are made. He is on his knees at 0958 in the generator housing perimeter. And he has been through approximately a million lives. He breathes. He looks at Seele under his left hand. Her eyes are closed. The warmth at the surface of her skin begins — the old gold and deep water returning to the surface, the butterfly emerging, the specific quality of Fuli's fulfillment of the pact expressing through the child of Anasrava in the way that Fuli's domain expresses through those it reclaims. Not the beginning of it. Not the potential. The full expression. The butterfly, at the full expression that Fuli's reclamation produces in the child of Anasrava — the specific Remembrance path power that has been building since the source's change in sub-level three, the full and complete expression of what Seele is and has always been building toward. The old gold and deep water at the surface of her skin is not faint. It is brilliant. Her eyes open. —— She looks at the generator housing ceiling above her. She looks at it with the quality of someone who is receiving information about the current situation — the Stellaron above, the combat ongoing, the generator housing perimeter, the warmth at the surface of her skin that is not the edge of the thing but the thing itself — and she is sitting up before the information is fully processed because sitting up is the correct next action and her body knows it. She sits up. She looks at Luka. He is on his knees beside her with his left hand that was on her face now at his side and the chrome arm at his side and he is looking at her with an expression she does not have a name for. She does not have a name for it because she has never seen it before. She has seen the stripped face and the no-management face and the full-weight-of-everything face and the nine-year-old-wall-form face. She has seen all of those. She has not seen this. This is the face of someone who has seen her not breathing and is now seeing her breathing and the distance between those two things is approximately a million lives in his face. She does not know about the million lives. She does not know about the pact. Fuli's mercy — the same mercy that sealed both of their memories at nine years old until they were ready to hold them — sealed this too. They will not know, right now, in the generator housing perimeter with the Stellaron above and the combat ongoing. They will know later, when the memory returns, when both of them are ready to receive what the pact cost. Right now she sees his face and she does not know what made it that way and she does not need to know right now. She just knows: that face is the most important thing she has ever seen. "Luka," she says. He says her name. He does not say it at full volume. He does not say it in the operational register or the shout-across-the-perimeter register or any of the registers that have been the catalogue. He says it in the register below all of those — the register that a person uses for the one word they mean more than any other word. "Seele," he says. She looks at him. She reaches out and she puts her hand on his face — his face, his specific face, twenty-one years old and one million lifetimes in it — and she says: "I'm here." "Yes," he says. "I'm here," she says again, because once is not enough and the second time is different from the first the way the second saying of Vera's name was different from the first and the third will be different from the second. "I know," he says. And the I know is the full version — all the weight it has ever had and significantly more besides. She looks at him. She looks at the warmth at the surface of his skin at the integration points — the chrome arm conducting, the spinal integration conducting, Vera's endurance in both of them warm and present. She looks at his face. She looks at the Stellaron above. The scythe is still in her right hand. The warmth at the surface of her skin is brilliant and complete and the butterfly is fully present and she is fully here and she has had enough of the Stellaron. "Get up," she says. He gets up. She gets up beside him. The old gold and deep water at the surface of her skin — at the full expression, Fuli's reclamation, the butterfly — extends outward from the surface. Not in the heat-shimmer way of the fragment's ambient glow. In the specific wing-quality that is the butterfly's visible expression — luminous, radiating, the warmth of the Remembrance domain expressed through the person who is most fully connected to it. The Stellaron looks at her. The combat awareness of a source at full manifestation reads the frequency of what is in front of it now, which is different from the frequency of the person who charged it three minutes ago. Significantly different. The Stellaron prepares the directed pulse. Seele looks at it. She looks at it with the cold. "Luka," she says. "Yes," he says, already moving. "Together," she says. "Together," he says. The butterfly spreads its wings — the specific luminous extension of the Remembrance domain's full expression, visible across the generator housing perimeter, visible from the outer wall, visible from the inhabited districts — and Seele charges the Stellaron. This is not the same charge as before. This charge carries approximately a million lives of reasons. Even if she does not know it yet. The battle resumes. Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 5: "Resonance" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text Pela's research hits its first wall on day six. She sends the update at 11 PM, which means she has been working since before the Sector C bloom and has not stopped, which means she has been awake for approximately twenty hours, which means the update is probably accurate and probably also missing things she would have caught at hour twelve. Gepard reads it at the Sector B observation post, standing under the cable spool array with his pauldron charging and his phone in his hand, and reads it twice. The summary is this: resonance dampening is theoretically viable. The pre-Separation research records confirm it has been done before — once, in a controlled environment, with equipment that no longer exists, by researchers whose methodology is described in terms that assume a baseline of knowledge Pela is currently reconstructing from fragments. The interrupting frequency is calculable. The delivery mechanism is not. The only confirmed method in the historical record required a geomarrow source of magnitude comparable to the Stellaron itself, channeled through a purpose-built array that took two years to construct and is now, presumably, somewhere under forty meters of Fragmentum-saturated permafrost. Thirteen days. He sends a reply that says: keep going, and puts the phone away. —— In the Underworld's outer corridor seven-alpha, Luka is doing something he told Sampo he would think about and has apparently finished thinking about. He moves through the tunnel with a handheld sensor Svarog modified for him — a standard atmospheric toxicity reader with a secondary crystallization detection module soldered to its side in a way that is not factory specification but works better than factory specification, which is Svarog's approach to most problems. The reading climbs steadily as he moves deeper into the outer corridor, the amber indicator light on the sensor shifting from yellow to a pale, persistent orange that he does not find comforting. The crystallization on the walls here is not the dramatic spire formations he's seen in salvage runs near the surface. It is thin. Almost delicate. A film of amber-black threading through the concrete like roots, following the seams between construction blocks, branching at junctions in patterns that his eye keeps trying to read as deliberate. The tunnel smells of cold stone and something underneath the cold stone — something with a low, metallic edge that is not quite any smell he knows and that his body responds to with a very specific and unhelpful variety of alertness. He photographs everything. He logs the sensor readings at every junction marker. He moves carefully and without noise, because Svarog told him to and because the tunnel has the quality of a space that is listening, which is an irrational thought he has anyway and which he does not write down in his log because Sampo will read it and say something. At junction seven-alpha-four, he stops. The crystallization on the north wall is different from the rest. It is not threading through the seams. It is growing over them — a solid, continuous sheet of amber-black, perhaps two meters wide, that has covered the construction markings entirely and is pressing outward from the wall surface in a slow, almost imperceptible convexity. He puts his hand near it, not touching, and the sensor in his other hand spikes into the red. He photographs it from four angles. He logs the sensor reading. He takes a step back. The crystallization does not move. He takes another step back. The amber light in the sheet pulses once — a single, slow rhythm, like a heartbeat, like a breath — and then is still. Luka stands in the cold of corridor seven-alpha-four for a moment longer, his sensor still reading red, his photograph log open, and he makes a decision that Sampo is going to tell him was unnecessary and Svarog is going to say nothing about while clearly thinking it was correct. He marks the junction on his map. He notates the pulse. He keeps going. —— The structural assessment of the Sector C wall comes back at noon on day seven with numbers that Gepard carries to Bronya's office and sets on her desk without preamble, because preamble would be a way of softening them and he has decided not to do that. She reads them. She sets the document down. She looks at the wall-mounted map with the bloom sites marked in amber. "The east wall can hold another impact at that force level," she says. "One. Maybe two, if the bloom growth rate stays below the Sector C peak." He sits down in the chair that has become, over the past week, functionally his chair — the one pulled from the corner, positioned across from her desk at an angle that is neither purely professional nor anything that could be called otherwise. "If the growth rate accelerates, one." "And Pela's dampening timeline." "She's working on the delivery mechanism. The theory is sound. The engineering—" He pauses. "She needs more time than we have." Bronya is quiet. Outside the office window, the pale northern light sits on the rooftops of Belobog's upper city, indifferent and cold. He watches her look at the map and thinks — again, always — about what it costs to sit in that chair and hold all of this. The Stellaron's voice. The feedback loop. The wall with one impact left in it. Thirteen days on Pela's conservative estimate, which is now twelve, and the Fragmentum learning their patrol schedules faster than they can change them. She is twenty-two years old. He has known her since she was seventeen, since the first time she stood at the front of a briefing room and told a group of soldiers twice her age exactly what they were doing wrong and exactly how to fix it, and the certainty in her voice was so complete that no one questioned it, and she was right. He had thought at the time that this was the most remarkable thing he had ever seen. He has been revising this assessment upward every year since. "Pela needs another approach," Bronya says. "A secondary option. Something that doesn't require equipment we don't have." She picks up a pen. "I've been looking at the pre-Separation records myself. There's a reference in the Architect's original survey — fragmentary, barely legible — to a technique called fractured resonance. Not dampening. Disruption." She writes something, slides the paper across the desk to him. "Instead of generating a counter-frequency to neutralize the signal, you introduce a dissonant frequency to fracture it. Like—" "Like breaking a harmonic," he says, reading. "Yes. The energy required is lower. The geomarrow source doesn't need to match the Stellaron's magnitude — it needs to be precisely calibrated to the dissonant interval." She pauses. "Which is a much harder calibration problem. But it's a solvable one." He looks at the equations she's written. They are, to his eye, significantly above his level of technical comprehension, but the shape of the argument is clear enough. "Does Pela know about this." "I sent it to her this morning. She's been looking at the same records, but from the dampening angle. This reframes the problem." She looks at him. "It might buy us time we don't otherwise have." "How much time do you need." "To verify the calibration and build the delivery mechanism?" She looks at the ceiling briefly. "If Pela has help and the right materials — five days. Six if something goes wrong." "We have twelve." "Then we have room." "If nothing else goes wrong." She looks at him. Her eyes are very level. "Yes," she says. "If nothing else goes wrong." He nods. He slides the paper back. He does not say what they are both thinking, which is that something else going wrong is not a question of whether but of when, because the Fragmentum has been wrong-footing them consistently for a week and there is no particular reason to believe it has exhausted its capacity to do so. "I'll need materials from the Energy Hub," she says. "Geomarrow grade-three or higher, at least four units. And I'll need Serval." He blinks. "Serval," he says. "She built the Energy Hub's current resonance array. She understands geomarrow harmonics better than anyone in Belobog." A pause. "Better than anyone I know of anywhere." He thinks about his sister. He thinks about the way she had looked, the last time he saw her, three weeks ago — tired but animated, the way she always is when she has a problem to work on, which is most of the time. He thinks about asking her to help calibrate a dissonant frequency to interrupt a Stellaron broadcasting a signal that is drawing Fragmentum toward a city of ten thousand people. "She'll say yes," he says. "I know." A pause. "That's not what I'm asking you." He looks at her. She is looking at the map, her pen tapping once against the desk, and he understands. She is not asking whether Serval will agree. She is asking whether he will ask her — whether he is willing to bring his sister into this. "I'll call her this afternoon," he says. Bronya looks at him. The fortress-hands posture loosens, just slightly, at the wrists. "Thank you." "Don't thank me until it works." "I'll thank you now," she says, with the precision of someone who has learned not to defer gratitude on the assumption that there will be a better moment later. "And again if it works." He stands. He picks up his coat. He is almost to the door when she says, without looking up from the map: "Gepard." "Yes." "The phone call last week. At 2 AM." A pause, the pen tapping once. "I've been thinking about what you said." He waits. "About concern being different from fear." She is still looking at the map. "I've been trying to practice that distinction." "How is it going." "Poorly," she says, which is the most honest thing she has said to him in a week, and he receives it accordingly. "Keep practicing," he says. He goes. —— Serval picks up on the second ring. "Gep." Her voice has the specific warmth of someone who is always genuinely pleased when he calls and has never been particularly restrained about showing it, which is one of approximately ten thousand ways she is his opposite. "I was just thinking about you. I found a photo from your first year in the Guard and you look absolutely — anyway. What's up." "I have a job for you," he says. "It's important." A pause. He can hear her reading the register of his voice. She has always been good at this — better than most people, which given what she does for a living makes sense. "How important." "Stellaron-level important." Silence. "Gep." "I need your help with a resonance problem. Geomarrow harmonic calibration. Bronya asked for you specifically — she said you built the Energy Hub array." "I designed the frequency architecture, yeah." Her voice has changed — not alarmed, exactly, but focused, the way it gets when a problem has her full attention. "What kind of resonance problem." He tells her. He gives her the version he would give anyone he trusts completely with technical information, which is the accurate version, which includes the Stellaron broadcasting, the feedback loop, the fractured resonance approach Bronya identified, and the twelve-day window. He gives it to her the way he gives Bronya her reports — without softening, without speculation, just the shape of the thing clearly described. When he finishes, Serval is quiet for a moment. "The dissonant interval calibration," she says. "That's the hard part. You need to know the Stellaron's exact broadcast frequency first, or you're calculating blind." "Bronya can give you the frequency. She's been hearing it." Another pause. Longer this time. "She's been — Gep. She's been hearing the Stellaron." "For two months." "And she's been—" He can hear Serval doing several calculations at once, the way she does, and then arriving at the one that matters. "She's okay." "She's managing." "That's not—" "I know," he says. "She's Bronya." A silence that has a very specific texture to it — the texture of two people who both know someone extremely well and are sharing, without saying it, a particular variety of worry that is inseparable from a particular variety of respect. Then Serval exhales. "When do you need me." "Tomorrow, if you can." "I'll be there tonight." A pause. "And Gep? The photo. You really did look absolutely terrible. You had this expression like someone had personally wronged you by asking you to stand still for a camera." "Serval." "I'm just saying. Some things don't change." The warmth is back in her voice, fully, unreservedly. "I'll see you tonight." She hangs up. He puts his phone in his pocket and stands in the corridor outside Bronya's office for a moment longer, listening to the palace settle around him — the distant sounds of the administrative wing, the muffled voices, the cold northern light coming through the high windows. He thinks about Serval's voice when he said she's been hearing the Stellaron, and the specific quality of that pause, and decides that he will not examine it too closely because examining it too closely means acknowledging something he is not ready to acknowledge in the middle of a twelve-day window with a stressed perimeter wall and a Fragmentum that is learning. He files it. He goes to find Pela. —— In the Underworld, Sampo delivers Luka's first tunnel report to Natasha's ward in person rather than by message, partly because the ward is on his way to the Tier Two bakery and partly because the report contains the notation about the pulse in corridor seven-alpha-four, and he wants to read her face when she reads it. She reads it at the front desk, standing, chart forgotten in her other hand. He watches her expression do the thing it does with information she doesn't like — a very controlled, very thorough stillness, the way a surface goes still when something is moving underneath it. "He saw it pulse," she says. "Once. Single rhythm. Then nothing." "That's not ambient crystallization behavior." "No," Sampo agrees. "It's not." She looks at the report for another moment. Then she sets it on the desk and looks at him — fully, the way she rarely looks at him when they are both aware that other people might see, the way that makes him feel, in a register he has learned not to examine at close range, like she is reading something he has not said. "You sent a twenty-year-old into a corridor with active crystallization," she says. "He's twenty-one. And he volunteered." "Sampo." "He went to Svarog first. He had modified equipment. He knows those tunnels better than the sensor maps." He meets her gaze, which is steadier than his. "I didn't send him anywhere he couldn't handle." "You don't know that." "I know Svarog wouldn't have let him go if he couldn't." She holds his gaze for a moment. He holds hers. This is a thing they do sometimes — this specific variety of standoff that is not actually a standoff because neither of them is angry, it is more like two people verifying each other's coordinates, making sure they are still pointing in compatible directions. She looks away first, which she almost never does. "Send him to me when he's done with the next walkthrough," she says. "I want to check him over." "He'll say he's fine." "He will. Send him anyway." "Natasha." She looks at him. He picks up the report and taps the junction notation. "The pulse. You know what it means." She is quiet. "The Fragmentum down here is connected to whatever is happening on the surface," he says. "Not just growing toward it. Communicating with it." He sets the report down. "Which means the tunnels are not a secondary vector. They're part of the same system." "I know," she says. "Pela needs to know." "I know." "So does—" "I know, Sampo." Her voice is not sharp, but it is final. She picks the report back up, and her hands are very steady. "I'll send it up today." A pause. "Both the finding and the implication." He looks at her. In the ward behind her, someone coughs. A door opens and closes. The ordinary sounds of a medical facility doing its ordinary work, sitting on top of something that is not ordinary at all. "Are you alright," he says, and he means it the way he always means it with her — stripped of any theater, just the question. She looks at him for a moment. "No," she says, which is the answer she only gives him, and which he receives accordingly. "But I will be. I'm always alright eventually." "I know," he says. "I'll bring the dark filling ones tomorrow." The corner of her mouth moves. Not quite a smile, but in the same neighborhood. "You don't have to." "I know." He picks up his coat. At the door he pauses, because something makes him pause — some instinct that has kept him alive through years of operating in spaces where instincts are the difference — and he turns back. "The pulse in seven-alpha-four," he says. "The single rhythm." He looks at her. "How long has it been doing that." She meets his gaze. Her hands are very still on the report. "I don't know," she says. He nods. He goes. —— Serval arrives at the palace at nine in the evening with a bag of equipment, six pages of frequency calculations she did on the train, and the particular energy of someone who has been running on scientific curiosity and personal loyalty since the moment she hung up the phone. Gepard meets her at the service entrance, because she does not have palace credentials and he has been managing the quiet circulation of unofficial visitors through the service entrance for two months now and has gotten efficient at it. She hugs him in the corridor, which he permits — he permits it specifically because she is Serval and because he has not seen her in three weeks and because the last time he went three weeks without seeing her before that was the year he spent his first rotation at the forward Restricted Zone post at nineteen years old, and the year after that she had shown up at the post with food and frequency calculations and complained at length about the structural inadequacy of his living quarters. Some things don't change. "You look tired," she says, pulling back and looking at him with the unguarded critical affection of a sibling. "You look like you did frequency calculations on a train." "I did frequency calculations on a train. They're good ones." She holds up the pages. "Where's Bronya." "Office. Second floor." She is already moving. He falls into step beside her. The palace corridor is quiet at this hour — the administrative shift long ended, most of the palace staff gone for the night, only the overnight guard rotation moving through the outer rings. Their footsteps echo. "Gep," Serval says, without looking at him. "Yes." "Two months." He knows what she means. He says nothing. "You didn't tell me she was hearing the Stellaron." "I didn't know you needed to know." "She is our Supreme Guardian and also a person I care about very much and also—" She stops. Starts again. "You could have told me." "She asked me not to tell people until she was certain." "And you—" Serval exhales. He can hear her making the decision to let it go, which costs her something, which he appreciates. "Right. Of course you did." A pause. "Is she okay." "She's managing." "Gepard." "She's Bronya," he says, which is the same answer he gave an hour ago on the phone, and which means the same thing now. Serval is quiet for a moment. He glances at her — at the way she is looking at the corridor ahead of them, the frequency pages in her hand, her brow slightly furrowed in the way that means she is processing something she is not going to say out loud right now and will probably bring up later at a significantly worse time. "What," he says. "Nothing." "Serval." "I said nothing." She glances at him sideways, and the corner of her mouth does something he recognizes — the specific compression of a smile being suppressed, the one that means she knows something and is deciding whether now is the time. She decides it isn't. "Later," she says. He files this under things that will come back to inconvenience him and does not push. The office door is open, which means Bronya heard them coming, or has been leaving it open, or both. She looks up when they appear in the doorway, and her expression does something he does not see often — a brief, complete, unguarded thing, the kind that happens when a face isn't ready for the room. Serval in the doorway. Gepard beside her. The frequency pages. "You came tonight," Bronya says. "He said twelve days," Serval says, crossing the room and dropping into the chair across from the desk with the ease of someone who has never once in her life stood on ceremony. "Show me the records." Bronya looks at her for a moment. Then she opens the desk drawer and produces a folder — the pre-Separation research records, the fractured resonance reference, the calculations she slid across the desk to Gepard that morning. She slides them across to Serval. Serval reads. The office is quiet. Gepard takes up his position near the window. Outside, Belobog's upper city is dark and cold and entirely unaware of the three people in this room and the thing they are attempting to build an answer to. "The dissonant interval," Serval says, still reading. "You're right, it's a calibration problem, not a magnitude problem." She looks up. "I need the broadcast frequency. Exactly." Bronya meets her eyes. "I know," she says. And then, with the careful precision of someone who has been measuring a thing for weeks and is only now saying the measurement out loud: "It's 4.7 kilocycles per Belobog standard second. With a harmonic overtone at 14.1." A pause. "It's been climbing. It went up by 0.3 cycles in the last week." Serval stares at her. "You've been measuring it," she says. "I've been hearing it," Bronya says. "I started mapping it when I realized it was changing." Serval looks at Gepard. He looks back. The specific texture of that glance — what it contains, what it asks, what it doesn't say — is something he will think about later, in the outpost at 2 AM, with more honesty than he currently has access to. "Okay," Serval says, and looks back at the pages. "Okay. Give me six hours." She starts working. Bronya makes tea. Gepard charges his armor. The office settles into the particular quiet of people doing focused work in the same space — the scratch of Serval's pen, the soft hum of the geomarrow in the walls, the distant sound of the Restricted Zone's night patrol calling in its first hourly check through the hardwired terminal two floors up. At some point past midnight, Gepard looks up from the patrol report he has been reading to find Bronya asleep in her chair, her tea half-finished, her head listing slightly to one side, the map on the wall in front of her still marked with its amber bloom sites and its fourteen-day clock. He does not wake her. Serval looks up from her calculations, takes in the scene, and looks at Gepard with an expression he has no immediate defense against — the one that is not quite a smile and not quite a question, that simply says: I see you. He looks back at his patrol report. "Gep," she says, very quietly. "Don't," he says, equally quietly. She makes the suppressed-smile expression again. She goes back to her calculations. Outside, the snow falls on Belobog. Inside, in the pale lamp-light of the second floor office, the fractured resonance solution takes shape one equation at a time, and the man near the window reads his patrol report three more times without retaining a word of it, and does not examine why. —— Notes: Luka saw the pulse in corridor seven-alpha-four and wrote it down anyway because he is built different, I said what I said. Natasha said she wasn't alright and she meant it and Sampo heard it and he brought it back to his chest and carried it with him out the door and I am FINE about this. Serval arrived with frequency calculations she did on a train and immediately sat down to work and I love her unreservedly. Her and Gepard in the corridor — "later" — I have been planning that exchange for three chapters. Later will come. It will come at a worse time, as promised. Bronya fell asleep in her chair. Gepard didn't wake her. Serval looked at him. He said don't. She didn't. He went back to the patrol report. No one said anything. NOTHING HAPPENED. He's fine. Everyone's fine. Twelve days. The calibration is underway. The tunnel is pulsing. Natasha sent the report. Chapter 6 is when the wall has to hold something it was not built to hold. I'm sorry. I'm not sorry. Comment please. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 6: "What the Wall Holds" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text Serval finishes the calibration at 4 AM. She does not announce this dramatically. She simply sets her pen down, rolls her neck once, and says: "I have it." Then she looks at the office ceiling with the expression of someone who has just confirmed something they suspected two hours ago and have been verifying since, and exhales through her nose. Gepard looks up from his patrol report, which he has been reading with declining success for the past three hours. Across the desk, Bronya stirs — she had fallen asleep sometime past midnight and has been listing gently to the left ever since, her head resting against the high back of the chair, her breathing quiet and completely unguarded in the way that sleep produces whether or not a person would consent to it if asked. He had watched this happen from the corner of his eye and made the decision not to comment on it, which he stands by. She comes awake the way she always does — quickly, without confusion, the shift from asleep to present happening in the space of one breath. Her eyes find the room, find Serval, find the pages, find Gepard. She straightens. "You have it," she says. Not a question. "4.7 with the 14.1 overtone — the dissonant interval sits at 7.2 kilocycles." Serval slides the calculation sheet across the desk. "It's precise. If you're off by more than 0.05 cycles in either direction you get dampening instead of disruption, which buys you maybe forty-eight hours before the signal re-establishes." She pauses. "But if it's right, it fractures the feedback loop entirely. The Fragmentum loses its orientation cue. It doesn't disappear, but without the signal drawing it, the convergence behavior stops." Bronya is reading the sheet, her finger tracing the intervals. "The delivery mechanism." "That's where Pela comes in." Serval taps the bottom of the page. "The frequency itself is a solved problem. Getting it into the Stellaron's broadcast range at sufficient amplitude requires a resonance array — not as large as the pre-Separation one, because we're disrupting rather than dampening, but it still needs geomarrow grade-three or higher, and it needs to be positioned within forty meters of the Stellaron's chamber." She looks at Bronya. "Which is inside the palace." "I know where the Stellaron's chamber is," Bronya says. "Right. Of course you do." Serval looks at her in the way she has been looking at her since she arrived — a long, careful look, taking inventory. "Bronya. The chamber. You've been down there." A pause. "A few times," Bronya says. Serval opens her mouth. She looks at Gepard. He looks back at her with an expression that says: I know, and: not now, and: I am asking you with everything I have to table this. She closes her mouth. She looks back at the calculations. "I'll talk to Pela in the morning," she says. "Array specifications by end of day. Build time after that — three days for the frame, two for the calibration rods, one day to tune and test." "Six days," Bronya says. "If everything goes right." "We have eleven." "Then we have room." Serval begins stacking her pages. "If nothing—" "Goes wrong," Gepard finishes. "I was going to say 'catastrophically sideways,' but yes." Bronya sets the calculation sheet down, precisely aligned with the edge of the desk. She looks at Serval for a moment with the expression she uses when gratitude is larger than she knows how to present — the compressed, direct version of it, no ceremony. "Thank you," she says. "For coming tonight." Serval looks at her, and something in her face softens — the softening that happens around people she has known long enough to worry about properly. "You could have called me sooner," she says. "Any of this. You didn't have to wait until it was an eleven-day problem." Bronya is quiet. "I know," she says. "I'm working on that." Serval glances at Gepard again, and this time the glance is different — not the inventory look, something more pointed, the kind that means she has filed a conclusion and will produce it at a time of her choosing. He looks steadily back at her and gives her nothing, which is all the confirmation she needs and he knows it. She stands, collects her pages, and slings her bag over her shoulder. "Don't either of you go to sleep before six. That's two hours, minimum. Pela will be coherent by eight and I need you both coherent when I brief her." She moves to the door, pauses in it the way she always pauses in doorways. "Also the frequency calculations are correct. I triple-checked them. Trust them." She goes. The office settles into the quiet that follows someone leaving — the silence with the shape of their absence in it. The lamp hums. Outside, the predawn dark sits on the upper city's rooftops, and Belobog sleeps. Gepard looks at Bronya. She is looking at the calculation sheet, her elbow on the desk, two fingers pressed to her temple. The lamp catches the edge of her hair — the loose strands that have come free of the clasp at the back of her neck over the course of the night — and he looks at the window instead. "You should sleep," he says. "You should sleep," she says. "You fell asleep in that chair an hour ago." "That doesn't count." She closes the folder. She pushes back from the desk. She looks at him across the small distance of it, and fatigue has worn down enough of the professional architecture between them that the room feels, at this hour, in this lamplight, like a different kind of space than it does in the morning. "Will you be at the brief." "Yes." "And the perimeter check after." "Yes." "And the evening patrol." "Bronya." "I'm making a list," she says, and the corner of her mouth moves — not quite the almost-smile, something smaller, something that only surfaces past a certain hour when she is too tired to keep it down. "I find it reassuring." He looks at her for a moment. He makes a decision that is not really a decision, which is to say he does not stop himself from saying: "I'll be where you need me. Same as always." She holds his gaze for one second longer than she needs to. "I know," she says quietly. "Goodnight, Gepard." "Goodnight." She picks up the folder and goes, and the office is empty, and the lamp hums, and he sits in the chair by the window for a moment in the dark and does not examine any of the things he is currently not examining. Then he goes to find somewhere to sleep for two hours. —— The morning brief goes well, which is to say it goes exactly as badly as expected and Pela immediately understands the calibration solution and starts improving it, which is the best possible outcome. Serval and Pela in the same room is a specific and productive variety of chaos — two people with different methodologies applied to the same class of problem, both very good at what they do and both aware of it, which creates a friction that generates results faster than either of them would alone. Within three hours they have a working array specification and a materials list. Within five, the materials have been requisitioned from the Energy Hub. By noon on day eight, construction has begun. Gepard spends the afternoon on the perimeter. The patrol randomization from four days ago has produced results — two minor convergence attempts on the North ridge, both caught early by the observation post and suppressed before they reached bloom density. The Fragmentum is still probing. Still looking for the gap. It hasn't found one yet. This is not comforting. Finding gaps is a learned behavior. He files this with Pela at 1700 hours and goes to check on Rikov. —— The Underworld medical ward is quieter than usual. Natasha is at the front desk, chart in hand, but there is something in the set of her shoulders tonight that is different — a controlled stillness, the surface-over-movement quality he noticed when Sampo first showed her Luka's tunnel report. He does not comment on it. "He's doing well. Crystallization fully cleared. Light duty tomorrow if he wants it." She pauses. "He definitely wants it." "I'll tell him to wait until the day after." "I already did. He accepted it from me with slightly more grace than he will from you, so I'd recommend leading with 'Natasha said so.'" "Noted." He pauses. "Is there something else." She meets his eyes. "Luka completed the second tunnel walkthrough today. The pulse in seven-alpha-four has a matching pattern in corridor nine-beta now. Same rhythm. Both tunnels." A pause. "They're synchronized." Gepard is quiet for a moment. "They're responding to the same source signal." "That's one interpretation." She looks at her hands. "If the resonance array works — if fractured resonance interrupts the signal — do the tunnels stop too." "Logically, if the source is disrupted, the responding behavior should stop." "Logically," she says, in a tone that respects logic while keeping a careful eye on its edges. "I'll make sure Pela has this before the array is tuned." "I sent it an hour ago. With a recommendation that outer district residents in the sectors nearest seven-alpha and nine-beta be offered temporary relocation to central housing." She meets his gaze steadily. "I'm not asking permission. I'm telling you so you can coordinate with whoever needs to know." "That would be Seele," he says. "I'll contact her directly." He looks at her for a moment — this woman who runs a ward in a converted warehouse and moves districts in her spare time. "Is there anything you need. For the ward, if the relocation starts." She blinks. The blink of someone not entirely accustomed to being asked. "Additional cots. Supplies if the construction team has injuries." "I'll arrange it." "Thank you." A pause. "Rikov is in the back if you want to see him." He goes. Rikov is sitting up — progress — and deeply bored — more progress — and lights up when Gepard comes in with the particular gratitude of a young soldier who has been lying in a ward bed for five days watching the work happen without him. Gepard spends twenty minutes telling him about the array construction, the calibration solution, the patrol results. He tells him more operational detail than he would give most soldiers in a ward bed, and he does so specifically so that Rikov understands that the six hours he spent at Sector C holding his position while the ground moved under him were part of something being answered. "Day after tomorrow," Gepard says, at the end. "Light duty." Rikov's expression does its calculation. "Natasha said—" "I know what Natasha said. Day after tomorrow." A pause. "That's — that's what she said too, sir." "I know." He stands. "Get some sleep." "Sir." The hesitation of someone working up to something. "The Sector C bloom. The patrol gap." He looks at the wall. "We changed everything after. Because we lost that ground." "Yes." "So it mattered," he says. "Even though we lost it." Gepard looks at him. He thinks about how to say what he believes — that the losses that teach you something are the ones you survive, and the surviving is made of the losing, not separate from it. He thinks about saying this to a twenty-year-old who has been in the Restricted Zone for two months and took a Fragmentum impact to the shoulder and did not break formation. "Yes," he says. "It mattered." And something in Rikov's face settles — not happy, not relieved, but the specific quality of a person who has found their footing on difficult ground and has decided to keep standing on it. —— Day nine arrives without incident. He does not trust this. The array frame is sixty percent complete. Serval has been in the construction chamber for most of the past forty-eight hours, sleeping in four-hour increments and working in eight-hour ones. She eats whatever Gepard puts in front of her without appearing to notice she is doing so. He has been doing this for both her and Bronya since day eight. He has not examined this. On the afternoon of day nine, Bronya comes down to the construction chamber. She stands in the doorway and looks at the array — the partial structure of geomarrow rods and frequency guides taking shape in the low lamplight, already humming faintly with Serval's charge. Her expression is the careful one. Serval looks up. "You're not supposed to be here yet. It's not calibrated." "I'm not touching it," Bronya says. She steps inside, her eyes on the array, and stops a meter out. Her hands go behind her back. She tilts her head very slightly, the listening posture, the one for the thing no one else can hear. "It's different down here," she says, to no one in particular. "Closer to the source — the signal is more present." Her eyes move to Serval. "Does 7.2 feel right to you. The dissonant interval." "My calculations—" "I know your calculations are right. I mean does it feel right." Serval blinks. She looks at the array, then at Bronya, then back at the array, recalibrating how she is thinking about the problem. "I've only worked with passive resonance before. You're hearing the active broadcast. If you can hear the frequency—" "I can tell you if the interval is right. When you test it." Bronya looks at her steadily. "I'll know." A pause. Then Serval turns back to the array and makes two adjustments to the calibration rods, integrating this without losing the thread. "In that case I need you here on test day." "Day eleven." "If the rods set correctly." A pause. "Which they will." Bronya nods. She looks at the array for one more moment — the expression of someone standing close to something that has been a sound in their head for two months and seeing it given form for the first time. Something that is almost, but not quite, relief. She turns to leave. She is almost at the door when she stops — not a planned pause, a genuine one, the kind the body makes before the mind catches up. She does not turn around. "Gepard," she says. "Yes." "The night of the Sector C bloom." A pause. "The six-hour suppression." Another pause. "Did you eat anything before that." He opens his mouth. Closes it. "I had something before the evening patrol," he says. Technically true. They both know it covers an eighteen-hour gap neither of them is addressing. She does not turn around. But the line of her shoulders changes — a very slight shift, a breath, like a decision being made. "There are things in the kitchen on the second floor," she says. "The good cabinet. Not the administrative staff one." A pause. "I had Pela label it." He stares at the back of her head. "Bronya—" "Don't read too much into it," she says, and walks out, and the door does not quite close behind her, and the array hums softly in the lamplight. Serval does not look at him. She does not look at him for a very long and deliberate period of time. "Serval." "I'm not saying anything," she says, to the calibration rod she is adjusting. "I'm a professional. I'm focused entirely on the work." "Good." "I will say it later." "I look forward to it," he says, flatly. She makes the suppressed-smile expression. He goes back to the materials list. —— Day ten is when the wall decides to make itself known. The structural alert comes through the hardwired terminal at 0600 hours — a stress reading on the eastern perimeter's compromised sections spiking overnight without a convergence event to explain it. Gepard reads it, reads it again, and calls the structural team. The team confirms it on-site at 0900: the wall is not responding to a new impact. It is responding to the residual Fragmentum crystallization in the ground beneath its foundation. The saturation from the Sector C bloom has been working its way under the base of the wall for ten days, and it has reached the permafrost layer — destabilizing the ground the wall stands on rather than striking the wall itself. It is, in its way, elegant. He files this thought under things he would rather not think the Fragmentum capable of, and calls Bronya. "How long," she says, before he finishes explaining. "Four to six days before the foundation compromise reaches critical. Day fourteen at the latest." "And the array test is day eleven." "Yes." "Construction completion—" "Day twelve if everything holds. Day thirteen if it doesn't." A silence. He can hear her running the timelines — that particular quality her silence gets when she is looking for the line where all the pieces land. He has been listening to this silence for years. He knows what it sounds like when she finds it. "The ground beneath the east wall," she says. "If we clear the surface saturation — the residual bloom debris — we slow the progression." "We've been clearing it. The rate isn't fast enough." "What if we accelerate it. Double the clearance crews." "We'd need to pull from patrol rotations." "Minimum viable perimeter coverage on North and West — the stable sectors — all remaining personnel on clearance in the East." She pauses. "Can we do both." He runs it. "Barely." "Barely is enough if the array works on schedule." "If." "Yes," she says. "If." A pause — shorter, different. Not the calculating silence. Something more personal. "Gepard. Can we hold it for five more days." He thinks about the wall. He thinks about the clearance math. He thinks about Rikov going back to duty tomorrow, and Mira with the dented pauldron, and Chen on the observation post rotation for ten days without complaint. He thinks about the Fragmentum probing the patrol schedule twice a day with the patience of something that does not get tired. He thinks about what the honest answer is. "Yes," he says. "We can hold it." A pause. "Then we hold it," she says. There is something in her voice when she says this — not quite the Commander's register, not quite the other one, but living exactly between them, in the specific frequency of two people who have been holding things together for long enough that the boundary between their efforts has become unclear. He does not name it. He files it with the patrol report he read four times and the almost-smile and the good cabinet, and he says: "I'll reorganize the crews this afternoon." "Good." A pause, so brief he nearly misses it: "The good cabinet. Pela put the grain things on the left. The rest is on the right." The smallest pause. "The right side is better." He stands in the Sector B post with his phone to his ear and looks at the structural team's numbers on the desk in front of him, and he breathes once, carefully, through his nose. "Understood," he says. She hangs up. He stands still for a moment. Then he picks up the crew rotation schedule, and begins the reorganization, and does not look at any of the things he is not supposed to be looking at. Outside, on the eastern perimeter, the wall holds. For now, it holds. —— Notes: The wall is not responding to a bloom. It's responding to the ground moving under it. The Fragmentum has been patient and thorough and doing the work quietly while everyone watched the surface. I have been sitting on this for two chapters. I feel appropriately terrible about it. Bronya told him which side of the cabinet is better. She told Pela to label it. She did this before the conversation happened, which means she did it before she knew she was going to tell him. I need everyone to sit with that for a moment. I'm not saying anything else about it. Serval said she'd say it later. She will. It will be at a worse time, as promised. Rikov got his footing back. He asked exactly the right question and he got exactly the right answer. He's going to be okay. Natasha sent the synchronized tunnel data and included a relocation recommendation in the same report without asking anyone's permission, because she never asks permission for things that are simply correct. The array hums. Day eleven is the test. Day fourteen is the wall's deadline. Five days of barely. Barely has to be enough. Chapter 7 is test day. Comment and tell me you're still breathing. Take care. <3 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 7: "7.2" Notes: (See the end of the chapter for notes.) Chapter Text Day eleven begins at 5 AM with Serval eating breakfast standing up over the construction chamber's worktable, reading her own calibration notes with the focus of someone looking for a mistake she has already decided isn't there but is going to verify anyway, because that is the kind of person she is. Gepard brings her the food. He brings Bronya's up to the office after. He does these things in the same order he has been doing them for three days, with the same lack of ceremony, and neither of them comments on it, which by this point is its own kind of comment. The array is complete. It occupies the center of the construction chamber — a structure roughly two meters tall and one and a half wide, geomarrow rods arranged in a precise radial geometry around a central frequency guide, each rod angled at an interval Serval spent two days calculating to the decimal. The whole thing hums with the low, resonant charge of grade-three geomarrow in proximity to a Stellaron source, a sound that Gepard can feel in the back of his teeth if he stands close enough, and which he suspects is considerably more present to Bronya than it is to anyone else in the room. Pela is already there when he arrives at 0700, running diagnostics on the connection between the array's output nodes and the frequency guide at its center. She looks up. "Charge is holding at ninety-two percent. The rod alignment is within tolerance on all twelve points." She pauses. "Serval's work is very good." "Tell her that," he says. "She'll appreciate it." "I told her at 0400 when I came to check the overnight charge readings." A pause. "She said she knew." He almost smiles. At 0730, Bronya arrives. She comes into the construction chamber the way she has been coming into every difficult room lately — precisely, with intention, her hands behind her back and her expression composed. She is wearing her armor today, all seven pieces charged and connected, which is the first time Gepard has seen her in full armor since the night at the outpost three weeks ago. The geomarrow pieces hum softly, the orange light along their edges steady, and the array in the center of the room seems to respond to her proximity — the hum climbing a fraction, a barely perceptible shift in frequency that Serval clocks immediately and notes on her pad. "The array responds to you," Serval says, which is not a greeting but is, from Serval, a form of it. "The Stellaron does too." Bronya stops at the threshold of the safe observation distance Serval marked on the floor with chalk — two meters from the array's outer ring. "Everything geomarrow-adjacent responds to prolonged Stellaron exposure. The armor has been doing it for weeks." She looks at the array. "I assumed you'd accounted for it." "I did." Serval makes another note. "It actually helps with the calibration test. Your proximity will amplify the signal we're measuring, which means any deviation from 7.2 will be more audible to you, not less." She looks up. "Ready?" Bronya looks at the array. She looks at it with the expression she has been wearing around it since the first time she came down — that complex, unreadable thing that Gepard has been trying not to watch too closely and has been failing at. She takes a breath. "Yes," she says. "Gepard, Pela — behind the secondary line." Serval points to the chalk mark six meters back. He and Pela move to it. "I'm going to run the array at fifteen percent output. Enough to generate a measurable signal at the frequency guide without approaching disruption threshold." She looks at Bronya. "You tell me what you hear." "Understood." Serval activates the array. The hum in the room changes. It does not get louder so much as it gets deeper — more present, more dimensional, the kind of sound that stops being a thing you hear with your ears and becomes a thing you feel in your chest and the floor and the walls simultaneously. The geomarrow rods light up in sequence, the orange charge flowing outward from the central guide in a slow pulse, and the air between the array and Bronya does something that is not quite visible — a shimmer, the specific visual texture of active frequency interference, like heat haze in the cold. Bronya is very still. She is standing at exactly two meters from the outer ring, her hands still behind her back, her eyes on the central frequency guide. Her expression has changed — not dramatically, not in any way Gepard could describe to someone who hadn't spent years learning to read it. The composed look has not left. But underneath it, something has shifted — a quality of attention that is no longer directed outward. She is listening. Twenty seconds pass. Thirty. "It's close," she says. Her voice is very even. "The interval is close. But it's pulling left." Serval's pen moves. "Lower or higher frequency." "Lower. By—" A pause. She tilts her head, the listening posture, more pronounced now. "A small amount. Less than the tolerance you said." "Less than 0.05." "Yes." Serval makes an adjustment to the central guide — a fine-motor calibration, the kind that requires the specific steadiness of hands that know what they're doing. She looks up. "Again." The frequency shifts. Even Gepard can feel the difference — not in his ears, in somewhere behind his sternum, a slight change in the quality of the pressure, like a key finding its lock. Bronya goes very still. Stiller than before. The stillness of someone who has just heard something that reorganizes the room around it. "That's it," she says. And the way she says it — quietly, with a precision that has nothing performed in it, nothing composed — is the way she says things when she is not managing the room or her expression or the distance between what she feels and what she shows. He watches this from six meters away and files it in the place where he keeps the things that are not his to look at directly, and looks away. "Confirming," Serval says, and checks the frequency guide reading. She looks at it for a moment. She looks at Bronya. "7.23." "I thought you said 7.2," Pela says. "I did. 7.23 is within the 0.05 tolerance — it'll still disrupt rather than dampen." She is still looking at Bronya. "But 7.23 is what she heard. And 7.23 is what I'm going to set it to." A pause. "Because she's been listening to this thing for two months and I've been listening for three days, and I know which one of us I'd rather trust on a 0.03 adjustment." The room is quiet for a moment. Bronya looks at Serval. Something in her face moves — not quite surprise, not quite gratitude. Something that is not used to being trusted without explanation. "Serval," she says. "Don't." Serval turns back to the array. "It's basic empirical methodology. You have the better data source." She makes the adjustment. "Now everyone stay quiet, I need to run the full output test at twenty percent and I need to listen." —— The full output test at twenty percent fills the chamber with sound. Not sound exactly — the word doesn't hold it. More like the room becomes the sound, every surface of it participating, the geomarrow in the walls and ceiling resonating with the array's frequency the way a struck bell makes the air around it participate in the note. Gepard feels it in his armor, in his back teeth, in the base of his sternum. Pela presses one hand flat against the wall beside her, feeling the resonance move through the stone. Across the room, at two meters from the array, Bronya closes her eyes. He watches this happen and cannot stop himself from watching it and does not try very hard to. The armor light along her shoulders and arms intensifies — responding to the frequency, the geomarrow in it singing toward the array with the specific pull of resonant proximity. Her breathing is slow and measured. The cables connecting her pauldrons to her breastplate sway very slightly, a visible indicator of the vibration moving through the room. And the expression on her face— He looks away. He looks at the array output reading on Pela's diagnostic screen. He reads the numbers without retaining them. After forty seconds, Serval shuts the array down. The silence that follows is total and slightly stunning — the specific silence of a room that was, a moment ago, not silent at all. It takes several seconds for it to stop feeling like an absence and start feeling like a room again. "Output is consistent across all twelve rods," Serval says, into the quiet. "Frequency held at 7.23 for the full forty seconds with a deviation of 0.007 at the seventeen-second mark — within acceptable range." She looks at Pelo. "The array works." Pela exhales. It is a very small exhale, the kind that is trying not to be a sigh of relief and is not entirely successful. "Construction to full output will take how long." "At full output — one hundred percent — the disruption radius extends approximately sixty meters from the array's center point." Serval crosses to the worktable where her notes are. "The Stellaron's chamber is twenty-three meters below us. The broadcast propagates through the geomarrow in the palace foundation, which actually helps — the foundation acts as an amplifier." She pauses. "If we run the array at full output for approximately four minutes, it should fracture the feedback loop entirely." "Should," Gepard says. "The theory is sound. The calibration is verified. The materials are correct." She looks at him. "Should is the honest word. I'm not going to tell you it will work with the absolute certainty of someone who has done this before, because no one living has done this before." She pauses. "But I think it will work." "When can we run full output," Bronya says. She has opened her eyes. She is looking at Serval with an expression that is very calm and very focused, and she is holding one hand — her left — loosely at her side with a slightly different quality than usual, the kind of stillness that comes from a hand that was just trembling and has been deliberately stilled. It takes him a moment to notice this. It takes him another moment to decide not to look at it. "Tomorrow," Serval says. "I want twenty-four hours to verify the rod settings and do one more diagnostic sweep. And I want—" She pauses. She looks at Bronya. "I want you rested. You're going to be in the room at full output, and I don't know exactly what the disruption frequency is going to feel like at that proximity if you've been hearing the source signal for two months." Bronya opens her mouth. "Don't," Serval says. "Don't tell me you'll be fine. I know you'll be fine. I'm saying I want you rested so that being fine requires less effort on your part." She looks at her steadily. "Sleep tonight. Actually sleep. Not working-until-midnight sleep." A long pause. "Alright," Bronya says. Serval looks at Gepard. The look says: make sure she does. He looks back. The look says: I know. —— They walk out of the construction chamber together — Bronya and Gepard, Pela already ahead of them down the corridor toward her office, Serval staying behind to run her post-test diagnostic. The palace corridor at mid-morning has its usual ambient activity — aides passing with folders, the distant sound of the administrative wing going about its business, the pale northern light unchanged and indifferent through the high windows. He walks beside her, not behind her, which has become the default in corridors without rank present. She does not correct it. They are three steps from the stairwell when she says: "The hand." He waits. "You noticed," she says. It is not an accusation. "Yes." She is quiet for one more step. Then: "It was the full output test. The frequency at that proximity — it interacts with the Stellaron's signal in a way that is difficult to—" She pauses, choosing the word with care. "Contain." "Does it hurt." "No." A pause. "It's more like being very loud, inside." She says this to the corridor ahead of them, not to him. "Too much information at once. The signal, the disruption frequency, the resonance in the armor — it stacks." She pauses again. "I've been managing it." "I know you have." "For two months," she adds, which is not something she has said to him directly before — the specific weight of the duration, laid out plainly, without the composure that usually accompanies it. He says nothing for a moment. They reach the stairwell and start up, their footsteps echoing in the narrow space. He is aware of the distance between them — the single step of it, the kind of distance that is maintained by habit rather than intention. "Tomorrow," he says. "When the array runs at full output. I'll be in the room." She glances at him. A quick, sideways thing. "Serval said the secondary line—" "I'll be at the secondary line." A pause. "But I'll be in the room." She looks at the stairs ahead. She says nothing for three steps, which is not the same as not answering. "The observation post rotation reported two convergence attempts on the North ridge this morning," she says, which is not the answer and is also not not the answer. "Both suppressed before bloom density." "I saw the reports." "The clearance crews are making progress on the east wall saturation." "Faster than yesterday." "We might have fifteen days instead of fourteen." "Possibly." They reach the second floor landing. She stops. He stops. The corridor branches here — her office to the left, the route to the Sector access to the right. The morning light comes through the landing window at a low angle and sits on the floor between them, pale and precise. She looks at him. He looks at her. "Gepard," she says. He waits. She looks at him for a moment with the expression that only comes out past a certain hour, or in outposts, or in empty stairwells with no one else present — the one that does not have any composure requirement on it, that is just her face, at a distance she would not usually allow. "The cabinet," she says. "I know," he says. "You said not to read too much into it." "I said that," she agrees. A pause. She looks at the corridor to the left. She looks back at him. And then she says, very quietly, very precisely, the way she says things she has measured before saying: "The right side is better because those are the things I know you like." She pauses. "Not because I happened to know what was available." He holds this. He holds it very carefully, the way you hold something that might be exactly what you think it is, with both hands, and do not move. "Bronya—" "Don't," she says, which is not unkind. "I'm not—" She stops. Starts again. "I'm not saying anything. I'm just telling you the accurate version." A pause. "Because you always give me the accurate version. It seemed fair." He looks at her. She looks back. The morning light sits between them, and the corridor is empty, and somewhere two floors below them the array hums with 7.23 kilocycles and the shape of tomorrow's answer. "Alright," he says. She nods. She turns toward the left corridor. She takes two steps, and stops, and does not turn around. "Sleep before the evening patrol," she says. "That's an order." "Understood." She goes. He stands on the landing for a moment. He breathes once, carefully, through his nose. Then he takes the right corridor, and does not examine anything, and is only partially successful. —— The rest of day eleven passes in the way that days pass when the important thing is tomorrow — with a specific quality of controlled waiting, every task having the secondary function of filling the hours until the thing that matters. The clearance crews report good progress on the east wall saturation by evening. Pela sends an update confirming the array diagnostic is clean. Serval sends a message to Gepard that reads: she's asleep, I checked, you're welcome, and which he reads at the Sector B post at 2230 hours while charging his armor and eating something from the right side of the good cabinet that he carried down from the palace kitchen three hours ago and which is, in fact, better than anything on the left side. He does not examine this either. In the Underworld, Luka finishes his third tunnel walkthrough. The pulse in both synchronized corridors is slightly stronger than the previous measurement — not alarmingly so, but trending in the direction that things have been trending for eleven days. He photographs the seven-alpha-four wall, the same sheet of amber-black that has been slowly pressing outward since his first visit, and notices that it has moved. Not grown. Moved. The convexity has shifted two degrees clockwise from the last photograph, oriented now not toward the inhabited sectors but toward a specific point directly above — toward the palace's foundation. He stands in corridor seven-alpha-four and looks at this for a long time. Then he sends the photograph to Sampo. Sampo looks at it for thirty seconds. Then he forwards it to Gepard with a message that contains no jokes, which is how Gepard knows it is serious, because Sampo's messages always contain at least one joke. Gepard reads it at 2310 hours. He looks at the photograph. He looks at the directional shift. He looks at the timestamp on Luka's log — the orientation change happened sometime in the last eighteen hours, which means it happened after the calibration test at fifteen percent output. The array reached forty meters of output radius at twenty percent. The Stellaron's chamber is twenty-three meters below. The crystallization in the tunnels responded to the test. He calls Serval. She picks up on the first ring, which means she was also awake. "I saw it," she says, before he speaks. "The test signal." "Reached the tunnels, yes. Which means the disruption signal will too — that's actually good news, it confirms the foundation is conducting the frequency the way the theory predicts." A pause. "The reorientation toward the palace is the Fragmentum below responding to the signal. Looking for the source." Another pause. "It's going to know what we're doing when we run full output tomorrow." "Can it stop us." "Not if we run the array before it has time to respond." Her voice is even, the focus that comes from a problem she is already three steps into solving. "If we run the array at dawn — before the patrol gap window the Fragmentum has been probing, before it can coordinate with the surface convergence behavior—" "We take away its response time," he says. "Exactly." A pause. "Gepard. Wake Bronya." He is already putting his coat on. —— She opens the office door thirty seconds after he knocks, which means she was not asleep, which means Serval's message was aspirational. She is still dressed — minus the armor, which is on its charging frame by the wall — and she has the calculation sheet in her hand and the expression of someone who has been reading the same page and seeing different things in it for the past hour. She looks at him. She looks at his coat, the urgency of it. "The tunnels," she says. "The test signal reached them. The crystallization reoriented toward the palace foundation." He steps inside. "Serval thinks we move at dawn. Before the Fragmentum can coordinate a response." She is quiet for exactly two seconds. Then: "Is the array ready for full output." "Serval says yes." "The diagnostic—" "She ran it at 2300. It's clean." Two more seconds. "The clearance crews on the east wall—" "Pela can coordinate while we're in the chamber. The perimeter doesn't need both of us." She looks at him. He looks at her. In the low light of the office, with her armor on its frame and the calculation sheet in her hand and the fourteen-day clock sitting at three days remaining, she is looking at him with the expression that lives between the Commander and everything else, the one that does not have a clean name for what it is. "Dawn," she says. "Dawn," he says. She sets the calculation sheet down on the desk, precisely, and picks up her charging cable, and begins putting her armor on. He crosses to the charging frame and hands her the pauldron before she has to reach for it — a thing he has done before, in the outpost, in the dark, without discussion. She takes it. She does not say anything. But she does not pull away from the brief overlap of their hands before the transfer is complete, which lasts for perhaps one second, which is one second longer than necessary. He steps back. She connects the pauldron. "Serval will meet us at the chamber," she says. "I'll message her." "I'll take the diagnostic readings when we arrive. You coordinate with Pela on the perimeter." "Already drafted the message." She looks at him over her shoulder, the second pauldron half-attached. Something in her face does the thing — the small, involuntary, only-past-a-certain-hour thing — and she turns back to the armor before it finishes. "Of course you have," she says. He sends the messages. Outside, Belobog sleeps. The wall holds. The tunnels pulse in their synchronized rhythm, reoriented now, pressing gently and patiently toward the signal they have heard. In the construction chamber below, the array hums at 7.23 kilocycles, fully charged, waiting. Dawn is in four hours. —— Notes: 7.23. Not 7.2. Because she heard it and Serval trusted her and that is the calibration they're going with and I will not be accepting criticism on this point. The hand. She said it was like being very loud inside. Two months of very loud, managed carefully, not mentioned, and he noticed the hand trembling and looked away and waited for her to say it. He always waits. She always says it eventually. This is the thing about them that will not stop making me lose my mind. The stairwell. "The right side is better because those are the things I know you like." The accurate version.
