The wings open. —— Seele and Luka clear the approach corridor at 0646. They come out of corridor seven into the outer district courtyard and find it — changed. The presence-field has had nine minutes to saturate the district since the full emergence, and nine minutes is enough to produce something that neither of them has encountered outside the sub-levels: the ambient formations in the open air, thick enough to see, the crystallization shimmer visible between every surface, the generator stacks in the adjacent sections all broadcasting the elevated thermal signature of proximity-field activation. And in the center of it: Gepard. Standing in the courtyard. Ice shield active. Looking at a seventy-meter armored Stellaron that is currently in the process of opening its ninety-meter wingspan in his direct vicinity. "He's already in there," Seele says. "He was always going to be already in there," Luka says. "We've been clearing a corridor for seventeen minutes," she says. "He is First Class Singularity," Luka says. "He does not wait for cleared corridors." She looks at the Stellaron. At the wingspan. At the presence-field discharge scorch in the courtyard stone that is twelve meters across. She looks at Gepard's ice shield, which is holding the secondary fragment dispersal. She does the calculation the way she does calculations — fast, specific, no wasted components. "The right wing joint," she says. "There — the articulation point where the wing attaches to the primary shoulder armor. The plates have to flex there for the wing to open. That is the structural weakness." "From above," Luka says. "From above," she says. "The geometry from the ground means hitting the joint at less than effective angle. We need height." She looks at the generator stacks in the adjacent section — the ones still standing, the ones above the presence-field direct saturation zone. "The stack access ladders." He is already looking at the stacks. "Forty meters," he says. "We are still twenty-five meters below the joint from the top of the stack." "It's closer than the ground," she says. "The arm," he says. "If the arm dissolves corridor formations through the notation network—" "Can you reach the notation network from forty meters?" He looks at his arm. At the chrome surface, the segmented banding, the source material conducting its warmth at the integration points. "I don't know," he says. "Then we go up," she says, "and you find out." She is moving toward the generator stacks before the sentence finishes. He follows — and this time he is at the same speed, and neither of them has to run to keep up. —— Clara is conducting the notation network from the workshop floor and she is terrified. She would not say this out loud. She is twelve years old and she has been in the outer machine district for eight months and she has been in Svarog's workshop for six of those months and she has faced corridor formations and sub-level approach routes and a first contact event with an Architect-built network that runs through the bedrock of a city. She is not terrified of small things. She is terrified of large things. She is twelve years old. Large things are allowed to be terrifying when they are actually large. The source code is active in her hand — not the fragment, not the workshop equipment. The source code itself, Topaz's gift and the Architects' design, flowing through her hand into the notation network the way it did the first time she touched the workshop wall and felt the district breathe. She is conducting the source energy away from the breach point, dispersing it through the outer notation channels, keeping the Stellaron's field from feeding back into the primary structure below. This is what she is doing. This is what Svarog told her to do and she is doing it and it is working, she can feel it working — the channels warm and open and running in exactly the directions the Architects built them to run. But the form is out now. Fully. She can feel it through the floor — not the geological rumble of the emergence but the presence of it, seventy meters of ancient armored Stellaron above the district she lives in, and the presence is not like the second heartbeat's warmth. It is immense and old and awake and not currently paying attention to Clara but not currently needing to pay attention to Clara because Clara is twelve meters below a generator floor conducting source energy through notation channels and is not currently in the form's immediate awareness. The Moles are beside her. This is not supposed to be happening. The Moles were evacuated with the residential sections. The Moles, however, have been navigating the outer machine district's secondary tunnels for two years and have a specific understanding of which evacuation routes double as re-entry routes if you know the junction layout, and so when Oleg arrived at the workshop twenty-two minutes ago from a direction that was very specifically not any of the official evacuation directions, followed immediately by Toma and then by Pino, Clara had looked at them and said — "You were evacuated," she said. "Yes," Oleg said. "And you came back," she said. "Yes," Oleg said. "Why," she said. "You're in here," Toma said. "By yourself." Clara had looked at them. At Toma's expression, which was the one that meant the decision was already made and the explanation was being provided retroactively. At Oleg, who had already sat down on the floor beside the wall and taken something out of his jacket. At Pino, who was reading the notation display on the secondary monitoring wall with the expression of someone learning how it works. "If Svarog asks," Clara said, "you were always here." "We were always here," Oleg agreed. "The notation network has a secondary channel in the northwest section," Clara said. "Pino — can you hold that channel open? It's simple. You just — keep your hand on the wall here and don't move it." "Simple," Pino said, and put his hand on the wall. That was twenty minutes ago. Pino has not moved his hand from the northwest channel. Toma is at the secondary sensor display reading the presence-field density changes in real time. Oleg is on the floor eating something from his jacket and providing periodic updates on what he is seeing through the ground-level window that faces the courtyard, which is the window that has the best angle on the battle that is developing in the outer machine district's center. "Big one is looking at the Captain," Oleg says. "And its wings are doing the opening thing." "Noted," Clara says. "The Captain is standing there," Oleg says. "Also noted," Clara says. "He is just — standing there," Oleg says. "In front of the big one's wings. Just. Standing there." "He is the strongest person on this planet," Clara says, with the specific patience of someone conducting a planetary-scale operation while also receiving live commentary. "Please continue monitoring and tell me if anything changes near the building." "The arm person and Seele are going up the generator stack," Oleg says. "Which stack?" "The south one. The tall one." Clara processes this. The south stack is forty meters. The south stack's base is twelve meters from the notation channel's primary junction in the outer section. If Luka uses the arm at the top of the south stack — "Pino," she says. "Yes?" Pino says, hand still on the wall. "The northwest channel. I need you to open it wider — not more energy, more width. Can you do that? It should feel like you're — like you're making a hallway bigger without changing how much air is in it." Pino looks at his hand. He looks at the notation display. He does the thing that Pino does when he is taking a technical instruction seriously, which involves his face going very still and his attention going very complete. "I think so," he says. "Give me thirty seconds." "Thirty seconds," Clara says. From outside: the sound of the Stellaron's presence-field discharge. A massive concussive sound. Then the specific sound of ice forming rapidly — large, structural, not the corridor variety. "The Captain made a wall," Oleg says. "How big?" Clara says. Oleg looks out the window. He is very quiet for two seconds. "Very," he says. —— The ice wall is sixteen meters tall and forty meters wide and it has stopped the second presence-field discharge. Not comfortably. The wall is cracking — not at the surface but at the molecular level, the Stellaron's discharge energy working through the ice structure the way heat works through stone. Gepard can feel the rate of degradation through the wall the way he can feel the ice he produced on the northern ridge in the storm, in the places where the ice is telling him how much time it has before it gives. He has four minutes before this wall fails. He has already moved. He is forty meters to the west of the wall's position, which is forty meters from where the Stellaron last had him tracked. The Stellaron is in the process of reorienting — the head moving, the golden armor articulating at every joint as the form brings its attention back around to the empty courtyard and begins reading for where he has gone. It is not fast. Seventy meters of Stellaron form is not fast in the sense of small-thing fast. But the scale of it means that not-fast still produces a coverage rate that makes hiding in an empty courtyard a temporary proposition. He looks at the wing joint. Seele and Luka are at the generator stack — he can see them at the top of the south stack from his current position, two figures forty meters up, the chrome arm visible from this distance as a specific quality of reflected light. He does not know what they are planning. He knows Seele and he knows that whatever she is planning from forty meters above the courtyard is specific and correct. He needs the Stellaron's attention on him. He steps out of the cover of the generator housing and he looks directly at the Stellaron. The Stellaron's head completes its sweep and finds him. Good. He brings the ice up — not the wall, not the shield. The lance. The concentrated burst that the northern ridge produced the specific need for: the ice compression that takes the temperature of his immediate field to the minimum sustainable for conversion and focuses the output to a single forward point. Not dispersed, not defensive. A single expression of ice temperature into a forward-moving projectile at the maximum compression rate his current state can produce. He fires it at the Stellaron's right eye. He is not trying to destroy the eye. He is trying to hurt it. There is a meaningful difference. He is trying to produce the specific physical response in a seventy-meter armored form that causes a seventy-meter armored form to close its right eye and swing its head toward the pain source, which will turn the head to the left, which will expose the right wing joint to the angle that two people forty meters up a generator stack need it exposed to. The lance hits the right eye. The Stellaron does not roar. It does something worse than roar — it produces the sound that is part geological and part frequency and part neither, the sound that cracked the district's windows at full emergence, and it swings its head to the right which is the wrong direction and brings the full weight of its armor-plated neck around in a lateral sweep that crosses the courtyard at the level of Gepard's position. He is already under it. Not significantly under it — the neck sweep passes three meters above his current position and the pressure wave from the displacement hits him like a wall. He hits the ground, rolls, comes up. His coat is gone. He does not address this. He looks at the wing joint. The head sweep turned the form to the right. The right wing joint is now at a different angle — not the one Seele needs, something worse from her position but something he can work with from his position. "SEELE!" he calls, at full volume, across the outer machine district courtyard. From the top of the generator stack, forty meters up: "WHAT?" "CHANGE OF PLAN!" he calls. "LEFT WING JOINT! I HAVE THE ANGLE FROM HERE!" A pause. "THAT IS A WORSE ANGLE FOR US—" "I KNOW! LEFT WING JOINT!" Another pause. Then: "WE NEED THIRTY SECONDS!" "THIRTY SECONDS!" he agrees, and turns back to the Stellaron, which has completed its reorientation and is looking at him again with the specific quality of something that has decided this small thing is worth continued attention. He has thirty seconds to hold its attention. He raises both hands. The ice comes up. —— "Thirty seconds," Seele says, and she is already moving to the left edge of the stack platform. The left wing joint from their current position is worse than the right was. The angle is forty degrees off the ideal, which means the arc from the scythe has to account for the angle difference, which means she needs to be off the stack and in the air for part of the approach to hit the joint at sufficient force. "You're going to jump," Luka says. "I'm going to jump," she says. "From forty meters," he says. "The wing is extended," she says. "The joint is fifty meters from the stack top. I can clear the distance." She measures it visually — the specific visual measurement of someone who has been running the outer machine district's secondary tunnels since she was seven and has a finely calibrated internal estimate of distance. "I can clear it if I go off this edge." "And if you miss—" "I won't miss," she says. "Seele—" "I WON'T MISS," she says, very clearly. She is already at the edge. "Hold the stack for the first fifteen seconds. Gepard is buying time. I need you at the base junction of the south stack in fifteen seconds — the notation network channel junction that Clara widened. I need you to hit the notation channel through the stack base at the same time I hit the wing joint." He looks at her. At the edge. At the fifty-meter gap between the stack and the Stellaron's left wing joint. He looks at the wing joint — the golden armor plates articulating against each other as the wing holds its extension, the gap between plates at the articulation point visible from this distance as a darkness in the gold. "Fifteen seconds from now," he says. "Fourteen," she says. "GO." He goes. She looks at the wing joint. She looks at the gap between the plates. At the angle. At the distance. She thinks about the tunnel drill — the geometry practice, the specific arc that the chamber's height allowed. The arc that is not the blade's language but the scythe's language. The arc that does not telegraph its intention in the wind-up. She thinks about the second heartbeat. About the warmth. About Vera's endurance in the stone and in Luka's arm. She does not think about the forty meters of air between her and the wing joint. She jumps. —— The jump is — something. Not the trajectory of a fighter, not the fall of a person who has misjudged a distance. The butterfly quality, the thing that has been building in her since the primary structure's warmth moved through her in the depression, the thing Natasha noted in the fracture recovery and Serval noted in the recorder data and that neither of them has found the full vocabulary for yet. In the forty-meter gap between the generator stack and the Stellaron's left wing joint, something in Seele's movement shifts. Not flight. Not the Herrscher-class expression of what she is growing into — that is not here yet, that is the future's version of her. This is the edge of it. The specific butterfly quality of someone who is in motion and whose motion is not quite subject to the same trajectory physics as the motion of someone who is not what she is becoming. She covers fifty meters of air. She hits the wing joint with the full downward drive of the scythe — both hands on the handle, the blade going into the gap between the articulation plates at the joint with the force of forty meters of approach and the specific angled geometry that makes the joint's structural weakness available to the weapon. The blade drives into the joint. The joint screams — not a mechanical screaming, not the sound of metal on metal. The sound of a Stellaron's armored form receiving a blow that has reached the interior. The form shudders. The entire seventy meters of it — a single full-body shudder, the armor plates rattling against each other, the wings losing their extension and the left wing beginning to fold under the pressure of the joint damage. The golden form sways. Not falls. Sways. But seventy meters swaying is a significant event in any city district. Seele's momentum carries her past the joint and she grabs the wing's leading edge with her free hand — chrome of the spinal artifact's material was not an accident, Svarog's design was deliberate, she is warm enough at the contact point for the wing surface to receive her like something it recognizes rather than something to shake loose — and she swings along the wing's length, uses the momentum to redirect, drops the fifteen meters from the wing's leading edge to the generator stack roof below. She hits the roof. Rolls. Comes up with the scythe. The joint is damaged. Not destroyed — Stellaron-grade armor does not destroy in one blow, not even a blow from someone who just covered fifty meters of air and drove a mechanical scythe into the articulation gap. But damaged. The wing is compromised. The form knows it and is responding to the knowledge. It roars. Not the geological-frequency sound. The real roar — the sound of a seventy-meter form that has just received injury and is orienting its full attention toward the source of the injury. Seele looks up at the form looking down at her. "Oh," she says. The form raises the right foreleg. —— The notation channel at the south stack's base junction opens wide at second thirteen. Clara said wider — not more energy, more width. Pino held the northwest channel open and Clara conducted the primary flow through it, and the result is that the notation channel at the south stack's base is now running at a width that is significantly above its standard configuration, the Architects' network expanded in the specific way that the source code allows when the right hand is on the right wall at the right moment. Luka puts his chrome hand on the south stack's base junction at second fourteen. The notation channel receives the arm's source material. The arm receives the notation channel. The two systems find each other the way they found each other in the corridor — the artifact material and the Architects' design speaking the same language, the network and the arm integrating through the contact point — and this time the channel is wide, this time the source energy running through it is not the corridor's ambient level but the full working flow of the primary notation network with Pino's northwest extension adding the additional width Clara needed. The presence-field formations in the immediate area of the south stack — every one of them, in a radius of thirty meters — dissolve. Thirty meters. Not eleven junctions. Thirty meters in every direction from the south stack, the Fragmentum crystallization receding from the surfaces, the air shimmer gone, the formation density dropping from saturation to baseline in four seconds. The south stack's immediate area is clear. "CLARA!" Luka shouts. "I SEE IT!" Clara shouts from inside the workshop, which is technically inside the thirty-meter clearing and therefore now has unobstructed sight lines to the courtyard. "THE NORTHEAST CHANNEL — CAN YOU HIT THE NORTHEAST CHANNEL FROM THE EAST STACK? THE PRIMARY JUNCTION IS AT THE BASE!" He looks at the east stack. Twenty-five meters from his current position. The presence-field between him and it is no longer the corridor-formation density but it is still active — the Stellaron's presence at seventy meters is not something thirty meters of clearing fully resolves. Then the form roars. He looks up. Seele is on the generator stack roof. The form's right foreleg is rising. The foreleg alone is the size of a building section. He does not think about the foreleg. He does not think about the clearing he just made or the channel he just found or the twenty-five meters to the east stack. He does not think about any of it. He runs. —— Gepard hits the Stellaron's right eye with the second lance at second twenty-six. He is out of thirty seconds by four seconds — the courtyard engagement has produced eleven ice structures in thirty seconds, which is a rate he has not operated at since the classified northern ridge debrief, and the structures have held the form's attention through the compound approach. The form has hit three of the structures and destroyed all three. The courtyard stone where the structures were is fused. The form's eye is watering — which is not a metaphor, seventy-meter forms have the physiological responses of animals and a lance to the eye at maximum compression produces tears, which produces a minor reduction in targeting precision, which has been the point. The second lance hits the right eye and the form throws its head back and the roar produces a pressure wave that crosses the courtyard and hits Gepard and he is already behind the generator housing at the southwest corner but the pressure wave finds him anyway and he is on one knee behind the housing when it passes. He gets up. He looks at the form. At the left wing joint — Seele hit it, he saw her hit it, he saw the shudder go through the form and the wing begin to fold. He looks at the right foreleg, which is rising. He looks at Seele on the generator stack roof. He has been at distance this entire engagement. Distance management, the Restricted Zone's primary lesson — know what can and cannot be addressed at range, address at range everything that can be, close only when closing is the only available option. He closes. Not at a run — at full expression. The speed that the First Class Singularity of the Silvermane Guard produces when speed is the correct variable, when the ice is directing the thermal differential toward propulsion rather than structure, the specific application that Dunn has seen twice in six years and described to a junior officer once as it is not a run, it is a different category of movement. He covers the courtyard in four seconds. He hits the right foreleg at the ankle joint — the same logic as the wing joint, the articulation point, the place where the armor has to allow movement and the plates have to flex. Not with ice this time. With physical force — the full weight of his body and momentum and the eight years of ice expression he has built into the structure of his impact, the defensive capability that has always been also the strongest offensive expression, because defense at the level of the First Class Singularity means that what he is made of is what the impact produces. The ankle joint of the right foreleg of the second Stellaron of Belobog takes the full force of Gepard Landau moving at a speed that is a different category from running. The foreleg buckles. The right foreleg of the Stellaron buckles at the ankle, the form dropping fifteen meters on its right side as the joint gives under the impact, and the right foreleg that was raised and aimed at Seele's position sweeps wide — wide, away from the stack, away from her — and crashes into the empty residential section three hundred meters east. The sound is the geological and frequency sound again, but louder. Closer. With the specific addition of a Stellaron that has just lost the use of one foreleg and is in the process of understanding what that means for its mobility. Gepard is on the courtyard stone, on his hands and knees, from the recoil of the impact. He breathes. He gets up. He looks at the form. The form looks at him. For the second time in this engagement, the second Stellaron of Belobog looks at Gepard Landau from a height of seventy meters and has the specific quality of something that is reevaluating its assessment of what it is looking at. "Yes," Gepard says, at conversational volume, to the seventy-meter form that is looking at him with new attention. "I know." From above, from the south generator stack, Seele's voice: "THAT WAS THE MOST EXTRAORDINARY THING I HAVE EVER—" "FOCUS!" Gepard calls. "I AM FOCUSED!" she calls back. From the east, from the direction of the east generator stack: a sound that is the notation network's field-clearing effect, thirty meters of Fragmentum crystallization dissolving simultaneously, the warm glow of source energy running through the district's geological structure visible at every notation-channel junction in the adjacent section. Luka has reached the east stack. The Stellaron, still assessing the rebalancing required to operate with a compromised right foreleg, turns toward the new signal. "Eyes on me!" Gepard says. He brings the ice up again. The Stellaron looks at him. And above the courtyard, on two generator stacks forty meters up, two people are getting ready for the second approach. —— The battle of the outer machine district has been running for eleven minutes when Bronya arrives at the district boundary. She does not arrive with the Silvermane Guard escort that Gepard's standing protocol requires for the Supreme Guardian in an active engagement zone. She arrives with Pela, who has been running the first Stellaron's real-time signal data since 0603 and who is holding the monitoring tablet with the focused intensity of someone who is watching several things simultaneously and has opinions about all of them. "Signal," Bronya says. "3.1," Pela says. "Holding. It has not climbed since 0604." She pauses. "It is attending — I mean this in the specific sense of the signal quality changing to active monitoring, which is different from dormant. The first Stellaron is listening to the battle. But it is not responding to it." "Not yet," Bronya says. "Not yet," Pela agrees. Bronya looks at the outer machine district. At the golden form visible above the generator stacks — the seventy-meter scale of it, the ninety-meter wingspan partially folded now on the left side, the form listing slightly to the right from the compromised foreleg. She looks at the presence-field formations in the surrounding district — the shimmer in the air, the crystallization on every surface within four hundred meters. She looks at Dunn's three Guard perimeter lines, holding the broader district boundary, the Wildfire medical units staged at the evacuation exits. She looks at Svarog. Svarog is still at the form's right flank — not twelve meters now, eight. Four arms active, the full combat array running, the oldest consciousness on the planet in active engagement with a Stellaron that is four times his physical height and has been attempting to address him since the full emergence and has not succeeded. This is what second on the planetary power hierarchy means, when the hierarchy is relevant. She looks at the form's position. At the compromised foreleg. At the left wing joint. She reaches for the first Stellaron's signal in the specific way she reaches for it — not the instruments, the attunement. The thing below instrument level. The first Stellaron at 3.1, attending, the quality of it like a large and patient thing watching a situation develop. She listens. And then she crosses the district boundary and goes in. Pela says: "Supreme Guardian—" "Monitor the signal," Bronya says, without looking back. "Tell me the moment it moves." "Guardian—" Pela's voice. The specific voice of a sixteen-year-old who has been given an instruction she is going to follow and who wants to register her objection to the instruction first. "Please be careful." Bronya walks into the outer machine district. "I always am," she says. —— The second Stellaron, at 0701, does something that none of Svarog's preliminary models predicted. It generates. Not the ambient presence-field formations — those have been continuous since the full emergence. Not the directed discharge from the form itself. The second Stellaron generates a formation — a primary-structure class form, the class that exists only in proximity to a Stellaron's direct field — from the space directly around its body. Not the ground, not the walls. The air. Three of them. Primary-structure class, each twelve meters, generating in the air around the Stellaron's body at a distance of twenty meters from the armor. Not attached to the form. Free-moving, in the air, each one tracking the heat signatures of the three fighters in the district below with the specific targeting behavior of something that has been directed to a purpose. "Svarog," Gepard says, from the courtyard, looking at the three aerial forms. His voice is entirely level. "Are those new?" "Yes," Svarog says, from the form's right flank. He is looking at the aerial forms with the specific quality of someone revising a model in real time. "I did not anticipate the aerial generation capability." "Can you address them?" "I can address the two on the right," Svarog says. "The left-flank aerial form is beyond my current engagement angle without abandoning the main flank position." "I have the left-flank form," Seele calls from the south stack. She has climbed back up — the first approach on the wing joint damaged it significantly, and she is not done with it. "LUKA — the east stack, the far side — the left-flank aerial form is at your angle from there—" "I'M AT THE EAST NOTATION CHANNEL," Luka calls back. "I CANNOT LEAVE THE CHANNEL—" "THE CHANNEL WILL HOLD FOR THIRTY SECONDS—" "WE DON'T KNOW THAT—" "I KNOW THAT," Clara says, from inside the workshop, loud enough to be heard through the wall. "THE CHANNEL WILL HOLD FOR NINETY SECONDS WITHOUT CONTACT! I JUST RAN THE CALCULATION!" A pause. Then Luka: "NINETY SECONDS?" "NINETY!" Clara says. Another pause. Then: "SEELE—" "GO!" Seele says. From the east stack, the sound of movement. —— The left-flank aerial form is at thirty meters altitude when Luka reaches the east stack's east edge. He looks at it. Twelve meters of primary-structure class formation in the air, generated from a Stellaron's direct field, not touching any surface, tracking the heat signatures below with the slow terrible accuracy of something that does not need to be fast because it is between its targets and the sky. He looks at his arm. He looks at the form. He looks at the distance between the stack edge and the form's position, which is forty meters horizontally and ten meters down from his current elevation. He calculates. Not whether. How. The notation channel runs through the geological structure of the outer machine district. The geological structure includes the generator stacks — the stacks are bolted to the bedrock, the bolts are mineral, the mineral is connected to the notation network at the channel junctions. He is standing on a generator stack that is connected to the notation network at its base. He is standing on a conductor. He puts his chrome hand flat against the stack surface. The channel is ninety seconds from its last contact charge. Clara said ninety seconds. He is eleven seconds past the break. The channel is seventy-nine seconds full. He does not have seventy-nine seconds. The aerial form is moving. He has twelve. He conducts everything the channel has in twelve seconds through the stack surface into the arm and out through the arm into — the air. The notation network does not run through the air. The notation network runs through mineral geological structure. The Architects built it through stone and crystal and the mineral structure of the outer machine district's foundation. It does not run through the air. Except. The Fragmentum crystallization — the ambient formation shimmer visible in the air of the outer machine district since the Stellaron's emergence — is mineral. The crystallization is a mineral structure grown from the Stellaron's presence-field. And the arm, conducting source energy from the notation network, is producing a field that the Fragmentum crystallization in the air responds to the same way the corridor formations responded to it. The air-shimmer between the east stack and the left-flank aerial form conducts. The formation dissolves. Twelve meters of primary-structure class formation, generated directly from the Stellaron's field, receiving the notation network's source energy through the Fragmentum shimmer in the air between them, and dissolving the way the corridor formations dissolved — not shattering, not exploding. Resorbing. The crystallization pulling back into the ambient shimmer and dispersing. Twelve seconds. Luka takes his hand off the stack. He breathes. Below him, in the courtyard, Gepard's voice: "Confirmed. Left-flank aerial clear." On the south stack: "WHAT DID YOU DO," Seele says. "I conducted through the Fragmentum shimmer," he says. "You conducted through the—" She stops. "That should not be possible. The shimmer is not a notation channel—" "It is a mineral structure," he says. "The network runs through mineral. The shimmer is mineral." Silence from the south stack. "Seele," he says. "I'm fine," she says. "I am standing here being perfectly fine about the fact that you just used the Fragmentum itself as a conductor for the notation network and it worked." A pause. "I am completely fine about it." "Good," he says. "I am NOT FINE about it," she says, at significant volume. "That was—" "Extraordinary?" he says. From below, from Gepard's direction, a sound that is very controlled and is very much not a laugh at all. —— The battle continues. Svarog addresses the two right-flank aerial forms with the four-arm configuration that he developed in the year that the Separation ended and the outer machine district needed something to maintain the perimeter against the first Fragmentum emergence from the bedrock. The forms dissolve under the specific force of what Svarog is, which is not a combat style anyone on Belobog has a classification for because the classification system predates Svarog's current capacity by several centuries. Gepard holds the courtyard. He has been holding the courtyard for thirty-seven minutes. The ice structures have numbered seventeen. Three are still standing — the rest failed under the form's presence-field discharges and the direct foreleg swipes that the form has begun attempting as a secondary addressing mode. He is at sixty percent, which is the Restricted Zone's threshold for the specific assessment of personal resource management that the northern ridge taught him: above sixty percent, full expression available. Below sixty percent, selection. He is selecting. The lance is the primary expression. The shield comes up when the discharge is aimed. The direct impact — the impact that buckled the right foreleg's ankle joint — is held in reserve because it is the most expensive expression and the joint has not recovered sufficiently to require a second such impact yet. He is selecting, and the form is tiring. Not collapsing — tiring. The left wing joint is damaged and the wing is partially folded. The right foreleg is compromised and the form is listing. The three aerial forms have been addressed. The form is still seventy meters tall and still producing presence-field discharges and still generating ambient formations in the surrounding district, but its rate is lower than the 0638 emergence peak and the rate is trending down. Svarog sees this. "The generation rate is declining," he says, from the right flank. "The form is spending internal resources," Gepard says, from the courtyard. "The engagement has been going for thirty-seven minutes. It has been dormant for twenty-two years. Its resource base is not infinite." "No," Svarog says. "It is not." "How long?" Gepard says. Svarog is quiet for a moment. The oldest consciousness on the planet running the full calculation. "If the engagement rate is maintained," he says, "and if the structural damage to the joint and foreleg is supplemented by two to three additional significant impacts — perhaps forty minutes. Perhaps more. Perhaps less." "Forty minutes," Gepard says. "Approximately," Svarog says. Gepard looks at the form. At sixty percent. Forty minutes at selection rate. He does the math. "Then we maintain," he says. "Yes," Svarog says. From above: two simultaneous impacts. Seele on the south stack approaching the left wing joint for the second time, the approach angle corrected for the form's current position, the scythe driving into the articulation gap with the full velocity of someone who has cleared fifty meters of air. And from the east stack, simultaneously, the notation-network-through-shimmer discharge from the chrome arm hitting the form's left shoulder armor at the joint between the shoulder plate and the neck plate — the crystallization in that joint dissolving from the source material's contact, the joint losing its structural integrity, the neck's range of motion suddenly limited on the left side. The form shudders. Again. Deeper this time. It makes the sound. Below, in the courtyard, Gepard raises the ice again. Forty minutes. They maintain. —— At 0712, Bronya reaches the center of the outer machine district and stands in the presence of the second Stellaron. Not in the battle path — to the east of the courtyard, in the clear lane that the notation-network field has produced around the east generator stack. The Fragmentum shimmer is thin here, the source energy conducting through the district's geological structure keeping the immediate area clear. She can see the full form from here — the compromised right foreleg, the partially folded left wing, the ongoing engagement with three fighters and one ancient machine in the courtyard and on the stacks. She can see Gepard in the courtyard. She watches him for three seconds. He does not see her — he is looking at the form. She listens for the first Stellaron. 3.1, attending. Watching. The quality of the large and patient thing that has been waiting for a long time for the specific development that will tell it the time has come to do what it has been building toward. It is listening to this battle. It is watching what Belobog is capable of. It is — interested, in the specific way of something that has a purpose that is not yet fully legible. She does not know what the first Stellaron's purpose is. She does not know what it is holding, what it has been building toward, what the gap between 3.1 and 4.7 contains. She knows it is watching her city fight. She knows it is listening to what Belobog does when confronted with something that should not be survivable. She looks at the second Stellaron. At the golden armor. At the ancient form that has been below her city since before the city existed, holding two children's memories in the specific stillness of something that keeps a promise regardless of the cost of keeping it. She says, quietly, in the specific way she says things she means completely: "Thank you for keeping them safe." The second Stellaron — mid-engagement, mid-listing, the right foreleg compromised and the left wing joint damaged — turns its head. It looks at her. Not the threat-assessment look it has been directing at the fighters. Not the presence-field-discharge look. Something else. The look of something that has been recognized, by the right person, for the right reason. Then it turns back to the battle. Bronya looks at the form for one more second. Then she takes the monitoring tablet from her coat — the first Stellaron's signal running in real time, 3.1 and holding — and she begins to plan. —— At 0723, the Stellaron generates its second wave of aerial forms. Six this time. They are not primary-structure class. They are — larger. Not a Fragmentum classification that exists in Pela's documents. Not the class from the sub-levels. Something that the Stellaron is producing from deeper reserves than the first wave — something that the twenty-two years of primary-field contact and the four minutes and eleven seconds that made it what it is have allowed it to hold in reserve for the engagement's second stage. Six of them. At forty meters altitude. Each eighteen meters. The outer machine district, which has been at absolute peril for forty-five minutes, has just become something else. Svarog looks up at the six aerial forms. Gepard looks up at the six aerial forms. On the south stack, Seele looks up. On the east stack, Luka looks up. There is a moment — three seconds — where nobody says anything. The six aerial forms are tracking. The generation rate has dropped significantly from the first wave's rate, which means the Stellaron is spending its reserve, which means this is the form telling them: this is what I have left, and I am using it. Which means the end of the battle is closer than it was. Which also means the next forty minutes are going to be considerably more difficult than the last forty-five. Seele says: "Well." Gepard says: "Svarog." "The three on the right," Svarog says. "The three on the left," Gepard says. "I have the one directly above the east stack," Luka says. "I have the two at angles from the south stack," Seele says. "That's six," Clara says, from inside the workshop, who has been monitoring the battle through the sensor data and the ground-level window and Oleg's live commentary for forty-seven minutes. "That is actually six. You have them all." "We have them all," Gepard confirms. He looks at the six forms. At the positions. At the angles. At the engagement that is about to become significantly more complicated. He is at fifty-three percent. He looks at the Stellaron. At the golden armor. At the ancient form that is currently spending its reserve. The end is close. They have to reach it. "Together," he says — not loudly, the word is not for the Stellaron, it is for the three people in the district who have been in this with him — and he raises the ice. The battle of the outer machine district enters its second stage. END OF CHAPTER 41 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 42: The Armor Breaks Chapter Text The six aerial forms are not the same as the first wave. The first wave — three primary-structure class forms at twelve meters, generated from the Stellaron's ambient field — those were the form's opening offer. A statement of capability. A declaration that the seventy-meter golden armored thing in the center of the outer machine district had resources it had not yet deployed and was choosing, in this moment, to deploy one portion of them. The second wave is not an offer. The second wave is eighteen meters per form, and the generation quality is different — denser crystallization, a different amber-to-black color ratio in the structure that means higher Fragmentum concentration, the articulation of the individual forms tighter and more purposeful than the broad sweeping presence-field forms of the first wave. These are forms that the Stellaron has produced from a deeper reserve, from the twenty-two years of primary-field proximity that the second Stellaron's dormancy accumulated while it held what it held. And they are not tracking the three fighters. They are tracking Bronya. —— She notices this at 0726. She has been at the east edge of the cleared notation-channel zone, monitoring the first Stellaron's signal on the tablet, watching the battle from the position that gives her the best sight lines to all four fighters without being in the direct engagement path. She has been watching Gepard hold the courtyard. She has been watching Seele's second approach on the left wing joint — the butterfly quality in the air between the south stack and the form, the movement that is not quite standard trajectory and that Bronya has been noting carefully because the specific quality of it is a thing she recognizes from somewhere old and important and she cannot place it yet. She has been watching the six aerial forms generate and the battle enter its second stage. And then she checks her position in relation to the six aerial forms and understands. Four of them are tracking the fighters. Standard distribution — the forms orienting toward the highest-output heat signatures in the district. Two of them are tracking Bronya. Not because she is a higher output than Gepard. She is not. She is considerably lower. Because the second Stellaron, which has been watching this district for twenty-two years and has spent the last four months doing something that amounts to recognizing two children it lost track of, has the specific attunement to the people it held in its memory that a Stellaron developed over decades of primary-field proximity possesses. And Bronya, who carries the first Stellaron's attunement in the way she has been carrying it since the construction chamber — who has been listening to the first Stellaron the way a person listens to something they have known a long time — reads to the second Stellaron as something significant. Not one of the two children. But adjacent to the thing it has been protecting. The Guardian. Bronya looks at the two aerial forms tracking her. She looks at the monitoring tablet. 3.1, holding. She looks at the two eighteen-meter forms, denser than the first wave, articulated and purposeful and currently twenty meters away and closing. She puts the tablet in her coat. She unslings the rifle from her back — the Guardian's rifle, the specific equipment that Bronya Rand inherited along with the title and the Stellaron and the responsibility and the specific weight of being twenty-three years old with the fate of a planet in her hands — and she holds it and she looks at the two forms. Then she looks at the Stellaron itself. "Not today," she says. She raises the rifle. —— The shot from the Guardian's rifle hits the left aerial form at the articulation point that is the same articulation weakness as all crystalline Fragmentum structures — the base junction, where the crystallization density is highest and therefore most brittle under concentrated directed force. The shot does not shatter the form. The Guardian's rifle is not a weapon designed to shatter eighteen-meter aerial Fragmentum forms, which were not a classified entity class until four minutes ago. What it does is crack the base junction. The left aerial form, with a cracked base junction, loses its tracking capability. The articulation through which it orients its targeting drops from the precise purposeful motion of the second wave to the broader less-controlled sweep of a form that is working around structural damage. It does not stop coming. But it is coming less accurately. Bronya has three seconds while it recalibrates. She uses them. She reaches for the first Stellaron — not the instruments, the attunement. The thing she has had since the construction chamber and which has been developing through nineteen days of listening and which she does not have a full vocabulary for yet but which has, in the last forty-seven minutes of standing in the presence of a fully manifested second Stellaron, been opening into something that is more than a listening. The first Stellaron is at 3.1, attending. She touches the edge of that attending — not the full contact of the construction chamber, not the direct interface that the Stellaron provides to the Guardian. The edge. The outermost layer of the first Stellaron's ambient output, which at 3.1 is considerably more present than it was at 2.4, and which in the presence of an active second Stellaron is active enough to be felt through the geological structure of the district the way a sound can be felt through a wall before it can be heard. She does not direct it. She does not channel it. She receives it — the specific attending quality of the first Stellaron, the large and patient thing that has been waiting for a very long time for the development that tells it the time has come — and she lets it flow through her hands and into the rifle and she fires. The second shot hits the right aerial form's base junction. The crack is larger. The form goes from purposeful to damaged in two seconds. Both aerial forms that were tracking her are now tracking her less accurately, their base junction integrity compromised, their articulation systems working around structural damage that the Guardian's rifle produced with shots that should not have been able to crack eighteen-meter second-wave aerial forms. Bronya looks at the rifle. She looks at her hands. She looks at the two compromised forms. Then she looks at the outer machine district — at the Silvermane Guard perimeter lines, at the Wildfire medical units that are currently doing considerably more than medical response because the presence-field formations that have been generating in the surrounding four blocks are no longer staying at the standard corridor class but are beginning to produce junction-level forms in the streets between the residential sections and the outer district boundary, the forms threatening the evacuation routes. She looks at Dunn's second perimeter line, which is holding but is holding harder than it was at 0638 and which has, in the last six minutes, contracted two hundred meters toward the district center under the pressure of the junction-level forms. She goes to the second perimeter line. —— Dunn is at the second perimeter line when Bronya arrives. He is in the process of directing three Guard units to hold the western junction against a formation cluster that has grown significantly in the last four minutes — the second Stellaron's second wave generating a field-intensification in the surrounding area that is pushing the ambient formations from corridor class into something between corridor and sub-level class, the formations in the open streets of the outer machine district's adjacent sections larger and denser than anything the Guard perimeter was briefed to expect. He sees Bronya coming from the district center. He says: "Supreme Guardian—" "Status," she says. He runs it quickly. "Western junction — formation cluster growing above briefed parameters. The perimeter has contracted two hundred meters in six minutes. The eastern access route — still clear, the evacuation is holding, but the formation density on the north side of the route is increasing." He pauses. "We have three Guard units managing the north side of the route alone. If the density increases further—" "How many of the Guard units are in direct engagement?" she says. "Fourteen," he says. "Against corridor and junction class. None against the aerial forms — those are above Guard engagement parameters and I pulled them back from direct aerial contact at 0717 per standing protocol." "Good," she says. "Keep them off the aerial forms. What the Guard can address is what the Guard addresses." She looks at the western junction cluster. At the fourteen units in direct engagement. At the second perimeter line's contracted position. "I need the western junction cluster reduced," she says. "The three units on the eastern access north side — consolidate them with the western engagement. I am going to the western junction." Dunn stares at her. "Supreme Guardian, the western junction has four junction-class forms. Above Guard engagement parameters." She looks at him. He looks at her. He says: "Right. The route is forty meters and then left at the generator housing. Cover is thin past the housing. Understood?" "Understood," she says. She goes to the western junction. —— The western junction has four junction-class forms and a formation cluster that is feeding them. Not feeding them in the intentional sense — the ambient Fragmentum in the surrounding area is at saturation density from the second Stellaron's second-wave field, and the formation cluster at the junction is pulling from that saturation and generating at a rate that is producing new forms faster than the Guard units can address the existing ones. Three Guard units are at the junction's south entrance, holding the line, four junction-class forms between them and the cluster. The cluster is generating its fifth form. Bronya assesses this in three seconds. The cluster is the problem. The individual forms are the symptom. Addressing the symptom without addressing the cluster is the reason three Guard units have been holding this junction for eleven minutes without progress. She looks at the cluster. At the base junction of the cluster's primary crystallization point — the point from which the generation is radiating, the structural center of the formation network at this location. She raises the rifle. The first Stellaron's attending quality is still present in her hands. Not directed. Not channeled. Present in the way that something you have been listening to for a long time becomes part of the background of your hands, part of the specific warmth that lives in your palms when you have been holding something that has been held long enough. She fires once. The shot hits the cluster's primary crystallization point. The first Stellaron's ambient attending quality in the shot means the impact is not a bullet. It is a bullet carrying the specific resonance of a Stellaron's signal through the geological structure of a planet — a signal that the Fragmentum crystallization at that junction has been exposed to through the first Stellaron's ambient output for as long as it has existed, a signal it recognizes, a signal it responds to. The cluster's crystallization destabilizes. The generation rate drops immediately — the fifth form collapses before it finishes generating, the fourth form losing the feed from the cluster, the generation network at the junction fracturing from the primary point outward. The four existing forms are still present. But they are no longer being fed. The three Guard units at the south entrance look at the cluster. At the collapsed fifth form. At the destabilized generation rate. The Guard unit on the left says, to nobody in particular: "What was that?" "The Supreme Guardian's shot," the unit on the right says. A pause. They both look toward the junction entrance. Bronya is already moving to the eastern access north side. —— Pela arrives at the workshop at 0729. She has been running the first Stellaron's real-time signal data from the palace analysis room since 0603, which means she has been running data for one hour and twenty-six minutes without a break and has been updating the monitoring tablet's projections twelve times in that period. She has been tracking the first Stellaron's attending quality — the signal quality change that began at 2213 last night and has held at 3.1 since 0604 without climbing — and she has been noting, every seven minutes, that it is holding. It is holding. She has also been tracking the outer machine district's presence-field density, the formation class evolution in the surrounding sectors, and the Guard perimeter status through Dunn's field report feed. She has done all of this from the palace analysis room. She has been told, by Bronya directly before Bronya crossed the district boundary, to monitor the signal and report the moment it moves. She has been monitoring. At 0728, the signal does not move. But the Guard perimeter status feed produces an entry that Pela reads three times: WESTERN JUNCTION CLUSTER NEUTRALISED — SG REPORT CONFIRMS GUARDIAN'S SHOT. She looks at this entry for four seconds. She looks at the signal data. She looks at the western junction entry again. Then she closes the analysis room and goes to the workshop. —— The workshop when she arrives is — more populated than she expects. Clara is on the workshop floor with both hands on two separate notation channel junctions, her expression the complete focused quality of someone who is doing two things simultaneously and aware that neither of them can be done partially. The Moles: Pino with his hand on the northwest channel wall, not moving, the specific still attention of someone following an instruction to the letter; Toma at the secondary sensor display, running the presence-field density updates and calling out changes in the specific calm voice that Toma uses when the information is significant and the appropriate response is clarity; Oleg on the floor, eating from the jacket, providing live commentary through the ground-level window with the periodic editorial observations of someone who has been watching an extraordinary battle for an hour and twenty minutes and has developed strong opinions about it. And Svarog's back room: the door open, the equipment running, the back room serving as the secondary notation network control point that it has been serving as for the past hour. Pela looks at all of this. She looks at Clara — at the two channel junctions, the focused expression, the twelve-year-old who has been conducting the primary notation network for an hour and twenty minutes. She looks at Toma's sensor display updates. She looks at Pino's absolutely motionless hand on the northwest channel wall. She looks at Oleg. "What is he eating?" she says. "The jacket has snacks," Toma says, without looking away from the display. "I see that," Pela says. "It has significant snack capacity," Toma says. "We have stopped asking about it." Pela looks at the sensor display over Toma's shoulder. Formation class data, presence-field density, the Guard perimeter positions updated every two minutes. She reads it in five seconds. "The northeast channel junction," she says. "On the secondary display — the third feed from the left. Is that a density spike?" Toma looks at the third feed. "Yes," he says. "It started three minutes ago. I flagged it. Clara said hold it." "Hold it as in—" "As in she knows about it and she is managing it through the primary channel," Toma says. "She has been managing the northeast spike and the primary channel and the northwest extension simultaneously for three minutes." Pela looks at Clara. Clara, without taking either hand off the junctions or opening her eyes, says: "Pela. Northeast secondary channel junction. On the wall to your left. Put your hand on it." Pela looks at the wall to her left. She looks at the junction notation — the pre-Svarog carving, the Architects' design, the channel that she has been reading data from for four months and has never touched. She puts her hand on it. The notation channel is warm. Not the temperature of the wall — the warmth of the source energy running through the Architects' network, the specific old-gold quality that the expedition reports have been describing since the first contact event and which Pela has been reading descriptions of and is now receiving directly through the palm of her hand. "Oh," she says. "Yes," Clara says. "Keep your hand there. Don't direct it. Just hold the channel open." Pela keeps her hand there. "The northeast density spike," Pela says. "The Guard perimeter data says the northeast access corridor has junction-class formations at two points—" "I know," Clara says. "The channel will reduce the generation rate in that corridor in forty seconds if you keep holding the junction." Pela keeps holding the junction. Forty seconds later, the northeast access corridor formation density on the sensor display drops from junction class to corridor class. She looks at her hand. "Clara," she says. "Yes?" Clara says. "How long have you known you could do this," she says. "Since the first time I touched the workshop wall," Clara says. "Eight months ago." Pela processes this. "Why didn't you tell anyone?" "I told Svarog," Clara says. "He said it was expected." Pela looks at Svarog's back room. At the open door. At the equipment running. "Expected," she says. "He says I'm part of the plan," Clara says. "He has been saying it for eight months. I am still working out what the plan is." Pela keeps her hand on the junction. She looks at the sensor display. She looks at the twelve-year-old conducting two channel junctions simultaneously with both hands, eyes closed, managing the primary notation network for a seventy-meter Stellaron battle with the specific focused calm of someone who has been doing hard things for long enough that hard things have become the standard. She looks at Oleg. "What is he eating?" she says again, because she genuinely wants to know. "No one knows," Toma says. "He pulls different things out every time. We have stopped trying to predict it." "Dark filling pastry," Oleg says, without looking away from the window. "Natasha's. She put them in the jacket pocket before the evacuation." He holds one out without looking. "You want?" Pela stares at him. "I — yes," she says, because she has been in the palace analysis room for one hour and thirty-one minutes without eating anything. She takes the pastry. She keeps her hand on the junction. She looks at the battle data on the sensor display. "The aerial forms," she says. "Second wave. The density on the monitoring feed — they're dropping." "I know," Clara says. "The arm is clearing them through the shimmer." "The arm conducting through the Fragmentum shimmer is not a modeled capability," Pela says. "No," Clara says. "But it is working." "I can see that," Pela says. "I'm not disputing that it's working. I'm noting that I did not model it." "Add it to the model," Clara says. Pela looks at her. At the twelve-year-old conducting two notation channels simultaneously while telling the head of Belobog's intelligence division to update her models. She opens her tablet. She starts updating the model. She keeps her hand on the junction. This is, she decides, an extremely unusual morning. —— Serval arrives at the Guard perimeter at 0731. She arrives from the direction of the Restricted Zone entrance — she has been running frequency analysis in the Restricted Zone's secondary monitoring station since 0500 this morning, which was before the Stellaron's emergence at 0617, which means she was in the Restricted Zone monitoring station when the emergence alert came through and she has been in the Restricted Zone for the last hour and fourteen minutes while the outer machine district has been doing what it has been doing. She comes out of the Restricted Zone entrance with her frequency equipment on her hip and her expression on the specific setting that it occupies when she has been running analysis for a very long time on something very significant and has arrived at a finding that requires immediate deployment. Dunn's first perimeter line is forty meters from the Restricted Zone entrance. She walks up to the Guard unit holding the northeast corner of the first perimeter line and says: "I need to get to the east generator stack." The Guard unit looks at her. At the frequency equipment. At the outer machine district behind it, where the Stellaron battle has been running for one hour and fourteen minutes. "The east generator stack is inside the engagement zone, ma'am," the Guard unit says. "I know where it is," Serval says. "I've been monitoring it from the Restricted Zone station for four months." She looks past the Guard unit at the district. At the shimmer of Fragmentum in the air. At the golden form visible above the generator stacks, listing on the compromised foreleg, the left wing partially folded. "My brother is in there." The Guard unit looks at her. "Move," Serval says. The Guard unit moves. —— She reaches Luka at the east stack at 0734. He is on the ground level, one hand on the east stack's base junction, the chrome arm conducting through the notation network in the thirty-second bursts that the channel can sustain before the recharge period. The sandevistan integration is running — she can see it in the quality of his stillness between the bursts, the specific readiness of the spine artifact when it is at operational ready, the slight shimmer-quality of the air around him that the speed integration produces when it is primed but not deployed. He looks like someone who has been going for an hour and is not done. She stops beside him. He looks at her. "You were in the Restricted Zone," he says. "I came out," she says. "The Restricted Zone monitoring station is safer than—" "Luka," she says. "I have been in a monitoring station for an hour and fourteen minutes while this has been happening." She looks at the form. At the aerial forms above the courtyard. At the east stack. "I am not going back to a monitoring station." She unslings the frequency equipment from her hip. It is not standard combat equipment. It has been — modified. The specific modifications of someone who has been studying Fragmentum resonance patterns for nine months and has built something into the equipment that was not in the standard configuration. "Tell me what you need." He looks at the equipment. "What did you do to it?" "I added a resonance disruptor," she says. "The Fragmentum crystallization at junction class and above has a structural resonance frequency — I have been calculating it for months. If I can broadcast the disruptive frequency at the right amplitude—" "You can destabilize the forms," he says. "Not destroy them," she says. "Reduce their structural integrity by enough that a standard impact address them faster. Or," she looks at the aerial forms above, "the arm's source material conductance becomes more effective if the crystallization's resonance is disrupted first." He looks at the aerial forms. At the six second-wave forms, four tracking the fighters, two tracking Bronya's position — which has changed, he has been watching Bronya at the Guard perimeter and the two forms that were tracking her are now tracking a moving position that is currently at the western junction, which means they are not in the direct engagement zone but they are not at the district boundary either. He looks at Serval. "How long to calibrate for the second-wave forms?" he says. "I've been calibrating since the first wave generated," she says. "Thirty seconds of broadcast time against the second wave should drop their integrity by forty percent." "What do you need from me?" "Hold the east notation channel for sixty seconds while I broadcast," she says. "The resonance disruptor needs the notation network's ambient field as a carrier. I need the channel open." He puts his hand on the east junction. "Sixty seconds," he says. She raises the frequency equipment. —— Sampo arrives at the Guard perimeter at 0733. He does not arrive from any specific direction that Dunn's perimeter log records. He is simply — there, at the inner boundary of the first perimeter line, in the specific way that Sampo has always been in places that the official record does not account for. He is wearing his jacket. He has the expression he wears when he is about to do something that is going to require the specific skill set of a person who has spent twenty years finding the gaps between the official version and the real version. He looks at the district. He looks at the Stellaron's form above the generator stacks. He looks at the formations in the streets between the residential sections — the junction-class forms that are pressing the Guard perimeter back, the generation clusters at the junction points that are feeding new forms faster than the perimeter can address existing ones. He looks at Dunn's three perimeter lines. At the contracted second line. At the third line, which is the last line before the evacuation routes. He looks at the geometry of it. He has been looking at this district's geometry since he was eleven years old. He knows where the gaps are — the gaps in the formation density, the gaps in the perimeter coverage, the gaps between the junction cluster points. He knows, because he grew up in these streets and has walked every secondary route in the outer machine district and the adjacent sections more times than he can count, exactly which approach routes are not in the formation clusters' direct generation field and which junction points, if addressed, would collapse the feeding network for the largest cluster groups. He looks at the secondary entrance to the inner district junction. The one that the Guard units have not covered because it is not a standard patrol route and is not in Dunn's field briefing. The one that goes past the old generator access point at junction nine, which is adjacent to the largest cluster in the northeast section. He goes to Dunn. "The northeast cluster," he says. Dunn looks at him. "Valerius," he says. "Sampo," Sampo says. "The northeast cluster. The Guard perimeter is addressing the cluster from the south entrance. The generation rate is outpacing the address rate because the cluster's primary crystallization point is on the north side of the junction and the Guard units cannot reach the north side from the south entrance." He pauses. "There is a secondary route that reaches the north side of the junction from the old generator access point. It is not in your briefing." Dunn looks at him. "Why isn't it in the briefing?" "Because it is not a standard patrol route," Sampo says. "It was a maintenance route before the generator network was updated fifteen years ago. It is not in any current map." He pauses. "I know where it is." Dunn looks at the northeast cluster density on the perimeter monitoring. The generation rate. The address rate. The gap between them. "Can you get one Guard unit through that route to the north side of the junction?" he says. "Two," Sampo says. "The route is narrow but manageable." "Two units," Dunn says. He looks at the northeast section. "Valerius—" "Sampo." "If I send two Guard units into a non-standard route on your knowledge of its location and the route is not passable—" "Then I am in the route with the two Guard units," Sampo says, "and we find an alternative. I would not send them somewhere I had not gone myself." He pauses. "I have been in that route. Twice this year. The Fragmentum density is manageable and the junction access on the north side is clear." Dunn looks at him for one second. "Take units seven and twelve," he says. "They know the northeast section. You lead." "Units seven and twelve," Sampo says. "Understood." He goes to find units seven and twelve. Dunn watches him go. "Who," the Guard unit beside Dunn says, "is that?" Dunn considers the question. "Someone the Supreme Guardian trusts," he says, which is the most accurate short answer he can produce. —— In the courtyard at 0736, Gepard is at forty-eight percent. The second-wave aerial forms have been in the engagement for ten minutes. He has addressed two of the four that were tracking the fighters — the concentrated ice lance at the base junction, the structural integrity compromised, the forms dropping in articulation precision and eventually collapsing under the degradation. The third and fourth are in process — the third is at sixty percent integrity, the fourth is fresh and tracking him with the specific accuracy of a form that has not yet been addressed. He is at forty-eight percent. He has been running the specific calculation of forty-eight percent for six minutes — the Restricted Zone's primary lesson, the assessment that the northern ridge enforced in the specific way of a year of solo survival: what can you address at your current state, what can you address at the state you will be in if you spend what it costs, and what cannot be addressed until you are at a different state entirely. Forty-eight percent can maintain. Forty-eight percent can hold the courtyard at selection rate. Forty-eight percent cannot produce the full expression — the impact that buckled the ankle joint, the maximum compression lance, the sixteen-meter ice wall. Those are above forty-eight percent. The Stellaron is tired. Not collapsing — tired. One hour and seven minutes of engagement, the right foreleg compromised, the left wing joint damaged by two of Seele's approaches, the aerial forms in their second wave costing it reserve it has been spending since the second wave generated. The generation rate from the primary form itself is lower. The presence-field discharges are coming at longer intervals. The golden armor, at every damaged joint point — the wing, the ankle, the left shoulder plate that Luka's notation-through-shimmer discharge hit — is showing the specific quality of material that has been under sustained impact stress. It is not breaking. But it is stressed. He needs three things. One: the second-wave aerial forms cleared. Two: someone to address the ankle joint of the left foreleg — the right foreleg is compromised, the left is the form's primary support, and a compromised primary support changes the engagement geometry significantly. Three: ten minutes at forty-eight percent maintenance, which will bring him to the fifty-five to sixty percent range, which is the range where the full expression becomes available again. He has one of those things. From the east stack, Serval's frequency disruptor has been running for twenty seconds against the second-wave forms. He can see the effect — the crystallization in the aerial forms is losing the dense quality, the amber-to-black ratio shifting, the structural coherence dropping. Forty percent integrity reduction from sixty seconds of broadcast, she said. He is going to use that forty percent reduction. He brings up the lance. Not maximum compression — sixty-five percent compression, the efficient expression, the one that produces effective force at the cost that forty-eight percent can sustain. He fires it at the fourth aerial form's base junction. The base junction — structurally disrupted by Serval's resonance disruptor — receives the sixty-five percent lance at forty percent reduced integrity. It is sufficient. The fourth form collapses. He looks at the third form. Sixty percent integrity. Serval's disruptor on it for thirty seconds now. The reduction is running. He raises the lance again. —— Seele is on the south stack at 0737 when she feels the shift. She has been on the south stack since the second approach on the left wing joint — the approach that drove the scythe into the articulation gap for the second time, the second impact that has left the left wing folded sixty percent, the joint damage now significant enough that the Stellaron is not attempting to open the wing to full extension. She has been on the south stack running the calculation of the third approach — the geometry, the angle, the distance, the point at which the wing joint's accumulated structural damage will be sufficient for a third approach to complete the collapse. And then she feels the shift. It is not a calculation. It is not a sensor reading. It is the specific feeling of something in her that has been building since the primary structure's warmth moved through her in the depression, since the old-gold quality conducted through her skin and her ribs resolved and the butterfly quality that Natasha noted and Serval measured became part of how she moves. It has been building since then. Small increments. The fifty-meter jump from the stack. The movement in the air that was not quite standard trajectory. The specific feeling in the wing approach of something that was present in her motion that is not the same as the standard physics of someone her mass at that velocity. The shift is — more. Not a transformation. Not the full expression of whatever she is growing toward — that is future-Seele, the version that exists after everything that is coming. This is the present-Seele increment, the second small one, the specific additional capacity that the full memory recovery and the primary structure's warmth and an hour and seventeen minutes of active engagement against a seventy-meter Stellaron have produced in her. She looks at the left wing joint. At the sixty-percent-folded wing. At the third approach geometry. She looks at her hands. The old-gold warmth from the primary structure is conducting through her hands the way it has been since the depression — inward through the palms, into the core, the specific warmth of something that recognized her and gave her something of itself. She has been feeling it since the deep entry. She has been feeling it more strongly since the branch floor memory. She feels it now in a way that is — active. Present. Not background warmth but foreground warmth. The butterfly quality not at the edges of her motion but at the center of it. She looks at the wing joint. She does not think about the physics. She goes. —— The third approach is not like the first two. The first approach was — extraordinary, the word she refuses to apply more than once to the same thing. The fifty meters of air, the wing joint, the full downward drive. The second approach was the correction — the angle adjustment, the geometry refined, the second impact on an already damaged joint. Both of those were Seele-class. This is something else. She goes off the south stack edge and the air receives her differently. Not in a way that is visible from the outside — not wings, not light, not any of the visible expressions of what she is growing toward. It is in the quality of the trajectory. The specific character of how she moves through forty meters of air toward the left wing joint, which is the character of someone whose relationship to trajectory physics is not the same as it was one hour ago. She covers sixty meters. Not the fifty of the first approach. The Stellaron has shifted position since then — the compromised right foreleg pulling the form three meters to the right, which has moved the left wing joint ten meters from the south stack. She covers sixty meters. She hits the left wing joint with the full drive of the scythe and the full additional force of whatever the old-gold warmth in her hands has become in the specific context of striking a Stellaron's armored articulation point. The blade goes into the joint and drives through the accumulated damage of the first two approaches and the scythe's edge — which is a mechanical scythe that Svarog built for her, which is made of material that came from the same source as the notation network and the arm's artifact — conducts the warmth through the impact point. The joint receives the warmth. The way the corridor formations received the arm's warmth. The way the cluster's crystallization received the Guardian's shot. The Fragmentum crystallization that constitutes the damage layer in the left wing joint's articulation gap receiving the old-gold quality of the Architects' source material conducting through a person who has been recognized by the second Stellaron's primary structure as something it knows. The joint does not crack. The joint collapses. The left wing — sixty percent folded, two approaches of structural damage, one joint collapse — drops. The full wing, ninety-meter span, dropping from its extended and partially-folded position to fully closed. The armor plates of the wing hitting the form's left side with a sound that is the geological-frequency sound and something additional — something that is the sound of a major structural element of a seventy-meter form ceasing to function. The form lists hard to the left. Then it does something that none of the four fighters in the engagement, none of the Guard perimeter units, none of the people in the workshop — that none of Belobog has seen or expected. It howls. Not the roar. Not the presence-field sound. The howl of something in genuine pain — the full seventy-meter resonance of something that has been holding and holding and holding and has just felt the thing that was structural fail. The howl crosses the outer machine district and hits the adjacent residential sections and bounces off the dome of Belobog at the frequency that the dome does not dampen and returns through the geological structure of the planet as a sub-bass vibration that every person still in the Underworld feels through the floor of wherever they are standing.
