In Natasha's ward, the instruments vibrate. In the palace analysis room, the signal monitoring equipment registers the howl as a secondary output on every frequency band simultaneously. In the workshop, Oleg stops eating. Everyone in the workshop looks at the window. "The wing is down," Oleg says. "I can see that," Clara says. She is looking at the notation network's response to the howl — the geological vibration propagating through the Architects' channels in ways that the network was not designed to carry, the source energy in the channels fluctuating at the impact frequencies. "Hold the northwest channel," she says to Pino. "Still holding," Pino says. He has not moved his hand from the northwest channel wall for one hour and forty-two minutes. His arm must be — "Still holding," he says again. —— The howl reaches Luka at the east stack and he knows what it means. The left wing joint. Seele's third approach. The accumulated damage of two hours of engagement plus whatever the warmth in her hands added to the third impact. The wing is down and the form is listing hard and the specific quality of the howl — the genuine-pain register, the involuntary production of something that is not a weapon and is not a tactic, just the full resonance of something that has been damaged at a fundamental level — tells him the form's structural integrity is approaching the threshold. He looks at the chrome arm. The arm is — warm. Warmer than the baseline. The sixty seconds of east notation channel contact that he held for Serval's broadcast has left the source material conducting at above-standard output — the charge rate elevated from the sustained contact, the arm running hotter than it has since the sub-level three approach. This is useful. This is the specific usefulness of a tool that has been warmed up and is now available at above-standard output. He looks at the form's right side. At the right shoulder joint — the area where his notation-through-shimmer discharge hit the shoulder plate's junction with the neck plate at 0711, the area where the crystallization in the joint dissolved and the neck's left-side range of motion became limited. The limitation is still present — the neck cannot turn full range to the left, which is why Gepard's ice lance at the right eye has been consistently more effective at producing head movement than the left-eye approach, because the neck can compensate for right-side restriction more easily than left. If the right shoulder joint's dissolved crystallization is the weak point — if the notation-through-shimmer discharge produced a permanent structural compromise in the joint's integrity rather than a temporary dissolution — then a second application of the same discharge at higher output, at the arm's current above-standard charge rate, might reach the interior. He looks at the shimmer in the air between the east stack and the form's right shoulder. The second-wave aerial forms have been reduced — Serval's disruptor and his conductance have cleared four of the six, the remaining two are at forty percent integrity from the continued disruptor broadcast. The shimmer is thinner than it was at 0723. Thinner shimmer means lower conductance efficiency. He needs to get closer. He looks at the thirty meters between the east stack and the form's position. At the presence-field density — lower than the 0638 peak, the form's resource spending dropping the field intensity in the surrounding area. Lower field means lower formation generation in the immediate space. Lower formation generation means the thirty meters is not the saturation-density obstacle it was at 0723. He steps off the east stack. Serval, from behind him: "LUKA—" "I need to be closer," he says. He is already moving. "Keep the disruptor running." He moves through the thirty meters between the east stack and the form's right side. The sandevistan integration produces the speed that this requires — not the full burst deployment, not the maximum expression that leaves afterimages and burns charge, but the operational speed of the spine running at standard ready, the specific pace that is a different category from running and that covers thirty meters in a time that the Guard units watching from the perimeter line later describe as: fast. Just. Fast. He stops eight meters from the form's right foreleg. He looks up at the right shoulder joint. Twenty-five meters above his current position. The dissolved crystallization of the junction — visible as a different-quality darkness in the armor, the gap where the standard crystal structure was dissolved by the earlier discharge. He raises the arm. He extends the segmented structure of the chrome into the first transformation — not the idle configuration, not the blade. The specific change that the arm's design allows when the arm's integration with the spine produces the speed to use it: the segments extending, spring-loaded, popping open into the spike configuration, the arm becoming something with reach beyond the standard limb length. Then further — the plasma state. The old-gold warmth of the source material in the chrome conducting outward from the integration points, meeting the above-standard charge from the sixty seconds of notation channel contact, producing the specific glow at the blade's edges that is not fire and is not the notation network's warmth and is both of those things simultaneously. The plasma blade state. He drives it into the dissolved crystallization of the right shoulder joint from eight meters below, the extended reach of the arm's spike configuration and the plasma state's conductance bridging the distance, the source material warmth channeling through the gap in the armor where the crystallization was dissolved. The right shoulder joint receives the source material warmth at above-standard output through a gap it cannot close. The interior of the right shoulder joint dissolves. Not the exterior armor. The interior — the crystallization that makes the articulation of the joint possible, the structural material that allows the form's right arm to move at the shoulder. The dissolution propagates from the interior contact point outward through the joint's crystalline structure, the source material conducting through every mineral pathway available, the interior dissolving in a wave from the contact point. The right arm of the second Stellaron drops. The right foreleg — already compromised at the ankle from Gepard's impact — drops from the shoulder. Not detachment. Collapse. The foreleg hitting the courtyard stone with the geological impact of a twenty-meter armor-plated limb dropping fifteen meters. The impact from the courtyard is — significant. The form lurches. Hard to the right now — right foreleg collapsed from the shoulder, right ankle already compromised, left wing dropped, listing hard. The form's balance is no longer a question. The form is going down. But it is not going down yet. The howl from the wing joint damage is still resonating. The form brings its head around — slowly, the left-side neck restriction still present, the movement labored — and it looks down at Luka. Eight meters away. Chrome arm extended into the plasma blade configuration. Source material warmth conducting at above-standard output. The form looks at him. Then it opens its mouth. The presence-field discharge that comes out of the form's mouth at point-blank range is not aimed. It is not tactical. It is the involuntary response of something in significant structural distress producing the maximum output of whatever it has left, directed at the thing that just dissolved its shoulder joint's interior. Luka is not there when it arrives. The sandevistan integration is fully deployed — not the operational speed, the full burst, the maximum expression that leaves the specific afterimage quality in the air that burns the charge reserve and produces the movement that is genuinely a different category from fast. He is eight meters left of his previous position when the discharge hits where he was. The discharge hits the generator housing at the east stack's base and the housing — twelve centimeters of reinforced metal — is not there anymore. He hits the ground at the new position and rolls and comes up. His heart is going considerably faster than standard. "That," he says, to nobody in particular, "was close." From the south stack, Seele's voice: "LUKA! ARE YOU—" "FINE!" he calls. "I AM FINE!" A pause. Then: "YOU ARE NOT FINE! THE GENERATOR HOUSING IS GONE!" "I AM NOT IN THE GENERATOR HOUSING!" he calls. "THE HOUSING WAS WHERE YOU WERE!" "I MOVED!" he calls. A silence that has a specific quality. "How fast," she says, at a slightly lower volume. "Full burst," he says. Another silence. "That is the third time you have made me think something very bad happened to you," she says. "I know," he says. "I need you to stop doing that," she says. "I will," he says. "After the battle." The silence that follows this has a quality of someone who is completely aware that "after the battle" is technically a reasonable qualifier and is choosing to feel that it is not a satisfying one. "The right arm dropped," she says, returning to operational register. "The shoulder joint." "The interior dissolved," he says. "The form is going down." "Not yet," she says. "Look." —— He looks. The form is not going down. Not yet. The right arm is collapsed. The left wing is folded. The right ankle is compromised. The form is listing hard to the right. These are four major structural elements in various states of failure or collapse, and the form is still standing, which says something about what seventy meters of second Stellaron means in terms of structural reserve. But the golden armor — at every damaged joint point, every structural stress location, every place where the engagement has been applying force for one hour and thirty-eight minutes — is doing something. It is cracking. Not the catastrophic cracking of imminent collapse. The cracking of something that has been under sustained stress and is approaching the threshold at which sustained stress produces visible change. The gold of the armor plates at the wing joint, the ankle joint, the shoulder joint, the fifteen locations where the ice structures have impacted, the two locations where Gepard's full expression has contacted — those locations are showing hairline fractures. Gold plate against gold plate, the hairlines propagating from the damaged points outward through the adjacent plating. The form is standing. But the armor is cracking. From the courtyard, Gepard is at fifty-two percent. Four minutes of maintenance at selection rate — the three aerial forms cleared, the fourth collapsing under Serval's disruptor, the courtyard engagement held while the arm and Seele's third approach did their work. Four minutes at selection rate brought him from forty-eight to fifty-two. Not the full expression. But closer. He looks at the cracking armor. He looks at the hairline fractures propagating from the shoulder joint. From the wing joint. From the ankle. He looks at the form's listing, compromised, sustained-stress position. He looks at the specific joint that has been the engagement's center point since Seele identified it in the first minute of the battle — the left wing joint, the articulation weakness, the structural point. The joint that three approaches have damaged into a folded wing. The joint where the armor-plate cracking is most advanced. He looks at the left wing joint. He looks at the fractures. He looks at his hands. Fifty-two percent is not the full expression. Fifty-two percent is not the ankle-joint impact or the maximum compression lance. Fifty-two percent is the selection-rate maintenance mode. The form is cracking. The structural reserve is approaching the threshold. He does not need the full expression. He needs the right expression at the right point. He takes a breath. He looks at the left wing joint — at the fractures, at the accumulated damage of Seele's three approaches, at the dissolved exterior crystallization from the third approach's old-gold warmth conduction. He moves. —— He crosses the outer machine district courtyard at not the full expression speed and not the maintenance rate. He crosses it at the specific speed that is between both of those — the speed that has been developing since the northern ridge, the speed that the eight years of Restricted Zone service and the year on the outer edge and the classified debrief have settled into the specific expression of Gepard Landau moving toward something at the pace that is exactly what the situation requires, which is not dramatic and is not held back and is precisely correct. He reaches the left wing joint's position. He looks up at it. Twenty-five meters up. The fold of the collapsed wing. The fractures at the articulation plate. He goes up. Not climbing. Not jump. The ice — the specific expression of his ice when it is being used as architecture rather than weapon or shield, the construction capability that he developed in the second year of Restricted Zone service when the outer patrols required crossing terrain that was not negotiable on foot. The ice builds under him as he ascends — not stairs, not a ramp, the organic geometry of ice architecture that has been building toward structural purpose for eight years, the support giving way under each footfall because the support is not designed for sustained load, it is designed for momentary contact, for ascent. He reaches twenty-five meters. He is at the left wing joint. He puts both hands against the wing joint's surface — the gold armor plate at the primary articulation point, the location where the fractures are most dense, the location where Seele's three approaches have built the structural damage that is visible as hairlines propagating through the plate from the impact point. He brings the ice. Not the lance. Not the wall. The third expression — the one that the northern ridge produced the understanding of, the one that Dunn has never seen and has no classification for because it is not a combat expression in the standard sense. It is the defensive expression turned inward. The specific application of what forty-eight degrees below ambient does to material that is already under sustained stress, already cracked, already at the threshold. Metal cracks when stressed. Metal cracks faster when the stress is applied at the already-cracked points at very low temperature. The left wing joint, at twenty-five meters, at the primary articulation plate, at the fracture network that has been building for one hour and forty-three minutes, receives the third expression of Gepard's ice at the concentrated point of the fracture network's center. The plate fractures. Not hairlines. The full fracture — the articulation plate cracking across its full width, from the fracture network's center to the plate edges, the gold armor material giving under the combined stress of Seele's three approaches and Luka's source-warmth conductance and one hour and forty-three minutes of engagement and thirty-two degrees below ambient applied to the already-cracked center. The fracture propagates. Through the left wing joint, through the adjacent plating, through the shoulder armor, through every connected plate at every damaged location — the fracture network that has been building across the form's full armor surface finding the critical threshold at the left wing joint and propagating outward from that point through every other damaged location simultaneously. The armor shatters. Not all of it. Not every plate on the form's body. The damaged plates — the stressed plates, the cracked plates, the plates at the joint points where one hour and forty-three minutes of engagement has built the fracture networks — those plates shatter simultaneously, the gold armor breaking apart at every damaged location in a cascade that starts at the left wing joint and propagates at the speed of sound through connected plate material. The gold armor of the second Stellaron breaks. The sound of it is — not the geological-frequency sound. Not the howl. Something that has no prior classification because nothing that has happened in Belobog in the recorded history of Belobog has produced this specific sound, which is the sound of a Stellaron's armor losing its structural integrity across multiple simultaneous points in a cascade failure. The gold fragments of the armor plates scatter from the form's body — not falling, scattering, the specific explosive dispersal of material under extreme internal stress fracturing simultaneously. The courtyard receives them. The generator stacks receive them. The Guard perimeter, two hundred meters away, receives the fragments that reach that distance. And what is left when the gold is gone— —— Is something else entirely. The form that was seventy meters of golden armored dragon is still seventy meters. But the armor is gone, and what remains is — not the bare structure underneath the armor, not the interior of the form. Something that was inside the form, something that the armor was containing or protecting or simply — holding around. Something that has its own quality and nature entirely distinct from the golden plating. The body of it is deep space. Not as a color description — as a material description. The body of the second Stellaron in its second stage is the specific quality of interstellar space brought to presence in a material form: the deep purple-black of the space between star systems, with the specific shimmer of starfield visible in the material — points of light moving through the body like stars seen through cloud, the nebula-texture of something that is not solid in the way that the golden armor was solid. The form is still enormous. Still seventy meters. But it is moving differently — the specific movement of something that is less governed by physical mass than the armored form was, something that is less in the outer machine district and more adjacent to it, the presence-field around it shifted from the armored form's concentrated ground-level saturation to something that radiates in every direction simultaneously. The head — the golden armor plates of the face are gone, but there is still a head, still a recognizable facing-forward orientation, and where the eye was in the armored form there is a single eye. Green. Not the red of the Everwinter Hill monster, not the amber of the Fragmentum formations. The specific green of something that has been looked at before — the specific green that Svarog's secondary optical array uses, the green of a consciousness that has been running for longer than the armor was running. This is not a damaged form revealing its interior. This is the second stage. Svarog, at what was the form's right flank, looks up at the new form for one full second. Then he says: "Ah." Not surprise. Recognition. The oldest consciousness on the planet recognizing something that has the quality of age. Something that is, in a meaningful sense, what the second Stellaron actually is when you remove the physical housing of the dormancy period and the memory-holding function and the twenty-two years of primary-field generation. "Ah," Svarog says again. More quietly. In the tone of someone greeting something they knew was there. In the courtyard, Gepard descends from twenty-five meters and lands on the courtyard stone. He looks at the second-stage form. He is at fifty-two percent. The armor-fracture expression cost him the four percent he gained in the maintenance period. He is at forty-eight percent again, which is where he was when he started the maintenance, and the form he is looking at is not the compromised-foreleg, damaged-wing-joint, depleted-reserve armored form that the armor-fracture produced from. The second-stage form looks back at him. "Svarog," he says. "Second stage assessment." "The physical constraints of the armored form are gone," Svarog says. "The presence-field is now omnidirectional rather than ground-level concentrated. The form's movement capability is significantly increased — it is no longer operating within the physical limitations of the armored body." He pauses. "The structural damage accumulated in the first stage does not transfer. The armor-shattering that ended the first stage was the first stage ending — the second stage begins fresh." From the south stack: "It begins FRESH?" Seele says. "Yes," Svarog says. From the east stack, Luka's voice: "Does it still have the aerial generation capability?" "Unknown," Svarog says. "The second stage's capabilities are not in any classification system I have access to. I am assessing in real time." A pause. "Fantastic," Seele says, with the specific flatness of someone who has just spent one hour and forty-three minutes breaking a thing's armor and has been informed that the armor-breaking was the first half of the problem. "Look at it this way," Luka says. "We have been going for one hour and forty-three minutes and none of us are dead." "That is a very low bar," Seele says. "It is a bar we are above," he says. "That is not nothing." Gepard looks at the second-stage form. At the starfield body. At the single green eye. At the presence-field radiating in every direction from a form that is no longer constrained by the physics of a seventy-meter armored body. He is at forty-eight percent. He has been at the threshold before. The northern ridge. The year at the outer edge. The specific threshold where the assessment says: this is the edge of what is available, and you have to decide whether what is available is enough. On the northern ridge, what was available was enough. He looks at the second-stage form. He decides. —— In the workshop, Oleg has stopped providing live commentary. This is the first time Oleg has stopped providing live commentary since the emergence at 0638. He stopped at the moment the armor shattered — at the specific sound of it, the cascade failure of the gold plates, the fragment dispersal. He stopped and he looked out the window and he has been looking out the window since then. Clara has her eyes open. She does not normally open her eyes during sustained notation network conductance — the visual input is noise when the task is tactile. She has her eyes open because the second stage's presence-field shift — from ground-level concentrated to omnidirectional — has changed what the notation network is carrying in a way that she needs to see as well as feel. The Architects' network was built for this. Not for this battle specifically — for this planet's relationship with its Stellarons. The notation network runs through the geological structure of the outer machine district because the outer machine district is where the second Stellaron has always been. The network has been running this planet's source energy through this district's stone for longer than the district has existed. It has handled the second Stellaron's dormancy, the memory-holding function, the emergence, and the first stage's field saturation. It is handling the second stage's omnidirectional presence-field. The channels are warm. Running. The source energy flowing through the notation channels in every direction, the Architects' design distributing the second-stage field through the geological structure in a way that reduces the formation generation in the surrounding district. Not eliminating — reducing. The formations in the adjacent residential sections are still present, still at junction class in the highest-density zones, but the omnidirectional field that should have immediately saturated the entire surrounding area is instead flowing into the notation channels and through them into distribution. This is what Clara has been doing for two hours. This is what the Architects built the network for. She looks at her hands — both still on the notation channel junctions — and she says, quietly, to the network: "Keep going." The network keeps going. Beside her, Pela is still holding the northeast junction. She has been holding it for thirty-two minutes. Her arm is definitely getting tired but she has not mentioned this because she is sixteen years old and the outer machine district is fighting a Stellaron and the northeast formation density is lower than it would be without her hand on the junction and those are the relevant facts. She looks at the tablet. At the first Stellaron's signal. 3.1. Holding. She exhales. —— Bronya is at the northern Guard perimeter line at 0741 when the armor shatters. She is addressing the third formation cluster that the northern perimeter has been managing — the cluster at junction seven of the northern access corridor, the cluster that Sampo's secondary route allowed units seven and twelve to address from the north side while the perimeter units addressed from the south. The pincer on the cluster's generation point has been running for four minutes. The generation rate is dropping. She hears the shattering from four hundred meters away. She looks at the outer machine district's center. At the golden fragments dispersing from the form's body. At the deep-space body that emerges from the dispersal — the starfield quality visible even from four hundred meters, the green eye visible as a specific point of light in the form's new face. She watches the second stage for three seconds. Then she looks at the tablet. 3.1. Holding. She breathes. She looks at the formation cluster. At the northern access corridor. At the Guard units in the secondary route holding the north side. At units seven and twelve, who have been following Sampo's lead through the non-standard route for twelve minutes and are currently at the generation point's north side. She looks at the cluster's primary crystallization point. She raises the rifle. The first Stellaron's attending quality is in her hands — warmer now than at the western junction, warmer than at the district boundary. Not because the first Stellaron is closer. Because she has been in the active field of the second Stellaron for one hour and forty-three minutes and the attending quality of something that has been listening to a battle — that has been watching what Belobog does when confronted with the unsurvivable — has a presence in her hands that it did not have at 0603. She fires. The cluster's generation point receives the shot. The crystallization destabilizes. The generation rate drops. The forms in the cluster — the junction-class forms that the northern perimeter has been managing — lose their feed simultaneously. Units seven and twelve hit the destabilized cluster from the north side. The cluster collapses. Sampo, in the secondary route entrance, watches the collapse. He looks at Bronya's position — four hundred meters away, the rifle still raised, the specific quality of someone who has been in a battle for one hour and forty-five minutes and is not done. He reaches into his jacket. Not a weapon. A communicator. He raises it and says, in the direction of the units at the formation cluster: "Northeast route is clear. The generation network at the northeast junction is addressed. Redirect units toward the eastern perimeter — the second stage's omnidirectional field is going to increase formation density on the east side." Dunn's voice, from the perimeter command position: "Who authorized the northeast redirect?" A pause. "The Supreme Guardian's shot cleared the northern cluster," Sampo says. "The redirect is consistent with the assessment. The eastern perimeter needs reinforcement." A longer pause. Then: "Acknowledged. Units seven and twelve redirect to eastern perimeter. Valerius—" "Sampo," Sampo says. "Sampo," Dunn says. "Stay on comms." "Understood," Sampo says, and he looks at the outer machine district and at the second stage's starfield body and at the four people in the engagement zone who have been going for one hour and forty-five minutes and who are going to need the perimeter to hold for however long comes next. He looks at his jacket. He looks at the secondary route entrance. At the approach from here to the formation cluster on the northwest side, which Dunn's perimeter is not covering because Dunn does not know the secondary route exists. He calculates. He goes. —— Serval, at the east stack, watches the second stage emerge from the gold fragments. She looks at the starfield body. At the green eye. At the form's movement — different, the specific difference of something that is no longer operating within the physical constraints of seventy meters of armored mass. The form is moving more than the first stage moved. The presence-field around it radiating in all directions. She looks at the frequency equipment. The resonance disruptor calibration was for the armored form's crystallization — the specific Fragmentum crystal structure that constituted the first-stage form's body and the aerial forms it generated. The second stage's body is not that crystal structure. The second stage's body is the starfield material — the deep-space nebula quality that is, as far as Serval's instruments are concerned, not in any documented Fragmentum classification. She is recalibrating. This is going to take approximately four minutes. In four minutes, the second stage's first actions will have happened and the engagement will have changed and the recalibration she is doing now will be running four minutes behind where it needs to be. She recalibrates anyway. Faster than she usually calibrates. Not perfectly — with the specific accepting imperfection of someone who has been running frequency analysis for nine months and knows the difference between the calibration that is correct and the calibration that is correct enough and correct fast. Three minutes. She can do this in three minutes. "Luka," she says. He is beside her. He came back to the east stack after the point-blank discharge that removed the generator housing. He has the chrome arm in standard configuration — the sandevistan integration running at operational ready, the arm still conducting the above-standard charge from the sustained notation channel contact. His heart rate is, she suspects, still going considerably faster than standard. "Yes?" he says. "Three minutes," she says. "I'm recalibrating. Don't go in until I have the second stage's resonance profile." He looks at the second-stage form. "Three minutes," he says. "Three minutes," she says. "And Luka—" He looks at her. She looks at him. At the chrome arm. At the spinal integration visible at the back of his neck where his shirt collar gaps from the battle. At her brother, twenty-three years old, who has been in this district for one hour and forty-eight minutes doing things that Serval's models did not anticipate and which she is still adding to her calibration parameters. She says: "You are extraordinary." He stares at her. "I am allowed to say that," she says. "I am your sister. It is specifically allowed." He continues staring at her. "Three minutes," she says. "Don't go in." He looks at the form. He does not go in. —— Gepard and the second stage of the second Stellaron meet in the outer machine district courtyard at 0743. The form is faster. The first stage's movement was the movement of seventy meters of armored mass — significant force, significant reach, significant inertia in every motion. The second stage's movement is — different. The starfield body does not move with the inertia of physical mass. It moves with the quality of something that is both here and not entirely here, something that is present in the outer machine district and also adjacent to it in a way that the armored form was not. The presence-field discharge from the second stage is different too. The first stage's discharge was concentrated — aimed, directional, the specific pointing of a force expression from a form with physical facing and physical joints and a physical discharge aperture. The second stage's discharge is omnidirectional at the base and then focused — the omnidirectional field radiation of the starfield body producing an ambient pressure in all directions simultaneously, and then, when the eye focuses, a concentrated expression of that omnidirectional pressure in a specific direction. The concentrated expression hits Gepard's ice shield at 0743:12. The shield holds. Barely. The second-stage's focused discharge against the first-stage's presence-field discharge is not the same category — the shield holds but the ice structure compresses rather than cracks, the material integrity maintained at the cost of a structural density reduction that Gepard has not encountered in the shield's history. The force is not breaking the ice. It is pushing it. Compressing it inward. He releases the shield before it becomes a liability — the compressed ice dispersing in a controlled expansion rather than a structural failure — and he moves. This is where forty-eight percent and the right expression become the relevant variables. The right expression for this engagement is not the lance. The lance is a forward-projectile expression — effective against a form that has a specific facing and a specific vulnerable point at that facing. The second stage has an omnidirectional presence field and a green eye that tracks without the physical head-turning constraint of the first stage's neck restriction. The right expression is the architecture. He builds ice across the courtyard in the specific geometric pattern that the northern ridge required — not in front of the form, around it. Not a wall, not a barrier. A topology. The ice structures placed not to stop the form's movement but to constrain it — to reduce the effective movement space available to a form that is not limited by physical mass inertia but is limited by the geometry of where the ice is. You cannot move through where the ice is, even if you are not entirely physically present, because the ice's thermal field produces a counter-pressure against the omnidirectional presence field that makes the ice's location a location the form does not prefer. The form does not prefer ice locations. Good. He builds the topology, and within the topology, he begins to read the second stage. —— At 0747, the second stage generates. Not aerial forms. Not the primary-structure class formations of the first stage. The second stage generates from its own body — the starfield material extending outward from the form's core, tendrils of the nebula-texture reaching from the body into the surrounding air, not attacking, exploring. The specific quality of something that is testing the environment it has emerged into. The tendrils reach the first ice structure of Gepard's topology. They stop. The cold of the ice structure — not the lance temperature, the ambient temperature of the topology, the sustained cold of multiple ice structures in proximity — is a counter-pressure against the starfield material. The tendrils stop at the boundary of the cold field. Then they change direction. Gepard watches the tendrils change direction. He watches them find the gap between the second and third ice structures of the topology and move toward it. He watches the second stage's starfield material probing the topology's geometry — finding the weak points, the gaps, the places where the thermal counter-pressure is lower. He watches and he thinks: it is intelligent. Not the intelligence of the armored form, which was the intelligence of something in pain and responding to the sources of the pain. The intelligence of the second stage is different — the specific intelligence of something that is assessing the environment it has emerged into with the patience of something that is not operating on a human timeline. The intelligence of something that has been in the bedrock of this planet for a very long time. He builds ice in the gap. The tendrils stop again. Across the courtyard, from the south stack: Seele's voice. "It's reading the ice topology," she says. "I know," he says. "It's not attacking the ice," she says. "It's reading it." "I know," he says. "What does that mean?" she says. He looks at the second stage. At the single green eye. At the tendrils reaching and stopping and changing direction. "It means," he says, "that it is significantly smarter than the first stage." He looks at the topology. At the gaps the tendrils found. "And it means we are going to have to be smarter than the topology." A pause. "Suggestions?" Seele says. "Working on it," he says. —— Three minutes pass. Serval completes the recalibration at 0748. She has the second stage's resonance profile — not complete, not perfectly calibrated, the correct enough and correct fast version. The starfield material has a resonance frequency, which means the resonance disruptor can address it, which means the forty percent integrity reduction from the disruptor broadcast is available for the second stage if the profile calibration is close enough. It is close enough. "Ready," she says. Luka looks at the second stage. At the topology Gepard has been building. At the tendril exploration of the topology's gaps. At the single green eye reading the courtyard's geometry. He looks at Seele on the south stack. She is already looking at him. She looks at the topology. At the gaps. At the specific gap on the form's left side — the side where the left wing joint was, the side where three of her approaches built the accumulated damage that ended the first stage. The left side of the form is where the first-stage damage was concentrated. She does not know if the second stage carries structural memory of the first stage's damage. But the gap in the topology on the left side is real. She looks at Luka. He looks at the gap. She looks at the gap. They look at each other. "Together," he says — not to Gepard this time. To her. "Together," she says. She goes off the south stack. He drives the chrome arm's spike configuration into the notation channel junction at the east stack base and conducts the source material warmth through the Fragmentum shimmer in the omnidirectional-field direction of the form's left side, the thirty-meter radius dissolving — not Fragmentum crystallization this time, the shimmer in the second stage's omnidirectional field, the presence-field ambient radiation dissolving from the source material's counter-frequency — and the left side of the form, cleared of the ambient field, is exposed. Seele covers sixty-five meters. The old-gold warmth in her hands is at full forward-expression — not the edges of the butterfly quality, not the small increment from the third approach. The sustained engagement, the second stage's omnidirectional field, the memory fully returned, the primary structure's warmth in her hands for two hours and eleven minutes — all of it present in the sixty-five meters of trajectory, all of it in the scythe's drive when it hits the second stage's left side at the specific point where the first-stage damage was concentrated. The scythe drives into the starfield material. The material is not physical in the same sense as the armor, but the scythe — Svarog's design, artifact material, source material warmth conducting through the blade — has the specific relationship to the Architects' source that the corridor formations and the armor plates did. The blade enters the starfield material and the warmth conducts. The second stage shudders. The first time the second stage has shuddered. The specific involuntary full-body quality — the tendril exploration of the topology stopping, the green eye orienting toward the left side, the starfield body reacting to a contact that has reached something interior. Not the armor. The armor is gone. What the scythe has reached is the interior of what the second Stellaron actually is. And from the east side, Luka drives the chrome arm — plasma blade configuration, notation-through-shimmer discharge, above-standard charge — into the dispersed ambient field of the left side, and the source material warmth meets the point where Seele's warmth has already been, and the two conductances meet in the second stage's interior. The form produces the sound. Not the geological-frequency sound. Not the howl from the first-stage structural failure. Something that is both of those things and older than both, the sound of something that is being addressed at the level of what it fundamentally is, not the housing or the armor or the field but the actual interior of the second Stellaron. The green eye, for the first time in the engagement, looks afraid. —— Gepard moves. Not toward the form. Through the topology. The geometry he has been building and reading and refining for five minutes with the specific patience of someone who spent a year on the northern ridge learning what ice topology teaches you about the nature of things that are constrained by it, which is: everything can be constrained if you understand what it prefers. He moves through the topology to the specific point — the left side of the form, cleared of ambient field by Luka's notation-through-shimmer discharge, the point where Seele's scythe and Luka's plasma blade have reached the interior of what the second Stellaron is. He is at forty-eight percent. The right expression. He puts both hands on the form. The contact is — different from the armor contact. The armor was cold in the way that mineral material is cold. The starfield material is cold in the way that interstellar space is cold, which is a different category entirely and which the ice expression of Gepard Landau has a specific relationship to, because ice at the compression levels he operates at is the closest thing on Jarilo-VI to the temperature of deep space. He does not use the lance. He does not use the architecture. He does not use the ankle-joint impact or the maximum compression or the sixteen-meter wall. He uses the third expression. The one Dunn has never seen. The one from the northern ridge. The one that takes the defensive expression and turns it inward — the ice, at the deepest compression he can produce at forty-eight percent, released not outward but inward from the contact point. Into the starfield material. Into the interior. Into what the second Stellaron is. The cold propagates. Not fast. Not explosive. The slow inevitable propagation of something that is moving through material that has no resistance to offer — the interior of the second Stellaron, which has been reached by Seele's warmth and Luka's conductance, receiving the ice's inward expression at the two contact points of their blades and conducting it through the interior toward the center. The form shudders again. Harder. The tendrils retract. All of them, simultaneously, back into the form's body — the starfield material pulling inward, the form contracting rather than expanding, the presence-field omnidirectional radiation dropping from its second-stage output to something closer to the first-stage's concentrated format as the form loses the omnidirectional expression. The green eye looks at him. At forty-eight percent. Both hands on the second Stellaron's interior-exposed left side. The ice propagating inward. "Yes," Gepard says, to the green eye, in the same conversational tone he used at 0617 when the form first cleared the surface and Svarog greeted it. "I know." —— The form produces the sound for the last time at 0751. The specific sound — the geological-frequency and the howl and the older thing — at the volume that carries across the outer machine district and into the adjacent residential sections and through the geological structure of Belobog into the first Stellaron's attending quality and out through the dome into whatever exists above the dome. The starfield body contracts. Not collapsed. Contracts — the specific drawing-in of something that is reaching its limit, the presence-field closing from omnidirectional to concentrated to absent, the starfield material pulling toward the center of the form in the specific way of something that is not being destroyed but is returning. The material pulling back toward the bedrock. Toward the space below the outer machine district where the second Stellaron has always been. The form does not collapse in the courtyard. It contracts toward its source. The seventy meters of starfield body reducing — sixty, fifty, forty — the green eye present until the last twenty meters, looking at the outer machine district, at the city above it, at the four fighters who have been going for one hour and fifty-three minutes. At Gepard. At Seele. At Luka. At Svarog. At the city. At the dome above the city. At whatever is above the dome. At ten meters, the green eye closes. The second Stellaron returns to the bedrock. —— The outer machine district is very quiet. The ambient Fragmentum in the surrounding area is still present — the formations at the junction points, the shimmer in the air, the elevated monitoring readings that the Guard perimeter and the notation network and Clara's sustained conductance have been managing for two hours. These do not vanish when the form returns to the bedrock. They reduce. Slowly. The presence-field has been withdrawn and without the field driving the generation rate, the ambient formations will decay to baseline over the next several hours. But right now, the district is very quiet. Gepard is in the courtyard with both hands on the stone where the second stage was. He stays there for six seconds. Then he lifts his hands and stands up. He is at thirty-nine percent. He looks at the courtyard. At the ice topology still standing in various states of structural integrity. At the gold armor fragments scattered across every surface within four hundred meters. At the generator housing that was not there anymore because the form's point-blank discharge took it. At the outer machine district — his district, the district he has been holding since 0617 — standing. Battered. But standing. From the south stack, Seele descends. From the east stack, Luka descends. From the right flank, Svarog's four primary arms lower from active configuration. From the Guard perimeter, after a moment of complete silence, Dunn's voice on the comms: "Sir. Form status?" Gepard looks at the bedrock. "Withdrawn," he says. "The form has returned to the source. The immediate engagement is ended." A pause on the comms. Dunn processing. "Understood, sir," Dunn says. Then, after another pause, in a voice that is not the professional register but is the eight-years-of-working-alongside-this-specific-person register: "Good work, sir." Gepard looks at the courtyard. At the topology. At the gold fragments. "Maintain the perimeter," he says. "The ambient formations need managed decay. Nobody enters the formation zone without equipment." "Understood, sir." He puts the comms down. He looks at Seele when she reaches the courtyard. She is holding the scythe in a way that tells him the left shoulder was involved in the third approach and is not entirely comfortable. She does not address this. She is looking at the bedrock. "It went back," she says. "Yes," he says. "It's not gone," she says. "No," he says. "Not gone. Returned." He looks at the bedrock. "It completed its function. Both its functions." He pauses. "The memory, and this." She looks at him. "This was a function?" He considers this. "The form that came out of the armor," he says. "The second stage. It was reading the topology. Reading us. The intelligent assessment." He pauses. "I don't think it was trying to kill us." "It produced point-blank discharges," Seele says. "It produced force," he says. "Against the things that were addressing it, in the ways that were necessary to continue existing. That is not the same as trying to kill us." He looks at the bedrock. "I think it was seeing what we are. What Belobog is. What we can do." He pauses. "I think the second stage was the Stellaron doing what it was always going to do after the function was complete. Deciding whether Belobog is what it has been holding these things for." She looks at the bedrock. At where the green eye closed. "And?" she says. He looks at the courtyard. At Luka arriving. At Svarog's four arms lowered. At the Guard perimeter, two hundred meters away, holding. At the notation network channels still warm in the geological structure below their feet — Clara still conducting, still holding, still running the system that kept the second Stellaron's field from overwhelming the surrounding district for two hours. He looks at Bronya at the northern perimeter, four hundred meters away, walking back toward the district center. "We're still here," he says. —— In the workshop, at 0754, Clara takes her hands off the notation channel junctions. She holds them in her lap. She looks at her palms. She looks at the two junctions. She looks at the sensor display, where the formation class readings are beginning their slow decay toward baseline. Pino takes his hand off the northwest channel wall. He looks at his arm. He opens and closes the hand. He looks at the northwest channel junction, which he has been holding open for two hours and eleven minutes without moving his arm. "Is it over?" he says. "For now," Clara says. He looks at his hand. "My arm is very tired," he says. "I know," Clara says. "Sit down." He sits down. On the floor. Immediately. Toma sits beside him. Oleg is already on the floor. He has produced, from the jacket, something that appears to be three additional dark filling pastries and is distributing them with the specific calm of someone who has been managing the workshop's morale with confectionery for two hours. Clara looks at the pastries. "Natasha made them?" she says. "Before the evacuation," Oleg says. "She put the whole batch in the pocket." Clara takes the pastry. She sits on the floor between Pino and the northwest channel junction. She eats it. It is very good. She looks at the sensor display. At the decay. At the notation network running its warm steady distribution of source energy through the geological channels of the outer machine district, the Architects' design functioning exactly as it was built to function. She looks at the wall of the workshop. At the pre-Svarog notation carving visible in the stone above the secondary bench. "They knew," she says. "Who?" Toma says. "The Architects," she says. "They built this network knowing this was going to happen. They built the channels for this specific thing — for the second Stellaron's field to be distributed through the district instead of overwhelming it." She pauses. "They knew both Stellarons were going to be here. They knew someone was going to have to hold the network while the battle happened." She looks at her hands. "They left the source code in the second Stellaron's deeper content for whoever was going to be in this workshop." She looks at the wall. "They planned for me," she says. Not with awe. With the specific quality of a twelve-year-old who has been told something very large is true and is deciding what to do with the information. From Svarog's back room, quietly: "Yes." She looks at the back room. At the open door. At the equipment still running. "You knew," she says. "I suspected," Svarog says. "For approximately eight months." She looks at the notation carving above the secondary bench. "You should have told me." "You were twelve years old," Svarog says. "You still are." She looks at her pastry. "That," she says, "is not a good reason." "No," Svarog says. "But it is the reason I had." She looks at the carving. At the notation. At the Architects' design that has been in this wall for longer than the outer machine district has had its current name. "When you have enough to say it," she says, "I want to hear it quickly." "Yes," Svarog says. "I know." She finishes the pastry. Outside, the outer machine district is beginning its slow return to the sounds it is supposed to make — the Guard perimeter settling into managed decay monitoring, the formation density dropping, the shimmer in the air thinning. It will be hours before the district is at baseline. It will be days before the generator housings are repaired. It will be weeks before the courtyard stone is replaced. But the district is standing. The second Stellaron has returned to its bedrock. And the first Stellaron — three hundred meters below the palace, holding at 3.1, attending — has been watching. It has seen what Belobog is. It is still listening. —— Bronya reaches the courtyard at 0759. She walks through the gold armor fragment field and the reduced-shimmer air of the courtyard and she looks at the four people standing in it — Gepard and Svarog and Seele and Luka — and she looks at the bedrock where the form returned. She looks at Gepard. He looks at her. One moment of just that. The two of them, in the courtyard of the outer machine district, with two hours of battle behind them and the city standing around them and the first Stellaron attending below. She walks to him. She stands in front of him. She looks at thirty-nine percent wearing itself in the quality of how he is standing — the ice expression lowered, the management present but at the lower register of someone who has been running at selection rate for forty-eight minutes and is past the threshold. She puts her hand on his face. "Come back," she says. Two hours late, said in the context of he already did. "Yes," he says. With the full weight of what it means from him, in this context, here. She does not move her hand. He does not move away from it. Behind them, from Luka's direction: "Should we—" "Give them a moment," Seele says, quietly. A pause. "Right," Luka says. They give them a moment. —— At 0803, Pela's tablet produces an alert. She is still in the workshop, hand no longer on the northeast junction because the northeast junction no longer needs holding, eating Oleg's second offering from the jacket — a specific small wrapped thing of unknown origin that is more edible than it looks. She looks at the tablet. The first Stellaron's signal. It is not moving. It is still at 3.1. But the attending quality of the signal — the specific change that happened at 2213 last night, the signal going from dormant warmth to something that Pela described as aware — has changed again. Not to a higher frequency. Not toward the 4.7 threshold. To something that her instruments cannot adequately classify. The signal is still at 3.1 kilocycles. The attending quality is different from the aware quality. It is — something her existing classification system was not built to describe, because the existing classification system was built for a Stellaron that was dormant or broadcasting, and the first Stellaron is neither. She looks at the classification for a long time. Then she writes, in the monitoring notes: ATTENDING → CONSIDERING. She looks at what she has written. She saves the file. She looks at Clara, who is sitting on the floor eating a pastry and looking at the notation carving above the secondary bench with the expression of someone who has been told something very large and is deciding what to do with it. "Clara," Pela says. Clara looks at her. "The first Stellaron," Pela says. "The signal quality changed. At 0803." She turns the tablet so Clara can see the monitoring note. "I don't have a classification for the new quality. I wrote considering. Is that—" She pauses. "Does that feel right? To you?" Clara looks at the tablet. She looks at the notation carving. She puts her hand briefly on the workshop wall — not the channel junction, just the wall. Feeling the source energy running through the geological structure below. "Yes," she says. "That's right." "It's considering," Pela says. "Yes," Clara says. "Considering what?" Pela says. Clara looks at the wall for a moment. "Whether it's time," she says. Pela looks at the monitoring note. At 3.1 and CONSIDERING. She saves it again. END OF CHAPTER 42 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 43: Faux 100% — Manchester Smash Chapter Text The second Stellaron does not stay in the bedrock. This is the first thing they learn after 0759. They learn it at 0811, when the geological monitoring stations throughout the outer machine district register a movement signature below the third sub-level — not the presence-field radiation of the dormant form, not the generation-rate of the memory-function. Movement. The armored-and-then-starfield form that returned to the bedrock at 0751 is moving through the geological structure of the outer machine district at a depth below the third sub-level. Moving downward. Pela reads this on the tablet at 0812 and says: "It's going deeper." She says it to the courtyard, where Gepard is standing with Bronya's hand on his face and the gold fragments of the first-stage armor on the stone around them and the second Stellaron in the bedrock below them, apparently descending. Everyone in the courtyard hears it. A pause. "Deeper," Gepard says, not moving. "The geological signature is consistent with a form moving through the mineral structure in the direction of—" Pela checks the bearing. "Below the third sub-level. Following the notation network's deepest channel." From the east position, Luka says: "The massive cave." Everyone looks at him. "Below the third sub-level," he says. "The notation network's deepest channel runs past the secondary shaft we used in sub-level three." He looks at Svarog. "There is a deeper formation. I felt it the second time I conducted through the east stack junction. The channel goes further than the third sub-level." Svarog is quiet for three seconds. The specific silence of someone who has information they have been holding. "Yes," he says. "You knew," Luka says. "I suspected," Svarog says. "The pre-Svarog notation carvings in the deepest sections reference a space below the third sub-level that I have not been able to access through the standard notation entry points. The geological structure there is — different. Pre-Separation material. Whatever the Architects built down there, they built it before they built the outer machine district above it." "How large?" Gepard says. Svarog considers. "Based on the notation references and the geological resonance data from the six years I have been monitoring the secondary signature — large," he says. "Significantly larger than the sub-level chambers." Another silence. "The Stellaron is retreating into it," Bronya says. She has lowered her hand from Gepard's face. She is reading the monitoring data on Pela's tablet. "Not to rest. The movement signature is — purposeful. It is going to ground it knows." "It has been dormant below the third sub-level for twenty-two years," Svarog says. "The deep formation is where it originated. Where the armor formed around the dormancy." He pauses. "It is going home." "And it expects us to follow," Seele says. Gepard looks at the bedrock beneath their feet. At the geological structure below it. At the third sub-level below that, and below that the secondary shaft they used in the solo expedition, and below that — something they have not been in. Something the Architects built before they built the outer machine district above it. He is at thirty-nine percent. He looks at the courtyard. At the engagement they just ended. At the gold fragments of the armor on the stone around them. "Medical assessment," he says. "All four fighters. Full team. We are not going in until I know what we are going in with." He does not phrase this as a suggestion. —— Natasha arrives at the courtyard at 0819. She comes from the northern perimeter medical station, which has been her operational position since 0617. She comes with the ward kit and the expression she wears when she has been running triage for two hours and sixteen minutes and is now being asked to assess four specific people who she has opinions about. She looks at the courtyard. She looks at the gold fragments. She looks at Gepard, and Svarog, and Seele, and Luka. She says: "Sit down. All of you." Gepard says: "I don't need—" "SIT DOWN," she says. He sits down. On the courtyard stone, with the gold fragments around him. This is not because he is incapable of standing. This is because Natasha has a specific voice she uses when the instruction is not negotiable, and everyone who has been in her ward more than twice knows to comply with that voice, and Gepard has been in her ward four times in the last year. She goes to him first. "Arms out," she says. She runs the assessment — the ice expression's thermal residual, the sustained depletion, the specific indicators of an extended high-output engagement. She checks his hands. His eyes. The specific indicators she has developed over six years of patching up the Silvermane Guard after events that are above the Guard's engagement parameters. "Thirty-nine percent," she says. He looks at her. "You can read—" "I've been Gepard Landau's doctor for six years," she says. "I can read your depletion level by your eye quality and your hand temperature. Yes." She moves to Seele. "You. Left shoulder." "It's manageable," Seele says. "I didn't ask if it was manageable. I asked—" She does not, in fact, ask again. She examines the shoulder. Her expression does something. "The ribs," she says. "Fine," Seele says. "Fine like they were fine before the sub-level entries, or fine like they are actually fine?" Seele is quiet for a moment. "Fine like the latter," she says. "The old-gold warmth. It has been — active during the engagement. The ribs are further along than they should be." Natasha looks at her hands — at Seele's hands, the old-gold quality that has been visible since the primary structure's warmth conducted through her in the depression. She holds one more second. Then she moves to Luka. The chrome arm. The sandevistan. The left shoulder from the ankle-joint impact recoil that she read in the quality of his movement from thirty meters away. "Full burst deployed?" she says. "Once," he says. "After the point-blank discharge." "Charge reserve?" "Running," he says. "Not full. Sixty percent on the sandevistan. The arm is—" He checks the integration. "Above standard. The notation channel contact charged it above baseline." She notes this. "And the left shoulder." "It's manageable," he says. She gives him the look that she gives Seele when people use the word manageable as a substitute for the truth. "It is," he says. "I have been running the left shoulder at reduced load for the last twenty minutes. It is manageable within the engagement parameters." She looks at him for two more seconds. Then she moves to Svarog, which is a different kind of assessment — the oldest consciousness on the planet does not have the same physiological indicators as human fighters, but Natasha has been the outer machine district's doctor for long enough to know what to look for. Combat array at what percentage. Four-arm active versus two-arm active. Optical array response time. "You're at seventy percent," she says. "Approximately," Svarog says. She goes back to the center of the courtyard and she looks at all of them. "Here is what I can tell you," she says. "Gepard is at thirty-nine percent and will not be above forty-five percent in the next sixty minutes regardless of rest. The depletion rate of a two-hour sustained engagement does not reverse in sixty minutes. Seele's left shoulder is compromised but functional and her ribs are further along than they should be and I am going to ask Serval about that when this is over." She looks at Seele when she says this. Seele does not look away. "Luka's left shoulder is manageable and his charge reserve is above engagement minimum and the arm is operating above standard. Svarog is at seventy." "And your recommendation?" Bronya says. Natasha looks at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Belobog, who has been in the outer machine district since 0603 and who has not sat down since she arrived and who is currently asking for a medical recommendation about sending four fighters into a space below the third sub-level to address a Stellaron that has retreated into it. "My recommendation," Natasha says, "is that you give them everything in the medical kit, give them twenty minutes, and then make the call." She pauses. "And that you don't let Gepard argue about the twenty minutes." Gepard opens his mouth. "Twenty minutes," Natasha says. He closes his mouth. —— Twenty minutes. The courtyard is not quiet during twenty minutes. The Guard perimeter is running managed decay monitoring on the ambient formations — the presence-field withdrawal has dropped the generation rate significantly but the existing formations still need addressed, and Dunn has three perimeter lines managing the decay in the surrounding four blocks. Sampo is at the northwest junction running the secondary approach route for the last two Guard units that need repositioning. Serval is running updated calibration on the frequency equipment for whatever the second stage's second-phase encounter will require in an underground space. Pela is monitoring the geological movement signature — the second Stellaron, still descending. In the workshop, Clara and the Moles are running the notation network's deep channel analysis. "The channel goes to it," Clara says. Both hands on the floor now — not the wall junctions, the floor itself, the geological layer closest to the deep channel that the notation network's lowest section runs through. "I can feel the cave from here. Through the notation." She pauses. "It's — very large." Oleg looks at her. "Very large like the sub-levels?" "Larger," she says. Pino makes a sound. "How do you feel about that?" Toma says. Not strategic concern — the genuine Toma question, the one that is always specifically about how the person feels. Clara looks at her hands. At the warmth running through the floor and into her palms, the Architects' design conducting the source energy through the notation channels and through the deep cave and through the geological structure of Belobog in every direction. She feels the cave — the size of it, the age of it, the specific pre-Separation quality of something that was built before the outer machine district existed, before the notation network existed above it. She also feels the Stellaron in it. The starfield body, settling. The green eye. The presence-field at depth, in the space the Architects built for this. "Large enough," she says. "For what?" Oleg says. "For what has to happen," she says. She looks at the workshop wall. At the pre-Svarog notation carving. At the plan she has been building in the twenty minutes since the courtyard assessment. "Pino," she says. "The northwest channel junction. One more time." He looks at his arm. He looks at the northwest channel junction. "One more time," he says. He puts his hand on the wall. Oleg reaches into the jacket and produces what appears to be the last dark filling pastry. He hands it to Pino. "For after," he says. Pino looks at the pastry. He looks at the junction. He keeps his hand on the wall. "After," he agrees. —— In the courtyard, at minute fourteen of twenty, Seele sits beside Bronya. Not in the tactical arrangement — beside her, on the courtyard stone with the gold fragments, the way two people sit when they have been fighting parallel battles for two hours and twenty minutes and the twenty minutes of enforced rest has produced a space that is not operational. They have not had this space in a long time. In the game-canon version of who they are to each other, Seele and Bronya have been best friends since before the main story's events — the specific best-friendship of two people who grew up in the same hard place and found each other and have been finding each other since. The Underworld and the Surface divided them for years. They have been in the same city again for months. And in those months, the tunnel entries and the palace briefings and the full operational pace of a city managing a dual-Stellaron situation have not left much space for just — being beside each other. This is that space. "Your left shoulder," Bronya says. "It's manageable," Seele says. "Natasha said it was compromised," Bronya says. "Natasha says everything is compromised," Seele says. "It is her professional framework." Bronya looks at her. At the shoulder. At the quality of how Seele is holding herself — the specific careful stillness that is the managed version of something that is being accounted for. "It hurt when you landed from the south stack," Bronya says. "I saw it from the northern perimeter. You landed and then you went very still for one second." Seele looks at her. "I have been watching you fight for two hours and twenty minutes," Bronya says. "I notice things." "You were at the northern perimeter," Seele says. "Four hundred meters," Bronya says. "I have good eyes." Seele looks at the gold fragments on the stone around them. "It's manageable," she says. "What about yours?" "My what?" "You have been at the Guard perimeter for two hours addressing junction-class formation clusters with a rifle," Seele says. "With something in the shots that destabilized the clusters faster than the caliber should allow. Your hands." Bronya is quiet for a moment. "The first Stellaron," she says. "The attending quality. It has been present in my hands since the construction chamber and it has been more present since — the active field here. The second Stellaron's presence makes the first Stellaron's attending quality more — foreground." She pauses. "It is not my hands. It is what is in them." "Does it hurt?" Seele says. Bronya considers this. "No," she says. "It feels like — listening. A frequency I have known for a long time, running through my hands." She pauses. "It is not uncomfortable. It is large." Seele looks at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Belobog, twenty-three years old, sitting in a gold-fragment-scattered courtyard in the outer machine district at 0831 in the morning with the first Stellaron attending at 3.1 and the second Stellaron retreating to the deep cave below. At her best friend, who has been carrying the weight of this planet since she was twenty-two years old and who is, in this fourteen-minute space, simply sitting beside her on the stone. "Bronya," she says. "Yes?" "When this is over," Seele says. "I want to have a conversation that is not about Stellarons or notation networks or monitoring data." Bronya looks at her. Her expression does the thing it does when something has arrived that she was not expecting and that she is receiving fully. "Yes," she says. "I would like that." "Good," Seele says. "I have many things to tell you." "I am certain you do," Bronya says. There is something in her expression that is not the Guardian-register. Something older and warmer and entirely hers. "I have things to tell you as well." Seele looks at her. "About Gepard?" she says. Bronya is quiet for one second. "Among other things," she says, with the specific quality of someone who is being very careful. Seele looks at the courtyard. At Gepard, twenty meters away, in conversation with Svarog. "He is the most obvious person," she says, "I have ever watched be completely unaware that he is being obvious." "He is not unaware," Bronya says. "He absolutely was," Seele says. "For a very long time." "He is aware now," Bronya says. The specific quality — the warmth, the fullness of it. "Good," Seele says. "He should be." They sit in the fourteen-minute space and the courtyard does its morning around them and the gold fragments catch the light, and Bronya is the Supreme Guardian and Seele is the girl who grew up in the Underworld below the outer machine district and they are also, underneath and above and through all of those things, each other's best friend, and the space is not long enough but it is enough for now. —— At minute twenty, Gepard stands. He looks at the four fighters. At Natasha's kit. At the monitoring data. At the geological movement signature that Pela is tracking — the second Stellaron, settled in the deep cave now. Not moving further. Waiting. He makes the assessment. Thirty-nine percent is not optimal. Thirty-nine percent is not the number he would choose for this engagement. But the Stellaron is not waiting for him to reach a better number, and the deep cave below the third sub-level is not going to become easier to reach over time, and the second stage of the second Stellaron that they have been fighting for two hours and twenty minutes has retreated into the space it knows best and is waiting for them to come to it. The assessment is: they go. "Svarog," he says. "The deep channel access is through the secondary shaft below the third sub-level," Svarog says. He has been running this analysis since Luka mentioned the channel connection. "The shaft extends below its mapped depth. The cave below is accessible from the shaft's lowest point. I have never entered the cave. But I have heard it." He pauses. "The channel resonance from below the third sub-level has been present in my geological monitoring for forty years. I have been waiting to see what was down there for forty years." Gepard looks at him. "You're coming." "Yes," Svarog says. "I am coming." Gepard looks at Seele and Luka. At Natasha's assessment of both of them. At the specific readiness in both of them — the quality of fighters who have been in a two-hour engagement and have not resolved and are in the specific state of still going, still here, not done. "I need to know," he says, "that both of you can function in a close-quarters underground engagement for an extended period at current capacity." Luka looks at him. "I can function," he says. Seele looks at him. "I can function," she says. "And I am going regardless of what your assessment is." He looks at her. At the shoulder. At the old-gold warmth in her hands. At the specific expression of someone who has run at an impossible thing and will run at it again. "I know," he says. "I am asking because I need to know what you have available, not because I am deciding whether you go." He pauses. "We fight differently if you are at half-shoulder capacity. I need to know that before we are in the cave." Seele looks at him. At the specific quality of someone who has been assessed for tactical purposes rather than protected from tactical purposes. "Half-shoulder on the left," she says. "The right is full. The old-gold warmth is active — it has been active since the third approach on the wing joint and it is more present than it has been. I do not have the full vocabulary for what that means in terms of output but I know it is available." "Good," he says. He looks at Luka. "The sandevistan. If the cave geometry requires close-quarters fast movement, I need to know your burst reserve." "Two full bursts," Luka says. "Three if I push the recharge. The arm is above standard charge on the notation conductance. The shimmer conductance should work through the cave's geological structure — if the Architects built it, it has the same mineral composition as the notation network." He pauses. "And I was wrong." Gepard looks at him. "In the courtyard," Luka says. "When it went back into the bedrock. I said to Seele I thought the second stage wasn't trying to kill us." He looks at the bedrock. "It retreated to a space it knows. It has had two hours to recover in the bedrock. It went to ground it built for this." He pauses. "It is going to try to kill us." "Yes," Gepard says. "It is." Luka nods. "Good," Gepard says. "That is the correct assessment. I did not want to correct it in front of you." A pause from Luka. Then: "Why not?" "Because," Gepard says, "you would have argued." Seele makes the real laugh — brief, involuntary, completely genuine. She catches it. Luka looks at her. She looks at the cave below. "He's not wrong," she says, at something just above a whisper. —— They descend at 0836. Svarog first — the oldest consciousness on the planet, at seventy percent, four arms active, entering the secondary shaft below the third sub-level with the specific quality of someone who has been waiting forty years for this exact descent. Then Gepard. Then Seele. Then Luka. The secondary shaft at the sub-level three depth is the same shaft they used in the solo expedition — the warm crystal hand-holds of the secondary shaft entry, the specific ambient old-gold quality of the Architects' material. Luka and Seele both put their hands on the shaft wall when they enter it. The warmth is there. Less than it was on the expedition, the second Stellaron's memory function complete and the source energy running differently through the cave system now. But there. Below the third sub-level, the shaft continues. This is new. In the expedition, the shaft ended at the third sub-level's floor — the depression floor, the primary structure, the contact point. Now the shaft continues past the floor. Not a crack, not a structural failure. A designed extension — the Architects' hand-holds continuing down the shaft past the third sub-level floor, into stone that is older and colder and different in composition from the sub-level material above. Luka puts his chrome hand on the shaft wall in the deep extension. The mineral composition is the same as the notation network. He can feel the channel running through the stone — deep, wide, more volume than any of the upper channel junctions. The source energy running at a rate that makes the east stack junction feel like a tributary compared to a river. "The channel down here," he says. "I feel it," Clara says. Everyone looks up. Clara is at the top of the shaft. Looking down at them. The Moles are behind her — Toma to her left, Oleg to her right, Pino already with one hand on the wall because that appears to be his natural state now. "Clara," Gepard says. "The plan was for you to manage the notation network from the workshop." "The plan was for me to manage the notation network," Clara says. "The notation network's primary junction in the cave's geological structure is at the base of this shaft. From the workshop I can manage the upper channels. From down here I can manage everything." She pauses. "And I can feel the Stellaron from up here. The Architects built the deep formation for a reason. I think the reason is going to need me in it." Gepard looks at her. At the twelve-year-old standing at the top of the secondary shaft with the Moles behind her and the source code active in her hand and the specific expression of someone who has made the decision and is informing him of it rather than asking. "The shaft is—" he starts. "I have been in the sub-levels," Clara says. "I have been conducting notation channels during a seventy-meter Stellaron battle for two hours and twenty minutes. The shaft has hand-holds and the Moles are coming with me and I am not managing the notation network from the workshop when the primary junction is at the bottom." A pause. "The Moles are twelve," Gepard says. "I am twelve," Clara says. "I know," he says. "Then you understand," she says, "that age is not the relevant variable." From behind Clara, Oleg says: "She argued better than she usually does." "I was conserving," Clara says, not looking at him. Gepard looks at Svarog. Svarog says: "The cave's geological structure is stable. The formation environment below the third sub-level does not have the active generation density of the sub-levels during the solo expedition. And the Architects built the deep formation for her specifically." A pause. "I will not pretend that I can argue against the Architects' design." Gepard looks at Clara. "Stay close to the shaft entrance," he says. "You do not advance into the main cave until the engagement zone is established. Understood?" "Understood," Clara says. "The Moles stay with her."
