"The primary junction contact. The cave-floor conductance. The whip on the left tendril at full-burst approach." She pauses. "I have been doing frequency analysis for nine months. I know what I saw. That was — above what I expected from you." He looks at her. At the specific quality of a compliment from Serval Landau, who does not compliment things she does not mean. "Thank you," he says. "The arm's data," she says. "Tonight." "Tonight," he agrees. She goes to the workshop. He watches her go. He thinks about the first time she walked into Svarog's workshop eight months ago with frequency equipment on her hip and decided, in real time, whether to be charmed or competitive. He thinks about the way she calibrated in three minutes against a form class that didn't exist. He thinks about the specific warmth of the organic thing that has developed across eight months of workshop mornings and corridor entries and now this. He does not say any of this out loud. He files it where he files things that don't need words. —— Seele finds Bronya at the edge of the courtyard when the crowd has thinned. Not the full crowd — some Guard units are still managing the decay in the adjacent sections, some Wildfire are at the evacuation boundary doing final assessment runs. But the main assembly has dispersed and the courtyard is at the quieter level of a post-battle outer machine district morning. Bronya is looking at the workshop. At the notation carving visible in the stone above the secondary bench through the open workshop door. She is not looking at it for tactical purposes. She is just looking. Seele stands beside her. "Your speech," Seele says. "I did not plan any of it," Bronya says. "I know," Seele says. "That's why it worked." Bronya looks at her. At the specific expression Seele has when she is stating a thing that is accurate and expects it to be received as accurate without decoration. "They deserved to know," Bronya says. "All of them. What they did." "Yes," Seele says. "They did." They look at the workshop together. At the notation. At the cave below them — the frozen cave, the golden form, the Architects' Great Resting Place. "When this is quieter," Seele says. "Not today, not tomorrow. When it's quieter." She pauses. "I want to take you down there." Bronya looks at her. "To the cave?" "To the cave," Seele says. "To the golden form and the frozen space and the Architects' ruins at the far end. And the notation." She pauses. "Clara found something in the notation. Pino found three words. I want to show you what they found." Bronya is quiet for a moment. She looks at the workshop and through it at the cave below and the notation on the cave wall that predates everything in the outer machine district. "Yes," she says. "I would like that." "When it's quieter," Seele says. "When it's quieter," Bronya agrees. They stand in the post-battle courtyard and the outer machine district runs its midday around them and the first Stellaron holds at 3.1 below and the city stands above and these two people — best friends, the specific best-friendship of two women who grew up in the same hard place and found each other and are still finding each other — stand beside each other in the gold fragment field and say nothing for a full minute and the nothing is full. "Luka," Bronya says, eventually. Not with preamble. The word alone. Seele looks at her. Bronya's expression is — the warm version, the one that has been building since the fourteen-minute space in the courtyard. "You are going to the moon," she says. Seele looks at the dome. At the pale reflected light of it. At whatever is above the dome, unreachable and real. "Yes," she says. "He meant it," Bronya says. Not a question. "Yes," Seele says. "I know," Bronya says. "He has been—" She stops. She starts again. "He has a specific quality when he talks about you. Since the beginning. Since the first time he came to the palace for the second Stellaron briefing and you were in the room." She pauses. "I noticed." Seele looks at her. "You noticed." "I notice things," Bronya says, with the specific quality she used in the fourteen-minute space when she said I have good eyes. Seele is quiet for a moment. Then: "He is going to drive me to extraordinary lengths of frustration for the rest of my life," she says, "and I am going to let him, and he is going to take me to the moon." Bronya looks at her. At the specific expression on Seele's face — the full visible thing, the unguarded version, the one that does not catalogue and does not manage. "Good," Bronya says. Simply. Warmly. Completely. —— Gepard is at the workshop entrance when Bronya returns to the courtyard. He is still at eighteen percent. This has not changed in the last hour and will not change significantly for several more hours, and the eighteen percent Gepard standing at the workshop entrance is very specifically not going to Natasha's ward until Natasha forces the issue, because Natasha is currently managing Sampo's post-battle processing and Luka's arm data download and does not have time to force the issue yet. Bronya comes to stand beside him. He looks at her. She looks at him. "The speech," he says. "I know," she says. "You said Svarog's name," he says. "Yes," she says. "He looked—" Gepard stops. He looks at the workshop entrance. At Svarog inside it, at the secondary bench, running his post-engagement system assessment with the four primary arms at rest configuration. "He looked like someone who has been doing the work for a very long time and has not been told by the right person that the work mattered," Gepard says. "And you were the right person." Bronya looks at Svarog. At the oldest consciousness on the planet, in the workshop he has been running for longer than the outer machine district has had its current name, in the back room doing the assessment of a seventy-percent engagement output at several centuries of age. "I think he has been waiting," she says, "for a Guardian who meant it. Who really meant it." "He has been waiting for a long time," Gepard says. "Yes," she says. "I think so too." She pauses. "I am going to talk to him more. When it is quieter. About what he knows and what he has seen and what this city has been since before the records go back." She pauses again. "And about what he suspects about Clara." Gepard looks at her. "Clara," she says. "The Architects. The source code." She pauses. "Svarog suspects something. He has been careful about saying it. But after the cave—" She pauses. "Clara said she was part of the plan. Svarog said he suspected it for eight months. The plan that was left in the notation, in the source code, in the cave below the third sub-level." She looks at the workshop. "I want to know what Svarog knows." "When it's quieter," he says. "When it's quieter," she agrees. They stand in the post-battle courtyard. Eighteen percent and the specific warmth of the post-assembly midday. The gold fragments catching the light. The outer machine district running its afternoon. "Gepard," she says. He looks at her. "When we were in the cave," she says. "When Pela sent me the signal update at 1047 and the preliminary read said the form had returned to the bedrock and was still." She pauses. "I was at the northern perimeter. I was addressing the last cluster." She pauses again. "I had been listening to the first Stellaron for four and a half hours while the second one was in the cave. And when the preliminary said it was over—" She stops. "What?" he says, quietly. "The first Stellaron's attending quality," she says. "In my hands. It changed." She looks at her hands. "Not the CONSIDERING change that Pela documented at 0803. A different change. Warmer. As if something had been confirmed." She pauses. "I don't know how to describe it with more precision. But it changed when the preliminary came through, and what it changed to felt like—" She stops again. "Like what?" he says. She looks at him. "Like recognition," she says. "Like something that has been watching us for a very long time has recognized, finally, that we are what it thought we were." Gepard is quiet for a long moment. "What does that mean for the first Stellaron?" he says. "I don't know," she says. "Not yet." She looks at the geological structure below their feet. "But I am going to find out. Before it becomes relevant." He looks at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Belobog who just gave the first honest unplanned speech of her Guardian career to three hundred and fourteen people and received three hundred and fourteen people's love in return. At the person who crossed the district boundary at 0700 and addressed junction-class formation clusters with a Guardian's rifle and the first Stellaron's attending quality in her shots. At Bronya, twenty-three years old, carrying the full weight of the world and meaning every word of it. "You will," he says. He means it entirely. She looks at him. At eighteen percent and the Ice Flames he does not yet know she knows about because Svarog has not told her and the classified debrief file is in a secure location she has not accessed yet. At the person who went into the cave at 0836 with thirty-nine percent and came out at 1047 with eighteen and the second Stellaron defeated. She reaches over and takes his hand. Not the face this time. The hand — the cold-warm of the ice expression's ambient output, the specific hand that drives ice lances and ice walls and ice architecture and, apparently, the impossible thing that the classified debrief calls unprecedented. She holds it. He does not move his hand away. The outer machine district does its afternoon around them. —— At the workshop at 1417, Luka is sitting on the secondary bench while Natasha runs the arm's integration readings. Serval has the frequency equipment positioned at the bench's edge, running the data download from the arm's integration log — the full engagement record, every conductance rate, every contact event, the shimmer conductance data and the primary junction contact data and the cave-floor conductance wave output. The download will take another forty minutes. She has been running it since 1310 and is not going to stop until it finishes. Seele is at the back of the workshop, reading the notation above the secondary bench. She has been reading it for twenty minutes. She has been in the workshop for eight months and has never spent twenty minutes reading the notation above the secondary bench. Something about the cave and the Architects' ruins at the far end has changed how the notation reads — not the notation itself, the reader. She looks at the characters and she can feel more than she could before. Not fluency. Something between fluency and the ambient warmth of the primary junction at cave depth. She does not say this out loud. She files it. Luka looks at her, across the workshop. At the butterfly warmth at the edges of her. At the scythe on the workshop table, the blade still carrying the residual old-gold warmth of the cave's primary junction conductance. At the shoulder that she will feel tomorrow and that tonight has a cold pack on it that Natasha placed with the specific firmness of a person whose recommendations are not optional. She feels him looking. She turns around. "What?" she says. "Nothing," he says. She gives him the sideways look. "That is not a nothing expression," she says. "I was thinking," he says, "about the cave." "Specifically?" she says. "Specifically," he says, "about the moment you hit the posterior top section from sixty meters of vertical drop and the form screamed." She looks at him. "That is what you are thinking about." "It was," he says, with the specific flatness, "the single most extraordinary thing I have seen anyone do. I am thinking about it." Serval, from behind the frequency equipment, says: "It was the most extraordinary thing I have seen in nine months of frequency analysis." She does not look up from the download. "I am documenting it." Seele looks at Serval. At the equipment. At the download running. "You are going to write all of this up," she says. "Every word," Serval says. "The most accurate technical documentation of a Stellaron engagement in the history of Jarilo-VI." Seele looks at Luka. He looks at her. The corner of her mouth does the thing — the real one. "Good," she says. "Put the sixty-meter drop in the executive summary." "Already have," Serval says. —— At 1509, when the workshop has cleared of everyone except Svarog in the back room running the post-engagement system check, Seele and Luka leave together. Not in separate directions. Together — the specific together of people who know where they are going and that they are going there together. Out of the workshop, into the outer machine district's afternoon. The formation decay monitoring is running at the Guard perimeter. The ambient Fragmentum in the surrounding area is at mid-decay — lower than the post-engagement peak, higher than the pre-emergence baseline. The generator stacks are in various states of repair. The courtyard is still in the gold fragment field — the cleanup is tomorrow's task, multiple tasks, spread across several days. They walk through the outer machine district. Not toward Natasha's ward. Not toward the secondary shaft entrance or the corridor or any of the operational locations. Toward the residential stack. Toward the small specific space on the third level with the lamp and the shelf and the worn book with the chapters about the sky. "You don't have to come," she says. "Your shoulder—" "My shoulder is—" he starts. "Don't say manageable," she says. "—fine," he says. "I was going to say fine." She gives him the look. "It's above manageable," he says. "Definitively." "Definitively above manageable," she says. "Yes," he says. "That is the accurate version." She looks at him. At the chrome arm catching the outer district's afternoon light. At the integration points conducting the warmth that has been present since the cave's primary junction — still above standard, still running warm, the cave's charge dissipating slowly over hours the way deep notation contacts always do. "The moon," she says. Not a question. Not a prompt. Just the word. "The moon," he says. "You have been saying it since the lamp-lit room," she says. "Since before the lamp-lit room," he says. "I have been thinking it since before I said it." She looks at him. "Since when?" He considers this. The honest version. "Since the corridor entries," he says. "Since you told me about the book. What your mother read you. The chapters about the sky." He pauses. "Since you told me about wanting it since you were four years old and I thought: I want to give her that." She is quiet for a moment. "That was months before the lamp-lit room," she says. "Yes," he says. "You carried it for months," she says. "Yes," he says. She walks beside him in the outer machine district's afternoon. The generator stacks. The junction notation. The blue-green residual bioluminescence in the courtyard stone — dim, visible, the cave's mineral light still present at the surface through the geological structure. She walks beside him and she thinks about months of him carrying the moon promise before he said it, and the corridor entries, and the branch floor, and the cave, and the arm's warmth when she puts her hand on it. "You are going to frustrate me," she says. "For the rest of my life. It is a fact." "Yes," he says. "I am going to let you," she says. "I know," he says. "Don't say I know," she says. "I am aware of that," he says, instead. She makes the sound — the real one, brief and genuine. He does not react to it. He has catalogued the real laugh since the branch floor and he does not make it self-conscious by reacting to it. He just walks beside her at the correct speed, the speed they figured out when they were eight years old in the formation chamber with Vera watching, the speed that keeps them together. At the door of the residential stack, she stops. She turns to him. She looks at him — at the chrome arm and the spine integration and the new haircut that she trimmed in the workshop before the last contact event, the specific version of him that has been building across eight months and the cave and the arm and all of it. She reaches up and takes his face in both her hands. Not the way he does it — the thumbs tracing the cheekbones, the careful hold. Her hands on his face the specific way that she has, the specific way that is hers and not anyone else's, the way that arrived fully formed the first time she did it because it was always going to be this way. "Luka," she says. "Yes," he says. "I love you," she says. Not the cave version, not the formation chamber version. The afternoon version. The residential stack doorway version. The version that exists when there is nothing pressing in on it and no extraordinary context and it is just the two of them in the outer machine district and it is simply true. "I love you," he says. Same register. Same weight. She pulls him down and kisses him — the afternoon version, the doorway version, the one that is not the lamp-lit room's full weight or the formation chamber's earned significance but is also not small. It is the version of two people who have been through the impossible and are standing on the other side of it and know exactly who they are to each other. He kisses her back. This part he has. He has always had this part. They go inside. —— At the workshop at 1622, Svarog is in the back room. Not working — being present. The post-engagement system check completed at 1540. The back room quiet. The workshop around him running its ordinary afternoon — the secondary sensor display on standby, the frequency equipment Serval left on charge, the notation channel junctions on the floor and walls running warm, the residual Ice Flames warmth still present in the geological structure above and through and below. He looks at his hands. At the four primary arms at rest configuration. At the workshop. At what happened today. He has been on this planet for a long time. He has seen the Separation and the pre-Separation and the things before both of those. He has been protecting humanity in the outer machine district since before the outer machine district had the name it has now. He has maintained the second Stellaron's geological monitoring for forty years. He has been watching Clara for eight months. He has been waiting for the plan to become legible. Today the plan became legible. He thinks about what Bronya said in the assembly. Your work. The oldest consciousness on the planet, at his secondary bench, and the Supreme Guardian of Belobog said your work to him in front of three hundred and fourteen people and meant it. He thinks about what Gepard's hands looked like. The Ice Flames. He has the geological monitoring data from the moment of the Manchester Smash impact — the conductance rate through the primary junction, the ice propagation wave, the specific additional quality of the blue-white fire propagating alongside it through the notation channels. He has been running the analysis since 1135. He has the classification now. He has had the suspicion for eight months — since the northern Sector A formations began running warmer than the first Stellaron's ambient output could account for, since the specific thermal signature in the patrol reports changed quality in the fourth month of the northern ridge mission classified follow-up. He knows what Gepard is becoming. He knows what the Ice Flames are. He looks at the workshop ceiling. At the notation above the secondary bench. At the pre-Svarog carvings that are the Architects' design, that Clara has been learning to read for eight months, that run into the cave below and the geological structure in every direction. He thinks about what comes next. The first Stellaron at 3.1, Considering. The gap between 3.1 and 4.7. The unknown variable. The thing that will push the first Stellaron's progression to its full activation — Pela does not know what it is, Bronya suspects it is something related to herself, Svarog has a hypothesis. He is going to need to tell Bronya. Not today. Today the city needs to be what it is — the city that defeated a Stellaron, the city that is running its afternoon with the specific quality of something that has been through the impossible and is standing on the other side. He is going to need to tell Bronya, at a time when she is ready to receive it, what he knows about the gap between 3.1 and 4.7 and what he suspects about the role she plays in it. He is also going to need to tell Gepard what the Ice Flames are. At a time when Gepard is above thirty percent. And Clara. He is going to need to tell Clara what he knows about the Architects and what the notation says and what the source code in the second Stellaron's deeper content was placed there for and what he suspects about her specifically. At a time when she is ready to receive all of it. Which, knowing Clara, will be very soon. He sits in the back room. He waits. He has been waiting for a long time. He knows how to wait. The notation network runs its warmth through the geological structure of the outer machine district in every direction. The cave below is frozen. The golden form rests in it. The Architects' Great Resting Place, doing exactly what the Architects built it for. Belobog defeated a Stellaron today. It is the beginning of the next thing. —— At 1847, Gepard is in Natasha's ward. Not because he chose to be — because Natasha found him at the workshop entrance at 1810 and said: you are at eighteen percent and it has been four hours and you are coming to the ward. He came to the ward. This is not compliance. This is the recognition that Natasha is correct and Natasha is always correct in these specific situations and arguing would cost energy he does not have. He is sitting in the examination chair. The assessment is complete. Natasha has noted, in his file, the depletion level, the standard recovery timeline, the specific indicators of extended high-output engagement that she has been noting in this file for six years. She has also noted, in a separate section of the file, something she writes as anomalous thermal signature at impact points — the specific warmth that was present in his hands after the Manchester Smash, the quality she has been looking at since 1810. She is not going to ask him about it tonight. She is going to let him recover and she is going to review Serval's frequency documentation when it is complete and she is going to come to her own conclusions with the information available. Then she will ask. "Three days," she says. "Minimum. Ward rest minimum. Restricted Zone engagement minimum." "The first Stellaron," he says. "Is at 3.1 and has been at 3.1 all day," she says. "Pela is monitoring it. Clara has the notation network. Three days." He looks at her. "And if the signal moves?" "Then you were resting when it moved and you are above eighteen percent when you need to respond," she says. "Three days." He looks at the ward door. At the courtyard outside it, visible through the window. At the gold fragment field, still there, catching the evening light. "Bronya," he says. "Bronya is fine," Natasha says. "I assessed her at 1610. The Guardian's rifle sustained output produced minor thermal residual in the hands. Cold packs. She is at the palace." She pauses. "She is also not at eighteen percent. She is considerably above eighteen percent and does not need three days." "Good," he says. "She does need sleep," Natasha says. "Which you do as well." He looks at the ward. At the examination chair. At the window with the courtyard outside. He thinks about the Ice Flames. He has been thinking about them, in the specific way that you think about something you have been carrying for eight months, since the Manchester Smash. He has been thinking about the way the cave received them — the notation network conducting them upward through the geological structure, the upper channels warm from them for hours afterward. He has been thinking about what Svarog's expression looked like when the Ice Flames came up. He has been thinking about what Svarog knows. "At a time when I'm above thirty percent," he says. Natasha looks at him. "What?" "Nothing," he says. "A conversation I need to have when I'm above thirty percent." She looks at him for three seconds with the diagnostic expression. "Sleep," she says. "And eat. And don't say you're not hungry." "I'm—" "Sampo's pastries were four hours ago," she says. "You need real food." He is, in fact, hungry. "Fine," he says. She goes to get real food. He sits in the examination chair. He looks at his hands. The Ice Flames are not there now. The standard ice expression at ambient — the cool that has been in his hands since the year he was seventeen and the ice came up for the first time in the Restricted Zone's secondary monitoring station on a solo training run. The standard expression. Familiar, old, entirely his. He knows what lives at the edge of it now. He has known since the northern ridge, the eleventh month, the Trampler formation. He has known for eight months, carrying it alone, the classified debrief receiving it and filing it as unprecedented and the three officials who sent him retiring six months later. Bronya is going to find out. He knows this. She is the Supreme Guardian and the classified files are available to her and she is specifically the kind of person who pursues things she needs to know. She is going to approach the retired officials. She is going to read the debrief. And when she reads it, she is going to come to him with the specific quality of someone who has received information about the person they care about most and wants to understand it from the source. He does not mind this. He is, in fact, ready for this. He looks at his hands. At the standard ice expression. At eighteen percent and three days of ward rest and the first Stellaron at 3.1 and Belobog's first Stellaron defeat in the history of the planet resting in the frozen cave below the outer machine district. He closes his hands. The ice, at its standard expression, is cold and familiar and entirely his. At the edge of it, very quiet, the blue-white fire waits. —— That night. The outer machine district does its evening — the generators cycling down to nighttime output, the residential sections lighting up as the evacuated residents return to their spaces, the Guard's nighttime rotation taking over from the day deployment. The ambient formations in the surrounding area are at thirty percent of the post-engagement peak and declining — by morning, Pela's model says, the surrounding district will be at pre-emergence baseline. In the cave below the third sub-level, the golden form rests in the ice. In the workshop, Svarog does his evening assessment of the notation network's conductance rate — the channels warm, the deep cave's primary junction running at above-standard output, the residual warmth of the Manchester Smash's Ice Flames still present in the upper channels. He notes this in the geological monitoring log. He notes the specific quality of what the Ice Flames produced in the notation channels: not the source material warmth of the arm's conductance, not the formation-dissolving quality of the shimmer conductance. Something that has not been in these channels before, and which the channels received without resistance, as if it was always part of what they were built to carry. He closes the monitoring log. He thinks about the Architects. He thinks about what they built and why and for whom, and about what the notation says in the sections he can read and the sections he cannot yet read and the sections that Clara is learning to read faster than anyone he has encountered in several centuries. He thinks about the first Stellaron at 3.1. He thinks about what CONSIDERING means. He closes the back room door. In Natasha's ward, Gepard is asleep. This has not happened before twenty-two hundred hours in six years of Gepard's medical file. Natasha notes the time: 2147. She writes, next to the anomalous thermal signature entry: recovery nominal. She does not note what she has decided about the thermal signature. She has decided something. It will wait for Serval's documentation to be complete, and then she will have her confirmation, and then she will note it properly. She closes the file. In the residential stack, the lamp is on. On the shelf above the window, the worn book with the chapters about the sky. The chapters about what the stars look like without a dome in the way. The chapters about the moon. Outside, the dome of Belobog. The pale reflected light of it. And beyond the dome, somewhere, unreachable yet but real — the moon. Promised. The distance between the promise and the keeping of it is the distance between where Belobog is and where it is going, and the direction is clear, and today of all days the direction is clear, because Belobog defeated a Stellaron today and the city that defeats a Stellaron is a city that is becoming something it has never been before. The lamp is on. The moon is promised. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1. Tomorrow: the cleanup begins. The generator housings. The courtyard stone. The formation decay monitoring. The debrief documentation. Serval's technical file. The cave access protocol. The ward's three-day recovery. The first conversations that the Considering quality of the first Stellaron's signal is going to eventually make necessary. Tonight: the outer machine district does its evening. The city loves its people. Its people love it back. END OF CHAPTER 44 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 45: The Day After Chapter Text The outer machine district at 0700 the morning after looks like the morning after. The gold fragments are still in the courtyard — the cleanup is today's first task, spread across the Guard units that Dunn assigned to non-combat district restoration at 0600. The generator housings that the second Stellaron's discharges took out are marked with repair crew assignments and staged materials. The courtyard stone that the presence-field discharge fused in the engagement is cordoned with warning markers because fused courtyard stone is a slip hazard and the Guard's maintenance protocol is extremely thorough about slip hazards even immediately following Stellaron battles. The notation network is running its morning conductance at above-standard warmth — the residual of the Manchester Smash's propagation still present in the upper channels, dissipating but not yet gone. The formation density in the surrounding four blocks is at twelve percent of the post-engagement peak. Pela's model said pre-emergence baseline by morning and the model is, as Pela's models typically are, correct. The shimmer in the air is gone. The generator stacks that survived produce their standard output. The residential sections' windows are cracked in four buildings — repair crews staged — but the structures are sound. The outer machine district is the morning after the day it defeated a Stellaron. This is an unprecedented category of morning after. Pela has noted this in the monitoring log: UNPRECEDENTED CATEGORY — NO PRIOR REFERENCE STANDARD. She noted it at 0547, when she arrived at the analysis room having slept for five hours and fifty-one minutes, which is the most sleep she has gotten in three days, and opened the log, and looked at the formation decay data confirming baseline recovery, and wrote the entry. Then she opened Serval's technical documentation — which Serval sent to her at 0112 in the morning with the subject line PRELIMINARY: STELLARON ENGAGEMENT DATA — and began reading. She has been reading it for one hour and thirteen minutes. It is the most detailed Stellaron engagement document in the history of Belobog. Possibly of Jarilo-VI. Pela is reading it with the specific quality of someone who built the models that the engagement ran inside of and is now receiving the full data of what actually happened in those models at ground level, from a source who was on the ground with frequency equipment and three months of advance calibration preparation. The arm's shimmer conductance data alone is going to require her to rebuild three sections of the Fragmentum interaction model. She sets this aside. She looks at the first Stellaron's signal. 3.1. Holding. The CONSIDERING quality still present in the data — the entry she wrote at 0803 yesterday morning is still the accurate description of the signal's character. Considering. The large and patient thing that watched everything that happened in the outer machine district yesterday with the specific attention of something that is in the process of deciding. It has not moved in twenty-six hours. She does not know whether this is reassuring or not. She decides that not having a prior reference standard is the correct framework and writes: CONSIDERING — DAY 2. SIGNAL STABLE. MONITORING CONTINUES. She closes the monitoring log. She opens Serval's documentation again. She goes back to the shimmer conductance section. —— Clara wakes at 0631. This is the latest she has woken in eight months. The standard is 0545 because the notation network's morning conductance has a specific quality at 0545 that she has been getting up to feel since the first time she accidentally felt it on the third morning after arriving at the workshop. The standard is 0545 and today it is 0631 because yesterday was the day it was and her body has made an executive decision about 0545 that Clara does not disagree with. She lies in her bunk in the residential stack and she looks at the ceiling. She thinks about the cave. She thinks about both hands on the notation channel junctions for two hours and eleven minutes. She thinks about the primary junction at cave depth and what it felt like when the Manchester Smash propagated through it — the specific wave of ice and fire conducting upward through the geological structure, the channels carrying something they had apparently been built to carry. She thinks about the Architects' notation in the cave. About Great Resting Place. About Pino finding three words. She thinks about what Svarog said. I suspected. For approximately eight months. She lies in her bunk for four minutes and thirty seconds — long enough to have thought all of those things — and then she gets up, because thinking requires action and the action is going to Svarog. —— Svarog is in the back room at 0638 when Clara arrives. He is not doing the system check — the system check was completed at 0310 this morning, well within the standard post-engagement assessment window. He is sitting in the back room in the specific configuration he uses when he is present rather than working, which is: four primary arms at rest, optical array at standard, the geological monitoring running on the secondary display in the corner. He looks at the monitoring display when Clara comes in. "The conductance is at above-standard warmth still," he says. "The Ice Flames' residual. It will dissipate by 1400." "I felt it when I woke up," she says. "Through the floor." She sits on the secondary bench across from him. She looks at him. At the optical array. At the four arms at rest. At the oldest consciousness on the planet in the back room of his own workshop the morning after the second Stellaron of Belobog was defeated. "You said you suspected for eight months," she says. "Yes," he says. "What did you suspect?" she says. He looks at her. He has been considering when to have this conversation since the assembly yesterday. He has decided that Clara's version of ready is not a future state — it is her baseline state, the natural mode of a twelve-year-old who grew up in the outer machine district and found the notation network with her hands and read the Architects' design through her palms and conducted the primary junction during a Stellaron battle without being told she could. She is ready. She has been ready. He has been the one who needed to be ready. "The Architects," he says. "Yes," she says. "The pre-Svarog notation sections," he says. "The sections in the outer machine district's geological structure that predate my arrival on this planet. I have been monitoring them since I arrived. I have been reading the notation in them since I had the vocabulary." He pauses. "There are sections I can read fully. There are sections I can read partially. There are sections that I cannot read — not because the language is beyond me, but because the sections are specifically designed to be read by a particular kind of reader." She looks at him. "What kind?" "Someone who carries the Architects' signal in their hands," he says. "Someone whose contact with the notation network produces the specific conductance quality that the network was designed to respond to." He pauses. "Someone like you." Clara looks at her hands. At the palms that have been on channel junctions and cave floors and notation carvings for eight months. At the warmth that is in them — not the notation network's warmth, something prior to it. Something that runs in her specifically. "I am part of the plan," she says. She has said this before — in the workshop yesterday, to Toma. "The Architects built the notation network to carry something like the Ice Flames. They left the source code in the second Stellaron's deeper content. They built the cave. They wrote Great Resting Place in the notation before it happened." She looks at Svarog. "They planned for me specifically." "Yes," he says. "What am I?" she says. Not with fear. With the direct quality she uses when she needs an accurate answer and is not interested in the managed version. Svarog is quiet for a long moment. Not hesitating — measuring. The specific measurement of someone who has a precise answer and is deciding how to give it to a twelve-year-old without the precision becoming a weight she cannot carry yet. He decides she can carry it. "The Architects," he says, "were not human. The entities that built the pre-Svarog notation sections, that placed the source code in the second Stellaron's content, that built the cave below the third sub-level and named it before the events that would name it — they were not human in any classification I have documentation for." He pauses. "They were something older and different and purposeful in ways that the geological record suggests operated on a timeline that makes my own arrival on this planet seem recent." He looks at her. "And I believe that you, Clara, carry a lineage that traces back to them." Clara looks at her hands. "A descendant," she says. "Yes," he says. "Not a direct descendant — the timeline does not allow for that, the Architects predate any biological human lineage I have documentation of. Something more complex. Something that the Architects built into the lineage of the people they were protecting, at a point in the geological record that I cannot fully reconstruct, to ensure that when the time came, there would be someone who could read what they left." He pauses. "Someone who could hold the notation network during the event the cave was built for." She looks at the cave below — or in the direction of the cave below, the floor of the workshop, the geological structure below the floor. At the golden form resting in the frozen cave. At the Architects' ruins at the far end. "They built all of it," she says. "The cave, the notation, the network, the source code — and they built me into the lineage to be here when it happened." "Yes," Svarog says. "I believe so." She looks at him. "Does Bronya know?" "No," he says. "I am going to tell her. When she comes to me — and she will come to me, she is the Supreme Guardian and she has the specific quality of pursuing things she needs to know. When she comes, I will tell her." "And Gepard?" she says. "Gepard will learn through Bronya," he says. "In the normal way." She thinks about this. She thinks about the cave and the notation and the primary junction and the Manchester Smash's Ice Flames conducting through channels the Architects built for exactly this. She thinks about being twelve years old and a descendant of the Architects and the most important conductor of the planetary notation network during a Stellaron battle. "The sections I can't read yet," she says. "Yes," he says. "The sections specifically designed for the kind of reader I am," she says. "Yes," he says. "I want to learn them," she says. "All of them. Every section I can't read yet — I want to read them. I want to understand the full notation." She pauses. "Can you teach me?" Svarog looks at her. At the twelve-year-old on the secondary bench with the Architects' lineage in her hands and the determination of someone who has been told something very large and has decided that the correct response is to learn more. "Yes," he says. "I can teach you." "Starting today?" she says. "Starting this morning," he says. She nods. She does not say thank you — she files it and reaches for the workshop wall and puts her palm against the notation carving above the secondary bench and lets the channel's warmth run through her palm. "This section," she says. "I know most of this one. There are four characters I don't have yet." "Show me which four," Svarog says, and he begins. —— At 0840, the Moles arrive at the workshop. This is not unusual. The Moles arrive at the workshop most mornings. What is unusual is the quality of their arrival — Toma first, with the expression of someone who has been processing the previous day since approximately 1900 last night and has arrived at a specific conclusion. Oleg second, with the jacket, from which he produces a wrapped item that he sets on the secondary bench immediately. Pino last, who goes directly to the northwest channel wall and puts his hand on it and says: "Still warm." "Still warm," Clara confirms, without looking up from the notation carving. Pino looks at the wall. He looks at his hand. He looks at Clara. "The Ice Flames," he says. "From yesterday." "From yesterday," Clara says. He holds his hand on the wall. He does not say anything else. This is Pino processing the Ice Flames through the notation channel warmth residual, which is a very Pino thing to do. "What is that?" Oleg says, pointing at the wrapped item on the secondary bench. "You put it there," Toma says. "I know I put it there," Oleg says. "I'm asking what it is in the context of whether someone is going to eat it or whether it is a workshop item." "It came from the jacket," Toma says. "So it is either edible or useful." "Both are possible," Oleg says. Clara looks at it. "It is a calibration chip," she says. "Standard frequency notation. Where did you get a calibration chip?" "The jacket," Oleg says. "Why does the jacket have a calibration chip?" Clara says. "The jacket has many things," Oleg says. "Not all of them can be explained." Clara looks at it. She looks at Oleg. "That chip is from the secondary sensor display," she says. "The one Serval left on the bench when she was downloading Luka's arm data last night." Oleg looks at the chip. "Oh," he says. "Put it back on the bench," Clara says. "I'm not sure how it got into the jacket," Oleg says. "The jacket took it," Toma says. "The jacket would not do that," Oleg says, without conviction. "Oleg," Clara says. "Putting it back," Oleg says. He puts it back on the bench. He produces a dark filling pastry from the jacket instead, which he eats, which is the appropriate item for 0840 in the morning. Toma sits on the floor across from Clara and looks at the notation carving she is working through. He looks at the four characters she has not read yet. He looks at the way her palm is on the wall and the warmth conducting through the contact. "You figured something out," he says. "This morning. Before we got here." She looks at him. "Svarog told me something." "Something large?" he says. "Yes," she says. He does not ask what. Toma has a specific intelligence for when something is being processed and needs space rather than questions. He looks at the notation. "We can help with the four characters," he says. "Pino and I have been reading the workshop notation for eight months. We know some of them." "I know," she says. "Not all of them," he says. "But some." "Yes," she says. She looks at the four characters. She looks at Toma. "This one," she says, pointing. "What do you read in this one?" He looks at it. He thinks. "Something like — carry," he says. "Or hold. Between the two." "Between carry and hold," Clara says. "Between carry and hold," he says. She looks at the character. At the warmth running through her palm. At the notation above the secondary bench that predates everything in the outer machine district. "Keep," she says. "Between carry and hold is keep. It means keep." Toma looks at it. He looks at Clara. "Keep," he says. "Yes. That's right." She looks at the other three characters. "Pino," she says. "Yes?" Pino says, from the northwest channel wall. "Come look at this section," she says. "I need the other three." Pino takes his hand off the wall. He comes to the notation carving. He puts his hand near — not on, near — and reads. —— Pela arrives at the workshop at 0914. She has Serval's technical documentation on the tablet and she has the arm integration data that Serval extracted and she has the formation decay monitoring update which confirms pre-emergence baseline at 0847 and she has the specific expression of someone who has been reading detailed technical documentation since 0547 and has arrived at conclusions that need external review. She looks at the workshop. At Clara and the Moles at the notation carving. At Svarog in the back room doorway watching them. At the secondary bench with the notation Svarog was teaching. At the calibration chip that is back in its proper place. She looks at Clara. "The shimmer conductance," she says. "Luka's arm conducting through the Fragmentum shimmer as a carrier for the notation network's source material." She puts the tablet on the secondary bench and points at the section she has been looking at since 0612. "Serval's data says the conductance rate was consistent with a notation channel junction at secondary level. Which means the shimmer in the air during the engagement was functioning as a secondary notation channel." Clara looks at her. "Yes." "The Fragmentum shimmer formed a notation channel," Pela says. "In the air." "The Fragmentum crystallization is mineral," Clara says. "The notation network runs through mineral. When the shimmer density was high enough, the arm's source material conductance found the path." "I know that's what happened," Pela says. "I have the data. What I don't have is the model for how the shimmer's mineral density threshold is calculated in relation to the arm's charge level and conductance range." She pauses. "I need to rebuild that section of the model and I need your input on the threshold parameters because your hands on the cave floor were the receiving point and you will have felt the conductance characteristics directly." Clara looks at her. "You want me to help rebuild the model?" "I want your data," Pela says. "Yes." Clara looks at the notation carving. She looks at the three characters she still has not read. She looks at Toma, who gives her the expression that means: this is also important and the notation will be here later. "The threshold parameters," Clara says. "I need a clean workbench and the secondary sensor display." "Both available," Pela says. Clara gets up from the floor. She goes to the secondary bench. She looks at Pela. "You have been reading Serval's documentation since before six in the morning," she says. "Since 0547," Pela says. "Have you eaten anything?" Clara says. Pela looks at her. At the twelve-year-old who is about to help her rebuild three sections of the Fragmentum interaction model and who is asking whether she has eaten. "No," she says. Oleg, from behind them, produces something from the jacket and holds it out. Pela looks at it. It is another dark filling pastry. "The jacket," Pela says. "The jacket," Clara confirms. "It has significant capacity," Pela says. "We have stopped asking about it," Clara says. Pela takes the pastry. She opens the secondary sensor display. She pulls up the Fragmentum interaction model. She looks at Clara. "Threshold parameters," she says. "Starting with the shimmer density minimum," Clara says, and she puts her palm on the secondary bench's wall-mounted notation junction and lets the channel's warmth tell her the number. —— The celebration is not formal. This is important. There is no formal celebration. What there is: Pela building the model with Clara and, eventually, Toma who turns out to have been tracking the notation network's conductance patterns with the specific thoroughness of someone who holds walls for hours and pays attention while doing it. What there is: Oleg producing things from the jacket at regular intervals — pastries, then the calibration chip again (he has already explained that this time it fell in on its own), then what appears to be a small tool that Svarog recognizes as belonging to the secondary monitoring array and receives without comment, then more pastries. What there is: Pino reading the notation carving, the fourth character, coming to Clara at 1015 with: "Lineage. I think the fourth one means lineage." She looks at it. She looks at Pino. "Yes," she says. "That's right." She looks at the full section — the four characters she now has, the larger context of the notation carving above the secondary bench. She reads it. She reads it again. She says, quietly: "Those Who Keep the Lineage." Pela stops mid-calculation. "What?" "The notation above the secondary bench," Clara says. "I've been reading sections of it for eight months. Svarog taught me more this morning. And Pino found the fourth character I was missing." She looks at the carving. "The full section reads: Those Who Keep the Lineage, the Network Knows Your Hands." The workshop is very quiet. Toma looks at Clara. At her hands. At the notation. "The notation network knows your hands," he says. "Yes," she says. "It has always known your hands," he says. "Yes," she says. "I think so." Pino looks at his hand. At the hand that has been on notation channel walls and cave floors and northwest channel junctions. At the hand that found Great Resting Place in the cave and lineage in the workshop notation. "Clara," he says. "Yes?" she says. "You are," he says, and then he stops because he is looking for the right word and the right word is large and he is twelve years old finding it in real time, "something specific," he finishes. "Yes," she says. "I think I am." And then Oleg, who has been listening to all of this from the floor while eating the pastry that was not the calibration chip, says: "That is the coolest thing that has ever happened in this workshop." Which is, given what has happened in this workshop in the last eight months, a statement of significant competition. But nobody disagrees with it. Pela, who has been standing very still since Those Who Keep the Lineage, looks at the notation carving. At Clara. At the model she is building with the conductance threshold parameters that Clara's hands know directly. She adds a new section to the model documentation: ARCHITECT LINEAGE — CONFIRMED. NOTATION NETWORK RECOGNITION — DIRECT. PRIMARY JUNCTION CONDUCTANCE — NATIVE CAPABILITY. She looks at what she has written. She looks at Clara. "Clara," she says. "Yes?" Clara says. "When you are ready to understand the full scope of what the Architects built and why," Pela says, "I want to be there for that conversation." Clara looks at her. At the sixteen-year-old head of Belobog's intelligence division asking to be included in the understanding of a planetary notation network that predates Belobog's records. "You already are," she says. "You've been at the notation junction since 0914." Pela looks at the wall. At the junction she has been standing near for the last hour and a half. She looks at her own hands. "I suppose I have," she says. —— At 1100, the Moles celebrate properly. Not with a formal structure — with the specific quality that twelve-year-olds celebrate, which is: loud, overlapping, involving food from the jacket, involving Toma's version of a recap that hits every beat of the last twenty-four hours in order of significance, and involving Pino and Clara sitting close together at the secondary bench while Pela pretends to be running the model update and is in fact listening to everything with the expression of someone who is storing memories with the specific intention of carrying them for a long time. Toma's recap: "The emergence at 0617, the corridor, the notation conductance through the shimmer — I want to pause on the shimmer conductance because that was—" "Extraordinary," Oleg says. "I was going to say unprecedented," Toma says. "But extraordinary works." "Both," Clara says. "Both," Toma agrees. "The cave floor. The primary junction. The Manchester Smash." He looks at them all. "We were there. All four of us. For the first defeat of a Stellaron in the history of Belobog." A pause. "We were there," Pino says. Simply, with the quality of something that has been turning over in him since yesterday and has just been said accurately. "Yes," Oleg says. He pulls something from the jacket. It is, to everyone's surprise, a small worn notebook — not food, not a tool. A notebook with something written on the first page. "I've been writing it down," he says. "Since the first corridor entry. What happened. In order." Everyone looks at him. He looks at the notebook. "I don't talk about it," he says. "But I write things down." He looks at Clara. "You found the notation. You read it. You conducted the cave floor and the northwest channel and the primary junction. The notation network knows your hands." He looks at the notebook. "I wanted to write it down before I forgot any of it." Clara looks at him. At the Oleg who eats from the jacket and provides live commentary on Stellaron battles and is in possession of workshop equipment he cannot explain and who has been writing things down. She reaches over and puts her hand on his arm. She does not say anything. She does not need to. He puts the notebook away. Pela sets down the tablet. "In approximately twelve years," she says, "when the full scope of what happened in the outer machine district this week is properly documented and understood, and when someone asks who the people were — I am going to say: a twelve-year-old girl who carries the Architects' lineage in her hands. Three other twelve-year-olds who held channel walls and wrote things down and found words in notation carvings. A sixteen-year-old intelligence head who was on a notation junction for ninety minutes eating pastries from a jacket of unknown capacity." She pauses. "Those were the people." The workshop is very warm. "The jacket has known capacity," Oleg says. "It is simply more than expected." "Yes," Pela says. "That is an accurate description of everyone in this room." —— Sampo goes down to the cave at 1130. He and Luka descend the secondary shaft in the same way Luka and Seele descended it for the solo expedition and the way the full group descended yesterday — the Architects' hand-holds, the extension below the third sub-level floor, the deep-channel mineral composition of the shaft walls conducting the primary notation network's warmth under every grip. Sampo does not say anything for the entire descent. Luka does not try to fill it. He knows what Sampo is descending toward and he knows that descending toward it requires its own space. At the cave floor, they stand. The cave is frozen. The ice on every surface — the walls, the ceiling, the floating formations in their stopped circulation, the ancient scaffolding, the Architects' ruins at the far end. The bioluminescent minerals visible through the ice as blue-green points, dim and present. The cave lit by its own captured light. And at the far end: the golden form. Sampo looks at it. He does not move toward it immediately. He stands at the shaft entrance, at the edge of the cave, and he looks at the golden armor from one hundred and thirty meters away. At the articulated plates. At the folded wing — the left wing joint that Seele addressed, the joint damage visible even in death. At the collapsed right arm. At the scale of it in the frozen space. He looks at the cave. He looks at the Architects' notation on the cave walls — the pre-Svarog language, the carvings that run from the shaft entrance to the far end, lining the cave's walls at the height where the notation network's channels run through the mineral structure. "The Architects built this," he says. "Yes," Luka says. "Before the district," Sampo says. "Before the district," Luka says. "Before most things above it." Sampo walks. He walks toward the golden form. Not fast — with the specific deliberate pace of someone who is approaching something that requires approach rather than arrival. Luka walks with him. The cave floor is frozen but the mineral warmth of the primary notation junction is in it — the old-gold conducting upward through the ice, visible as a very faint warmth glow in the floor beneath the ice layer. The cave is cold. It is the cold of something preserved and the cold of ice that has not been ice for twenty-four hours yet, settling, becoming part of the cave's permanent state. Sampo stops three meters from the golden form's right side. He looks up at the armor. At the scale of it — from this distance, the armor fills his sight line. Gold on every surface. The articulation of each plate catching the dim bioluminescence and throwing it back as a low warm light. The wing. The head, facing forward, the eyes closed, the expression of the form in death not the threat-expression or the intelligence-expression of the engagement but something else. Something at rest. "It held them," Sampo says. "Yes," Luka says. "For twenty-two years," Sampo says. "Two children's most important memories. It held them until they were ready." He pauses. "Because Vera gave it the reason to." Luka is quiet. "I was at the gap in the stone," Sampo says. "In the memory — the full memory, when it came back. I was at the gap in the stone at junction twelve when it happened. I tried to go through the gap at three minutes forty-two seconds and I couldn't. The activation pressure stopped me." He looks at the golden form. "I heard her through the gap. I screamed her name." He pauses. "I counted to four minutes and eleven seconds." "I know," Luka says. "How do you know?" Sampo says. "Because I was nine years old and I knew you grew up in the outer machine district," Luka says. "And when Natasha told Gepard about the dark winter and the records purge, and when Svarog's monitoring data showed three signatures at junction twelve in the activation records — I worked out that the third signature was someone who could not get through." He pauses. "I didn't know it was you until you already knew. I didn't want to tell you what I suspected before you had the memory." Sampo looks at him. At the chrome arm. At the spinal integration visible at the back of Luka's neck. At the person who has been in the outer machine district for four months on tunnel entries and is twenty-three years old and has been carrying this specific piece of information without using it. "You're going to be very annoying to know," Sampo says. "You have been saying that since you found me in the district doing repair work," Luka says. "It remains true," Sampo says. He looks at the golden form. He looks at the frozen cave. He looks at the Architects' notation on the wall to his left — the pre-Svarog carving, the language that Clara has been learning to read. "Sparkle told me," he says, "that the path of Elation continues. Past Belobog. That when this is over — when both Stellarons are resolved and the people here are taken care of — the path goes outward." He pauses. "She is going to come back for that conversation." "I know," Luka says. "Natasha said she would not ask me to stay still for her sake," Sampo says. "I know," Luka says. "She meant it," Sampo says. "I know," Luka says. "I am going to be very difficult about leaving," Sampo says. "When the time comes." "I know," Luka says. "For someone who grew up in the outer machine district and is currently conducting notation channels through his arm," Sampo says, "you are very wise." "I grew up watching Svarog," Luka says. Sampo looks at the golden form for another long moment. Then he puts his hand on the armor — the right side of the chest plate, the specific location that is not a damaged joint or a structural weak point, just the armor itself. The gold under his palm. He stands there for sixty seconds. Then he takes his hand back. "Vera," he says. The name in the cave, in the frozen space, with the golden form and the Architects' notation. Said quietly and with the specific quality of someone who has been carrying a name-shaped absence for twenty-two years and is saying the name now, in the place that is most right. The cave receives it. The notation network's warmth, running through the cave's geological structure at primary junction depth, conducts the word through the mineral channels the way it conducts everything — upward, outward, through every junction to the surface. "Thank you," Sampo says. To the cave, to the form, to the Architects' design, to the twenty-two years of holding something precious until the people who needed it were ready. He puts his hand in his jacket and he stands in the cave for one more minute. Then he looks at Luka. "Can we go up?" he says. "I need to see Natasha." "Yes," Luka says. "We can go up." They ascend. —— Gepard's first full day of ward rest is spent in the specific way of someone who is at ward rest and is not fully reconciled to it. He is not uncooperative. Natasha's three-day minimum is correct and he knows it is correct and he has not argued about it. What he is: at twenty-four percent at 0900, reading the full engagement report that Pela sent to his ward tablet at 0800, and sitting with what the numbers in it mean. The report is comprehensive. Pela's reports are always comprehensive. This one covers the full engagement timeline from 0617 to 1047, the formation class progression, the presence-field density at every monitoring point, the fighter assessment data, the notation network's conductance behavior during the engagement, and the first Stellaron's signal quality through the full five hours. He reads it twice. He looks at the numbers. Forty-eight percent at the start of the cave descent. Thirty-nine at the cave's second-stage contact. Eighteen at the Manchester Smash's conclusion. The specific depletion rate of a sustained high-output engagement plus the Manchester Smash expression, which the report classifies as — unprecedented is the word Pela uses for the Ice Flames, consistent with the classified debrief — and which produced the full cave-freeze at thirty-one percent of his ceiling. Thirty-one percent. He looks at this number. The full engagement — courtyard, cave, the complete second Stellaron battle — took him from full capacity to eighteen percent. In terms of output and depletion, the Manchester Smash alone accounted for thirteen percentage points. The preceding courtyard engagement, two hours and eleven minutes of sustained combat at selection rate with full expressions deployed, accounted for the rest. He needed eighty percent of what he has to defeat the second Stellaron. He sits with this number in Natasha's ward at 0900 on the morning after and he thinks about what it means. Not strategically — he is at twenty-four percent and strategic assessment requires more capacity than that. Personally. The specific way that a fighter sits with a result. What it means: the second Stellaron of Belobog, in the cave below the third sub-level, required eighty percent of what Gepard Landau has at present to defeat. What it also means: he finished. The second Stellaron is in the golden form in the frozen cave and he is in Natasha's ward at twenty-four percent and both of those things are true simultaneously. The math is: it was survivable. The math is also: barely. He thinks about the northern ridge. The year. The year of learning what his limits are at the edge of mortality pressure — the specific education of someone who is pushed past where they thought the ceiling was and discovers there is more past the ceiling, that the ceiling is not fixed, that the press of death produces things that the standard training conditions never will. The Ice Flames came from the northern ridge. From the year. From the specific night in the eleventh month when the Trampler formation arrived and the classified report later called it maximum mortality risk and he was too busy staying alive to classify it. He is at twenty-four percent. He has been through the maximum-mortality year. He has the Ice Flames at thirty-one percent of his ceiling. The second Stellaron took eighty percent. He sits with these numbers and he does not reach for a conclusion because the numbers do not, by themselves, tell him what comes next. What he knows: he finished. What he does not know: what the next thing is. The first Stellaron. 3.1. Considering. He does not know what CONSIDERING means in terms of timeline. He does not know what it looks like when the first Stellaron crosses from 3.1 to 4.7 or what happens between those numbers. He does not know the unknown variable that Pela's model cannot account for. He does not know any of these things at twenty-four percent in Natasha's ward. He closes the report. He opens the patrol folder that Dunn sent to his ward tablet instead — the formation decay monitoring, the district restoration progress, the normal operations of the Silvermane Guard on a normal post-engagement morning. He reads the patrol folder with the professional attention it deserves. He notes the formation decay baseline confirmation at 0847. He notes the generator housing repair crew assignments. He notes the courtyard gold fragment cleanup progress — fifty percent complete at 0900, full completion expected by 1400. He reads the patrol folder until 1100. Then he sets it down. He looks at the ward door. At the courtyard outside through the window — the gold fragment cleanup progress, the Guard units working, the outer machine district's morning. He thinks about Bronya's speech. He thinks about three hundred and fourteen people and the sound they made. He thinks about what Svarog's expression looked like when the Ice Flames came up. He picks up the ward tablet. He opens a new document. He writes three words at the top of it: NORTHERN RIDGE: NOTES. He looks at the three words for a long moment. He has not written about the northern ridge since the classified debrief, which was not his writing — it was the three officials' account of what he reported in the debrief session, his own words translated into their institutional language. He wants to write the real version. Not for the classified record. Not for anyone else. For the eventual conversation — the one he knows is coming, with Bronya, when she finds the debrief and comes to him with the specific quality of someone who has received information about the person they care about most. He wants to have the real version ready. He writes: Month one. The northern ridge at extension range. The first time the ice produced something it had not produced before. What it felt like. He writes slowly. The ward is quiet. The outer machine district does its morning outside the window. At twenty-four percent, with three days of ward rest ahead, Gepard Landau writes the northern ridge in his own words for the first time. —— Bronya visits at 1400. She comes with the monitoring tablet — the first Stellaron's data, the formation decay update, the Pela report — and sets all of it on the ward chair beside his bed and looks at him. "Twenty-four percent at 0900," she says. "Pela's update says twenty-eight at 1200." "Twenty-nine," he says. "She checked at 1200. I checked at 1345." "Nine percent in seven hours," she says. "Standard recovery rate for the first day," he says. She looks at him. At the ward tablet on his lap — she can see the document open on it, the three words at the top, the paragraph below. He does not close it when she looks. He looks at the document and then at her and then he turns the tablet slightly so she can see the header: NORTHERN RIDGE: NOTES. She looks at it. She does not ask what it is. She looks at it and then at him and she understands — in the specific way that Bronya understands things, below the data layer, in the attunement — what it is and why it exists. "When you are ready," she says. "When I'm ready," he says. She looks at him. At the Ice Flames she does not yet know about in their official capacity — she will find the classified debrief, she is already thinking about where the classified files for the northern ridge mission are, she will read it and come to him. But not today. Today: the ward. Twenty-nine percent. The document with three words at the top. She sits in the ward chair. She pushes the monitoring tablet to one side. She puts her hands in her lap and she looks at him with the expression that has been developing since the stairwell — the one that is not the Commander's and is not the Guardian's but is specifically hers, the one that exists in the space where those two registers are not required. "The speech," he says. "I know," she says. "Three hundred and fourteen people," he says. "I know," she says. "You said Svarog's name," he says. "I needed to say Svarog's name," she says. "He has been doing the work for a very long time without the right person acknowledging it." She pauses. "I am the right person. So I said it." He looks at her. "You are the right person," he says. Simply. As a fact. She looks at her hands. At the warmth in them — the first Stellaron's attending quality, running through her palms at a level that is higher than it was before the battle, higher than it was before the CONSIDERING quality appeared in Pela's data. She has been noticing this since yesterday morning. The warmth is more present. More foreground. She does not have a vocabulary for it yet. She is building one. "The first Stellaron," she says. "It changed when you used the Manchester Smash." He looks at her. "The signal—" "Not the signal," she says. "In my hands. The attending quality." She holds her hands up slightly. "It changed when the Manchester Smash propagated through the geological structure. The Ice Flames conducting through the notation channels — the first Stellaron's attending quality in my hands felt the conductance. And the warmth changed." She pauses. "Became more present. More — interested." He is quiet for a moment. "Interested in what?" "I don't know yet," she says. "But it was specific. The change was specific to the Manchester Smash." She looks at him. "The first Stellaron heard the Ice Flames in the notation network." He looks at his hands. At the standard ice expression. At the ambient cold. At the thing that lives at the edge of it that only the northern ridge produced and that the first Stellaron, apparently, has been attending to. "I'll need to know more," he says, "about what the first Stellaron is and what it is building toward." "Yes," she says. "So will I." She pauses. "I am going to speak to the retired officials. The three who sent you on the northern ridge mission." He looks at her. "The classified debrief," she says. "I know where the file is. I am going to read it. And then I am going to find the officials and ask them what they sent you into and what you came back with and what they wrote unprecedented about." She holds his gaze. "Not today. When you are above thirty percent. But it is coming." "I know," he says. "And then I am going to come to you," she says, "and you are going to tell me the version that is not in the debrief. The real version." He looks at the ward tablet. At the document. At NORTHERN RIDGE: NOTES. "Yes," he says. "I will." She looks at him for a long moment. Then she reaches over and takes his hand — the right hand, the standard ice expression ambient in the skin. She holds it. "You scared me," she says. "In the cave. When Pela's signal update said the Manchester Smash had deployed." She pauses. "I was at the northern perimeter and I felt it through the geological structure and I felt the first Stellaron's attending quality change and I thought—" She stops. "What?" he says, quietly. "I thought: he had better come back up," she says. "That was the exact thought. He had better come back up." She looks at his hand. "And then you did. And then I came down the shaft and you were at the east end of the cave eating Natasha's pastries." "Oleg's pastries," he says. "Natasha made them," she says. "Oleg distributed them," he says. She looks at him. At the corner of her mouth — the involuntary thing, the almost-smile that has been developing since the very beginning. "You were at eighteen percent eating a pastry," she says. "I was," he says. "You need to stop doing things that require pastries at eighteen percent as recovery," she says. "I'm in the ward," he says. "For three days. No pastries required." She looks at him. At the specific quality of someone who is at twenty-nine percent and in the ward and is still, demonstrably, entirely themselves. She holds his hand. "Three days," she says. "Three days," he agrees. "And then you tell me what the northern ridge actually was," she says. Not a question. "And then I tell you," he says. She does not move her hand. He does not move his. The ward is quiet. The outer machine district does its afternoon outside the window. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1 in the data on the tablet she has pushed to one side. The warmth in her hands — the attending quality, more present than before the battle, more foreground since the Manchester Smash — runs through her palms and into his, and where it meets the standard ice expression of his hands it does not cool and the ice does not warm. It simply coexists. Two things that should not exist together, existing together, exactly as they are. —— At 1700 in the outer machine district, the gold fragment cleanup reaches ninety percent. The Moles are helping. This was not assigned to them — they arrived at the courtyard at 1430 with the specific quality of children who have been in a workshop doing notation analysis and model building since 0900 and are ready for something that involves physical action, and Dunn's cleanup crew accepted the assistance with the specific professionalism of Guard personnel who know that twelve-year-olds with clear purpose are not to be argued with. Oleg, specifically, is excellent at gold fragment cleanup. The jacket appears to have a compartment that is specifically suited to the collection of gold armor fragments, which is either a coincidence or evidence of something about the jacket that has passed beyond the reach of explanation. "The jacket," Toma says. "The jacket," Oleg agrees, collecting another fragment. Pino is at the far end of the courtyard, at the notation channel junction that runs under the courtyard stone. He has his boot on the stone — not his hand, his boot, because the courtyard is cold from the morning's cleanup work. He is feeling the notation network's warmth through his boot. "Still warm," he says. "Still warm," Toma confirms. Clara is at the workshop entrance, watching the cleanup. She is thinking about Those Who Keep the Lineage, the Network Knows Your Hands, and about what the remaining notation sections in the workshop and the cave will say when she can read them fully. She is thinking about Svarog teaching her and Pela building the model and Pino finding the words. She is thinking about being twelve years old and part of the Architects' plan. She is thinking about the cave below, where the golden form rests in the ice. Where Vera is in the stone. She looks at the courtyard. At the gold fragment cleanup. At the outer machine district doing its evening. At the Guard units and the Moles and the notation channel warmth in the courtyard stone. She feels, below everything, the first Stellaron. 3.1. Considering. She has been feeling the first Stellaron for approximately three months — not the way Bronya feels it, not the Guardian's attunement. The Architect lineage's attunement. Something older and different from either, the specific frequency of something that was built into her lineage by entities who knew both Stellarons were going to be here and who left the people they were protecting the tools to handle it. The first Stellaron is at 3.1. It is considering something. Clara looks at the cave below and the notation network in the stone around her and the city above and the dome above the city. She goes back to the workshop. She puts her hand on the notation carving above the secondary bench. She begins to read the next section. —— In the residential stack at 1900, the lamp is on. Seele is reading. Not the frequency analysis documentation — not anything operational. The worn book with the chapters about the sky, her mother's book, the book with the chapters about the moon. She has not read it in a long time. She has been thinking about the moon promise and what Bronya said — he is going to take you to the moon — and the specific warmth of being known by the right person, and she has taken the book off the shelf and she is reading the chapter about the moon's specific quality of reflected light. Luka is on the floor across from her, doing push-ups. Not for any operational reason. Not in preparation for anything immediate. Just because he woke up this morning at full arm-charge-dissipated baseline and his body wanted to move and the specific physical training that he has been building since the sub-level three expedition requires daily maintenance. The chrome arm takes the push-up load differently than the biological arm did — the integration points conducting the force through the spinal artifact, the full circuit of spine-and-arm running as one system, the weight distribution different from any prior configuration. He has been figuring out the new distribution for three weeks. He goes down. He goes up. "You are doing push-ups," Seele says, without looking up from the chapter. "Yes," he says. "In the residential stack," she says. "The floor is flat," he says. She looks up from the chapter. At the push-ups. At the chrome arm taking the load. At the spinal integration visible at the back of his neck in the lamp's light. She looks at him in the lamp-lit room the way she has been looking at him since the cave, which is the specific way of someone who is looking at the person they are looking at and not needing to manage what that means. "How many are you doing?" she says. "Until I get to three hundred," he says. She looks at the number. "You are at approximately one hundred and forty," she says. "Yes," he says. "That is one hundred and sixty more," she says. "Yes," he says. She looks at the chapter. She looks at the moon. She looks at him. "The arm's load distribution," she says. "You've been figuring it out." "Every day," he says. "It takes the load differently. The integration changes the force pathway." "Is it better?" she says. He considers this while going down and coming back up. "Different," he says. "Not better or worse yet. The output will be better when I know the distribution. Right now I'm learning the distribution." She watches him for a moment.
