At the specific focus of someone who is learning the new version of himself with the same thoroughness he brought to the corridor entries and the sub-level approaches and the cave. "You're going to get stronger," she says. "Yes," he says. "Much stronger," she says. "Yes," he says. "And the arm will help," she says. "The arm helps," he says. "The training helps. They are different kinds of help." She looks at the chapter. At the moon. She looks at the shelf where the book was. She puts the book down and looks at the ceiling and thinks about the vertical sixty-meter drop in the cave and what she felt in those sixty meters and what it is going to become when she has had months of practice with it rather than hours. "I'm going to get stronger too," she says. "I know," he says. "Don't say I know," she says. "I am aware of it," he says. "And glad of it. And specifically interested in seeing how the butterfly quality develops in the next six months." She looks at him. At two hundred and twenty push-ups roughly. "You are interested in my power development," she says. "I am interested in everything about you," he says. "Including your power development." She is quiet for a moment. "That is," she says, "an extremely specific kind of charming." "I know," he says. "I said don't say I know," she says. "I am aware of how charming it is," he says. She picks up the book and throws it at him. He catches it with the chrome hand — the articulated fingers closing on the spine of the book without fumbling, the integration responding faster than he expects — and holds it and looks at her. "The moon chapter," she says. "What about it?" he says. "Read it," she says. He looks at the book. He looks at her. He gets up off the floor — two hundred and thirty, he is going to finish the three hundred later — and he sits beside her on the low seat by the window and he opens the book to the chapter and he reads it. She leans her head against his shoulder. The lamp is on. The chapter about the moon. The chrome arm warm where it is closest to her. The outer machine district outside the window doing its evening. The moon is promised. —— In the workshop at 2200, Svarog is alone. Not doing the system check — the system check was complete at 1800. Just present. The back room quiet. The geological monitoring running at standard. The notation network's conductance at the upper level of the above-standard warmth from the Manchester Smash residual — it will dissipate by 0600 tomorrow. He looks at the geological monitoring data from today. The first Stellaron's signal: 3.1. CONSIDERING — DAY 2. Signal stable. He looks at this. He looks at the section of the geological monitoring that shows the Fragmentum density in the northern Sector A — the section that has been at elevated levels since the second Stellaron's emergence. The formation density there did not return to pre-emergence baseline with the rest of the district. It is at pre-emergence baseline plus eleven percent. He has been watching this eleven percent. It is new. It is not a residual of the second Stellaron's presence-field. The second Stellaron's field withdrew at 0751 yesterday and the Fragmentum density in the surrounding district has been in full decay since then. All districts except northern Sector A. Northern Sector A is eleven percent above pre-emergence baseline. Northern Sector A is where the first Stellaron's ambient output concentrates. Has been concentrating, with increasing specificity, since the signal moved from 2.4 to 3.1 at the second Stellaron's defeat. The first Stellaron's ambient output in northern Sector A is eleven percent above what it was before the second Stellaron defeated. CONSIDERING, the signal quality says. Svarog looks at the eleven percent. He writes it into the geological monitoring log with the specific care of someone noting the beginning of something that is going to require very careful noting. He looks at the Fragmentum density. He looks at the first Stellaron's signal. He thinks about Bronya's hands. He closes the monitoring log. He sits in the back room and he waits, the way he has always waited — patiently, with the full weight of everything he has seen and everything he knows and everything he suspects, for the city to be ready for what comes next. The city is becoming something it has never been before. The next thing is coming. END OF CHAPTER 45 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 46: What the City Builds Next Chapter Text The outer machine district on day two of recovery looks like a district that has decided to return to itself. The gold fragment cleanup finished at 1400 yesterday. The courtyard stone is clean except for the fused section in the eastern quadrant — cordoned with the Guard's orange marking tape, specialist equipment requisitioned. The three generator housings that the second Stellaron's discharges took out: two completed, one still waiting on materials from the surface supply depot. The notation network's above-standard warmth: fully dissipated as of 0632, the Ice Flames' residual gone from the upper channels, the system back to standard operational temperature. At 0547, Pela was already at the analysis room, reading Serval's technical documentation on the tablet. The document arrived at 0112 with the subject line PRELIMINARY: STELLARON ENGAGEMENT DATA and runs to sixty-eight pages. She has read forty-one of them. She will read the rest by noon. At 0632, she set the documentation aside and opened the first Stellaron's monitoring data. 3.1. CONSIDERING — DAY 2. She looked at the northern Sector A formation density overlay she had been building since the morning of the battle. The ambient Fragmentum density in northern Sector A had not returned to pre-emergence baseline with the rest of the district. Everywhere else: clean decay, back to normal by 0847 as the model predicted. Northern Sector A: eleven percent above pre-emergence baseline, and it had been eleven percent above since the second Stellaron went quiet. She had been running the model for forty-eight hours. She needed Svarog's geological dataset to complete it, but even with what she had, the preliminary output was producing a number she kept recalculating because it seemed too soon and kept coming out the same. She wrote: NORTHERN SECTOR A — 11% ABOVE PRE-EMERGENCE BASELINE. FIRST STELLARON AMBIENT OUTPUT ELEVATED. MODEL INCOMPLETE WITHOUT GEOLOGICAL DATASET. REQUIRES SVAROG. She closed the monitoring log. She went back to page forty-two of Serval's documentation and the shimmer conductance section. —— Natasha's morning rounds at 0730. Gepard is at thirty-seven percent. She confirms this from his eyes and his hand temperature before he says a word — six years of this specific file have given her a reading method that the instruments only confirm. She sits in the ward chair and does the full assessment. "The hands," she says. "Hold them out." He does. She examines the ice expression's output — the ambient cold, the integration of the standard expression with the recovery state. She looks at the specific thermal signature she noted in his file two days ago. The anomalous warmth at the impact points that she has been cross-referencing with Serval's documentation since 0112 when the document arrived. She closes his hand gently and stands. "Three more days," she says. "Natasha—" "You went to eighteen percent," she says. "Three more days. The depletion rate from the Manchester Smash does not follow a standard recovery curve — I have nothing in six years of your file to compare it to. I am being conservative because I do not have a reference." She picks up her kit. "Eat real food this morning. I sent for it. It arrives at 0800." She pauses at the door. "How is the writing?" He looks at the ward tablet. "Fourteen pages. Month three." She nods. Something passes through her expression — she does not name it. "Good," she says, and goes. He looks at the ward tablet. NORTHERN RIDGE: NOTES. Fourteen pages. He has been writing since 0500 in the slow hours before rounds, the real version coming out differently than he expected — less event-by-event, more the quality of each month, the specific thing that the northern ridge produces in a person when the mortality pressure is constant enough for long enough. He writes it for himself. He writes it because when Bronya reads the classified debrief — and she will, she is the Supreme Guardian and the file is in the secure section of the palace array and she has known it exists since she first read the file index — he wants to have the real version ready. Not to show her. Just to have it. He opens month four and begins. —— Serval arrives at the ward at 1000 with the technical documentation under her arm and the look of someone who has been awake since midnight doing something she is extremely pleased about. She drops into the ward chair with the ease of someone who has been in Gepard's various ward rooms enough times to have opinions about which chairs are better, and she sets the documentation on the side table. "The shimmer conductance data," she says, without preamble. "I need you to understand what Luka did in the cave, because it is relevant to the first Stellaron preparation work I am starting." "Walk me through it," he says. "The shimmer — the ambient Fragmentum in the air during the battle — conducted the arm's source material influence to the right tendril's connection point on the form's body," she says. "We knew this. But when I ran the engagement data, I found that the conductance during the cave floor contact was not a wave propagating outward from the contact point. It was simultaneous. The entire cave floor at once, not sequentially." She taps the documentation. "At primary junction charge, the arm's source material saturated the complete mineral structure of the cave instantaneously. Not in a radius. The whole floor." "Simultaneously," he says. "Simultaneously," she says. "Which means at primary junction charge, the arm is not operating through the notation channels specifically — it is saturating all mineral matter in contact with the primary junction. The channels are the most efficient path, but they are not the only path." She looks at him. "The ceiling on what the arm can do has not been found yet. The cave engagement was the highest charge contact Luka has had. The primary junction is the source of the entire Architects' network. He has not held contact with it for longer than three seconds." Gepard is quiet for a moment. "And when he does?" "I do not know," she says. "Which is the point. I am building the model for the first Stellaron engagement and I am working with incomplete data on two of the four fighters." She pauses. "The other incomplete data set is yours." He looks at her. "The Ice Flames," she says. "I have the cave engagement output from the Manchester Smash — the propagation rate through the notation network, the ice and fire conducting together through the primary junction at primary output. I have the fact that the notation channels carried both expressions simultaneously without structural loss." She opens the documentation to the relevant section. "The Architects built those channels to the highest possible standard. The fact that they carried the Ice Flames is a testament to that standard. But I do not know the ceiling of the Ice Flames expression. The cave engagement was thirty-one percent. What does the Ice Flames expression look like at sixty percent? At eighty? I have no data." He looks at his hands. At the standard ice expression, ambient, the cool he has lived with since seventeen years old. At the edge of it, quiet, the thing that only the northern ridge produced. "Month eleven of the northern ridge," he says. "The first time the Ice Flames came up. I was at maximum mortality pressure and I pushed past where I thought the ice expression could go." He pauses. "I could not reproduce it deliberately afterward. On the ridge, I reproduced it twice more — both times under equivalent pressure. Both times it went further than the first time." He looks at Serval. "The press of death does something to the ceiling. The ceiling is not fixed." Serval stares at him. "The ceiling moves under mortality pressure?" "That is my experience," he says. "I cannot give you a rate. I cannot give you a measurement. I know it moves because it moved three times on the ridge and the third time was further than the first." She sits back. She looks at the documentation. She looks at the ceiling. She is clearly doing six things simultaneously in her head, because she is Serval Landau and that is her default operating mode. "That makes the model significantly harder to build," she says. "A variable ceiling under pressure is—" She stops. She starts again. "Okay. What I can model is the baseline expression — the standard Manchester Smash at thirty-one percent. What I cannot model is the upper range. I need you to understand that when I present the first Stellaron preparation data, there is going to be a variable in your column that I cannot constrain." "I know," he says. "That does not bother me." "It bothers me," she says. "I like constrained variables." "I know," he says, with the specific edge of someone who has been Serval's brother for twenty-eight years. She looks at the documentation. She looks at him. "You have been carrying the Ice Flames alone for eight months," she says. "Yes," he says. "The three officials retired within six months of the debrief," she says. "And you have been—" She stops herself from finishing the sentence the way she started it. She tries again. "Are you alright? Not tactically. Actually." He looks at the ward tablet. At fourteen pages of the northern ridge in the real version. At month four. "I will be more alright when Bronya reads the debrief and comes to me," he says. "There are things I have been carrying alone that need to be carried with someone." She looks at him — her younger brother, who has the ice expression and the Ice Flames and eight months of classified unprecedented in his file and fourteen pages written in a ward room at five in the morning. "Good," she says. "She is going to read it." "I know," he says. "And then she is going to come here and you are going to tell her the real version," she says. "Yes," he says. "And then it will not only be yours to carry," she says. He looks at the ward window. At the outer machine district. At the city. "And then it will not only be mine," he says. Serval picks up the documentation. She stands. "Eat your breakfast," she says. "Natasha will have sent for actual food. Don't let it go cold." She goes. —— On day two of recovery, Luka is at the secondary shaft entrance at 0900. He has been coming here every morning since the cave engagement — not to descend, just to the entrance, one palm on the shaft wall for sixty seconds. The secondary junction at this depth runs above the standard corridor junctions but below the primary junction at cave depth. Sixty seconds at this contact charges the arm to forty percent above standard. He pulls his hand back, shakes out the integration, and goes. He went from here to the workshop. Serval was at the secondary bench running the arm's engagement data through a comparative analysis she had been building since the documentation was complete. He sat across from her and they spent two hours going through the shimmer conductance parameters together — him providing the tactile data from the cave floor contact, the exact quality of the simultaneous dissolution wave, what it felt like when the whole cave floor received the conductance at once; her translating that into numbers and thresholds and model sections. She told him the ceiling was probably higher than the cave engagement showed. He told her he had been thinking the same thing since the right tendril dissolved. "The primary junction contact," she said. "Sustained, not burst. Three seconds is what you've been doing. I want to know what ten seconds produces." "Does anyone need to be there when I find out?" he said. She looked at him with the look she uses when someone has said something reasonable that she has already been planning around. "When Gepard is out of the ward," she said. "Yes. Everyone who is going to be in the first Stellaron engagement needs to be there. We are building the data set together." He thought about the first Stellaron at 3.1, Considering. About fourteen to eighteen weeks — Pela's preliminary model number, the one she had been circling for forty-eight hours without Svarog's geological data to confirm it. He thought about the cave and what the full primary junction output felt like when the right tendril dissolved and wondered what ten seconds would produce. "When Gepard is out of the ward," he said. "When Gepard is out of the ward," she agreed. That was this morning. Now it is 1400 and Luka is on the floor of the residential stack doing push-ups because the arm's load distribution is a problem he has been solving daily since the integration and today the solution is moving slightly. The chrome arm takes the weight differently than the biological arm did. The spinal integration changes the force pathway through the torso. He has been recalibrating his center of gravity for three weeks. Two hundred and forty-one. Two hundred and forty-two. Seele is on the low seat by the window reading. Not frequency data — her mother's book. He has seen her return to it three times since the cave engagement. Each time she opens it to the moon chapter, reads for a while, looks at the dome through the window, and puts it down with an expression that has no performance in it at all. "You are working out the arm's weight distribution again," she says, without looking up. "I'm forty-seven push-ups from three hundred," he says. "You were forty-seven push-ups from three hundred twenty minutes ago," she says, turning a page. "The distribution is harder than you expected." "The integration changes the force pathway through the torso," he says. "When the arm braces the impact, the spinal artifact takes the compression differently than the biological shoulder did. The balance point shifts." She looks up from the book. She looks at the chrome arm taking the load on the downstroke, the integration points conducting, the spine visible at the back of his neck. She watches him for a moment. "It's going to be better once you find it," she says. "The distribution. When you have it, the output is going to be higher than the biological shoulder ever was." "That's the point," he says. "I know the point," she says. "I'm saying it's going to be significantly higher. Not slightly. The spinal integration plus primary junction charge plus the correct load distribution—" She closes the book. "You are going to hit things considerably harder than you currently do." He finishes the push-up he is on. He looks at her. "How do you know?" "Because I have been watching you work out the distribution for three weeks," she says, "and the improvement rate is not linear. Each week the distribution settles a percentage point further and the output I can observe in your movement quality increases faster than the distribution would suggest." She pauses. "Serval's documentation has the cave engagement data. The torso force pathway during the full burst approach on the left tendril. Even with the compromised distribution you had in the cave, the output was—" She looks for the word. "Significant." He is quiet for a moment. Then: "You have been reading Serval's documentation." "I read everything relevant to people I care about," she says. He looks at her. She looks back at him with the sideways look, the fully-adult version of it, the version that has no management in it. "You read sixty-eight pages of technical frequency analysis," he says. "Fifty-one," she says. "I am not interested in the shimmer conductance threshold calculations in section nine. Everything else." She picks up the book again. "Finish your push-ups." He looks at her for one more second. The corner of his mouth does the thing. He goes back to push-ups. "The moon chapter," he says, at push-up two hundred and sixty. "Mm," she says. "You have read it four times since the cave," he says. "I know how many times I have read it," she says. "What are you looking for?" he says. She is quiet for a moment. Not because she does not have an answer — she has the answer, she is deciding whether to give the real version or the managed one. She gives the real one. "I am trying to understand what my mother saw in it," she says. "She brought me to the outer district entrance at night to listen to the second heartbeat. She kept this book. She read me the chapters about the sky." She pauses. "I am trying to understand whether she knew something. Whether she was pointing me somewhere, with the book and the heartbeat and the district entrance, without knowing she was doing it." He stops the push-ups. He gets up and sits beside her on the low seat. She does not move away. She holds the book in both hands and looks at the dome through the window. "I think," he says, carefully, "that she loved you. And she brought you to the things she loved so that you would love them too. The heartbeat because she felt it and wanted you to feel it. The book because the moon mattered to her and she wanted it to matter to you." He pauses. "I don't think she was pointing you anywhere. I think she was giving you the things that were hers." Seele looks at the dome. "And then she was gone," she says. "And the things that were hers became the things that are mine." "Yes," he says. She looks at the book. She opens it to the moon chapter — not to read, just to have it open. She looks at the specific page that her mother's hands touched how many times, the slight worn quality of the page corner. "When you take me there," she says. "To the moon. Will you read it with me first? The full chapter, not just the passage you read the other night." He looks at the chapter. At her. At the lamp that is not yet on, the afternoon light sufficient. "Yes," he says. "Tonight if you want." She leans her head against his shoulder. "Tonight," she says. —— Bronya and Seele are in the Restricted Zone at 1500. The outer perimeter ledge — the place Bronya has been coming since before the formal appointment, the city spread below in every direction, the Everwinter wind from the north cold with altitude rather than underground cold. Seele had not been to this specific location before. She looked at the city from the ledge for a full minute before saying anything, taking in the specific perspective of Belobog from above. "You come here to think," she said. "Since I was twenty-two," Bronya said. "I needed to understand the weight of the title before it arrived. I came here and looked at the city and tried to work out what I was agreeing to." "Did you work it out?" Seele said. "Eventually," Bronya said. "It took longer than I expected." Now they are standing at the ledge's edge with the wind pulling at them and the city below and the first Stellaron somewhere beneath the palace in the geological structure, its attending signal warm in Bronya's hands. "The voices," Seele says. She has known about the voices since the early entries — it came up in a briefing when Bronya was explaining the first Stellaron's signal behavior, and Seele had noted it and filed it and has been thinking about it since. "Are they changing?" "Since the battle," Bronya says. "They are — closer. Not words yet. But the quality of them is different. More present." She looks at her hands. "The attending signal is louder since the Manchester Smash conducted through the geological structure. As if the first Stellaron heard something it recognized and moved a step toward me in response." "Has Svarog heard them? The voices?" "No," Bronya says. "I have not told Svarog about the voices specifically. I have told him the signal quality has changed. He gave me a different number — the northern Sector A density at eleven percent above pre-emergence baseline, the first Stellaron's ambient output elevated. He has the geological dataset." She pauses. "He has been building toward telling me something for days. I can feel it in how he looks at the monitoring display when I walk into the back room." "He will tell you when he has enough to say," Seele says. "That is how he operates. He does not speculate aloud." "I know," Bronya says. "I trust that. I just—" She stops. Tries again. "The voices are mine to figure out. Whatever they mean, whatever the first Stellaron is trying to reach toward — that is something I am going to have to sit with and work out for myself. No one can do that part." Seele looks at her. At the Supreme Guardian of Belobog, standing in the Restricted Zone wind with the city below her and a Stellaron's voices in her hands. "No," she says. "That part is yours. But what you do with what you figure out — that is not only yours." Bronya looks at her. "You have Svarog and Pela and Gepard and me," Seele says. "When you know what the voices mean, you tell us. That is not a negotiation." She holds Bronya's gaze. "Bronya. You are not doing the first Stellaron alone." The wind comes from the north and moves between them. "I know," Bronya says. She says it with the full weight of meaning it — not the managed version of knowing something, the actual version. "Thank you." "Don't thank me," Seele says. "Just tell us when you know." Bronya looks at the city. At the outer machine district below — the generator stacks, the courtyard, the notation channels in the geological structure. At the cave below that, invisible from here but present. "Luka," she says. "What about him?" Seele says. "Last night," Bronya says. "Coming back from the ward, I passed the residential stack. The lamp was on." "It was on when I turned it on," Seele says. "He was reading," Bronya says. "Through the window. I couldn't see what — I didn't stop. But the lamp was on and he was there and—" She pauses. "It made me glad." Seele looks at the dome. At the pale reflected light. "I made him read the moon chapter," she says. "How?" Bronya says. "I threw the book at him." Bronya turns to look at her. A beat. Then the involuntary laugh — brief, complete, entirely hers, the warmest sound Bronya makes. "You threw your mother's book," she says. "He caught it with the chrome hand," Seele says. "First try." "Of course he did," Bronya says. The laugh is still in her voice. Then something shifts — the humor settling into something quieter and warmer. "Seele," she says. "Mm." "I am glad it is him," Bronya says. "Specifically. The specific person." Seele looks at the dome for a moment. Then at Bronya. At her best friend, who grew up in a different part of the same hard city and found her way back to her and means it every time she says something. "Me too," Seele says. They stand in the Restricted Zone wind with the city below. —— Sampo comes to the ward at 1400. He sits in the chair across from Natasha's desk, the one he has claimed through repetition, and she does the chart update while he settles in. He does this sometimes in the afternoons on recovery days — comes without a specific agenda, stays for an hour, says more than he usually says. "He is writing about the northern ridge," Sampo says. "You don't know that," Natasha says, not looking up. "I noticed the document title when I passed the ward window," he says. "NORTHERN RIDGE: NOTES. I was not trying to read it. It was large text." A pause. "How long was the mission?" "You should ask him," she says. "I know," Sampo says. "I will, when he is above fifty percent and not at the part of the writing where you need quiet." He drums his fingers on his knee once, then stops. "Three officials sent him. Three officials retired within six months of the debrief. That is a specific pattern. Something they saw in the debrief was enough to—" He pauses. "They did not know what to do with what came back with him. So they retired." Natasha sets the chart down. She looks at him. "Where are you going with this?" "I'm going here," he says. "Vera's endurance is in the cave. It has been there for twenty-two years in a Stellaron's field. The specific quality of a person who looked at every hard thing and said: I am still here. That quality, preserved." He looks at the desk. "Whatever the northern ridge produced in Gepard — it is the same quality. The specific product of maximum mortality pressure that lasts long enough to change the ceiling." He pauses. "I know what that costs. The dark winter cost me something similar. Not the same form, different expression. But the same root." He looks at Natasha directly. "He has been carrying it alone." "Yes," she says. "Bronya is going to read the debrief," he says. "Yes," she says. "And then she will come to him," he says. "And he will not be carrying it alone anymore." "Yes," she says. He is quiet for a moment. "Good," he says. Just that. He looks at the ward door. "Sparkle," he says, changing subject with the ease of someone who has compartmentalised a large thing and is choosing to put it down for a moment. "She said she would come back." "I know she said that," Natasha says. "I have been thinking about what she said," he says. "About the path continuing past Belobog. About walking toward something rather than away from it." He looks at his jacket. "For twenty-two years, every choice I made was a running-away choice dressed up as something else. Toward profit, toward opportunity, toward the interesting thing — but underneath it, always away from the gap in the stone at junction twelve. Away from not knowing." He pauses. "I know now. I have the name. I have the memory." He looks at her. "I do not have a reason to run from anything anymore." She looks at him across the desk. "Which means," he says, "that what comes next is an actual toward. Not a disguised away." He pauses. "The path of Elation. Sparkle described it as — the thing that makes the worst moments bearable is also the thing that makes the best moments luminous. The laughter and the chaos and the performance, yes, but underneath it the specific orientation toward life that laughs not because things are fine but because you insist on them being worthwhile." He looks at the desk. "The dark winter. Running through the gap that would not let me through, screaming into it, coming back every day of my life since as someone who runs toward things even when he doesn't know why." He pauses. "That was always the path. I just did not have the name for it." Natasha is very still. "Sampo," she says. "I know what I'm saying," he says. "I am not deciding tonight. The first Stellaron is coming. Both plots resolve first." He looks at her. "But I wanted you to hear the real version of it. Not the performance version." He pauses. "When Sparkle comes back for the conversation — I want you to be there. For it." She looks at him for a long moment. "For which part?" she says. "For the part where I figure out what toward looks like," he says. "Whether it ends with me staying in Belobog or leaving it." He holds her gaze. "I want you to be part of figuring that out." She stands up from the desk. She goes around it and sits in the secondary chair, closer. "Then I will be there," she says. "When she comes back. I will be there." He looks at her. At the woman who has been putting things in his jacket pocket without saying why and calling him on every piece of performance he has ever delivered and staying. "Thank you," he says. "Don't thank me," she says. "Tell me the rest of what Sparkle said. The actual theology of it, if there is one. What does the path of Elation look like in practice, beyond the Masked Fools and the performances?" He looks at the ceiling. Then at her. Then he tells her. —— Pela arrives at Svarog's workshop at 1600 with the model and the specific expression of someone who has been sitting on a number for forty-eight hours and needs it confirmed or demolished. "The northern Sector A formation density," she says, setting the tablet on the secondary bench without preamble. "Eleven percent above pre-emergence baseline. I have been running the model. If the first Stellaron's ambient output is eight percent above its previous baseline — which the northern Sector A data suggests — then the projected activation timeline is fourteen to eighteen weeks from the second Stellaron's defeat." She looks at Svarog. "I need your geological dataset to confirm or correct this. I have been running the model with what I have and the preliminary output is consistent across twelve recalculations, but my dataset only goes back to the beginning of Belobog's formal monitoring records. You have forty years of geological monitoring above that." Svarog looks at the tablet. He looks at the model output. He looks at the fourteen to eighteen weeks and the northern Sector A density and the eight percent elevated ambient output. "Your model is correct," he says. "The geological dataset confirms the projection." Pela looks at him. "You already knew." "I have been building toward telling Bronya since the signal moved to 3.1," he says. "I was waiting for an independent confirmation before presenting a timeline to the Supreme Guardian. A timeline of this consequence requires more than one dataset." He looks at her. "Your model is the confirmation." She absorbs this. "You were waiting for me to run the model," she says. "Yes," he says. She looks at the tablet. Fourteen to eighteen weeks. "We need to tell Bronya tonight," she says. "I will tell her when she returns from the Restricted Zone," he says. "I will give her the data — the timeline, the northern Sector A density, the geological monitoring cross-section. What the data means in terms of the first Stellaron's approaching activation." He pauses. "What I cannot tell her is what the first Stellaron's activation will look like. The forty-year dataset gives me the rate and the direction. It does not give me the form." "Nobody knows the form," Pela says. "No," he says. "That is a variable that will be discovered in the event itself." She stands at the secondary bench and looks at the model. Fourteen to eighteen weeks. The first Stellaron at 3.1. Northern Sector A at eleven percent above baseline and rising. She looks at Clara, visible through the workshop, at the notation carving above the secondary bench with her palm on the wall, reading. She looks at the cave below — invisible, present, the golden form in the Great Resting Place. "The cave sections," she says. "The notation in the cave. Clara has not started on those yet." "She will get to them," Svarog says. "She is learning the remaining characters. When she has them, we will go to the cave and she will read what the Architects left there." "Will it tell us anything about the first Stellaron's form when it activates?" Pela says. Svarog is quiet for a moment. "The notation in the cave predates any human record on this planet," he says. "What it contains about the first Stellaron is a genuine unknown. I have been monitoring the outer district for forty years and I have not been able to read those sections." He looks at Clara. "She will be the first." Pela follows his gaze. At Clara, twelve years old, palm on the wall, reading notation that is older than the outer machine district. "How long does she need?" she says. "Eleven characters," he says. "At the rate she is learning — a week, perhaps less." "Then we have time before the timeline becomes critical," Pela says. "Fourteen weeks minimum." "We have time," Svarog says. "What we do with it matters." Pela looks at the model. She opens a new section: FIRST STELLARON — PROJECTED ACTIVATION: 14-18 WEEKS POST-SECOND STELLARON DEFEAT. GEOLOGICAL CONFIRMATION: SVAROG. DATASET: 40-YEAR GEOLOGICAL MONITORING + CURRENT FORMATION DENSITY. She saves it. She looks at the characters Clara still needs. She looks at the cave below. She looks at the model. She goes to the secondary bench and sits beside Clara. "The characters you are working on," she says. "Let me see them. I have been reading the notation in my peripheral vision for eight months — I may have picked something up without realising." Clara looks at her. She looks at the four remaining characters in the current section. "This one," she says, pointing. "I have three interpretations and none of them are fully satisfying." Pela looks at it. She has seen it in the geological monitoring data's overlay annotations — Svarog uses it in the geological notes. "In the geological monitoring context," she says slowly, "Svarog uses it adjacent to formation activity. Something like — emergence, or transition." Clara looks at the character. She looks at the wall. She puts her palm on the junction and reads the full section with that substitution. Her expression changes. "Threshold," she says. "It means threshold." She looks at the section above the secondary bench. At the notation characters, now more complete. She reads what she has. She does not say what it says. Not yet — the section is not fully read. But her expression is the one she wears when something large has just become more legible. "The cave sections," she says. "I need to read the cave sections." "We go to the cave when you have the remaining characters," Svarog says, from the back room doorway. She nods. She goes back to the next character. She works faster. —— Bronya reads the classified debrief at 2100 in the palace. She had known the file existed since the second week of the Guardianship — the secure array index lists it: NORTHERN RIDGE — MISSION REPORT — CLASSIFIED: SUPREME GUARDIAN ACCESS ONLY. She had noted it and moved on because the first weeks of the Guardianship were not a time for reading classified files about missions that predated her appointment. She had come back to it in her mind several times over eight months, most recently when the first Stellaron's signal began its escalation and she started paying closer attention to everything in the classified archive. Tonight Svarog told her the timeline: fourteen to eighteen weeks. He gave her the geological data, the northern Sector A density, the model that Pela confirmed independently. He gave her everything he had in the dataset. He did not speculate about what the first Stellaron's activation would look like — only what the rate and direction said. After Svarog left at 2030, she sat at the desk for twenty minutes. Then she opened the secure array and pulled the northern ridge file. Twenty-two minutes to read it. The three officials wrote in the institutional language of a classified debrief, which means every significant thing is described in the flattest possible terms. She has been the Supreme Guardian for long enough to read classified language fluently. She reads through the flat terms and she understands what they mean. Month eleven. Maximum mortality risk assessment. Encounters classified above existing threat parameters. The ice expression demonstrating capabilities not previously documented. The fighter sustained. The fighter extracted. Classification: unprecedented. Unprecedented. In a sixty-page document, one word. She closes the file. She picks up the ward communicator and writes: I need to come to the ward tonight. Not for the debrief. For the other thing. She sends it. His response comes in forty seconds: Come. She goes. —— He is sitting in the chair by the window when she arrives. The ward tablet is on his lap — the document open, the title visible as she comes through the door. She sees it. She sits on the edge of the bed across from him and sets nothing down. She is holding nothing. She came here with the classified file read and nothing else. "Unprecedented," she says. "That's the word they used," he says. "They wrote two sentences about month eleven," she says. "Maximum mortality risk. Ice expression demonstrating capabilities not previously documented." She looks at him. "Two sentences for the most significant thing that happened in a year-long mission." "They did not know how to write about it," he says. "I did not know how to explain it to them. The debrief was—" He pauses. "I gave them what I could. The external events. The timeline. What the encounters were. What I survived and how." He pauses. "I did not know how to explain what changed in me during month eleven. I still do not have complete language for it." "Tell me what you have," she says. He looks at the ward tablet. At the fourteen pages. He sets it aside. He talks. He does not use the debrief's language. He does not use the flat institutional terms. He uses the real version — the quality of the northern ridge in month one, the specific escalation across the year, what the mortality pressure does to a person over that length of time. The specific night in month eleven when the Trampler formation arrived and the ice expression went somewhere it had never gone before and he was too occupied with surviving to understand what had happened until it was over. The blue-white fire at the edge of the ice. The first time. What it felt like from the inside. He talks about the two subsequent times — month eleven, week three; month twelve, the final engagement before extraction. How each time it went further. How the press of death kept moving the ceiling he thought was fixed. Bronya listens. She does not interrupt. She does not ask clarifying questions while he is talking. She receives it, the real version, in the specific way she receives information that costs something to give — fully, without hurry. When he finishes, she is quiet for a long moment. "You came back with something that has no prior documentation in the history of Belobog," she says. "Possibly in the history of Jarilo-VI. And the three officials who saw the debrief retired within six months, and you have been carrying it alone, and tonight Svarog told me there are fourteen to eighteen weeks before the first Stellaron activates." "Yes," he says. "The Manchester Smash conducted through the notation network," she says. "The Ice Flames and the ice together. Serval told me the channels carried both expressions without structural loss." She pauses. "She said the Architects built the notation network to the highest possible standard. That it was equipped to handle what you put into it, not because it was designed for it specifically, but because it was simply built to withstand anything the planet required." "That is what she told me as well," he says. "Then the Ice Flames are yours," she says. "Not predicted, not prepared for. Yours, developed through a year that no one prepared you for either. And the notation network happened to be adequate because the Architects built something that could hold the most extreme thing this planet might ever need it to hold." She looks at him. "That is a remarkable coincidence." "Or the Architects were very thorough," he says. "Or both," she says. He looks at her. At the Supreme Guardian who just told him what fourteen to eighteen weeks means. At the person who has the first Stellaron's attending signal in her hands and the voices she has been hearing and the full weight of Belobog on her shoulders. At Bronya. "The voices," he says. She looks at him. "How do you know about the voices?" "Seele told me," he says. "Not in detail. She mentioned you had been hearing them. Not words." "Not words yet," she says. "Since the battle they are closer to the threshold. I do not know what they mean. I do not know what the first Stellaron is trying to reach toward. That is something I have to figure out myself — no one can do that part." She pauses. "But I am telling you about them because you are the person who needs to know." She looks at her hands. "Whatever I figure out, I am not keeping it from you." "I know," he says. "And whatever the northern ridge produced," she says, "you are not carrying it alone anymore." She meets his eyes. "That is not a request. That is how this works." He looks at his hands. At the standard ice expression. At the edge of it, quiet, the blue-white fire that came from a year of maximum mortality on the northern ridge and that the notation network carried without breaking when he finally used it. "Fourteen to eighteen weeks," he says. "Fourteen to eighteen weeks," she says. They sit in the quiet ward. The outer machine district does its night outside the window. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1 in the monitoring data. The northern Sector A crystallization ticks upward in Pela's hourly update, one tenth of a percent higher than the previous reading, the rate so slow it would take an instrument to catch it. But the rate is there. The direction is there. The timeline is there. He reaches over and takes her hand. Not the face, not the forehead — the hand, the same way she took his hand the afternoon he was reading the patrol folder and she came to the ward with Svarog's news. He holds it. The warmth of the first Stellaron's attending signal runs through her palm and into his hand and the standard ice expression does not warm it and the warmth does not cool. They simply exist together, the warmth and the cold, the same way two people sit with something large and neither of them moves away from it. "Tell me what Svarog said," he says. "Everything. I want to know what you know." She tells him. They stay in the ward until late. END OF CHAPTER 46 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 47: The Distance Between 3.1 and 4.7 Chapter Text The outer machine district on day three of recovery looks almost normal. Almost. The generator housings are repaired — all of them, the last materials from the surface supply depot arriving at 0800 and the repair crew finishing by 1100. The courtyard stone is clean, the fused section in the eastern quadrant still cordoned with the Guard's orange marking tape, no longer the visual center of the space but present as a reminder. The formations in the surrounding four blocks have returned to pre-emergence baseline. The shimmer is gone from the air. The notation network runs its standard morning conductance at standard temperature. Northern Sector A is different. Pela's 0700 monitoring read: eleven point six percent above pre-emergence baseline. Yesterday it was eleven. The day before, eleven. The increment is small enough that a single reading cannot confirm it — it takes the comparison across days to see the shape. But it is there. A tenth of a percent per day, patient and geological, the kind of rate that belongs to something operating on a timeline much longer than the people watching it. Pela has been watching it since 0547. She is at the analysis room with the geological dataset that Svarog transferred last night fully integrated into the model. She ran the recalculation at 0600, incorporating the sixty-year monitoring record into the twelve parameters it changed. The overall projection held — fourteen to eighteen weeks. But the dataset produced a detail she had not expected: the escalation rate is non-linear. The curve starts slow. Weeks one through three: the current rate, a tenth of a percent per day. Then, at approximately week four, the rate increases. Not to crisis level — twelve, fifteen percent per day rather than one tenth. But the shape of the curve is not a straight line. It steepens. She has the day of first steepening calculated to three significant figures. Day twenty-five. Twenty-two days from today. She opens the briefing document she is preparing for Bronya's 0900 slot. She writes the number at the top of the page: DAY 25. She writes it twice, the second time because the first time felt too small for what it means. —— Bronya is at the palace window at 0700 when the voices change. She has been at the window since 0640 — the habit she developed in the first weeks of the Guardianship, standing at the south window in the early morning before the day assembles itself around her. The city below, the outer machine district's generator stacks, the first light coming through the dome. She usually uses this time to think. Lately she has been using it to listen, not to the city but to the attending quality in her hands, which has been growing warmer every morning. This morning the voices do something they have never done. They become directional. She feels it shift — the ambient surrounding quality of the signal, always present, always radiating outward like the warmth from a lamp, suddenly orienting. Pointing. Not toward her. She is the one receiving. Pointing toward itself, toward the source. The way a sound does not point to you but points you toward it. Below and to the left. The palace's west foundation. Three hundred meters of geological structure and the first Stellaron's dormancy below. She presses her palm flat against the window glass. Cold morning glass. Through it she feels the attending quality conduct — and then something else, something she has not felt in this specific form before. The distance. Not distance in space but something like it — the gap between a listener and the thing being listened to. She can feel both ends of the gap. Herself here, at the window. The source there, below and to the left. And the gap has a measurable quality. A width she could not have named two weeks ago and now can. It is smaller than it was two weeks ago. She takes her palm off the glass and looks at her hand. Then she goes to find Svarog. —— Clara is at the workshop when Bronya arrives at 0715. She is early — earlier than the Moles, earlier than the standard notation session. She has been at the secondary bench since 0630 because she woke up thinking about the remaining characters she needs to learn before the cave sections, and lying in bed thinking about characters she needs to learn felt like a waste of time. She has her notebook open and the lamp on and her palm on the workshop wall in the specific contact that is not the junction but is the notation wall itself, the background warmth rather than the channel warmth. She looks up when Bronya comes through. "Svarog is in the back room," Clara says. "He has been there since before I arrived, which means before 0630." "Does he sleep?" Bronya says, genuinely asking. "He says the system check takes the time it takes," Clara says. "I have never asked whether that counts as sleeping." She pauses. "I think it might be something adjacent. Not the same thing. Adjacent." Bronya looks at her. At the notebook open on the bench, the characters transcribed, the notation carving above her. "What are you working on?" "The characters I still need for the cave sections," Clara says. "Eleven remaining. I have been going through them with Svarog in sequence." She turns the notebook so Bronya can see the page. "This one — I have three possible readings and none of them sit right. Svarog says I am close on the second interpretation but I am missing something." Bronya looks at the character. She looks at Clara. "Show me the second interpretation," she says. Clara traces the strokes of the character slowly with her finger above the notebook — not touching, just tracing. "The vertical component anchors it. The branching at the top reads like expansion or opening. But the base stroke is closed, which pulls it back. Expansion contained." She frowns. "Which is — threshold? Edge? The thing right before a change?" Bronya watches her. At the twelve-year-old working through a notation character that predates the city above them by an unknowable margin, frowning at it like it has personally failed to cooperate. "What if the base stroke is not closing it," Bronya says slowly, "but grounding it. Expansion anchored to something fixed." Clara looks at the character. She puts her palm on the wall and reads it again with Bronya's interpretation in mind. Something shifts in her expression — the frown releasing, replaced by something more focused. "Potential," she says. "Not threshold. Something that could expand but has not yet." She looks at Bronya. "How did you—" "I have been feeling something very similar in my hands for months," Bronya says. "The first Stellaron's attending signal. Something large with its expansion still contained." She pauses. "The character looked like how it feels." Clara stares at her. Then she looks at the character. Then back at Bronya. "You read the notation," she says. "I read one character," Bronya says. "With your work to stand on." Clara turns back to the notebook and writes the interpretation with the speed of someone getting something down before it slips. Then she looks at Bronya again with an expression that is — measuring, the way she measures everything. "Would you want to learn the notation?" she says. "Formally. With me." Bronya looks at the wall. At the pre-Svarog carvings, older than the outer machine district. "I think," she says carefully, "that would be useful." "Good," Clara says. "Sit down. We'll start with the base vocabulary." "I have a meeting with Svarog at—" "Svarog knows every character already," Clara says. "I will be more useful." She points at the secondary bench. "Sit." Bronya sits. Svarog, from the back room, produces a sound that is the closest he comes to a laugh. —— In the back room twenty minutes later, Bronya looks up from the notation exercise Clara has given her and says: "The voices changed this morning." Clara and Svarog both look at her. "Directional," Bronya says. "Below and left. The west side of the palace foundation." She sets down the notation exercise. "They have never had direction before. Always ambient, always surrounding. This morning they oriented toward the source." She pauses. "I felt the gap between myself and the first Stellaron. I could feel both ends of it." Svarog is quiet for a moment. "Directional is new," he says. "Can you tell me what it means?" Bronya says. "I can tell you what the geological monitoring suggests," he says. "The signal at 3.1 is not only escalating toward activation. Some portion of its output is orienting toward you specifically. The attending quality in the data has been showing this asymmetry since day one — not radiating equally in all directions, concentrated toward the palace." He pauses. "What that means in terms of what the first Stellaron intends — that is beyond the dataset." "Beyond the dataset," Bronya says. Not dismissively. She is filing it. "The gap you felt," Svarog says. "The distance between yourself and the source. You said it felt smaller than two weeks ago." "Significantly smaller," she says. "Then the directionality is not new," he says. "It has been building since the signal moved to 3.1. What is new is that you can now feel the gap clearly enough to measure it." He looks at her. "You are getting closer to understanding it. Not because the Stellaron is moving. Because you are." Bronya sits with that. Clara is very still at the secondary bench, notebook open, not writing. Just listening. "I am going to start recording the voices every morning," Bronya says. "The quality, the direction, what the gap feels like. Not for the monitoring record — for myself. So that when they become words, I have the record of how they arrived." "That is a good practice," Svarog says. "And when they become words," Bronya says, "I am going to tell you both immediately." She looks at Clara. At the twelve-year-old with the notation exercise and the Architects' lineage. "Both of you. Not just Svarog. Both." Clara looks surprised for a half second — the genuine surprise of someone who did not expect to be included in that category. Then she straightens slightly and nods, with the specific quality of someone accepting a responsibility. "Both," she says. —— Pela's briefing at 0900 runs fifty minutes. She presents the non-linear escalation curve. She presents day twenty-five. She presents the three projection scenarios and the conservative, central, and aggressive estimates. She presents what the sixty-year geological dataset changed and what it confirmed. Gepard is in the briefing room too — Natasha released him at 1400 yesterday and Bronya added him to the 0900 briefing at 0800. He sits across the table from Pela and listens to the curve. "Day twenty-five," he says, when she reaches the inflection point. "Twenty-two days. What does the rate look like after that inflection?" "The sixty-year dataset shows a secondary inflection at approximately day forty-five," Pela says. "The rate increases again — steeper. And then, if the escalation follows the dataset's shape, a plateau phase before the final approach to 4.7." She pulls up the curve on the display. "The plateau could last anywhere from two weeks to six weeks based on the dataset variance. It is the period of highest uncertainty in the model." "The plateau before the final approach," Gepard says, looking at the curve. "Is there precedent in the dataset for what triggers the transition out of plateau into final approach?" "No documented trigger," Pela says. "The dataset shows the transition occurring, but the geological monitoring cannot tell us what internal process initiates it. We know the rate increases. We do not know why at that specific moment." "So the timeline's final weeks are the hardest to predict," he says. "Correct," she says. "The fourteen-to-eighteen-week projection is for the full activation. The last two to four weeks of that range are the most uncertain portion." He looks at the curve. Bronya looks at the curve. The curve sits on the display between them, slow and non-linear and patient in the way that geological processes are patient, and the plateau phase in the middle of it is the blank space where the model cannot reach. "Pela," Bronya says. Pela looks at her. "Are you eating before you come in?" Bronya says. Pela opens her mouth. Closes it. "I—" "Seven hours of sleep and breakfast before the analysis room," Bronya says. "This is a long engagement. The model needs you at full function for the full duration, not running on five hours and nothing until noon." She pauses. "I know the impulse. I have the same impulse. I am telling you to resist it, because you will be more useful across fourteen weeks well-rested than you will be brilliant and depleted by week four." Pela looks at her for a moment. She is sixteen years old and she has been running the best model she can on the worst sleep schedule she can manage and being told by the Supreme Guardian to sleep more is not a thing she expected to receive in a crisis briefing. "I will adjust," she says. "Good," Bronya says. "Continue." Gepard catches Pela's expression as she turns back to the display — the very brief, very sixteen years old flicker of I was not expecting that and also she is right. He looks at his patrol folder to give her the privacy of the adjustment. —— After the briefing, Gepard goes to the northern monitoring post. Dunn is there. He has been there since 0600 — he had the early rotation and stayed past changeover because the northern Sector A crystallization had been producing a reading pattern in the overnight data that he wanted to see confirmed in the morning read. "The inward orientation is more pronounced," Dunn says, when Gepard arrives. He points at the monitoring display. "Not visible to the eye yet — the density increment is too small. But the formation geometry in the northern crystallization zone has been shifting. Three overnight readings show the alignment trending toward the palace foundation." Gepard looks at the display. At the crystallization geometry data. At the small, patient shift in the formation alignment that the monitoring instruments are barely catching. "It has been trending how long?" "Since the second Stellaron went quiet," Dunn says. "I have been watching it for three days. The first two days I thought it was noise in the instrument. Third day it was consistent." He pauses. "I was going to put it in the daily report tonight but since you're here—" "Good catch," Gepard says. "Add it to the monitoring parameters. I want the crystallization geometry tracked alongside the density reading in every daily report going forward." Dunn nods. He looks at the display. He looks at Gepard. "You look better than you did leaving the ward yesterday," he says. "I am at fifty-one percent," Gepard says. "That is better than eighteen," Dunn says. "Significantly better than eighteen," Gepard agrees. He looks at the northern section. At the outer machine district below, the generator stacks, the courtyard where the cleanup finished yesterday afternoon, the notation channels in the geological structure below. He thinks about the year on the northern ridge. About month five, when the ice deepened in the stillness of the first extended position. About twenty-two days and a steepening curve. "When the rate increases at day twenty-five," he says, "we are going to see it here first. Before the signal instruments. Before the palace monitoring." "Because the geological structure in the northern section is the most direct ambient output path," Dunn says. Not confirming — he already worked this out. "Yes," Gepard says. "Daily reports. Any change in the geometry, the density, the thermal signature of the crystallization." "Already started," Dunn says. "The 0700 read is filed. 1300 read will go in at 1300." A pause. "Sir. The Manchester Smash." He stops himself. He tries a different approach. "I felt it through the ground from the perimeter. Two hundred meters from the cave entrance, standing on six meters of geological structure above the cave floor." Another pause. "I have been your second for eight years. I have never felt anything like that through the ground." Gepard looks at him. Dunn is twenty-four years old and has been holding the Guard's most demanding patrol rotation for three days while his captain was in the ward, and the thing he is trying to say is not a tactical assessment. "You will feel it again," Gepard says. "When the first Stellaron activates. The cave was thirty-one percent." Dunn looks at him. "How much is there past thirty-one percent?" "I don't know the exact ceiling," Gepard says honestly. "I know it moves under pressure. The year on the ridge moved it three times." Dunn holds this. He is quiet for a moment. "Then let's hope the pressure does its work," he says. "It will," Gepard says. He picks up the monitoring tablet. "The 1300 read. I will be at the outer wall. Send it to me there." "Done," Dunn says. Gepard turns to go. Dunn hesitates for half a second — the specific hesitation of someone with something else to say that exists outside the patrol rotation. "Serval," Dunn says. Gepard turns back. "Her resonance disruptor calibration data," Dunn says. With the careful precision of someone constructing a reasonable sentence around an unreasonable amount of personal interest. "She sent it to the monitoring stations for the formation class update. I was reading through the engagement documentation and the disruptor section—" He stops. He recalibrates. "It is excellent work. The technical methodology is—" Another stop. "I wanted to know if she is working on an updated version for the first Stellaron's formation class." Gepard looks at him. He has known Dunn for eight years. He knows the quality of this sentence. He knows it has nothing to do with resonance disruptors. "She is at the workshop most mornings," Gepard says. "Building the first Stellaron preparation data." "Right," Dunn says. "Good." A beat. "I might stop by. For the formation data." "For the formation data," Gepard says. "Yes," Dunn says. Gepard goes to the outer wall. —— Serval arrives at Svarog's workshop at 1000. She has been at the palace monitoring station since 0700, pulling the formation class data from the northern Sector A sensor array — the specific dataset she needs to build the frequency calibration profile for whatever the first Stellaron's ambient field produces. The second Stellaron gave her the secondary-wave aerial form data. The first Stellaron's formation class is an unknown that she needs to start modeling now, twelve weeks minimum before activation, because twelve weeks is approximately how long it takes her to build a new resonance calibration from scratch. She comes through the workshop door with the equipment case and the expression of someone who has solved the first problem of the morning and is already formulating the second. Clara looks up from the notation exercise she is working through with Svarog. "Serval," she says. "Good morning." "Morning," Serval says. She sets the equipment case on the secondary bench. She looks at the notation exercise Clara is working through. "Is that—" She leans in. "Are those pre-Svarog characters?" "Yes," Clara says. "Can you actually read them?" Serval says, with the specific interest of someone who has been adjacent to the notation network for months through the frequency analysis and has always treated it as geological data rather than language. "Most of them," Clara says. "The ones I can't read yet I'm learning." She pauses. "Bronya helped me with one this morning. The potential character." Serval stares at her. "Bronya read a pre-Svarog notation character." "She said it looked like how the first Stellaron's signal feels," Clara says. Serval looks at the character Clara points to. At the vertical component and the branching expansion and the grounding base stroke. She looks at it for a long moment. "Huh," she says. "All right. I have been running formation frequency analysis on the geological structure above the cave for four months and never once thought of it as language." She straightens up. "I am going to need the cave's mineral composition frequency map at primary junction depth. The first Stellaron's formation class — I need to know whether it is going to interact with the notation network's conductance field or operate independently." "Why would it interact?" Clara says. "Because the second Stellaron's forms interacted with the shimmer conductance," Serval says. "The shimmer was the second Stellaron's ambient field made mineral. If the first Stellaron produces a similar ambient field — and the northern Sector A crystallization suggests it does — then its formation class will have a relationship to the notation network's mineral structure." She opens the equipment case. "I need to know whether that relationship is constructive or destructive before I build the calibration." "I can give you the cave's junction warmth frequency from primary contact," Clara says. "The notation conducts at a specific rate at primary depth — I have it from the battle and from yesterday's session with Luka." "That is exactly what I need," Serval says. She pulls out a frequency recorder. "Walk me through it." Clara walks her through it. Svarog watches them from the back room doorway. The oldest consciousness on the planet, watching a twelve-year-old with the Architects' lineage and a twenty-eight-year-old frequency analyst build the data bridge between the notation network and the first Stellaron preparation work, without either of them having been asked to build it or knowing that it is the specific thing the preparation needs. He goes back to his monitoring display. —— Dunn arrives at the workshop at 1100. He stands at the door for a moment. He has the formation data from the northern Sector A monitoring post — he was going to send it to Serval's station, which is how he would normally send data, but sending it directly has the specific disadvantage of not producing any of the other outcomes that a visit to the workshop produces. He goes in. Clara is at the secondary bench with Serval. They are bent over the frequency recorder, Clara tracing the notation channel warmth frequency while Serval adjusts the equipment parameters, both of them talking quickly in the overlapping shorthand of two people who have been building the same thing from different ends. Serval looks up when he comes in. "Dunn," she says. Not surprised — she is too deep in the calibration to be surprised by much. "What do you need?" "Northern Sector A formation data," he says. He sets the monitoring printout on the bench near her. "The crystallization geometry shift from the overnight read. I thought you would want it for the calibration profile." She looks at the printout. She picks it up and reads the crystallization geometry data with the speed of someone who has been reading this specific data type for nine months. "The alignment trending toward the palace foundation," she says. "Since when?" "Three days of consistent overnight reads," he says. "Gepard confirmed this morning — tracking it in the daily reports going forward." She reads the data again. "The alignment trend means the formation geometry of the first Stellaron's ambient field is not uniform," she says, more to herself than to him. "It concentrates toward the palace. Which matches Pela's monitoring asymmetry in the signal quality." She sets the printout down. She looks at Dunn. "When you have the 1300 read, send it directly to me instead of the station. I want the geometry data as it comes in." "Done," he says. He should go. He has the 1300 read to collect and the secondary monitoring post to check and the afternoon patrol rotation to confirm. He does not immediately go. "You were in Serval's band," Clara says, from the secondary bench, with the specific directness of someone who has been told this as a piece of context and sees no reason not to deploy it. Dunn goes very still. Serval looks at Clara. "What?" she says. "You were in Serval's band," Clara says to Dunn. "When you were younger. Gepard mentioned it. He said you played guitar." "He mentioned it," Dunn says. His voice is completely professional. His face is not doing what his voice is doing. Serval stares at him. "You played guitar," she says. "I played rhythm guitar," he says. "For approximately eight months. Before I joined the Guard at eighteen." "Why did you never—" She stops. She starts again. "You have been my brother's second for eight years and you were in my band and you never said anything?" "There was not a natural moment," he says. "There were many natural moments," she says. "Any conversation about music! Any of the times I have been in the Guard briefings! Eight years of—" She stops. She looks at him with the expression of someone reformatting a large amount of context. "You quit the band right before the lineup changed," she says. "I always thought it was because you lost interest." "I joined the Guard," he says. "You joined the Guard two weeks after you quit the band," she says. "I remember the timeline. We were sixteen." She looks at him. "Why did you quit?" He holds her gaze for one beat longer than a purely professional conversation requires. Then: "The data for the 1300 read," he says. "I will send it directly." He goes. The workshop door closes. Clara looks at Serval. Serval is looking at the workshop door with the expression of someone who has just been given a piece of information they did not have and is not sure yet what to do with it. "I should not have said that," Clara says. "No," Serval says. She is still looking at the door. "You probably should not have." A pause. "It's fine. Go back to the junction warmth frequency." "Are you sure?" Clara says. "Clara," Serval says. "Right," Clara says, and goes back to the junction warmth frequency. Serval picks up the frequency recorder. She looks at the door again for one more second. She goes back to the calibration. —— The sustained primary junction analysis runs from 1000 to 1200 in the cave. Luka, Seele, and Serval descended at 0930. The cave at mid-morning is the cave — the frozen surfaces, the bioluminescent light through the ice, the golden form at the far end, the Architects' notation running the cave wall. Seele went immediately to the notation wall. Luka went to the primary junction contact point. Serval set up the frequency equipment at the shaft entrance. The methodology: sustained contact at measured intervals. Five seconds. Ten. Fifteen. Twenty. Thirty. At five seconds: the arm reaches full primary junction capacity. At ten seconds: above-capacity charge, the integration running above the designed maximum. Sustainable — he can feel the difference between at-capacity and above-capacity the same way you can feel the difference between a full breath and the absolute edge of the lungs. Uncomfortable at the far edge. Not painful. At fifteen seconds: Serval reads the instruments and says, "Stop." She reads them again. She recalculates. She says: "At fifteen seconds, the arm's conductance pattern changed quality. The output became consistent with the notation channels' own conductance frequency, not the arm's source material output. The arm stopped feeding the channels and started operating as one." "A notation channel," Luka says. "Functionally, at fifteen seconds of sustained primary junction contact, the arm is an extension of the notation network," she says. "Not conducting through it. Part of it." She looks at the cave floor. At the primary junction below the ice. "The ceiling on what you can do is not the arm's charge capacity." "What is it?" Seele says, from the notation wall. Serval looks at her instruments. "The full notation network at maximum designed output," she says. "Which I need Svarog's dataset to calculate. But it is going to be a significant number." She pauses. "The second Stellaron battle — you were running secondary junction contacts, thirty-second burst intervals. At primary junction charge, sustained fifteen-second contact, you become the network's full output conduit." She looks at Luka. "Whatever the network can produce, you can produce." He looks at the cave floor. At the ice. At the primary junction below his feet. He thinks about the cave floor dissolving simultaneously during the battle — the right tendril connection point gone in a wave that hit the whole floor at once. He thinks about what the whole floor simultaneous dissolution felt like from the inside. The difference between secondary junction burst and primary junction sustained is the difference between that and something larger. "What does the network's maximum designed output look like in practice?" he says. "I will tell you tonight," she says. "When I have run the calculation." She starts packing the equipment. Then she stops. She looks at him with the expression that preceded her calling him extraordinary in the outer machine district, the not-technical register. "The cave engagement. The left tendril at full burst, the right tendril dissolution, the cave floor—" She shakes her head slightly. "I have been frequency-analyzing Fragmentum interactions for nine months and I have never measured output characteristics like yours. Even without the primary junction ceiling established." She looks at him directly. "You understand what I am building the data set toward." "The first Stellaron," he says. "The first Stellaron is going to be significantly harder than the second," she says. "I need accurate numbers for everyone in the engagement before it arrives. I need to know the ceiling of every variable in my column." She pauses. "Tonight I will have the ceiling for yours. I want you in the workshop when I present it." "Tonight," he says. She finishes packing the equipment. Seele, from the notation wall, without turning around: "Serval." "Yes?" Serval says. "The fifth character in the third grouping," Seele says, tracing the wall. "I have four of the five. The fifth I can read individually — it means something adjacent to recognition or identification. But in this grouping it feels wrong. The surrounding characters don't support that reading." Serval looks at the grouping Seele is tracing. She frowns. "Why are you asking me? I cannot read notation." "You have been reading formation frequency data that runs through the notation channels for nine months," Seele says. "The formation frequency patterns carry the notation channel's conductance signature. You know the frequency quality of specific channels." She pauses. "The character I cannot read — is it in a channel you have measured?" Serval stares at her. "You want me to identify a notation character by its frequency signature." "Can you?" Seele says. Serval sets the equipment down. She takes out the frequency recorder. She holds it near the wall — not contact, proximity. She runs the measurement. She reads the frequency output. She reads it again. "The frequency signature of this character's conductance is—" She pauses. "It is the resonance I associate with the deep junction return signal. The conductance that comes back up through the channel after it reaches the primary junction." She looks at Seele. "That character is associated with return." Seele looks at the grouping. Recognition — return — direction and what is needed. "It means echo," she says slowly. "Or reflection. The thing that comes back." She traces the grouping again with this reading. "The cave receives what arrives and echoes it toward what is needed. The cave echoes." She takes her hand off the wall. She turns to look at the cave. At the ice on every surface. At the golden form. At the Architects' ruins. "It is a transmitter," she says. "The cave is a transmitter," Luka says. "Yes," she says. "Whatever enters the cave — the Manchester Smash, the battle's energy, the memory retrievals — the cave receives and transmits. Toward what is needed." She pauses. "Which means the Great Resting Place is not passive. It is active. It is always transmitting." The cave is very quiet. "We should tell Clara," Luka says. "Tonight," Seele says. "Tonight when Serval presents the ceiling calculation. Everyone together." —— They ascend. —— Bronya visits the ward at 1400. Not because Gepard is still in it — Natasha released him yesterday — but because the ward is where Natasha is, and Natasha is who she wants to speak to. Natasha looks up from the chart update when Bronya comes through. "The Supreme Guardian in my ward without a medical emergency," she says. "This is notable." "I need your opinion on something," Bronya says. She sits in the ward chair across from the desk. Not the patient chair — across from the desk. The visitor position. "Not a medical opinion." Natasha sets the chart down. "All right," she says. "Pela," Bronya says. "She is sixteen years old and she has been running the Stellaron engagement model on five hours of sleep and no breakfast and she will not stop unless someone applies consistent pressure because she considers rest a negotiable resource when the problem is interesting enough." Bronya pauses. "I told her this morning to sleep and eat. She said she would adjust. In your experience, does she actually adjust when told?" Natasha looks at her for a moment. "In my experience, Pela adjusts the stated behavior while maintaining the problematic pattern through alternative methods," she says. "She will sleep seven hours and eat breakfast and then stay at the analysis room for three additional hours in the evening." She pauses. "She needs someone checking in. Not the Supreme Guardian — someone she does not feel she needs to perform adequacy for." "Who does she not perform for?" Bronya says. "Clara," Natasha says. "They have been building the Fragmentum model together and Clara tells her things like go eat before you tell me the calculation and Pela goes." She pauses. "Also, for some reason, Oleg. She eats whatever he takes from the jacket without questioning it, which suggests she has decided he requires no performance from her." Bronya is quiet for a moment. "Clara," she says. "Of course." "She was on a notation junction for two hours and eleven minutes during the Stellaron battle," Natasha says. "And she fed Pela a pastry. Pela did not argue." She picks up the chart.
