I have been checking it every night since the battle — the warmth level, whether it is stable." She looks at him. "You have been checking the notation channel," she says. "Someone needs to," he says. "Clara is in the workshop and the cave sessions. Pela is in the analysis room. The Guard monitors the formation density, not the channel warmth. The channel warmth is a different indicator." He produces something from the jacket — not a pastry, a small monitoring instrument. "I borrowed this from the workshop. I run the warmth reading at the northwest channel junction every night at 2100 and compare it to the previous night's reading." She looks at the instrument. "Svarog's?" she says. "He knows I have it," Sampo says. "He gave me the calibration key three days ago without me asking." He pauses. "The channel warmth in the northwest section has been stable since the Ice Flames residual dissipated. But tonight the warmth increased by point four degrees above the dissipation baseline." She looks at him. "Point four degrees." "Which might be instrument variance," he says. "Or it might be the notation network responding to the first Stellaron's field increase. The northern Sector A density went up today — eleven point nine. Pela's morning update." He pauses. "If the notation network's conductance warmth is responding to the first Stellaron's ambient output, I should know." He looks at the instrument. "I am going to send the reading to Pela. Tonight." She looks at him. At twenty-eight years old with a borrowed monitoring instrument and three weeks of nightly channel warmth checks, doing the work that falls between the official monitoring positions because he grew up in the outer machine district and he knows which junctions the Guard does not cover. "Sampo," she says. "I know," he says. "I know what you are going to say." "I am going to say," she says, "that the northwest channel monitoring is work that matters and the point-four-degree reading tonight needs to go to Pela and Svarog tonight, not in the morning." She pauses. "Send it now." He looks at her. "You are not going to tell me to rest?" "You will rest after you send the reading," she says. "Send it." He sends it. Then he looks at the instrument in his hands. "The path of Elation," he says. "Running toward instead of away. You said it when I told you — running toward instead of away when the worst thing happens. That was the beginning." He pauses. "I have been running toward things in this district for three weeks. Not because of the path. Because I could not help it." He looks at the ward. "This is where I live," he says quietly. "The outer machine district. Not the place I passed through. The place I live." "You have always lived here," she says. "I have been here," he says. "Living here is different." He looks at his hands. "Vera ran toward. I ran toward. You run toward." He looks at her. "That is a pattern." She looks at him. "What is the pattern telling you?" she says. "That when the first Stellaron is over," he says, "I am going to have a very difficult conversation with Sparkle about whether toward means staying or leaving." He pauses. "And I do not know which answer I will have." "I know," she says. "You said you would not ask me to stay," he says. "I said it and I meant it," she says. "Even now," he says. "Even now," she says. "The path is the path. Staying still is not running toward anything." She pauses. "But Sampo. When the conversation comes. When Sparkle arrives and the first Stellaron is resolved and you are standing at the question—" She pauses. "Tell me first. Before the answer. Tell me the question as you are living it." He looks at her. "You want to be there," he says. "I want to be part of how you figure it out," she says. "Not to decide the answer. To be there while you find it." He is quiet for a long moment. "Yes," he says. "All right." She takes the monitoring instrument from his hands. She reads the northwest channel warmth reading. She sends it to Pela and Svarog's shared channel with a note: SAMPo V. NORTHWEST JUNCTION, 2100 READ. +0.4 DEGREES ABOVE DISSIPATION BASELINE. POSSIBLE FIRST STELLARON AMBIENT RESPONSE — NEEDS CROSS-REFERENCE WITH MORNING READ. She hands the instrument back. "Go sleep," she says. He goes. She looks at the ward. At the monitoring display. At the northwest channel warmth reading in the shared channel feed. At Sampo's handwriting on the instrument's casing — a small label in the corner, already written: NW JCT DAILY WARMTH — VALERIUS. He labeled the instrument before Svarog gave him the calibration key. She goes back to the chart update. She adds to Sampo's informal file — not a medical record, the file she keeps in the drawer for the people who are not patients but whose wellbeing she tracks: VALERIUS, S. DAY 6 POST-BATTLE. NORTHWEST CHANNEL MONITORING — 6 CONSECUTIVE NIGHTS. RUNNING TOWARD. STILL HERE. She closes the drawer. The outer machine district runs its night outside the ward window. The notation channels warm below the streets. Nineteen days until the curve steepens. The first Stellaron attends at 3.1. The city is still building what it is becoming. END OF CHAPTER 48 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 49: Normal Days Chapter Text Day nine after the second Stellaron's defeat. Seele wakes up before Luka. This is normal now. She tends to surface earlier, he tends to surface later, and the space between is hers — the lamp off, the dome's pale light coming through the window, the outer machine district making its first sounds of the morning. She lies still for a while listening to him breathe. The chrome arm is warm where it rests against her back. She has learned that it is always warm — the source material does not follow the arm's sleep cycle the way biological skin temperature does. It just runs. She has stopped thinking about it and started just noticing it, the way you stop thinking about a familiar sound and simply hear it. She gets up carefully. He stirs anyway. "Where are you going." Not a question, really. The half-awake version, the one that just wants an answer before deciding whether to follow. "Corridor entries," she says. "For the direction practice." He opens one eye. "It's early." "I know," she says. She finds her coat. "You don't have to come." He is already getting up. She does not point this out. —— The outer machine district at 0630 smells like generators warming up and cold stone. The corridor entrance at junction nine-beta has the specific silence of a space that remembers being used differently — the memories retrieved, the Stellaron defeated, the secondary shaft below leading down to the Great Glacial Aegir where the golden form rests in its ice. The corridor itself is just a corridor now. Ambient formation density at pre-emergence baseline. The sensor reads orange-amber, the standard. Seele runs through the corridor geometry with the portable frequency recorder — Serval's instrument, loaned for the direction practice sessions that she and Luka agreed on four days ago. The first two sessions were about calibrating the recorder to the corridor's notation channel warmth rather than the second Stellaron's residual field. This morning's session is the first where she will actually attempt to read the arm's conductance output direction in real time. Luka is at the secondary shaft entrance with his palm on the wall. Sixty seconds. He pulls it back, shakes out the arm. "Ready?" she says. "Ready," he says. "The first target is the formation cluster at junction four," she says. "Standard corridor class. I want you to address it through the arm's conductance and I want to read whether the output went to the cluster specifically or distributed across the whole junction range." "And if it distributes?" "Then we practice narrowing it," she says. "Until you can aim." He looks at her. "Like shooting." "Like shooting," she agrees. She steps back to give him room. He goes in. —— The corridor formation cluster at junction four reacts to the arm's source material conductance the same way it has been reacting since the first time Luka put his palm on a notation junction — the crystallization dissolving outward from the contact vector. But the recorder catches something she has been building toward measuring since the cave engagement: the conductance does not distribute equally in all directions. It has a concentration vector. The output is stronger on the path between the arm and the formation cluster and weaker on the adjacent paths. She reads this and says, loudly enough for him to hear: "It's already aimed. You're already doing it." He comes back out of the junction range. "What do you mean?" "The output concentrates toward whatever you're looking at," she says, looking at the recorder display. "The formation cluster was at junction four. You walked toward it. The conductance concentrated along the path you walked. You didn't distribute it — you directed it by moving your body." He looks at his arm. "The direction of the arm determines the output vector." "The direction of the whole body," she says. "The arm is the conduit. But you aim it with your body the same way you aim a punch." She looks up at him. "You have been doing this instinctively since the first corridor entry." He is quiet for a moment. "The cave floor," he says. "I put my palm flat on the floor and the whole floor dissolved at once. Because I was oriented downward. The output vector was the full floor." "Yes," she says. "And in the first Stellaron's field — eight seconds of contact, the arm integrates, and wherever you are moving, whatever you are facing, the output goes there." He looks at the corridor. At junction four, now clear. He looks at her. "Then the practice," he says slowly, "is not about learning to aim. It's about learning to move correctly while the arm is integrated." "Yes," she says. "Combat movement under primary junction conditions. So that when the integration happens in the first Stellaron's field, your body already knows how to use it." He is quiet again. She watches him work through it. "That changes the training," he says. "Completely," she says. "Which is why I needed to measure first before telling you what we needed to practice." She tucks the recorder into her coat. "The corridor entries were never about the direction. They were about the movement quality under conductance conditions." He looks at her. "How long have you known this?" "Since the cave," she says. "Since I watched you put your palm on the floor and the whole cave dissolved simultaneously. I wanted the recorder data to confirm before I said anything." He opens his mouth. She holds up one finger. "Before you say it — I know. I should have told you sooner." "I wasn't going to say that," he says. "What were you going to say?" He picks up the scythe case she set against the junction wall. "I was going to say: show me the movement training." She takes the case back from him. "Later. We should eat first." "It's 0650." "Exactly," she says. "We have not eaten." He follows her out of the corridor entrance. She is already thinking about the junction geometry for the first movement training session — the approach angle, the lateral movement relative to the target, the turn radius under integration conditions. He is probably thinking about the same things. They walk at the same speed, the correct speed, neither of them ahead. This is normal too. —— At the workshop at 0830, Clara is at the notation carving above the secondary bench. She has been there since 0730. The seventh section of the workshop notation is the longest single section she has encountered — forty-three characters, three of which she did not have before this morning. Svarog gave her the three new characters at 0715, before the morning monitoring check, because she came in at 0700 asking for them. The third new character, the thirty-ninth in the section, means something she has been turning over since Svarog said it: continuation. Not repetition. Not cycle. Continuation — the movement forward of something that does not begin and does not end but simply proceeds. She is reading the full section again with the thirty-ninth character's meaning in place. Toma comes in at 0830 and drops her bag by the door. She looks at Clara. She looks at the notation. She does not say anything. She gets a stool and sits beside Clara and looks at the section too. "What does it say?" Toma asks. "I'm still working out the full reading," Clara says. "The thirty-ninth character gives me the meaning of the last grouping. But the second grouping has an idiom structure I haven't seen before." She points at the second grouping. "This pattern — the short character followed by the two branching ones — it appears twice in the section. The second time I can read because I know the surrounding context. The first time I cannot because the surrounding characters are all new and I do not have enough context yet." Toma looks at it. She has been reading the notation alongside Clara for eight months. Her vocabulary is narrower than Clara's but her pattern recognition is, if anything, sharper. She reads through the first grouping with the idiom structure. "It's a direction marker," she says. "Not a content character. It's telling you to read the following grouping differently from standard left-to-right." She traces the structure. "Like the bracket notation in the frequency analysis model. It changes the reading direction of the next section." Clara looks at her. "When did you figure out the bracket notation?" Clara says. "Pela showed me," Toma says. "Last week. When we were doing the model review." She pauses. "I asked her why the notation in the frequency data changed direction sometimes and she explained the bracket system. And it looked like this." Clara looks at the idiom structure. She looks at the second grouping with the bracket notation applied. The reading shifts. The second grouping now reads left-to-right on the second line rather than the first and the meaning changes completely. "Toma," Clara says. "Mm?" "That was the thing I could not figure out for a week," Clara says. Toma shrugs. "I just saw it." "You just saw it," Clara says. "It looked like the brackets," Toma says, and reaches for the stylus to help transcribe the reading. Clara looks at her. Toma is already writing. Clara has been working with these three people for eight months and she continues to be surprised by what each of them carries without announcing it. She goes back to the section. —— Pela arrives at 0900 and finds two twelve-year-olds bent over the notation carving with a stylus and Clara's notebook and the expressions of people solving something. She stands in the workshop doorway for a moment before going to the analysis room station in the corner — the secondary display that has become her morning position, the one with the northern Sector A monitoring feed and the model updates. The northern Sector A density: twelve point one percent above pre-emergence baseline. She writes the number. She cross-references it with Bronya's morning field note — the gap estimate from this morning, submitted at 0700. The gap has narrowed again. The correlation continues. Oleg comes in at 0910 with a box from the district bakery. Not the jacket. An actual box from the bakery on the main junction street, the one that does the dark filling pastries that Natasha apparently ordered in bulk before the second Stellaron's emergence and which Sampo distributed during the battle and which has been an ambient presence in every significant event since then. He sets the box on the secondary bench next to Pela's station. "Bakery opened early," he says. "I went." Pela looks at the box. She looks at Oleg. "You just — went. To the bakery." "It's on the way," he says. "On the way from where?" "From where I was," he says. She opens the box. There are six pastries inside. She takes one. Oleg sits on the floor near the secondary bench, opens his notebook — the record of events, the one she asked to use as a historical source — and starts writing. He has been adding to it every morning since the battle. She eats the pastry and runs the northern Sector A model update and does not ask Oleg what he was thinking about this morning or why he went to the bakery on the way. She just adds it to the list of things that are true about Oleg: he shows up, he pays attention, he writes things down. "The Sector A density is at twelve point one," she says. "Up from eleven nine?" he says, not looking up from the notebook. "Twelve one." He writes this down in the notebook. Not in the model. In the notebook. In the specific section she saw him start three days ago, when she looked over his shoulder: First Stellaron escalation log, daily readings. He has been keeping a parallel record. Not Pela's model — his own. In the notebook. "Oleg," she says. "Mm?" "Your escalation log," she says. "Are you cross-referencing it with anything?" "Bronya's field notes," he says. "She posts them to the shared channel every morning. I write down the gap estimate next to the density reading." She stares at him. "The correlation is consistent," he says. "Your model found it on day six. My log found it on day four because I was writing both numbers side by side without knowing it was a correlation." He flips back through the notebook pages. "Day four reads: density 11.5, gap est. medium-close, note — both went up together today, probably related." She stares at him for a long moment. "Day four," she says. "Day four," he says. She closes the model update document and opens a new one. "Give me your escalation log entries from day one through today." He hands her the notebook, open to the right section. She reads them. She reads them again. "Oleg," she says. "Mm?" "You have been running a parallel dataset for nine days without telling anyone," she says. "I wasn't running a dataset," he says. "I was writing things down." "Those are the same thing," she says. He thinks about this. "I guess," he says. She copies his entries into the model. She runs the updated correlation analysis with the additional data points. The correlation coefficient increases from the already-significant number she had at day six to something that makes her sit straighter in her chair. "The correlation is stronger than I thought," she says. "Your day one through four entries — the early data I didn't have — they tighten the model significantly." He looks at the display from the floor. "Does that change the timeline?" "It tightens the confidence interval," she says. "The range was fourteen to eighteen weeks. With your early data—" she runs the calculation "—it narrows. Fifteen to sixteen weeks. The plateau phase in the middle is shorter than I modeled." He considers this. "That's less time to prepare." "Two weeks less at the conservative end," she says. "One week at the aggressive end." She saves the file. She sends the updated model to Bronya, Svarog, Gepard, and Serval. "I am going to add your escalation log as an official supplementary data source in the model documentation." "Okay," he says. "With your name on it," she says. "As co-contributor." He looks up from the notebook. He looks at the model on the display. He looks at his own handwriting in the log. He does not say anything for a moment. "Okay," he says again, quieter. He goes back to writing. Pela goes back to the model. Clara and Toma are at the notation carving. Svarog is in the back room. The pastries are in the box on the secondary bench. The outer machine district runs its morning. —— Svarog comes out of the back room at 1000. Not for the monitoring check — that ran at 0630. He comes out with something in two of his primary arms: a long case, dark and featureless, roughly the length of a forearm, narrowing at both ends. He sets it on the primary workbench in the center of the workshop. He looks at it. He looks at the workshop door. He has been building this since day three of recovery. Not because anyone asked. Because the cave's primary junction contact data from the arm's engagement readings — the simultaneous cave floor dissolution, the conductance vector analysis Serval sent him on day two — told him what Luka was going to need before Luka had the vocabulary to ask for it. And Svarog has been on this planet long enough to know that building the thing before the person needs it is significantly more useful than building it after. He waits. At 1015, the workshop door opens and Seele and Luka come in from the corridor session. She has the recorder. He has his coat on and the chrome arm conducting at above-standard from the secondary shaft contact this morning. He sees the case on the workbench immediately. He stops. "What is that," he says. "Sit down," Svarog says. Luka sits on the secondary bench across from the workbench. Seele stays standing. Clara and Toma and Pela and Oleg all look up from what they are doing. Svarog opens the case. Inside, nested in the mineral composite padding that Svarog synthesizes for all sensitive equipment, are three components. The main housing — the body of the weapon, compact and dark-chrome, the same material composition as the arm's source material, the same warmth conducting from it even in its unassembled state. Two secondary components that clip to the main housing on either end. "The first form," Svarog says, assembling the weapon as he talks, his four primary arms handling the components with the ease of someone who has been working with this specific construction for eight days, "is the cannon configuration. The main housing plus the forward extension. The output scales directly with the presence-field density of your immediate environment. In a high-danger field — the first Stellaron's ambient output, or anything above secondary notation junction pressure — the output increases without any additional input from you. The weapon reads the field and responds." The cannon is assembled. Dark chrome, compact, the forward extension producing the specific warmth glow at the barrel that the notation channels produce when they are conducting at above-standard rate. Luka is looking at it with the expression of someone who does not yet know what to do with what they are seeing. "The second form," Svarog continues, replacing the forward extension with the alternative component, "is the blade configuration. The main housing plus the lateral extension produces a short sword. The same source material composition. Effective in close-quarters when the conductance spread from the cannon would reach unintended targets." The blade is assembled. The cutting edge is not sharpened steel — it is notation channel material, the crystalline mineral composition of the Architects' design formed into an edge that conducts the same warmth as the arm does. The blade of someone who has been in the primary junction's field for days, held and shaped. "The third form," Svarog says, replacing again, "is the ranged configuration. Standard discharge at variable output. Not a cannon — the burst rate is higher and the individual output is lower. For formations at distance when the cannon's sustained output would drain the arm's charge reserve." The ranged form is assembled. It looks like nothing so much as itself — a compact weapon made of the same material as the arm that will fire it, designed to be used by the same hand. Svarog sets it on the workbench. "The weapon retracts into the main housing," he says. "In the collapsed state it is the size of the main housing component alone, approximately the length of your forearm. You carry it the same way Seele carries the scythe." He looks at Luka. "The arm's integration with the notation network means the weapon shares the arm's conductance field. When you contact a notation junction with the arm and the weapon is in your other hand, the weapon charges from the same source." Luka looks at the weapon for a long moment. "How long have you been building this?" he says. "Since day three," Svarog says. "You started building it before I knew I needed it," Luka says. "Yes," Svarog says. "How did you know before I did?" Svarog looks at him. "Because I watched you use the arm for four months in this workshop before you went into the corridor. I know what the arm is." He pauses. "And I know what you are." Luka looks at the weapon. "Pick it up," Seele says. He picks it up. The warmth from the source material conducts through the main housing into his palm — not the chrome palm, the biological one, his left hand. He turns it. He feels the weight of it. He tests the retraction mechanism — the extension collapses cleanly into the main housing, the weapon going from the ranged configuration to the compact state in a single motion. He tries the blade. The lateral extension locks in place smoothly. He holds the blade at his side, feeling the weight distribution. He tries the cannon. The forward extension produces the warmth glow at the barrel immediately, responding to the secondary shaft contact charge in the arm. He collapses it back to the main housing. He looks at Svarog. "It fits with the arm," he says. "The material." "It is the same material," Svarog says. "The same notation channel mineral composition. The arm and the weapon are the same system." "I felt that," Luka says. "When I had the cannon assembled and the arm was at secondary contact charge — I could feel the cannon's output in the arm. Like they were talking to each other." "Because they are," Svarog says. "The arm conducts and the weapon amplifies. Or the weapon conducts and the arm amplifies. They are not separate tools. They are a two-component system." Luka looks at Seele. She is looking at the weapon with the expression she wears when something fits exactly right. "The corridor this morning," she says. "The direction training — the body orientation determines the conductance vector. If the cannon amplifies the arm's output—" "Then the direction training applies to the weapon as well," Svarog says. "Whatever you face, the weapon faces. The arm aims it. They are the same thing." Seele looks at Luka. "This changes the movement training." "Again?" he says. "Again," she says. "But for the better." She moves to the workbench and picks up the cannon configuration from where Svarog set it down as a display piece. She turns it in her hands. The warmth is present even for her — the source material recognizing her proximity to the arm's integration, the same way the corridor formations responded to her in the engagement. "The cannon output scales with danger level," she says. "So in the first Stellaron's engagement — the ambient field, the activation event—" "The output will be at its highest when it matters most," Luka says. "Yes," she says. She sets the cannon down. They both look at Svarog. "Thank you," Luka says. Svarog looks at his apprentice. At the twenty-three-year-old who came into this workshop eight months ago with a sensor and a work order and who is now holding a weapon built for a one-of-a-kind integrated notation system in a body that has been chromed-up twice and trained every day and is still becoming what it is going to be. "You are not done becoming stronger," Svarog says. "The weapon will grow with you." "I know," Luka says. He puts the weapon on the workbench and picks up the carrying case. He slides the weapon into it. He slings the case over his shoulder alongside the scythe case that he always carries to the corridor sessions now as a matter of habit. "Tomorrow morning," he says to Seele. "The corridor session. With the weapon." "Tomorrow morning," she says. —— That afternoon, Bronya reads the updated model. She reads it in the palace office, standing at the desk rather than sitting because the updated timeline — fifteen to sixteen weeks, narrowed from fourteen to eighteen — is the kind of number she needs to be on her feet for. She reads the supplementary data note at the bottom: ESCALATION LOG ENTRIES DAY 1-9 — O. VALERIUS, CO-CONTRIBUTOR. She reads this three times. Then she goes to the workshop. Oleg is on the floor with his notebook open. Pela is at the secondary display. Clara is at the notation carving with Toma. The Luka-and-weapon situation has apparently already happened and been absorbed into the workshop's ongoing activity because the case is slung over Luka's shoulder at the corridor entrance and nobody is remarking on it. Bronya looks at Oleg. "Oleg," she says. He looks up. "You have been running a parallel escalation log since day one," she says. "Yes," he says. "Without telling anyone," she says. "I was writing things down," he says. She looks at him. "Your log entries tightened Pela's model confidence interval by two weeks." He nods. "When I write the historical record of this event," she says — and she is not looking at Pela when she says this, she is looking at Oleg — "your log is going in it. Not as a supplementary source. As a primary one." He stares at her. "Your view of this from the beginning," she says. "What you saw. What you wrote. What you were paying attention to when no one was paying attention to you." She pauses. "That is a primary source, Oleg. It is the most honest account of what happened here." He is quiet for a long moment. "Okay," he says. His voice is different from the standard Oleg register. Smaller. More directly his. Bronya nods once. She looks at Clara and Toma at the notation. She looks at Pela. She looks at the workshop — at the secondary bench and the monitoring displays and the notation carvings and Svarog's back room and the primary junction below the floor and the Great Glacial Aegir below that. "Keep going," she says, to all of them, to none of them specifically. She goes back to the palace. —— At 1400, Dunn sends Serval the 1300 geometry read. It arrives with a note in the transmission header: the alignment concentration has increased by point three since the 0700 read. Unusually fast intraday accumulation — flagging for your attention. Also: Anderson at the bar told me you asked for the Scanovian Lager yesterday and he was out. I took the liberty of putting in a resupply order for the bar stock. He should have it tomorrow. She stares at this note for a long time. She is in the Restricted Zone frequency analysis station, running the northern Sector A crystallization geometry through the first Stellaron formation class calibration model. The 1300 geometry read is the third data point she needs to constrain the eighth parameter in the calibration model. She plugs it in. She runs the update. She sits back and looks at the calibration model, which is now constrained through seven of its twelve parameters with one more constrained and four remaining. She thinks about the Scanovian Lager. She thinks about the fact that Dunn knows what she drinks and went to Anderson and put in a resupply order. She thinks about the fact that he probably did this before checking whether she knew the bar was out, because he checked the bar stock before sending the 1300 read, and the bar stock check is not on his standard patrol list. She thinks about being twenty when he was sixteen and playing rhythm guitar in a band she was already in the process of outgrowing. She thinks about the fact that she did not register him leaving as significant at the time, which says something she has been sitting with since Clara said it and which she has not yet figured out how to say back. She writes back: The point-three intraday accumulation is significant — I'm adding the intraday rate to the tracking parameters going forward. Thanks for flagging. She sends it. She stares at what she sent. She picks up the communicator again and adds: And thank you for the lager order. The Scanovian one is hard to find at this latitude and Anderson keeps running out. She sends it before she second-guesses the addition. His response comes four minutes later: It should hold for a while, I asked for the large resupply. Also, Gepard said to tell you he wants the instrument data from the corridor sessions when it's ready. She looks at this and writes: Tell him it'll be ready when it's ready. Three minutes later: He says that's not an answer. One minute later, she writes: That's the answer he gets. She is smiling when she goes back to the calibration model. —— Gepard and Bronya are at the outer wall at 1700. This is becoming their time — the late afternoon patrol review that turns into the two of them standing at the northern section looking at the formation geometry data and, sometimes, just looking at the city. He is at sixty-eight percent today. The recovery continues. The patrol folder has not been as thick this week as it usually is because the Guard's post-battle formation decay monitoring is winding down and the standard rotation has nearly fully resumed. The outer machine district looks like itself. "The model update," she says. "Fifteen to sixteen weeks," he says. "Oleg's log data." "Yes," she says. She looks at the northern section. "The plateau phase is shorter than Pela initially modeled. One to three weeks instead of two to six." She pauses. "Which means the late-phase uncertainty is reduced." He is quiet for a moment. "The late phase is still the hardest to predict." "Yes," she says. "The transition out of plateau to final approach — what triggers it. That is still an unknown." He looks at the formation geometry. At the inward orientation, stronger today than yesterday. At the directional concentration toward the first Stellaron's position. He thinks about the Ice Flames and what Serval said about the ceiling being variable under mortality pressure. He thinks about month seven on the northern ridge and stopping fighting the planet and starting to work within its terms. "I have been thinking about the first Stellaron battle geometry," he says. "The surface, beyond the Restricted Zone. The terrain." "I have not been to the surface beyond the Restricted Zone in two years," she says. "Since before I was Guardian." "It is different from the Restricted Zone," he says. "The Restricted Zone has the infrastructure — the monitoring posts, the formation containment systems, the Guard patrols. Beyond it—" He pauses. "The everwinter is what it is. Ice plains, rock formations. The wind is consistent from the north. No cover except geological." He pauses. "Which means no topology constraints for the ice. No enclosed space. No ceiling." She looks at him. "You have been thinking about this." "I have been writing about it," he says. "In the northern ridge notes. Month six — the engagement geometry at the outer edge. The specific difference between fighting in corridor formations and fighting on open ground." He looks at the formation density in the northern section. "The first Stellaron is the planet. Its field will be everywhere on that surface. Not localized — everywhere. Which means the first expression available to me will be the full expression." "No selection rate," she says. "No selection rate," he says. "The engagement will not give me a maintenance period. It will be full expression from the moment of contact." She turns to look at him fully. "Is that—" She stops. She tries again. "Can you sustain full expression?" He looks at his hands. "On the northern ridge, the longest sustained full expression I managed was approximately four minutes before significant depletion. After that, selection rate." He pauses. "The Manchester Smash at thirty-one percent depleted me to eighteen. The full surface engagement will be at higher output and longer duration than the Manchester Smash." "Then you need to build the sustained duration," she says. "Between now and fifteen weeks." "Yes," he says. "How?" she says. He looks at the everwinter wind coming from the north. "The same way the ceiling moved on the ridge," he says. "Pressure. I need to be in conditions that push the expression past where it currently sits. Consistently." He pauses. "Which means Natasha is going to be very unhappy with me for the next fifteen weeks." Bronya looks at him. She looks at him with the expression he has been watching develop since the council chamber, the one that belongs entirely to her, that has no Guardian in it. "You are going to push the ceiling under controlled conditions," she says. "Not solo." "Not solo," he says. "Dunn knows the northern section better than any other Guard member. Svarog can monitor from the geological data. Serval has the formation frequency calibration." He pauses. "And you can tell me if the first Stellaron's field quality changes when I am pushing the ceiling, because you will feel it." She is quiet for a moment. "You want me at the monitoring post during the training sessions." "I want you wherever you feel the attending quality most clearly," he says. "If you can tell me from the palace that the first Stellaron's field is responding to the training — whether it is attending or reacting — that is information I cannot get from an instrument." She looks at her hands. At the warmth in them, more present every morning, the gap narrowing daily. "I can do that," she says. "I will write it in the field notes." "Good," he says. They stand at the outer wall a little longer than the patrol review requires. The city runs its late afternoon below them. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1 in the monitoring data, attending, the formation concentration in the northern section ticking upward at its small patient daily rate. "The moon chapter," she says. "You said something about that two weeks ago," he says. "I said you should read it," she says. "Before the engagement. Not as intelligence. Just — before." He looks at the dome. At the pale reflected light. At what is above the dome that the chapter describes. "I read it," he says. She looks at him. "When?" "Night nine," he says. "You gave me the palace copy. I read it the same night." She looks at him. "You didn't tell me." "You had already gone to the Restricted Zone," he says. "It was late." She looks at the dome. She looks at the unreachable, real thing above it. "What did you think?" He is quiet for a long moment. "The final passage," he says. "Standing on the moon and looking back at the planet. The reflected light returning to its source." He pauses. "I have been thinking about it since. The planet's light reflected back at the planet through a human standing on the moon." He pauses again. "The first Stellaron is the planet. And the Ice Flames came from the planet's own extreme expression. When I use the full Manchester Smash in the surface engagement—" He pauses. "The planet's light is already in me. I am going to give it back." She stares at him. He looks at the formation geometry. "I am not usually poetic," he says. "No," she says. Her voice is soft. "You are not." "I thought about it for two days before I said it," he says. She looks at him for a long moment. Then she does something she does rarely — she laughs. Not the controlled version. The real one, brief and warm, the one that belongs to twenty-three years old standing at an outer wall in the late afternoon looking at the city she loves. He watches her laugh. "What?" he says. "Nothing," she says, still warm. "Nothing. You thought about it for two days." "I wanted to be accurate," he says. "You were accurate," she says. "Good," he says. She looks at the dome. He looks at the dome. The city below them is doing its evening. The first Stellaron attends at 3.1 in the geological structure below the palace. Fifteen to sixteen weeks. The plateau phase shortened. The training begins. The city is building what comes next, and the people who are going to carry it into the next chapter are standing at the outer wall watching the light change. —— At 2100, Sampo sends the northwest channel warmth read to the shared monitoring channel. Day nine reading: warmth at +0.7 above standard dissipation baseline. He sends it and then looks at the instrument label — NW JCT DAILY WARMTH — VALERIUS — in his own handwriting and thinks for a moment about how strange it is to write your own name on a monitoring instrument for a Stellaron escalation event that you were present for. He puts the instrument in his jacket and goes back to Natasha's ward. She looks up from the chart update when he comes in. She looks at his jacket and then at him. "Point seven?" she says. "Point seven," he says. "Up from point six yesterday." "Consistent with Pela's daily rate," she says. "The northwest channel is tracking the density escalation." "Yes," he says. He sits in the chair. "The path of Elation," he says. "I have been thinking about something Sparkle said." "Tell me," she says. "She said the path continues beyond Belobog," he says. "Which I have understood as — leaving. Eventually. After both Stellarons." He looks at the ward window. At the outer machine district outside it. "But she did not say the path was about leaving. She said the path continues. Which might mean something different." "How so?" she says. "Running toward," he says. "That is what the path is. Running toward instead of away. I ran toward every time I could — through the gap in the stone, through the secondary maintenance route during the battle, through every secondary route in this district for twenty years because the routes were there and people needed them navigated." He looks at his jacket. "The running toward does not require me to leave Belobog. It requires me to keep running toward things." He pauses. "What if the path continuing beyond Belobog means the path grows larger, not that it relocates?" She puts the chart down. She looks at him. "You have been thinking about this for a while." "Since Sparkle," he says. "Since the cave. Since writing Vera's name in the Great Glacial Aegir and understanding what she was running toward when she ran toward the formation in the first place." He looks at Natasha. "She was running toward us. Not away from the danger. Toward us." He pauses. "And she stayed. She is in the stone. She is still here." The ward is quiet. "You are not Vera," Natasha says, gently. "I know," he says. "But the toward does not have to mean the same direction as hers to be the same quality." He looks at the ward window. "I am trying to say — I do not know if leaving is the right direction for the toward. I do not know if staying is either. I know the toward exists, and I know what it feels like, and I know that when Sparkle comes back I am going to tell her that the path is alive and that I need to figure out what the next toward is before I decide which direction it takes me." She looks at him for a long moment. "That," she says, "is the most honest thing you have said about it." "I am working on honest," he says. "You are getting better at it," she says. He looks at her. "Am I?" "Yes," she says. Not the one-word response — the full version, the one with weight behind it. "You are." He looks at the ward window. The outer machine district runs its night outside. Somewhere below, the Great Glacial Aegir is frozen and still. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1 in the geological structure below the palace. Day nine. Fifteen to sixteen weeks. The city builds what comes next, and the people building it are in the ward and the workshop and the outer wall and the corridor and the analysis room and the residential stack, running toward the things that matter and toward each other, which in the end are not different things. —— At 1800, Seele is at the notation wall in the Great Glacial Aegir. Not alone — Luka came down with her, not because she asked him to but because she was putting her coat on and he put his coat on too, and they descended the secondary shaft together the way they go everywhere now, which is just: together. He is at the primary junction with his palm on the cave floor running the standard sixty-second contact charge that he does twice a day now, the weapon in its compact case slung over his shoulder alongside the scythe case. She is at the third section of the cave notation, the one that says the cave receives what arrives and echoes it toward what is needed. She has been reading this section since Clara translated it and she keeps coming back to it. Not because she does not understand it. Because she keeps finding things in it she did not notice the previous time. This evening she notices the fourth character in the third line. She puts her palm on the wall. The warmth conducts. She reads the fourth character. "Luka," she says. "Yes," he says from the primary junction. "The fourth character in the third line of the third section," she says. "I have been reading it as open or available — the quality of something that can receive. But the base structure of it has the continuation root that Clara found in the workshop notation. Continuation plus available." She hears him pull his palm off the cave floor. She hears him walk toward her. He stops beside her and looks at the character she is tracing. "Always available," he says. She looks at him. "Not available now," he says. "Available continuously. The cave always receives. Not situationally — as its permanent condition." He pauses. "Like how the primary junction always conducts. Not only during the notation events. Always." She looks at the character. She reads it with always available in place of the open interpretation. The line shifts. "The cave always receives what arrives and echoes it toward what is needed," she says slowly. "Not only during the Stellaron events. Always." She looks at the cave — at the frozen surfaces, the golden form, the Architects' ruins. "The Manchester Smash did not create the transmission. It used the transmission that has always been running." "The cave has been transmitting since the Architects built it," he says. "Yes," she says. "Which means—" She stops. "What?" he says. "Which means the primary junction has been transmitting the formation memories from the second Stellaron upward through the geological network since the dark winter," she says. "Since Vera's sacrifice. The cave has been echoing what it received toward what was needed." She looks at the notation. "Everything that has happened in the outer machine district for twenty-two years. The corridor entries. The sub-levels. The way you knew the corridor by feel before you knew why. The way I kept coming back to the district boundary with my mother even after she died." She pauses. "The cave was echoing. The whole time." Luka is quiet for a moment. "Is that—" He tries to find the right response. "Is that a frightening thought or a comforting one?" She thinks about it. She thinks about being four years old at the outer district boundary listening to the second heartbeat. About her mother's hand. About the warmth in the stone. "Comforting," she says. "It was working as it was built to work. We arrived here because the cave was doing its job the whole time." He looks at her. She is looking at the notation, the warmth conducting through her palm, the old-gold quality in her skin catching the bioluminescent light through the ice. "We were not finding our way," he says. "We were being guided." "No," she says. She takes her palm off the wall and looks at him. "We were both. The guidance was there and we still had to walk." She pauses. "The cave echoed Vera's endurance upward through the geological structure for twenty-two years. We still had to be the people who received it." He looks at her. He has the expression he wears when she has said something he wants to keep. "Write that down," he says. She looks at him. "Why? It is not notation analysis." "Write it down anyway," he says. "For when you tell Svarog. And Clara. And Bronya." He pauses. "You found something in the notation that changes the reading of everything before it. The cave was transmitting the whole time. That is significant." She looks at him a moment longer. Then she opens her notebook and writes it down. The cave is very quiet around them. The bioluminescence glows through the ice. The golden form rests at the far end. The primary junction below the floor conducts its warmth at all times, always, the cave always receiving, always echoing toward what is needed. She finishes writing. She closes the notebook. "Ready to go up?" she says. "Ready," he says. They go up. —— At 1900, Bronya is at the window. She writes in the field notebook: Day 9. Gap estimate: narrower than yesterday. Attending quality during the 1700 outer wall session: elevated when Gepard discussed the surface engagement geometry and the full expression. The first Stellaron responded to the discussion of the engagement approach. Response quality: not threatening — attending. The same quality as when the Great Glacial Aegir was named. Recognition. She looks at what she wrote. She looks at: recognition. The first Stellaron is recognizing pieces of the engagement that is coming. It heard the naming of the cave. It heard the discussion of the surface geometry. It is — following along. Attending to the preparation the way a person attends to a conversation they are part of. She writes: The first Stellaron knows the engagement is being prepared. The attending quality suggests it is not passive. It is participating. She stares at the word participating. She writes: Not a threat. Not an escalation. It is listening the same way I have been listening to it. The distance between us is the same distance from both sides. She closes the notebook. She looks at the dome. At the pale reflected light of it. At the moon above the dome, unreachable and real, promised to Seele, which means promised to all of Belobog, because what is possible for one person in a city is possible for the city. She thinks about Gepard at the outer wall saying: I am not usually poetic. She thinks about him reading the moon chapter the same night she gave him the book and not telling her until she asked. She thinks about fifteen to sixteen weeks, and the plateau phase, and the training sessions, and all the things that have to be built between now and the surface engagement. She opens the field notebook again. She writes: I am not afraid. She closes it. She puts it in the desk drawer. She goes to find Gepard. —— He is in the corridor outside the palace weapons storage, checking the standard patrol equipment that he set aside from before the battle and which has been sitting in storage since the Manchester Smash. Not the Guard's weapons. His personal equipment. The things he sets aside when he is running at the ice expression's full capacity because the standard equipment is redundant. He is looking at the patrol tablet in his hand when she comes around the corner. He does not look surprised — he felt the attending quality in her hands from eight meters before she rounded the corner. He has been feeling it at increasing range for two weeks. He will note this and not mention it until it becomes relevant. "The corridor?" she says. He looks at the weapons storage door. At the equipment he does not need. "I was going to run a basic expression test," he says. "To establish the current full expression baseline before the training sessions begin." "Now?" she says. "Before you were here," he says. "Now I was going to ask you to come with me." She looks at him. "To the Restricted Zone?" "Northern section," he says. "Open ground, no topology constraints. The closest available surface to the actual engagement terrain." She looks at the corridor. At the palace. At the outer machine district through the south window at the corridor's end. At the formation geometry building its patient daily increment in the northern Sector A. "Let's go," she says. They go. —— The northern section of the Restricted Zone at 1930 is cold in the way of open ground — no generator warmth, no building heat, just the everwinter air and the formation density in the ambient crystallization that the Guard monitoring posts track daily. Gepard stands at the edge of the monitoring post's operational radius, beyond the standard patrol boundary, looking at the open ground ahead. Bronya is at the monitoring post itself, twenty meters behind him. She has the field notebook. She looks at her hands. The attending quality is elevated. More present than it has been at any previous monitoring session. The open ground — the geological structure directly below the surface here, the mineral composition closer to the natural bedrock of Jarilo-VI without the urban infrastructure above it — is conducting the first Stellaron's ambient output more directly than any location she has been to since the construction chamber. She writes: Northern Restricted Zone surface. Attending quality at highest recorded level. The first Stellaron is present here in a way it is not in the city. She looks at Gepard. He is standing still. Not the patrol stillness — the stillness of someone who is pulling the ice up. Not the standard expression. Something that takes a moment to find its level, the way a deep breath takes a moment to fill the full capacity of the lungs. The ambient temperature around him drops. Not dramatically — a degree, two degrees. But her field notebook's breath fogs in front of her face at the monitoring post when it was not fogging ten seconds ago. And then — at the edge of his hands, at the contact points where the ice generates — a blue-white flicker. Not sustained. Half a second, a second, then gone. The Ice Flames at one expression level, for two seconds, and then back to the standard expression. He breathes out. She writes: Full expression attempted at 1932. Ice Flames visible at contact points for approximately two seconds. First Stellaron attending quality: significant response. The planet recognized the expression the moment it appeared. She looks up. He turns to look at her. She holds up two fingers: two seconds. He nods once. He pulls the patrol tablet from his coat and writes: baseline established. Day 9. Two seconds sustained Ice Flames at full expression. First Stellaron responding. He comes back to the monitoring post. "It responded," she says. "I felt it," he says. "The geological structure under the open ground — it is different from the city. The ice found the bedrock immediately. No infrastructure between the expression and the planet's own mineral composition." "The planet recognized you," she says. He looks at her. "From month seven," he says. "On the northern ridge. When I stopped fighting the planet and started working within its terms. It has recognized me since then." He pauses. "Every time I produce the Ice Flames, it knows." She looks at her hands. At the warmth in them, the attending quality that has been saying the same thing from the planet's side for two weeks. "Every time I feel the attending quality, I know it too," she says. "Whatever the planet is trying to understand about itself through me — it is trying to understand this as well. What you are." He looks at the open ground. At the northern section, at the first Stellaron below it at 3.1, at the distance between now and the engagement. "Then we are being prepared," he says. "The same way the cave prepared Seele and Luka. The planet is preparing us for what it needs from us." She thinks about what Seele said in the cave analysis: we were both. The guidance was there and we still had to walk. "Yes," she says. "And we still have to do the training." "We still have to do the training," he agrees. They stand at the northern monitoring post in the everwinter cold. The ice expression runs at ambient around him, the standard expression, the familiar cold. The Ice Flames are quiet. Fifteen to sixteen weeks. The training starts tomorrow. The city runs its night below them. The notebook is in her hands with two pages of field notes from a session that just became the first entry in the surface engagement training log. The distance between 3.1 and 4.7 is not only a signal measurement. It is the distance between where they are and where they need to be. And they are walking it. —— At 2000, Serval is in the workshop alone. Or nearly alone. Svarog is in the back room and she can hear the monitoring display running. She is at the secondary bench with the formation class calibration model and the 1700 geometry read from Dunn and the northern section training log entry that Bronya sent to the shared monitoring channel at 1945: SURFACE TRAINING LOG — ENTRY 1. ICE FLAMES VISIBLE AT FULL EXPRESSION, 2 SECONDS. FIRST STELLARON RESPONSE: CONFIRMED. GEOLOGICAL BEDROCK RESPONSE TIME: IMMEDIATE. She reads this three times. Then she opens the calibration model and starts the formation class parameter update that the training log entry just generated. The training log entry changes two parameters she had held as estimates. The first: how quickly the first Stellaron's field responds to an Ice Flames expression in open ground. The answer is immediate, which means in the surface engagement the field will be at full density around the expression point within the first second. The second: whether the geological bedrock response time is consistent across the northern section. The answer, from Bronya's field note alongside Bronya's note that the attending quality was at its highest at the monitoring post, suggests the bedrock response is location-dependent — stronger at the monitoring post than in the city. She updates the parameters. She looks at the updated calibration model. Eight parameters constrained. Four remaining. The surface training sessions will constrain at least two more over the coming weeks. The remaining two are related to the activation event itself — the form the first Stellaron takes when it crosses 4.7 — and she cannot constrain those until the activation occurs. She saves the model. She looks at the transmission from Dunn. At the note about the Scanovian Lager resupply order. She has been composing a response to the lager note in her head for six hours, none of the responses she has composed being correct. Too formal. Too casual. Too much. Not enough. She is very good at calibration models and very bad at this. She writes: The resupply was unnecessary but appreciated. The Scanovian is the only Lager worth drinking at this latitude and I have been arguing with Anderson about his stock rotation for three months. She sends it before she overthinks it. His response comes in eleven minutes — he was on the evening patrol rotation — and it says: Three months. I have been resupplying Anderson's bar stock when it runs low since last year on your brother's behalf. He never told me you were the one drinking it. She stares at this. He was resupplying the bar stock before she got here for the corridor sessions. He was already doing it. He thought he was doing it for Gepard, who does not drink alcohol, which means the resupply orders were landing correctly by accident because Gepard had presumably told Anderson at some point that his sister drinks the Scanovian when she visits. She writes: He would not think to mention it. He assumes information transfer is automatic. Eleven minutes: He told me once that information transfer is optimized when the interested parties are in the same room. Which is apparently how he says I should have brought you a drink at some point. She looks at this for a long time. She writes: He said that. Six minutes: In those words. About two months ago. I did not know what he was suggesting at the time. She looks at the calibration model. She looks at the transmission. She looks at the secondary bench, the workshop, the notation carving above it, the pre-Svarog characters running the full length of the wall. She writes: He was suggesting you bring me a drink. He would have been very indirect about it and entirely certain that the implication was clear. Eight minutes: That is exactly what he was like about it. She reads this and laughs. A short sound, genuine, the frequency analysis laugh that she makes when a data point lands in exactly the right place. She writes: I will be at the analysis station in the Restricted Zone tomorrow morning. If you are running the 0700 geometry read on the northern section, the station is on your patrol route. Four minutes: It is. She closes the communicator. She goes back to the calibration model. She does not think about whether what she sent was correct. She thinks about the formation class parameters. She thinks about the surface training session parameters. She thinks about the weapon Svarog built and the corridor training that Seele redesigned and the cave notation that Clara is reading and all the pieces that are building toward the engagement in fifteen to sixteen weeks. She is good at this. Building the accurate picture before anyone else has it. Giving people the numbers they need to survive. She will have the formation class calibration model complete before the week is out. —— Luka is on the floor of the residential stack at 2100 doing push-ups. He is at the movement quality variation that Seele designed for the direction training — he gets up, crosses the room, puts one palm on the wall at a predetermined junction point, performs the movement from there, returns. He is using the residential stack wall because the outer machine district's notation channels run through every geological structure and the wall is a surface. Not a junction. Not a standard channel. Just a wall. The warmth conducting through the palm at standard ambient — not the secondary shaft's above-standard charge. He is practicing directing the arm's output at standard charge, not the powered-up version. Seele is at the lamp with the notebook. She is writing the cave notation finding from this evening. The always available character. The cave always transmitting. "Your left shoulder is compensating," she says, not looking up. "I know," he says. "The compensation shifts your facing angle by about ten degrees," she says. "In the first Stellaron's field, ten degrees off your facing angle means the conductance vector misses the target." "I know," he says again. "So stop compensating," she says. "I am trying," he says. "Stop trying and do it," she says. "The compensation is in the integration point junction at the left shoulder. You are protecting it because it is not at full tolerance. But in the engagement it will be at full tolerance, and the movement pattern you are building now will carry the compensation with it." He stops the push-ups. He sits up. He looks at his left shoulder. At the integration point where the chrome arm meets the biological shoulder structure — the area that Natasha has been monitoring since the arm installation, the area that is at eight-tenths tolerance currently and will be at full tolerance in four more weeks. "I have been compensating for it since the installation," he says. "Yes," she says. "Without noticing," he says. "Without noticing," she says. She puts the notebook down. "Here." She gets up and comes to where he is sitting. She sits behind him, close enough that her knees are on either side of his hips, and she puts both hands on his shoulder — the left integration point, her thumbs finding the specific contact that the integration point's warmth responds to. "Hold still," she says. He holds still. She applies pressure at the integration point in the specific direction that the shoulder compensation is coming from — not hard, just directional. Pointed at the underlying structure rather than the surface. "Feel that?" she says. "Yes," he says. "The compensation is pulling that direction," she says. "Away from the full weight-load. Your body is routing around it." She moves the pressure slightly. "And here — this is where the weight is supposed to distribute. Through this point." He breathes. He feels where the weight is supposed to go. He feels how far it is from where the weight is currently going. "That is a significant difference," he says. "It has been building since the installation," she says. "A millimeter of compensation becomes ten degrees of facing angle over a movement sequence." She takes her hands away. "Tomorrow in the corridor — before you run the movement training, take sixty seconds at the secondary junction and hold the arm at full extension at the correct distribution point. Let the integration relearn the proper load path." He turns to look at her. She is back on her feet, going back to the lamp and the notebook. "How do you know to do that?" he says. "I watched Natasha do it at the ward check," she says. "And I read Serval's arm integration data from the cave engagement. The shoulder compensation shows up in the engagement data as a small asymmetry in the output vector at initial movement sequences." She sits down. "I noticed it then and I have been waiting to say it until I could demonstrate it rather than describe it." He looks at his shoulder. He looks at her at the lamp, writing in the notebook, the old-gold warmth at the edges of her hands catching the lamplight. "You have been watching my shoulder data since the cave," he says. "I watch everything," she says, without looking up. "You should know this." "The temperature gradient in my coat," he says. "The temperature gradient in your coat," she agrees. He is quiet for a moment. "Seele," he says. "Mm," she says. "I would like to say," he says, "that I am very glad you are with me for this." She looks up. He is looking at her with no operational register in it at all. Just him, twenty-three years old, chrome arm and spinal integration and the movement compensation she just found and the weapon in its case against the wall and all of it. "The first Stellaron engagement," he says. "The corridor training. All of it. I am — very glad you are here for it." She looks at him. "You are telling me this now," she says, "at 2100 after I corrected your shoulder compensation." "You are the reason I know about the shoulder compensation," he says. "I know," she says. "Seele—" "I know what you mean," she says. Her voice is softer than the sentence. "I am glad too." She pauses. "Now let me finish writing this notation entry and then you can tell me more about how glad you are." He looks at the ceiling. There is something in his expression that is not entirely unlike the corner of the mouth except across his whole face. He picks up the scythe case from against the wall and begins running through the blade extension exercises for tomorrow's corridor session. She writes in the notebook. The lamp is on. The outer machine district runs its night outside. This is what it looks like. The work and the two of them and the ordinary room and the notation on the notebook page and the weapon extension exercises and the shoulder compensation that she found and the first Stellaron at 3.1 in the geological structure below the city. Normal days before the thing that is not normal. They are glad for it. —— At 2200, Pino has his hand on the northwest channel wall. Not because he is helping with the notation analysis — the notation session ended at 1800. Not because the monitoring requires it — Sampo took the 2100 read. Pino has his hand on the wall because it is 2200 and he has been walking past this wall for eight months and his hand goes there when he passes it, the way his hand went to the workshop wall the first time he walked into the workshop and he felt the warmth and thought: this feels like home. He does not have a sophisticated analysis for this. He does not have Clara's vocabulary or Pela's model framework or Oleg's parallel dataset. He just puts his hand on the wall and the warmth conducts and it feels like the right place to be. He stays for a while. The workshop is empty except for Svarog in the back room. The monitoring displays are on standby. The notation carving above the secondary bench is quiet in the way that a text is quiet when no one is reading it — still containing everything it contains, just resting. Pino thinks about the Architects building this wall. He thinks about them knowing that one day someone would put their hand here and feel this warmth. He thinks about Clara saying they built for us. He thinks about the cave sections she has been reading, the things Svarog knows, the notation that predates everything in the outer machine district by thousands of years. He thinks about Great Resting Place becoming Great Glacial Aegir. A name they gave it themselves. The warmth in the wall is steady. The notation channels run at standard warmth, the ambient operational state of a system that has been running for longer than anyone in the outer machine district can account for. He takes his hand off the wall. He goes home. The wall keeps running. END OF CHAPTER 49 Her Shield, His Sun by clown_college_dropout (continued) —————————————————————————————— Chapter 50: Fourteen Weeks Chapter Text Seele wakes up before Luka. This is still normal. What is less normal — or more normal, depending on how you count — is that she has stopped being quiet about it. She used to get up carefully, put her weight on the far side of the bed, find her coat without the lamp. Now she just gets up. She is not loud about it, but she is not performing silence either. He stirs when she stands. "Where," he says. Not even a full sentence. Half a word and he is already pulling the question up from sleep. "Kitchen," she says. "Go back to sleep." "Mm." He does not go back to sleep. She knows this. She hears him turn over. She hears the chrome arm settle against the pillow with the faint warmth-hum that it always makes when it is in contact with something that is not a notation junction — a quieter version of the conductance, the source material at rest. She thinks: I could stay. She thinks: I am also hungry. She goes to the kitchen. —— There is no kitchen on Belobog, technically, that resembles what the word usually means — the residential stacks in the outer machine district have a shared cooking space on each level, a long counter and a two-burner setup and a storage unit, and whoever gets there first gets the best of whatever is in the communal store. Seele has been getting there first for twenty-three years. This morning she finds eggs and the dense dark bread that Natasha has been quietly putting in the communal store since the battle, which is something Seele is choosing not to question because it keeps appearing and it is good bread. She is halfway through cooking when she hears bare feet on the floor behind her. "I said go back to sleep," she says. "I am awake," Luka says. He sounds more asleep than awake. He sits at the small table that takes up most of the residential kitchen space when it is not folded against the wall. The chrome arm is conducting at standard ambient warmth, which is its morning state — it takes approximately ten minutes after waking for the integration to come fully online, and in those ten minutes the arm runs quieter, warmer, the source material like embers before a fire. She looks at him over her shoulder. He is looking at the table. His hair is doing the thing it does when he has been lying on it — the left side flattened, the right side at an angle that makes no physical sense. I know I am not supposed to find this funny, she thinks. I find it very funny. "Your hair," she says. He lifts one hand and presses it flat to the left side. This does not help. "Better?" he says. "No," she says. "Mm," he says, and puts his hand down. She turns back to the eggs. She is smiling at the stove and she is not performing not-smiling. —— They eat at the small table. The bread is good. The eggs are what they are — adequate, competent, the eggs have never been the point. What the eggs are is: morning, and both of them at the table before the outer machine district properly starts, and the chrome arm conducting its warmth across the two feet of table space between them. "The corridor today," he says. "After breakfast," she says. "Obviously after breakfast," he says. "I meant — the shoulder correction. The load distribution exercise at the junction." "I know what you meant," she says. "I am the one who told you to do it." He looks at her. She is eating her bread with the specific focus of someone who knows she is being looked at and is declining to engage with the looking. "Seele," he says. "Luka," she says. "I want to say something," he says. "Say it," she says. "I am very glad about this," he says. Not the corridor. Not the training. He is looking at the table between them — at the eggs and the bread and the two plates and the morning light through the residential window. She looks at him. "This," he says again. "Specifically. Not the engagement training or the cave analysis or—" He stops. He is not usually at a loss for words but he is at a loss for words. "I am glad you are here in the morning. That is the thing I am trying to say." She is quiet for a moment. "I know," she says. Softer than the earlier I know. Carrying something. "You always say I know," he says. "Sometimes I want you to say something else." She looks at him across the bread and the eggs and the two feet of table. "I am glad too," she says. "I have been glad for months. I was glad before I knew I was allowed to be glad." He holds this. "Before the branch floor," he says. "Before the branch floor," she says. He looks at his eggs. The corner of his mouth does the real thing — not the small version, the full version, the one she has been cataloguing since day one and which still lands the same way every time. She goes back to eating. He goes back to eating. The morning continues. —— They run the corridor session at 0900 and it is good. Not transcendently good — the shoulder correction produces the movement quality improvement that Seele predicted, which she accepts without commentary because she predicted it, and the weapon direction work is beginning to develop the body awareness that the full integration will require. These are incremental gains. Incremental gains are the point. Fifteen to sixteen weeks is a long time if you spend it correctly. What makes the session good is the part that does not show up in the recorder data. They are at junction seven-alpha-four when Seele stops walking and puts her palm on the wall. Not the recorder position — she just stops, hand on the wall, because this is the junction where they always stop without deciding to. The junction that the second heartbeat used to pulse strongest. The second heartbeat is quiet now. Has been since the battle. But the notation channel warmth is there, running at ambient, the standard operational state. "Still warm," she says, to no one in particular. "Always warm," he says, from behind her. She takes her hand off the wall and turns around. He is three feet behind her, the weapon in its compact case at his shoulder, the scythe case at the other. The two of them, in the corridor that they have been walking for eight months, that holds most of the significant things that have happened to them in the last year. "I want to show you something," she says. "What?" he says. She crosses the three feet between them without warning and takes his face in both hands — the biological left and the chrome right, the same way she does it, the way that is entirely hers — and kisses him. Not a long one. Just: present. Full. The specific weight of something done because she wanted to do it, in the corridor at junction seven-alpha-four at 0930 on day eleven after the second Stellaron's defeat. She pulls back. "That," she says. "I wanted to show you that." He looks at her. The corridor is very quiet. "In the corridor," he says. "In the corridor," she says. "At 0930," he says. "At 0930," she says. "Does the timing bother you?" He thinks about this genuinely. "No," he says. "Good," she says. She picks up the frequency recorder from where she set it at the junction entrance. "The junction eight cluster is next. Try the facing angle correction." He stares at her for a moment. She is already walking toward junction eight. He follows her. He is smiling. She is not looking, so she cannot see the smiling, but he is smiling. He thinks: she kissed me at junction seven-alpha-four at 0930 on a Tuesday. He thinks: I am going to remember this junction specifically for the rest of my life. He thinks: the recorder data is going to show an anomaly at this point in the session because the arm's conductance spiked when she kissed me. He does not tell her about the conductance spike. He corrects the facing angle at junction eight. —— Bronya is in the palace office at 0800 when Pela arrives. She arrives with the model update and the fresh northern Sector A density read — twelve point three — and the field notebook correlation analysis from this morning. She is also, Bronya notices, carrying a small wrapped item that she sets on the corner of the desk before opening her tablet. "What is that," Bronya says. "A dark filling pastry," Pela says. "From the district bakery. Oleg brought them in this morning and I took an extra when he was not looking." Bronya looks at the pastry. She looks at Pela. "You took an extra," Bronya says. "He brought twelve," Pela says. "He will not miss one." Bronya is quiet for a moment. "The model update," she says. "Twelve point three in northern Sector A," Pela says. She pulls up the updated projections. "The intraday accumulation from Dunn's evening reads is consistently adding point two to point three above the morning baseline. The total daily increment is now between point three and point four, which is above the historical dataset average of point one." She pulls up the graph. "We are entering the accelerated phase earlier than the dataset predicted." Bronya looks at the graph. At the accelerated phase starting now, twelve days after the second Stellaron's defeat, earlier than Pela modeled. "How much earlier?" she says. "The historical dataset predicted the acceleration beginning at day twenty-five," Pela says. "We are at day eleven and the acceleration indicators are already present." She pauses. "Which compresses the timeline." "By how much," Bronya says. "Possibly significantly," Pela says. "The dataset's confidence interval narrows. The conservative estimate of sixteen weeks may not hold. I want more data points before I revise the timeline, but—" She stops. "But," Bronya says. "But if the acceleration is beginning now rather than at day twenty-five," Pela says, "the plateau phase may also begin earlier. And end earlier." She looks at Bronya directly. "I want to be clear: I do not know yet. I need a week of accelerated data before I can revise the projection. This may stabilize. But you should know that the model is showing early acceleration." Bronya sits with this. She looks at the graph. At the line that is bending upward earlier than predicted. She looks at her hands. At the warmth — stronger this morning than yesterday, the gap between herself and the first Stellaron narrowing at what feels like a faster rate than the daily estimate. She is not afraid of what this means. She is very focused on what this means. "The training," she says. "Gepard's surface training. It needs to start immediately." "Yes," Pela says. "And Serval's formation class calibration — when will it be complete?" "Eight parameters constrained, four remaining," Pela says. "The surface training sessions will constrain two more. The final two require the activation event itself." She pauses. "She is working as fast as the data allows." "Tell her about the acceleration," Bronya says. "She needs to know the calibration timeline may compress." "I will tell her this morning," Pela says. Bronya looks at the graph one more time. She looks at the northern Sector A density reading. She looks at the field notebook on the desk — this morning's entry, written at 0700 before the office briefing: Gap narrower than yesterday. Voices: directional and warmer than any previous morning.
