Something has changed in the quality. Still not words. But closer. She looks at Pela. "How are you sleeping?" she says. Pela blinks. "Seven hours," she says. "Clara has been — consistent about it." Bronya looks at the pastry on the corner of the desk. "Eat that before we continue," she says. Pela eats the pastry. Bronya waits for her. Then they continue. —— At the workshop at 1100, Clara is teaching Oleg the notation vocabulary. Not because Svarog asked her to, not because the notation analysis requires it, not because Oleg asked. She just decided this morning that Oleg has been putting his hand on walls for eight months and should know what the walls are saying. She started with the five characters that appear most frequently in the workshop notation — the ones that are the foundational vocabulary, the building blocks that the other characters extend from. She wrote them in the notebook and gave it to him. "Read the first one," she said. He looked at it. He looked at her. He said: "It looks like a person leaning." "What does a person leaning mean to you?" she said. "It means they are tired," he said. "Or they are listening." She looked at the character. She looked at Oleg. "That is—" she started. "Wrong?" he said. "No," she said. "The character means rest-and-receive. The Architects used it for states of active but still reception." She paused. "The leaning you described — leaning to listen — that is not wrong. That is a reading." He looked at the character again. "Leaning to listen," he said. "Leaning to listen," she said. That was forty minutes ago. They are now on character three and Oleg has produced three readings from his own observations that are not the standard vocabulary translation but are also not wrong — they are the intuitive version of the meaning, the felt understanding rather than the technical one. Svarog has been listening from the back room without commenting. "This one looks like a door that is open but not swinging," Oleg says. "What does that mean?" Clara says. "It means it has been open for a while," Oleg says. "Like it was opened and then stayed that way. Not a new door. An already-opened door." Clara reads the character. The standard translation is: continuous or persistent state. She has been reading it that way for months. But Oleg's version — an already-opened door — is the same meaning arrived at from the experience of having opened many doors in the outer machine district and knowing the difference between a newly opened one and one that has been standing open. "You are good at this," she says. "I am not reading it," he says. "I am looking at it." "Those are the same thing," she says. "Are they?" he says. She considers this. "Maybe not," she says. "Maybe looking at it and reading it are two different inputs that produce the same output." "Like my hand on the wall," he says, "and your hand on the junction. Both feel the warmth. Different input, same warmth." She looks at him. He looks at character four in the notebook. She thinks: he has been paying attention to the right things all along and nobody noticed because he was quiet about it. She thinks: Pela noticed when she asked to use the notebook. I noticed when he read the character. I wonder when Toma noticed. I wonder when Pino noticed. "What does this one look like?" she says, pointing at character four. He looks at it. "A wave that did not break," he says. "Like when you see a wave approach the shore and then it does not crash, it just — dissolves into the water." She looks at character four. The standard translation is: potential energy, or force that did not discharge. "That," she says, "is exactly right." He looks at her. "Really?" "The Architects used this character for any energy that existed but did not discharge," she says. "Force-not-released. Power-in-waiting." She looks at the character. "A wave that did not break is exactly that." He looks at the character for a long time. "I feel like I should have been paying more attention," he says. "You have been paying more attention than most," she says. "You wrote it all down." He looks at his notebook — the record of events that Bronya said was the most honest account of what happened here. He looks at character four. He writes in the notebook: character 4 — wave that did not break. Force waiting. Clara watches him write. She thinks: this is what it looks like when someone understands a thing fully. Not the technical version. The whole version. She moves to character five. —— At 1300, Gepard runs the second surface training session. The northern section of the Restricted Zone. Open ground, geological bedrock close to the surface. Dunn at the monitoring post. Bronya's field notebook at the secondary position. The first session established the baseline: two seconds of sustained Ice Flames at full expression. Today's target: four seconds. He stands at the edge of the open ground. The wind from the north. The formation geometry in the ambient crystallization around him — slightly higher density than yesterday, the acceleration Pela found already visible in the field. He does not check the sensor reading. He feels the density in the cold. He brings the ice up. The first expression is the standard — the full ice, the ambient temperature drop, the same expression that has been in his hands since he was seventeen years old. He holds this for ten seconds. The standard expression at ten seconds costs him nothing significant. This is baseline maintenance. He has been doing this every morning since the ward. Then he goes past it. The Ice Flames. He has been thinking about the ceiling-movement phenomenon since the first training session. Since the northern ridge, the ceiling moved three times — each time under maximum mortality pressure. Here there is no mortality pressure. He is on open ground in the Restricted Zone with Dunn behind him and Bronya's field notebook at the secondary position. He is safe. Safety does not produce the ceiling shift. So what he is trying to find is not the ceiling. He is trying to find the territory below the ceiling — the consistent Ice Flames expression that he can sustain without the mortality pressure, that will be available to him at any point in the surface engagement as a reliable resource rather than a desperate resource. The ceiling will move when it needs to. What he needs to build is the floor below it. He pushes the expression. The Ice Flames come up at the contact points. One second. Two seconds — the baseline from yesterday. He holds it. Three seconds. He is aware of the cost in a way he was not in the cave, because in the cave the Manchester Smash was a single deployment at maximum output. Here he is trying to hold. Four seconds. The expression cuts out. Not his choice — the expression hits its current limit and the Ice Flames drop back to standard ice. He breathes. He looks at his hands. Four seconds. Up from two. At the monitoring post, Bronya writes: Day 12. Surface session 2. Ice Flames sustained for four seconds. First Stellaron response: immediate, stronger than yesterday. Attending quality elevated for the full duration of the Ice Flames expression. The planet was with him for every second of the four. She pauses. She adds: It feels like encouragement. —— Dunn comes to Serval at the frequency analysis station at 1400. He has the daily geometry read and the training session data from the monitoring post. He comes at 1400 because the morning patrol ends at 1345 and the secondary patrol briefing is not until 1600 and the frequency analysis station is between both. This is the reason he tells himself. It is also the truth. Serval looks up when he comes through the station door. "Four seconds," he says, before she asks. He has learned that the first thing she wants is the training data. "Ice Flames held for four seconds at full expression. Monitoring post sensor logged the first Stellaron's field density response as a point seven increment during the expression window." She reaches for the tablet. She writes the data point. She pulls up the calibration model. She finds the parameter it updates. She inputs the value. She does not look at him while she does any of this. He stands in the doorway. He thinks: I have been standing in doorways around Serval for about two weeks now and I should probably stop standing in doorways and sit down. He sits down in the secondary chair that the station has for exactly this purpose. She updates the model parameter. She saves. She looks at the updated calibration. She says: "Point seven." "During a four-second sustained expression," he says. "The response rate during a single-second flash at full expression was point two in the first session. The sustained expression produces a significantly higher response rate." She looks at him. He thinks: she is doing the thing where she processes faster than the conversation. "The response rate scales with duration," she says. "Not just with output level. The longer the expression is sustained, the stronger the field responds." She pulls up a second display. "Which means in the surface engagement — if the Ice Flames can be sustained for thirty seconds, sixty seconds—" "The first Stellaron's field response might reach—" he starts. "Full field saturation," she finishes. "Around the expression point." "Is that good or bad?" he says. "Both," she says. "Full field saturation amplifies the arm's output through the notation network because the field density increases the shimmer conductance." She pauses. "But it also means the first Stellaron's full attention is on Gepard at that point. He becomes the center of the field event." "Which is what we want," he says. "If he is the center, Bronya's approach from inside is less exposed." She looks at him. He has been reading the engagement briefings. "You have been reading the engagement briefings," she says. "Since day three of recovery," he says. "When Pela sent the first model update to all monitoring stations." She holds her tablet and looks at him for a moment. He looks back. She thinks: he has been reading the engagement briefings. He has been tracking the training data alongside the calibration model. He sent the geometry reads before she asked. He put in the Scanovian resupply— She stops herself. The resupply is from the pasted story, wrong universe. She shakes that association loose. She thinks: he has been useful in ways that she did not assign him to be useful in. He did them without being asked. He came to the station. "The parameter eight constraint," she says. "The surface field density response. Your geometry reads have been the primary input." She pauses. "And now the training session data. Which you brought directly." "You needed it," he says. "Obviously." "You are supposed to be on patrol," she says. "Patrol ended at 1345," he says. "Secondary briefing is at 1600." "I know your rotation," she says. "I looked it up." He stares at her. She stares back. She has the expression of someone who said a true thing without completely planning to say it, and is now present with the truth of it without having prepared a follow-up. "You looked up my rotation," he says. "I needed to know when the geometry reads would arrive," she says. "You could have asked me," he says. "I could have," she says. Neither of them says anything for a moment. He thinks: she looked up my rotation. He thinks: she has been looking up my rotation. He thinks: she is twenty-eight years old and I am twenty-four and she left the band and joined her research career and I left the band and joined the Guard and we have been in the same city for three years and I have been sending her bar resupplies without knowing I was sending them to her. He thinks: I should say something. "The calibration model," he says. "When will parameter nine be constrained?" She looks at the model. "The next training session. If the duration increases to six or eight seconds, the field response data will give me the second data point I need for the regression." She pauses. "Which means I need the session data the same day." "I will bring it," he says. "At 1400," she says. "At 1400," he says. She goes back to the model. He looks at the calibration display. He does not know most of what the calibration model contains but he knows that the data points he brings every day are going into it and that the model is building toward the engagement that everyone in this city is building toward and that this is work that matters. He stands up. He has the secondary patrol briefing at 1600. "Landau," he says. She looks up. "The rhythm guitar section," he says. "From the lineup. I practiced for three months before I left." She holds her tablet. "I know," she says. "You were not bad." "I was not great," he says. "You were not bad," she says again. Firm. Meaning it. He holds this. "I should have stayed through the lineup change," he says. "I would have, if I had known you were taking it harder." She looks at him. "You could not have known," she says. "I did not announce it." "No," he says. "But I was there." She looks at the calibration display. She looks at her hands on the tablet. She says, without looking at him: "You came back." He looks at her. "Not in the band," she says quickly. "Just — you came back. You have been showing up. The geometry reads. The training data." A pause. "It counts." He stands in the station doorway for a moment. "The 1400 session data," he says. "Tomorrow." "Tomorrow," she says. He goes. She sits in the station for a moment after he leaves. She thinks: I told him it counts. She thinks: it does count. She goes back to the calibration model. —— Sampo and Natasha are at the outer machine district junction twelve at 1600. No particular reason. Natasha had a thirty-minute window between the ward's afternoon assessment schedule and the early evening run, and she came to find Sampo, and Sampo was at junction twelve looking at the secondary shaft entrance. They are standing at the junction twelve wall now, not doing anything specific. "The northwest channel warmth," Sampo says. "What about it?" Natasha says. "Up from last night," he says. "Point eight above dissipation baseline. The acceleration Pela found is showing in the channel reads too." He looks at the shaft entrance. "The cave is responding to the first Stellaron's acceleration." "The cave always transmits," she says. She has been reading Clara's notation findings. "Yes," he says. "Which means the cave is transmitting the acceleration upward through the geological network." He looks at the wall. "The outer machine district has been inside a Stellaron event for longer than anyone knew. The second heartbeat was always the second Stellaron. The warmth in the walls was always the notation network. The district itself is—" He stops. "What?" she says. "The district is the interface," he says. "Between the planet's deepest structure and the city above it. Everything that happened here — the dark winter, the memories, the battle — it all happened at the interface point." He pauses. "Which is why we are all here. Not by accident." She looks at him. "Vera ran toward junction twelve," she says. "Yes," he says. "The interface point. She ran toward the most connected place in the city." He looks at the shaft entrance. "And stayed there." Natasha stands beside him at junction twelve. She can feel the warmth through the wall without touching it — the notation channel running behind the stone, the ambient operational state of a system that has been running since before the district existed above it. She has been in the outer machine district for six years. She has been standing near this wall for six years. She has never thought about what was running through it. She thinks about it now. "When the first Stellaron activates," she says. "It will be at the surface. Not here." "No," he says. "Not here." "But it will be felt here," she says. "Everything is felt here," he says. "The district is the interface." She looks at the shaft entrance. At the corridor below it. At the Great Glacial Aegir below that. "Vera is in the stone," she says. "Yes," he says. "And she is running toward it," she says. "Still. The endurance that she gave to the second Stellaron — it is distributed through the district's geological structure now. Through the notation channels." She looks at the wall. "She is still running toward." Sampo is quiet for a long moment. "Toward us," he says. They stand at junction twelve for a while without saying anything else. The outer machine district runs its afternoon. The notation channels run their warmth through the stone behind them. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1 in the geological structure below the palace, attending, its daily increment accumulating toward the acceleration that Pela is modeling and the plateau that comes after and the activation that comes after that. Natasha takes Sampo's hand. He does not flinch. He holds it. They are standing at the interface between the planet's deepest structure and the city where they have spent their whole lives, and the warmth in the stone is Vera's endurance, and they are still here. "I think," Sampo says, quietly, "that toward means staying." She looks at him. "For now," he says. "Not forever, maybe. But right now — toward is here." He looks at the shaft entrance. "There is still work to do. And you are here. And the district is here." He looks at her. "The toward is clear." She holds his hand. "Then stay," she says. "For now." "For now," he says. They stay at junction twelve until the ward's evening schedule requires Natasha back, and then they walk back together, and the outer machine district does its evening around them. —— At 2000, Seele is reading. Not the notation notebook. Not the moon chapter. A novel — an actual novel, a printed paper book that she found in the residential stack's communal book exchange two days ago and has been working through in the evenings. She is three-quarters through it. She is not going to tell anyone what it is about because it is embarrassing. It is excellent. It is a deeply embarrassing excellent novel. Luka comes in at 2005 from the secondary shaft contact session. He looks at the book. She pulls it slightly toward herself. "What is that," he says. "A book," she says. "What book," he says. "A novel," she says. He comes to sit beside her on the low seat. He leans to look at the cover. She tilts it away. He tilts back. She tilts further. He puts his chrome arm around her shoulders and leans past her to see the cover. She holds the book flat against her front. "You are thirty years old," he says. "Wait, you are twenty-three." "I know how old I am," she says. "You are hiding a book from me," he says. "I am not hiding a book," she says. "I am maintaining my privacy." "Those are the same thing," he says. "They are not," she says. He has his arm around her shoulders and he is warm and she is not going to win this because she does not actually want to win this, which is the specific problem with having him at this proximity. She hands him the book. He reads the cover. He reads it again. He looks at her. "It is an excellent novel," she says. "It is a romance novel," he says. "It is an excellent excellent novel," she says. He opens the back cover. He reads the description. His expression does the thing where it is doing several things simultaneously and the thing at the front is not the most interesting thing. "Is it—" he starts. "It is very well written," she says. "The character development is rigorous." He reads the back cover again. "The character development," he says. "The secondary characters especially," she says. He closes the book. He holds it for a moment. He gives it back to her. "How far through are you?" he says. "Three quarters," she says. "And?" he says. She opens the book to her place. "And she should not trust the second option," she says. "She has been given two options and she is going to choose the wrong one and I can see it coming." He settles back against the low seat. His arm is still around her shoulders. "Is she going to fix it?" he says. "Probably," she says. "The first option comes back in the last quarter. He has been present the whole time and she was not paying attention." He looks at her. "She should have paid attention," he says. "She was paying attention to the wrong things," she says. "She should have paid attention to the right things," he says. "Yes," she says. "She should have." She looks at the page. "She gets there eventually." She reads. He does not move. The chrome arm is warm against her shoulder. The lamp is on. Outside, the outer machine district is running its night, the notation channels warm in the geological structure below, the first Stellaron at 3.1 in the palace foundation, the Great Glacial Aegir frozen and still below the outer machine district. After a while she says: "Would you like me to read it to you?" He says: "Yes." She reads to him. At some point around the three-quarter mark, where she predicted the wrong choice, the wrong choice happens. She stops reading. She does not say anything. He looks at the page. "She chose wrong," he says. "She chose wrong," she says. "As predicted," he says. "As predicted," she says. They sit with this. "Read the next part," he says. She reads the next part. —— In the palace at 2200, Bronya is writing in the field notebook. Day 12 entry, evening addition. She writes: The attending quality did not diminish after the training session ended. It has remained elevated through the full afternoon and evening. In previous sessions the quality returns to baseline after the expression ends. Tonight it has not. She pauses. She writes: I think the first Stellaron remembers what happened today. I think it is holding the expression in the way that a person holds a conversation after it ends — still thinking about it, still with the warmth of it. She stops writing. She reads what she wrote. She has been building this vocabulary for twelve days. She has been finding the words for what she is experiencing. She thinks: Jarilo-IV remembering the Ice Flames. Holding the warmth of them the way you hold the warmth of something that mattered. She writes: The planet is thinking about today. She closes the notebook. She goes to the adjacent corridor. Gepard is at the patrol terminal running the evening update, the same thing he does every evening when he is at full operational capacity. He looks up when she comes through. "The attending quality is still elevated," she says. "From the training session. It did not return to baseline." He looks at her. "The first Stellaron is still attending to what happened today," she says. He looks at his hands. "It remembers the Ice Flames," he says. "Yes," she says. He looks at the patrol terminal. He looks at the city-status display. He looks at her. "Then we should keep going," he says. "We are going to keep going," she says. He looks at her for a moment. At the warmth in her hands. At the field notebook she is still holding. At twenty-six years old with the planet's consciousness in her hands and the most focused expression she gets, which is the same expression she has when she is reading the patrol brief or the model update or the historical record — entirely present, entirely her. He crosses the corridor. He does not say anything. He just — puts his arms around her. Both of them. Not the forehead or the hand or any of the component gestures. The whole thing. She stills for one second. Then she leans into it. He is warm. The ice expression at ambient produces a specific cold that she has known since the construction chamber, and underneath it he is warm the way people are warm, and she has been carrying the first Stellaron in her hands for twelve days of acceleration and before that months of attending and before that the whole tenure of the Guardianship. She thinks: this is what I was going to tell him in the construction chamber. That it is bearable because he is here. That the large things are large but they are not only large. That there are also these moments in the palace corridor at 2200 with both arms and the warmth and the cold. She does not say any of this. She says: "Good session today." He says: "Four seconds." She says: "Tomorrow we try for six." He says: "Yes." They stand in the corridor. The patrol terminal runs its update. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1 in the geological structure below the palace, thinking about today, keeping the warmth of what it saw. Fourteen weeks, give or take. The city is building toward it. —— At 0930 the next morning, Seele finishes the novel. She reads the last page and closes the book. She looks at the cover. She looks at the wall across from the low seat. She thinks about the ending for a moment. She is satisfied. She thinks: the character development was rigorous. She thinks: the second option never had a chance. She thinks: I read this entire thing in four days and told no one about it except Luka and I am not going to tell anyone else. She puts it back in her coat pocket. She will return it to the communal book exchange. Someone else should read it. Luka comes out of the kitchen. "You finished it," he says. He read her face before she said anything. "She fixed it," Seele says. "The first option came back in the last forty pages." "Did she pay attention?" he says. "She paid attention," Seele says. "Good," he says. He hands her a cup of hot water — the outer machine district communal kitchen has no tea but it has hot water and she has been drinking it in the mornings since she was seven and this is not something that is going to change. He sits beside her. "What is the next one?" "The exchange had three options," she says. "I am considering." "Which three?" he says. She describes them. He listens with the specific quality of someone who has opinions about stories but is not going to offer his opinions until he has all the information. She describes the second option. He says nothing. She describes the third. He makes a sound. "That one," he says. "You have not read the description," she says. "The description you gave sounded like the kind of story where something important is true from the beginning and everyone takes too long to realize it," he says. "I find those satisfying." She looks at him. "That is very specific," she says. "I have opinions about stories," he says. "You have never told me you have opinions about stories," she says. "You have never asked," he says. She looks at him for a moment. He is drinking from his own cup. The chrome arm is conducting its morning warmth. His hair is doing the thing again — slightly more chaotic today than yesterday, which she suspects has to do with the angle he slept at because she knows exactly which position he was in when he fell asleep reading the novel with her last night and it was not a position favorable to hair. She thinks: twenty-three years old and opinions about stories and hair that does inexplicable things and the chrome arm running warm and the weapon in its case against the wall and a year of the most significant things that have ever happened to her or anyone around her. She thinks: I am so glad. She says: "Tell me one." "One what?" he says. "One opinion about stories," she says. He considers. "I think," he says, "that the best stories are the ones where the people in them do not know they are in a story. They are just doing the things that seem right and the things that seem necessary and the things they cannot help but do, and the story is what those things add up to." She looks at him. "We are in that kind of story," she says. "Yes," he says. "I think so." She drinks the hot water. He drinks his. Outside the outer machine district is doing its morning, the notation channels warm in the stone, the Great Glacial Aegir below, the first Stellaron at 3.1, the training beginning its second week. "The third book," she says. "The third book," he agrees. —— That afternoon, Gepard runs a different kind of session. Not the Ice Flames training. Not the surface expression baseline work. Serval asked for a different data set: the arm's output under the Ice Flames expression, measured simultaneously with the expression itself, to test whether the weapon amplifies the ice at the same ratio it amplifies the standard arm conductance. The session is in the northern Restricted Zone. Luka with the weapon. Gepard with the full expression. Serval with the frequency recorder and the calibration model. Dunn with the monitoring instruments. Bronya at the secondary position with the field notebook. Luka has not seen the Ice Flames in person before. He has heard about them — the cave, the training log, Svarog's back room — but he has not been present for them. Gepard warned him before the session: the expression at four seconds is not the cave. The cave was a single deployment of the Manchester Smash. This is the sustained expression. He watches Gepard pull the ice up. He has seen the standard expression many times — the ambient cold, the specific drop in temperature around Gepard's hands. He has felt it from three meters at the cave, from forty meters in the battle. He knows what the standard expression looks like. The Ice Flames are different. At the two-second mark he sees them — at the contact points of Gepard's hands, at the generation origin of the ice expression, blue-white fire at the edge of the cold. Not fire-temperature. Not warmth. The opposite of warmth, the fire of extreme cold at the edge of itself. The specific impossible thing that should not exist and does. He thinks: oh. He thinks: I understand now why Svarog went still. He puts his palm on the ground. He contacts the geological bedrock — the mineral composition, the notation network running through it at ambient rate. He brings the weapon up in the cannon configuration. He looks at the contact vector between the arm's conductance and the formation density that the first Stellaron's attending quality is raising in the ground around the expression. He fires. The cannon output at secondary shaft charge, in a field elevated by the Ice Flames' first Stellaron response — the output is at a level he has not measured before. Significantly higher than the standard secondary shaft charge in a neutral field. The first Stellaron's field response to the Ice Flames is amplifying his own output. From behind: Serval's voice. "Hold the reading — five more seconds." He holds. The recorder data is moving fast. He can feel the field density building around the expression point — the first Stellaron attending at full concentration, everything the planet has orienting toward the four seconds of Ice Flames on the open ground. His cannon is conducting through a field that is becoming, second by second, the most responsive environment he has been in outside the cave's primary junction. "Done," Serval says. "Gepard, pull the expression." The Ice Flames drop. The field density eases. The recorder settles. Luka takes his palm off the ground. He looks at the weapon. "How much higher?" he says to Serval. She reads the recorder. She reads it again. She says, with the specific flatness of someone who needs a moment to fully believe a data point: "In an Ice Flames field at four seconds — your cannon output matches the primary cave junction at fifteen-second contact." He looks at her. "The primary junction at fifteen seconds," he says. "Yes," she says. "Without touching the primary junction. Just by being in the Ice Flames field at that density." He looks at Gepard. Gepard looks at him. "The two-component system," Gepard says, slowly. "Yes," Serval says. "The arm and the weapon are the same system. But the Ice Flames are also part of the system." She looks at the recorder. "In the surface engagement — if Gepard can hold the full expression for any sustained duration — your output through the weapon reaches the primary junction level without requiring the primary junction." She looks at all of them. "The first Stellaron's own field," she says, "amplifies the weapon. The planet amplifies you when Gepard is in full expression." She pauses. "The system is not just Gepard and Luka and the notation network. The system includes the planet itself." Nobody says anything for a moment. Bronya, from the secondary position: "I felt it," she says. "When the Ice Flames were active — the attending quality intensified. The first Stellaron was responding to the Ice Flames at full field concentration." She looks at her notebook. "And then when the cannon fired—" She pauses. "The planet felt something it recognized." They all look at her. "The weapon fires the planet's own material," she says. "The arm is notation network mineral composition. The weapon is the same material. When the cannon fires in the Ice Flames field — the planet is receiving its own signal back." She looks at the field notebook. "Recognition. The same quality as when Gepard named the Great Glacial Aegir. The planet recognized itself." Serval looks at the recorder data. She writes: SIMULTANEOUS ICE FLAMES AND CANNON — CANNON OUTPUT REACHES PRIMARY JUNCTION LEVEL WITHOUT JUNCTION CONTACT. PLANET FIELD AMPLIFIES WEAPON WHEN GEPARD IN FULL EXPRESSION. SYSTEM COMPONENT COUNT: ARM, WEAPON, ICE FLAMES, PLANET FIELD. THE SYSTEM IS COMPLETE. She caps her pen. She looks at the open ground where the Ice Flames were four seconds ago. "Well," she says. "Well," Luka says. Gepard looks at his hands. At the standard ice expression, settled back to ambient. Dunn says, to nobody in particular: "That was something." "Yes," Gepard says. "Sir," Dunn says. "What the Ice Flames do to the field — if the field is the planet—" He stops. "Say it," Gepard says. "When you use the full expression in the surface engagement," Dunn says, "are you — fighting the planet? Or working with it?" Gepard looks at the open ground. At the geological bedrock. At the mineral composition of the formation density around him — the same mineral composition as the weapon, the arm, the notation network, all of it the same system. "Working with it," he says. "The planet is doing what the planet does. The Ice Flames are what the planet produced in me through the northern ridge. The weapon is built from the planet's own material. We are all — expressions of the same thing." He looks at Bronya. "She is hearing the planet from the inside. I am expressing the planet from the outside. The same planet." Bronya looks at him across the open ground. She thinks: this is what I was trying to say to Svarog on day nine and did not have the words for. This is what the notation means when it says the cave receives what arrives and echoes it toward what is needed. The planet is working with itself, through all of us, the way a body works through all of its systems. She writes in the field notebook: The engagement is not a battle against the first Stellaron. It is the planet completing something. We are how the planet completes it. She closes the notebook. "Tomorrow," Gepard says, to the group. "Tomorrow," Serval confirms. They go back to the city. —— That night, Bronya and Gepard are at the outer perimeter ledge of the Restricted Zone. Not for training. Not for the monitoring report. It is 2100 and the city is doing its night below them and the Everwinter wind is from the north and they are standing at the ledge edge because she said come to the ledge this morning and he came. "The moon chapter," he says. "You keep thinking about it," she says. "Yes," he says. She looks at the dome. At the pale light. At the unreachable real thing above it. "What part?" she says. "The standing on the moon and looking back," he says. "The reflected light returning to its source." He pauses. "Today — the session. The cannon returning the planet's own signal to the planet. The Ice Flames that are the planet's extreme expression in me, given back to the planet through the expression." He pauses. "That is what the moon chapter describes. Standing on the external thing and returning the light to the source." She looks at the dome. "We are all returning the light," she says. "Everything that has been given to us — the Ice Flames, the notation recognition, the attending quality, the corridor warmth — we are giving it back." "In the engagement," he says. "Before the engagement too," she says. "The training. The notation reading. Oleg's log. Sampo's channel reads. All of it." She looks at the city below. "We have been returning the light since the beginning. We did not know that is what we were doing." He looks at the dome. She looks at him. At twenty-five years old with the Ice Flames in his hands and the northern ridge behind him and the surface engagement ahead. At the person who says things he has thought about for two days and means them completely. She takes his hand. He does not move it. The Everwinter wind comes from the north. The city runs its night. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1, attending, thinking about today. Fourteen weeks. They stand at the ledge with the city below and the dome above and the planet thinking about what it saw. —— At 1500, Clara takes Oleg to Boulder Town. Not for any reason connected to the cave or the notation or the first Stellaron preparation. He said at lunch that he had never been to the Fighter's Circuit and she said you have never been to the Fighter's Circuit and he said no and she said let's go and they went. The Fighter's Circuit preliminary rounds run on Wednesdays. It is Wednesday. Oleg looks at the fight ring the way he looks at most things — with complete attention and no commentary until he has watched long enough to understand what he is looking at. Clara has been to Boulder Town three times now: the day with the Moles during the nineteen days, the day after the battle when they cleaned up the courtyard, and now. She knows where the good spots are. She takes Oleg to the good spots. They stand at the railing and watch the first preliminary bout. "The one on the left is going to win," Oleg says, after approximately forty seconds. "How do you know?" Clara says. "The one on the right is favoring their left side," he says. "They were hit there in the last fight. You can see it in how they stand." Clara looks at the one on the right. She sees it — the very slight lean away from the left, the arm held fractionally higher on that side. She would not have noticed it if he had not pointed it out. She thinks: this is what Oleg does. He watches things until he understands them and then he says the accurate thing. He does this with notation characters. He does this with density readings. He does this with fights. "When did you start going to the Fighter's Circuit?" she says. "Always," he says. "My father took me when I was small. He was from Boulder Town." He watches the fight. "He liked the Circuit. He said you learn a lot about people by watching how they fight." "What do you learn?" Clara says. "Whether they panic," he says. "Some people fight well until they are losing, and then they panic and make mistakes. Some people fight badly until they are losing, and then they get calm and start making good decisions. You can tell which one a person is by watching them take the first hit." Clara watches the one on the right take a hit to the favored side. They flinch. Their guard drops for two seconds. The one on the left presses the opening. "Panic," Clara says. "Yes," Oleg says. The one on the left wins. They stay for three more bouts. Oleg is correct about all of them in the first forty seconds of watching. Clara stops predicting independently and starts trying to see what Oleg sees in the way the fighters hold themselves before the first hit. "You could be doing something useful with this," she says, during the fourth bout. "I am doing something useful with this," he says. "I am here." "I mean in terms of preparation data," she says. "Knowing how people respond to the first hit — that is relevant to the surface engagement." He looks at her. "Clara," he says. "What?" she says. "We are at the Fighter's Circuit," he says. "On a Wednesday afternoon. We are not at the workshop." She looks at him. "There is a very large dangerous event coming," he says. "That is true. It is also Wednesday. Both of these things are true at the same time." She looks at the fight ring. The fourth bout is at its midpoint. The one on the left is getting tired. "You are right," she says. "Often," he says. She looks at him. He looks at the fight ring with the expression he gets when he thinks something is funnier than he is letting on. It is a very specific expression. She has been cataloguing it. "Very rarely," she says. "Often," he says, with the same expression. "Sometimes," she says. He produces something from his jacket. Not a pastry — a small packet of roasted seeds that are the standard Boulder Town fight-watching snack. He holds the packet out. She takes a handful. "The one on the left is going to lose this one," he says. "I thought you said they were winning," she says. "They were winning," he says. "Now they are tired and the one on the right is fresh and has been waiting for exactly this moment." He watches. "Thirty seconds." The one on the right does something quick and unexpected. The one on the left, tired, reacts too slowly. The bout ends. "Twenty-eight seconds," Clara says. "Close enough," he says. She eats the roasted seeds and watches the next bout start and thinks about nothing except what she is looking at right now. Oleg is here. The fight ring is lit. The crowd is loud. It is Wednesday. The first Stellaron is building toward something in the geological structure below the city. It will still be building toward something when the fight ends. For right now: this. —— At 2000, Pela is in the analysis room when the model does something unexpected. She has been running the acceleration projections with the early-onset data — the point-three-to-four daily increment that started at day eleven rather than day twenty-five as the historical dataset predicted. She has been building the updated timeline projection. She has been careful and specific and the model is solid. Then the model does something unexpected. She runs it twice. She runs it three times. She sits back. She has been building this model for thirteen days. She has been updating it with every new data point. She has Oleg's parallel log as supplementary data, Bronya's field notes as the Guardian signal correlation data, Dunn's daily geometry reads as the formation alignment data, the training session outputs from Serval, the northwest channel warmth reads from Sampo. All of it. And the model, with all of it, is saying something she did not expect to say. She checks the northern Sector A density projection against the historical dataset's acceleration curve. She checks the Guardian signal gap narrowing rate against the attending quality escalation. She checks the formation geometry concentration rate against the first Stellaron signal quality change log. The model says: the plateau phase that was predicted between the acceleration and the final approach — the model cannot confirm it exists. Not that the plateau is shorter. That there may not be a plateau. She stares at this for a long time. She writes to Svarog: I need to talk to you about the model. Tonight if possible. He responds in two minutes, which is fast for Svarog: Come to the workshop. She goes. —— Svarog looks at the model for seven minutes without speaking. She has been in the workshop long enough to know that Svarog's seven-minute silences are the productive kind. She sits at the secondary bench and waits. "The plateau," he says finally. "May not exist," she says. "Or may be so short it is effectively not a plateau." She pulls up the projection. "The historical dataset shows a plateau phase between the acceleration and the final approach. But the historical dataset is based on forty years of dormancy monitoring. It has never observed an active acceleration." She pauses. "The current acceleration began at day eleven. By day fourteen we were already above the dataset's expected acceleration rate. By day eighteen—" She pulls up the projection line. "The projection suggests the final approach phase may begin directly after the acceleration peak, without a plateau interval." Svarog looks at the projection. "When is the acceleration peak," he says. She swallows. "The model says the acceleration peak is somewhere between day twenty and day twenty-five," she says. "Which means the final approach could begin as early as—" "Three weeks," he says. "Or less," she says. She looks at him. At the oldest consciousness on the planet, who has been monitoring the first Stellaron's signal for forty years, looking at a model that is telling him the timeline is not what either of them thought it was. "Is the model wrong?" she says. She says it as a genuine question. She has built this model carefully and she believes in it, but Svarog has forty years of data she did not have two weeks ago. "No," he says. "The model is not wrong." He looks at the projection. "I have been watching the signal quality for forty years. I have seen the acceleration indicators before — they are there in the geological data at very low amplitude. They began approximately six months before the second Stellaron's defeat." He pauses. "They are not low amplitude now." "When did they reach this level before?" she says. He looks at her. "They have not," he says. "The historical dataset does not contain an acceleration at this rate. What you are seeing in the model is genuine. The plateau may not exist." She breathes. "I need to tell Bronya," she says. "Yes," he says. "Tonight." "Will she—" She stops. She starts again. "The training sessions. The weapon calibration. Serval's model. The surface engagement preparation. It is all built on fifteen to sixteen weeks." "Yes," Svarog says. "And if the plateau does not exist," she says, "and the final approach begins at day twenty to twenty-five—" She does the math that she has already done three times. "We have three weeks. Maybe less." Svarog looks at the projection. "Go tell Bronya," he says. She goes. —— Bronya is at the outer perimeter ledge when Pela finds her. She is standing at the ledge edge with the field notebook and the Everwinter wind from the north, and she is looking at the dome, and when she hears Pela's footsteps she turns. She looks at Pela's face. "Tell me," she says. Pela tells her. Bronya listens to all of it. She does not interrupt. She does not look at the monitoring data on Pela's tablet while Pela is speaking — she looks at Pela directly, listening to the explanation of what the model found and what it means and why it is not wrong and what the projection says. When Pela finishes, Bronya is quiet for a moment. She looks at the dome. She looks at the field notebook in her hand. She opens it. She reads this morning's entry: The planet is thinking about today. She reads yesterday's: The first Stellaron is still attending to what happened. It did not return to baseline. She reads day nine: The gap is narrower every morning. She closes the notebook. She looks at Pela. At sixteen years old at 2030 on a Wednesday night, standing at the outer perimeter ledge of the Restricted Zone because she found something in the model and came immediately. "You did not wait until morning," Bronya says. "No," Pela says. "Good," Bronya says. "That was the right call." She looks at the city below. "Three weeks. Maybe less." "Maybe less," Pela says. Bronya looks at the dome. She looks at the attending quality in her hands — the warmth, the gap between herself and the source. She tries to estimate the gap. Not the daily increment. The absolute distance remaining. She thinks: it is closer than I thought. She thinks: I have been feeling it narrow every morning and I knew the direction but not the destination. She thinks: the model is not wrong. She looks at Pela. "Who else needs to know tonight?" she says. "Gepard," Pela says. "Svarog already knows. Serval should know for the calibration model. Clara should know for the cave timing." "Tonight," Bronya says. "All of them tonight." She starts moving toward the Restricted Zone entrance. "Come with me." Pela falls in step with her. They are moving fast, not running, but with the particular pace of the Supreme Guardian of Belobog who has received significant information and is acting on it. Pela matches the pace without effort. She has been matching this pace for months. The city below the perimeter ledge runs its night, unaware. The first Stellaron holds at 3.1, attending, thinking about today. The acceleration is building in the geological structure at the pace that Pela's model found and Svarog confirmed. Three weeks. Maybe less. The training accelerates tomorrow. END OF CHAPTER 50
